METRO.3 Transfer rules (gates)
METRO.3.1 Checkpoint gate
Predicate: (\mathsf{ChkValid}(h(\mathsf{Chk}),h(\mathsf{Hdr}),h(\mathsf{LedHdr})))Requires: checkpoint object + header hashesFails with: checkpoint hash mismatch / policy mismatch / missing store snapshotMitigates: stabilize checkpoint (mandatory normalizers + audits) if permitted
METRO.3.2 Convention gate
Predicate: (h(\mathsf{SpecOut})=h(\mathsf{SpecIn})) or explicit bridge chain presentRequires: Spec hashes and bridge availabilityFails with: missing bridge class / missing post-bridge normalizer / implicit coercionMitigates: insert minimal certified bridge chain + required normalizers; else halt
METRO.3.3 Witness gate
Predicate: required witness set verifies and binds to (\mathsf{Ctx})Requires: provenance witness; method ids; LCD objects; deterministic verifiersFails with: binding mismatch / over-claim / missing LCD / unsupported methodMitigates: proof regeneration / reroute to supported method / escalation only if explicitly permitted
METRO.3.4 Evidence gate
Predicate: retention policy satisfied; mandatory detector evidence presentRequires: detector schedule + retention class capsFails with: missing mandatory evidence / nondeterministic omissionMitigates: deterministic truncation of optional evidence; else halt if mandatory evidence infeasible
METRO.3.5 Policy compatibility gate
Predicate: policy hashes match or explicit policy-bridge receipt consumedRequires: runtime header hashes; corridor hash; verifier set hashFails with: policy mismatch / disclosure mismatch (for public mode)Mitigates: policy migration/bridge if supported and authorized; else re-execute under required policy

ABSTRACT
This treatise specifies a single, executable framework that completes ordinary mathematical practice by making representation change a first-class, machine-checkable object. The framework defines a closed operational semantics in which every admissible mathematical act—definition, computation, transformation, approximation, proof, optimization, composition, reuse, repair, and governance—is realized as a finite program whose acceptance is determined by explicit predicates and locally verifiable witnesses, and whose “truth in the system” is defined by deterministic replay of an append-only ledger under pinned policies.
A. The completion target: representation transport as the missing operational layer
Ordinary practice routinely changes representations—coordinate systems, bases, discretizations, conventions, units, normalization schemes, branch choices, encodings, naming regimes—while treating the preservation of meaning as implicit. The framework completes this practice by formalizing representation transport as a typed, contract-bearing morphism with explicit invariants, explicit tolerances, explicit convention fingerprints, explicit budgets, explicit scope, explicit verification methods, and explicit replay obligations. Meaning is not carried by interpretation; it is carried by certified transport.
B. Canonical objects, lenses, and typed meaning
All artifacts are typed COS objects with canonical serialization, deterministic hashing, and Merkle commitments. A representation is a lens[L=(D,\mathcal{A},\mathcal{I},\mathsf{Adm},\mathsf{NF},\mathsf{Spec},\mathsf{ObsAlg}),]where (D) is a carrier domain; (\mathcal{A}) is an address algebra defining canonical indexing, ordering, and encodings; (\mathcal{I}) is an invariant schema; (\mathsf{Adm}) is an admissibility predicate; (\mathsf{NF}) is a canonical normal form operator; (\mathsf{Spec}) is the convention bundle hash (Sign/Scale/Index/Unit/Domain); and (\mathsf{ObsAlg}) is the observable algebra from which invariant evaluators are allowed to draw. Equality is replaced by typed equivalence under declared invariants and tolerances; identity is replaced by canonical bytes and hashes.
C. Legal transports as contract objects
A representation move is a legal transport contract[\mathsf{LT}=(h(L_{\mathrm{in}}),h(L_{\mathrm{out}}),h(\mathsf{SpecIn}),h(\mathsf{SpecOut}),h(\mathsf{Impl}),h(\mathcal I),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}),h(\mathcal{B}),h(\mathcal{S}),h(\mathcal{W}),h(\mathcal{R}),h(\mathsf{Loss})?),]binding input/output lenses and conventions, an implementation descriptor, invariant clauses and tolerances, budgets, scope, witness profile, replay profile, and optional quotient/loss semantics. A transport is admissible only when its declared predicates pass under the corridor policy (\Omega): type correctness, domain admissibility, convention compatibility (or explicit certified bridging), invariant preservation (exact or tolerance-bounded), budget feasibility (compute/write/branch/tolerance), contradiction consistency, and deterministic replay constraints.
D. Corridor policy (\Omega): legality, safety, and discipline
The corridor policy (\Omega) is a pinned legality environment that enforces:
compute/write/branch/expansion caps and tolerance budgets with pinned accumulation operators,
scope constraints (primary vs secondary writes; protected namespaces; isolation sets),
determinism constraints (canonical ordering rules; numeric policies; seed discipline),
contradiction policies for commitments (per-namespace predicates; allowed resolution kinds; required proof methods),
mandatory detector schedules and evidence retention classes,
throttling and windowed rate limits,
governance hooks for admission, supersession, deprecation, and publication requirements.
(\Omega) separates exploration from commitment, forbids silent coercions, forbids silent approximation inflation, forbids silent state mutation, and ensures every weakening of guarantees is explicit and receipted as a new claim boundary rather than being implicit behavior.
E. Kernel semantics: state substrates, deltas, projector, and verdicts
Execution runs over a minimal fixed kernel of substrates: commitment store, relation store, candidate store, proof store (plus link index), together with headers, tolerance ledger, and step index. Each step computes:
deltas (store-local primitive operation sequences, canonically ordered and hashed),
witnesses (typed, provenance-bound),
cost vector (quantized; policy-pinned),then applies a legality projector that runs mandatory detectors, verifies witnesses, checks budgets/scope/contradictions, and returns a verdict:
ACCEPT: apply all deltas,
PARTIAL: apply a deterministic acceptance mask (drop only policy-declared discardable operations, typically in candidate/evidence regions),
REJECT+REVERT: apply no deltas; post-state equals pre-state; emit deterministic failure evidence.
This projector is the mechanized boundary between “proposed computation” and “committed mathematical act.”
F. Witness system: local verification and anti-laundering
The witness system defines a finite universe of witness types with deterministic polynomial-time verifiers. Every witness binds to the step context:[(\mathsf{op_id},h_P,h_{\mathsf{pre}},r_\Delta,r_{\mathsf{src}},h_{\mathsf{post}},h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{hdr}}),]preventing certificate reuse outside context. Proof objects are separate from authorization: a proof object can be referenced, but a step-local proof witness must bind it into the step context, ensuring authorization cannot be laundered. Proof methods are governed by a pinned method registry and constrained by verification levels and disclosure policies; over-claims are rejected deterministically.
G. Ledger and replay: “truth in the system”
Execution emits an append-only ledger of canonically encoded step records: pre/post hashes, per-store hashes, per-store delta hashes, delta root, witness hash set, evidence hash set, cost vectors, verdicts, and masks. Replay verification deterministically recomputes state transitions from genesis (or from checkpoints), verifies witness bindings and proofs, checks detector obligations, and localizes earliest divergence. A result is “true in the system” iff independent replay under the pinned verifier set reproduces identical post hashes and all required verifiers pass.
Receipts are compact commitments to ledger identity, final state hash, output pin roots, witness roots, optional evidence roots, and policy hashes. Receipt algebra defines refinement/strength preorders, sequential composition via dependency proofs, parallel merge under non-contradiction, and explicit supersession graphs.
H. Deterministic compilation and routing
Given an intent, the compiler deterministically produces:
a goal specification (targets, context, constraints),
a feasibility-checked route through the transform network (library entries + bridges + normalizers),
an executable micro-program (op ids + parameter hashes),
a witness plan (required witness types/methods/LCD),
a ledger plan (detector schedule, partial acceptance policy, caps),
optional explainability artifacts (structured tie-break evidence and infeasibility certificates).
Route selection is deterministic via a lexicographic cost functional prioritizing exactness, feasibility under (\Omega), minimal tolerance usage, minimal hop count, minimal witness burden, minimal protected writes, and lexicographic tie-breaks.
I. Normalization and convention discipline
Convention fingerprints (Sign/Scale/Index/Unit/Domain) are explicit and hashed. Composition is permitted only when convention hashes match or when certified bridges are inserted. Required post-bridge normalizers are mandatory. This eliminates silent sign flips, silent rescaling, silent index permutations, silent unit conversions, silent branch changes, and silent naming drift—the dominant sources of hidden error in applied mathematics and computation.
J. Search under constraint, ambiguity objects, and certified selection
Exploration is permitted only as bounded candidate generation with deterministic enumeration, quantized scoring, deterministic truncation, and explicit branch caps. Ambiguity sets are first-class objects. Transition from exploration to commitment requires certified selection criteria and selection proofs; no commitment is permitted “because it scored best” without proof-carrying justification and feasibility certification under (\Omega).
K. Repair, optimization, stability, and tunneling
Defects are detected by deterministic detectors producing structured evidence. Certified repair is a legal program that applies minimal certified bridges, regrids, renormalizers, reroutes, or proof regeneration steps, emits completion proofs, and yields repair receipts. Optimization is proof-carrying rewriting: program transforms are accepted only with equivalence proofs and corridor non-weakening proofs. Long-chain control is enforced by checkpoints, tolerance ledgers with forced mitigations, loop diagnostics (holonomy) on closed routes, and certified tunneling jumps that move between representational regions through stability anchors and minimal bridge chains.
L. System architecture and conformance
The reference architecture decomposes into object layer, verifier layer, execution layer, and compilation layer with deterministic APIs and typed error contracts. Conformance is specified as executable plans producing receipts: canonical encoding tests, numeric policy tests, verifier conformance tests, kernel/projector conformance tests, detector schedule conformance tests, and end-to-end interoperability tests. An implementation is conformant iff it reproduces identical bytes, hashes, ledgers, receipts, and deterministic failure evidence under identical policy hashes.
M. Closure and completion
The framework is representationally complete for practice in the following operational sense: every admissible practice act can be expressed as a program over the kernel, accepted only under explicit contracts and witnesses, and certified by replayable ledgers and composable receipts. Soundness is bounded by declared invariants, tolerances, verification levels, conventions, and disclosure modes. Completeness is representational: expanding what can be done is a matter of admitting additional lenses, transforms, bridges, invariants, and proof methods under the same discipline, with conformance receipts. The system is self-hosting: it can define, admit, compile, execute, verify, repair, and optimize its own artifacts under bounded policies without uncontrolled recursion.

CHAPTER 1 — FOUNDATIONS: OBJECTS, REPRESENTATIONS, AND MEANING (Final Draft)
1.1 Mathematical objects as typed entities
1.1.1 Type tags and semantic domains
1.1.1(a) Type universe and semantic carriers.Fix a countable set of type identifiers (\mathsf{TypeId}) and a family of semantic carrier spaces ({\mathsf{Sem}[t]}_{t\in\mathsf{TypeId}}). A typed mathematical object is a pair[o := (t,,\mathbf{b})\in \mathsf{TypeId}\times {0,1}^{8\ast},]where (t) is a type tag and (\mathbf{b}) is a canonical byte encoding. The semantic meaning of (o) is defined only via a typed decoder[\mathsf{Dec}_t:{0,1}^{8\ast}\to \mathsf{Sem}[t],]together with a well-formedness predicate (\mathsf{WF}_t(\mathbf{b})). An object is well-formed iff (\mathsf{WF}_t(\mathbf{b})=\mathsf{true}) and (\mathsf{Dec}_t(\mathbf{b})) is defined.
1.1.1(b) Canonical encoding and object identity.Each type (t) provides a canonical encoder (\mathsf{Enc}_t:\mathsf{Sem}[t]\to{0,1}^{8\ast}) such that for all (x\in\mathsf{Sem}[t]),[\mathsf{WF}_t(\mathsf{Enc}_t(x))=\mathsf{true},\qquad \mathsf{Dec}_t(\mathsf{Enc}_t(x)) = x.]Object identity is content-addressed by a fixed cryptographic hash function (H:{0,1}^{8\ast}\to{0,1}^{256}). The object hash is[h(o) := H!\big(\mathsf{Tag}(t),|,\mathbf{b}\big),]where (\mathsf{Tag}(t)) is the canonical encoding of (t), and (|) is byte concatenation. Two objects (o,o') are identical iff (h(o)=h(o')). Collision-resistance is an external assumption; within the framework, identity is defined by this hash.
1.1.1(c) Typed schemas and parametric types.Types may be parametric. A parametric type is specified by a schema (\sigma) with parameter space (\mathsf{Par}[\sigma]). The instantiated type id is (t=\langle\sigma,\theta\rangle) for (\theta\in\mathsf{Par}[\sigma]), and the semantic carrier is (\mathsf{Sem}[\sigma,\theta]). Parameter values must be canonically encoded and included in the type tag encoding to ensure that[(\sigma,\theta)\neq(\sigma,\theta') \implies \mathsf{Tag}(\langle\sigma,\theta\rangle)\neq \mathsf{Tag}(\langle\sigma,\theta'\rangle).]
1.1.1(d) Observable semantics.Each type (t) exposes a finite set of admissible observables (\mathsf{Obs}[t]). An observable is a deterministic map[\phi:\mathsf{Sem}[t]\to \mathbb{R}^k]with fixed output dimension (k) and a canonical quantization policy (Section 1.4.4). Observables are the only permitted interface for invariant measurement and certification, ensuring that all verification statements reduce to deterministic computations over typed decodings.
1.1.2 Equality vs equivalence vs refinement
1.1.2(a) Definitional equality (byte-level).For objects of identical type tag (t), define definitional equality[o \equiv o' \iff \mathbf{b}=\mathbf{b}' \quad (\text{bytewise}).]Definitional equality implies identical hashes and identical decoded semantics.
1.1.2(b) Semantic equality (type-level).For (o=(t,\mathbf{b})) and (o'=(t,\mathbf{b}')) well-formed, define semantic equality[o =_t o' \iff \mathsf{Dec}_t(\mathbf{b}) = \mathsf{Dec}_t(\mathbf{b}').]Canonical encoding requires (\mathsf{Enc}_t) to select a unique representative for each semantic value; thus, under the canonicalization axioms of 1.4, (o=_t o') implies (o\equiv o').
1.1.2(c) Contracted equivalence under invariants.Fix an invariant family (\mathcal{I}) (Section 1.3). For objects (o,o') of possibly different types (or different representations of the same underlying entity) define ((\mathcal{I},\varepsilon))-equivalence[o \sim_{\mathcal{I},\varepsilon} o'\iff\forall I\in\mathcal{I}:\ \big|I(o)-I(o')\big|\le \varepsilon_I,]where (I(o)) denotes the invariant evaluation over the object via the declared evaluation interface, and (\varepsilon_I) is the tolerance bound for invariant (I). Equivalence is meaningful only relative to an explicit invariant set and explicit tolerances.
1.1.2(d) Refinement preorder and strength ordering.Define a refinement relation (\preceq) between objects, proofs, or claims as follows. A claim (C'=(\mathcal{I}',\varepsilon')) refines (C=(\mathcal{I},\varepsilon)) iff[\mathcal{I}\subseteq \mathcal{I}'\quad \text{and}\quad \forall I\in\mathcal{I}:\ \varepsilon'_I\le \varepsilon_I.]A verified artifact produced under (C') is acceptable anywhere (C) is required. This induces a partial order on verification strength, and forbids upgrading claim strength without explicit re-verification under a stronger contract.
1.1.3 Context, parameters, and scoping
1.1.3(a) Context objects and binding discipline.A context is a finite map (\Gamma) from canonical keys to object hashes:[\Gamma:\ \mathsf{Key}\rightharpoonup {0,1}^{256}.]A context is well-formed iff every referenced hash resolves to a well-formed typed object, and keys satisfy canonical naming rules (Section 1.4.2). Contexts are canonically encoded and hashed:[h(\Gamma)=H(\mathsf{Enc}_\Gamma(\Gamma)).]
1.1.3(b) Parameter schemas and canonical parameterization.A parameter schema (\Pi) is a finite list of named fields with declared types. An instantiated parameter map (P) must satisfy: (i) all required fields present, (ii) values well-formed and type-correct, (iii) canonical encoding order by lexicographic field name, and (iv) inclusion of referenced object hashes rather than raw content. The parameter hash is[h(P)=H(\mathsf{Enc}_P(P)).]All computation, verification, and replay is conditioned on (h(P)); no implicit parameters are permitted.
1.1.3(c) Scope boundaries and write targets.A scope is a set of allowed write targets (\mathsf{Scope}\subseteq \mathsf{Target}), where targets include commitment namespaces, proof stores, relation stores, and candidate stores (formalized in later chapters). A computation step is scope-correct iff every emitted delta is confined to allowed targets. Scope is part of the corridor policy (\Omega) (defined in later chapters) and is referenced here as an abstract predicate (\mathsf{ScopeOK}(\cdot)).
1.1.3(d) Referential transparency and closure.All semantic functions in this treatise are defined to be referentially transparent: identical typed inputs and identical parameter hashes imply identical outputs. Any operation requiring nondeterminism must be expressed as a deterministic function of an explicitly pinned seed object (whose hash is included in the context and parameters). Thus “randomness” is a typed dependency, not an ambient behavior.
1.1.4 Canonical interfaces for object evaluation
1.1.4(a) Evaluation interface.Each type (t) may expose evaluation procedures through a fixed interface[\mathsf{Eval}_t:\ (\mathsf{Sem}[t]\times \mathsf{Ctx}\times \mathsf{Par})\to \mathsf{Sem}[t']\cup \mathsf{Err},]where (\mathsf{Ctx}) is a resolved context, (\mathsf{Par}) is a resolved parameter structure, and (\mathsf{Err}) is a typed error object. (\mathsf{Eval}_t) must be deterministic and total over well-formed inputs or return a deterministic error.
1.1.4(b) Observable interface.Each observable (\phi\in\mathsf{Obs}[t]) is implemented as a deterministic computation (\mathsf{ObsEval}_{t,\phi}) returning a quantized numeric vector. Observables are the sole admissible inputs to invariant evaluators; invariants cannot depend on untyped external behavior.
1.1.4(c) Local-check interface for proofs.Proof objects must supply a local-check payload sufficient to verify the claimed property deterministically in time polynomial in the proof size and local-check size. Each proof type declares a verification program id (\mathsf{VerId}) and required local-check object hashes. The verifier is a deterministic function[\mathsf{Verify}(\mathsf{VerId},\ \text{proof},\ \text{resolved local-check objects},\ \text{context},\ \text{parameters})\in{\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}}.]
1.1.4(d) Error interface and failure codes.Errors are first-class typed objects, with canonical failure codes and evidence payload hashes. Any failure must be representable as a typed error whose hash can be pinned and replay-verified. Silent failures, implicit coercions, and undefined behavior are prohibited by the evaluation interface contract.
1.2 Representations (lenses) as typed carriers
1.2.1 Carrier domain (D) and admissible payloads
1.2.1(a) Lens definition.A lens is a triple[L=(D,\mathcal A,\mathcal I),]where (D) is a carrier domain, (\mathcal A) is an address algebra, and (\mathcal I) is an invariant schema. A lens is itself a typed object with canonical encoding and a stable identifier (h(L)).
1.2.1(b) Payloads in a lens.A payload in lens (L) is an element (p\in D) represented as a typed object (o_p) whose semantic decoding yields (p). Admissibility is determined by a predicate[\mathsf{Adm}_L(p)=\mathsf{true/false},]which must be deterministic and is part of lens definition. If (\mathsf{Adm}_L(p)=\mathsf{false}), the payload is illegal for operations requiring that lens.
1.2.1(c) Total vs partial carriers.A carrier domain may be total (all decoded objects admissible) or partial (only a subset admissible). Partiality must be explicit via (\mathsf{Adm}_L). Any operation mapping into (D) must produce either an admissible payload or a typed error object; no out-of-domain results may be committed.
1.2.1(d) Carrier compatibility.Two lenses (L_1=(D_1,\mathcal A_1,\mathcal I_1)) and (L_2=(D_2,\mathcal A_2,\mathcal I_2)) are carrier-compatible for a proposed transport (T) only if the transport declares a deterministic carrier map (\widetilde T:D_1\to D_2) and the address algebras are compatible under declared convention rules (Section 1.4.2 and Chapter 7 in the treatise outline).
1.2.2 Address algebra (\mathcal A): indices, ordering, encoding
1.2.2(a) Address algebra components.An address algebra is a triple[\mathcal A=(\mathsf{Idx},\ \preceq,\ \mathsf{Enc}),]where (\mathsf{Idx}) is an index set, (\preceq) is a total order on (\mathsf{Idx}), and (\mathsf{Enc}:\mathsf{Idx}\to {0,1}^{8\ast}) is a canonical encoding.
1.2.2(b) Address isomorphisms.An address isomorphism between (\mathcal A_1) and (\mathcal A_2) is a bijection (\pi:\mathsf{Idx}_1\to\mathsf{Idx}_2) such that[i\preceq_1 j \iff \pi(i)\preceq_2 \pi(j),]and the encodings are consistent under a declared bridge object. Composition across lenses is permitted only when either (i) the address algebras match, or (ii) an explicit certified bridge transform provides the mapping (\pi).
1.2.2(c) Canonical enumeration and determinism.All set-like collections indexed by (\mathsf{Idx}) must be enumerated in (\preceq)-order, and all iteration over indices must follow this canonical enumeration. Any deviation constitutes nondeterminism and is illegal for replayable execution.
1.2.2(d) Addressability as extraction primitive.Addresses serve as the extraction boundary: any object claiming to be a component, coefficient, sample, vertex, or residue must carry an address. The address encoding is included in canonical hashing of the object, ensuring that extraction is stable under replay and cannot be altered by representation changes.
1.2.3 Invariant schema (\mathcal I): exact and tolerance-bounded
1.2.3(a) Invariant identifiers and domains.An invariant schema (\mathcal I) is a finite set of invariant identifiers (\mathsf{InvId}) together with evaluation functions[I:\mathsf{Sem}[t]\to \mathbb{R}^{k_I}]for declared object types (t), implemented via observable interfaces. Each invariant includes an admissibility predicate specifying when it applies.
1.2.3(b) Exact invariants.An invariant (I) is exact if its tolerance is (\varepsilon_I=0) and equality is measured after canonical quantization. Exact invariants are enforced as hard constraints: violation implies illegality for any step claiming to preserve (I).
1.2.3(c) Tolerance-bounded invariants.A tolerance-bounded invariant specifies a bound (\varepsilon_I>0) and a norm (|\cdot|_I) on (\mathbb{R}^{k_I}). Preservation under a transport (T) is:[\big|I(p)-I(\widetilde T(p))\big|_I \le \varepsilon_I.]The tolerance (\varepsilon_I) is an explicit parameter of the contract; it cannot be increased without producing a refinement artifact under the framework’s refinement ordering.
1.2.3(d) Invariant applicability and composability.Invariant sets compose by intersection under transform composition unless explicitly changed by a declared quotient contract (formalized later). If a transform does not claim to preserve an invariant, it must declare so; invariants may not be implicitly assumed across a chain.
1.2.4 Interpretation/evaluation maps and observables
1.2.4(a) Interpretation map.Each lens (L) provides a deterministic interpretation map[\llbracket\cdot\rrbracket_L : D \to \mathsf{Sem}[\mathsf{PayloadType}(L)],]which converts carrier elements into semantic objects for invariant evaluation and proof checking. (\llbracket\cdot\rrbracket_L) is part of the lens object and is referenced by hash.
1.2.4(b) Observable algebra.Each lens defines an observable algebra (\mathsf{ObsAlg}(L)\subseteq \mathsf{Obs}[t]) that determines what measurements are permitted in that representation. Invariants are functions of observables; therefore, the lens constrains which invariants can be evaluated without further transport.
1.2.4(c) Evaluation under context.Interpretation and observable evaluation may depend on context (\Gamma) and parameters (P), but only through explicitly referenced hashes. Any dependence on ambient state is prohibited. Thus[I_L(p;\Gamma,P)]is deterministic for fixed (p,\Gamma,P).
1.2.4(d) Observational equivalence.Two payloads (p\in D) and (q\in D') are observationally equivalent under an invariant set (\mathcal I) if all invariants in (\mathcal I) evaluate to equal (or tolerance-bounded) values after their respective lens interpretations. This formalizes “same meaning, different representation” only through explicit observables and contracts.
1.3 Invariants as enforceable contracts
1.3.1 Invariant families: algebraic, analytic, probabilistic, geometric
1.3.1(a) Algebraic invariants.Algebraic invariants encode structural laws: associativity constraints on operations, commutation relations, group actions, ring identities, homomorphism properties, and discrete symmetries. They are expressed as equalities of observable evaluations on canonical test sets or as verifiable identities with bounded local-check data.
1.3.1(b) Analytic invariants.Analytic invariants include norms, energies, inner products, Lipschitz bounds, spectral norms, monotonic functionals, and stability measures. They require explicit quantization and norms (|\cdot|_I), and are enforced under declared tolerances with explicit accumulation rules.
1.3.1(c) Probabilistic invariants.Probabilistic invariants include mass conservation, positivity, normalization, entropy constraints, divergence bounds, stationarity residuals, and mixing-rate signatures. They require explicit definitions of the measure space or discrete support, plus explicit quantization and deterministic evaluation.
1.3.1(d) Geometric invariants.Geometric invariants include metric preservation, orientation conventions, curvature constraints, holonomy/loop defect measures, and coordinate-free quantities. In this treatise, geometric invariants are enforced only through explicit observables and certified transport, never via informal geometric intuition.
1.3.2 Invariant measurement and quantization
1.3.2(a) Quantization policy.Every numeric invariant evaluation must be reduced to a canonical quantized value. A quantization policy is a typed object (Q) specifying representation (fixed-point, rational, bounded float with pinned rounding), scale factors, and canonical rounding mode. Quantization is applied before comparison.
1.3.2(b) Measurement function.An invariant measurement is a deterministic function[\mathsf{Meas}_I:\ (o,\Gamma,P)\to \mathbb{Z}^{k_I}]returning quantized integers representing the invariant value. This ensures comparisons are exact at the byte/bit level.
1.3.2(c) Comparison and tolerance checking.Tolerance checking is performed on quantized values using a declared metric (|\cdot|I). For scalar invariants, (|\cdot|) is used; for vector invariants, a declared norm (e.g., (\ell\infty), (\ell_2)) is used with explicitly pinned implementation.
1.3.2(d) Measurement evidence objects.Every invariant check may emit an evidence object containing: invariant id, pre/post quantized values, computed difference, tolerance, and the hashes of the objects measured. Evidence is a typed object eligible for pinning and replay verification.
1.3.3 Invariant composition across transform chains
1.3.3(a) Invariant transport semantics.For a sequence of transports (\widetilde T_1,\dots,\widetilde T_n), the invariant claim set is[\mathcal I_{\text{chain}}=\bigcap_{k=1}^n \mathcal I(\widetilde T_k),]unless a transform declares a quotient contract that explicitly changes the meaning of “preserved” (formalized later in the treatise).
1.3.3(b) Tolerance accumulation.For each invariant (I), tolerances accumulate via a declared operator (\oplus_I):[\varepsilon^{(n)}_I = \varepsilon^{(1)}_I \oplus_I \cdots \oplus_I \varepsilon^{(n)}_I.]The accumulation operator must be pinned as part of the policy so that compilation and verification use identical rules.
1.3.3(c) Chain-level admissibility.A chain is admissible under an invariant budget iff for every enforced invariant (I),[\varepsilon^{(n)}_I \le \varepsilon^{\max}_I,]where (\varepsilon^{\max}) is part of the corridor policy. Chain admissibility is a compile-time predicate when all tolerances are known, and a run-time predicate when some bounds are empirical but still deterministically computed.
1.3.3(d) Strength monotonicity.A chain may not claim a stronger invariant set or a tighter tolerance at its end than is supported by its weakest link, unless it includes an explicit strengthening step whose correctness is itself certified. Strengthening is never implicit.
1.3.4 Failure thresholds and near-violation semantics
1.3.4(a) Three-valued invariant verdict.Invariant checks return a verdict in ({\mathsf{OK},\mathsf{NEAR},\mathsf{FAIL}}) where:
(\mathsf{OK}): drift (\le \varepsilon_I\cdot \alpha)
(\mathsf{NEAR}): (\varepsilon_I\cdot \alpha < \text{drift} \le \varepsilon_I)
(\mathsf{FAIL}): drift (> \varepsilon_I)with fixed (\alpha\in(0,1)) pinned by policy.
1.3.4(b) Failures as enforceable illegality.A (\mathsf{FAIL}) verdict on any invariant declared as hard by the corridor policy renders the step illegal. No commitment derived from an illegal step may be accepted.
1.3.4(c) Near-violations as mandatory mitigation triggers.A (\mathsf{NEAR}) verdict triggers mandatory mitigation actions specified by policy: normalization insertion, rerouting to exact transforms, tightening of branch caps, or recomputation with stricter local-check data. Mitigation actions must be expressed as explicit transforms, not narrative advice.
1.3.4(d) Failure evidence and auditability.All (\mathsf{NEAR}) and (\mathsf{FAIL}) events produce evidence objects that bind: invariant id, measured values, thresholds, and the step context hashes (parameters, pre/post). This makes failures replayable and extractable as structured artifacts.
1.4 Canonicalization as the basis of determinism
1.4.1 Canonical serialization and hashing
1.4.1(a) Canonical serialization axiom.For each type (t), (\mathsf{Enc}_t) must be a function (not a relation): the same semantic value yields exactly one byte encoding. Canonicalization forbids degrees of freedom in field ordering, integer formatting, whitespace, floating formatting, or collection ordering.
1.4.1(b) Canonical decoding axiom.(\mathsf{Dec}_t) must be total over well-formed bytes and must reject ill-formed bytes deterministically. Decoder behavior is part of the trusted computing base and must be version-hashed and pinned.
1.4.1(c) Hash completeness axiom.Every external dependency required to interpret or verify an object must be referenced by hash, either within the object’s encoding or within an explicitly bound context object. No unverifiable external dependencies are permitted.
1.4.1(d) Hash stability theorem (deterministic identity).If all encoders and naming rules are canonical and all operations apply deltas in canonical order, then for any execution step with identical typed inputs and identical parameter hashes, the resulting object hashes are identical. This theorem is the determinism backbone used by replay verification.
1.4.2 Canonical naming and addressing rules
1.4.2(a) Canonical key grammar.Define a permitted alphabet and a deterministic normalization function (\mathsf{NormKey}) mapping any admissible string to a canonical key. Keys are valid iff (\mathsf{NormKey}(k)=k). All keys in contexts, pins, and indices must be valid.
1.4.2(b) Namespace partitioning.Keys live in disjoint namespaces for different artifact classes (objects, transforms, proofs, plans, receipts, etc.). A namespace is a prefix with a pinned schema describing allowed suffix structure. Cross-namespace aliasing is illegal unless represented by an explicit alias object.
1.4.2(c) Address encoding.Addresses for indexed objects are encoded via the lens address algebra (\mathcal A) and included in object encodings. Two objects with different addresses are different objects, even if their numeric payloads match, unless an explicit equivalence relation states otherwise.
1.4.2(d) Name-based determinism constraints.Any operation that enumerates keys, addresses, or prefixes must do so in canonical lexicographic order, with pinned collation rules. Any dependency on platform-specific ordering is illegal.
1.4.3 Canonical ordering of collections and operations
1.4.3(a) Collection canonical forms.All collections are declared as either set-like or sequence-like in their schemas.
Set-like collections must be sorted canonically and deduplicated under declared equality.
Sequence-like collections preserve order but require canonical index encodings for each element.
1.4.3(b) Delta ordering.Every state update is represented as a delta: an ordered list of primitive operations. Deltas must be ordered canonically by (operation kind, target key/address, operation fields). This makes state hashes stable and replayable.
1.4.3(c) Witness ordering.Witness references are ordered canonically by (witness type id, witness hash). Any step record that does not respect canonical witness ordering is invalid for replay.
1.4.3(d) Deterministic traversal and tie-breaking.All algorithms that choose among equal-cost or equal-score candidates must define deterministic tie-breakers pinned by policy (lexicographic on hashes/ids). “Arbitrary choice” is prohibited.
1.4.4 Deterministic numeric handling and quantization policy
1.4.4(a) Numeric domains and admissible arithmetic.All numeric computation is performed in explicitly declared numeric domains: integers, rationals, fixed-point, or bounded floating with pinned rounding. Mixed arithmetic requires explicit coercion rules as typed objects. Implicit promotions are illegal.
1.4.4(b) Quantization object (Q).A quantization policy is a typed object specifying: scaling factor(s), rounding mode, overflow behavior, and canonical encoding. For any real-valued intermediate (x), the quantized representation is[\mathsf{Quant}_Q(x) \in \mathbb{Z}]computed deterministically. For vectors/tensors, (\mathsf{Quant}_Q) is applied elementwise with pinned layout ordering.
1.4.4(c) Floating computation constraints.If floating arithmetic is permitted, the framework pins: (i) precision (e.g., binary32/binary64), (ii) rounding mode, (iii) exceptional value behavior, and (iv) canonical serialization of floating arrays. Any numeric result that affects hashing must be quantized or otherwise deterministically normalized before hashing.
1.4.4(d) Numeric reproducibility theorem (operational).Given pinned numeric policies and canonicalization, any computation step whose inputs are identical and whose parameters reference the same numeric policy hashes yields identical quantized outputs and thus identical hashes. Therefore numeric behavior becomes a verified dependency, not an implementation accident.
CHAPTER 1 — FOUNDATIONS: OBJECTS, REPRESENTATIONS, AND MEANING
CHAPTER 2 — LEGAL TRANSFORMS: CONTRACT OBJECT AND SEMANTICS (Final Draft)
2.1 The legal transform contract (LTC)
2.1.1 Contract fields and required hashes
2.1.1(a) Legal transform object.
A legal transform contract is a typed object (\mathsf{LT}) whose decoded semantic form is the tuple
[
\mathsf{LT}
(\mathsf{lt_id},,L_{\mathrm{in}},,L_{\mathrm{out}},,\widetilde{T},,\mathcal{I}{\mathsf{LT}},,\mathcal{B}{\mathsf{LT}},,\mathcal{S}{\mathsf{LT}},,\mathcal{W}{\mathsf{LT}},,\mathcal{R}_{\mathsf{LT}}),]where:
(\mathsf{lt_id}\in{0,1}^{256}) is the canonical identifier (H(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{LT}))) of the contract object under canonical serialization.
(L_{\mathrm{in}}=(D_{\mathrm{in}},\mathcal{A}{\mathrm{in}},\mathcal{I}{\mathrm{in}})) and (L_{\mathrm{out}}=(D_{\mathrm{out}},\mathcal{A}{\mathrm{out}},\mathcal{I}{\mathrm{out}})) are lens objects (Chapter 1).
(\widetilde{T}) is a deterministic carrier map from (D_{\mathrm{in}}) to (D_{\mathrm{out}}), represented by either (i) a pinned program reference or (ii) a pinned rule identifier with pinned implementation digest.
(\mathcal{I}{\mathsf{LT}}\subseteq \mathcal{I}{\mathrm{in}}\cap \mathcal{I}_{\mathrm{out}}) is the invariant contract for this transform (Section 2.1.2).
(\mathcal{B}_{\mathsf{LT}}) is the budget profile (Section 2.1.3).
(\mathcal{S}_{\mathsf{LT}}) is the scope declaration (Section 2.1.3).
(\mathcal{W}_{\mathsf{LT}}) is the witness profile (required witness types and verifier rule identifiers).
(\mathcal{R}_{\mathsf{LT}}) is the replay profile (Section 2.1.4).
A transform is admissible within the framework only if it is present as a well-formed (\mathsf{LT}) object and all referenced hashes resolve to well-formed objects under the canonical object standard.
2.1.1(b) Contract hash components (required).Each (\mathsf{LT}) canonically commits to the following component hashes:
Lens hashes: (h(L_{\mathrm{in}})), (h(L_{\mathrm{out}})).
Program / rule hash: (h(\widetilde{T})), the hash of the carrier-map specification, including any pinned code digest and rule id.
Invariant schema hash: (h(\mathcal{I}_{\mathsf{LT}})), including invariant identifiers, norms, and tolerance profiles.
Budget profile hash: (h(\mathcal{B}_{\mathsf{LT}})).
Scope profile hash: (h(\mathcal{S}_{\mathsf{LT}})).
Witness profile hash: (h(\mathcal{W}_{\mathsf{LT}})), including method identifiers for proof witnesses.
Replay profile hash: (h(\mathcal{R}_{\mathsf{LT}})), including canonicalization and parameter schema digests.
These hashes are fields of the canonical encoding of (\mathsf{LT}). Any contract interpretation is illegal unless it uses exactly the referenced component objects by hash.
2.1.1(c) Contract well-formedness predicate.A contract (\mathsf{LT}) is well-formed iff all conditions hold:
(WF1) (L_{\mathrm{in}},L_{\mathrm{out}}) are well-formed lenses.
(WF2) (\widetilde{T}) is referenced by hash and is a deterministic map specification.
(WF3) (\mathcal{I}{\mathsf{LT}}\subseteq \mathcal{I}{\mathrm{in}}\cap \mathcal{I}_{\mathrm{out}}) (by invariant identifiers).
(WF4) (\mathcal{B}{\mathsf{LT}}), (\mathcal{S}{\mathsf{LT}}), (\mathcal{W}{\mathsf{LT}}), (\mathcal{R}{\mathsf{LT}}) are well-formed by their own schemas.
(WF5) All referenced verifier rule ids exist in the pinned verifier registry.
(WF6) All numeric policies referenced by the contract (quantization, rounding, tolerances) are pinned by hash.
2.1.1(d) Contract identity and versioning.Contracts are immutable: any change in any field produces a different (\mathsf{lt_id}). Versioning is represented by explicit version fields included in the contract encoding; compatibility between versions is not assumed and must be established by explicit bridge transforms or explicit supersession proofs.
2.1.2 Declared invariants and tolerance profiles
2.1.2(a) Invariant contract.(\mathcal{I}{\mathsf{LT}}) is a finite set of invariant clauses. Each clause is a tuple[\mathsf{InvClause} = (\mathsf{inv_id},,\mathsf{inv_eval_ref},,|\cdot|{\mathsf{inv}},,\varepsilon_{\mathsf{inv}},,\mathsf{applicability}),]where:
(\mathsf{inv_id}) uniquely identifies the invariant.
(\mathsf{inv_eval_ref}) references a deterministic invariant evaluator by hash (built from observables per Chapter 1).
(|\cdot|_{\mathsf{inv}}) is the norm used for drift measurement.
(\varepsilon_{\mathsf{inv}}\ge 0) is the bound for this transform (quantized under pinned policy).
(\mathsf{applicability}) is a predicate specifying when the invariant is required (e.g., only for a particular subclass of payloads).
Invariant evaluation is performed on the interpreted semantics (\llbracket p\rrbracket_{L}) and on quantized values per the pinned numeric policy.
2.1.2(b) Preservation predicate.Let (p\in D_{\mathrm{in}}) be admissible. Let (p'=\widetilde{T}(p)\in D_{\mathrm{out}}). For each applicable invariant clause (I), the preservation predicate is[\big| I(\llbracket p\rrbracket_{L_{\mathrm{in}}}) - I(\llbracket p'\rrbracket_{L_{\mathrm{out}}}) \big|{\mathsf{inv}}\le \varepsilon{\mathsf{inv}}.]Exact invariants have (\varepsilon_{\mathsf{inv}}=0). Violation of any applicable invariant is a legality failure under the contract.
2.1.2(c) Tolerance profile and accumulation interface.Each (\mathsf{LT}) declares a tolerance vector (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathsf{LT}}) indexed by (\mathsf{inv_id}). The contract additionally pins a tolerance composition operator (\oplus{\mathsf{inv}}) for each invariant, used to accumulate drift budgets over chains. The operator must be deterministic and pinned by hash. Any chain-level claims must compute cumulative tolerances using these pinned operators.
2.1.2(d) Claim strength binding.Any proof object emitted by the transform that claims equivalence or preservation must bind to (\mathcal{I}{\mathsf{LT}}) and (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathsf{LT}}) by hash. Claims cannot be interpreted with a stronger invariant set or tighter tolerance than those bound hashes.
2.1.3 Budget and scope declarations
2.1.3(a) Budget profile.The budget profile (\mathcal{B}{\mathsf{LT}}) is a tuple[\mathcal{B}{\mathsf{LT}}=(b_{\mathrm{comp}},,b_{\mathrm{write}},,b_{\mathrm{branch}},,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathrm{max}}),]where:
(b_{\mathrm{comp}}) is the maximum compute cost attributable to executing (\widetilde{T}) and mandatory checks.
(b_{\mathrm{write}}) is a structured write budget by target class (commitment store, relation store, candidate store, proof store; formalized by the execution model later in the treatise).
(b_{\mathrm{branch}}) is a maximum branching allowance for any exploration performed as part of the transform.
(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathrm{max}}) is a maximal admissible tolerance vector when the transform includes approximate components (may be identical to or stricter than (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathsf{LT}})).
Budget values are quantized under pinned numeric policy and interpreted as hard caps unless explicitly overridable by a budget-justification witness as permitted by the corridor policy.
2.1.3(b) Scope profile.The scope profile (\mathcal{S}_{\mathsf{LT}}) is a typed object specifying allowed write targets and forbidden targets. It partitions targets into primary and secondary classes. Primary targets are those the transform is allowed to write without additional authorization; secondary writes require explicit witness authorization.
Scope correctness is a predicate (\mathsf{ScopeOK}(\Delta,\mathcal{S}_{\mathsf{LT}})) evaluated on the proposed delta bundle (\Delta). If (\mathsf{ScopeOK}) fails, the transform application is illegal regardless of invariant preservation.
2.1.3(c) Budget feasibility predicate.Let (\mathbf{c}(p,\mathsf{LT},P)) denote the deterministic cost vector computed from the execution trace of (\widetilde{T}) and mandatory checks under parameter map (P). A transform application is budget-feasible iff[\mathbf{c}\preceq \mathcal{B}_{\mathsf{LT}}]componentwise, and branching and write budgets are not exceeded. Any policy-driven override must be represented explicitly by a budget-justification witness and must remain within corridor-imposed maxima.
2.1.3(d) Separation discipline.A contract may declare that it performs exploration (candidate generation) as part of execution. Such exploration must be bounded by (b_{\mathrm{branch}}) and must not commit to protected targets unless the required proof witnesses are produced and verified. Exploration without proof cannot directly produce commitments.
2.1.4 Replay hooks and canonical parameterization
2.1.4(a) Replay profile.The replay profile (\mathcal{R}_{\mathsf{LT}}) pins all degrees of freedom that would otherwise allow replays to diverge, including:
canonical encoders/decoders for all involved object types,
canonical ordering rules for deltas and witnesses,
canonical numeric policies (quantization, rounding),
canonical parameter schema hash (h(\Pi_{\mathsf{LT}})),
canonicalization digest hash (h(\mathsf{Canon})) binding naming and ordering policies.
2.1.4(b) Parameter schema and parameter hash.Each (\mathsf{LT}) declares a parameter schema (\Pi_{\mathsf{LT}}). Any instantiated parameter map (P) must conform to (\Pi_{\mathsf{LT}}), must be canonically encoded, and yields a deterministic parameter hash (h(P)). All execution and witness binding is conditioned on (h(P)). Two parameter maps are equal iff their canonical encodings are bytewise equal.
2.1.4(c) Deterministic execution interface.The contract must supply a deterministic evaluator interface[\mathsf{Run}{\mathsf{LT}}:\ (p,\Gamma,P)\to (p',\Delta,W,\mathbf{c})\ \cup\ \mathsf{Err},]where (\Gamma) is a resolved context, (\Delta) is a delta bundle, (W) is a witness bundle, and (\mathbf{c}) is the computed cost vector. (\mathsf{Run}{\mathsf{LT}}) must be deterministic for fixed inputs and must commit all dependencies by hash.
2.1.4(d) Replay correctness target.Replay correctness means that re-running (\mathsf{Run}_{\mathsf{LT}}) on identical pre-state, identical inputs, and identical parameter hash yields identical outputs, deltas, witnesses (up to canonical ordering), and computed costs, and that all verifier checks succeed under the same corridor policy and verifier set hashes.
2.2 Typed transport and admissibility
2.2.1 Type correctness predicates
2.2.1(a) Typed domains.Let (\mathsf{Type}(L)) denote the declared payload type for lens (L). A payload object (o) is type-correct for (L) iff (o) is well-formed and (\mathsf{Type}(o)=\mathsf{Type}(L)) (or matches a declared subtype relation pinned by schema).
2.2.1(b) Input admissibility.A transform application (\mathsf{LT}) to payload (p) is type-correct only if[p\in D_{\mathrm{in}}\quad\text{and}\quad \mathsf{Adm}{L{\mathrm{in}}}(p)=\mathsf{true}.]If a payload is not admissible, execution must halt with a typed error and may not emit deltas targeting protected stores.
2.2.1(c) Output type correctness.Let (p'=\widetilde{T}(p)). Output type correctness requires[p'\in D_{\mathrm{out}}\quad\text{and}\quad \mathsf{Adm}{L{\mathrm{out}}}(p')=\mathsf{true}.]If (\widetilde{T}) is partial, then for inadmissible inputs it must return a typed error deterministically.
2.2.1(d) Contracted typing consistency.All internal objects produced by execution (proof objects, evidence objects, intermediate artifacts) must satisfy their declared schemas and must be hash-addressable. Any schema violation is a legality failure.
2.2.2 Domain/range totality vs partiality
2.2.2(a) Total transforms.A transform is total iff for every admissible (p\in D_{\mathrm{in}}), (\widetilde{T}(p)) is defined and admissible in (D_{\mathrm{out}}). Totality must be declared in the contract and is subject to verification at admission time via proof artifacts or by explicit restriction of (D_{\mathrm{in}}) to a subset where totality holds.
2.2.2(b) Partial transforms.A transform is partial iff there exists admissible (p\in D_{\mathrm{in}}) such that (\widetilde{T}(p)) is undefined or inadmissible. Partiality must be declared. For partial transforms, the contract must specify:
a deterministic domain predicate (\mathsf{Dom}_{\mathsf{LT}}(p)) (the admissible subdomain),
a deterministic error schema for out-of-domain inputs,
whether out-of-domain behavior is a hard failure or may be handled by a certified repair path.
2.2.2(c) Range constraints.Range restrictions must be explicit as a predicate (\mathsf{Ran}_{\mathsf{LT}}(p')). A transform that can produce multiple representations of the same semantic entity must normalize (p') to a canonical representative under its replay profile or must declare a non-preservation/loss profile (Section 2.3.4).
2.2.2(d) Compatibility with composition.When composing transforms, totality/partiality propagates: a composed transform is total only on the subset of inputs where each component is defined and admissible. Any compiler or reasoning step must treat partial transforms as partial unless a certified restriction is inserted and proven.
2.2.3 Preconditions and admissibility guards
2.2.3(a) Guard predicate set.Each (\mathsf{LT}) declares a finite set of deterministic guard predicates (\mathsf{Guard}_k) evaluated on ((p,\Gamma,P)). Guards include:
convention hash compatibility requirements,
parameter-domain constraints,
required pinned dependency availability,
admissibility restrictions stronger than lens admissibility.
2.2.3(b) Guard enforcement.A transform application is admissible only if all guards evaluate to (\mathsf{true}). If any guard fails, execution must produce a typed error and must not commit protected deltas.
2.2.3(c) Guard evidence.Optionally, guard evaluation may emit a guard-evidence object containing which guard failed and the hashes of the relevant inputs. Guard evidence is a typed artifact suitable for logging and replay verification.
2.2.3(d) Guard stability.All guard predicates must be referentially transparent, must depend only on hash-referenced inputs, and must be pinned by hash as part of (\mathcal{R}_{\mathsf{LT}}). Changing guard logic requires a new contract version.
2.2.4 Output typing and schema validation
2.2.4(a) Output object schema.The output of a transform is not only (p') but also the set of emitted artifacts ((\Delta,W)). Each artifact must match a declared schema, and schemas must be pinned by hash. Schema validation is deterministic and part of the legality predicate.
2.2.4(b) Structural validation.Validation includes:
correct type tags,
correct field presence and order,
canonical encoding conformance,
address algebra conformance for indexed objects,
quantization conformance for numeric fields.
Failure of structural validation is a hard failure.
2.2.4(c) Cross-artifact consistency.Artifacts must be internally consistent: proof objects must reference the correct input and output hashes; evidence objects must bind to measured objects; deltas must target allowable scope. Cross-artifact consistency is enforced prior to commitment.
2.2.4(d) Output determinism constraint.All output objects must be canonicalized and hashed as part of the step record. If two replays produce structurally identical but bytewise different outputs, the transform is illegal due to replay violation.
2.3 Meaning preservation and equivalence claims
2.3.1 Exact preservation vs bounded drift
2.3.1(a) Exact transforms.A transform is exact for an invariant set (\mathcal{I}) iff (\varepsilon_I=0) for all applicable invariants and the preservation predicate holds. Exactness is a property relative to the declared invariants, not an absolute claim.
2.3.1(b) Approximate transforms.A transform is approximate iff it declares at least one (\varepsilon_I>0). Approximation is never implicit. Approximate transforms must:
declare per-invariant tolerances,
declare accumulation operators for chain composition,
declare whether the approximation is numerical (quantization error) or structural (model reduction),
include in their proof/evidence objects the computed drifts and comparison results on the declared local-check basis.
2.3.1(c) Drift measures.For each invariant (I), define a drift value[d_I(p) := \big| I(\llbracket p\rrbracket_{L_{\mathrm{in}}}) - I(\llbracket \widetilde{T}(p)\rrbracket_{L_{\mathrm{out}}}) \big|_{\mathsf{inv}}.]The transform is legal for (p) only if (d_I(p)\le \varepsilon_I) for all applicable (I). Drift values may be recorded as evidence artifacts.
2.3.1(d) Long-chain stability requirement.A transform that is often used in long chains must declare whether its tolerances are intended to be composable without additional normalization. If not, it must declare mandatory normalization checkpoints as part of (\mathcal{R}_{\mathsf{LT}}).
2.3.2 Equivalence classes induced by invariants
2.3.2(a) Equivalence relation.Given ((\mathcal{I},\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})), define the induced relation (\sim_{\mathcal{I},\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}) over objects by the invariant comparisons. This relation is an equivalence relation when:
invariants define symmetric comparisons,
tolerances are symmetric bounds,
and invariants satisfy triangle-like closure under (\oplus_I) where required.
When these conditions do not hold, the relation is treated as a directed pre-order.
2.3.2(b) Canonical representatives.If a canonical normal form operator (\mathsf{NF}_{\mathcal{I}}) is pinned for a family of objects, then equivalence can be tested by equality of canonical representatives. A transform may declare that it outputs canonical representatives, thereby strengthening replay and reducing witness burdens in downstream composition.
2.3.2(c) Invariant-lens coupling.Equivalence is meaningful only when invariant evaluators are applicable in the relevant lenses. If an invariant cannot be evaluated in a lens without auxiliary transport, that auxiliary transport must be explicit and certified. No invariant may be treated as “obvious” outside its evaluation domain.
2.3.2(d) Equivalence evidence.Whenever a transform claims equivalence between two objects (e.g., for optimization or bridge correctness), it must produce a proof object binding: the compared object hashes, the invariant-set hash, the tolerance-profile hash, the local-check data hash, and the verifier-rule id.
2.3.3 Refinement relations and strength ordering
2.3.3(a) Strength preorder on transform executions.An execution instance of (\mathsf{LT}) produces a verified claim (C=(\mathcal{I},\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})). For two executions (C_1,C_2) we define (C_2\preceq C_1) (stronger) iff (\mathcal{I}_1\subseteq \mathcal{I}_2) and (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_2\le \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_1) componentwise.
2.3.3(b) Refinement by re-verification.Strengthening a claim requires producing an additional certification transform whose invariant set strictly contains the prior set or whose tolerances are tighter, and whose proof objects verify the stronger statement. Strengthening cannot occur by reinterpretation.
2.3.3(c) Refinement compatibility under composition.If a downstream transform requires (C), it may accept any upstream artifact with a claim (C'\preceq C). This is the formal basis for replacing approximate results with exact ones and for accepting tighter proofs without modifying downstream code.
2.3.3(d) Strength monotonicity in libraries.Library admission rules must ensure that transform entries have explicit strength declarations. Any “hub” or canonical transform must record the strongest claim it can support under default policies, and any weaker claim must be represented as a separate contract or as a refinement relationship explicitly proven.
2.3.4 Explicit non-preservation (loss profiles)
2.3.4(a) Loss profile object.A transform that is not invertible or that intentionally discards information must declare a loss profile (\mathcal{L}_{\mathsf{LT}}), a typed object containing:
the dropped invariants (\mathcal{I}_{\mathrm{drop}}),
the quotient relation or abstraction map (q) if meaning is preserved only modulo a relation,
the preserved observable subset (\mathsf{Obs}_{\mathrm{keep}}),
any irreversible compression parameters.
2.3.4(b) Quotient-preservation predicate.For lossy transforms that preserve meaning only modulo a quotient, the preservation predicate is stated in the quotient space:[q(\llbracket p\rrbracket_{L_{\mathrm{in}}}) \sim_{\mathcal{I}{\mathsf{LT}},\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}} q(\llbracket \widetilde{T}(p)\rrbracket{L_{\mathrm{out}}}).]The quotient relation must be pinned by hash and must be referenced by the proof objects that justify the transform.
2.3.4(c) Composition of lossy steps.Composition of lossy transforms is legal only when the quotient relations compose coherently. The composed loss profile is the union of dropped invariants and the composed quotient mapping. Any ambiguity in quotient composition is illegal unless resolved by an explicit bridge transform.
2.3.4(d) Honesty constraints.A transform with a declared loss profile may not be used in any route that requires invertibility or preservation of dropped invariants unless an explicit recovery transform is provided and certified under the same corridor policy. Claims of exactness are illegal for lossy transforms.
2.4 Determinism and replay correctness conditions
2.4.1 Deterministic parameter hashing
2.4.1(a) Canonical parameter encoding.Parameter maps are encoded by sorting keys lexicographically, encoding values canonically (hash references for objects, quantized integers for numeric values), and including schema identifiers. The parameter hash is the hash of this canonical encoding.
2.4.1(b) Parameter completeness.All degrees of freedom affecting execution must be included in the parameter map or in referenced context hashes. Hidden defaults are illegal. If a default exists, it must be pinned as an explicit object and referenced by hash.
2.4.1(c) Parameter reproducibility.Any implementation must accept the parameter hash and be able to reconstruct the exact parameter object bytes used at execution time. Parameter reproducibility is mandatory for replay verification.
2.4.1(d) Parameter stability across versions.Changing a parameter schema requires a new contract version. Schema-bridging must be done by explicit transforms, never by implicit migration.
2.4.2 Deterministic application ordering
2.4.2(a) Deterministic evaluation order.If (\widetilde{T}) internally enumerates sets, indices, candidates, or sub-objects, it must do so in pinned canonical order. Any non-deterministic iteration order is illegal.
2.4.2(b) Deterministic delta ordering.The produced delta bundle must be canonically ordered by the rules pinned in (\mathcal{R}_{\mathsf{LT}}). Two executions producing deltas with the same semantic effect but different byte ordering are not replay-equivalent and are illegal.
2.4.2(c) Deterministic truncation and caps.If the transform performs bounded exploration, truncation must be deterministic by a pinned comparator and tie-break rules. The truncation mask is a deterministic function of inputs and must be replay-derivable.
2.4.2(d) Deterministic failure behavior.Failure conditions must produce a deterministic typed error with deterministic evidence hashes. Undefined behavior, partial writes on failure, or platform-dependent exceptions are illegal.
2.4.3 Deterministic witness ordering
2.4.3(a) Witness canonical ordering.Witness references are ordered by (witness type id, witness hash). The witness list hash is included in the step record. Any change in witness ordering changes the record hash and breaks replay.
2.4.3(b) Witness completeness predicate.For a transform with witness profile (\mathcal{W}{\mathsf{LT}}), define (\mathsf{WitOK}(W,\mathcal{W}{\mathsf{LT}})) as the predicate that all required witness types exist and each required verifier rule id is satisfied. A transform is legal only if (\mathsf{WitOK}) is true.
2.4.3(c) Step-local binding requirement.All witnesses must bind to the same step context: step id, parameter hash, pre-state hash, delta root, and post-state hash. Any witness not bound is invalid. Binding rules are deterministic and pinned in the verifier registry.
2.4.3(d) Non-reuse across steps.Witnesses cannot be reused across steps unless re-bound by a new step-local provenance binding. This prevents transplanting valid proofs into invalid contexts and is a hard legality requirement.
2.4.4 Replay associativity constraints
2.4.4(a) Composition associativity vs replay associativity.
Carrier-map composition is associative as functions:
[
(\widetilde{T}_3\circ \widetilde{T}_2)\circ \widetilde{T}_1
\widetilde{T}_3\circ (\widetilde{T}_2\circ \widetilde{T}_1).]Replay associativity is stronger: it requires that the canonical parameterization, delta ordering, witness ordering, and canonicalization checkpoints produce identical ledger commitments for either parenthesization when the same composed transform is expressed and executed.
2.4.4(b) Replay associativity predicate.Let (\mathsf{LT}_{321}=(\mathsf{LT}_3\circ\mathsf{LT}_2)\circ\mathsf{LT}1) and (\mathsf{LT}'{321}=\mathsf{LT}_3\circ(\mathsf{LT}_2\circ\mathsf{LT}_1)) be two legal compositions with identical carrier maps. Replay associativity requires that for any admissible input under a shared corridor policy, the resulting post-state hashes and receipt hashes are identical.
2.4.4(c) Conditions ensuring replay associativity.Replay associativity holds when:
canonical serializers and canonical naming rules are identical across components,
parameter hashing and schema inclusion are canonical and stable,
witness ordering rules are identical and pinned,
normalization checkpoints are inserted deterministically at the same boundaries.
If any of these differ, composition must be represented by an explicit bridge transform that reconciles the mismatch.
2.4.4(d) Associativity as an admission condition.Any transform library that claims closure under composition must restrict admission to transforms whose replay profiles satisfy replay associativity with the library’s canonical conventions or must require explicit bridges for any non-associative composition boundary. No transform is admitted on the basis of semantic intent alone; it must satisfy replay-algebra constraints.
CHAPTER 2 — LEGAL TRANSFORMS: CONTRACT OBJECT AND SEMANTICS
CHAPTER 3 — CORRIDOR POLICY ((\Omega)): BUDGETS, SCOPE, AND SAFETY (Final Draft)
This chapter specifies the corridor policy (\Omega): a pinned, immutable legality environment that governs every execution step and every legal transform application. (\Omega) makes resource limits, write permissions, determinism, and contradiction resolution explicit, machine-checkable, and replay-auditable. No step is admissible unless it satisfies (\Omega) in addition to the transform’s own contract.
3.1 Corridor definition and components
3.1.1 Compute budget model
3.1.1(a) Compute units and deterministic cost accounting.Fix a compute-cost domain (\mathbb{C}\subseteq \mathbb{R}{\ge 0}) with canonical quantization (Q{\mathsf{comp}}) (Chapter 1). Each execution step deterministically emits a compute cost (c_{\mathsf{comp}}\in\mathbb{C}) defined as a function of:
the executed carrier-map program (or rule id) and its declared cost model,
the sizes of decoded objects accessed by hash,
the number of primitive operations performed in the kernel,
the number and complexity class of verifier invocations,
any explicitly permitted bounded exploration.
Compute cost is a reported and verified artifact, not an informal metric. The cost computation procedure is pinned by hash and versioned; cost claims are invalid if the cost model hash differs from the corridor’s pinned cost model.
3.1.1(b) Compute budget envelope.(\Omega) includes a compute budget (b_{\mathsf{comp}}\in\mathbb{C}). For a step to be compute-feasible, it must satisfy[c_{\mathsf{comp}}\le b_{\mathsf{comp}}.]If the corridor permits budget escalation, escalation must occur through an explicit budget-justification artifact (Section 3.2.4) whose verified requested compute budget (b'{\mathsf{comp}}) is bounded above by a corridor-defined maximum (b^{\max}{\mathsf{comp}}).
3.1.1(c) Compute cost determinism.Compute cost must be deterministic under replay: for identical pre-state hash, identical parameter hash, identical referenced object hashes, and identical corridor policy hash, re-execution yields identical (c_{\mathsf{comp}}). Any drift in computed cost under replay is a determinism failure and renders the step illegal under (\Omega).
3.1.1(d) Compute budget as a hard constraint.Compute feasibility is a hard constraint: if (c_{\mathsf{comp}}>b_{\mathsf{comp}}) and no admissible, verified budget-justification artifact exists for the step, the step is illegal and must not commit protected writes.
3.1.2 Write budgets by substrate/target
3.1.2(a) Target classes and write budget structure.Fix a finite set of target classes (\mathsf{Target}) partitioned into the following canonical categories:
(\mathsf{Commit}): commitment targets (pinned records, receipts, plans, canonical artifacts),
(\mathsf{Rel}): relation targets (typed edges, dependency links, structural adjacency),
(\mathsf{Cand}): candidate targets (hypotheses, route candidates, competing interpretations),
(\mathsf{Proof}): proof targets (proof objects, evidence objects, rewrite proofs, dependency proofs).
A corridor policy provides a write budget function[b_{\mathsf{write}}:\mathsf{Target}\to \mathbb{W},]where (\mathbb{W}\subseteq \mathbb{R}{\ge 0}) is a quantized nonnegative domain. Each step emits a deterministic write-cost function[c{\mathsf{write}}:\mathsf{Target}\to \mathbb{W}]measuring the amount of write activity proposed (and, after legality projection, accepted) per target class.
3.1.2(b) Write-cost attribution.Write cost is computed deterministically from the canonical delta bundle (\Delta). For each target (t\in\mathsf{Target}), (c_{\mathsf{write}}(t)) is a deterministic function of:
the number of primitive writes in (\Delta) targeting (t),
the canonical serialized byte size of the written objects or records,
any target-specific integrity overhead mandated by (\Omega) (e.g., contradiction scans for (\mathsf{Commit})).
The write-cost model for each target is pinned by hash in (\Omega).
3.1.2(c) Write feasibility predicate.A step is write-feasible iff[\forall t\in\mathsf{Target}:\ c_{\mathsf{write}}(t)\le b_{\mathsf{write}}(t),]unless an admissible budget-justification artifact exists that raises a subset of budgets within corridor-defined maxima.
3.1.2(d) Commitment primacy constraint.(\Omega) may impose stricter budgets on (\mathsf{Commit}) than on (\mathsf{Rel}), (\mathsf{Cand}), or (\mathsf{Proof}). Such asymmetry is permitted and is the standard mechanism by which the corridor enforces the separation of exploration from commitment: high churn is permitted in candidates and relations within caps; commitments remain scarce and integrity-controlled.
3.1.3 Branch caps and expansion caps
3.1.3(a) Branching model.Fix a canonical branching measure (\mathsf{Br}(\Delta)\in\mathbb{N}) computed from the candidate-generation portion of the delta bundle (\Delta) (including candidate additions, route proposals, hypothesis forks). The corridor includes a branch cap (b_{\mathsf{branch}}\in\mathbb{N}) and requires[\mathsf{Br}(\Delta)\le b_{\mathsf{branch}}]unless a verified budget-justification artifact explicitly authorizes a higher branch count bounded by a corridor maximum (b^{\max}_{\mathsf{branch}}).
3.1.3(b) Expansion depth model.Fix an expansion-depth parameter (\lambda\in\mathbb{N}) representing the maximum allowed internal expansion of step-local working state (e.g., recursion depth, unrolling depth, local-check breadth). (\Omega) includes an expansion cap (b_{\lambda}\in\mathbb{N}) and requires[\lambda\le b_{\lambda}.]Expansion depth is a declared parameter and is included in the step’s parameter hash. Any expansion beyond (b_\lambda) is illegal.
3.1.3(c) Deterministic truncation under caps.When (\Omega) permits partial acceptance for candidate churn (formalized later in the treatise), truncation must be deterministic: candidates are ordered by a pinned comparator and tie-break rules (typically lexicographic on canonical encodings/hashes), and the truncation mask is a deterministic function of the pre-state, the proposed candidates, and (\Omega).
3.1.3(d) Caps as anti-divergence constraints.Branch and expansion caps are designed to prevent divergence and uncontrolled recursion. They are enforced as hard constraints when exceeded without authorization; when the corridor permits truncation, the truncation outcome is still deterministic and replay-verifiable.
3.1.4 Determinism and contradiction policies
3.1.4(a) Determinism policy component.(\Omega) includes a determinism profile (\Omega_{\mathsf{det}}) that pins:
canonical ordering rules for deltas, witnesses, and enumerations,
canonical encoding and hashing versions for all relevant objects,
numeric policies (quantization, rounding, overflow behavior),
tie-break rules for all selection operations,
the permitted mechanism for pseudo-randomness (if any), including a pinned seed object and pinned generator algorithm.
Any computation that depends on ambient randomness, platform iteration order, or unspecified floating behavior violates (\Omega_{\mathsf{det}}) and is illegal.
3.1.4(b) Replay lock as corridor requirement.Determinism is enforced by a replay condition: for a step with pre-state hash (h_{\mathrm{pre}}), parameter hash (h_P), and resolved input hashes (R_{\mathrm{src}}), re-execution under the same (\Omega) yields the same post-state hash (h_{\mathrm{post}}) and the same ordered witness set. Violation is a hard failure.
3.1.4(c) Contradiction policy component.(\Omega) includes a contradiction policy (\Omega_{\mathsf{contr}}) which defines:
a contradiction predicate (\mathsf{Contradict}) over the commitment store,
the set of commitment namespaces that are contradiction-controlled,
admissible resolution kinds (Section 3.4.2),
mandatory proof obligations for resolution (Section 3.4.3).
Contradiction policy is part of the corridor rather than the transform contract because it governs global coherence of commitments across all transforms.
3.1.4(d) Hard vs soft corridor constraints.(\Omega) classifies constraints into:
Hard constraints: determinism, provenance binding requirements (as defined by the witness system), contradiction policy for protected commitments, and verifier success for required proofs. Any violation yields illegality.
Soft constraints (only if explicitly permitted by (\Omega)): secondary writes, candidate churn beyond caps (handled by deterministic truncation), and optional proof emissions. Soft constraints may be handled by deterministic omission or truncation, never by nondeterministic behavior.
3.2 Budget algebra and accumulation
3.2.1 Cost vectors and accounting
3.2.1(a) Step cost vector.For each step (t), define a deterministic cost vector[\mathbf{c}t=\big(c{\mathsf{comp},t},\ c_{\mathsf{write},t},\ c_{\mathsf{branch},t},\ \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}t\big),]where (c{\mathsf{write},t}:\mathsf{Target}\to\mathbb{W}) and (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t) is the tolerance consumption vector indexed by invariant identifiers (Section 3.2.2). The computation of (\mathbf{c}_t) is pinned by (\Omega) and must be replay-deterministic.
3.2.1(b) Remaining budget state.The corridor maintains an implicit remaining budget state (\mathbf{b}t) at step (t), with initial (\mathbf{b}0=\mathbf{b}{\Omega}). Remaining budgets evolve deterministically:[\mathbf{b}{t+1}=\mathbf{b}_t\ominus \mathbf{c}_t,]where (\ominus) is componentwise subtraction for compute/write/branch budgets and the tolerance update operator for (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) (Section 3.2.2). Remaining budgets are not permitted to be negative. Budget state evolution is included in the ledger semantics (formalized later), and is replay-verifiable.
3.2.1(c) Transform-local vs corridor-global budgets.Each legal transform contract includes its own budget profile (\mathcal{B}_{\mathsf{LT}}) (Chapter 2). A step applying (\mathsf{LT}) must satisfy both:
Transform feasibility: (\mathbf{c}t\preceq \mathcal{B}{\mathsf{LT}}),
Corridor feasibility: (\mathbf{c}_t\preceq \mathbf{b}_t).
This enforces that transforms cannot exceed their own declared resource envelope and cannot exceed the global corridor constraints.
3.2.1(d) Accounting integrity.All costs and remaining budgets are bound to the step context by hash, and any cost-affecting operation must be reflected in (\mathbf{c}_t). Omitted accounting is illegal: a step cannot “hide” compute or writes by failing to report them, because replay verification recomputes cost deterministically from the canonical delta and verifier invocations.
3.2.2 Tolerance accumulation rules
3.2.2(a) Tolerance vector.Let (\mathsf{InvId}) be the set of invariant identifiers relevant to an execution. The corridor includes a tolerance budget vector[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega}=(\varepsilon^{\Omega}I){I\in\mathsf{InvId}},]with each component quantized and pinned. Each step emits a tolerance consumption vector (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}t) with components (\varepsilon{I,t}\ge 0) representing the declared tolerance usage for invariant (I) by that step.
3.2.2(b) Accumulation operators.
For each invariant (I), (\Omega) pins an accumulation operator (\oplus_I) determining how tolerance usage composes over a chain. The cumulative usage after (t) steps is
[
\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_{I,t}
\varepsilon_{I,1}\oplus_I \varepsilon_{I,2}\oplus_I \cdots \oplus_I \varepsilon_{I,t}.]The accumulation operators may encode worst-case, independent-noise, or other explicitly declared models; the model must be pinned and deterministic. No implicit accumulation is permitted.
3.2.2(c) Tolerance feasibility.A step sequence is tolerance-feasible iff[\forall I\in\mathsf{InvId}:\ \varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_{I,t}\le \varepsilon^{\Omega}_I\quad\text{for all }t.]If a step would cause feasibility violation and no admissible budget justification exists, the step is illegal.
3.2.2(d) Near-violation thresholds.(\Omega) pins a near-threshold fraction (\alpha_I\in(0,1)) for each invariant or globally (\alpha). When (\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_{I,t}) exceeds (\alpha_I\varepsilon^{\Omega}_I), the corridor declares the state near on invariant (I), requiring deterministic mitigation actions as specified by policy (e.g., forced normalization insertion, reroute to exact transforms, or tightened caps), represented as explicit legal steps.
3.2.3 Feasibility predicates and hard stops
3.2.3(a) Step feasibility predicate.
Define the corridor feasibility predicate (\mathsf{Feasible}_{\Omega}) for a proposed step execution outcome ((\Delta,W,\mathbf{c}t)) as:
[
\mathsf{Feasible}{\Omega}(\Delta,W,\mathbf{c}_t)
\mathsf{CompOK}\wedge \mathsf{WriteOK}\wedge \mathsf{BranchOK}\wedge \mathsf{TolOK}\wedge \mathsf{ScopeOK}\wedge \mathsf{DetOK}\wedge \mathsf{ContrOK},]where each conjunct is determined by the corresponding component of (\Omega) and is deterministic.
3.2.3(b) Hard-stop conditions.A step triggers a hard stop (illegal execution) if any of the following hold:
compute cost exceeds allowed compute budget and no admissible justification exists,
any write cost exceeds allowed write budget for a protected target and no admissible justification exists,
branching exceeds cap and the corridor does not permit deterministic truncation or no admissible justification exists,
tolerance feasibility is violated for any hard invariant,
scope is violated (writes to forbidden targets or unauthorized secondary writes),
determinism predicates fail (non-canonical ordering, non-deterministic outputs, replay mismatch),
contradiction policy is violated for protected commitments without an admissible resolution proof.
Hard stops prohibit protected commits; any state change must be rejected and reverted under the execution semantics specified later in the manuscript.
3.2.3(c) Soft-stop conditions and corridor-defined degradations.If (\Omega) explicitly permits degradations, the corridor may allow deterministic omission or truncation for specific soft constraints (notably candidate churn and certain secondary writes). Such degradations must be:
explicitly authorized by (\Omega),
deterministic,
recorded as evidence in the step record (including the truncation/omission mask).
No degradation is permitted for determinism failures, proof-verifier failures for required witnesses, or contradiction violations on protected commitments.
3.2.3(d) Earliest-failure localization.The corridor requires that feasibility checking be structured so that the earliest failing predicate can be deterministically identified and logged with a typed evidence object. This supports reproducible debugging, certified repair planning, and replay-auditable failure localization.
3.2.4 Budget justification as an explicit artifact
3.2.4(a) Budget-justification object.A budget escalation is legal only if accompanied by a typed budget justification artifact[\mathsf{BJ}=(\mathsf{req},\ \mathsf{reason},\ \mathsf{proof},\ \mathsf{bind}),]where:
(\mathsf{req}) specifies requested overrides: ((b'{\mathsf{comp}}, b'{\mathsf{write}}, b'_{\mathsf{branch}}, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}'^{\Omega})) for a subset of components,
(\mathsf{reason}) is an enumerated code permitted by (\Omega),
(\mathsf{proof}) is a hash reference to a proof object whose verifier rule id is pinned by (\Omega),
(\mathsf{bind}) binds the justification to the step context (pre-state hash, parameter hash, delta root, and expected post-state hash).
3.2.4(b) Admissibility constraints on justifications.A budget justification is admissible only if:
all requested budgets do not exceed corridor maxima,
the reason code is allowed by (\Omega),
the referenced proof verifies deterministically under the pinned verifier set,
the binding matches the step context.
Justifications that do not bind to the exact step context are invalid.
3.2.4(c) Justification semantics.If admissible, (\mathsf{BJ}) replaces the relevant budget components for the step (or for a declared finite segment) by the requested values. The substitution rule must be pinned: either step-local substitution only, or window-local substitution over a fixed number of steps, where the window is identified deterministically by ledger indices.
3.2.4(d) Auditability of escalations.Every escalation event must be logged with:
the original budgets,
the requested budgets,
the reason code,
the proof hash,
the bound step context.
Escalations are therefore verifiable and extractable as structured artifacts, and cannot be hidden.
3.3 Scope discipline: exploration vs commitment
3.3.1 Primary vs secondary write rules
3.3.1(a) Primary target declaration.Each legal transform contract declares a primary target class (\mathsf{Prim}(\mathsf{LT})\in\mathsf{Target}) indicating the principal store it is permitted to write without additional authorization. The corridor policy constrains allowable primary writes by transform class and by plan segment.
3.3.1(b) Secondary write permissions.Any write to a target class (t\neq \mathsf{Prim}(\mathsf{LT})) is a secondary write. Secondary writes are forbidden unless authorized by an explicit witness whose verifier rule id is permitted by (\Omega) and whose binding matches the step context. Authorization must specify:
the target class,
the set of keys/addresses to be written,
the reason and contract clause permitting the write.
3.3.1(c) Scope predicate.Define (\mathsf{ScopeOK}_{\Omega}(\Delta,W)) to be true iff:
all primary writes are to permitted primary targets,
all secondary writes are authorized by admissible, verified scope-authorizing witnesses,
no writes target forbidden namespaces,
all written keys/addresses satisfy canonical naming and addressing rules.
Violation of (\mathsf{ScopeOK}_{\Omega}) is a hard stop.
