3.3.1(d) Commitment scarcity and commitment gating.(\Omega) may require that any write to (\mathsf{Commit}) targets be accompanied by explicit proof obligations beyond basic schema validation (e.g., equivalence proofs, consistency proofs). These obligations are expressed as required witness conditions in the corridor policy and enforced uniformly across transforms.
3.3.2 Forbidden implicit state mutation
3.3.2(a) Explicit delta requirement.All state change is represented only by explicit deltas (\Delta) over primitive operations. Any state mutation not expressible as a delta over pinned primitives is illegal. This includes hidden caches, implicit global variables, non-recorded updates, or external side effects.
3.3.2(b) No ambient dependence.Step execution may depend only on:
decoded objects referenced by hash,
the explicit parameter map (P),
the explicit context (\Gamma),
the pinned corridor (\Omega),
the pinned verifier set and canonicalization policies.
Dependence on system time, OS randomness, hardware nondeterminism, or platform-specific ordering violates determinism and is illegal under (\Omega).
3.3.2(c) No implicit coercions.Any coercion (type promotion, unit conversion, index shift, sign convention change, scaling normalization, domain restriction) must be executed as an explicit legal transform step or as an explicit, contract-declared internal substep whose effect is reflected in deltas and whose correctness is certified. Silent coercions are prohibited.
3.3.2(d) No implicit deletion or overwrite.Deletion, overwrite, and merge operations in protected targets are forbidden unless explicitly permitted by (\Omega) and accompanied by admissible proofs as required. In particular, destructive edits to commitments require explicit contradiction resolution obligations.
3.3.3 Allowed secondary writes via witness gating
3.3.3(a) Witness-gated scope extensions.(\Omega) defines a finite set of admissible scope-extension witnesses. Each such witness type has:
an explicit schema,
a deterministic verifier rule id,
a binding requirement to the step context,
a target authorization specification.
Scope extensions are not discretionary: a secondary write is legal iff the corresponding witness verifies.
3.3.3(b) Authorization granularity.Scope authorizations must be granular: the witness must enumerate (by canonical key/address ranges or explicit lists) the specific items it authorizes. Blanket authorization without a target set is illegal unless the corridor explicitly permits a fixed, finite wildcard pattern pinned by hash.
3.3.3(c) Secondary writes as derived consequences.When secondary writes are permitted, they must be derivable from primary operations and proof obligations. For example: a proof produced in (\mathsf{Proof}) may authorize a commitment to (\mathsf{Commit}) only if the proof object hash is referenced by the commitment delta and the corresponding authorization witness verifies.
3.3.3(d) Audit trails for scope extensions.All scope extensions must be logged as evidence: the step record must include the scope-extension witness hash, the authorized target set, and the corresponding delta hashes. This guarantees that scope expansions are replay-verifiable and cannot be smuggled.
3.3.4 Throttling policies and rate limits
3.3.4(a) Rate limit objects.(\Omega) may include rate-limit constraints specified as typed objects, such as:
maximum commitment writes per (k) steps,
maximum new namespaces created per plan,
maximum proof objects added per window,
maximum candidate churn per window.
Rate limits are evaluated deterministically from the ledger prefix and thus are replay-checkable.
3.3.4(b) Window definitions.A window is defined deterministically either by step count or by explicit segment markers pinned in the plan. Time-based windows are prohibited unless time is treated as a pinned input. Window boundaries must be derivable under replay.
3.3.4(c) Throttling actions.When a rate limit is reached, (\Omega) defines deterministic actions:
reject additional writes to the limited target (hard stop), or
permit deterministic omission of soft writes (if classified as soft), or
require an explicit budget justification to proceed.
No throttling action may introduce nondeterminism.
3.3.4(d) Throttling evidence.Throttle events produce a typed evidence object: limit id, current counts, threshold, window definition hash, and affected delta references. This enables deterministic extraction and analysis of corridor pressure.
3.4 Contradiction handling and resolution policy
3.4.1 Contradiction predicates on commitments
3.4.1(a) Commitment store and contradiction domain.Let (\mathsf{CommitStore}) be the commitment map from canonical keys to commitment records (typed objects by hash). (\Omega) designates a subset of commitment namespaces (\mathsf{ContrCtl}\subseteq \mathsf{KeySpace}) as contradiction-controlled.
3.4.1(b) Contradiction predicate.Define a deterministic contradiction predicate[\mathsf{Contradict}{\Omega}:\ (\mathsf{CommitStore},\Delta{\mathsf{Commit}})\to {\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}},]which evaluates whether applying the proposed commitment delta (\Delta_{\mathsf{Commit}}) would violate corridor-defined consistency constraints. The predicate is pinned by hash and may incorporate:
mutual exclusivity rules among claim types,
uniqueness constraints on canonical identifiers,
incompatibility constraints on invariant declarations,
dependency-cycle constraints for commitments declared acyclic.
3.4.1(c) Contradiction as a hard failure.If (\mathsf{Contradict}_{\Omega}=\mathsf{true}), the step is illegal unless an admissible contradiction-resolution proof is present and verified. Contradictions cannot be committed by omission, truncation, or partial acceptance for protected commitments.
3.4.1(d) Contradiction detection evidence.When a contradiction is detected, the step must emit a typed evidence object containing: the conflicting keys, the conflict kind, the relevant commitment hashes, and the predicate id. Evidence must bind to the step context so it is replay-verifiable.
3.4.2 Resolution kinds (disambiguate/merge/overwrite)
3.4.2(a) Resolution kind enumeration.(\Omega) defines an allowed set (\mathsf{ResKind}\subseteq{\mathsf{DISAMBIGUATE},\mathsf{MERGE},\mathsf{OVERWRITE}}), with each kind precisely specified.
3.4.2(b) Disambiguation.(\mathsf{DISAMBIGUATE}) resolves contradiction by partitioning a key or claim into non-conflicting sub-claims with distinct canonical identifiers. Disambiguation requires a canonical renaming scheme and an explicit proof that the partition preserves declared invariants for downstream dependents (or explicitly records loss where appropriate).
3.4.2(c) Merge.(\mathsf{MERGE}) resolves contradiction by asserting that two previously distinct committed objects are equivalent under a declared invariant set and tolerance profile. Merge introduces a new commitment stating the equivalence relation and may require updating dependency edges. Merge is permitted only if the corridor policy allows identity merges for the relevant namespaces and if the merge proof verifies.
3.4.2(d) Overwrite.(\mathsf{OVERWRITE}) resolves contradiction by selecting one commitment as authoritative and marking the other as superseded. Overwrite is permitted only if the corridor policy allows overwrite for the namespace and only with an explicit proof object justifying supersession (e.g., refinement ordering, stronger proof, stricter tolerance, or correction of a detected defect).
3.4.3 Proof obligations for resolution
3.4.3(a) Resolution proof artifact.Any contradiction resolution requires a typed consistency proof artifact[\mathsf{CP}=(\mathsf{conflicts},\ \mathsf{kind},\ \mathsf{inputs},\ \mathsf{proof},\ \mathsf{outputs},\ \mathsf{bind}),]where:
(\mathsf{conflicts}) enumerates conflicting keys and commitment hashes,
(\mathsf{kind}\in\mathsf{ResKind}) is the resolution kind,
(\mathsf{inputs}) references all consumed objects by hash,
(\mathsf{proof}) references a proof object with a pinned verifier rule id,
(\mathsf{outputs}) references the intended post-resolution commitments by hash,
(\mathsf{bind}) binds the artifact to the step context (pre-state hash, parameter hash, delta root, post-state hash).
3.4.3(b) Resolution verification conditions.A resolution is admissible only if:
the resolution kind is allowed by (\Omega) for the relevant namespaces,
the proof object verifies deterministically under the corridor’s verifier set,
the outputs satisfy schema validation and canonical naming rules,
the resolution does not introduce new contradictions in protected namespaces.
3.4.3(c) Dependency integrity obligations.If a resolution changes the identity of a committed object (merge or overwrite), the corridor may require explicit dependency-update obligations: downstream artifacts that reference the old object must either (i) be shown compatible under refinement/equivalence or (ii) be pinned as consuming the superseding commitment. These updates are expressed as explicit deltas and are themselves subject to proof obligations where required.
3.4.3(d) No silent contradiction masking.Contradiction resolution cannot occur by deleting conflicting artifacts without proof, by ignoring conflict checks, or by downgrading contradiction-controlled namespaces into non-controlled namespaces. Any such attempt violates (\Omega_{\mathsf{contr}}) and is illegal.
3.4.4 Post-resolution consistency checks
3.4.4(a) Post-resolution contradiction scan.After applying a resolution, (\Omega) requires a deterministic post-resolution scan:[\mathsf{Contradict}_{\Omega}(\mathsf{CommitStore}',\varnothing)=\mathsf{false},]where (\mathsf{CommitStore}') is the post-step commitment store and (\varnothing) indicates no additional proposed delta. This ensures the store is globally consistent under the corridor’s contradiction predicate.
3.4.4(b) Invariant and refinement validation.If the resolution claims refinement or equivalence, the corridor requires that the relevant invariant evaluations and tolerance checks be performed and recorded as evidence objects bound by hash. For overwrite, refinement strength ordering must be explicitly shown or explicitly declared as a correction justified by a defect evidence chain.
3.4.4(c) Canonicalization enforcement.All post-resolution keys, aliases, and dependency records must satisfy canonical naming and addressing rules. Any non-canonical naming in post-resolution state is illegal because it breaks determinism and replay.
3.4.4(d) Resolution audit record.Every resolution step must emit an audit record (typed evidence object) containing:
resolution kind,
conflicting keys/hashes,
proof artifact hash,
affected namespaces,
any dependency-update artifacts.
This audit record is part of the replay-verifiable trace and is required for later extraction, verification, and public proof of consistency where disclosures are permitted.
CHAPTER 3 — CORRIDOR POLICY ((\Omega)): BUDGETS, SCOPE, AND SAFETY
CHAPTER 4 — EXECUTION KERNEL: STATE, PRIMITIVES, AND STEP SEMANTICS (Final Draft)
This chapter specifies the execution kernel: the minimal operational substrate that makes the framework executable, deterministic, and replay-verifiable. The kernel defines (i) the runtime state and its identity, (ii) the minimal primitive operations over state, (iii) the step function that executes a single contracted action, and (iv) verdict semantics including partial acceptance and revert. All higher constructs—legal transforms, compilation, optimization, verification receipts—reduce to finite programs whose execution is mediated solely by this kernel under the corridor policy (\Omega).
4.1 Runtime state model
4.1.1 Stable anchor and checkpointing
4.1.1(a) Runtime instance and stable anchor.A runtime instance is determined by a fixed tuple of pinned policy and implementation digests:[\mathsf{RuntimeHdr} :=(h_{\Omega},,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},,h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},,h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},,h_{\mathsf{CostModel}}),]where:
(h_{\Omega}) is the corridor policy hash,
(h_{\mathsf{Canon}}) is the canonicalization policy hash (naming, ordering, encoding),
(h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}}) is the canonical object standard hash (type tags, encoders/decoders),
(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}) is the verifier set hash (witness verifiers and method registries),
(h_{\mathsf{CostModel}}) is the cost accounting model hash.
A stable anchor is a designated checkpoint state (\Psi^\star) satisfying the fixed-point property under the kernel’s legality projector (\mathsf{Project}) (Section 4.3.3):[\mathsf{Project}(\Psi^\star,\Delta=\varnothing,W=\varnothing,\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{0},\Omega)= (\Psi^\star,\mathsf{ACCEPT},m^\star),]where (\varnothing) denotes empty deltas, (\mathbf{0}) is the zero cost vector, and (m^\star) is the all-accept mask. This enforces that a stable anchor is already canonical, contradiction-consistent, and within corridor budgets.
4.1.1(b) Checkpoint object.A checkpoint is a typed object (\mathsf{Chk}) whose canonical fields include:[\mathsf{Chk}:=(h_{\mathsf{state}},,h_{\mathsf{commit}},,h_{\mathsf{rel}},,h_{\mathsf{cand}},,h_{\mathsf{proof}},,\mathsf{step_index},,\mathsf{hdr}),]where (h_{\mathsf{state}}) is the full state hash (Section 4.1.3), the remaining hashes are per-store hashes, (\mathsf{step_index}\in\mathbb{N}) is the ledger position, and (\mathsf{hdr}= \mathsf{RuntimeHdr}) binds the checkpoint to the runtime instance. Checkpoints are content-addressed by hash and may be committed as artifacts subject to (\Omega).
4.1.1(c) Mandatory checkpointing and replay boundary.Every successful step (verdict (\mathsf{ACCEPT}) or (\mathsf{PARTIAL})) defines a post-step checkpoint (\mathsf{Chk}{t+1}). The checkpoint is the canonical replay boundary: all subsequent steps bind provenance to (h{\mathsf{state}}) of the checkpoint they consume. Any “jump” in computation must reference a checkpoint hash; computation that cannot be anchored to a checkpoint is illegal under determinism requirements.
4.1.1(d) Revert target.Each step has an explicit revert target equal to the pre-step checkpoint (\mathsf{Chk}t). If a hard-fail condition occurs (Section 4.4.3), the kernel must produce verdict (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}) and restore the state exactly to the pre-step checkpoint hash:[h(\Psi{t+1}) = h(\Psi_t),]with evidence logged. No partial mutation of protected stores is permitted on revert.
4.1.2 Substrate stores (commitments/relations/candidates/proofs)
4.1.2(a) Commitment store.The commitment store is a finite map[\mathsf{CommitStore}:\ \mathsf{Key}\to \mathsf{PinRecord},]where (\mathsf{Key}) is a canonical key space (Chapter 1) and a pin record is the typed tuple[\mathsf{PinRecord}:=(\mathsf{pin_id},\mathsf{pin_type},h_{\mathsf{value}},h_{\mathsf{prov}},h_{\mathsf{meta}}),]with:
(\mathsf{pin_id}\in\mathsf{Key}) canonical,
(\mathsf{pin_type}) an enum,
(h_{\mathsf{value}}) the hash of a typed object stored in the proof store (or an external commitment by hash),
(h_{\mathsf{prov}}) the provenance witness hash binding the pin insertion/update to the step context,
(h_{\mathsf{meta}}) optional metadata hash (also a typed object).
The commitment store is contradiction-controlled by (\Omega) (Chapter 3) and is the only substrate that constitutes committed truth within the runtime instance.
4.1.2(b) Relation store.The relation store is a typed, directed multigraph:[\mathsf{RelStore}:=(V,E,\mathsf{type},\mathsf{wgt},\mathsf{prov}),]where (V\subseteq \mathsf{NodeId}) is a set of canonical node identifiers, (E\subseteq \mathsf{EdgeId}) is a set of edge identifiers, and each edge (e\in E) has:
endpoints ((\mathsf{src}(e),\mathsf{dst}(e))\in V\times V),
type (\mathsf{type}(e)\in\mathsf{EdgeType}) (pinned enum),
weight (\mathsf{wgt}(e)\in\mathbb{Z}) (quantized under pinned policy),
provenance hash (\mathsf{prov}(e)=h_{\mathsf{prov}}).
The relation store is not truth by itself; it is a structured dependency and adjacency substrate whose mutation is gated by scope policy and, where applicable, by explicit merge/consistency proofs.
4.1.2(c) Candidate store.The candidate store is an ordered multiset or distribution:[\mathsf{CandStore}:={(c_i,s_i,h_{\mathsf{prov},i})}{i=1}^M,]where each candidate (c_i) is a typed object hash (or a canonical encoding referenced by hash), (s_i\in\mathbb{Z}) is a quantized score, and (h{\mathsf{prov},i}) binds the candidate insertion to the step provenance. Candidates are maintained in canonical order determined by a pinned comparator:[(c_i,s_i)\prec (c_j,s_j)\ \iff\ (s_i > s_j)\ \lor\ (s_i=s_j\ \land\ h(c_i) < h(c_j)),]unless overridden by a different comparator explicitly pinned by the runtime header. Candidate churn is controlled by branch caps and may be deterministically truncated.
4.1.2(d) Proof store.The proof store is a content-addressable map:[\mathsf{ProofStore}:\ {0,1}^{256}\to \mathsf{ObjBytes},]where each stored object must satisfy (h(o)=H(\mathsf{Tag}(t)|\mathbf{b})) for its type tag (t) and canonical bytes (\mathbf{b}). The proof store also supports a deterministic link index:[\mathsf{LinkIndex}:\ {0,1}^{256}\to ({0,1}^{256})^\ast,]mapping an object hash to a canonically ordered list of referenced object hashes. Proof objects, evidence objects, plans, receipts, rewrite proofs, and dependency proofs are stored in this substrate; commitments reference them by hash.
4.1.3 State hashing and identity
4.1.3(a) Per-store canonical encodings.Each store has a canonical encoding function pinned by (h_{\mathsf{Canon}}):
(\mathsf{Enc}_{\mathsf{Commit}}) encodes (\mathsf{CommitStore}) as sorted ((\mathsf{pin_id},\mathsf{PinRecord})) pairs in lexicographic order by (\mathsf{pin_id}).
(\mathsf{Enc}_{\mathsf{Rel}}) encodes (\mathsf{RelStore}) by canonical node ordering then canonical edge ordering by ((\mathsf{type},\mathsf{wgt},\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst},\mathsf{EdgeId})).
(\mathsf{Enc}_{\mathsf{Cand}}) encodes (\mathsf{CandStore}) in canonical candidate order.
(\mathsf{Enc}_{\mathsf{Proof}}) encodes the proof store by a canonical commitment set to the object hash set and an optional canonical summary digest (the proof store may be too large to serialize fully; the required commitment mechanism is pinned and must be replay-verifiable).
4.1.3(b) Per-store hashes.Define per-store hashes:[h_{\mathsf{commit}}:=H(\mathsf{Enc}{\mathsf{Commit}}(\mathsf{CommitStore})),\quadh{\mathsf{rel}}:=H(\mathsf{Enc}{\mathsf{Rel}}(\mathsf{RelStore})),][h{\mathsf{cand}}:=H(\mathsf{Enc}{\mathsf{Cand}}(\mathsf{CandStore})),\quadh{\mathsf{proof}}:=H(\mathsf{Enc}{\mathsf{Proof}}(\mathsf{ProofStore},\mathsf{LinkIndex})).]The proof-store encoding must ensure that if the set of stored object hashes and their bytes are identical, then (h{\mathsf{proof}}) is identical across machines.
4.1.3(c) Full state hash.A runtime state is[\Psi := (\mathsf{hdr},\mathsf{CommitStore},\mathsf{RelStore},\mathsf{CandStore},\mathsf{ProofStore},\mathsf{LinkIndex},\mathsf{LedgerHdr},\mathsf{step_index}),]where (\mathsf{LedgerHdr}) is the ledger header commitment (defined in the ledger chapter of the treatise). The full state hash is[h(\Psi) := H!\big(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{hdr})\ |\ h_{\mathsf{commit}}\ |\ h_{\mathsf{rel}}\ |\ h_{\mathsf{cand}}\ |\ h_{\mathsf{proof}}\ |\ \mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{LedgerHdr})\ |\ \mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{step_index})\big).]Two states are identical iff their full state hashes are equal.
4.1.3(d) State identity invariants.State identity satisfies:
Determinism: identical pre-state hash and identical step inputs produce identical post-state hash when the step verdict is (\mathsf{ACCEPT}) or (\mathsf{PARTIAL}).
Revert correctness: (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}) implies post-state hash equals pre-state hash.
Header binding: the state hash commits to (\mathsf{RuntimeHdr}); mixing states across runtime instances is ill-typed.
4.1.4 State deltas and delta commitments
4.1.4(a) Delta bundle.A step proposes a delta bundle[\Delta := (\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}},\Delta_{\mathsf{rel}},\Delta_{\mathsf{cand}},\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}}),]where each (\Delta_{\bullet}) is a finite sequence of primitive operations over the corresponding store (Section 4.2). Each sequence must be canonically ordered under pinned ordering rules.
4.1.4(b) Delta hashes.Each store delta has a canonical encoding (\mathsf{Enc}{\Delta\bullet}) and hash:[h(\Delta_\bullet):=H(\mathsf{Enc}{\Delta\bullet}(\Delta_\bullet)).]Define the delta root as a Merkle root over the ordered list ((h(\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{rel}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{cand}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}}))), using a pinned Merkle construction.
4.1.4(c) Delta admissibility conditions.A delta bundle is admissible for projection only if:
every primitive operation is well-formed by schema,
all referenced object hashes resolve (or are introduced in the same step’s proof delta in a way permitted by scope rules),
all targeted keys and identifiers are canonical,
all quantized numeric fields satisfy pinned numeric policies.
Failures here are hard failures (Section 4.4.3).
4.1.4(d) Delta commitments in step records.Every step record must commit to:
the ordered delta hashes (h(\Delta_\bullet)),
the delta root,
and the ordered witness list hash (Section 4.3.2 and 4.3.4).This ensures that replay verification can recompute legality and post-state hashing using only committed artifacts.
4.2 Minimal primitive operation set
4.2.1 Commitment primitives (pin/add/update/check)
4.2.1(a) Primitive set.The minimal commitment primitive set is:
(\mathsf{PinAdd}(\mathsf{pin_id},\mathsf{pin_type},h_{\mathsf{value}},h_{\mathsf{prov}},h_{\mathsf{meta}})),
(\mathsf{PinUpdate}(\mathsf{pin_id},h_{\mathsf{value}}',h_{\mathsf{prov}}',h_{\mathsf{meta}}')),
(\mathsf{PinCheck}(\mathsf{pin_id})\to (\mathsf{exists},\mathsf{PinRecord?})),
(\mathsf{PinConflict}(\mathsf{pin_id},\mathsf{candidate_record})\to{\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}}) (a deterministic predicate pinned by (\Omega)).
No destructive delete primitive is part of the minimal kernel. Deletion, if permitted, must be represented as a typed transform with explicit contract and proofs, and must compile down to explicitly allowed updates (e.g., supersession records) rather than raw deletion.
4.2.1(b) PinAdd semantics.(\mathsf{PinAdd}) proposes insertion of a new commitment record. It is well-formed iff:
(\mathsf{pin_id}) is canonical,
(\mathsf{pin_type}) is valid,
(h_{\mathsf{value}}) resolves to a typed object,
(h_{\mathsf{prov}}) resolves to a provenance witness binding this insertion to the step context.
If (\mathsf{pin_id}) already exists, (\mathsf{PinAdd}) is not directly applicable and must be converted into a (\mathsf{PinUpdate}) with explicit contradiction resolution if required by (\Omega).
4.2.1(c) PinUpdate semantics.(\mathsf{PinUpdate}) proposes updating the value hash and metadata for an existing pin. It is applicable only if:
(\mathsf{PinCheck}(\mathsf{pin_id})) returns (\mathsf{exists}=\mathsf{true}),
corridor scope permits updates for the namespace,
contradiction policy does not forbid overwrite without an explicit resolution proof.
Any update that changes a contradiction-controlled claim requires explicit resolution artifacts as mandated by (\Omega).
4.2.1(d) PinCheck and conflict checks.(\mathsf{PinCheck}) and (\mathsf{PinConflict}) are deterministic read primitives used by projection. They must not mutate state. (\mathsf{PinConflict}) must be pinned by hash as part of (\Omega) and is applied during projection to determine if proposed commitment deltas would violate contradiction policy.
4.2.2 Relation primitives (edge/add/update/query)
4.2.2(a) Primitive set.The minimal relation primitive set is:
(\mathsf{EdgeAdd}(\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst},\mathsf{etype},w_q,h_{\mathsf{prov}})),
(\mathsf{EdgeUpdate}(\mathsf{edge_id},w_q',h_{\mathsf{prov}}')),
(\mathsf{EdgeQuery}(\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst},\mathsf{etype})\to \mathsf{EdgeId}^\ast),
(\mathsf{EdgeTypePolicy}(\mathsf{etype})\to(\mathsf{merge_capable},\mathsf{cycle_rules},\mathsf{weight_bounds})) (pinned policy object).
4.2.2(b) EdgeAdd semantics.(\mathsf{EdgeAdd}) is well-formed iff:
endpoints are canonical node identifiers,
(\mathsf{etype}) is a valid edge type,
(w_q) satisfies quantization and corridor weight bounds,
provenance hash binds the addition to the step context.
If (\mathsf{etype}) is merge-capable, additional corridor obligations apply (Chapter 3): a merge requires explicit consistency proof; otherwise the operation is a hard failure.
4.2.2(c) EdgeUpdate semantics.(\mathsf{EdgeUpdate}) updates a specific edge’s weight or metadata. Updates must preserve edge typing and satisfy bounds. Edge identity is stable by (\mathsf{edge_id}), and updates must bind provenance and satisfy scope constraints.
4.2.2(d) EdgeQuery semantics.(\mathsf{EdgeQuery}) is a deterministic read primitive. The returned edge id list is canonically ordered. Queries cannot affect replay because they are derived from the canonical graph encoding and are thus deterministic for fixed state.
4.2.3 Candidate primitives (add/prune/sort/truncate)
4.2.3(a) Primitive set.The minimal candidate primitive set is:
(\mathsf{CandAdd}(h_c,s_q,h_{\mathsf{prov}})),
(\mathsf{CandPrune}(h_c,h_{\mathsf{prov}})),
(\mathsf{CandSort}()\to \mathsf{CandStore}) (canonical reorder),
(\mathsf{CandTruncate}(b_{\mathsf{branch}})\to m_{\mathsf{cand}}) (deterministic truncation mask).
4.2.3(b) CandAdd semantics.A candidate is represented by its hash (h_c) and a quantized score (s_q). (\mathsf{CandAdd}) is well-formed iff:
(h_c) resolves to a typed object (or a canonical candidate encoding object),
(s_q) satisfies pinned score quantization,
provenance hash binds the addition to the step context.
Candidate additions are subject to corridor branch caps.
4.2.3(c) CandPrune semantics.Candidate pruning is permitted only under corridor policy. If pruning requires justification (e.g., dominance proof), such justification must be provided via an admissible witness; otherwise pruning is disallowed or treated as a hard failure depending on (\Omega). Pruning cannot remove required ambiguity sets unless a resolution proof exists (encoded as explicit policy constraints in (\Omega)).
4.2.3(d) Deterministic truncation and canonical ordering.(\mathsf{CandSort}) enforces canonical ordering under the pinned comparator. (\mathsf{CandTruncate}(b)) returns a mask (m_{\mathsf{cand}}) indicating which candidates remain after truncation to cap (b). The mask is a deterministic function of the pre-state candidate list and proposed candidate additions, and must be reproducible under replay.
4.2.4 Proof primitives (add/link/verify/prune)
4.2.4(a) Primitive set.The minimal proof primitive set is:
(\mathsf{ObjAdd}(h_o,t,\mathbf{b})),
(\mathsf{ObjLink}(h_o,[h_{r_1},\ldots,h_{r_k}])),
(\mathsf{ObjVerifyLocal}(h_o,\mathsf{ver_id},h_{\mathsf{lcd}})\to {\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}}),
(\mathsf{ObjPrune}(h_o)) (permitted only if explicitly justified by corridor policy and proof obligations).
4.2.4(b) ObjAdd semantics.(\mathsf{ObjAdd}) is well-formed iff:
(t) is a valid type tag,
(\mathbf{b}) is well-formed under (t),
(h_o = H(\mathsf{Tag}(t)|\mathbf{b})) holds exactly.
The kernel must reject any object whose claimed hash does not match its canonical bytes.
4.2.4(c) ObjLink semantics.(\mathsf{ObjLink}) records a canonical, ordered reference list for (h_o). All referenced hashes must either already exist in (\mathsf{ProofStore}) or be introduced in the same step in a manner permitted by scope rules. Links enable dependency retrieval and proof reuse by hash.
4.2.4(d) Local verification and pruning.(\mathsf{ObjVerifyLocal}) runs a deterministic verifier identified by (\mathsf{ver_id}), consuming local-check data referenced by (h_{\mathsf{lcd}}). Verification must be deterministic and polynomial-time in the size of the local-check data. (\mathsf{ObjPrune}) is forbidden by default; if enabled by (\Omega), pruning requires explicit dominance/equivalence justification and must not break replay commitments.
4.3 Step function: expand → apply → project
4.3.1 Expand: scratch space, snapshots, counters
4.3.1(a) Step descriptor.A step is initiated by a step descriptor[\mathsf{StepDesc}:=(\mathsf{op_id},,h_{\mathsf{lt}},,h_P,,h_\Gamma,,\lambda),]where:
(\mathsf{op_id}) is a canonical step opcode identifier (a typed token; it may be a direct reference to a legal transform contract id or to a macro-expanded program identifier),
(h_{\mathsf{lt}}) is the hash of the legal transform contract to be applied (or (\varnothing) if the step is a pure kernel primitive program with its own contract object),
(h_P) is the parameter hash,
(h_\Gamma) is the context hash,
(\lambda) is the declared expansion depth.
All fields are committed in the step record and are inputs to provenance binding.
4.3.1(b) Expand operator.Define the expand operator[\mathsf{Expand}(\Psi_t,\mathsf{StepDesc},\Omega) := \mathsf{WS}_t]producing a workspace (\mathsf{WS}_t) containing:
pre-state hash (h_{\mathsf{pre}}=h(\Psi_t)),
per-store pre hashes ((h_{\mathsf{commit}},h_{\mathsf{rel}},h_{\mathsf{cand}},h_{\mathsf{proof}})),
resolved context (\Gamma) and parameter object (P) (resolved by hash),
step-local counters initialized to zero: compute cost, write cost by target, branch count, tolerance usage,
empty proposed delta bundle (\Delta=\varnothing),
empty witness list (W=\varnothing),
canonical step id (\mathsf{step_id}:=H(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{StepDesc})|h_{\mathsf{pre}})).
(\mathsf{Expand}) must not mutate any committed store.
4.3.1(c) Expand feasibility checks.(\mathsf{Expand}) performs deterministic prechecks:
(\lambda\le b_\lambda) (Chapter 3),
(h_{\mathsf{lt}}) resolves to a well-formed contract if present,
(h_P,h_\Gamma) resolve and are well-formed,
all referenced policy hashes match the runtime header.
Failure produces (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}) with a typed error artifact.
4.3.1(d) Snapshot semantics.The snapshot hashes in (\mathsf{WS}_t) are immutable during the step and are used to bind all witnesses and evidence to the pre-step state. Any step whose witness bindings do not match the snapshot hashes is illegal.
4.3.2 Apply: opcode-driven delta + witness emission
4.3.2(a) Apply operator interface.Define the apply operator[\mathsf{Apply}(\mathsf{WS}_t) := (\Delta_t, W_t, \mathbf{c}_t, E_t),]where:
(\Delta_t) is a proposed delta bundle (Section 4.1.4),
(W_t) is a witness bundle (ordered list of witness hashes),
(\mathbf{c}_t) is the deterministic cost vector (Chapter 3),
(E_t) is an optional ordered list of evidence object hashes (guard failures, near-threshold signals, measurement evidence), produced deterministically.
(\mathsf{Apply}) may read from the state stores via deterministic queries; it must not mutate committed stores.
4.3.2(b) Contracted execution.If (\mathsf{StepDesc}) references a legal transform contract, (\mathsf{Apply}) executes the transform’s deterministic runner interface (Chapter 2) on the resolved input payload(s), context (\Gamma), and parameters (P), producing:
a carrier output (p') (if applicable),
a proposed delta bundle (\Delta_t),
internal proof objects and evidence objects added to (\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}}),
any candidate or relation deltas if permitted by scope and contract,
cost accounting outputs.
All produced artifacts must be canonicalized in (\mathsf{Apply}) prior to return.
4.3.2(c) Witness emission obligations.(\mathsf{Apply}) must emit the witness set required by:
the transform witness profile (\mathcal{W}_{\mathsf{LT}}) (if a transform contract is used),
the corridor policy (\Omega) (scope extensions, contradiction resolution, budget justifications, required proof checks).
Witness emission includes, at minimum, a provenance binding witness that commits to:
(\mathsf{op_id}),
(h_P),
(h_{\mathsf{pre}}),
the delta root (computed from (\Delta_t)),
(h_{\mathsf{post}}) (computed after projection; the provenance witness may bind to an expected post-state commitment placeholder that is resolved during projection by deterministic substitution rules pinned in (\Omega)).
All witness hashes must resolve to typed witness objects stored (or introduced) in the proof store delta (\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}}), unless already present.
4.3.2(d) Delta and witness canonicalization.Prior to returning, (\mathsf{Apply}) must:
canonically order each (\Delta_\bullet),
compute (h(\Delta_\bullet)) for each store delta and the delta root,
canonically order witness references by (witness type id, witness hash),
compute the witness list hash (h(W_t)),
compute the reported cost vector (\mathbf{c}_t) deterministically from the execution trace and delta bundle under the pinned cost model.
Any non-canonical delta ordering or witness ordering is a determinism violation.
4.3.3 Project: legality projector and acceptance mask
4.3.3(a) Projector interface.Define the legality projector[\mathsf{Project}(\Psi_t,\Delta_t,W_t,\mathbf{c}t,\Omega,\mathsf{StepDesc}):= (\Psi{t+1},\mathsf{verdict}_t,m_t,E'_t),]where:
(\Psi_{t+1}) is the post-step state (equal to (\Psi_t) on revert),
(\mathsf{verdict}_t\in{\mathsf{ACCEPT},\mathsf{PARTIAL},\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}}),
(m_t) is an acceptance mask (Section 4.4.2),
(E'_t) is an evidence list including feasibility results and any detector evidence required by (\Omega).
4.3.3(b) Feasibility and verification checks.(\mathsf{Project}) executes the following deterministic checks in a fixed order pinned by (\Omega):
Schema validation of (\Delta_t) and all referenced objects.
Scope check (\mathsf{ScopeOK}_\Omega(\Delta_t,W_t)) (Chapter 3).
Budget feasibility for compute/write/branch/tolerance components (Chapter 3), including admissible budget justifications.
Witness completeness and verification: for each required witness class, verify deterministically using the pinned verifier set; enforce binding to ((\mathsf{op_id},h_P,h_{\mathsf{pre}},\text{delta root},\text{post hash commitment})).
Contradiction check for commitment deltas under (\Omega), and resolution proof verification if contradictions arise.
Determinism checks: confirm canonical ordering of deltas and witnesses and confirm numeric policies are satisfied.
Any failure among the hard constraints yields (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}).
4.3.3(c) Acceptance mask computation.If no hard-fail occurs, (\mathsf{Project}) computes a deterministic acceptance mask (m_t) that filters proposed deltas into accepted deltas (\Delta_t^{(m_t)}). The mask is computed as a deterministic function of:
pre-state hashes,
the proposed deltas,
verified witness set,
corridor policy (\Omega),
and any applicable truncation policies for candidates.
Mask computation must be replay-derivable; it must not depend on unspecified implementation choices.
4.3.3(d) Canonical application order and post-checks.Accepted deltas are applied in the fixed canonical order:
apply accepted commitment delta,
apply accepted relation delta,
apply accepted candidate delta (including deterministic truncation),
apply accepted proof delta,with within-store ordering equal to the canonical delta ordering. After applying accepted deltas, (\mathsf{Project}) performs post-resolution contradiction scans (Chapter 3) and any mandatory invariant checks designated by (\Omega) as projection-time checks (e.g., near-threshold checks for tolerance budgets). Evidence objects for near/violation are emitted deterministically.
4.3.4 Deterministic post-state hash computation
4.3.4(a) Post-store hashes.After applying (\Delta_t^{(m_t)}), compute post-store hashes ((h'{\mathsf{commit}},h'{\mathsf{rel}},h'{\mathsf{cand}},h'{\mathsf{proof}})) using the canonical store encodings pinned by (h_{\mathsf{Canon}}).
4.3.4(b) Post-state hash.Compute[h_{\mathsf{post}}:=h(\Psi_{t+1})]using the state hash definition (Section 4.1.3), including the unchanged runtime header and the updated step index.
4.3.4(c) Step record commitments.The step record (defined in the ledger chapter of the treatise) must commit to:
(\mathsf{op_id}), (h_P), (h_\Gamma), (\lambda),
(h_{\mathsf{pre}}), (h_{\mathsf{post}}),
per-store pre and post hashes,
delta hashes and delta root,
ordered witness list hash and ordered evidence list hash,
cost vector (\mathbf{c}_t),
verdict (\mathsf{verdict}_t) and acceptance mask (m_t).
All commitments are canonically encoded and hashed; any mismatch in replay invalidates the ledger.
4.3.4(d) Provenance completion.If the provenance witness emitted by (\mathsf{Apply}) binds to a placeholder post commitment, (\mathsf{Project}) must finalize provenance binding deterministically by substituting the computed (h_{\mathsf{post}}) into the provenance witness object under pinned substitution rules, and must commit the finalized witness hash in the witness list. This substitution mechanism must be explicit and deterministic; otherwise provenance binding is incomplete and the step is illegal.
4.4 Verdicts and partial acceptance
4.4.1 Accept vs partial accept vs reject+revert
4.4.1(a) Verdict set.The kernel produces exactly one verdict per step:
(\mathsf{ACCEPT}): all required primary effects are committed and no disallowed effects are committed; optional effects may be committed if certified.
(\mathsf{PARTIAL}): at least one effect is committed, and at least one proposed effect is deterministically discarded due to soft constraints (caps, optional scope, uncertified secondary writes), while all hard constraints are satisfied.
(\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}): no effects are committed; the state is reverted exactly to the pre-state checkpoint.
4.4.1(b) Primary-effect criterion.A step descriptor determines a primary target class via its referenced contract. A step can be (\mathsf{ACCEPT}) or (\mathsf{PARTIAL}) only if the primary target’s accepted delta is consistent with scope and witness requirements and the post-step state passes contradiction policy and determinism policy checks. If the primary target delta cannot be accepted due to any violation, the verdict must be (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}).
4.4.1(c) Commitment integrity under partial acceptance.Partial acceptance must never allow contradiction-controlled commitments to enter an inconsistent state. If any contradiction-controlled commitment delta would be applied without required resolution proofs, the verdict is (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}) regardless of the acceptability of other deltas.
4.4.1(d) Replay invariants for verdicts.Verdicts are replay-invariant: re-execution under identical inputs yields the same verdict. Any divergence in verdict indicates nondeterminism and is illegal.
4.4.2 Mask semantics and deterministic truncation
4.4.2(a) Mask structure.The acceptance mask is a structured object[m_t := (m_{\mathsf{commit}},m_{\mathsf{rel}},m_{\mathsf{cand}},m_{\mathsf{proof}},m_{\mathsf{op}}),]where:
(m_{\mathsf{commit}},m_{\mathsf{rel}},m_{\mathsf{cand}},m_{\mathsf{proof}}\in{0,1}) indicate whether each store delta is accepted at the store level,
(m_{\mathsf{op}}) is an optional within-store mask specifying which primitive operations are accepted when partial acceptance discards a subset (e.g., discarding unauthorized secondary writes while keeping authorized ones; truncating candidates to cap).
The mask is deterministically computed by (\mathsf{Project}) and is committed in the step record.
4.4.2(b) Filtering semantics.Define filtered deltas[\Delta'\bullet := \mathsf{Filter}(\Delta\bullet,m_{\mathsf{op}},\Omega)]as the deterministic selection of operations that satisfy scope, witness, and budget constraints. Store-level acceptance applies:[\Delta^{(m_t)}\bullet :=\begin{cases}\Delta'\bullet,& m_\bullet=1,\\varnothing,& m_\bullet=0.\end{cases}]Filtering must be canonical: operations are considered in canonical order and either retained or removed deterministically.
4.4.2(c) Candidate truncation mask.If the candidate store exceeds the branch cap (b_{\mathsf{branch}}), (\mathsf{Project}) must compute a truncation mask (m_{\mathsf{cand}}) by sorting candidates under the pinned comparator and keeping exactly the first (b_{\mathsf{branch}}). Ties are broken deterministically by candidate hash. The truncation mask is committed in (m_{\mathsf{op}}) and is replay-derivable.
4.4.2(d) Deterministic omission of uncertified secondary writes.If (\Omega) classifies certain secondary writes as soft and discardable, the projector must omit those operations deterministically when their authorizing witnesses are missing or fail verification, while still permitting acceptance of the step if the primary target effects remain legal. Such omissions must be recorded by (m_{\mathsf{op}}).
4.4.3 Hard-fail conditions
A step must produce (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}) if any of the following hard-fail conditions occur:
4.4.3(a) Determinism failures.
non-canonical delta ordering,
non-canonical witness ordering,
dependence on non-pinned randomness or non-pinned ambient state,
replay mismatch in any verified component during projection-time checks.
4.4.3(b) Witness failures for required witnesses.
missing required witness types per contract or (\Omega),
any required witness fails deterministic verification,
any witness fails step-local binding to ((\mathsf{op_id},h_P,h_{\mathsf{pre}},\text{delta root},h_{\mathsf{post}})).
4.4.3(c) Scope and budget violations (hard).
write to forbidden targets,
unauthorized secondary write to protected namespaces,
compute/write/branch/tolerance cost exceeds corridor budgets without admissible budget justification,
expansion depth exceeds cap.
4.4.3(d) Contradiction violations.
commitment delta introduces contradiction in a contradiction-controlled namespace without admissible resolution proof,
resolution proof fails verification,
post-resolution contradiction scan fails.
Hard-fail conditions prohibit protected commits; the post-state must equal the pre-state and the ledger must record the failure evidence deterministically.
4.4.4 Commit extraction and artifact emission
4.4.4(a) Output artifacts as typed objects.Every step emits a set of typed artifacts, each referenced by hash, including at minimum:
the step record object hash (ledger record),
the post-step checkpoint object hash (\mathsf{Chk}_{t+1}) for (\mathsf{ACCEPT}) or (\mathsf{PARTIAL}), or the pre-step checkpoint for (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}),
any new proof/evidence objects introduced in (\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}}^{(m_t)}),
any new or updated commitment pin records introduced in (\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}}^{(m_t)}).
Artifacts are part of the proof store by hash and may be referenced by later steps.
4.4.4(b) Commitment extraction rule.The set of newly committed pins in a step is the set of (\mathsf{PinAdd}) and (\mathsf{PinUpdate}) operations actually applied under (\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}}^{(m_t)}). Each such pin record must reference (h_{\mathsf{prov}}) that binds to the step context and must reference values that resolve to typed objects. A pin is not considered committed unless it appears in the post-step commitment store and is included in (h'_{\mathsf{commit}}).
4.4.4(c) Evidence emission and detector integration.When projection emits near-threshold or failure evidence objects (Chapter 3), these evidence hashes must be added to the proof store and referenced in the step record. Evidence emission is deterministic; omission of required evidence is illegal.
4.4.4(d) Extractability and replay closure.The kernel guarantees that all artifacts necessary to independently verify the step are recoverable from:
the runtime header hashes,
the step record commitments (pre/post hashes, delta hashes, witness hashes),
the proof store objects referenced by those hashes.
Thus extraction is a mechanical procedure: given a step record and access to the referenced hashes, a verifier can replay the step and confirm the post-state hash and committed artifacts without relying on any narrative description.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 4 — EXECUTION KERNEL: STATE, PRIMITIVES, AND STEP SEMANTICS
CHAPTER 5 — WITNESS SYSTEM: PROOF OBJECTS AND LOCAL VERIFICATION (Final Draft)
This chapter specifies the witness system: the finite set of typed proof-bearing artifacts and deterministic verifiers that enforce legality of state transitions. Witnesses are mandatory for any effect that changes protected stores, for any equivalence or correctness claim, for any contradiction resolution, and for any corridor escalation. Every witness is a hash-addressed object whose validity reduces to a deterministic polynomial-time verification procedure under a pinned verifier set and pinned canonicalization policy. The witness system is designed to prevent proof reuse out of context (certificate laundering) via step-local provenance binding to parameters, pre-state hash, delta commitments, and post-state hash.
5.1 Witness taxonomy and schemas
5.1.1 Commitment witness schema
5.1.1(a) Commitment witness type and scope.A commitment witness authorizes mutation of the commitment store. Let (\mathsf{WType}) be a fixed enum of witness types. Define the commitment witness type (\mathsf{WType}=\mathsf{COMMIT}). A commitment witness is a typed object[W_{\mathsf{C}} := (\mathsf{wtype},,\mathsf{bind},,\mathsf{pin_set},,\mathsf{val_refs},,\mathsf{just}),]with (\mathsf{wtype}=\mathsf{COMMIT}), where the remaining components are specified below. Commitment witnesses are required whenever a step applies any accepted commitment delta (\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}}^{(m)}\neq\varnothing).
5.1.1(b) Binding component.(\mathsf{bind}) is a fixed-format binding record:[\mathsf{bind} :=(\mathsf{op_id},,h_P,,h_{\mathsf{pre}},,r_{\Delta},,r_{\mathsf{src}},,h_{\mathsf{post}},,h_{\Omega},,h_{\mathsf{hdr}}),]where:
(\mathsf{op_id}) is the canonical step opcode identifier,
(h_P) is the parameter hash,
(h_{\mathsf{pre}}) is the pre-state hash,
(r_{\Delta}) is the delta root (Merkle root over per-store delta hashes),
(r_{\mathsf{src}}) is the source root (Merkle root over hashes of all inputs consumed by the step),
(h_{\mathsf{post}}) is the post-state hash,
(h_{\Omega}) is the corridor policy hash,
(h_{\mathsf{hdr}}) is the runtime header hash (committing to canonicalization/object/validator versions).
All fields are canonically encoded. This binding is not optional: a commitment witness without (\mathsf{bind}) is ill-formed.
5.1.1(c) Authorized pin-set component.(\mathsf{pin_set}) is a canonically ordered list of authorized pin operations:[\mathsf{pin_set} := [\pi_1,\ldots,\pi_k],]where each (\pi_i) is either:
(\mathsf{PinAdd}(\mathsf{pin_id},\mathsf{pin_type},h_{\mathsf{value}},h_{\mathsf{meta}})), or
(\mathsf{PinUpdate}(\mathsf{pin_id},h_{\mathsf{value}}',h_{\mathsf{meta}}')).Each pin id must be canonical, each value hash must resolve to a typed object, and the list must be ordered lexicographically by (\mathsf{pin_id}) then by operation kind. The commitment witness authorizes precisely these operations; any applied pin mutation outside (\mathsf{pin_set}) is illegal.
5.1.1(d) Justification component.(\mathsf{just}) is either empty (for non-conflicting additions) or a reference to a consistency proof witness hash (Section 5.4.1) when any of the following apply:
the pin id already exists (overwrite/merge),
the corridor’s contradiction predicate flags a conflict on insertion,
the pin operation changes a contradiction-controlled namespace,
the pin operation introduces a new alias record that could alter identity mapping.In these cases, (\mathsf{just}) must reference a verified consistency witness whose binding matches (\mathsf{bind}).
5.1.2 Relation witness schema
5.1.2(a) Relation witness type and scope.A relation witness authorizes mutation of the relation store (typed multigraph). Define the relation witness type (\mathsf{WType}=\mathsf{REL}). A relation witness is a typed object[W_{\mathsf{R}} := (\mathsf{wtype},,\mathsf{bind},,\mathsf{edge_set},,\mathsf{node_refs},,\mathsf{just}),]with (\mathsf{wtype}=\mathsf{REL}). A relation witness is required for any accepted relation delta (\Delta_{\mathsf{rel}}^{(m)}\neq\varnothing).
5.1.2(b) Edge-set component.(\mathsf{edge_set}) is a canonically ordered list of authorized edge operations:[\mathsf{edge_set} := [\epsilon_1,\ldots,\epsilon_\ell],]where each (\epsilon_j) is either:
(\mathsf{EdgeAdd}(\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst},\mathsf{etype},w_q)), or
(\mathsf{EdgeUpdate}(\mathsf{edge_id},w_q')).Endpoints and ids must be canonical, (\mathsf{etype}) must be valid, and weights must satisfy corridor bounds and quantization policy. Ordering is lexicographic by ((\mathsf{etype},\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst},\mathsf{edge_id})).
5.1.2(c) Merge-capable edge constraints.If any (\mathsf{EdgeAdd}) uses an edge type classified by corridor policy as merge-capable, then (\mathsf{just}) must reference a merge-authorization consistency witness (Section 5.4.2) binding the exact cluster identifiers (or node ids) implied by the merge. Without such authorization, any merge-capable edge mutation is a hard failure under (\Omega).
5.1.2(d) Relation witness minimality and completeness.A relation witness must authorize all applied relation operations. If the applied delta is a strict subset of (\mathsf{edge_set}) due to partial acceptance, the acceptance mask must record which authorized operations were accepted. The witness remains valid only if the accepted operations are a subset of those authorized and the binding matches the step context.
5.1.3 Routing/candidate witness schema
5.1.3(a) Routing witness type and scope.A routing witness authorizes candidate-store mutations and, when permitted, certified routing/tunneling proposals. Define (\mathsf{WType}=\mathsf{ROUTE}). A routing witness is a typed object[W_{\mathsf{A}} := (\mathsf{wtype},,\mathsf{bind},,\mathsf{cand_set},,\mathsf{cap_decl},,\mathsf{tun_decl}),]with (\mathsf{wtype}=\mathsf{ROUTE}). A routing witness is required for any accepted candidate delta (\Delta_{\mathsf{cand}}^{(m)}\neq\varnothing).
5.1.3(b) Candidate-set component.(\mathsf{cand_set}) is a canonically ordered list:[\mathsf{cand_set}:=[(h_{c_1},s_1),\ldots,(h_{c_M},s_M)],]where each (h_{c_i}) is a candidate object hash and (s_i\in\mathbb{Z}) is a quantized score. The list must be sorted by the pinned candidate comparator and must be duplicate-free under canonical candidate equivalence (hash equality unless explicitly bridged by an equivalence proof). Candidate objects referenced must resolve by hash.
5.1.3(c) Cap declaration and truncation binding.(\mathsf{cap_decl}) includes:
the branch cap (b_{\mathsf{branch}}) enforced for this step,
the comparator id hash,
the deterministic tie-break rule id hash.If (M>b_{\mathsf{branch}}), the projector will truncate deterministically; the routing witness remains valid only if it binds the cap and comparator/tie-break metadata used for truncation.
5.1.3(d) Tunnel declaration (optional, policy-gated).If (\Omega) permits tunnel proposals, (\mathsf{tun_decl}) may include a list of tunnel intents:[\mathsf{tun_decl}:=[(\mathsf{chk_ref},,\mathsf{from},,\mathsf{to},,h_{\mathsf{plan}})]^\ast,]where (\mathsf{chk_ref}) is a checkpoint hash that must equal (h_{\mathsf{pre}}) or another explicitly referenced checkpoint in (r_{\mathsf{src}}), and (h_{\mathsf{plan}}) is a route-plan object hash. Tunnel declarations are admissible only if the corridor’s scope allows them and all referenced objects resolve by hash. Tunnel declarations are proposals only; commitment of route plans is governed by commitment witnesses.
5.1.4 Proof/equivalence witness schema
5.1.4(a) Proof witness type and scope.A proof witness authorizes insertion of proof objects and equivalence claims into the proof store and, when permitted, pruning justified by dominance/equivalence. Define (\mathsf{WType}=\mathsf{PROOF}). A proof witness is a typed object[W_{\mathsf{P}} := (\mathsf{wtype},,\mathsf{bind},,\mathsf{method_id},,h_{\mathsf{in}},,h_{\mathsf{out}},,h_{\mathsf{lcd}},,h_{\mathsf{claim}}),]with (\mathsf{wtype}=\mathsf{PROOF}). Proof witnesses are required for any accepted proof delta (\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}}^{(m)}\neq\varnothing) that introduces or relies on proof objects.
5.1.4(b) Method identifier and verifier binding.(\mathsf{method_id}) references a pinned verifier rule in the verifier registry. The method identifier determines:
the expected schemas of (h_{\mathsf{in}}), (h_{\mathsf{out}}), and (h_{\mathsf{lcd}}),
the deterministic verification procedure (\mathsf{Verify}_{\mathsf{method_id}}),
the admissible claim strength (Section 5.3.4).
5.1.4(c) Local-check data requirement.(h_{\mathsf{lcd}}) references a local-check data object that must include (directly or by hash references):
a finite test basis specification,
any deterministic checker program id or rule parameters,
explicit tolerance bounds for this claim,
any convention fingerprints and policies required to interpret the check.Local-check data must be sufficient to verify the claim deterministically in polynomial time relative to its size.
5.1.4(d) Claim object and hash.(h_{\mathsf{claim}}) references a typed claim object binding:
invariant set hash (h(\mathcal I)),
tolerance profile hash (h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})),
any quotient/loss profile hashes if applicable,
verification level id (Section 5.3.4),
and a deterministic interpretation policy hash.A proof witness is valid only if it binds to the claim object hash and the claim object is consistent with the method id’s admissible claim class.
5.2 Provenance binding and anti-laundering
5.2.1 Step-local binding requirements
5.2.1(a) Step context object.For each step, define the step context[\mathsf{Ctx}:=(\mathsf{op_id},,h_P,,h_{\mathsf{pre}},,r_{\Delta},,r_{\mathsf{src}},,h_{\mathsf{post}},,h_{\Omega},,h_{\mathsf{hdr}}).](\mathsf{Ctx}) is fully determined by the runtime header, the pre-state, the parameter hash, the delta commitments, the source commitments, and the post-state.
5.2.1(b) Provenance witness as binding root.Define the provenance witness type (\mathsf{WType}=\mathsf{PROV}). A provenance witness is the typed object[W_{\mathsf{V}} := (\mathsf{wtype},,\mathsf{ctx}),]with (\mathsf{wtype}=\mathsf{PROV}) and (\mathsf{ctx}=\mathsf{Ctx}). The provenance witness must be present in every step that is not (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}), and may also be present in rejected steps as evidence binding.
5.2.1(c) Step-local admissibility predicate for witnesses.A witness (W) of any type is admissible for step context (\mathsf{Ctx}) iff:
it contains a binding record equal to (\mathsf{Ctx}) bytewise, or
it contains a binding record that references the provenance witness hash (h(W_{\mathsf{V}})) for this step and includes no conflicting binding fields.This requirement is a hard constraint under (\Omega).
5.2.1(d) Single-context rule.All witnesses accepted in a step must bind to the same (\mathsf{Ctx}). Mixed-context witness sets are illegal even if each witness is individually well-formed.
5.2.2 Binding to params/pre/deltas/post
5.2.2(a) Parameter hash binding.Witnesses must bind to (h_P) so that any change in parameters invalidates the witness. This prohibits reuse of a witness across parameterizations of the same transform.
5.2.2(b) Pre-state hash binding.Witnesses must bind to (h_{\mathsf{pre}}) so that any change in pre-state invalidates the witness. This prohibits reuse across different input states and prevents “proof laundering” by transplanting a proof into an unrelated ledger position.
5.2.2(c) Delta root binding.Witnesses must bind to (r_{\Delta}). Because (r_{\Delta}) commits to per-store delta hashes, any change in proposed operations invalidates the witness. This enforces that proofs and authorizations match exactly the operations they purport to justify.
5.2.2(d) Post-state hash binding.Witnesses must bind to (h_{\mathsf{post}}). A witness that authorizes operations must also authorize the resulting post-state commitment. This eliminates “open-ended” witnesses that could authorize multiple inconsistent outputs.
5.2.3 Source-hash commitments and input tracing
5.2.3(a) Source root definition.(r_{\mathsf{src}}) is a Merkle root over all hashes of input objects consumed by the step, including:
payload hashes read from stores,
referenced proof objects used for verification,
policy objects referenced by hash (if policy permits including them in source root; at minimum, policy hashes are committed via (h_{\Omega}) and (h_{\mathsf{hdr}})),
any external dependency hashes (if allowed) represented as typed commitment objects.
5.2.3(b) Mandatory source completeness.If a verifier requires access to an object by hash, then that hash must be included in (r_{\mathsf{src}}) or must be derivable as part of the step’s proof delta additions. “Hidden inputs” to verification are illegal.
5.2.3(c) Input tracing schema.For any step producing commitments, an input trace object may be emitted, binding:[\mathsf{Trace}:=(h_{\mathsf{pre}},,r_{\mathsf{src}},,\mathsf{consumed_pins},,\mathsf{consumed_proofs}),]where (\mathsf{consumed_pins}) is a canonically ordered list of commitment keys/hashes consumed and (\mathsf{consumed_proofs}) is a canonically ordered list of proof object hashes consumed. This object supports extraction and dependency proofs in later chapters.
5.2.3(d) Deterministic dependency reconstruction.Given a step record, (r_{\mathsf{src}}) must be sufficient to reconstruct, deterministically, the set of objects the verifier must resolve to replay-verify the step. Any dependence on external discovery is prohibited.
5.2.4 Non-transplantability theorem
5.2.4(a) Binding predicate.Define (\mathsf{Bound}(W,\mathsf{Ctx})) to be true iff witness (W) is well-formed and its binding fields match (\mathsf{Ctx}) exactly (or match via an explicit reference to the provenance witness hash that itself matches (\mathsf{Ctx})). All accepted witnesses must satisfy (\mathsf{Bound}).
5.2.4(b) Non-transplantability statement.Let (\mathsf{Ctx}\neq \mathsf{Ctx}'). If (W) is a witness such that (\mathsf{Bound}(W,\mathsf{Ctx})=\mathsf{true}), then (\mathsf{Bound}(W,\mathsf{Ctx}')=\mathsf{false}). In particular, any change in any component among (\mathsf{op_id},h_P,h_{\mathsf{pre}},r_{\Delta},r_{\mathsf{src}},h_{\mathsf{post}},h_{\Omega},h_{\mathsf{hdr}}) makes (W) invalid.
5.2.4(c) Consequence: prevention of certificate laundering.Certificate laundering consists of reusing a valid witness (W) to authorize a different step. The non-transplantability statement implies laundering is impossible without either:
producing a new witness bound to (\mathsf{Ctx}') (which must verify under the same verifiers), or
breaking collision resistance of the hash function or forging a verifier outcome.
Thus laundering is prevented structurally by binding to step commitments.
5.2.4(d) Replay enforcement.Replay verification recomputes (\mathsf{Ctx}) from recorded fields and rejects any witness set containing a witness not bound to the recomputed (\mathsf{Ctx}). Therefore non-transplantability is not merely a design claim; it is a mechanically enforced property of ledger verification.
5.3 Local verification regime
5.3.1 Deterministic verifier API
5.3.1(a) Verifier set and version pinning.The verifier set (\mathsf{VerSet}) is a pinned finite set of deterministic verifier functions indexed by witness type and (for proof witnesses) by method id:[\mathsf{VerSet} := {\mathsf{Verify}{\mathsf{PROV}},\mathsf{Verify}{\mathsf{COMMIT}},\mathsf{Verify}{\mathsf{REL}},\mathsf{Verify}{\mathsf{ROUTE}},\mathsf{Verify}{\mathsf{PROOF}},\mathsf{Verify}{\mathsf{CONSIST}},\mathsf{Verify}{\mathsf{BUDGET}}},]each with a pinned implementation digest hash included in (h{\mathsf{hdr}}).
5.3.1(b) Uniform verifier signature.Each verifier is a deterministic function:[\mathsf{Verify}_\tau(W,\mathsf{Ctx},\mathsf{Resolve})\to {\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}},]where:
(W) is the decoded witness object,
(\mathsf{Ctx}) is the step context,
(\mathsf{Resolve}) is a deterministic resolver that maps object hashes to canonical bytes and type tags, failing deterministically if missing.
5.3.1(c) Pre-verification canonical checks.Every verifier must first check:
(W) is well-formed for its schema,
(\mathsf{Bound}(W,\mathsf{Ctx})=\mathsf{true}),
all referenced object hashes resolve and are well-formed,
ordering constraints in the witness payload are canonical.
Failure of any of these yields (\mathsf{false}).
5.3.1(d) Output binding requirement.For witnesses authorizing writes, the verifier must confirm that the witness’s authorized operation set is consistent with the committed delta root (r_{\Delta}) and the post-state hash (h_{\mathsf{post}}). This ensures witnesses authorize the actual state transition.
5.3.2 Polynomial-time constraints and bounded checks
5.3.2(a) Polynomial-time requirement.All verifiers must run in time polynomial in the size of:
the witness object bytes,
the local-check data bytes referenced by the witness,
the sizes of any explicitly referenced objects required for verification.
No verifier may require unbounded search, nondeterministic exploration, or external oracles.
5.3.2(b) Bounded local-check principle.Correctness of transforms is enforced via bounded local checks. For any proof witness method (\mathsf{method_id}), the verifier must specify:
a bounded test basis size limit (as a function of corridor caps),
a deterministic test generation rule pinned by hash,
explicit tolerance bounds and quantization rules.
5.3.2(c) Verifier cost accounting.Verifier invocations contribute deterministically to the step compute cost (c_{\mathsf{comp}}) under the pinned cost model. Therefore verifier complexity is not hidden; it is budgeted. Any attempt to execute more verification work than budgeted is illegal unless a budget escalation witness is present and verifies.
5.3.2(d) Soundness boundary.Verifier acceptance proves only the property stated by the method id under the declared local-check regime and claim strength (Section 5.3.4). No witness is interpreted as proving more than its declared verification level permits.
5.3.3 Test basis commitments and checker programs
5.3.3(a) Test basis object.A test basis is a typed object (\mathsf{TB}) whose canonical fields include:[\mathsf{TB}:=(\mathsf{basis_kind},,\mathsf{basis_data_refs},,\mathsf{gen_rule_id},,\mathsf{params},,\mathsf{size_bound}),]where (\mathsf{basis_data_refs}) is a canonically ordered list of hashes referencing basis vectors, probe signals, probe distributions, or canonical index sets. The test basis hash must appear in the proof witness’s local-check data object.
5.3.3(b) Checker program object.A checker program is a pinned deterministic procedure referenced by a program id hash (\mathsf{chk_id}). The checker consumes:
the decoded input object(s),
the decoded output object(s),
the test basis object,
method parameters from (h_{\mathsf{lcd}}),and returns (\mathsf{true}) iff the method’s predicate holds within declared tolerances. Checker program identity and version are committed by hash; replacing a checker changes (\mathsf{chk_id}) and invalidates proofs unless reissued.
5.3.3(c) Local-check data schema.For method (\mathsf{method_id}), local-check data must bind:
(h(\mathcal I)) invariant set hash,
(h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})) tolerance profile hash,
(\mathsf{chk_id}) checker program id hash,
(\mathsf{TB}) test basis hash,
any convention fingerprint hashes necessary to interpret the objects being checked,
the verification level id (\mathsf{VL}).
5.3.3(d) Deterministic generation and replay.If the test basis includes generated tests (rather than explicit listed vectors), generation must be deterministic and pinned. If pseudo-random generation is permitted by (\Omega), it must be derived from a pinned seed object whose hash is included in (r_{\mathsf{src}}) and committed in (\mathsf{Ctx}). Otherwise, only non-random deterministic generation is admissible.
5.3.4 Verification levels and claim strength
5.3.4(a) Verification level enum.Define a pinned enum of verification levels (\mathsf{VL}\in{\mathsf{VL0},\mathsf{VL1},\mathsf{VL2},\mathsf{VL3},\mathsf{VL4}}). The semantics of each level is pinned by a policy object referenced by hash and includes admissible claim classes. The minimum required semantics are:
(\mathsf{VL0}): fixed finite test basis only.
(\mathsf{VL1}): finite test basis plus algebraic side conditions (e.g., generator relations, structural identities) encoded as additional checks.
(\mathsf{VL2}): deterministic expanded test suite derived from pinned seeds or deterministic coverage rules, within corridor caps.
(\mathsf{VL3}): bounded coverage guarantees (e.g., deterministic (\varepsilon)-net over a parameter grid) within caps.
(\mathsf{VL4}): external mechanized proof artifact hook by hash (still verified locally by checking inclusion and correctness of a mechanized proof certificate under a pinned proof-checker digest).
5.3.4(b) Claim strength binding rule.Every proof witness includes (\mathsf{VL}) in its claim object (h_{\mathsf{claim}}). A claim is interpreted only at its declared level. It is illegal to interpret a (\mathsf{VL0}) proof as justifying a claim whose admissible class requires (\mathsf{VL2}) or higher.
5.3.4(c) Strength preorder over verification.Define a preorder (\preceq) on proof strength by:[(\mathcal I_2,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_2,\mathsf{VL}_2)\preceq (\mathcal I_1,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_1,\mathsf{VL}_1)\iff\mathcal I_1\subseteq \mathcal I_2,\ \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_2\le \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_1,\ \mathsf{VL}_2\ge \mathsf{VL}_1,]with (\mathsf{VL}) ordered by the enum order. Downstream requirements may accept proofs that are stronger in this preorder.
5.3.4(d) Prohibited over-claims.A proof witness is invalid if its claim object asserts:
invariants not checked by the method id’s checker program,
tolerances tighter than those encoded in local-check data,
a higher verification level than the local-check data actually satisfies,
exactness where a loss/quotient profile is declared elsewhere in the step context.Such mismatches are detected deterministically by the proof verifier and result in witness failure.
5.4 Consistency and escalation witnesses
5.4.1 Consistency proofs for contradiction resolution
5.4.1(a) Consistency witness type and schema.A consistency witness authorizes contradiction resolution in the commitment store (and, when applicable, relation merges). Define (\mathsf{WType}=\mathsf{CONSIST}). A consistency witness is a typed object[W_{\mathsf{K}} := (\mathsf{wtype},,\mathsf{bind},,\mathsf{conflict_set},,\mathsf{res_kind},,h_{\mathsf{proof}}),]where:
(\mathsf{conflict_set}) is a canonically ordered list of conflicting keys and their current hashes,
(\mathsf{res_kind}\in{\mathsf{DISAMBIGUATE},\mathsf{MERGE},\mathsf{OVERWRITE}}),
(h_{\mathsf{proof}}) references a proof object whose method id is permitted by (\Omega) for this namespace and resolution kind.
5.4.1(b) Consistency verifier obligations.The consistency verifier must check:
binding matches (\mathsf{Ctx}),
the conflict set matches the contradiction evidence derivable from (h_{\mathsf{pre}}) and the proposed commitment delta,
the resolution kind is permitted by (\Omega),
(h_{\mathsf{proof}}) verifies deterministically under its method id and local-check data,
the post-step commitment store satisfies the corridor contradiction predicate.
5.4.1(c) Disambiguation obligations.If (\mathsf{res_kind}=\mathsf{DISAMBIGUATE}), the proof must bind the canonical renaming scheme and show that all resulting keys are canonical and that downstream dependencies either remain valid or are updated by explicit, authorized deltas.
5.4.1(d) Overwrite and supersession obligations.If (\mathsf{res_kind}=\mathsf{OVERWRITE}), the proof must establish that the overwritten artifact is superseded under an explicit refinement or correction criterion pinned by policy, and must bind the supersession record(s) inserted into the proof store and commitment store.
5.4.2 Merge authorization and cluster integrity
5.4.2(a) Merge authorization witness.A merge authorization is a specialization of the consistency witness where (\mathsf{res_kind}=\mathsf{MERGE}) and the conflict set refers to identity clusters in the relation store or to equivalence-class commitments. The proof must establish equivalence under an explicit invariant set and tolerance profile and must bind the resulting merged identifier(s).
5.4.2(b) Cluster integrity constraints.When merges are applied, the corridor requires that cluster integrity be maintained:
no implicit transitive closure merges are committed unless explicitly authorized,
merge-capable edge types must be explicitly listed in the corridor merge policy,
any merge that changes the induced partition must be accompanied by a merge proof.
5.4.2(c) Merge proof local-check requirements.Merge proofs must provide local-check data sufficient to verify equivalence on a declared test basis and under a declared verification level. If the merge claim affects commitments, the proof must also bind to the contradiction policy namespace and the resulting post-commitment store hash.
5.4.2(d) Merge audit trail.Every merge must emit an audit record (typed evidence object) containing:
pre-merge identifiers,
post-merge identifier,
proof hash,
invariant/tolerance hashes,
verification level id,all bound to (\mathsf{Ctx}). This supports replay-verifiable extraction of identity changes.
5.4.3 Budget escalation justification
5.4.3(a) Budget escalation witness type.Define (\mathsf{WType}=\mathsf{BUDGET}). A budget escalation witness is a typed object[W_{\mathsf{B}} := (\mathsf{wtype},,\mathsf{bind},,\mathsf{req},,\mathsf{reason},,h_{\mathsf{proof}}),]where:
(\mathsf{req}) is a requested override tuple (compute/write/branch/tolerance components) expressed in the corridor’s quantized units,
(\mathsf{reason}) is an enumerated reason code permitted by (\Omega),
(h_{\mathsf{proof}}) references a proof object whose method id is permitted by (\Omega) for budget escalation.
5.4.3(b) Budget verifier obligations.The budget verifier must check:
binding matches (\mathsf{Ctx}),
requested budgets do not exceed corridor maxima,
the reason code is permitted by policy for this step class,
the proof verifies deterministically,
the requested budgets are sufficient to cover the observed costs (\mathbf{c}_t) committed by the step record.
5.4.3(c) Scope coupling to escalation.Budget escalation does not grant scope. Any escalation witness must not be interpreted as authorizing additional write targets beyond those permitted by (\Omega) and the step’s contract. If scope expansion is allowed, it must be authorized by explicit scope-extension rules and corresponding witnesses.
5.4.3(d) Escalation recording and replay.Escalation witnesses must be included in the ordered witness list and their hashes included in the witness root. Replay verification recomputes feasibility under the substituted budgets and confirms that without the escalation the step would violate feasibility, ensuring escalations are meaningful, bounded, and auditable.
5.4.4 Evidence objects and failure codes
5.4.4(a) Evidence object schema.An evidence object is a typed object[\mathsf{Ev} := (\mathsf{ev_type},,\mathsf{bind},,\mathsf{code},,\mathsf{details},,\mathsf{refs}),]where:
(\mathsf{ev_type}) identifies the evidence class (guard failure, budget near-violation, invariant drift, contradiction detection, verifier failure, truncation event),
(\mathsf{bind}) binds to (\mathsf{Ctx}),
(\mathsf{code}) is a failure/near code from a pinned enum,
(\mathsf{details}) is a canonical, schema-validated payload,
(\mathsf{refs}) is a canonically ordered list of referenced hashes (inputs, deltas, proofs, policies).
5.4.4(b) Failure code enum.(\Omega) pins a failure code enum containing at minimum:
(\mathsf{E_TYPE}): type mismatch / schema invalid,
(\mathsf{E_SCOPE}): scope violation,
(\mathsf{E_BUDGET}): budget infeasible without admissible escalation,
(\mathsf{E_BRANCH}): branch cap violation,
(\mathsf{E_DET}): determinism violation,
(\mathsf{E_PROV}): provenance binding failure,
(\mathsf{E_VERIFY}): verifier failure,
(\mathsf{E_CONTR}): contradiction without admissible resolution,
(\mathsf{E_INVAR}): invariant drift beyond tolerance,
(\mathsf{E_REPLAY}): replay mismatch.
Each code has pinned semantics and required detail fields.
5.4.4(c) Near-violation evidence.Near-violations are distinct from failures. (\Omega) pins near-threshold policies and requires evidence emission when a metric is within the near band, including:
remaining tolerance budgets per invariant,
remaining branch cap headroom,
remaining write budget headroom for protected targets,
recommended mandatory mitigation actions as policy ids (not narrative).
5.4.4(d) Evidence admissibility and retention.Evidence objects are admissible only if schema-valid and bound to (\mathsf{Ctx}). Required evidence objects must be present when their triggering conditions occur; omission is a hard determinism failure because it changes the replay-visible artifact set. Evidence retention policy (whether evidence must be committed or may remain as proof-store-only artifacts) is specified by (\Omega) and enforced by scope and write budgets.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 5 — WITNESS SYSTEM: PROOF OBJECTS AND LOCAL VERIFICATION
CHAPTER 6 — CANONICAL OBJECT STANDARD: TYPED HASH-ADDRESSED DATA (Final Draft)
This chapter specifies the Canonical Object Standard: a single, strict data model for all artifacts in the framework. The standard defines (i) canonical encodings, (ii) typed content-addressing by hash, (iii) core object families used by transforms, execution, and verification, (iv) proof object families required for local verification, and (v) privacy-by-commitment modes with explicit threat boundaries. Every runtime store, witness, ledger record, plan, receipt, and proof reduces to a Canonical Object Standard (COS) object.
6.1 Canonical encodings
6.1.1 Field ordering and integer encodings
6.1.1(a) Canonical schema declaration.Every COS object belongs to a type (t\in\mathsf{TypeId}) with an associated schema (\sigma(t)). A schema is a finite ordered list of fields:[\sigma(t)=\big[(f_1,\tau_1),(f_2,\tau_2),\ldots,(f_m,\tau_m)\big],]where each ((f_i,\tau_i)) is a field name and field type. The schema order is normative: canonical encoding writes fields in exactly this order and forbids omission of required fields. Optional fields are present only when explicitly set and are preceded by a presence bit in canonical form.
6.1.1(b) Canonical map encoding.A field type may be a finite map (\mathsf{Map}[K,V]). Canonical encoding of a map is:
encode the number of entries as a canonical unsigned integer,
sort keys by canonical byte encoding of the key value,
encode each key then value in sorted order.
Maps with duplicate keys under canonical key equality are ill-formed. The sort order is bytewise lexicographic on canonical key encodings.
6.1.1(c) Integer encoding (CanonicalVarInt).Fix a global integer encoding (\mathsf{CVI}) (CanonicalVarInt). For all integers:
Unsigned integers (u\in\mathbb{N}) are encoded in base-128 little-endian varint form with minimal length: each byte stores 7 bits; the high bit indicates continuation; the final byte has high bit 0; leading zero groups are forbidden.
Signed integers (z\in\mathbb{Z}) are encoded using ZigZag mapping (z\mapsto u\in\mathbb{N}) followed by unsigned CVI encoding:[u =\begin{cases}2z, & z\ge 0,\-2z-1, & z<0.\end{cases}]Any other integer representation is illegal.
6.1.1(d) Length-prefixing and byte strings.Byte strings (\mathbf{b}\in{0,1}^{8\ast}) are encoded as: CVI(length in bytes) followed by raw bytes. Strings are UTF-8 byte strings with canonical normalization: the UTF-8 sequence must be valid and must not contain overlong encodings; canonical form is the exact UTF-8 bytes (no alternate normalizations). Invalid UTF-8 is illegal for string-typed fields.
6.1.2 Deterministic floating/quantized representations
6.1.2(a) Prohibition of unconstrained floating.No COS object may contain an unconstrained floating value that can alter its canonical bytes across platforms. Any numeric quantity that participates in hashing must be either:
an integer (CVI), or
a quantized fixed-point integer with explicit scaling, or
a byte-exact IEEE-754 encoding whose normalization rules are pinned and enforced (Section 6.1.2(c)).
6.1.2(b) Quantized numeric type.Define a quantization policy object (Q) with canonical fields:[Q := (\mathsf{scale},\ \mathsf{round},\ \mathsf{overflow},\ \mathsf{dtype}).]A quantized scalar value is represented as an integer (q\in\mathbb{Z}) interpreted as (q/\mathsf{scale}). The encoding stores ((h(Q), q)), where (h(Q)) is the hash of the pinned quantization policy. All quantized computations used for verification must reduce to integer arithmetic under (Q) and are deterministic.
6.1.2(c) IEEE-754 canonical floats (when explicitly allowed).If floating is allowed for a specific type, the type schema must explicitly reference a pinned FloatPolicy object (F) specifying:
precision: binary32 or binary64,
rounding mode: one of a pinned finite set,
NaN policy: NaNs are forbidden unless explicitly permitted; if permitted, NaNs must be canonicalized to a single fixed bit pattern pinned in (F),
signed zero policy: either preserve sign bit or normalize (-0) to (+0) (pinned).A float field is then stored as raw IEEE-754 bytes under (F) with no platform-dependent transformations.
6.1.2(d) Complex numbers.Complex values are encoded either as:
two quantized integers ((q_{\Re},q_{\Im})) under a shared quantization policy (Q), stored as ((h(Q),q_{\Re},q_{\Im})), or
two canonical IEEE-754 floats under a shared FloatPolicy (F), stored as ((h(F),\mathbf{b}{\Re},\mathbf{b}{\Im})).Mixed representations are illegal.
6.1.3 Set-like vs sequence-like collections
6.1.3(a) Collection kind declaration.Every collection field declares its kind:
Set-like: order-insensitive; duplicates forbidden under canonical equality.
Sequence-like: order-sensitive; duplicates permitted; order is part of meaning.The collection kind is part of the schema and thus part of the object hash.
6.1.3(b) Canonical encoding of set-like collections.A set-like collection ({x_i}) is canonically encoded by:
encoding its cardinality as CVI,
encoding each element to bytes using its canonical encoder,
sorting elements by bytewise lexicographic order of their encodings,
verifying strict uniqueness (no equal encodings),
emitting the sorted encodings in order.If any element cannot be canonically encoded or duplicates are present, the object is ill-formed.
6.1.3(c) Canonical encoding of sequence-like collections.A sequence-like collection ([x_1,\ldots,x_n]) is canonically encoded by:
encoding its length as CVI,
encoding each element in index order using canonical encoders.No sorting is performed. Any dependence on platform ordering is forbidden.
6.1.3(d) Canonical equivalence for collection elements.For element types that may have multiple semantic representations (e.g., due to allowed floating NaNs or alternate internal forms), the canonicalization rules in the element’s schema must define a unique canonical byte encoding before inclusion in any collection. Collection validity depends on canonical element bytes, not semantic comparisons.
6.1.4 Cross-platform byte stability
6.1.4(a) Byte stability axiom.For each type (t), (\mathsf{Enc}_t) must satisfy: for any semantic value (x\in\mathsf{Sem}[t]), any conforming implementation produces identical bytes (\mathsf{Enc}_t(x)) on any platform. Byte stability is enforced by (i) pinned integer encodings, (ii) pinned float policies when floats are permitted, (iii) canonical ordering rules for maps and sets, and (iv) prohibition of ambient dependencies.
6.1.4(b) Endianness and array layout.All multi-byte numeric encodings inside arrays must use fixed endianness specified by schema (default little-endian) and must encode:
dtype tag (explicit),
shape vector (CVI per dimension),
stride policy (either fixed row-major or explicitly pinned),
data bytes in canonical contiguous order.Non-contiguous storage is illegal unless a pinned stride policy specifies deterministic traversal and serialization order.
