6.1.4(c) Versioning and compatibility.Encoders and decoders are versioned by hash. An object’s type tag must include a schema hash or schema version id such that decoder selection is deterministic. Compatibility between versions is not assumed; any migration must be represented as an explicit legal transform with proofs where required.
6.1.4(d) Canonical hash function binding.The hash function (H) is treated as a fixed primitive. The runtime header commits to a hash-function identifier hash. All object hashes and Merkle roots are defined relative to that hash function; mixing hashes across different (H) is ill-typed.
6.2 Core object families
6.2.1 Signals/grids/samples
6.2.1(a) Grid specification object.A grid object (\mathsf{Grid}) is a typed object with canonical fields:[\mathsf{Grid}:=(\mathsf{dim},\ \mathsf{shape},\ h(Q_{\mathsf{spacing}}),\ \mathsf{spacing}_q,\ \mathsf{boundary},\ \mathsf{index_mode},\ \mathsf{origin}).]
(\mathsf{dim}\in\mathbb{N}) is the dimension,
(\mathsf{shape}\in\mathbb{N}^{\mathsf{dim}}) is the size per axis,
(\mathsf{spacing}_q\in\mathbb{Z}^{\mathsf{dim}}) is quantized spacing,
(\mathsf{boundary}) is a pinned enum (periodic, fixed, reflect, etc.),
(\mathsf{index_mode}) is a pinned enum (0..N-1, centered, etc.),
(\mathsf{origin}) is a quantized coordinate vector.
6.2.1(b) Sample array object.A sample array object (\mathsf{Arr}) is:[\mathsf{Arr}:=(\mathsf{dtype},\ \mathsf{shape},\ \mathsf{layout},\ h(Q)\ \text{or}\ h(F),\ \mathsf{data_bytes}),]where (\mathsf{data_bytes}) is the canonical contiguous serialization. If quantized, the array stores integer values and references (h(Q)). If floating, the array stores raw IEEE-754 bytes and references (h(F)).
6.2.1(c) Signal object.A signal object (\mathsf{Sig}) binds a grid to samples:[\mathsf{Sig}:=(h(\mathsf{Grid}),\ h(\mathsf{Arr}),\ h(\mathsf{Spec}),\ \mathsf{tags}),]where (h(\mathsf{Spec})) is an optional convention fingerprint hash (e.g., transform convention spec) and (\mathsf{tags}) is a canonical set of typed tags (set-like). The samples are referenced by hash, not embedded, to support reuse and privacy by commitment.
6.2.1(d) Deterministic derived signals.Any derived signal (e.g., windowed, resampled, normalized) must be represented as a new (\mathsf{Sig}) object with an explicit derivation proof or derivation transform reference. Silent in-place modification of sample arrays is illegal.
6.2.2 Operators/gates/transforms
6.2.2(a) Operator object.An operator object (\mathsf{Op}) represents an executable transform or primitive action and has canonical fields:[\mathsf{Op}:=(\mathsf{op_kind},\ h(L_{\mathrm{in}}),\ h(L_{\mathrm{out}}),\ h(\mathsf{SpecIn}),\ h(\mathsf{SpecOut}),\ h(\mathsf{impl}),\ h(\mathsf{proof}),\ \mathsf{params_schema}).]
(\mathsf{op_kind}) is a pinned enum identifying the operator family,
(h(\mathsf{impl})) references an implementation descriptor (program hash or rule hash),
(h(\mathsf{proof})) references a proof object certifying the operator’s declared invariants (as required by its contract),
(\mathsf{params_schema}) is a schema hash for parameters used by the operator.
6.2.2(b) Gate object.A gate is an operator intended for reuse and routing. Gate objects must additionally carry:
a stable alias key (stored in commitments, not in the gate object),
a declared invariant set hash and tolerance profile hash,
a declared verification level and test basis hash for its proof.
6.2.2(c) Transform contract object reference.Any operator that is admissible for execution must either:
embed a reference to a legal transform contract hash (h(\mathsf{LT})), or
be itself a contract object under a designated contract schema.Operators without explicit contract references are ill-formed for execution.
6.2.2(d) Convention fingerprints.Operators must carry input and output convention fingerprints (spec hashes) sufficient to forbid implicit convention drift during composition. Composition requires equality of adjacent spec hashes or insertion of explicit convention-bridge operators whose correctness is certified by proof objects.
6.2.3 Distributions/candidate sets
6.2.3(a) Candidate set object.A candidate set object (\mathsf{CandSet}) is:[\mathsf{CandSet}:=(h(\mathsf{Context}),\ h(\mathsf{Items}),\ h(\mathsf{Scores})?,\ h(\mathsf{Comparator}),\ \mathsf{cap}).]
(\mathsf{Items}) is a sequence-like list of candidate hashes in canonical order,
(\mathsf{Scores}) is an aligned sequence-like list of quantized integers if present,
(h(\mathsf{Comparator})) pins the ordering rule used,
(\mathsf{cap}) is the branch cap applied.
6.2.3(b) Discrete distribution object.A discrete distribution object (\mathsf{Dist}) is:[\mathsf{Dist}:=(h(\mathsf{Support}),\ h(Q_{\mathsf{prob}}),\ h(\mathsf{ProbVec}),\ h(\mathsf{NormProof}),\ \mathsf{mode}),]where (\mathsf{ProbVec}) is a quantized integer vector, and (h(\mathsf{NormProof})) references a proof object certifying normalization (e.g., sum equals 1 in quantized units or within tolerance).
6.2.3(c) Constraint transcript objects (optional).When candidate generation or distribution fitting requires deterministic transcripts (e.g., pruning traces, constraint satisfaction traces), these are stored as trace proof objects (Section 6.3.2) and referenced by hash, rather than embedded in candidates.
6.2.3(d) Deterministic sampling policy (when permitted).If sampling from distributions is permitted, a sampling policy object (\mathsf{SampPol}) must be pinned and referenced by hash, including generator algorithm id and seed hash. Sampling outcomes that affect state must be committed as explicit objects; silent sampling is prohibited.
6.2.4 Plans/ledgers/receipts
6.2.4(a) Plan object.A plan object (\mathsf{Plan}) is the canonical compilation output:[\mathsf{Plan}:=(h(\mathsf{Goal}),\ h(\Omega),\ h(\mathsf{Graph}),\ h(\mathsf{Route}),\ h(\mathsf{Program}),\ h(\mathsf{WPlan}),\ h(\mathsf{LPlan}),\ h(\mathsf{Canon}),\ h(\mathsf{VerSet})).]All referenced components are typed objects. The plan hash (h(\mathsf{Plan})) uniquely identifies the intended execution.
6.2.4(b) Ledger header object.A ledger header (\mathsf{LedHdr}) commits to:[\mathsf{LedHdr}:=(h_{\Omega},h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h_{\mathsf{CostModel}},h_{\mathsf{genesis}}),]where (h_{\mathsf{genesis}}) commits to the initial state hash and initial stores. This header is immutable for the ledger.
6.2.4(c) Step record object.A step record (\mathsf{StepRec}) commits to:[\mathsf{StepRec}:=(\mathsf{step_index},\mathsf{op_id},h_P,h_\Gamma,\lambda,h_{\mathsf{pre}},h_{\mathsf{post}},h(\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{rel}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{cand}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}}),r_\Delta,h(W),h(E),\mathbf{c},\mathsf{verdict},m),]where (h(W)) is the ordered witness list hash and (h(E)) is the ordered evidence list hash. All encodings are canonical.
6.2.4(d) Receipt object.A receipt object (\mathsf{Rcpt}) is a compact verification artifact:[\mathsf{Rcpt}:=(h(\mathsf{Plan}),h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),h_{\mathsf{final}},r_{\mathsf{pins}},r_{\mathsf{wits}},h_{\Omega},h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}),]where (r_{\mathsf{pins}}) is a Merkle root over committed pin records relevant to the plan outputs and (r_{\mathsf{wits}}) is a Merkle root over all witness hashes used. Receipts are verifiable by deterministic replay or by verifying a referenced ledger prefix.
6.3 Proof object families
6.3.1 Equivalence proofs
6.3.1(a) Equivalence proof schema.An equivalence proof object (\mathsf{ProofEq}) is:[\mathsf{ProofEq}:=(\mathsf{claim_kind},h_{\mathsf{lhs}},h_{\mathsf{rhs}},h(\mathcal I),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}),\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{TB}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(\mathsf{Evidence})?).]
(h_{\mathsf{lhs}},h_{\mathsf{rhs}}) reference typed objects,
(h(\mathcal I)) and (h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})) bind the invariant set and tolerances,
(\mathsf{VL}) binds verification level,
(h(\mathsf{TB})) binds the test basis,
(h(\mathsf{ChkProg})) binds the checker program identity.
6.3.1(b) Verification semantics.The verifier for (\mathsf{ProofEq}) must:
resolve and decode (\mathsf{lhs},\mathsf{rhs}),
resolve (\mathsf{TB}) and (\mathsf{ChkProg}),
deterministically evaluate the checker predicate on the test basis,
confirm drift bounds (\le \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) for the declared invariants,
confirm convention fingerprints required by the checker are present and match.
6.3.1(c) Exact vs bounded equivalence.Exact equivalence is represented by (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}=\mathbf{0}). Bounded equivalence uses explicit quantized tolerances. No proof may be interpreted as exact unless (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}=\mathbf{0}) in the proof object.
6.3.1(d) Equivalence claim boundaries.Equivalence is defined only under the declared invariants and verification level. Any interpretation beyond the claim object’s bound ((\mathcal I,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon},\mathsf{VL})) is illegal.
6.3.2 Trace proofs (e.g., sieve/constraint transcripts)
6.3.2(a) Trace proof schema.A trace proof object (\mathsf{ProofTr}) is:[\mathsf{ProofTr}:=(\mathsf{trace_kind},h(\mathsf{InputSpec}),h(\mathsf{Trace}),h(\mathsf{VerifierRule}),h(\mathsf{Bound})?,h(\mathsf{Evidence})?).]The trace is a transcript of a deterministic procedure (e.g., a constraint satisfaction sequence, a pruning chain, a sieve transcript) that can be verified locally.
6.3.2(b) Transcript compression and local verifiability.Transcripts may be compressed, but the verifier must remain polynomial-time in transcript size. If the transcript is probabilistic, randomness must be pinned (seed hash in source commitments) and the procedure must be reproducible. If probabilistic replay is disallowed by (\Omega), probabilistic traces are illegal.
6.3.2(c) Bound proofs.If a trace claims a bound (e.g., error bound, coverage bound), (h(\mathsf{Bound})) references an explicit bound object containing quantized parameters. Bound semantics must be verifiable from the transcript and pinned policies.
6.3.2(d) Trace usage discipline.Trace proofs are the canonical way to justify operations that would otherwise require unbounded search. Any transform claiming correctness “by search” must instead provide a trace proof object or be rejected.
6.3.3 Rewrite proofs (program equivalence)
6.3.3(a) Rewrite proof schema.A rewrite proof (\mathsf{ProofRw}) certifies equivalence between two programs (or plan segments):[\mathsf{ProofRw}:=(h_{\mathsf{prog}},h_{\mathsf{prog'}} ,h(\mathcal I),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathrm{in}}),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathrm{out}}),h(\mathsf{SpecSet}),\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{TB}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),\mathsf{rule_id},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?).]The spec set binds convention fingerprints that must be preserved or bridged explicitly in the rewritten program.
6.3.3(b) Rewrite legality conditions.A rewrite is legal only if the verifier confirms:
the original and rewritten programs are well-formed and canonically encoded,
convention fingerprints are identical or bridged by explicit bridge steps in (\mathsf{prog'}),
equivalence holds under ((\mathcal I,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathrm{out}})) at verification level (\mathsf{VL}),
the rewrite does not weaken corridor policy requirements.
6.3.3(c) Evidence requirements.Rewrite proofs must include evidence sufficient to identify the matched pattern and the applied rule window (by program indices and hashes) so that replay can confirm the rewrite corresponds to the claimed rule application.
6.3.3(d) Deterministic optimizer compatibility.Optimizer determinism requires that rule sets and tie-break policies be pinned. The rewrite proof binds (\mathsf{rule_id}) and thus binds to a pinned rule object. Any unpinned rewrite logic is illegal.
6.3.4 Dependency proofs (consumption links)
6.3.4(a) Dependency proof schema.A dependency proof (\mathsf{ProofDep}) certifies that a downstream plan consumes artifacts produced by upstream receipts:[\mathsf{ProofDep}:=(h(\mathsf{DownPlan}),[h(\mathsf{UpRcpt}i)]{i=1}^k,[h(\mathsf{PinRec}j)]{j=1}^m,[\mathsf{MerkleProof}{i,j}],r{\mathsf{src}},h_{\Omega},h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}).]Each (\mathsf{MerkleProof}_{i,j}) proves that (\mathsf{PinRec}j) is included in upstream receipt (i)’s pin-root (r{\mathsf{pins}}).
6.3.4(b) Provenance coupling.The dependency proof binds (r_{\mathsf{src}}), requiring that downstream step provenance source commitments include the consumed pin-record hashes. This prevents claiming dependency without actual consumption.
6.3.4(c) Verification procedure.A verifier accepts (\mathsf{ProofDep}) iff:
all referenced upstream receipts are valid under their ledger header commitments or under a trusted receipt verification policy,
each Merkle proof verifies membership of the consumed pin record hash in the upstream pin root,
downstream provenance roots reference those consumed hashes,
corridor and canonicalization policy hashes are compatible (equal or bridged by explicit policy-bridge receipts if the framework permits such bridging).
6.3.4(d) Non-equivocation boundary.Dependency proofs prevent equivocation at the commitment level: a downstream result cannot claim reliance on an upstream artifact without proving membership and provenance consumption. They do not, by themselves, guarantee semantic correctness beyond the upstream receipts’ declared invariant contracts.
6.4 Privacy by commitment
6.4.1 Hash-only disclosure mode
6.4.1(a) Commitment-only representation.In hash-only mode, public disclosure consists only of:
object hashes,
receipt hashes and their pinned policy hashes,
Merkle roots and membership proofs for requested outputs,
method identifiers and verification levels,
local-check data hashes (optionally withheld; see 6.4.2).Raw object bytes and private payloads may remain undisclosed.
6.4.1(b) Verifiability condition.Hash-only mode is verifiable iff all verifier-required objects are either disclosed or their bytes are accessible to the verifier through an authorized channel. When bytes are withheld, verification can still proceed only if the proof system for that method supports verification from disclosed local-check data alone and treats withheld bytes as opaque commitments, which must be explicitly declared by the method’s claim semantics.
6.4.1(c) Public commitment objects.If a hash is disclosed publicly, it must correspond to a well-formed object under the canonical object standard. Publishing a hash without a well-formed type tag and schema is not meaningful within the framework and is treated as an external reference.
6.4.1(d) Scope of claims under hash-only.Hash-only disclosure does not expand claim strength. Any claim remains bounded by the invariants, tolerances, and verification level recorded in proof objects and receipts.
6.4.2 Selective reveal of local-check data
6.4.2(a) Local-check disclosure principle.Selective reveal discloses exactly the minimal data required for verifier execution:
test basis objects,
checker program identifiers and required parameters,
quantization policies,
convention fingerprints,
bound objects (tolerances, caps) used by proofs.
All other payloads may remain committed by hash.
6.4.2(b) Local-check object partition.Each proof method defines a partition of referenced hashes into:
required-to-reveal: must be disclosed for verification,
optional-to-reveal: may be disclosed to increase transparency,
private-by-default: may remain hidden while still allowing verification in the method’s declared model.This partition is pinned as part of the method registry and is itself hash-committed.
6.4.2(c) Deterministic redaction markers.When bytes are withheld, a redaction marker object (\mathsf{Redact}) may be published, containing:
the hash of the hidden object,
its type tag hash,
a disclosure policy id hash.This enables verifiers to confirm the existence and type of hidden objects without revealing contents.
6.4.2(d) Verification boundaries under selective reveal.Selective reveal supports mechanical verification only when the verifier’s required object bytes are available. If required bytes are withheld, the receipt remains a commitment but cannot be verified by a verifier lacking access; this limitation is explicit and must be represented as a verification scope boundary in the receipt metadata.
6.4.3 Redaction policies and verifiability
6.4.3(a) Redaction policy object.A redaction policy (\mathsf{RP}) is a typed object:[\mathsf{RP}:=(\mathsf{allowed_fields},\mathsf{forbidden_fields},\mathsf{hash_substitution_rule},\mathsf{proof_requirements}),]specifying which fields may be revealed, which must remain hidden, how hidden fields are substituted by hashes, and what additional proofs are required to preserve verifiability under redaction.
6.4.3(b) Redaction-preserving canonical encoding.Redacted objects must remain canonically encodable. The substitution rule must produce a unique canonical encoding for the redacted view (e.g., replace hidden sub-objects by their hashes and include a redaction marker). Redaction must not introduce ambiguity in object bytes.
6.4.3(c) Redaction-aware proofs.If an object is redacted, any proof that depends on its hidden components must either:
operate solely on disclosed local-check data and committed hashes (explicitly declared), or
include a redaction-aware proof object whose method verifier can verify claims without direct access to hidden bytes.Absent such a method, verification cannot proceed, and the boundary must be explicit.
6.4.3(d) Auditability of redaction.Redaction is auditable: the receipt must commit to the redaction policy hash and must include the set of redacted object hashes. Thus an auditor can determine exactly what was withheld and under which policy, without requiring narrative explanations.
6.4.4 Threat model and boundaries
6.4.4(a) Adversary classes.The threat model distinguishes adversaries by capability:
Passive observer: reads disclosed hashes, receipts, and local-check data.
Malicious executor: attempts to fabricate ledgers, witnesses, or receipts.
Witness launderer: attempts to reuse valid witnesses in invalid contexts.
Convention smuggler: attempts to silently change conventions without bridges.
Approximation smuggler: attempts to present bounded results as exact.
Replay diverter: attempts to induce nondeterministic replay.
Resource adversary: attempts to exhaust budgets or trigger uncontrolled exploration.
6.4.4(b) Defenses provided by COS commitments.COS provides defenses by construction:
collision-resistant content hashing binds object identity,
canonical encodings bind cross-platform bytes,
provenance binding binds witnesses to step context,
convention fingerprints bind representation choices,
receipts commit to policy hashes and witness roots,
dependency proofs bind upstream consumption.
6.4.4(c) Soundness boundary.Soundness is bounded: acceptance of a proof witness establishes only the method’s predicate under the declared invariants, tolerances, and verification level, with required local-check data. No claim is made beyond those declared bounds. Any stronger interpretation is illegal.
6.4.4(d) Non-goals and explicit limits.The COS does not provide:
confidentiality by itself (hash-only mode is a commitment, not encryption),
liveness guarantees under adversarial resource exhaustion,
global semantic correctness beyond declared invariants and local-check regimes,
protection against hash-function compromise.These boundaries are explicit and must be respected by any system-level interpretation of receipts and proofs.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 6 — CANONICAL OBJECT STANDARD: TYPED HASH-ADDRESSED DATA
CHAPTER 7 — NORMALIZATION AND CONVENTION DISCIPLINE (Final Draft)
This chapter specifies the normalization layer that eliminates silent representational degrees of freedom. The layer comprises (i) convention fingerprints (specification objects hashed and pinned), (ii) canonical normal forms for major object families, (iii) a compatibility protocol that enforces composition safety, and (iv) mandatory normalization insertion points in execution and compilation. Together, these rules prevent semantic drift caused by hidden sign conventions, scaling factors, index permutations, unit systems, parameter domains, and representation-dependent encodings.
7.1 Convention fingerprints
7.1.1 Sign conventions and orientation
7.1.1(a) SignSpec object.A sign convention is represented by a typed specification object[\mathsf{SignSpec} := (\mathsf{domain_tag},\ \mathsf{sign_bits},\ \mathsf{orientation},\ \mathsf{conjugation_policy},\ \mathsf{phase_policy}),]where:
(\mathsf{domain_tag}) identifies the operator family (e.g., transform kernels, differential operators, modular mappings) and binds the semantic context in which “sign” is interpreted,
(\mathsf{sign_bits}) encodes the canonical sign choices (e.g., exponent sign, forward/inverse sign, left/right action sign),
(\mathsf{orientation}) encodes handedness and direction conventions (e.g., clockwise vs counterclockwise, increasing vs decreasing coordinate orientation),
(\mathsf{conjugation_policy}) specifies whether conjugation is implicit (forbidden) or explicit (required), and the canonical form in which it must appear,
(\mathsf{phase_policy}) specifies the representation of phase equivalences, including whether phase is treated as exact or modulo a declared quotient (quotients must be explicit and are never inferred).
The sign fingerprint is[h_{\mathsf{Sign}} := h(\mathsf{SignSpec}),]computed under canonical object encoding (Chapter 6). Sign equivalence is defined solely by equality of sign fingerprints.
7.1.1(b) Orientation invariants.If an operator claims to preserve an orientation-sensitive invariant (e.g., a directed ordering, a handedness-dependent action), then the contract’s invariant set must include an explicit orientation invariant and the operator’s (\mathsf{SignSpec}) must specify the orientation convention under which the invariant is evaluated. An orientation change without a certified bridge is illegal.
7.1.1(c) Conjugation and duality operations.Any operation that replaces an object (x) by (\overline{x}) (complex conjugation), (x^{-1}) (inverse), or an adjoint (x^\ast) must be expressed as an explicit transform step or as an explicit, contract-declared internal substep whose effect is reflected in deltas and whose correctness is certified. No “implicit conjugation by convention” is permitted. If a convention requires conjugation, the convention must be represented as an explicit operator object (or an explicit bridge) and pinned by hash.
7.1.1(d) Sign compatibility predicate.Two consecutive transform contracts (\mathsf{LT}_1,\mathsf{LT}2) are sign-compatible iff[h{\mathsf{Sign}}(\mathsf{LT}1.\mathsf{SpecOut}) = h{\mathsf{Sign}}(\mathsf{LT}_2.\mathsf{SpecIn}),]or there exists a certified bridge transform (\mathsf{LT}b) whose spec mapping satisfies[h{\mathsf{Sign}}(\mathsf{LT}_1.\mathsf{SpecOut}) \xrightarrow{\mathsf{LT}b} h{\mathsf{Sign}}(\mathsf{LT}_2.\mathsf{SpecIn}),]with the mapping committed in the bridge’s contract.
7.1.2 Scaling/normalization factors
7.1.2(a) ScaleSpec object.Scaling and normalization choices are represented by:[\mathsf{ScaleSpec} := (\mathsf{domain_tag},\ \mathsf{scale_mode},\ \mathsf{norm_factors},\ \mathsf{unitarity_tag},\ \mathsf{rounding_policy_hash}),]where:
(\mathsf{scale_mode}) indicates the convention class (e.g., unitary, forward-only scaling, inverse-only scaling, symmetric split),
(\mathsf{norm_factors}) is a canonically encoded map of required scaling constants (each represented as a quantized rational or fixed-point integer under pinned policy),
(\mathsf{unitarity_tag}) declares whether the operator is intended to be norm-preserving and under which invariant definitions,
(\mathsf{rounding_policy_hash}) pins numeric handling (Chapter 1 and 6).
The scale fingerprint is (h_{\mathsf{Scale}}:=h(\mathsf{ScaleSpec})).
7.1.2(b) Scaling invariants and admissible drift.Any transform that is intended to preserve an energy/norm invariant must declare that invariant and must specify the scale normalization convention under which it holds. If scaling differs across adjacent transforms, the discrepancy is not an “implementation detail”; it is a convention mismatch requiring an explicit scale bridge.
7.1.2(c) Exact vs approximate scaling.Scale bridges are classified as:
Exact: scaling factors are representable exactly in the pinned numeric domain (e.g., rationals, exact integers) and the bridge proof claims (\varepsilon=0).
Approximate: scaling factors require bounded approximation; the bridge must declare explicit tolerance consumption per invariant and must be admissible under the corridor tolerance budget.
Exactness is determined by the declared numeric domain and the canonical quantization policy; it is not inferred from floating values.
7.1.2(d) Scale compatibility predicate.Adjacent transforms are scale-compatible iff output and input scale fingerprints match, or a certified scale bridge exists and is inserted. No implicit rescaling is permitted.
7.1.3 Index ordering and centering
7.1.3(a) IndexSpec object.Index ordering and centering conventions are represented by:[\mathsf{IndexSpec} := (\mathsf{domain_tag},\ \mathsf{index_mode},\ \mathsf{origin_rule},\ \mathsf{permutation_rule},\ \mathsf{layout_policy}),]where:
(\mathsf{index_mode}) specifies canonical index sets (e.g., (0..N-1), centered indexing, modular residue domain),
(\mathsf{origin_rule}) pins how the origin is chosen for centered systems,
(\mathsf{permutation_rule}) pins any deterministic permutation required to map between indexings,
(\mathsf{layout_policy}) pins array layout and stride policies (Chapter 6).
The index fingerprint is (h_{\mathsf{Index}}:=h(\mathsf{IndexSpec})).
7.1.3(b) Address algebra coupling.Indexing conventions must be consistent with the lens address algebra (\mathcal{A}). If the address algebra differs, it must be bridged by an explicit address bridge transform (an index permutation transform) whose mapping is pinned by hash and whose correctness is certified.
7.1.3(c) Permutation legality.Any index permutation used to adapt between conventions is a semantic operation. It must be represented explicitly as a transform with:
a deterministic permutation object (or rule id) pinned by hash,
a proof witness that the permutation preserves declared invariants (or an explicit loss profile if it does not),
explicit scope and budget declarations.
7.1.3(d) Index compatibility predicate.Adjacent transforms are index-compatible iff their index fingerprints match, or a certified index bridge exists and is inserted. Any attempt to reorder indices inside an operator without declaring it is an illegal implicit coercion.
7.1.4 Units and parameter domains
7.1.4(a) UnitSpec and DomainSpec objects.Units and parameter domains are represented by two typed specifications:
UnitSpec[\mathsf{UnitSpec}:=(\mathsf{unit_system},\ \mathsf{base_units},\ \mathsf{derived_units},\ \mathsf{conversion_policy}),]where conversions are explicit, typed, and pinned.
DomainSpec[\mathsf{DomainSpec}:=(\mathsf{param_schema_hash},\ \mathsf{constraints},\ \mathsf{boundary_policy},\ \mathsf{branch_policy}?),]where:
(\mathsf{constraints}) is a canonically encoded set of constraints (inequalities, discrete membership, modular constraints) represented by pinned predicate objects,
(\mathsf{boundary_policy}) pins how boundary cases are handled,
(\mathsf{branch_policy}) is included when multi-valued functions are involved, and must be explicit.
The fingerprints are (h_{\mathsf{Unit}}:=h(\mathsf{UnitSpec})) and (h_{\mathsf{Domain}}:=h(\mathsf{DomainSpec})).
7.1.4(b) Unit conversions are transforms.Any unit conversion is a bridge transform. It must declare:
input and output UnitSpec hashes,
a deterministic conversion rule object pinned by hash,
proof obligations for invariants affected by unit conversion,
explicit tolerance consumption if conversion is approximate (e.g., due to quantization).
7.1.4(c) Parameter-domain guarding.A transform may only be executed with parameters in its declared DomainSpec. If parameters are out of domain, the step must fail deterministically with a typed error object. Domain relaxation is not permitted by default; it must be expressed as a different transform contract or as a certified bridge that maps from a larger domain to the required domain.
7.1.4(d) Domain compatibility predicate.Two transforms are domain-compatible for composition only if the intermediate parameter-domain semantics are consistent; otherwise a domain bridge is required. If the composition would require crossing a forbidden boundary (per DomainSpec), the route is inadmissible under the compatibility protocol.
7.2 Canonical normal forms for objects
7.2.1 Normal forms for arrays/signals
7.2.1(a) Array normal form operator.For each array type (Chapter 6), define a deterministic normal form operator[\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{Arr}}:\ \mathsf{Arr}\to \mathsf{Arr}]satisfying:
Idempotence: (\mathsf{NF}{\mathsf{Arr}}(\mathsf{NF}{\mathsf{Arr}}(x))=\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{Arr}}(x)).
Determinism: identical input bytes yield identical output bytes.
Canonical layout: array bytes are serialized into the canonical layout policy of the relevant IndexSpec and layout policy.
Numeric normalization: apply pinned float/quantization normalization (e.g., canonical NaN handling, signed-zero policy, quantized rounding) before hashing.
7.2.1(b) Signal normal form operator.Define:[\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{Sig}}:\ \mathsf{Sig}\to \mathsf{Sig}]which enforces:
canonical grid encoding (GridSpec normalization),
canonical sample array normal form,
canonical convention spec binding (SignSpec/ScaleSpec/IndexSpec/UnitSpec/DomainSpec hashes),
canonical tag normalization for set-like tags.
Signals are in canonical form iff applying (\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{Sig}}) yields identical bytes.
7.2.1(c) Address-consistency normalization.If a signal’s lens address algebra implies a specific ordering, then the sample array must be reordered to match canonical address order. This reordering is an explicit normalization action; it is either:
part of (\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{Sig}}) when the lens explicitly declares that canonicalization includes reindexing, or
performed by an explicit index bridge transform when reindexing is not declared as a pure normalization.
Which case applies must be pinned by the lens and by the normalization policy.
7.2.1(d) Normalization correctness contract.Normalization operators must declare:
the invariant subset they preserve exactly,
any tolerances they consume,
whether they are permitted to change representations while preserving invariant meaning.Normalization that changes meaning outside its declared invariants is illegal.
7.2.2 Normal forms for operators
7.2.2(a) Operator normal form.Define:[\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{Op}}:\ \mathsf{Op}\to \mathsf{Op}]that enforces:
canonical ordering of parameter schema fields,
canonical spec binding: operator input and output convention fingerprints must be present and must reference pinned spec objects by hash,
canonical implementation reference: program hashes and rule ids must be canonical and version-pinned,
canonical proof reference: proof hash must reference a proof object whose claim binds the operator’s declared invariants and tolerances.
7.2.2(b) Eliminating implicit defaults.Any operator that omits required spec fields is ill-formed; if defaults exist, they must be represented as explicit pinned objects referenced by hash. (\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{Op}}) must not insert defaults from ambient configuration; it may only replace explicit default references by their canonical forms.
7.2.2(c) Canonicalization of convention blocks.Convention specs embedded in operators (or referenced by hash) must themselves be in normal form under:[\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{Spec}}:\ \mathsf{Spec}\to \mathsf{Spec},]which normalizes internal map ordering, numeric representations, and constraint encodings. Operators are not canonical unless their referenced specs are canonical.
7.2.2(d) Operator equivalence classes.Two operators are considered exactly identical iff their canonical bytes are equal. Any weaker equivalence must be represented by an explicit equivalence proof object (Chapter 6) binding the operator hashes and the invariant/tolerance profile. Operator equivalence is never inferred from textual descriptions.
7.2.3 Normal forms for sets/distributions
7.2.3(a) Set normal form.For set-like objects, (\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{Set}}) sorts elements by canonical element bytes and removes duplicates (duplicates render the object ill-formed unless the schema explicitly defines multiset semantics). Sorting order and duplication criteria are pinned by schema.
7.2.3(b) Distribution normal form.For discrete distributions:[\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{Dist}}:\ \mathsf{Dist}\to \mathsf{Dist}]enforces:
canonical ordering of support elements (by canonical bytes),
aligned canonical ordering of probability vectors,
canonical normalization proof references,
canonical quantization policy binding.
If the distribution includes a normalization proof, the proof must verify under the pinned method and local-check data; otherwise the distribution is ill-formed for any use that assumes normalization.
7.2.3(c) Candidate normal form.Candidate sets must be in canonical order under the pinned comparator; ties must be broken deterministically. Candidate equivalences (if any) must be represented explicitly by equivalence proof objects; absent such proofs, candidates are distinct by hash.
7.2.3(d) Probability mass and tolerance.If probabilities are represented with tolerances (approximate normalization), the tolerance profile must be explicit and pinned; the normal form must include evidence of the tolerance-bound check and must bind to the relevant quantization policy.
7.2.4 Normal forms for proofs and plans
7.2.4(a) Proof normal form.For each proof object family, define a normal form operator (\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{Proof}}) that canonicalizes:
ordered lists of referenced hashes (sorted where schema declares set-like),
local-check data references (in canonical order),
claim object binding ((h(\mathcal I),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}),\mathsf{VL})),
method id and checker program id hashes.
A proof object is canonical iff (\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{Proof}}(p)\equiv p).
7.2.4(b) Plan normal form.A plan object is canonical iff:
route lists are in canonical order as produced by deterministic routing,
program encodings are in canonical program normal form (defined by the program encoding rules in the execution kernel and macro system),
witness plans and ledger plans are canonically encoded (sorted by step index, witness type),
all referenced policy hashes match the runtime header.
7.2.4(c) Receipt normal form.Receipts are canonical iff:
they bind the plan hash and ledger header hash,
Merkle roots are computed using the pinned Merkle policy and canonical ordering,
the witness root commits to the ordered witness set.
7.2.4(d) Normal form as equality predicate.Exact equality for proofs, plans, and receipts is definitional equality of canonical bytes. Any equivalence between two non-identical plans must be certified by a rewrite proof object that binds program hashes, invariant/tolerance profiles, and convention fingerprints.
7.3 Compatibility protocol
7.3.1 Match required convention hashes
7.3.1(a) Convention bundle.Each transform contract and operator must declare an input convention bundle and output convention bundle:[\mathsf{SpecIn} := (h_{\mathsf{Sign}},h_{\mathsf{Scale}},h_{\mathsf{Index}},h_{\mathsf{Unit}},h_{\mathsf{Domain}}),\quad\mathsf{SpecOut} := (h'{\mathsf{Sign}},h'{\mathsf{Scale}},h'{\mathsf{Index}},h'{\mathsf{Unit}},h'_{\mathsf{Domain}}),]with omissions forbidden unless the domain tag declares a vacuous convention component (which must be explicit and pinned).
7.3.1(b) Compatibility predicate.Two consecutive transforms (\mathsf{LT}_1,\mathsf{LT}_2) are compatible iff[\mathsf{SpecOut}(\mathsf{LT}_1)=\mathsf{SpecIn}(\mathsf{LT}_2)]componentwise. Compatibility is a hard requirement for composition unless bridged.
7.3.1(c) Cross-component coupling.Some convention components are coupled (e.g., index ordering affects scaling factors for certain transforms; units affect parameter domains). Any coupled constraints must be represented explicitly as part of DomainSpec constraints and verified as preconditions in the transform contract. The compatibility protocol treats coupled constraints as part of Spec matching and does not permit local “fixups.”
7.3.1(d) Compatibility in routing graphs.Compilation must treat each transform edge as annotated by (\mathsf{SpecIn}) and (\mathsf{SpecOut}). A route is admissible only if every consecutive pair satisfies compatibility or is separated by a certified bridge chain whose net effect maps the outgoing spec to the incoming spec.
7.3.2 Insert certified bridges on mismatch
7.3.2(a) Bridge transform definition.A bridge is a legal transform (\mathsf{LT}_b) whose primary purpose is convention alignment. It must declare:
(\mathsf{SpecIn}(\mathsf{LT}_b)) and (\mathsf{SpecOut}(\mathsf{LT}_b)),
an explicit mapping statement between convention fingerprints (e.g., sign flip, scale rescale, index permutation, unit conversion, domain restriction),
a proof obligation (equivalence or correctness) under a declared invariant subset and tolerance profile.
7.3.2(b) Minimal bridge set and closure.The library of bridges must include at minimum:
sign bridge(s),
scale bridge(s),
index/permutation bridge(s),
unit conversion bridge(s),
domain restriction bridge(s) (when allowed),
grid/discretization bridge(s) (if discretization is a first-class convention in the domain).
Each bridge must be admissible under the corridor policy and must have a verified proof object establishing its correctness under declared invariants.
7.3.2(c) Bridge admissibility conditions.A bridge may be inserted only if:
its SpecIn matches the current SpecOut at the insertion point,
its SpecOut moves closer to the target SpecIn under deterministic route selection,
its budget and scope are feasible under (\Omega),
its proof witness verifies at the required verification level.
7.3.2(d) Bridge insertion as compilation output.Bridge insertion is not an execution-time improvisation. It is a compilation-time decision committed in the plan object. If a mismatch is discovered at execution time (due to incorrect plan or corrupted state), the step must fail with convention drift evidence; repair is performed by compiling a new plan segment that explicitly inserts the required bridge transforms.
7.3.3 Forbid implicit coercions
7.3.3(a) Coercion classes (forbidden unless explicit).The following coercions are forbidden unless represented as explicit transforms (or explicit, contract-declared normalization steps consistent with this chapter):
sign convention changes,
scaling or normalization adjustments,
index reordering, centering shifts, or permutation,
unit conversions,
parameter domain relaxation or branch selection changes,
dtype promotions or precision changes that affect canonical bytes,
layout or stride changes that affect canonical bytes.
7.3.3(b) Coercion detection.A coercion attempt is detected by mismatch of convention fingerprints at boundaries. Additionally, coercion attempts may be detected by:
a change in object normal forms without an explicit normalization step recorded in the plan,
or a change in spec fingerprints in an operator object without an accompanying bridge.
Coercion detection produces typed evidence objects bound to step context.
7.3.3(c) Coercion illegality.If a transform produces an output whose SpecOut differs from the declared SpecOut of its contract, the step is illegal (determinism violation). If a transform consumes an input whose SpecIn does not match its declared SpecIn and no bridge exists in the executed plan, the step is illegal (convention mismatch). In neither case may the system “adapt.”
7.3.3(d) Normalization vs coercion boundary.Normalization is permitted only for operations declared as canonicalization within the same convention fingerprint class, i.e., normalization must not change (\mathsf{Spec}) hashes. Any operation that changes a component of the convention bundle is a bridge (a transport) and must be represented as such.
7.3.4 Deterministic tie-break policies
7.3.4(a) Route selection determinism.When multiple legal bridge sequences exist, compilation must choose deterministically using a pinned cost functional:[\mathrm{Cost}(\text{route})=\alpha\cdot(#\text{edges})+\beta\cdot(\text{budget cost})+\gamma\cdot(\text{tolerance cost})+\delta\cdot(\text{witness burden}),]with coefficients pinned by the routing policy. Exactness is prioritized by setting (\gamma) sufficiently large or by using lexicographic precedence where (\varepsilon=0) routes are always preferred.
7.3.4(b) Tie-break rule.If costs are equal, ties are broken lexicographically by the sequence of transform identifiers (bytes) and parameter hashes. No randomized tie-break is permitted.
7.3.4(c) Deterministic bridge minimality.A plan that inserts non-minimal bridges (when a cheaper legal bridge path exists) is not illegal, but it is not canonical. Canonical plans are those produced by deterministic minimality rules. Any claim that an output is “the canonical result” must bind to a canonical plan hash.
7.3.4(d) Stability of canonical planning.Canonical route selection depends only on pinned objects: transform library hashes, bridge library hashes, corridor policy hash, and routing policy hash. Therefore, identical environments produce identical canonical plans and identical plan hashes.
7.4 Normalization insertion points
7.4.1 Before commitments
7.4.1(a) Commitment prerequisite.Any object referenced by a commitment pin must be in canonical normal form for its type family. Commitment insertion requires that the committed value hash refer to canonical bytes; otherwise a normalization step must occur before the commitment step.
7.4.1(b) Canonicalization witness requirements.If normalization produces a new object, it must be stored in the proof store and referenced by hash. The commitment witness must bind to the normalized object hash. If a commitment step attempts to pin a non-canonical object, the projector must reject the step (scope/determinism violation).
7.4.1(c) Contradiction-controlled namespaces.For contradiction-controlled commitments, the contradiction predicate is evaluated on canonical pin record encodings. Therefore non-canonical encodings can create apparent contradictions or hide real ones; the corridor forbids committing non-canonical bytes.
7.4.1(d) Canonical alias discipline.Alias records (if permitted) must be canonical objects and must bind target pin id and target value hash. Normalization does not resolve aliases; alias resolution is explicit and must be deterministic.
7.4.2 After bridges
7.4.2(a) Bridge postcondition.A bridge transform must be followed by a normalization checkpoint that ensures:
the output object is in normal form for the target convention bundle,
any induced permutations or scalings are reflected in canonical bytes,
any tolerance consumption is recorded and evidenced as required by (\Omega).
7.4.2(b) Mandatory normalization step.If a bridge changes IndexSpec or ScaleSpec, normalization must immediately enforce the canonical array layout and numeric representations consistent with the new specs. Deferring normalization is prohibited if it would allow subsequent transforms to read non-canonical intermediate objects under a new convention bundle.
7.4.2(c) Proof binding across bridge boundaries.Any proof witness that claims equivalence across a bridge boundary must bind both the pre-bridge and post-bridge convention fingerprints. Otherwise, equivalence claims are ill-typed and invalid.
7.4.2(d) Bridge evidence.Bridge steps must emit evidence objects indicating:
source spec hash,
target spec hash,
bridge transform id,
proof hash used to justify the mapping.These evidence objects are included in step records and are replay-verifiable.
7.4.3 At checkpoints and long chains
7.4.3(a) Checkpoint normal form invariant.Every checkpoint state must satisfy: all stored commitments and all referenced proof objects are in canonical normal form. This is a global invariant enforced by projection. A checkpoint that violates normal-form invariants cannot be produced by legal execution.
7.4.3(b) Long-chain drift control.For chains exceeding a corridor-defined length threshold (L_{\mathsf{norm}}), the plan must insert normalization checkpoints at deterministic intervals. The interval policy is pinned by (\Omega) and may depend on:
cumulative tolerance usage per invariant,
cumulative candidate churn,
cumulative proof-store growth.
7.4.3(c) Canonical recompression.Where applicable (e.g., proof-store deduplication, canonical linking normalization), long-chain normalization may include deterministic recompression steps provided they do not change committed hashes without explicit supersession rules. Recompression that changes object hashes is a transform and requires explicit commitments and proofs.
7.4.3(d) Holonomy and closed-loop normalization.If a route forms a closed loop (start and end lens identical), the plan must include a loop-consistency check as an explicit proof step when the route claims exact preservation of certain invariants. Loop checks are normal-form sensitive; the route must normalize at declared loop boundaries to prevent spurious drift due to representational artifacts.
7.4.4 Under near-violation detectors
7.4.4(a) Near-threshold triggers.When corridor policy detects near-violation of:
tolerance budgets,
branch caps,
write budgets for protected targets,
determinism risk thresholds (e.g., floating usage near non-canonical regions),the policy mandates deterministic mitigation actions. One permitted mitigation is insertion of a normalization step that reduces representational drift or reprojects to canonical form.
7.4.4(b) Mandatory mitigation as explicit steps.Mitigation is not advisory. If (\Omega) marks a state as near under a required detector, the next legal plan segment must include a mandated normalization or reroute step identified by policy id. Execution that ignores mandated mitigation is illegal if the corridor classifies the detector as hard.
7.4.4(c) Normalization as tolerance management.Normalization steps may reduce cumulative tolerance usage by reprojecting to a canonical representative, but any such reduction must be justified by explicit proof obligations defined by the normalization transform’s contract. Tolerance “reset” is illegal unless certified.
7.4.4(d) Evidence and audit.Near-violation events and the resulting normalization actions must be linked by evidence objects: the near-evidence hash must be included in the normalization step’s source root, and the normalization step must emit evidence indicating which near-condition it mitigated and under which policy id. This creates an auditable chain from detector trigger to mitigation action.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 7 — NORMALIZATION AND CONVENTION DISCIPLINE
CHAPTER 8 — TRANSFORM ALGEBRA: COMPOSITION, INVERSES, QUOTIENTS (Final Draft)
This chapter defines the algebraic structure induced by legal transforms under the execution kernel and corridor policy: how transforms compose, when inverses exist, how lossy steps are represented as quotient-preserving morphisms, and how program equivalence yields canonical “laws” as equivalence classes of certified programs. The purpose is to make mathematical derivation identical to composition in a rigorously typed algebra of certified transport objects, with replay-deterministic semantics and explicit correctness boundaries.
8.1 Composition laws
8.1.1 Contract intersection for invariants
8.1.1(a) Composable transforms.Let (\mathsf{LT}_1=(L_0,L_1,\widetilde{T}_1,\mathcal{I}_1,\mathcal{B}_1,\mathcal{S}_1,\mathcal{W}_1,\mathcal{R}_1)) and(\mathsf{LT}_2=(L_1',L_2,\widetilde{T}_2,\mathcal{I}_2,\mathcal{B}_2,\mathcal{S}_2,\mathcal{W}_2,\mathcal{R}_2)) be legal transform contracts. They are composable only if their boundary lens compatibility holds:
(h(L_1)=h(L_1')), or
there exists an explicit certified bridge chain (Chapter 7) mapping (L_1) to (L_1') that is inserted as part of the composed route.
No implicit lens coercion is permitted.
8.1.1(b) Carrier-map composition.If composable, the composed carrier map is:[\widetilde{T}_{21} := \widetilde{T}_2\circ \widetilde{T}_1.]The composition is interpreted operationally via the execution kernel: a composed transform is not merely a formal function; it is a sequence of executable steps whose legality is verified.
8.1.1(c) Invariant contract under composition.The invariant contract of the composition is:[\mathcal{I}_{21} := \mathcal{I}_1 \cap \mathcal{I}2,]with intersection defined by invariant identifiers. For each invariant (I\in\mathcal{I}{21}), the composed tolerance profile is computed by the pinned accumulation operator (\oplus_I) (Chapter 3):[\varepsilon^{(21)}_I := \varepsilon^{(1)}_I \oplus_I \varepsilon^{(2)}I.]Any attempt to claim preservation of an invariant not in (\mathcal{I}{21}) is illegal.
8.1.1(d) Applicability and domain restrictions.If either transform declares applicability predicates for certain invariants or declares domain restrictions for correctness, the composed transform inherits the strongest restrictions: an invariant clause in (\mathcal{I}_{21}) is applicable only on the intersection of applicability sets. Likewise, if either transform is partial, the composition is partial on the intersection domain where both are defined.
8.1.2 Budget composition and cap propagation
8.1.2(a) Budget algebra.Each transform carries a budget profile:[\mathcal{B}k=(b{\mathsf{comp},k},,b_{\mathsf{write},k},,b_{\mathsf{branch},k},,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\max}k).]Define a deterministic budget composition operator (\oplus{\mathsf{B}}) pinned by policy:[\mathcal{B}_{21} := \mathcal{B}2 \oplus{\mathsf{B}} \mathcal{B}_1,]with the following canonical component rules:
Compute budgets add:[b_{\mathsf{comp},21} := b_{\mathsf{comp},2} + b_{\mathsf{comp},1}.]
Write budgets add per target:[b_{\mathsf{write},21}(t) := b_{\mathsf{write},2}(t) + b_{\mathsf{write},1}(t),\quad \forall t\in\mathsf{Target}.]
Branch caps propagate by minimum (to preserve feasibility under corridor caps):[b_{\mathsf{branch},21} := \min(b_{\mathsf{branch},2},b_{\mathsf{branch},1}),]unless the composition explicitly inserts a budget escalation witness whose legality is verified under (\Omega).
Tolerance maxima compose under the same per-invariant accumulation as tolerances:[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\max}{21}:=\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\max}{1}\oplus \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\max}_{2}.]
8.1.2(b) Corridor feasibility as a composition constraint.A composed route is admissible under corridor policy (\Omega) only if the composed budget is feasible against corridor budgets (Chapter 3). If the composed budget exceeds corridor bounds, the route is inadmissible unless it includes admissible budget escalation steps.
8.1.2(c) Scope composition.Scope profiles compose by conservative intersection: the composed transform may only perform writes permitted by both components unless a separate scope-extension step is inserted and certified:[\mathcal{S}_{21} := \mathcal{S}_1 \cap \mathcal{S}_2.]Any scope expansion must be explicit and witness-gated under (\Omega).
8.1.2(d) Cap propagation and partial acceptance.If candidate churn is involved, truncation and partial acceptance rules are those of (\Omega) applied at each step boundary. Caps are enforced locally; composition cannot “average out” cap violations. Any per-step cap violation without admissible justification yields reject+revert regardless of downstream feasibility.
8.1.3 Witness union with ordering constraints
8.1.3(a) Witness profiles under composition.Each transform declares a witness profile (\mathcal{W}k) consisting of required witness types and verifier method ids. For a composed transform, the required witness profile is the union:[\mathcal{W}{21} := \mathcal{W}1 \cup \mathcal{W}2 \cup \mathcal{W}{\mathrm{bridge}},]where (\mathcal{W}{\mathrm{bridge}}) includes any witnesses required by inserted bridges, normalizers, or repair steps.
8.1.3(b) Witness ordering and determinism.Witness lists are ordered canonically by (witness type id, witness hash). Composition requires ordering stability: the concatenation of step witness lists must preserve canonical ordering within each step and must produce deterministic witness roots for receipts. Witness order is not a presentation detail; it is part of replay identity.
8.1.3(c) Binding separation across steps.Even though the composed transform is conceptualized as one morphism, operationally it is a sequence of steps. Witnesses are step-local; binding (Chapter 5) requires that witnesses produced or used in step (t) bind to that step’s context. Therefore (\mathcal{W}_{21}) is enforced as a per-step requirement set, not a global pool.
8.1.3(d) Bridge witnesses as boundary witnesses.If composition relies on a bridge between convention fingerprints or lens boundaries, the bridge step’s proof witness must bind both the outgoing spec of (\mathsf{LT}_1) and the incoming spec of (\mathsf{LT}_2). Without this binding, the composition boundary is ill-typed and illegal.
8.1.4 Replay associativity requirements
8.1.4(a) Replay associativity definition.Replay associativity is the requirement that any two parenthesizations of a composed transform that denote the same carrier map yield identical execution commitments under the same pinned canonicalization, verifier set, and corridor policy. Formally, let[\mathsf{LT}_{(32)1} := (\mathsf{LT}_3\circ \mathsf{LT}_2)\circ \mathsf{LT}1,\quad\mathsf{LT}{3(21)} := \mathsf{LT}_3\circ (\mathsf{LT}2\circ \mathsf{LT}1),]with identical underlying carrier map. Replay associativity requires that for all admissible inputs and identical parameters, the post-state hash and receipt commitments are identical:[h{\mathsf{post}}^{(32)1}=h{\mathsf{post}}^{3(21)}\quad \text{and}\quad\mathsf{Rcpt}^{(32)1}\equiv \mathsf{Rcpt}^{3(21)}.]
8.1.4(b) Conditions for replay associativity.Replay associativity holds when:
canonical parameter hashing rules are identical across compositions,
bridge and normalization insertion points are determined solely by pinned rules and thus coincide,
delta ordering and witness ordering policies are identical and pinned,
per-step cost accounting and feasibility evaluation are deterministic and identical.
Any deviation requires representing the two parenthesizations as distinct plans, not as equal.
8.1.4(c) Associativity as a library admission constraint.Any transform library that claims closure under composition must restrict admission to transforms whose replay profile (\mathcal{R}) is compatible with the library’s canonicalization and compatibility rules. Otherwise, the library must require explicit bridges that restore replay associativity at boundaries.
8.1.4(d) Associativity evidence.If a system claims replay associativity for a composed macro, it must be able to produce a rewrite proof (Section 8.4.3) certifying equivalence of the two parenthesized programs under the declared invariants and convention fingerprints. Associativity is therefore not assumed; it is either enforced structurally by canonicalization or certified by proofs.
8.2 Invertibility and groupoid structure
8.2.1 Exact inverses and certificates
8.2.1(a) Invertible legal transform.A legal transform (\mathsf{LT}:L_{\mathrm{in}}\Rightarrow L_{\mathrm{out}}) is exactly invertible iff there exists a legal transform (\mathsf{LT}^{-1}:L_{\mathrm{out}}\Rightarrow L_{\mathrm{in}}) such that:[\mathsf{LT}^{-1}\circ \mathsf{LT} \equiv \mathsf{id}{L{\mathrm{in}}},\quad\mathsf{LT}\circ \mathsf{LT}^{-1} \equiv \mathsf{id}{L{\mathrm{out}}},]where identity transforms are legal transforms with empty deltas and zero cost (subject to (\Omega)).
8.2.1(b) Identity transform contract.For each lens (L), define the identity transform:[\mathsf{id}_L := (L,L,\mathrm{id}_D,\mathcal{I}_L,\mathcal{B}_0,\mathcal{S}_0,\mathcal{W}_0,\mathcal{R}_0),]where (\mathcal{B}_0) and (\mathcal{W}_0) are minimal and (\mathcal{R}_0) fixes canonical hashing and parameterization. Identity is legal under any corridor with nonnegative budgets.
8.2.1(c) Inverse certificates.Exact invertibility must be supported by proof objects certifying:
left inverse property,
right inverse property,
and convention compatibility at both boundaries.These certificates must be locally verifiable under the witness system; otherwise invertibility is not admitted as a property.
8.2.1(d) Invertible sub-algebra (groupoid).Let (\mathcal{L}) be the set of lenses and let (\mathcal{G}\subseteq \mathcal{L}\times\mathcal{L}) be the set of invertible legal transforms. Under composition, identities, and inverses, ((\mathcal{L},\mathcal{G})) forms a groupoid provided replay associativity holds (Section 8.1.4). In general, the framework yields a partial groupoid: only some transforms are invertible.
8.2.2 Approximate inverses with bounds
8.2.2(a) Approximate inverse definition.A legal transform (\mathsf{LT}) has an approximate inverse (\mathsf{LT}^{-1}) under invariant set (\mathcal{I}) and tolerance profile (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) iff:[\mathsf{LT}^{-1}\circ \mathsf{LT} \sim_{\mathcal{I},\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}} \mathsf{id}{L{\mathrm{in}}},\quad\mathsf{LT}\circ \mathsf{LT}^{-1} \sim_{\mathcal{I},\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}} \mathsf{id}{L{\mathrm{out}}}.]The equivalence relation must be explicit and certified by proof witnesses at a declared verification level.
8.2.2(b) Bound objects and accumulation.Approximate inverse claims must include explicit bound objects:
per-invariant drift bounds,
per-composition accumulation rules,
and any assumptions (e.g., bounded input norms) required for the bound to hold.Bounds are part of the claim object hash and thus part of replay commitments.
8.2.2(c) Feasibility under corridor tolerance budgets.Approximate inverses consume tolerance budgets in chains. Any use of an approximate inverse is legal only if cumulative tolerance remains within corridor budgets and near-threshold mitigation policies are obeyed.
8.2.2(d) Routing consequences.The routing compiler must prefer exact inverses when exactness is required. If only approximate inverses exist, the compiler must surface this as a constraint: it can route only if the goal permits bounded drift and the corridor tolerance budgets permit the inverse chain.
8.2.3 Partial invertibility on subspaces
8.2.3(a) Subspace restriction.A transform may be invertible only on a restricted domain (D^\star\subseteq D_{\mathrm{in}}) or on a restricted subspace defined by a predicate (\mathsf{Dom}^\star(p)). Such restriction must be explicit, pinned, and included in the transform contract.
8.2.3(b) Subspace projector.If invertibility holds only after projecting into a representable subspace, the projector must be an explicit transform (P) with its own contract, invariants, and proofs. The restricted inverse claim is then:[\mathsf{LT}^{-1}\circ \mathsf{LT}\circ P \equiv P \quad \text{(exact or bounded)}.]Implicit projection is illegal.
8.2.3(c) Witness obligations.Partial invertibility claims must include:
a proof that inputs satisfy the restriction predicate (or a proof that the projector enforces it),
a proof that the inverse reconstructs within the declared invariants,
and evidence of convention compatibility for the restricted domain.
8.2.3(d) Routing consequences.The compiler may use partial inverses only when it can prove (by pinned predicates and proofs) that the computation stays within the invertible subspace. Otherwise, the route is inadmissible.
8.2.4 Algebraic consequences for routing
8.2.4(a) Invertible routes and reversible compilation.If a goal requires reversible transport (e.g., transform to a representation, compute, then return without loss), the compiler must restrict to invertible transforms and certified bridges that preserve invertibility. This is a groupoid routing problem: find a path in the invertible subgraph.
8.2.4(b) Witness burden and route cost.Invertible paths may have lower tolerance consumption but higher witness burden if they require additional convention bridges or normalizers. Route cost functions must penalize witness burden and tolerance usage explicitly; the chosen path is the lexicographically earliest minimizer under pinned weights.
8.2.4(c) Identity and loop checks.For invertible routes, loop checks become meaningful: a route that claims exactness must have zero loop defect under declared invariants. The framework enforces this either structurally (via canonicalization) or by requiring explicit loop-equivalence proofs.
8.2.4(d) Non-invertible hubs and quotient routing.If a route requires a non-invertible step (lossy compression, quotienting, projection), the compiler must route in the quotient category (Section 8.3) and must forbid any subsequent requirement for exact reconstruction unless an explicit recovery transform exists and is certified.
8.3 Lossy transforms and quotient contracts
8.3.1 Declared equivalence relations
8.3.1(a) Equivalence relation object.A lossy transform must declare a quotient equivalence relation (\approx) as a typed object:[\mathsf{EqRel}:=(\mathsf{carrier_tag},\ \mathsf{rule_id},\ h(\mathsf{params}),\ h(\mathsf{chkProg}),\ h(\mathsf{TB}),\ \mathsf{VL}),]where (\mathsf{rule_id}) and (\mathsf{chkProg}) identify a deterministic checker program that decides whether two elements are equivalent under the declared relation, at the declared verification level.
8.3.1(b) Correctness boundary.The equivalence relation must be explicit and cannot be inferred from informal statements such as “phase doesn’t matter” or “ordering irrelevant.” If a transform discards information, it must name what is identified by the quotient relation and must bind it by hash.
8.3.1(c) Equivalence relation admissibility.For an equivalence relation to be admissible:
its checker must be deterministic and polynomial-time in local-check data,
the relation must be reflexive and symmetric on the declared test regime; if transitivity is not globally verified, the claim must be restricted to a verification level whose semantics do not over-claim transitivity.
8.3.1(d) Interaction with invariants.Any invariant claimed to be preserved by a lossy transform must be invariant under the declared equivalence relation. This must be enforced by proof obligations: the transform must certify that invariant evaluations are equal (or tolerance-bounded) on equivalence classes.
8.3.2 Quotient maps and preserved observables
8.3.2(a) Quotient map object.A lossy transform contract includes a quotient map (q) as a typed object:[\mathsf{QuotMap}:=(\mathsf{from_type},\mathsf{to_type},\mathsf{rule_id},h(\mathsf{params}),h(\mathsf{impl})),]where (\mathsf{impl}) references a deterministic implementation for mapping a representative element to a quotient representative.
8.3.2(b) Preserved observables set.The transform must declare a preserved observables subset (\mathsf{ObsKeep}) as a typed set object. Meaning preservation is interpreted only through observables in (\mathsf{ObsKeep}). Any observable outside this set may change arbitrarily under the transform.
8.3.2(c) Quotient preservation predicate.For a lossy transform (\mathsf{LT}) with quotient map (q), meaning preservation is:[q(\llbracket p\rrbracket_{L_{\mathrm{in}}}) \sim_{\mathcal{I}{\mathsf{LT}},\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}} q(\llbracket \widetilde{T}(p)\rrbracket{L_{\mathrm{out}}}),]where (\mathcal{I}_{\mathsf{LT}}) includes invariants defined only on (\mathsf{ObsKeep}). This predicate must be checked by proof witnesses as required by the contract.
8.3.2(d) Canonical quotient representatives.If a quotient map admits multiple representatives, the contract must define a canonical representative selection rule, pinned by hash. Without canonical representatives, replay determinism fails; thus canonical selection is mandatory for quotient maps used in committed state transitions.
8.3.3 Composition of quotient-bearing steps
8.3.3(a) Quotient composition law.Let (\mathsf{LT}1) and (\mathsf{LT}2) be quotient-bearing transforms with quotient maps (q_1,q_2). The composed quotient map is:[q{21} := q_2\circ q_1,]and the composed equivalence relation is the coarsest relation consistent with both:[\approx{21} := \mathrm{closure}(\approx_1 \cup \approx_2)]subject to the checker programs and claim strength. If closure properties cannot be certified at the declared verification level, then the composition must explicitly downgrade claim strength or restrict applicability.
8.3.3(b) Preserved observables under composition.The preserved observable set composes by intersection:[\mathsf{ObsKeep}_{21} := \mathsf{ObsKeep}_1 \cap \mathsf{ObsKeep}2.]Any attempt to claim preservation of an observable not in (\mathsf{ObsKeep}{21}) is illegal.
8.3.3(c) Compatibility with bridges and normalization.Convention bridges may be required between quotient steps. Bridges must respect quotient semantics: they must preserve equivalence classes or explicitly refine/coarsen the quotient relation under certified proofs. A bridge that changes quotient semantics without declaring it is illegal.
8.3.3(d) Routing in the quotient category.When quotient steps exist, routing is performed in a quotient-enriched category: morphisms carry equivalence relations and quotient maps. A route is admissible only if the goal’s required invariants are preserved in the quotient sense and if the corridor policy permits the associated information loss.
8.3.4 Honesty constraints (no invertibility claims)
8.3.4(a) No invertibility without explicit recovery.A quotient-bearing transform is not invertible in general. The system forbids claiming invertibility unless the contract provides an explicit recovery transform (\mathsf{Rec}) and explicit conditions under which recovery holds (e.g., restricted domain, side information stored as commitments, or additional invariants). Recovery claims must be certified.
8.3.4(b) No hidden exactness.It is illegal to label a lossy transform as exact by setting (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}=\mathbf{0}) unless the contract explicitly states that the transform is exact on the quotient and that all preserved invariants are evaluated in the quotient space. Exactness must match the declared semantic domain.
8.3.4(c) Loss profile binding.Every lossy transform must declare a loss profile object binding:
dropped invariants,
dropped observables,
quotient relation hash,
quotient map hash,
and the verification level at which the quotient correctness is established.This loss profile hash must be included in proof claims and receipts whenever the transform contributes to a result.
8.3.4(d) Downstream constraint propagation.Downstream transforms and goals must respect loss: any goal requiring reconstruction of dropped observables is inadmissible if the route contains a lossy step that drops them and no recovery path exists. The compiler must reject such routes deterministically.
8.4 Program equivalence and normal forms
8.4.1 Program encoding and hashing
8.4.1(a) Program as a typed object.A program is a finite sequence of step descriptors:[\Pi := [(\mathsf{op_id}1,h{P_1}),\ldots,(\mathsf{op_id}n,h{P_n})],]encoded as a sequence-like COS object under canonical encoding (Chapter 6). Each (\mathsf{op_id}k) and (h{P_k}) is a fixed-length encoding. The program hash is:[h(\Pi):=H(\mathsf{Enc}(\Pi)).]
8.4.1(b) Program semantics.The meaning of (\Pi) is defined by executing the kernel step function sequentially, subject to (\Omega), starting from a specified pre-state checkpoint:[\mathrm{Exec}(\Pi,\Psi_0):=\Psi_n.]Execution is deterministic and replay-verifiable when all steps satisfy corridor and witness requirements.
8.4.1(c) Parameter binding completeness.Programs must not contain implicit parameters. All parameters influencing execution are included in (h_{P_k}) and in referenced context hashes. Any external dependency not committed by hash invalidates determinism.
8.4.1(d) Program identity boundaries.Program identity is not defined by informal equivalence of code; it is defined by canonical encoding and hash. Any equivalence between two distinct program hashes must be established by certified equivalence proofs.
8.4.2 Canonical program normal form
8.4.2(a) Step normal form.A step is in step normal form iff:
(\mathsf{op_id}) is canonical and references a pinned contract or macro id,
the parameter object referenced by (h_P) is in canonical form,
any required normalization steps and bridges mandated by compatibility protocol are explicitly present in the program sequence.
8.4.2(b) Program normal form operator.Define a deterministic normalization operator:[\mathsf{PNF}:\ \Pi \to \Pi^\star,]which:
canonicalizes parameter encodings,
expands macros to canonical step sequences when macro expansion is part of the canonical form,
inserts mandatory normalization steps at pinned insertion points,
ensures all compatibility requirements are met by explicit bridges,
orders any commutable, proof-certified subsegments into a canonical order via certified rewrite rules.
(\mathsf{PNF}) is pinned and versioned; different (\mathsf{PNF}) hashes define different canonical program normal forms.
8.4.2(c) Idempotence and determinism.(\mathsf{PNF}) must satisfy:[\mathsf{PNF}(\mathsf{PNF}(\Pi))=\mathsf{PNF}(\Pi),]and identical input program bytes yield identical output program bytes.
8.4.2(d) Normal form as canonical representative.Two programs are exactly equivalent under the canonical normal form policy iff:[\mathsf{PNF}(\Pi_1)\equiv \mathsf{PNF}(\Pi_2)\quad (\text{bytewise}).]If (\mathsf{PNF}) is not complete for a program family, equivalence must be established by explicit rewrite proofs (Section 8.4.3).
8.4.3 Certified equivalence proofs
8.4.3(a) Program equivalence claim.A program equivalence proof is a rewrite proof object (Chapter 6) binding:
(h(\Pi)), (h(\Pi')),
invariant set hash (h(\mathcal I)),
tolerance profiles (h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathrm{in}})), (h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathrm{out}})),
convention fingerprint set hash,
verification level,
test basis hash,
checker program id hash,
rewrite rule id hash.
8.4.3(b) Equivalence verification.A verifier accepts a program equivalence proof iff:
both programs are well-formed under the COS program schema,
the proof’s method id and checker program id are valid and pinned,
local-check evaluation establishes equivalence under declared invariants and tolerances at the declared verification level,
convention fingerprints are preserved or explicitly bridged in the rewritten program,
the rewrite does not violate corridor scope or determinism constraints.
8.4.3(c) Structural vs behavioral equivalence.Program equivalence may be:
structural: both programs reduce to identical canonical normal forms under (\mathsf{PNF}),
behavioral: both programs produce equivalent outputs on a declared test regime under ((\mathcal I,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})).Behavioral equivalence must state its verification level explicitly; it cannot be interpreted as global identity beyond the declared regime.
8.4.3(d) Equivalence closure and composition.Certified program equivalence is compositional: equivalence proofs compose by concatenation if:
invariant sets and tolerance profiles are compatible (refinement ordering),
convention fingerprints match or are bridged,
the composed proof remains within corridor feasibility.Composition of equivalence proofs must itself be representable as a proof object with deterministic verification.
8.4.4 Equivalence classes as laws
8.4.4(a) Definition of a law.A law in this framework is an equivalence class of programs under certified equivalence:[[\Pi] := {\Pi' \mid \Pi'\sim_{\mathcal I,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon},\mathsf{VL}} \Pi \ \text{via certified proofs}}.]A law is not a statement written in symbols; it is a stable class of executable derivations whose equivalence is mechanically verifiable under pinned policies.
8.4.4(b) Canonical law representative.If (\mathsf{PNF}) is defined for a program family, the canonical representative of a law is (\mathsf{PNF}(\Pi)). Otherwise, a canonical representative may be defined by selecting the lexicographically minimal program hash among those in the equivalence class, provided membership is certified by proofs. The selection rule must be pinned by policy.
8.4.4(c) Law portability via receipts.A law becomes portable when paired with a receipt that commits to:
the program (or plan) hash,
the ledger header hash,
the final state hash,
output pin roots and witness roots,
policy hashes.Receipts allow independent replay verification and enable reuse without re-deriving upstream results, subject to dependency proofs.
8.4.4(d) Honesty constraints for laws.A law’s meaning is bounded by:
the invariant set (\mathcal I),
the tolerance profile (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}),
the verification level (\mathsf{VL}),
the convention fingerprints.Any claim that exceeds these bounds is illegal. Laws are therefore precise, auditable, and representation-safe.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 8 — TRANSFORM ALGEBRA: COMPOSITION, INVERSES, QUOTIENTS
CHAPTER 9 — DRIFT DETECTION: DEFECTS, EVIDENCE, REPAIR TRIGGERS (Final Draft)
This chapter specifies the drift-detection layer: a deterministic family of defect detectors that (i) identify illegal divergence between declared contracts and executed reality, (ii) emit canonical evidence objects bound to step provenance, and (iii) deterministically trigger certified repair actions or halting. Drift detection is not heuristic. It is an enforceable legality mechanism integrated into the execution kernel’s projection operator and into compilation/routing. A computation is admissible only if required detectors return non-failing verdicts under the corridor policy (\Omega).
9.1 Defect taxonomy
A defect is a condition under which a step or chain of steps violates the explicit constraints of the framework: convention discipline, invariant contracts, tolerance budgets, or replay determinism. Defects are classified into a finite set of defect classes; each class has a deterministic detector and a canonical evidence schema.
9.1.1 Convention drift defects
9.1.1(a) Definition (convention drift).Let a step apply a transform contract (\mathsf{LT}) with declared convention bundles (\mathsf{SpecIn},\mathsf{SpecOut}) (Chapter 7). Let the actual inputs consumed by the step (as resolved from the pre-state and source commitments) have an observed convention bundle (\mathsf{SpecObsIn}), and let the produced intermediate or output artifacts have an observed convention bundle (\mathsf{SpecObsOut}), both computed deterministically from pinned spec objects referenced by those artifacts. A convention drift defect occurs when at least one of the following holds:
Input mismatch: (\mathsf{SpecObsIn}\neq \mathsf{SpecIn}(\mathsf{LT})) and no explicit bridge chain is present in the executed plan segment that maps (\mathsf{SpecObsIn}\to \mathsf{SpecIn}(\mathsf{LT})).
Output mismatch: (\mathsf{SpecObsOut}\neq \mathsf{SpecOut}(\mathsf{LT})) and the contract does not declare that the output spec is variable (variable output specs are forbidden unless a declared spec-selection rule is pinned and included in parameters).
Silent convention mutation: the step’s delta bundle implies an index permutation, sign flip, scaling change, unit conversion, or domain/branch selection change without an explicit bridge transform whose contract declares that mutation.
Convention drift is a hard illegality for transforms that require spec compatibility (all standard transports), unless the corridor explicitly classifies the mismatch as soft and allows deterministic omission of the affected outputs (only permitted for non-committed exploratory artifacts).
9.1.1(b) Convention boundary points.Convention drift is evaluated at the following boundaries:
Transform boundary: between (\mathsf{SpecOut}(\mathsf{LT}k)) and (\mathsf{SpecIn}(\mathsf{LT}{k+1})) in a route.
Bridge boundary: between a bridge’s declared mapping and the observed spec change; any observed spec change not accounted for by a bridge is illegal.
Commit boundary: before writing a commitment record, the committed object’s spec bundle must match the canonical required bundle for that namespace and type; otherwise the commit is illegal.
All boundary points are deterministically identified by plan structure and by the executed step descriptor sequence.
9.1.1(c) Convention drift severity.Convention drift detector returns:
(\mathsf{OK}): all required spec equalities hold and all spec changes are justified by explicit bridges.
(\mathsf{FAIL}): any mismatch occurs without an inserted certified bridge and without an explicit, admissible contract clause permitting that mismatch.
No (\mathsf{NEAR}) state is defined for convention drift: spec mismatches are categorical, not continuous.
9.1.1(d) Convention drift evidence requirements.Evidence for convention drift must include:
expected spec bundle hash (from contract or route plan),
observed spec bundle hash (from actual objects),
boundary location identifier (step index, transform id, boundary kind),
required bridge class identifiers (sign/scale/index/unit/domain/grid) as a canonical set,
the list of missing or invalid bridge ids (if any),
binding to the step context (\mathsf{Ctx}) (Chapter 5).
Evidence is a typed object stored in the proof store and referenced by the step record.
9.1.2 Invariant drift defects
9.1.2(a) Definition (invariant drift).Let (\mathsf{LT}) declare invariant contract (\mathcal{I}{\mathsf{LT}}) with tolerance vector (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathsf{LT}}) (Chapter 2). For each applicable invariant (I\in\mathcal{I}{\mathsf{LT}}), define the drift measure for a step input (p) and output (p') as:[d_I := \big| I(\llbracket p\rrbracket{L_{\mathrm{in}}}) - I(\llbracket p'\rrbracket_{L_{\mathrm{out}}})\big|_I,]where all values are computed deterministically on quantized measurements per pinned numeric policies (Chapters 1 and 6). An invariant drift defect occurs if there exists an applicable invariant (I) such that:[d_I > \varepsilon_I.]If the invariant is declared hard by (\Omega), drift beyond (\varepsilon_I) is a hard failure. If the invariant is declared soft and the contract permits partial acceptance by discarding uncertified secondary outputs, the projector may permit partial acceptance only if no committed artifacts depend on the violated invariant.
9.1.2(b) Invariant applicability and guard coupling.Invariants may be conditional on applicability predicates (Chapter 2). An invariant drift defect is evaluated only when the applicability predicate is true under the step context. Applicability predicates are pinned and deterministic; any mismatch in applicability due to hidden parameters is prohibited by the canonical parameterization rules.
9.1.2(c) Invariant drift severity.Invariant drift detector returns:
(\mathsf{OK}): (d_I \le \alpha_I \varepsilon_I) for all applicable invariants, where (\alpha_I\in(0,1)) is a pinned near-threshold fraction (Chapter 3).
(\mathsf{NEAR}): (\alpha_I \varepsilon_I < d_I \le \varepsilon_I) for at least one applicable invariant, and no applicable invariant exceeds (\varepsilon_I).
(\mathsf{FAIL}): (d_I > \varepsilon_I) for at least one applicable invariant.
Near-threshold fractions and norms are part of the invariant clause and corridor policy and thus are replay-deterministic.
9.1.2(d) Invariant drift evidence requirements.Evidence for invariant drift must include, for each invariant that is (\mathsf{NEAR}) or (\mathsf{FAIL}):
invariant id,
quantized pre and post invariant values,
computed drift (d_I) and threshold (\varepsilon_I),
the norm id hash used,
applicability predicate id hash,
references to measured object hashes,
binding to (\mathsf{Ctx}).
