The evidence object is canonically encoded as a sequence-like list in a fixed invariant-id ordering.
9.1.3 Approximation accumulation defects
9.1.3(a) Definition (tolerance budget accumulation).Let (\Omega) define tolerance budgets (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega}) and per-invariant accumulation operators (\oplus_I) (Chapter 3). Each step (t) consumes declared tolerance usage (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}t), either explicitly from the transform contract or implicitly via declared bridge/normalizer steps whose tolerance usage is pinned. Define the cumulative tolerance usage for invariant (I) at step (t) as:[\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}{I,t} := \varepsilon_{I,1}\oplus_I \cdots \oplus_I \varepsilon_{I,t}.]An approximation accumulation defect occurs if there exists an invariant (I) such that:[\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_{I,t} > \varepsilon^\Omega_I.]This defect captures “approximation creep”: a chain of individually admissible approximate steps exceeding corridor tolerance budgets.
9.1.3(b) Coupling to claim strength and verification level.Tolerance usage (\varepsilon_{I,t}) must be consistent with proof verification levels (Chapter 5): a step cannot claim (\varepsilon=0) while using an approximate computation mode. Approximation accumulation is therefore checked jointly against:
contract-declared tolerance usage,
corridor budgets,
and the proof objects’ claim bindings.
Any inconsistency between these components is treated as a determinism/contract defect and results in failure.
9.1.3(c) Accumulation severity.Approximation accumulation detector returns:
(\mathsf{OK}): (\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_{I,t}\le \alpha_I \varepsilon^\Omega_I) for all invariants,
(\mathsf{NEAR}): for some (I), (\alpha_I \varepsilon^\Omega_I < \varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_{I,t}\le \varepsilon^\Omega_I),
(\mathsf{FAIL}): for some (I), (\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_{I,t} > \varepsilon^\Omega_I).
The near-threshold fractions (\alpha_I) are pinned in (\Omega) or in invariant policy objects.
9.1.3(d) Accumulation evidence requirements.Evidence must include:
the full cumulative tolerance vector (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}}_t),
the corridor budget vector (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega}),
the accumulation operator ids ({\oplus_I}) or a single pinned operator bundle hash,
the step-wise contributions (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_k) for a bounded prefix window sufficient to reproduce the cumulation (window size pinned by (\Omega)),
binding to (\mathsf{Ctx}).
Evidence is required for (\mathsf{NEAR}) and (\mathsf{FAIL}) outcomes when (\Omega) marks tolerance budgets as hard or mitigation-required.
9.1.4 Replay divergence defects
9.1.4(a) Definition (replay divergence).Replay divergence is a failure of determinism: re-execution of a step or ledger prefix yields different committed outputs than recorded. Let a step record commit ((h_{\mathsf{pre}},h_{\mathsf{post}},r_\Delta,h(W),\mathbf{c})). During replay verification, recompute:
deltas (or verify delta hashes) and apply accepted deltas under the deterministic projector,
witnesses and their verification,
post-state hash (\widehat{h}_{\mathsf{post}}),
and cost vector (\widehat{\mathbf{c}}) (where required by policy).
A replay divergence defect occurs if any of the following holds:
(\widehat{h}{\mathsf{post}}\neq h{\mathsf{post}}),
recomputed delta hashes or delta root differ from recorded,
recomputed witness list hash differs from recorded,
required verifier outcomes differ from recorded,
recomputed cost vector differs from recorded when cost determinism is mandated.
Replay divergence is always a hard failure.
9.1.4(b) Divergence localization.Replay divergence evidence must identify the earliest divergence point among:
commitment store hash,
relation store hash,
candidate store hash,
proof store hash,
or header/policy mismatch.Localization is deterministic because store hashes are recomputed in a fixed order.
9.1.4(c) Severity.Replay divergence has only (\mathsf{OK}) or (\mathsf{FAIL}). There is no (\mathsf{NEAR}): determinism is categorical.
9.1.4(d) Evidence requirements.Evidence must include:
recorded vs recomputed post-state hash,
recorded vs recomputed per-store hashes,
earliest divergence store id,
recorded vs recomputed witness list hash (if witness divergence),
recorded vs recomputed delta root (if delta divergence),
runtime header hash used for replay,
binding to (\mathsf{Ctx}) of the failing step.
This evidence must be emitted deterministically by the replay verifier and is part of the verification trace.
9.2 Detector interfaces
9.2.1 Deterministic detector API
9.2.1(a) Detector function signature.A detector is a deterministic function:[\mathsf{Detect}_k:\ (\Psi_t,\mathsf{StepDesc},\Delta_t,W_t,\mathbf{c}_t,\Omega)\to (\mathsf{flag}_k,E_k),]where (\mathsf{flag}_k\in{\mathsf{OK},\mathsf{NEAR},\mathsf{FAIL}}) (or ({\mathsf{OK},\mathsf{FAIL}}) for categorical detectors) and (E_k) is a typed evidence object hash. A detector must be referentially transparent: identical inputs produce identical outputs.
9.2.1(b) Detector domain restrictions.Detectors may require resolution of object hashes referenced by deltas and witnesses. Any required object must be available via the resolver under the step’s source root commitments or via proof delta additions in the same step. If required objects are missing, the detector must return (\mathsf{FAIL}) with evidence code (\mathsf{E_MISSING_DEP}) bound to (\mathsf{Ctx}).
9.2.1(c) Detector ordering.Detectors run in a fixed canonical order pinned by (\Omega). Detector order is part of determinism: evidence emission depends on which detector triggers first in some policies. Therefore the detector schedule is committed in the ledger plan and enforced by the projector.
9.2.1(d) Detector admissibility.A detector is admissible only if its implementation digest hash is included in the runtime header and if its parameters (thresholds, norms, accumulation operators) are pinned by hash. Unpinned detectors are illegal.
9.2.2 Evidence object schemas
9.2.2(a) Unified evidence schema.All evidence objects conform to a canonical evidence schema:[\mathsf{Evidence}:=(\mathsf{defect_class},\mathsf{severity},\mathsf{code},\mathsf{payload},\mathsf{refs},\mathsf{bind}),]where:
(\mathsf{defect_class}\in{\mathsf{CONVENTION},\mathsf{INVARIANT},\mathsf{ACCUMULATION},\mathsf{REPLAY}}),
(\mathsf{severity}\in{\mathsf{OK},\mathsf{NEAR},\mathsf{FAIL}}),
(\mathsf{code}) is a pinned enum value identifying the specific defect subtype,
(\mathsf{payload}) is a schema-validated, canonical payload specific to the defect class,
(\mathsf{refs}) is a canonically ordered list of referenced hashes (objects, specs, plans, proofs, deltas),
(\mathsf{bind}) is the step binding record equal to (\mathsf{Ctx}).
9.2.2(b) Payload minimality.Evidence payloads must include sufficient information to:
reproduce the detector outcome deterministically under replay, and
determine the required repair class (bridge type, normalization, reroute, halt).Payloads must not include extraneous non-canonical text; evidence is structural.
9.2.2(c) Evidence hashing and storage.Evidence objects are stored in the proof store and referenced by hash in the step record. Evidence ordering in a step is canonical: evidence hashes are sorted lexicographically by ((\mathsf{defect_class},\mathsf{code},h(\mathsf{Evidence}))) under a pinned ordering rule.
9.2.2(d) Evidence completeness requirement.If a detector returns (\mathsf{NEAR}) or (\mathsf{FAIL}) and (\Omega) requires evidence retention for that defect class, omission of the evidence object is a determinism violation and is a hard failure.
9.2.3 Binding evidence to provenance roots
9.2.3(a) Provenance coupling rule.For any detector evidence (E_k) produced in step (t), the step’s source root (r_{\mathsf{src}}) (Chapter 5) must include (h(E_k)) (or a commitment to the evidence list hash) under a pinned inclusion rule. This ensures that evidence is step-local and cannot be swapped without invalidating witness bindings.
9.2.3(b) Evidence-to-witness binding.If a witness justifies a repair or an escalation in response to a defect, the witness must reference the relevant evidence hash(es) in its input references. This binds the justification to the detected defect and prevents “generic” justifications detached from concrete failures.
9.2.3(c) Evidence reuse prohibition.Evidence objects are step-specific: because they bind (\mathsf{bind}=\mathsf{Ctx}), they cannot be transplanted across steps. Any attempt to reuse evidence in a different step fails binding checks.
9.2.3(d) Evidence chain formation.Repairs form an evidence chain: a repair step must include in (r_{\mathsf{src}}) the evidence hash that triggered it, and must emit new evidence showing the defect is resolved. This yields a replay-verifiable causal chain from defect to repair to resolution.
9.2.4 Severity levels and thresholds
9.2.4(a) Severity policy object.(\Omega) pins a severity policy object (\mathsf{SevPol}) specifying, per defect class and subtype:
whether (\mathsf{NEAR}) is defined,
near-threshold fractions (\alpha),
escalation requirements under (\mathsf{NEAR}),
whether the defect is hard (must halt or revert) or soft (permits partial acceptance or mitigation).
9.2.4(b) Hardness classification.By default:
Convention drift and replay divergence are hard failures.
Invariant drift is hard for invariants declared hard, soft only for invariants declared soft and only when no protected commitments depend on the violated invariant.
Accumulation defects are hard for invariants with hard tolerance budgets; near states trigger mandatory mitigation when required by (\Omega).
9.2.4(c) Threshold determinism.All thresholds are quantized values pinned by hash. Any threshold computed from data (e.g., a tolerance derived from parameter values) must be computed deterministically and included in the parameter hash, not recomputed ad hoc.
9.2.4(d) Severity-to-verdict mapping.(\Omega) defines a deterministic mapping from detector severities to step verdict constraints:
any hard (\mathsf{FAIL}) forces (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}),
a hard (\mathsf{NEAR}) forces acceptance only if a mandated mitigation action is included in the plan segment or the step produces a mitigation commitment (policy-defined),
soft (\mathsf{NEAR}) may permit (\mathsf{PARTIAL}) with deterministic omission of uncertified secondary outputs,
soft (\mathsf{FAIL}) is prohibited; failures are hard by definition unless (\Omega) explicitly allows discarding the violating outputs and reclassifying the goal as non-committing (this requires explicit goal semantics and is not permitted for standard commitments).
9.3 Mandatory detector schedule
Detectors are integrated into execution. The schedule is fixed by (\Omega) and by the ledger plan, and is enforced by the kernel projector (Chapter 4). The schedule is partitioned into four phases.
9.3.1 Pre-apply checks
9.3.1(a) Pre-apply scope.Pre-apply detectors run before the carrier-map execution. They depend only on:
the pre-state (\Psi_t),
the step descriptor (\mathsf{StepDesc}),
resolved parameters and context by hash.
9.3.1(b) Required checks.Pre-apply checks include:
Spec feasibility: verify that required (\mathsf{SpecIn}) is available and matches the current state’s relevant spec commitments, or that the plan includes explicit bridges to make it so.
Domain feasibility: verify parameters satisfy DomainSpec constraints; otherwise fail deterministically.
Budget feasibility (static): verify declared budgets and caps for the step do not trivially exceed corridor maxima; if they do, require an explicit budget escalation witness in the plan or declare inadmissible.
9.3.1(c) Evidence emission.Any pre-apply failure emits evidence bound to (\mathsf{Ctx}) (where (\mathsf{Ctx}) is computable from pre-state and step descriptor even before post-state hash is known; the binding uses a placeholder post commitment that is deterministically finalized only if execution proceeds; if execution does not proceed, evidence binds to a pre-apply binding variant pinned by (\Omega)).
9.3.1(d) Pre-apply failure handling.Pre-apply hard failures cause (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}) without executing apply. No proof-store additions or candidate churn are permitted on pre-apply failure unless (\Omega) permits emission of failure evidence objects (which must be scope-allowed and budgeted).
9.3.2 Pre-project checks
9.3.2(a) Pre-project scope.Pre-project checks run after (\mathsf{Apply}) has produced ((\Delta_t,W_t,\mathbf{c}_t)) but before the projector computes acceptance masks and commits any deltas.
9.3.2(b) Required checks.Pre-project checks include:
Delta schema validity: all proposed deltas and referenced objects must be well-formed.
Witness completeness (presence): required witness types must be present (verification may be deferred to project-time).
Static scope feasibility: verify that proposed delta targets are not categorically forbidden by (\Omega); if categorically forbidden, fail immediately.
Cost feasibility: verify (\mathbf{c}_t) is within corridor budgets or that an admissible budget escalation witness is present.
9.3.2(c) Evidence emission and binding.Pre-project evidence is emitted for any failures or near-threshold conditions and must be included in the evidence list committed by the step record. Evidence binding requires inclusion in (r_{\mathsf{src}}) as specified in Section 9.2.3.
9.3.2(d) Pre-project failure handling.If any pre-project hard failure occurs, the verdict must be (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}). If only near conditions occur, the step may proceed to project-time with mandatory mitigation obligations as required by (\Omega).
9.3.3 Project-time checks
9.3.3(a) Project-time scope.Project-time detectors run inside the legality projector (\mathsf{Project}), at the point where acceptance masks are determined and before deltas are applied. Project-time is where hard legality is enforced.
9.3.3(b) Required checks.Project-time checks include:
Witness verification: all required witnesses verify deterministically, including step-local binding.
Convention drift detection: verify spec matching or presence of explicit bridges; fail if mismatch.
Invariant drift detection: evaluate declared invariants where applicable and classify (\mathsf{OK}/\mathsf{NEAR}/\mathsf{FAIL}).
Contradiction detection: apply contradiction predicate to proposed commitment delta; require resolution proofs as mandated.
9.3.3(c) Mask computation interaction.Project-time detection is integrated with mask computation:
If a defect is hard (\mathsf{FAIL}), the mask is irrelevant and the verdict is (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}).
If a defect is soft (\mathsf{NEAR}), the mask may discard uncertified secondary outputs or trigger deterministic truncation.
If invariant drift is (\mathsf{NEAR}) under a hard invariant, the corridor may require insertion of an explicit normalization step; if not present, the step is illegal.
9.3.3(d) Project-time evidence commitments.All project-time evidence objects must be committed in the step record. Failure to commit required evidence is a determinism failure and results in (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}).
9.3.4 Post-commit audits
9.3.4(a) Post-commit scope.Post-commit audits run after accepted deltas have been applied and post-state hashes computed, but before the step record is finalized. These audits enforce invariants of the state itself, independent of any single transform.
9.3.4(b) Required audits.Post-commit audits include:
Post-resolution contradiction scan for contradiction-controlled namespaces.
Canonical normal form checks for committed objects: any committed value must be in canonical normal form under Chapter 7.
Tolerance ledger update and accumulation check: update (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}}) and detect (\mathsf{NEAR}/\mathsf{FAIL}).
Replay precheck: compute and commit the step record components such that the replay verifier can recompute them deterministically; any ambiguity in canonical encoding is a hard failure.
9.3.4(c) Audit-triggered mitigation.If an audit produces (\mathsf{NEAR}) on a hard policy component (e.g., tolerance accumulation), (\Omega) may require that the next plan segment include deterministic mitigation steps; the audit must emit evidence specifying the required mitigation policy id(s). Mitigation is enforced at compile-time for subsequent segments and at run-time by rejecting steps that ignore required mitigation.
9.3.4(d) Audit evidence retention.Audit evidence is retained according to (\Omega)’s evidence retention policy. If retention is mandatory, evidence objects are committed to the proof store and referenced by the step record. Evidence omission under mandatory retention is a determinism failure.
9.4 Repair triggers and escalation rules
Repairs are not informal procedures. Repairs are explicit, certified transform sequences compiled as plans, executed under the kernel, and verified by receipts. This section defines deterministic rules mapping defect evidence to repair classes, escalation, or halting.
9.4.1 When to bridge vs reroute
9.4.1(a) Bridge triggers (convention defects).If a defect is a convention drift defect and the mismatch is in one of the convention components ({\mathsf{Sign},\mathsf{Scale},\mathsf{Index},\mathsf{Unit},\mathsf{Domain}}), the default repair is bridge insertion:
revert to the last stable checkpoint,
compile a repair plan inserting the minimal certified bridge chain mapping observed spec to required spec (Chapter 7),
execute the repair plan,
resume the original route.
Bridge selection is deterministic by the routing cost functional; ties are broken lexicographically.
9.4.1(b) Reroute triggers (invariant drift and accumulation).If a defect is invariant drift (\mathsf{NEAR}) or (\mathsf{FAIL}), or tolerance accumulation (\mathsf{NEAR}/\mathsf{FAIL}), and the cause is not a convention mismatch but an approximation or transform choice, the default repair is rerouting:
replace approximate edges by exact edges where available,
insert canonical normalization checkpoints where required,
reduce the number of approximate steps,
or change the representation route to reduce drift under the declared invariants.
Reroute decisions are deterministic and must be compiled into a new plan; no run-time improvisation is permitted.
9.4.1(c) Bridge vs reroute tie-breaking.If both bridge and reroute could repair a defect, (\Omega) pins a deterministic preference policy. The canonical preference order is:
bridge when the defect is convention drift,
reroute when the defect is invariant drift or accumulation,
normalize when near-threshold conditions indicate drift is due to non-canonical intermediates rather than transform choice.This preference order is pinned; alternative orders require explicit policy override.
9.4.1(d) Repair feasibility constraint.A repair is admissible only if it is feasible under (\Omega): budgets, scope, determinism, and contradiction policy must hold. If no feasible repair exists under current (\Omega), the system must halt with a non-repairable defect outcome.
9.4.2 When to tighten tolerances
9.4.2(a) Tightening as refinement.Tightening tolerances means producing stronger claims: smaller (\varepsilon) for invariants or higher verification level for proofs (Chapter 5). Tightening is represented by a refinement relation and requires re-verification, not reinterpretation.
9.4.2(b) Tightening triggers.Tolerance tightening is triggered deterministically when:
a route depends on approximate equivalence proofs whose verification level is insufficient for the downstream claim class,
repeated near-threshold invariant drift suggests the current tolerance profile leaves inadequate headroom for long-chain stability,
accumulation near-threshold indicates impending corridor exhaustion for required invariants,
the goal demands exactness ((\varepsilon=0)) but the current route uses bounded steps.
9.4.2(c) Tightening actions.Tightening actions include:
recompile with (\varepsilon=0) constraints, forcing exact transforms,
insert stronger proof methods (higher verification level) for critical equivalences,
reduce approximation allowances in the corridor policy only if (\Omega) permits policy refinement and the refinement is certified by a policy-update transform (policy changes are otherwise immutable).
9.4.2(d) Tightening feasibility and evidence.Tightening must be feasible under budgets. If tightening increases proof burden or compute cost, the plan must include admissible budget escalation witnesses or must choose alternative routes. Tightening decisions must cite the evidence hashes that triggered tightening and must produce receipts showing the strengthened claim boundaries.
9.4.3 When to halt (non-repairable defects)
9.4.3(a) Non-repairable classes.The system must halt under any of the following:
Replay divergence defect ((\mathsf{REPLAY}) (\mathsf{FAIL})): determinism violation indicates an invalid runtime or invalid canonicalization/verifier implementation state. In-chain repair is forbidden; the correct action is to halt and require correction of the trusted computing base or canonicalization policy mismatch.
Convention drift defect with no admissible bridge chain: if the required bridge class is not present in the transform library or is infeasible under (\Omega), no repair exists.
Invariant drift (\mathsf{FAIL}) on a hard invariant with no alternative route or normalization capable of restoring legality within corridor budgets.
Accumulation defect (\mathsf{FAIL}) on a hard tolerance budget with no reroute capable of reducing cumulative usage or no policy-permitted reset with certified justification.
9.4.3(b) Halt artifact.Halting produces a typed halt artifact (\mathsf{Halt}) containing:
the failing defect class and evidence hash,
the earliest failing step index,
the minimal required repair class list (bridge/reroute/normalize/TCB-fix) as canonical tokens,
the current runtime header hash and policy hashes,
a deterministic diagnosis summary expressed as structured fields (not narrative).
9.4.3(c) Halt determinism.Halting must be deterministic: identical failing conditions produce identical halt artifacts. Any ambiguity in halt reporting is a determinism violation.
9.4.3(d) Post-halt state.On halt, the state remains at the last stable checkpoint, and no further steps are admissible under the same plan. Subsequent actions require compiling a new plan or updating the runtime header/policy via explicit, certified policy-update mechanisms (if such mechanisms are permitted).
9.4.4 Repair receipts and auditability
9.4.4(a) Repair as plan + execution.A repair is represented by:
a repair plan object (\mathsf{Plan}_{\mathsf{rep}}) whose goal is to restore legality (e.g., align specs, restore canonical normal forms, reroute to exact transforms),
an execution trace under the kernel yielding a ledger segment,
a receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}_{\mathsf{rep}}) certifying the repair.
Repairs are therefore first-class, replay-verifiable computations.
9.4.4(b) Repair receipt schema requirements.A repair receipt must commit to:
repair plan hash,
ledger header hash,
final state hash after repair,
pin roots and witness roots for artifacts produced by the repair,
policy hashes and runtime header hash,
the defect evidence hash(es) that triggered the repair, included in the repair plan’s source commitments.
9.4.4(c) Repair audit chain.Auditability requires a deterministic chain:[\text{defect evidence} \to \text{repair plan} \to \text{repair execution ledger} \to \text{repair receipt} \to \text{resumed plan receipt}.]Each arrow is realized by hash references and dependency proofs (Chapter 6). In particular:
the repair plan must include the defect evidence hashes in its context,
the repair execution steps must include those hashes in provenance source roots,
the resumed plan’s steps must include the repair receipt hash as an upstream dependency proof if they rely on the repaired state.
9.4.4(d) Public verification boundary.Repair receipts are verifiable to the same extent as standard receipts: verification may be hash-only or selective reveal depending on disclosure policy. The repair receipt’s guarantees are bounded by the invariants and verification levels declared in its proof objects. No repair can claim to restore more than it certifies; any stronger claim requires additional certified steps.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 9 — DRIFT DETECTION: DEFECTS, EVIDENCE, REPAIR TRIGGERS
CHAPTER 10 — CERTIFIED REPAIR: BRIDGES, REGRIDS, RENORMALIZERS (Final Draft)
This chapter specifies certified repair: the only admissible mechanism for correcting defects detected by the drift-detection layer. Repairs are not privileged procedures. They are legal transform programs compiled, executed, ledgered, and receipted under the same kernel and corridor policy as all other computation. The repair system is complete relative to the defect taxonomy: every repairable defect class has a deterministic repair family, and every repair family is implemented as a finite composition of certified bridges, regrids, or renormalizers, with explicit invariants, explicit budgets, explicit witnesses, and deterministic replay.
10.1 Repair as legal transform programs
10.1.1 Repair contracts and invariants
10.1.1(a) Repair program as a legal transform.A repair action is a legal transform contract (\mathsf{LT}{\mathsf{rep}}) whose purpose is to map a defective pre-state (or defective boundary condition) into a post-state that satisfies corridor legality and eliminates the triggering defect evidence. Formally, a repair is a program (\Pi{\mathsf{rep}}) compiled from (\mathsf{LT}_{\mathsf{rep}}) and executed under the kernel (Chapter 4). The repair is not “outside” the system: it is a first-class transport object and is subject to all requirements of Chapter 2.
10.1.1(b) Repair goal specification.A repair contract must declare an explicit repair goal:[\mathsf{Goal}_{\mathsf{rep}} := (\mathsf{defect_class},\ h(E),\ \mathsf{target_predicate}),]where:
(\mathsf{defect_class}) is the defect class being repaired,
(h(E)) is the hash of the triggering evidence object,
(\mathsf{target_predicate}) is a deterministic predicate pinned by hash specifying the postcondition (e.g., spec-hash equality restored, invariant drift reduced below threshold, tolerance ledger brought under budget, canonical normal form restored).
The repair is correct only if the postcondition predicate verifies deterministically on the post-state and is logged as evidence.
10.1.1(c) Repair invariant contract.A repair contract must declare the invariants it preserves or restores. Repair invariants are divided into:
Hard preservation invariants: invariants that must remain unchanged by repair (e.g., committed facts unrelated to the defect, identity of protected pins outside affected namespaces).
Restoration invariants: invariants whose violation triggered the defect and which must be restored to (\mathsf{OK}) status (or brought to a specified bound) by repair.
Bridge invariants: invariants guaranteeing that the repair operation changes only the intended representational degrees of freedom (e.g., sign flip bridge preserves norm; index shift preserves multisets of values).
The repair contract must encode these invariant clauses explicitly and bind them by hash, like any legal transform.
10.1.1(d) Repair minimality constraint (policy-controlled).(\Omega) may require repairs to be minimal: among all admissible repairs that satisfy (\mathsf{target_predicate}), the system must select the deterministic minimal-cost repair under pinned cost functionals (hop count, budget, tolerance, witness burden). Minimality is enforced by compilation; any deviation produces a different repair plan hash and is therefore non-canonical, though still legal if it satisfies all constraints.
10.1.2 Repair budgets and scope constraints
10.1.2(a) Repair budget profile.Repairs are budgeted like any other transform. The repair contract specifies (\mathcal{B}{\mathsf{rep}}) and must satisfy corridor feasibility. Repairs are often more expensive than regular steps; therefore the corridor may reserve a dedicated repair budget reserve (b{\mathsf{rep}}) or may require explicit budget escalation witnesses for repairs. Any repair that exceeds corridor budgets without admissible escalation is illegal.
10.1.2(b) Repair scope constraints.Repairs modify state only through explicit deltas and must obey scope. The repair scope (\mathcal{S}_{\mathsf{rep}}) must be as narrow as possible. In particular:
convention repairs may modify operator/spec artifacts and associated intermediate objects but may not modify unrelated commitments,
grid repairs may modify discretization-dependent objects but must preserve commitments whose meaning is invariant under regridding (or else explicitly declare loss profiles),
contradiction repairs may modify only the conflicting commitment namespace and any dependency edges required by policy.
Scope constraints are enforced by the kernel projector and are not bypassable.
10.1.2(c) Repair isolation.A repair plan must include an explicit isolation statement: the set of commitment keys, relation nodes/edges, and proof objects it may alter or introduce. Isolation is represented as a typed set object and referenced by the repair contract and by the repair witnesses. Any repair that writes outside its isolation set is illegal.
10.1.2(d) Repair budget accounting and evidence.Repair steps must emit cost vectors and any near-threshold budget evidence exactly as ordinary execution does. In particular, if repair increases tolerance consumption (e.g., an approximate regrid), the tolerance usage must be explicit and must be feasible under (\Omega). “Repair by silently loosening tolerances” is prohibited.
10.1.3 Repair witness requirements
10.1.3(a) Mandatory repair provenance binding.Every repair step must include a provenance witness binding to the pre-state hash, the triggering evidence hash(es), the repair plan hash, deltas, and the post-state hash. The repair provenance source root must include:
(h(E)) (trigger evidence),
the pre-repair checkpoint hash,
the repair plan hash,
any upstream receipt hashes the repair depends on.
This prevents repairs from being decoupled from the defect they claim to fix.
10.1.3(b) Repair proof witnesses.Repairs must include proof witnesses appropriate to their class:
Convention bridges require equivalence proof witnesses justifying the spec mapping.
Regrids require error-bound proof witnesses and stability proofs under declared norms.
Renormalizers require proofs that canonical normal forms preserve declared invariants.
Proof regeneration repairs require verification-level proofs showing the strengthened check passes.
All witnesses are locally verifiable and bound to step context (Chapter 5).
10.1.3(c) Repair completion witness.A repair plan must emit a repair completion proof object (P_{\mathsf{rep}}) certifying that the target predicate holds in the repaired post-state. (P_{\mathsf{rep}}) binds:
the defect class,
the triggering evidence hash,
the repair plan hash,
the post-state hash,
and the detector id(s) whose (\mathsf{OK}) status constitutes completion.
This converts “we repaired it” into a verifiable artifact.
10.1.3(d) Contradiction and merge witness coupling.If the repair resolves contradictions or merges identities, the repair must include the appropriate consistency/merge witnesses and must trigger post-resolution contradiction scans. Any repair that changes contradiction-controlled namespaces without explicit resolution proofs is illegal even if the final state appears consistent.
10.1.4 Repair replay and receipts
10.1.4(a) Repair ledger segment.Repairs are executed under the kernel and yield a ledger segment. The segment must be replay-verifiable under the same runtime header and corridor policy (or under an explicitly bridged policy if policy-bridging is permitted). If a repair requires updating the runtime header (e.g., changing verifier set), this is a different runtime instance and must be represented as an explicit runtime migration with its own receipt and policy constraints.
10.1.4(b) Repair receipt schema.A repair receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}_{\mathsf{rep}}) must commit to:
repair plan hash,
ledger header hash,
final repaired state hash,
pin root for any new commitments introduced by repair,
witness root for all witnesses used,
policy hashes,
and the triggering evidence hash(es).
10.1.4(c) Repair composability.Repair receipts compose with subsequent receipts via dependency proofs: any subsequent step that relies on repaired artifacts must reference (\mathsf{Rcpt}_{\mathsf{rep}}) as an upstream dependency. This creates an auditable chain from defect evidence to repair to continued computation.
10.1.4(d) Repair verification boundary.Repair receipts guarantee only what they certify: elimination of the triggering defect class at the specified boundary under the declared invariants and verification levels. Repairs do not retroactively strengthen prior proofs; if a prior artifact is invalid, repair cannot make it valid without explicit regeneration and re-issuance.
10.2 Convention bridge transforms
Convention bridges are certified transforms whose sole purpose is to map one convention fingerprint to another without changing the underlying meaning relative to declared invariants. Bridges are required whenever adjacent transforms do not match convention fingerprints (Chapter 7) and no implicit coercion is allowed.
10.2.1 Sign/phase bridges
10.2.1(a) Sign bridge contract.A sign bridge (\mathsf{LT}_{\pm}) is a legal transform with:
(\mathsf{SpecIn}) containing (h_{\mathsf{Sign}}^{\mathrm{in}}),
(\mathsf{SpecOut}) containing (h_{\mathsf{Sign}}^{\mathrm{out}}),
a deterministic mapping rule (e.g., sign flip in exponent convention, orientation reversal, conjugation insertion),
and a proof witness that the bridge preserves the declared invariant subset (\mathcal{I}_{\mathrm{bridge}}) (typically norm/energy, invertibility class, set-level invariants).
10.2.1(b) Phase equivalence and quotient policy.If the sign bridge affects phase and the domain treats phase modulo a quotient, the bridge must declare a quotient contract. Phase is never dropped implicitly. Any “global phase” equivalence must be an explicit equivalence relation object bound by hash, and the bridge proof must verify in that quotient space.
10.2.1(c) Sign bridge local-check regime.Sign bridges must include local-check data specifying:
a finite test basis adequate to detect sign errors,
explicit convention fingerprints for both sides,
and tolerance bounds (typically exact, (\varepsilon=0), unless numerical constraints force bounded approximation).Sign bridge proofs at insufficient verification level for the claimed invariants are invalid.
10.2.1(d) Sign bridge admissibility and insertion.Sign bridges are inserted only when required by spec mismatch and selected deterministically as the minimal-cost bridge chain. If multiple sign bridges exist (e.g., conjugation-based vs index-reversal-based), selection is deterministic by pinned cost functionals.
10.2.2 Scale/normalization bridges
10.2.2(a) Scale bridge contract.A scale bridge (\mathsf{LT}{\times \alpha}) maps (h{\mathsf{Scale}}^{\mathrm{in}}\to h_{\mathsf{Scale}}^{\mathrm{out}}) by applying deterministic scaling factors. Scaling factors must be represented in the pinned numeric domain:
exact rational/fixed-point scaling yields (\varepsilon=0),
approximate scaling requires explicit tolerance usage.
10.2.2(b) Preservation obligations.Scale bridges must prove preservation of the invariants they claim (e.g., energy invariants under unitary normalization) and must explicitly declare any invariants that are changed (loss profile). If scaling changes norm, then norm preservation cannot be claimed.
10.2.2(c) Cross-scale canonicalization.Scale bridges must produce outputs that are immediately canonical under the target ScaleSpec’s numeric policies. If the scale bridge changes quantization policy, it must explicitly declare this and the output must carry the new quantization policy hash.
10.2.2(d) Scale bridge evidence.Scale bridge steps must emit evidence of the applied scaling: the scaling factor object hash, pre/post spec hashes, and the bound proof hash. This evidence is required for later audit and for preventing silent rescaling.
10.2.3 Index permutation bridges
10.2.3(a) Index bridge contract.An index bridge (\mathsf{LT}{\pi}) maps (h{\mathsf{Index}}^{\mathrm{in}}\to h_{\mathsf{Index}}^{\mathrm{out}}) by applying a deterministic permutation (\pi) on the address algebra. The permutation is a typed object (\mathsf{Perm}) that is either:
an explicit list mapping indices to indices (bounded size), or
a pinned rule id describing a computable permutation (e.g., centered-to-uncentered shift, reversal, modular residue shift).
10.2.3(b) Preservation obligations.Index bridges must prove that:
the permutation is bijective over the index set,
any invariants claimed are preserved (e.g., multiset of values, (\ell_2) norm, energy),
and the output address algebra matches the declared target address algebra.
10.2.3(c) Interaction with sign and scale.Index shifts often induce phase multipliers or scaling adjustments in certain domains. Such coupling must not be implicit: if the intended mapping requires an index permutation plus a phase multiplier, the repair must be represented as a composed bridge chain (index bridge + sign/phase bridge + scale bridge as needed), each with its own contract and proof.
10.2.3(d) Canonical permutation application.Permutation must be applied to canonical array layout and must result in canonical output bytes under the target layout policy. Any dependence on platform-specific array iteration is illegal.
10.2.4 Unit/parameter domain bridges
10.2.4(a) Unit conversion bridges.A unit bridge maps (h_{\mathsf{Unit}}^{\mathrm{in}}\to h_{\mathsf{Unit}}^{\mathrm{out}}) by applying a deterministic conversion map. Conversion maps are typed objects specifying exact ratios or explicit approximate conversion bounds. Unit conversion is always an explicit transform; if a downstream transform expects parameters in a particular unit system, a unit bridge must be inserted.
10.2.4(b) Parameter domain restriction bridges.A domain bridge maps an input parameter domain to a stricter domain required by a transform contract (e.g., enforcing positivity constraints, restricting to a principal branch, enforcing boundedness). Domain bridges must:
declare the restriction predicate,
declare whether they reject out-of-domain inputs or project them into-domain (projection requires explicit semantics and proofs),
produce deterministic error artifacts on rejection.
10.2.4(c) Branch selection as domain bridging.For multi-valued functions (log, argument, analytic continuation), branch selection is treated as part of DomainSpec. A “branch bridge” is a domain bridge that changes branch policy. It must carry explicit proofs preventing branch laundering: branch changes are illegal unless the bridge explicitly records the branch mapping and proves consistency under declared invariants.
10.2.4(d) Bridge admissibility under (\Omega).Unit and domain bridges may be hard-gated by (\Omega): some domains may not be bridged (e.g., forbidden branch crossings). If (\Omega) forbids a domain bridge, the route is non-repairable and must halt.
10.3 Grid and discretization bridges
Grid bridges are certified transforms that change discretization (resolution, spacing, boundary conditions, index modes) while controlling stability and error bounds. Grid bridges are central to repair of discretization mismatch defects and to any computation that composes discrete and continuous representations.
10.3.1 Refinement/coarsening maps
10.3.1(a) GridSpec objects and compatibility.A grid specification (\mathsf{GridSpec}) is a typed object (Chapter 6) defining shape, spacing, boundary, and index conventions. A grid bridge maps (\mathsf{GridSpec}{\mathrm{in}}\to \mathsf{GridSpec}{\mathrm{out}}). Compatibility is not assumed; it is proven.
10.3.1(b) Refinement map.A refinement map (R:\mathsf{Grid}{\mathrm{coarse}}\to \mathsf{Grid}{\mathrm{fine}}) is represented as:
an interpolation operator (linear/spline/other) with pinned coefficients/rules, or
an operator that inserts zeros (for certain spectral grids),together with explicit boundary behavior. Refinement is a transform that changes the carrier domain and must be declared.
10.3.1(c) Coarsening map.A coarsening map (C:\mathsf{Grid}{\mathrm{fine}}\to \mathsf{Grid}{\mathrm{coarse}}) is represented as:
subsampling with explicit anti-aliasing filters,
averaging/projection operators,
restriction operators on multigrid hierarchies,each pinned and deterministic.
10.3.1(d) Deterministic map selection.If multiple refinement/coarsening methods exist, selection is determined by a pinned policy rule and committed in parameters. No adaptive choice is permitted unless adaptivity is deterministic and depends only on committed hashes.
10.3.2 Stability and error bounds
10.3.2(a) Error metric and bound objects.A grid bridge must declare:
an error metric (|\cdot|) (norm id pinned),
an error bound object (B) specifying (\varepsilon_{\mathrm{grid}}) and any assumptions (e.g., smoothness class, bandlimit assumptions, bounded variation constraints).
10.3.2(b) Proof obligations.A grid bridge must produce a proof object certifying that:[| C(R(x)) - x | \le \varepsilon_{\mathrm{grid}}\quad \text{(when applicable)},]or, in general, that the mapping error between input and reconstructed output is bounded under declared assumptions and within corridor tolerance budgets. If the bridge is exact (rare), it must certify exactness ((\varepsilon_{\mathrm{grid}}=0)).
10.3.2(c) Stability constraints.Stability requires that the bridge not amplify norm beyond declared limits:[|R(x)|\le \kappa_R |x|,\quad |C(y)|\le \kappa_C |y|]for pinned (\kappa_R,\kappa_C). These constants are part of the bridge contract and are verified on a declared local-check basis or by an explicit bound proof.
10.3.2(d) Anti-aliasing and boundary discipline.If coarsening involves subsampling, anti-aliasing filter policy must be explicit. Boundary conditions must match DomainSpec/IndexSpec/UnitSpec compatibility rules. Any implicit boundary handling is illegal and is treated as convention drift or domain drift.
10.3.3 Invertibility tiers
10.3.3(a) Tier classification.Grid bridges are classified into invertibility tiers:
Tier 0 (Exact bijection): refinement and coarsening compose to identity exactly on the declared domain.
Tier 1 (Certified approximate inverse): refinement/coarsening have an approximate inverse with explicit (\varepsilon) bounds.
Tier 2 (Projection / lossy): coarsening discards information; invertibility holds only in a quotient or restricted subspace (bandlimited, smoothness-constrained).
Tier 3 (Non-invertible): only one-way mapping; no reconstruction claim.
The tier is a mandatory contract field and must be consistent with the declared quotient/loss profile (Chapter 8).
10.3.3(b) Restricted-domain invertibility.Tier 1 and Tier 2 bridges may be invertible only on a restricted subspace (e.g., bandlimited signals). Such restriction must be explicit as a DomainSpec predicate. The bridge must include either:
a proof that inputs satisfy the restriction predicate, or
an explicit projector transform that enforces the restriction.
10.3.3(c) Tier-consistent routing.A route that requires exact reconstruction cannot include Tier 2 or Tier 3 grid bridges unless a recovery mechanism exists and is certified. The compiler enforces this by treating invertibility tier as a routing constraint.
10.3.3(d) Tier evidence and audit.Each grid bridge execution must emit evidence including:
input/output GridSpec hashes,
invertibility tier,
error bound object hash,
stability constant hashes,
and proof hash verifying the tier’s claim.
10.3.4 Enforced tolerance accounting
10.3.4(a) Tolerance consumption model.Grid bridges must declare their tolerance consumption vector (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathsf{grid}}) and must be accounted against corridor tolerance budgets. If a grid bridge is approximate, it contributes to accumulation in the tolerance ledger (Chapter 3), and near-threshold detectors must trigger mitigation if required.
10.3.4(b) Drift detector integration.Grid bridge tolerance consumption must be reflected in:
approximation accumulation detectors,
invariant drift detectors (if invariants are checked across grid boundaries),
and convention drift detectors (if grid changes imply index/scale changes).
Any mismatch between declared tolerance consumption and observed drift evidence is a defect.
10.3.4(c) No silent tolerance reset.A grid bridge cannot “renormalize” or “reset” tolerances without explicit proof. Any claim that the bridge reduces tolerance usage must be justified by a proof object specifying why the projection reduces error bounds and must be permitted by (\Omega). Otherwise, tolerance only accumulates.
10.3.4(d) Budget escalation limitations.Grid bridge tolerance escalation (loosening bounds) is not permitted as a repair unless explicitly allowed by corridor policy and justified by a budget escalation witness with a proof that the downstream goal remains admissible under the relaxed tolerances. Absent such permission, tolerances cannot be loosened as part of repair.
10.4 Renormalization and projection repairs
Renormalization repairs restore canonical forms, enforce feasibility constraints, regenerate proofs under stricter checks, and deterministically rerun segments. They are used when drift is caused by non-canonical intermediates, near-threshold accumulation, or insufficient proof strength.
10.4.1 Canonical projections
10.4.1(a) Projection transform definition.A canonical projection is a legal transform (P) mapping objects to canonical representatives under a declared invariant set. Projections are used to:
remove representational slack,
enforce domain constraints (e.g., normalization of distributions),
enforce canonical encodings and ordering,
reduce drift accumulation by returning to a canonical representative when permitted.
10.4.1(b) Projection invariants.A projection must declare:
invariants it preserves exactly,
invariants it preserves within tolerances,
invariants it may alter (loss profile).
If a projection changes meaning, it is a lossy transform and must declare a quotient contract. Projection cannot be hidden as “normalization” if it changes invariants.
10.4.1(c) Projection correctness proof.Projection correctness must be certified by proof objects showing:
idempotence: (P(P(x))=P(x)) (exact or bounded on a local-check basis),
compatibility with convention fingerprints (no spec changes unless P is a bridge),
and preservation of required invariants for the subsequent route segment.
10.4.1(d) Projection insertion policy.Projection repairs are inserted deterministically:
before commitments,
after bridges,
at checkpoints,
and under near-threshold detector triggers (Chapter 7).Insertion points are pinned by (\Omega) and by plan structure; execution-time “extra normalization” is prohibited unless already planned or explicitly triggered by a mandated mitigation policy.
10.4.2 Constraint-preserving repairs
10.4.2(a) Feasibility constraints as predicates.Constraint-preserving repairs enforce feasibility constraints such as:
positivity, normalization, boundedness,
contradiction-free commitment constraints,
domain restrictions for transforms,
admissibility predicates for lenses.
Constraints are represented as pinned predicates referenced by DomainSpec and corridor policy.
10.4.2(b) Repair strategies.Constraint-preserving repairs may take one of two forms:
Rejection repair: reject and rerun with corrected parameters or corrected route, leaving state unchanged.
Projection repair: apply a certified projection into the feasible subset (only if the projection’s semantic effect is declared and certified).
Both forms must be represented as explicit plans and executed under the kernel.
10.4.2(c) Constraint evidence binding.Constraint violations produce evidence objects. Repair steps must include those evidence hashes in their provenance source roots and must emit completion evidence showing the constraint predicate now holds.
10.4.2(d) No “silent clamp.”Any clamp, clipping, or forced feasibility projection is illegal unless represented as a constraint-preserving projection with explicit contract, invariants, and proofs. Informal “clip to [0,1]” without declaration is prohibited.
10.4.3 Proof regeneration under stricter checks
10.4.3(a) Regeneration trigger.Proof regeneration is required when:
a proof witness fails verification (proof drift defect),
a proof is at insufficient verification level for the downstream claim class,
or near-threshold evidence indicates the existing proof’s test regime is too weak to maintain confidence under long-chain composition.
Regeneration is a repair action that replaces invalid or insufficient proofs with stronger proofs, without altering the underlying committed object unless necessary.
10.4.3(b) Regeneration contract.A proof regeneration repair must declare:
the target proof object(s) to replace (by hash),
the strengthened claim object (larger invariant set, smaller tolerances, higher verification level),
the new local-check data and test basis objects,
the method id and checker program id hashes,
and the invariant that the underlying referenced objects remain unchanged (unless regeneration also involves re-derivation).
10.4.3(c) Regeneration feasibility and budgets.Stricter proofs increase compute cost and proof store writes. The repair must be feasible under (\Omega) or must include admissible budget escalation. If feasibility cannot be met, the system must halt with non-repairable defect evidence indicating “insufficient budget for required proof strength.”
10.4.3(d) Supersession and audit.Regenerated proofs supersede prior proofs via explicit supersession records in the commitment store (if proofs are pinned) or via replacement of proof references in operator objects. All supersessions must be contradiction-safe and must produce evidence linking the old proof hash, new proof hash, and the reason (defect evidence hash). Proof supersession is auditable and replay-verifiable.
10.4.4 Deterministic rerun strategies
10.4.4(a) Rerun as a plan transformation.Rerun is a repair strategy that re-executes a plan segment under corrected conditions (bridges inserted, normalization added, stronger proofs used, caps adjusted within allowed policy). Rerun is expressed as compiling a new plan (\mathsf{Plan}') whose route differs deterministically from the original by repair steps.
10.4.4(b) Rerun boundary determination.The rerun segment boundary is determined by the earliest failing step index in defect evidence. The repair plan must:
revert to the last stable checkpoint prior to the earliest failing step,
apply necessary repair steps,
re-execute the minimal suffix segment required to reach the goal.
Boundary determination is deterministic: the earliest failing step index is part of defect evidence.
10.4.4(c) Deterministic re-execution constraints.Rerun must not introduce nondeterminism. If the original segment used any pseudo-randomness permitted by (\Omega), rerun must use the same pinned seed unless the repair explicitly changes the seed by a permitted policy action (seed changes are treated as semantic changes and must be declared).
10.4.4(d) Rerun receipts and dependency links.A rerun produces a new receipt. Downstream steps must reference the rerun receipt as a dependency if they consume artifacts produced by the rerun segment. The original failed receipt (if any) must not be used as a dependency. The system must provide deterministic evidence linking the failed segment to the rerun segment, producing a certified “repair chain” that is audit-ready.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 10 — CERTIFIED REPAIR: BRIDGES, REGRIDS, RENORMALIZERS
CHAPTER 11 — TRANSFORM LIBRARY: CATALOG, HUBS, AND EXTENSIONS (Final Draft)
This chapter defines a canonical, closed catalog of legal transforms and bridges, together with a disciplined extension mechanism for domain-specific transforms. The library is an executable object: it is a hash-addressed set of transform contracts, program implementations, convention fingerprints, proof methods, and verified receipts that jointly guarantee legality, replay determinism, and safe composition.
11.1 Library object model
11.1.1 Transform entries and metadata
11.1.1(a) Transform entry object.A transform library entry is a typed COS object (\mathsf{Entry}) with canonical fields:[\mathsf{Entry}:=(\mathsf{entry_id},\ \mathsf{kind},\ h(\mathsf{LT}),\ h(\Pi),\ h(\mathsf{SpecIn}),\ h(\mathsf{SpecOut}),\ h(\mathcal I),\ h(\mathcal B),\ h(\mathcal S),\ h(\mathcal W),\ h(\mathcal R),\ h(\mathsf{Tests}),\ h(\mathsf{Admit})).]
(\mathsf{entry_id}\in{0,1}^{256}) is the hash of the canonical encoding of (\mathsf{Entry}).
(\mathsf{kind}\in{\mathsf{TRANSFORM},\mathsf{BRIDGE},\mathsf{HUB},\mathsf{NORMALIZER},\mathsf{REPAIR}}) is a pinned enum.
(h(\mathsf{LT})) references the legal transform contract (Chapter 2).
(h(\Pi)) references the canonical program implementation (Chapter 8; executed via the kernel in Chapter 4).
(h(\mathsf{SpecIn}),h(\mathsf{SpecOut})) reference convention bundles (Chapter 7).
(h(\mathcal I)), (h(\mathcal B)), (h(\mathcal S)), (h(\mathcal W)), (h(\mathcal R)) reference the invariant, budget, scope, witness, and replay profiles, respectively.
(h(\mathsf{Tests})) references a test-basis and checker-program bundle required for admission and for reuse.
(h(\mathsf{Admit})) references an admission certificate bundle (Section 11.1.3) binding the entry to at least one verified receipt.
11.1.1(b) Entry semantics.An entry is admissible for execution only if:
(h(\mathsf{LT})) resolves to a well-formed contract and (h(\Pi)) resolves to a well-formed program object,
(\mathsf{SpecIn},\mathsf{SpecOut}) resolve and are in canonical normal form,
the entry’s witness profile uses only verifier rule ids present in the pinned verifier set,
the admission bundle verifies that the entry has at least one reference receipt under a compatible runtime header.
11.1.1(c) Entry locality and determinism.Entry evaluation is fully determined by:
the entry hash (\mathsf{entry_id}),
the parameter hash (h_P),
the resolved input hashes (source root),
and the corridor policy hash (h_\Omega).No library entry may depend on ambient defaults or unspecified conventions; any default must be an explicit pinned object referenced by hash in either (\mathsf{LT}) or (h_P).
11.1.1(d) Entry dependency graph.Each entry may reference dependency entries via a canonical set (\mathsf{Deps}\subseteq{0,1}^{256}) stored in (\mathsf{Admit}). Dependencies include:
required bridges for spec compatibility,
required normalizers inserted by policy,
required proof method registries,
and any prerequisite operator objects.Dependency references enable deterministic closure checks and deterministic route compilation.
11.1.2 Versioning and compatibility hashes
11.1.2(a) Version fields.Every entry includes:[\mathsf{ver}:=(\mathsf{major},\mathsf{minor},\mathsf{patch}),]encoded canonically. Any change to semantics (contract fields, program, verifier method, canonicalization rules) must increment (\mathsf{major}) or (\mathsf{minor}) according to a pinned versioning policy. The version tuple is included in the canonical bytes and thus changes (\mathsf{entry_id}).
11.1.2(b) Compatibility vector.An entry declares a compatibility vector:[\mathsf{Compat}:=(h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h_{\Omega}^{\min},h(\mathsf{SpecIn}),h(\mathsf{SpecOut})),]where:
(h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}) are required runtime header components,
(h_{\Omega}^{\min}) is a minimal corridor policy constraint (e.g., required determinism strictness; required evidence retention),
(h(\mathsf{SpecIn}),h(\mathsf{SpecOut})) are convention bundles.A plan may use an entry only if its runtime header hashes match exactly and its corridor policy satisfies (h_{\Omega}\succeq h_{\Omega}^{\min}) under a pinned policy-preorder (Chapter 3), or if an explicit policy-bridge mechanism exists and is admissible (policy bridges are optional and must be explicitly defined).
11.1.2(c) Lens and schema compatibility.For composition, lens identities must match by hash or by explicit bridge insertion. Schema compatibility is enforced at the COS layer: if an entry expects a specific schema hash for inputs, it must be declared in the contract and verified by schema validation at execution time. Schema mismatch is not repairable by reinterpretation; only explicit transforms may migrate objects between schemas.
11.1.2(d) Supersession and deprecation.Deprecation is represented by explicit metadata objects:
(\mathsf{DeprecatedBy}:\mathsf{entry_id}\to \mathsf{entry_id}'),
(\mathsf{CompatNotes}) as structured fields,both pinned in the commitment store. Supersession is legal only if a refinement or equivalence proof exists showing that the new entry satisfies at least the guarantees required by downstream uses (Chapter 2 and Chapter 8).
11.1.3 Required invariants and witnesses
11.1.3(a) Admission certificate bundle.Admission into the library requires a typed admission bundle:[\mathsf{Admit}:=(h(\mathsf{RefPlan}),h(\mathsf{RefRcpt}),h(\mathsf{TestRun}),h(\mathsf{Claims}),h(\mathsf{Deps}),h(\mathsf{PolicyBind})).]
(h(\mathsf{RefPlan})) is a reference plan that constructs or validates the entry.
(h(\mathsf{RefRcpt})) is a receipt verifying that executing (\mathsf{RefPlan}) succeeded under a reference runtime header and corridor policy.
(h(\mathsf{TestRun})) references evidence that required tests ran and passed (including checker program id and test basis hash).
(h(\mathsf{Claims})) binds the entry’s invariant set, tolerance profile, verification level, and any quotient/loss profile.
(h(\mathsf{Deps})) is the canonical dependency set.
(h(\mathsf{PolicyBind})) binds the admission to specific policy hashes (at minimum canonicalization, object standard, verifier set).
11.1.3(b) Minimal witness discipline.Every entry must declare a complete witness profile:
provenance binding is mandatory for every step,
commitment authorization is mandatory for any commitment store write,
proof witnesses are mandatory for any equivalence, bridge correctness, pruning, or loss/quotient claim,
consistency witnesses are mandatory for contradiction resolution or identity merges,
budget witnesses are mandatory for any escalation beyond corridor caps.An entry that cannot be executed without requiring a witness type not supported by the runtime verifier set is inadmissible.
11.1.3(c) Invariant completeness for entries.An entry’s invariant contract must be complete with respect to its stated semantic role. In particular:
any entry claiming safe composition must declare the convention fingerprints it preserves or changes,
any entry that changes representation must declare what invariants are preserved exactly and what invariants are preserved within tolerance,
any entry that discards information must declare a quotient contract (Chapter 8),
any entry that is invertible must declare inverse existence and provide certificates or proofs for inversion (Chapter 8).
11.1.3(d) Admission-time legality guarantees.Admission guarantees only that the entry is:
well-formed under COS,
executable under the kernel with deterministic replay,
verifiable under the pinned verifier set,
convention-safe as declared by its spec bundle and bridges,
and supported by at least one verified receipt under declared policy bindings.Admission does not assert global mathematical truth beyond the entry’s declared invariants, tolerances, and verification level.
11.1.4 Storage/pinning scheme and aliases
11.1.4(a) Canonical pin namespaces.Library artifacts are stored by pinning entry hashes and aliases in the commitment store under canonical namespaces:
(\mathsf{TL}/\mathsf{LT}/\langle\mathsf{entry_id}\rangle\to h(\mathsf{Entry})),
(\mathsf{TL}/\mathsf{HUB}/\langle\mathsf{name}\rangle\to h(\mathsf{Entry})),
(\mathsf{TL}/\mathsf{BRIDGE}/\langle\mathsf{class}\rangle/\langle\mathsf{entry_id}\rangle\to h(\mathsf{Entry})),
(\mathsf{TL}/\mathsf{NORM}/\langle\mathsf{name}\rangle\to h(\mathsf{Entry})),
(\mathsf{TL}/\mathsf{REPAIR}/\langle\mathsf{name}\rangle\to h(\mathsf{Entry})),where keys obey canonical naming rules (Chapter 6–7).
11.1.4(b) Alias objects.Aliases are explicit typed objects:[\mathsf{Alias}:=(\mathsf{target_pin},h_{\mathsf{target_value}}),]pinned under (\mathsf{TL}/\mathsf{ALIAS}/\langle\mathsf{name}\rangle). Aliases do not change meaning; they provide stable naming for navigation. Any alias mutation is contradiction-controlled and requires consistency proofs under (\Omega).
11.1.4(c) Pinning discipline.Pinning an entry hash into the commitment store is permitted only if:
the entry is well-formed and admissible,
the admission bundle verifies,
any contradiction predicate for the namespace passes or is resolved by a consistency witness,
and the value hash references a canonical object in the proof store.Commit operations are ledgered and receipted like any other commitment.
11.1.4(d) Library integrity constraints.The library commitment store must satisfy:
no two distinct entries share the same alias in a contradiction-controlled namespace without explicit disambiguation,
all pinned entries reference compatible policy hashes (or are explicitly partitioned by policy namespace),
dependency sets of pinned entries resolve to pinned entries (or explicit external commitments),
and the library itself has a library-root hash (Merkle root over pinned entry references) enabling compact commitment to the entire catalog state.
11.2 Hub transforms and connectivity
11.2.1 Definition of hub centrality
11.2.1(a) Transform graph.Define the transform network as a directed multigraph:[G=(\mathcal{V},\mathcal{E}),]where (\mathcal{V}) are lens identifiers and (\mathcal{E}) are transform entries. Each edge (e\in\mathcal{E}) is annotated by:[\mathsf{ann}(e):=(h(\mathsf{SpecIn}),h(\mathsf{SpecOut}),h(\mathcal I),\boldsymbol{\varepsilon},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{W},h(\Pi)).]
11.2.1(b) Hub centrality functional.A hub is an entry selected by a centrality functional combining:
connectivity (in/out degree over admissible edges),
betweenness under the library’s deterministic route cost functional,
strength (verification level and invariant breadth),
invertibility tier (exact inverse, approximate inverse, or quotient-bearing).Centrality is computed deterministically from (G) under fixed policy weights pinned by the library.
11.2.1(c) Hub designation.An entry is designated a hub iff it satisfies:
high centrality score relative to a pinned threshold,
stable convention specification (strict SpecIn/SpecOut),
strong contract discipline (minimal loss; explicit quotient if lossy),
and reusable receipts for its canonical instantiations (Section 11.2.4).
Hub designation is a pinned metadata record; it is not inferred at run time.
11.2.1(d) Hub neighborhood definition.Each hub defines a neighborhood: the set of lenses and transforms that are reachable within a bounded hop count under exact or near-exact constraints. Neighborhoods are used to accelerate routing and to define canonical “fast routes” in compilation.
11.2.2 Hub invariants and stronger contracts
11.2.2(a) Strong contract requirement.Hub entries must satisfy strengthened contract requirements:
explicit convention bundles with no optional fields,
explicit test basis and checker programs at a minimum verification level pinned by library policy,
explicit inverse or explicit quotient/loss profile (no ambiguous middle state),
explicit canonical normalization insertion points associated with the hub.
11.2.2(b) Hub invariant scope.Hubs must declare invariant sets that enable broad composition. At minimum, a hub must declare:
convention preservation or convention mapping discipline,
determinism and replay invariants,
and at least one domain-relevant invariant family (algebraic/analytic/probabilistic/geometric) appropriate to the hub’s domain tag.
11.2.2(c) Strong witness discipline.Hub proofs must be supported by locally verifiable witnesses at or above the library’s required hub verification level. Hub admission requires a reference receipt that includes:
proof witness verification,
and (where required) inverse verification or quotient correctness verification.
11.2.2(d) Hub stability policy.A hub’s admissible parameter domain must be stable: parameter defaults are forbidden; all parameters must be pinned and hashed. Any hub parameter that influences conventions (sign, scale, index, units, branch) must be part of SpecIn/SpecOut rather than left as an untracked parameter degree of freedom.
11.2.3 Hub test bases and verifiers
11.2.3(a) Hub test bundle object.Each hub has a (\mathsf{Tests}) object:[\mathsf{Tests}:=(\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{TB}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(\mathsf{Claims}),h(\mathsf{Coverage})),]binding verification level, test basis, checker program, invariant claims, and (optionally) deterministic coverage parameters.
11.2.3(b) Canonical basis discipline.Hub test bases must be:
finite and explicitly specified or deterministically generated under pinned rules,
sensitive to the hub’s defining equivalences and convention degrees of freedom,
and bounded so that verification is polynomial-time and feasible under corridor budgets.
11.2.3(c) Verifier method registry.Each hub proof method id must be registered in the verifier set and pinned by hash. The method registry binds:
input/output schema requirements,
required local-check objects,
admissible verification levels,
and admissible claim classes.
Any hub entry referencing an unregistered method id is inadmissible.
11.2.3(d) Test adequacy obligations.If a hub claims a property that is not implied by its test regime at the declared verification level, the claim is invalid. The admissible claim boundary is enforced by the proof verifier: claim objects that overstate what the verification level supports are rejected.
11.2.4 Hub reuse and cached receipts
11.2.4(a) Receipt-backed reuse.A hub entry is reusable without recomputation when a downstream plan can reference an upstream verified receipt for the hub instantiation. Reuse is performed by dependency proofs (Chapter 6): the downstream provenance source roots include the upstream hub receipt hash and membership proofs showing the needed hub artifact is included in the upstream receipt’s pin root.
11.2.4(b) Cached hub instantiations.The library may maintain a cache namespace:[\mathsf{TL}/\mathsf{CACHE}/\mathsf{HUB}/\langle\mathsf{hub}\rangle/\langle\mathsf{spec_hash}\rangle/\langle\mathsf{param_hash}\rangle\to h(\mathsf{Rcpt}),]where the cached object is a receipt hash proving the hub instantiation. Cache entries are contradiction-controlled and must be superseded only by refinement (stronger proofs) or by explicit deprecation.
11.2.4(c) Reuse legality.Reuse is legal only if:
the downstream runtime header hashes match the upstream receipt’s policy hashes (or are bridged by explicit policy-bridge receipts if permitted),
the downstream plan’s required invariants and verification levels are satisfied by the upstream receipt’s claim strength,
and the dependency proof verifies membership and provenance binding.
11.2.4(d) Deterministic reuse selection.If multiple cached receipts exist for the same hub instantiation, the library selects deterministically by the strongest-claim preorder:
prefer higher verification level,
prefer smaller tolerance bounds,
prefer larger invariant sets,
tie-break lexicographically by receipt hash.This selection is pinned as a policy; any alternative selection produces a different plan hash.
11.3 Bridge catalog and closure
11.3.1 Minimal bridge sets for conventions
11.3.1(a) Bridge classes.The library defines a finite bridge class set corresponding to convention components:[\mathsf{BridgeClass}\in{\mathsf{SIGN},\mathsf{SCALE},\mathsf{INDEX},\mathsf{UNIT},\mathsf{DOMAIN},\mathsf{GRID}}.]Each class has canonical spec mapping semantics and required proof method families.
11.3.1(b) Minimal bridge basis requirement.For a library to claim convention-closure for a domain tag, it must include at least one admissible bridge entry for each convention component that can differ across admissible transforms in that domain tag. If a component is declared vacuous for a domain tag, that declaration must be explicit and pinned.
11.3.1(c) Bridge specialization by domain tag.Bridge semantics depend on domain tag: index bridges for arrays differ from residue-domain bridges for modular arithmetic; unit bridges differ from scale normalization bridges. Domain tag specialization is encoded in the bridge contract and in the bridge class metadata.
11.3.1(d) Bridge identity and provenance.Each bridge entry must bind:
its SpecIn and SpecOut,
the explicit mapping rule object hash,
and a proof method id validating the mapping.Bridges are not assumed correct; they are admitted only with verified receipts.
11.3.2 Bridge composition and cost
11.3.2(a) Bridge chain.A bridge chain is a sequence of bridge entries whose SpecOut/SpecIn match at each boundary. The chain is admissible only if the compatibility protocol holds (Chapter 7) and each bridge proof verifies under the corridor budgets.
11.3.2(b) Bridge cost functional.Bridge selection uses the same deterministic route cost functional:[\mathrm{Cost} = \alpha(#\text{steps})+\beta(\text{budget})+\gamma(\text{tolerance})+\delta(\text{witness burden}),]with coefficients pinned by library policy. (\gamma) penalizes approximate bridges; (\delta) penalizes high witness burden; (\beta) penalizes expensive or wide-scope bridges.
11.3.2(c) Minimal-bridge property.When repairing convention drift, the repair system must select the minimal-cost bridge chain that maps observed spec to required spec, subject to corridor feasibility and required verification level. Minimality is deterministic; ties are broken lexicographically by bridge entry id sequence.
11.3.2(d) Bridge chain normalization.Bridge chains must be followed by mandatory normalization checkpoints (Chapter 7). The library includes normalizer entries that are designated required post-bridge steps; omission is illegal when required by policy.
11.3.3 Bridge admissibility proofs
11.3.3(a) Bridge proof obligations.Each bridge must provide a proof object certifying:
the mapping between spec fingerprints is correct under declared invariants,
the mapping does not introduce hidden convention changes beyond those declared,
and (if claimed) the bridge is invertible or has an approximate inverse with explicit bounds (Chapter 8).
11.3.3(b) Local-check adequacy.Bridge proof methods must include test bases sensitive to the bridge’s degrees of freedom: sign flips must detect orientation errors; scale bridges must detect normalization discrepancies; index bridges must detect permutation errors; unit bridges must detect conversion errors; domain bridges must detect forbidden-branch changes; grid bridges must detect aliasing and stability violations.
11.3.3(c) Proof strength constraints.Bridge admissibility is conditioned on verification level: a bridge that can affect downstream exactness claims must meet the required verification level threshold pinned by the library. If the bridge is approximate, it must explicitly consume tolerance budgets and be admissible under (\Omega).
11.3.3(d) Bridge audit records.Executing a bridge step must emit evidence objects binding:
observed and required spec hashes,
bridge entry id,
proof hash and verification level,
tolerance consumption (if any).This evidence is required for repair audit chains.
11.3.4 Closure conditions for composition
11.3.4(a) Convention closure.A library is convention-closed for a domain tag iff for any pair of admissible transforms (\mathsf{LT}_1,\mathsf{LT}_2) with compatible lenses, whenever (\mathsf{SpecOut}(\mathsf{LT}_1)\neq \mathsf{SpecIn}(\mathsf{LT}_2)), there exists an admissible bridge chain mapping (\mathsf{SpecOut}(\mathsf{LT}_1)\to \mathsf{SpecIn}(\mathsf{LT}_2)) under the library’s policies.
11.3.4(b) Witness closure.The library is witness-closed iff all witness types and method ids required by any entry’s witness profile are present in the verifier set and pinned by the runtime header. Any entry requiring an unsupported witness is inadmissible; therefore closure is checked by validating witness profiles at admission time.
11.3.4(c) Replay closure.Replay closure requires that composing entries yields replay-associative behavior under pinned canonicalization. If two composition routes yield the same semantic carrier map but different replay artifacts, they are not equal in the system unless a certified rewrite proof establishes equivalence under declared invariants and verification level.
11.3.4(d) Corridor closure.Closure is relative to corridor policy: a route may exist in the library but be infeasible under a given (\Omega) due to budgets, caps, or scope restrictions. Therefore closure claims must be parameterized by a corridor class (\Omega_{\mathrm{class}}) specified as a pinned policy subset; compilation treats (\Omega) feasibility as part of route admissibility.
11.4 Extension transforms and domain plugins
11.4.1 Domain-specific lens families
11.4.1(a) Lens family definition.A domain plugin introduces new lens families (L=(D,\mathcal A,\mathcal I)) with:
carrier domains (D) defined as typed COS object families,
address algebras (\mathcal A) pinned by hash (indices, order, encoding),
invariant schemas (\mathcal I) declared explicitly and compatible with Chapter 2.
Lens families must declare convention bundles (Chapter 7) and canonical normal forms (Chapter 7) for their object families.
11.4.1(b) Lens admissibility predicates.Each new lens must include admissibility predicates (\mathsf{Adm}_L) pinned by hash. Admissibility must be deterministic and must not depend on external data not committed by hash.
11.4.1(c) Inter-lens compatibility.If a plugin introduces multiple lenses intended to be connected by transforms, it must also provide:
convention specs for each lens,
and bridge/normalizer entries where convention mismatches can arise.
11.4.1(d) Integration into transform graph.New lenses become nodes in the transform graph (G). Plugin transforms become edges annotated with SpecIn/SpecOut, invariants, budgets, witnesses, replay profiles, and admission receipts. Routing becomes possible only when entries are admitted under the library closure conditions.
11.4.2 Domain-specific invariants
11.4.2(a) Invariant registry extension.A plugin may extend the invariant registry by introducing new invariant identifiers and evaluators. Each invariant evaluator must:
be deterministic,
be defined via observable interfaces,
return quantized values under pinned numeric policy,
and be applicable only under explicitly declared conditions.
11.4.2(b) Invariant interaction with conventions.Domain-specific invariants must declare which convention components they depend on. If an invariant’s evaluation changes under sign/scale/index/unit/domain modifications, then bridges affecting those components must include proof obligations preserving that invariant (or must declare it dropped).
11.4.2(c) Invariant accumulation operators.If a plugin introduces approximate transforms consuming tolerances for new invariants, it must provide pinned accumulation operators (\oplus_I) for those invariants, so that tolerance ledgers (Chapter 3) remain well-defined and deterministic.
11.4.2(d) Hard vs soft invariant classification.The corridor policy (\Omega) classifies invariants as hard or soft. Plugins must specify default classifications for their invariants, but corridor policy may override classification in a controlled, pinned manner. Any change in classification affecting legality must be reflected in policy hashes and thus in receipts.
11.4.3 Domain-specific proof methods
11.4.3(a) Method id admission.A plugin may introduce new proof methods by adding method ids to the verifier registry. Each method id must specify:
required input/output object schemas,
required local-check data schema,
checker program id and implementation digest,
admissible verification levels and claim classes,
and polynomial-time verification guarantees relative to local-check size.
11.4.3(b) Local-check completeness.A proof method is admissible only if it provides sufficient local-check data to verify the declared claim boundary. Claims must not exceed what the verification level supports. Any method that requires unbounded search is inadmissible under the witness system.
11.4.3(c) Method compatibility with privacy modes.Methods must declare which data must be disclosed for verification (hash-only vs selective reveal). If a method cannot be verified without revealing private payload bytes, that boundary must be explicit in the method policy and in receipts.
11.4.3(d) Method regression and supersession.If a method id is updated, it must be versioned and pinned. Existing proofs remain bound to old method digests; they are not implicitly upgraded. Supersession requires explicit proof regeneration steps and receipts.
11.4.4 Integration constraints and testing
11.4.4(a) Integration constraints.A plugin is integrated only if:
all object schemas conform to COS encoding rules (Chapter 6),
all convention specs conform to Chapter 7 and have admissible bridges if needed,
all transforms have legal contracts and executable programs,
all required witnesses are supported by the runtime verifier set,
all entries have admission receipts under the target runtime header hashes.
11.4.4(b) Deterministic test suites.Plugins must provide deterministic test suites as plan objects and receipts:
schema validation tests,
convention-compatibility tests,
proof method verification tests,
replay determinism tests,
and representative transform composition tests (including bridge insertion and normalization points).Tests are not informal; they are executions with receipts.
11.4.4(c) Compatibility regression discipline.Any plugin update must re-run its admission tests and produce new admission receipts. Compatibility claims must be expressed as explicit supersession proofs or as explicit incompatibility markers in metadata. Silent breakage is prohibited by the library pinning discipline.
11.4.4(d) Library-root auditability.The entire library state, including plugin entries, must be commit-able as a library-root object:
a Merkle root over pinned entry references,
a hash over the verifier registry,
a hash over canonicalization policy,
and a hash over corridor class constraints.This enables audit and reproducible reconstruction of the transform catalog state used by any plan and receipt.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 11 — TRANSFORM LIBRARY: CATALOG, HUBS, AND EXTENSIONS
CHAPTER 12 — COMPILATION: FROM INTENT TO CERTIFIED ROUTES (Final Draft)
This chapter specifies the deterministic compilation pipeline that converts a human intent into a certified execution plan. Compilation is not advisory: it is a formal transformation from text to a typed goal specification, to a feasibility-checked route through the transform library, to an executable micro-program, to an explicit witness plan and ledger plan. Compilation is correct only if the resulting plan is executable under the corridor policy (\Omega), convention-safe under the normalization discipline, and replay-verifiable under the execution kernel and witness system.
12.1 Intent normalization
12.1.1 Goal types (pin/edge/proof/route/eval)
12.1.1(a) GoalType enum.Define a pinned finite enum (\mathsf{GoalType}) with canonical tags:
(\mathsf{PIN}): commit a new artifact (or update an admissible existing artifact) in the commitment store.
