(\mathsf{EDGE}): commit a relation structure in the relation store (optionally accompanied by supporting commitments).
(\mathsf{PROOF}): produce a proof object (and optionally pin it).
(\mathsf{ROUTE}): produce a route plan artifact (a compiled plan without executing it, unless explicitly requested).
(\mathsf{EVAL}): evaluate an input payload through a route and produce specified output artifacts (pins/proofs/edges).
Goal types are disjoint; any request that ambiguously spans multiple goal types must be normalized into a canonical primary goal type plus explicit secondary targets, or rejected as ill-typed.
12.1.1(b) Goal object schema.A normalized goal is a COS object:[\mathsf{Goal}:=(\mathsf{goal_type},\ \mathsf{obj_type},\ h(\mathsf{Targets}),\ h(\mathsf{Context}),\ h(\mathsf{Constraints})),]where:
(\mathsf{goal_type}\in\mathsf{GoalType}),
(\mathsf{obj_type}) is a canonical object-type token (Section 12.1.2),
(\mathsf{Targets}) encodes required outputs (pin ids, edge targets, proof ids),
(\mathsf{Context}) encodes domain parameters and referenced artifacts,
(\mathsf{Constraints}) encodes invariants, tolerances, determinism, budgets, and policy overrides.
12.1.1(c) Canonical target specification.Targets are encoded as a set-like object:[\mathsf{Targets}:={\mathsf{TargetRec}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{TargetRec}_m},]each (\mathsf{TargetRec}) being a typed record:[\mathsf{TargetRec}:=(\mathsf{target_kind},\ \mathsf{id},\ \mathsf{schema_hash}?,\ \mathsf{namespace}?),]where (\mathsf{target_kind}\in{\mathsf{PIN},\mathsf{EDGE},\mathsf{PROOF},\mathsf{RECEIPT}}), (\mathsf{id}) is a canonical identifier (pin id bytes, edge id bytes, or proof hash), and (\mathsf{schema_hash}) is included when the target requires a specific schema. Targets must be canonicalized and sorted by ((\mathsf{target_kind},\mathsf{id})).
12.1.1(d) Goal legality constraints.A goal is admissible for compilation only if:
all target identifiers are canonical and belong to allowed namespaces under (\Omega),
any required schema hashes resolve to known schemas under the object standard,
required proof or receipt targets are compatible with disclosure constraints (if privacy modes are active),
the goal does not request forbidden actions (e.g., committing contradictions without explicit resolution targets).
12.1.2 Object types and schema inference
12.1.2(a) ObjectType tokenization.Define (\mathsf{ObjType}) as a pinned namespace of canonical tokens identifying object families to be produced or consumed (signals, operators, proofs, plans, receipts, etc.). Each (\mathsf{ObjType}) token binds:
expected output schema hash(es),
admissible input lenses (or admissible start lens inference rules),
required convention bundles (spec fingerprints),
required witness method families and minimum verification level.
12.1.2(b) Schema inference function.Compilation includes a deterministic schema inference function:[\mathsf{InferSchema}:\ (\mathsf{goal_type},\ \mathsf{obj_type},\ \mathsf{Context})\to h(\mathsf{SchemaReq}),]where (\mathsf{SchemaReq}) is a typed object listing required schemas for outputs and (when applicable) required schemas for inputs. (\mathsf{InferSchema}) is deterministic and pinned by hash; no heuristic or model-dependent inference is permitted.
12.1.2(c) Object-type disambiguation.If multiple (\mathsf{ObjType}) tokens are consistent with the input text, disambiguation proceeds deterministically by:
exact token match precedence (explicit canonical names in text),
then minimal additional context requirements,
then minimal route cost under default constraints,
then lexicographic tie-break on (\mathsf{ObjType}) token bytes.
The chosen (\mathsf{ObjType}) is committed in the normalized goal object and thus in the plan hash.
12.1.2(d) Object-type admissibility predicates.Each (\mathsf{ObjType}) declares admissibility predicates on context and constraints (e.g., required parameters present, required domain constraints satisfied). Compilation must evaluate these predicates deterministically; failure produces a typed error goal artifact and prevents route construction.
12.1.3 Context extraction and hashing
12.1.3(a) Context object schema.A context is a typed COS object:[\mathsf{Context}:=(h(\mathsf{Bindings}),h(\mathsf{Refs}),h(\mathsf{Specs}),h(\mathsf{Defaults})),]where:
(\mathsf{Bindings}) is a canonical map from keys to typed values or object hashes (e.g., grid sizes, moduli, domain parameters),
(\mathsf{Refs}) is a canonical set of referenced artifacts (pin ids and object hashes),
(\mathsf{Specs}) is the convention bundle required or implied (spec hashes),
(\mathsf{Defaults}) records explicitly applied defaults (not implicit).
All keys must be canonical; all referenced hashes must resolve or be declared as external commitments by hash.
12.1.3(b) Deterministic extraction function.Compilation includes a deterministic extraction function:[\mathsf{ExtractContext}:\ \text{Text}\to \mathsf{Context},]implemented by a pinned parser and a pinned normalization dictionary mapping synonyms to canonical keys and tokens. Extraction must be purely functional: identical input text yields identical (\mathsf{Context}) bytes.
12.1.3(c) Context hashing.The context hash is:[h(\mathsf{Context}) := H(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{Context})).]All compilation decisions—route selection, parameter binding, witness plan selection—must be functions of (h(\mathsf{Context})) and other pinned policy hashes. No uncommitted state may influence compilation.
12.1.3(d) Reference resolution policy.If the context references pin ids, compilation must resolve them deterministically via commitment store lookup:
(\mathsf{GET_PIN}(\mathsf{pin_id})) returns a pin record or null.If required references are missing, compilation must either:
include explicit prerequisite steps (e.g., compile a plan to construct missing artifacts), or
reject as infeasible under the goal and constraints.Prerequisite selection is deterministic and committed in the output plan.
12.1.4 Constraint normalization and defaults
12.1.4(a) Constraints object schema.Constraints are a typed COS object:[\mathsf{Constraints}:=(h(\mathcal I_{\mathrm{req}}),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\max}),h(\Omega{\mathrm{req}}),h(\mathsf{DetReq}),h(\mathsf{PolicyOverrides}),h(\mathsf{PrivacyMode})).]
(\mathcal I_{\mathrm{req}}) is the required invariant set,
(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max}) is the maximum allowable tolerance profile,
(\Omega_{\mathrm{req}}) encodes corridor requirements (budgets, caps, scope),
(\mathsf{DetReq}) encodes determinism strictness (e.g., forbidding pseudo-random tests),
(\mathsf{PolicyOverrides}) is a canonical set of allowed overrides (if any),
(\mathsf{PrivacyMode}) encodes disclosure requirements (hash-only vs selective reveal).
12.1.4(b) Defaulting discipline.Defaults are never implicit. If the input does not specify a constraint field, compilation must insert an explicit default object by hash from a pinned default registry keyed by ((\mathsf{GoalType},\mathsf{ObjType})). The default registry itself is pinned by hash, and the specific default objects applied are recorded in (\mathsf{Context}.\mathsf{Defaults}) so the plan hash reflects default choices.
12.1.4(c) Constraint feasibility precheck.Before route construction, compilation performs feasibility prechecks:
required invariants (\mathcal I_{\mathrm{req}}) are supported by at least one route in the library graph for the inferred start/goal lenses,
required verification level is supported by available proof methods,
(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max}) is compatible with corridor tolerance budgets,
scope requirements do not forbid necessary commitments for the goal type.
Failure yields a typed compile-failure artifact with evidence.
12.1.4(d) Constraint canonicalization.Constraints are canonicalized by:
sorting invariant identifiers,
canonical encoding of tolerances in pinned quantized units,
canonical encoding of corridor policy requirements,
canonical ordering of override permissions.Two constraints objects are equal iff their canonical bytes are equal.
12.2 Route graph construction
12.2.1 Nodes (lenses, pinned artifacts)
12.2.1(a) Node types.Route construction operates over a directed multigraph (G=(\mathcal{V},\mathcal{E})) with node set (\mathcal{V}) partitioned into:
Lens nodes: each node is a lens hash (h(L)).
Artifact nodes: each node is a pinned artifact identifier (pin id) or an object hash representing a target artifact class.
Meta nodes: optional nodes representing goal endpoints (e.g., “commit target pin exists”), used for compilation convenience but canonically encoded.
Node identity is canonical and hash-addressable.
12.2.1(b) Node availability.Lens nodes are available if the lens object resolves by hash in the library or is defined by a domain plugin admitted into the library. Artifact nodes are available if:
the pin id exists in the commitment store, or
the plan includes prerequisite steps to construct it, or
the goal explicitly targets creating it.
12.2.1(c) Start and goal node inference.Compilation deterministically selects start and goal nodes:
Start lens node (v_s) is inferred from the current payload’s declared lens or from context tokens.
Goal node (v_g) is inferred from (\mathsf{ObjType}) and (\mathsf{GoalType}) and typically includes a commitment pin target or a proof/receipt target.
Inference rules are pinned and deterministic; the inferred node ids are stored in the plan object.
12.2.1(d) Node annotations.Nodes may be annotated with:
required spec fingerprints for admissible traversal,
required schemas,
and admissibility predicates.Annotations are derived from pinned objects and are deterministic functions of context and constraints.
12.2.2 Edges (transforms, bridges)
12.2.2(a) Edge definition.Each edge (e\in\mathcal{E}) is a transform entry id (\mathsf{entry_id}) referencing a library entry (\mathsf{Entry}) (Chapter 11). The directed endpoints of (e) are:
the input lens node (h(L_{\mathrm{in}})),
the output lens node (h(L_{\mathrm{out}})),as declared by the entry’s legal transform contract.
12.2.2(b) Bridge edges.Bridge entries (Chapter 10–11) are edges whose primary purpose is convention alignment. Bridges are included in (\mathcal{E}) and treated uniformly by routing, with their own costs, witness requirements, and tolerance consumption.
12.2.2(c) Artifact edges.Some edges may target artifact nodes rather than lens nodes (e.g., “pin this hub entry,” “emit receipt object,” “construct plan object”). These are represented as transforms whose output lens is a commitment/proof/plan artifact lens; the artifact lens is a lens node in (\mathcal{V}), ensuring uniform typing.
12.2.2(d) Edge availability and admission.An edge is available only if its entry’s admission bundle verifies under the current runtime header hashes and the corridor policy class required by the entry. Non-admitted entries are excluded from the graph; compilation never routes through non-admitted edges.
12.2.3 Edge annotations (cost, witnesses, tolerances)
12.2.3(a) Annotation schema.Each edge carries a deterministic annotation record:[\mathsf{Ann}(e):=(h(\mathsf{SpecIn}),h(\mathsf{SpecOut}),h(\mathcal I_e),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_e),h(\mathcal{B}_e),h(\mathcal{S}_e),h(\mathcal{W}_e),h(\mathcal{R}_e),h(\Pi_e),h(\mathsf{Tests}_e)).]
12.2.3(b) Edge cost model.Compilation defines an edge cost tuple:[\mathsf{Cost}(e):=(\varepsilon\text{-class},\ c_{\mathsf{hop}},\ c_{\mathsf{budget}},\ c_{\mathsf{wit}},\ c_{\mathsf{write}}),]where:
(\varepsilon\text{-class}) is a categorical flag indicating whether the edge is exact ((\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_e=\mathbf{0})) or approximate ((\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_e\neq\mathbf{0})),
(c_{\mathsf{hop}}=1),
(c_{\mathsf{budget}}) is a deterministic scalar computed from the edge budget profile and corridor cost weights,
(c_{\mathsf{wit}}) is a deterministic scalar computed from witness burden (counts and weights by witness type, with proof witnesses weighted highest),
(c_{\mathsf{write}}) penalizes protected commitments, with higher penalties for writing contradiction-controlled namespaces.
Cost weights are pinned by the routing policy object and thus fixed for canonical compilation.
12.2.3(c) Tolerance consumption annotation.The tolerance vector (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_e) is per-invariant. The routing cost includes a tolerance penalty computed deterministically from (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}e) and (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\max}) from constraints. Exactness preference is enforced lexicographically: exact routes dominate approximate routes unless the goal explicitly permits approximation and no exact route exists.
12.2.3(d) Witness feasibility annotation.Witness feasibility for an edge is determined by whether all witness types and method ids required by (h(\mathcal{W}_e)) exist in the runtime verifier set and satisfy minimum verification level constraints. Edges requiring unavailable methods are removed during feasibility pruning.
12.2.4 Feasibility pruning under (\Omega)
12.2.4(a) Corridor feasibility pruning.Given corridor policy (\Omega) and constraints (\mathsf{Constraints}), define feasibility predicates:
(\mathsf{BudgetOK}(e,\Omega)): edge budgets do not exceed corridor maxima and can be satisfied without forbidden escalation.
(\mathsf{ScopeOK}(e,\Omega)): edge scope does not require forbidden write targets.
(\mathsf{TolOK}(e,\Omega)): edge tolerance consumption is compatible with corridor tolerance budgets for required invariants.
(\mathsf{DetOK}(e,\Omega)): edge replay profile and canonicalization requirements are compatible with the runtime header.
Edges failing any hard feasibility predicate are removed.
12.2.4(b) Spec compatibility feasibility.Edges that require spec inputs are feasible from a node only if:
the node’s outgoing spec bundle matches the edge’s (\mathsf{SpecIn}), or
a bridge chain exists that maps the node’s spec to the edge’s (\mathsf{SpecIn}) within feasibility constraints.
Bridge availability is checked by search in the bridge subgraph and must respect (\Omega) budgets and (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max}).
12.2.4(c) Required-invariant feasibility.If the goal requires an invariant set (\mathcal I_{\mathrm{req}}), compilation prunes edges whose invariant contracts cannot support those invariants along any route segment that reaches the goal. Specifically, if an edge drops a required invariant via a loss/quotient profile and no recovery is permitted, any route containing that edge is inadmissible.
12.2.4(d) Partiality feasibility.If an edge is partial, feasibility requires that its domain predicate be satisfiable under the known context and constraints, or that the plan includes explicit prerequisite transforms to enforce the domain predicate (e.g., domain restriction transforms or canonical projections). If no such enforcement exists, the edge is treated as infeasible.
12.3 Deterministic shortest-legal path selection
12.3.1 Cost functional definition
12.3.1(a) Route cost as lexicographic tuple.For a route (r=(e_1,\ldots,e_k)), define cumulative properties:
hop count (k),
cumulative budget cost (C_{\mathsf{budget}}=\sum_i c_{\mathsf{budget}}(e_i)),
cumulative witness burden (C_{\mathsf{wit}}=\sum_i c_{\mathsf{wit}}(e_i)),
cumulative write penalty (C_{\mathsf{write}}=\sum_i c_{\mathsf{write}}(e_i)),
cumulative tolerance usage (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}}=\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{e_1}\oplus\cdots\oplus \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{e_k}) (per-invariant accumulation operators pinned).
Define the route cost as a lexicographic tuple:[\mathsf{RCost}(r):=(\varepsilon\text{-priority},\ C_{\varepsilon},\ k,\ C_{\mathsf{budget}},\ C_{\mathsf{wit}},\ C_{\mathsf{write}},\ \mathsf{LexKey}(r)),]where:
(\varepsilon\text{-priority}=0) if (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}}=\mathbf{0}) on required invariants, else (1),
(C_{\varepsilon}) is a deterministic scalar penalty computed from (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}}) relative to (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max}),
(\mathsf{LexKey}(r)) is the canonical lexicographic key of the route: the byte sequence of edge ids and parameter-binding placeholders (defined in 12.3.4).
This tuple is minimized lexicographically; this enforces exactness preference, then minimal tolerance, then minimal hop count, then minimal budgets and witness burden.
12.3.1(b) Feasibility constraints.A route (r) is admissible only if:
every edge is feasible under (\Omega),
all spec compatibilities are satisfied, with explicit bridges included in the route,
cumulative tolerance usage satisfies corridor budgets and (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max}),
required witnesses for all edges are supported by the verifier set,
route reaches the goal node and satisfies goal constraints.
Feasibility is a hard predicate; the cost functional applies only among feasible routes.
12.3.1(c) Deterministic shortest-path algorithm.Route selection is performed by a deterministic shortest-path algorithm over (G) using (\mathsf{RCost}) as the path ordering. The algorithm must:
compute costs deterministically,
apply a deterministic priority queue ordering by (\mathsf{RCost}),
record predecessor pointers deterministically (tie-break rules fixed),
and return the lexicographically earliest minimum-cost route.
Any nondeterminism in path selection violates determinism policy and invalidates the plan.
12.3.1(d) Route admissibility certificate.Compilation produces a route admissibility evidence object containing:
the selected route edge id list,
the computed cumulative cost tuple,
the feasibility predicates satisfied,
and the policy hashes used for computation.This object is stored in the proof store and referenced by the plan, enabling deterministic explanation of route choice.
12.3.2 Exactness preference and tolerance penalties
12.3.2(a) Exactness precedence.Exactness is a categorical preference: routes with (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}}=\mathbf{0}) on required invariants are strictly preferred over any route with nonzero tolerance usage, regardless of hop count or budget, unless the constraints explicitly permit approximation and an exact route does not exist.
12.3.2(b) Per-invariant tolerance penalties.If approximation is permitted, tolerance penalties are computed per invariant:[C_{\varepsilon}:=\sum_{I\in\mathcal I_{\mathrm{req}}} w_I \cdot \phi_I!\left(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_I,\varepsilon^{\max}_I\right),]where (w_I) are pinned weights and (\phi_I) is a pinned monotone penalty function (e.g., (\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_I/\varepsilon^{\max}_I) or a convex penalty). Penalty functions and weights are pinned by routing policy; no ad hoc penalties are permitted.
12.3.2(c) Tolerance feasibility gating.Any route with (\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_I>\varepsilon^{\max}_I) for any required invariant (I) is infeasible. Routes approaching near-threshold may be feasible, but the compiler must insert mandatory normalization checkpoints if required by (\Omega)’s mitigation policies.
12.3.2(d) Tolerance-aware bridge selection.Bridge chains are selected with tolerance awareness: if two bridge chains both align conventions but one introduces nonzero tolerance usage (approximate bridge) and the other is exact, the exact chain is strictly preferred. If only approximate bridge chains exist, their tolerance usage contributes to route tolerance accumulation and may render the route infeasible.
12.3.3 Witness-load penalties
12.3.3(a) Witness burden measure.Witness burden for an edge (e) is computed deterministically from its witness profile:[c_{\mathsf{wit}}(e):=\sum_{\tau\in\mathsf{WTypes}(e)} \omega_\tau \cdot n_\tau(e),]where:
(n_\tau(e)) is the required count (or minimum occurrence count) of witness type (\tau) for executing (e),
(\omega_\tau) is a pinned weight (proof witnesses typically highest; provenance mandatory but lower weight),
witness method ids may add additional fixed costs depending on verification level.
12.3.3(b) Proof-strength constraints.If a goal requires a minimum verification level (\mathsf{VL}{\min}), edges whose proof methods do not support (\mathsf{VL}{\min}) are infeasible. Among feasible edges, those achieving higher verification level may be preferred or penalized depending on policy; the preference is pinned. Canonical policy prefers meeting (\mathsf{VL}_{\min}) with minimal witness burden unless stronger proofs are required for long-chain stability.
12.3.3(c) Commitment witness penalties.Commitment writes to contradiction-controlled namespaces impose additional witness burden via consistency proofs. The cost functional includes a commitment write penalty; thus the compiler prefers routes that reuse existing pinned artifacts and receipts over routes that emit new commitments, when both satisfy the goal.
12.3.3(d) Witness-driven route pruning.If the corridor policy (\Omega) caps total witness burden per plan segment or limits proof-store write budgets, the compiler must prune routes exceeding those limits. This integrates witness feasibility into corridor feasibility deterministically.
12.3.4 Lexicographic tie-break rules
12.3.4(a) Route lexicographic key.Define the lexicographic key of a route (r=(e_1,\ldots,e_k)) as:[\mathsf{LexKey}(r):=\mathsf{Enc}\big([(\mathsf{entry_id}(e_1),h(\Pi_{e_1})),\ldots,(\mathsf{entry_id}(e_k),h(\Pi_{e_k}))]\big),]where (h(\Pi_{e_i})) is the canonical program hash of the entry, and the list is sequence-like in route order. This key is a byte string used only for tie-breaking among equal-cost routes; it must be deterministic and depends only on pinned objects.
12.3.4(b) Deterministic predecessor selection.In the shortest-path algorithm, if two predecessor candidates yield identical (\mathsf{RCost}), the predecessor producing smaller (\mathsf{LexKey}) is chosen. This ensures stable plan construction and stable plan hashes across implementations.
12.3.4(c) Bridge insertion tie-breaks.When multiple bridge chains yield identical cost, ties are broken lexicographically by the sequence of bridge entry ids. When multiple normalization insertion variants satisfy policy equally, ties are broken lexicographically by normalizer entry id sequence. No randomness is permitted.
12.3.4(d) Deterministic failure tie-breaks.If no feasible route exists, compilation must return a canonical failure artifact whose reported “closest” failure reason is deterministic: choose the lexicographically earliest minimal infeasibility certificate among candidate routes under a pinned ordering of defect classes and boundary locations. This yields reproducible failure diagnostics.
12.4 Output plans
12.4.1 Program emission (micro-program)
12.4.1(a) Program construction.Given a selected route (r=(e_1,\ldots,e_k)), compilation emits a micro-program (\Pi) by concatenating each entry’s canonical program template (\Pi_{e_i}) and binding parameters for each step:[\Pi := \Pi_{e_1}\ \Vert\ \Pi_{e_2}\ \Vert\ \cdots\ \Vert\ \Pi_{e_k},]where (\Vert) is concatenation of sequence-like program objects. Any mandatory bridges and normalization steps are included as explicit edges in (r) and thus appear in (\Pi).
12.4.1(b) Parameter binding for each step.Each program step has an associated parameter map (P_t) derived deterministically from:
the global context (\mathsf{Context}),
the constraints (\mathsf{Constraints}),
entry-specific required parameter schema,
and any derived parameters (e.g., spec hashes, quantization policy hashes) required for determinism.Parameter maps are canonically encoded and hashed to (h(P_t)). The program is encoded as a sequence of ((\mathsf{op_id}_t,h(P_t))) pairs.
12.4.1(c) Normalization insertion during emission.Normalization insertion points mandated by policy (Chapter 7) are enforced at compile time by inserting explicit normalizer entries into the route. The emitted program therefore contains normalization steps explicitly; no implicit runtime normalization is permitted unless it is a mandatory mitigation step whose insertion is itself pinned by (\Omega) and referenced in the ledger plan.
12.4.1(d) Program feasibility certificate.Compilation emits a program feasibility evidence object binding:
program hash (h(\Pi)),
the route edge sequence,
the cumulative cost tuple,
and the feasibility predicates satisfied under (\Omega).This evidence object is stored and referenced by the plan.
12.4.2 Witness plan emission
12.4.2(a) Witness plan schema.A witness plan (\mathsf{WPlan}) is a COS object:[\mathsf{WPlan}:=[\mathsf{WReq}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{WReq}_n],]where each (\mathsf{WReq}_t) is:[\mathsf{WReq}_t := (\mathsf{step_index},\mathsf{op_id},h(P_t),\mathsf{req_types},\mathsf{req_methods},\mathsf{req_lcd},\mathsf{prov_mandatory}),]with:
(\mathsf{req_types}) a bitset over witness types,
(\mathsf{req_methods}) a canonical set of method ids required (for proof witnesses),
(\mathsf{req_lcd}) a canonical set of local-check data hashes required,
(\mathsf{prov_mandatory}=\mathsf{true}) for all steps.
12.4.2(b) Witness obligations derivation.Witness obligations are derived deterministically from:
each entry’s witness profile,
corridor policy mandatory witness requirements (contradiction resolution, budget escalation),
and any normalization or bridge steps inserted.Witness plan derivation does not depend on execution-time choices.
12.4.2(c) Optional vs required witnesses.The witness plan distinguishes:
required witnesses (absence is hard failure),
conditional witnesses (required only if a predicate triggers, e.g., contradiction resolution required only if contradiction predicate is true),
and optional evidence witnesses (emitted for audit, but not required unless (\Omega) mandates evidence retention).Conditional witness predicates are pinned and must be replay-deterministic.
12.4.2(d) Witness plan correctness.A plan is well-formed only if every step’s required witness types are supported by the runtime verifier set and the required verification level constraints are satisfiable. Otherwise, compilation must reject the plan as infeasible.
12.4.3 Ledger plan emission
12.4.3(a) Ledger plan schema.A ledger plan (\mathsf{LPlan}) is a COS object:[\mathsf{LPlan}:=[\mathsf{LStep}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{LStep}_n],]where each (\mathsf{LStep}_t) specifies:[\mathsf{LStep}_t := (\mathsf{step_index},\mathsf{op_id},h(P_t),\mathsf{primary_target},\mathsf{allowed_secondary},\mathsf{detectors},\mathsf{partial_policy},\mathsf{budget_caps}).]
(\mathsf{primary_target}\in\mathsf{Target}) is derived from the entry contract.
(\mathsf{allowed_secondary}) is a canonical set of permitted secondary targets, witness-gated.
(\mathsf{detectors}) is the canonical set of detector ids to run at each phase (pre-apply, pre-project, project-time, post-commit), pinned and ordered.
(\mathsf{partial_policy}) declares whether partial acceptance is permitted and in what form (e.g., candidate truncation allowed; commitment partial acceptance forbidden in contradiction-controlled namespaces).
(\mathsf{budget_caps}) binds per-step caps derived from (\Omega) and entry budgets.
12.4.3(b) Detector schedule integration.The ledger plan encodes the mandatory detector schedule (Chapter 9). Detector ids and their order are pinned; each step record must include evidence hashes when detectors trigger evidence emission under (\Omega). The ledger plan therefore precommits the enforcement regime.
12.4.3(c) Partial acceptance semantics commitment.If partial acceptance is permitted, the ledger plan must specify:
deterministic truncation comparator ids,
cap values,
and mask encoding policies.This ensures that partial acceptance outcomes are replay-deterministic and cannot be altered by implementation choices.
12.4.3(d) Ledger plan feasibility.The ledger plan is feasible only if:
all detector computations are within corridor compute budgets (or admissible escalations are planned),
all required evidence retention writes are within write budgets,
and the step count and expansion caps are within corridor limits.Otherwise, compilation must reject the plan.
12.4.4 Plan hashing and pinning
12.4.4(a) Plan object and plan hash.The compiled plan is a COS object:[\mathsf{Plan}:=(h(\mathsf{Goal}),h(\mathsf{Context}),h(\mathsf{Constraints}),h(\mathsf{Graph}),h(\mathsf{Route}),h(\Pi),h(\mathsf{WPlan}),h(\mathsf{LPlan}),h_{\Omega},h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}).]The plan hash is (h(\mathsf{Plan})) under canonical encoding (Chapter 6). Identical inputs and policies yield identical plan hashes.
12.4.4(b) Route object.The route object (\mathsf{Route}) is a sequence-like list of entry ids and any required bridge/normalizer entry ids, in execution order:[\mathsf{Route}:=[\mathsf{entry_id}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{entry_id}_k].]Route equality is definitional equality of canonical bytes; any equivalence between different routes must be established by certified rewrite proofs.
12.4.4(c) Pinning policy for plans.Plans may be pinned as commitments under a canonical namespace:[\mathsf{PLAN}/\langle h(\mathsf{Plan})\rangle \to h(\mathsf{Plan}),]subject to corridor scope and write budgets. Pinning a plan requires a commitment witness; the plan hash must resolve to a well-formed plan object in the proof store. Plan pinning may be optional under (\Omega), but if a plan is used as a dependency by later steps, its hash must be committed or otherwise available via source root commitments.
12.4.4(d) Compilation correctness condition.Compilation is correct only if:
(\mathsf{Plan}) is well-formed under COS,
the route is feasible under (\Omega) and compatibility protocol,
the witness plan and ledger plan are complete and supported by the verifier set,
executing (\Pi) under the kernel from the declared start checkpoint will either (i) produce the target artifacts with a valid receipt, or (ii) fail with a deterministic defect evidence chain consistent with the ledger plan.This correctness condition is mechanical: it refers only to hashed artifacts, pinned policies, and deterministic execution semantics.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 12 — COMPILATION: FROM INTENT TO CERTIFIED ROUTES
CHAPTER 13 — EXECUTION PROTOCOL: RUN, LOG, VERIFY
This chapter specifies the end-to-end execution protocol that (i) runs a compiled plan step-by-step under the execution kernel and corridor policy, (ii) produces an append-only ledger whose records are canonically encoded and hash-chained, (iii) defines the replay verifier that independently re-derives post-state hashes and validates witness obligations, and (iv) defines compact verification receipts that enable third parties to verify outcomes mechanically with minimal disclosure.
13.1 Runtime loop specification
13.1.1 Step execution order
13.1.1(a) Inputs and outputs of execution.Execution consumes:
a compiled plan object (\mathsf{Plan}) with components ((\Pi,\mathsf{WPlan},\mathsf{LPlan})),
an initial checkpoint (\mathsf{Chk}_0) whose state hash (h(\Psi_0)) is consistent with the runtime header hashes,
a corridor policy (\Omega) bound by (h_\Omega) in the runtime header.
Execution produces:
a ledger (\mathsf{Ledger}) consisting of a header (\mathsf{LedHdr}) and a sequence of step records (\mathsf{StepRec}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{StepRec}_n),
a final checkpoint (\mathsf{Chk}_n),
a receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}) binding the plan, ledger header, final state, and output commitments.
13.1.1(b) Program-induced step descriptors.Let the compiled micro-program be:[\Pi := [(\mathsf{op_id}1,h{P_1}),\ldots,(\mathsf{op_id}n,h{P_n})],]canonically encoded as a COS program object. For each index (t\in{1,\ldots,n}), the runtime constructs a step descriptor:[\mathsf{StepDesc}t := (\mathsf{step_index}=t,\ \mathsf{op_id}t,\ h{P_t},\ h{\Gamma_t},\ \lambda_t),]where (h_{\Gamma_t}) is a context hash (either plan-global or step-specific as specified by (\mathsf{Plan})) and (\lambda_t) is the declared expansion depth (pinned by the plan and bounded by (\Omega)).
13.1.1(c) Execution order and single-authoritative transition function.The runtime loop is the unique legal execution order:
(\mathsf{Expand}(\Psi_{t-1},\mathsf{StepDesc}_t,\Omega)\to \mathsf{WS}_t),
(\mathsf{Apply}(\mathsf{WS}_t)\to (\Delta_t,W_t,\mathbf{c}_t,E_t)),
(\mathsf{Project}(\Psi_{t-1},\Delta_t,W_t,\mathbf{c}_t,\Omega,\mathsf{StepDesc}_t)\to(\Psi_t,\mathsf{verdict}_t,m_t,E'_t)),
(\mathsf{LogStep}(\cdot)\to \mathsf{StepRec}_t) (Section 13.2).
No other order is admissible. Any system that commits effects without this sequence is not executing the framework.
13.1.1(d) Step-local commitments.For every step (t), the runtime must compute and commit:
(h_{\mathsf{pre},t}=h(\Psi_{t-1})),
(h_{\mathsf{post},t}=h(\Psi_t)) (or (h_{\mathsf{post},t}=h_{\mathsf{pre},t}) if (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT})),
delta hashes and delta root (r_{\Delta,t}),
ordered witness list hash (h(W_t)),
ordered evidence list hash (h(E^\star_t)), where (E^\star_t) is the canonical union of (E_t) and (E'_t) (with ordering pinned by (\Omega)),
the cost vector (\mathbf{c}_t),
the verdict (\mathsf{verdict}_t) and acceptance mask (m_t).
13.1.2 Mandatory checks per step
13.1.2(a) Plan-bound obligations.For each step (t), the runtime must enforce:
the witness obligations (\mathsf{WReq}_t) from (\mathsf{WPlan}),
the detector schedule, scope limits, and partial acceptance policy from (\mathsf{LPlan}),
the corridor feasibility predicates under (\Omega),
the contract obligations of the transform referenced by (\mathsf{op_id}_t) (if (\mathsf{op_id}_t) is a transform entry).
These obligations are binding: the runtime may not weaken them and may not skip them.
13.1.2(b) Mandatory detector phases.Per (\mathsf{LPlan}), detectors run in four phases for each step:
pre-apply (feasibility of parameters/specs),
pre-project (delta and witness presence checks),
project-time (hard legality: witness verification, spec checks, invariant checks, contradiction checks),
post-commit (state-level audits: contradiction scan, canonical normal-form checks, tolerance-ledger updates).
Each detector emits ((\mathsf{flag},E)) deterministically and evidence retention is enforced by (\Omega).
13.1.2(c) Witness verification and binding checks.The projector must verify:
presence of required witness types and method ids per (\mathsf{WReq}_t),
step-local binding for each witness to the step context (\mathsf{Ctx}_t),
local-check verification for proof witnesses using the pinned checker programs and test bases,
correctness of any scope-extension witnesses for secondary writes,
correctness of any consistency witnesses for contradiction resolution,
correctness of any budget escalation witnesses (if applicable).
Any required witness failure is a hard stop yielding (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}).
13.1.2(d) Budget and scope enforcement.For each step:
budget feasibility must hold for compute/write/branch/tolerance components, with no implicit escalations,
scope feasibility must hold for all proposed deltas; unauthorized writes are hard failures unless (\Omega) explicitly classifies them as discardable and partial acceptance is permitted by (\mathsf{LPlan}).
13.1.3 Verdict computation and masking
13.1.3(a) Verdict determination rule.The projector computes the verdict (\mathsf{verdict}_t\in{\mathsf{ACCEPT},\mathsf{PARTIAL},\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}}) as a deterministic function of:
(\Psi_{t-1}),
(\Delta_t),
(W_t),
(\mathbf{c}_t),
detector outcomes and evidence,
and (\Omega) plus (\mathsf{LPlan}) step policy.
Hard failures (determinism failures, required witness failures, forbidden scope writes, budget infeasibility without admissible escalation, contradiction violations) force (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}).
13.1.3(b) Acceptance mask computation.If hard failures do not occur, the projector computes an acceptance mask (m_t) that determines which store deltas (and which individual operations within store deltas) are applied. The mask is deterministic and committed in the step record. Mask structure includes:[m_t := (m_{\mathsf{commit}},m_{\mathsf{rel}},m_{\mathsf{cand}},m_{\mathsf{proof}},m_{\mathsf{op}}),]where (m_{\mathsf{op}}) encodes within-store selection such as candidate truncation masks and omission of unauthorized secondary operations.
13.1.3(c) Partial acceptance semantics.(\mathsf{PARTIAL}) is permitted only when the primary target effects remain legal and any discarded effects are discardable under (\Omega) and (\mathsf{LPlan}). Partial acceptance may discard:
candidate additions beyond cap (by deterministic truncation),
uncertified secondary writes that are explicitly discardable,
optional proof emissions that are not required for legality.
Partial acceptance must never commit contradiction-controlled commitments without full legality.
13.1.3(d) Revert semantics.If (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}), the post-state equals the pre-state:[\Psi_t=\Psi_{t-1},\quad h_{\mathsf{post},t}=h_{\mathsf{pre},t}.]The step record must still commit the attempted deltas and witness list hashes (as produced) and the evidence explaining rejection, enabling replay to deterministically reproduce the rejection and its cause.
13.1.4 Commit extraction rules
13.1.4(a) Definition of committed outputs.An output artifact is committed iff it is reachable from the post-state commitment store (\mathsf{CommitStore}_t) by canonical key lookup and its value hash resolves to a well-formed COS object in the proof store, with provenance binding to the committing step context.
13.1.4(b) Output set for a plan.Let (\mathsf{Targets}) be the plan’s requested outputs. The execution result outputs are:
the set of pin records in (\mathsf{CommitStore}_n) whose (\mathsf{pin_id}) is in (\mathsf{Targets}) (for (\mathsf{PIN}) targets),
the set of relation edges created/updated as specified by (\mathsf{Targets}) (for (\mathsf{EDGE}) targets),
the set of proof objects specified by hash (for (\mathsf{PROOF}) targets),
the receipt object itself (for (\mathsf{RECEIPT}) targets, when requested).
13.1.4(c) Canonical extraction procedure.Extraction is deterministic:
resolve (\mathsf{Targets}) from the plan hash,
for each target pin id, perform canonical (\mathsf{GET_PIN}(\mathsf{pin_id})),
verify the pin record schema and canonical naming,
resolve value hash and provenance hash,
verify that provenance witness binds to the step context that introduced or last updated the pin record.
If any required target is missing, execution is considered incomplete and must return a failure receipt or a deterministic failure artifact (depending on (\Omega)).
13.1.4(d) Output integrity under partial acceptance.If any target depends on a step that ended in (\mathsf{PARTIAL}), the output remains valid only if all required commitment deltas for that target were accepted and all required witnesses verified. Any target that depends on discarded deltas is absent by definition and cannot be claimed as produced.
13.2 Ledger format
13.2.1 Header commitments
13.2.1(a) Ledger header object.The ledger begins with a header (\mathsf{LedHdr}) committing to:[\mathsf{LedHdr}:=(h(\mathsf{Plan}),\ h_\Omega,\ h_{\mathsf{Canon}},\ h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},\ h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\ h_{\mathsf{CostModel}},\ h_{\mathsf{genesis}},\ \mathsf{hdr_ver}),]where (h_{\mathsf{genesis}}) commits to the initial checkpoint (\mathsf{Chk}_0) and initial store hashes, and (\mathsf{hdr_ver}) is a pinned ledger format version.
13.2.1(b) Header immutability.All step records are interpreted relative to (\mathsf{LedHdr}). If any component hash differs (policy mismatch), replay verification is ill-typed and must fail. Any change in (\Omega) or verifier set requires a new ledger header and therefore a new ledger instance.
13.2.1(c) Genesis commitment.The genesis object (h_{\mathsf{genesis}}) binds:
(h(\Psi_0)),
per-store hashes at step 0,
and the initial commitment store pin root (if any).Genesis is the base of the hash chain; it enables independent replay from step 0.
13.2.1(d) Ledger identity.The ledger header hash (h(\mathsf{LedHdr})) is the ledger identity. Receipts reference the ledger by (h(\mathsf{LedHdr})) and a final state hash. Ledger identity is therefore compact and content-addressed.
13.2.2 Step record schema
13.2.2(a) Step record fields.Each step record (\mathsf{StepRec}_t) is a typed object committing to:
indices and identifiers: (\mathsf{step_index}=t), (\mathsf{op_id}t), (h{P_t}), (h_{\Gamma_t}), (\lambda_t),
state hashes: (h_{\mathsf{pre},t}), (h_{\mathsf{post},t}),
per-store hashes (pre and post): (h_{\mathsf{commit},t}^{\pm}), (h_{\mathsf{rel},t}^{\pm}), (h_{\mathsf{cand},t}^{\pm}), (h_{\mathsf{proof},t}^{\pm}),
delta hashes: (h(\Delta_{\mathsf{commit},t})), (h(\Delta_{\mathsf{rel},t})), (h(\Delta_{\mathsf{cand},t})), (h(\Delta_{\mathsf{proof},t})),
delta root (r_{\Delta,t}),
ordered witness list hash (h(W_t)),
ordered evidence list hash (h(E^\star_t)),
cost vector (\mathbf{c}_t),
verdict (\mathsf{verdict}_t) and mask (m_t),
previous record hash (h(\mathsf{StepRec}_{t-1})) to form a hash chain (with (h(\mathsf{StepRec}_0):=h(\mathsf{LedHdr}))).
13.2.2(b) Canonical ordering of referenced lists.Within (\mathsf{StepRec}_t), any list-like field is either set-like or sequence-like by schema:
witness references and evidence references are set-like and canonically sorted by pinned rules,
program steps are sequence-like and preserve order,
delta operations are sequence-like but canonically ordered inside each delta by pinned delta-ordering rules.
13.2.2(c) Context binding.Each step record implicitly defines the step context (\mathsf{Ctx}_t) by the committed fields. Witness verifiers reconstruct (\mathsf{Ctx}_t) deterministically from (\mathsf{StepRec}_t) and (\mathsf{LedHdr}).
13.2.2(d) Step record validity.A step record is valid iff:
it is well-formed under COS,
its pre hash matches the previous step’s post hash (except (t=1) where pre hash matches genesis),
and it is replay-verifiable (Section 13.3).
13.2.3 Canonical encoding and hashes
13.2.3(a) Canonical encoding.Ledger header and step records are canonically encoded under COS rules (Chapter 6):
fixed field order,
canonical integer encoding (CVI),
canonical map and set encoding,
pinned numeric policies for quantized components.
No ledger element may contain non-canonical bytes; otherwise replay is ill-typed.
13.2.3(b) Hash chain.Define:[h(\mathsf{StepRec}_t) := H(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{StepRec}_t)),]with (\mathsf{StepRec}t) containing (h(\mathsf{StepRec}{t-1})). This creates an append-only chain: any modification to any earlier record changes all later hashes. The chain provides tamper-evidence for the ledger.
13.2.3(c) Deterministic recomputation.Replay verification recomputes:
post-store hashes by applying accepted deltas,
post-state hash from post-store hashes and header commitments,
and therefore confirms that the committed hashes match deterministic recomputation.
13.2.3(d) Ledger size and optional compression.Ledger compression is permitted only if:
the compressed ledger preserves the ability to reconstruct canonical step records (or preserves them by hash reference to an immutable store),
and the compression mechanism is itself a legal transform with explicit contracts and proofs.No “opaque compression” that breaks independent replay is permitted.
13.2.4 Merkle structures for outputs
13.2.4(a) Pin root.For a set of output pins (\mathcal{P}\subseteq \mathsf{CommitStore}n), define the pin root:[r{\mathsf{pins}} := \mathsf{MerkleRoot}\big(\mathrm{Sort}({\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{PinRecord})\mid \mathsf{PinRecord}\in \mathcal{P}})\big),]where sorting is lexicographic on canonical bytes and (\mathsf{MerkleRoot}) uses a pinned Merkle hash construction.
13.2.4(b) Witness root.Define:[r_{\mathsf{wits}} := \mathsf{MerkleRoot}\big(\mathrm{Sort}({h(W)\mid W\ \text{is a witness used in the executed plan}})\big),]where the witness set includes at minimum all required witnesses referenced in step records. Ordering is canonical.
13.2.4(c) Evidence root (optional).If (\Omega) requires evidence root commitments, define:[r_{\mathsf{ev}} := \mathsf{MerkleRoot}\big(\mathrm{Sort}({h(E)\mid E\ \text{is an evidence object retained under policy}})\big).]Evidence roots provide compact commitments to defect/mitigation history.
13.2.4(d) Membership proofs.A membership proof is a Merkle proof object (\mathsf{MP}) that proves inclusion of a leaf (encoded pin record bytes or witness hash bytes) in a specified root. Membership proofs are used in receipts and dependency proofs; they are verifiable deterministically under the pinned Merkle policy.
13.3 Replay verifier
13.3.1 Recompute deltas deterministically
13.3.1(a) Replay input.Replay verification consumes:
ledger header (\mathsf{LedHdr}),
step record sequence (\mathsf{StepRec}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{StepRec}_n),
a resolver that maps all referenced object hashes (pins, proofs, witnesses, policies) to canonical bytes,
the pinned verifier set (\mathsf{VerSet}) referenced by (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}).
13.3.1(b) Delta recomputation modes.Two modes are permitted, pinned by policy:
Delta-trust mode: treat delta bytes as committed and recompute only their hashes and the resulting state hashes by applying them.
Full re-execution mode: re-run (\mathsf{Apply}) deterministically from inputs and verify that re-executed deltas match the committed delta hashes.Full re-execution mode is stronger but more expensive; admissibility depends on (\Omega) and available artifacts.
13.3.1(c) Deterministic application under mask.Replay must recompute the acceptance mask (m_t) as a deterministic function of the pre-state, deltas, witnesses, and (\Omega), and apply (\Delta_t^{(m_t)}) in the canonical store order and canonical within-store operation order. Mask recomputation must match the committed mask; mismatch is a replay divergence defect.
13.3.1(d) Post-hash recomputation.After applying accepted deltas, replay recomputes per-store post hashes and the full post-state hash (h_{\mathsf{post},t}). Any mismatch with the committed step record fields is a replay divergence defect.
13.3.2 Verify witness bindings and proofs
13.3.2(a) Step context reconstruction.For each step (t), the verifier reconstructs (\mathsf{Ctx}_t) from:
(\mathsf{LedHdr}) policy hashes,
step descriptor fields in (\mathsf{StepRec}_t),
(h_{\mathsf{pre},t}), (r_{\Delta,t}), (r_{\mathsf{src},t}) (as committed or reconstructed), and (h_{\mathsf{post},t}).All witness verification is relative to (\mathsf{Ctx}_t).
13.3.2(b) Mandatory witness verification order.Witness verification proceeds deterministically:
verify provenance witness (\mathsf{PROV}) first (binding root),
verify any budget escalation witnesses,
verify any scope-extension witnesses,
verify proof witnesses (equivalence/bridge/trace/rewrite/dependency proofs),
verify consistency witnesses (contradiction resolution, merges),
verify commitment and relation witnesses authorizing writes.
Any required witness failure yields replay failure for the step.
13.3.2(c) Local-check evaluation.For each proof witness, the verifier resolves:
method id and checker program id,
local-check data object,
test basis object,
referenced input/output object hashes,and executes the checker deterministically under the numeric policy and convention fingerprints. Verification level constraints must be satisfied; claim overreach is rejected.
13.3.2(d) Witness-to-delta consistency.Verifiers must check that authorized operation sets in commitment/relation/routing witnesses are consistent with the committed delta hashes and the accepted mask. This prevents a witness from authorizing different operations than those actually applied.
13.3.3 Detect divergence and locate earliest fault
13.3.3(a) Divergence detection criteria.A divergence occurs if any of the following mismatches are detected for any step:
pre hash mismatch with chain continuity,
delta hash mismatch,
mask mismatch,
witness list hash mismatch,
post-store hash mismatch,
post-state hash mismatch,
detector evidence mismatch when evidence retention is mandatory.
13.3.3(b) Earliest fault localization.The verifier must report the smallest (t) such that step (t) fails. Within that step, it must localize divergence to:
commitment store, relation store, candidate store, proof store, or header/policy mismatch,by comparing per-store post hashes in canonical order. This localization is deterministic.
13.3.3(c) Evidence emission on replay failure.Replay failure produces a typed evidence object containing:
recorded vs recomputed hashes,
earliest fault step index,
divergence store id,
and any witness or proof method id implicated.Evidence binds to the reconstructed step context and is suitable for triggering certified repair (Chapter 10) or halting (Chapter 9).
13.3.3(d) Replay failure classification.Replay failure is classified as:
execution divergence: recomputed state differs despite matching policies and artifacts,
artifact missing: required objects could not be resolved by hash,
policy mismatch: header hashes do not match the verifier’s runtime header,
method mismatch: required verifier method ids not supported by (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}).Classification is a pinned enum and is deterministically computed.
13.3.4 Verification result semantics
13.3.4(a) Verification outputs.Replay verification returns a typed result:[\mathsf{VerifyResult}:=(\mathsf{status},\ h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),\ t^\star,\ h(\mathsf{Evidence})?,\ h_{\mathsf{final}}?),]where (\mathsf{status}\in{\mathsf{OK},\mathsf{FAIL}}), (t^\star) is the earliest failing step index (or (0) if (\mathsf{OK})), and optional fields are present when applicable.
13.3.4(b) Meaning of (\mathsf{OK}).(\mathsf{OK}) means:
the ledger chain is continuous,
all required witnesses verify under the pinned verifier set,
all detector commitments required by (\Omega) are satisfied,
all step post hashes equal recomputed post hashes,
and the final state hash equals the ledger’s committed terminal hash.It does not assert any semantic truth beyond the declared invariants, tolerances, verification levels, and convention fingerprints committed by proofs and receipts.
13.3.4(c) Meaning of (\mathsf{FAIL}).(\mathsf{FAIL}) means at least one of:
a step record is ill-formed,
chain continuity fails,
a required witness fails,
determinism/replay constraints fail,
or required artifacts cannot be resolved.(\mathsf{FAIL}) invalidates receipts that reference the failed ledger header unless those receipts are explicitly marked as failure receipts (if the system supports them) and bounded to the failure state.
13.3.4(d) Partial acceptance and verification.A verified ledger may contain (\mathsf{PARTIAL}) steps. (\mathsf{OK}) verification requires that the committed masks are reproduced deterministically and that the resulting post-state hashes match. Partial acceptance does not weaken verification; it changes which deltas were applied, and that applied set is part of the verifiable record.
13.4 Verification receipts
13.4.1 Receipt schema and commitments
13.4.1(a) Receipt object.A receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}) is a COS object:[\mathsf{Rcpt}:=(h(\mathsf{Plan}),h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),h_{\mathsf{final}},r_{\mathsf{pins}},r_{\mathsf{wits}},r_{\mathsf{ev}}?,h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{mode}),]where:
(h_{\mathsf{final}}) is the final state hash,
(r_{\mathsf{pins}}) commits the output pin set relevant to the plan targets,
(r_{\mathsf{wits}}) commits the witness set used,
(r_{\mathsf{ev}}) optionally commits evidence retention when required,
(\mathsf{mode}) encodes disclosure mode (hash-only vs selective reveal).
13.4.1(b) Output binding.The receipt binds outputs by Merkle roots, not by narrative. For any output pin, a membership proof can show inclusion in (r_{\mathsf{pins}}). For any witness, a membership proof can show inclusion in (r_{\mathsf{wits}}).
13.4.1(c) Policy binding.The receipt commits to the runtime header hashes and corridor policy hash. Therefore any verification claim is relative to those pinned versions. Policy mismatch invalidates the receipt for verifiers operating under a different runtime header.
13.4.1(d) Receipt validity predicate.A receipt is valid iff:
the referenced ledger verifies under replay (or the verifier accepts a policy-defined verification shortcut such as verifying a ledger prefix plus membership proofs),
(h_{\mathsf{final}}) matches the ledger’s terminal state hash,
the pin root matches the extracted pin records for the plan’s targets,
the witness root matches the witness hashes referenced by the ledger for the executed plan segment.
13.4.2 Minimal verification procedure
13.4.2(a) Full verification.Full verification consists of:
resolve (\mathsf{LedHdr}) and step records (or resolve the ledger by (h(\mathsf{LedHdr}))),
replay-verify the ledger under the pinned verifier set,
confirm terminal state hash equals (h_{\mathsf{final}}),
extract target pin records from the terminal commitment store,
compute (r_{\mathsf{pins}}) deterministically and compare to receipt,
compute (r_{\mathsf{wits}}) deterministically and compare to receipt.
13.4.2(b) Output-only verification (membership proof verification).When the verifier already trusts the ledger validity (e.g., because it is verified once and cached), verifying a receipt can be reduced to:
verifying (h(\mathsf{Plan})) matches the intended plan,
verifying membership proofs for each output pin record under (r_{\mathsf{pins}}),
verifying membership proofs for required witnesses under (r_{\mathsf{wits}}),
confirming policy hash compatibility.
This supports scalable reuse without repeated full replay.
13.4.2(c) Evidence verification (when required).If (r_{\mathsf{ev}}) is present, a verifier may require membership proofs for key evidence objects (e.g., mitigation evidence, repair completion evidence) when auditing defect/repair chains. Evidence verification is mechanical via Merkle proofs and object hash resolution.
13.4.2(d) Verification result object.Receipt verification produces a typed verification result object binding:
receipt hash,
status (\mathsf{OK}/\mathsf{FAIL}),
evidence hash on failure,
and (optionally) the verified output pin record hashes.
13.4.3 Selective disclosure procedures
13.4.3(a) Disclosure mode declaration.The receipt includes (\mathsf{mode}\in{\mathsf{HASH_ONLY},\mathsf{SELECTIVE}}). The mode determines which objects must be disclosed to enable verification of proof witnesses:
in (\mathsf{HASH_ONLY}), the receipt may be a commitment that cannot be independently verified without access to required proof objects,
in (\mathsf{SELECTIVE}), the receipt is accompanied by disclosed local-check data sufficient for independent verification of required proofs.
13.4.3(b) Local-check disclosure set.Selective disclosure provides:
test basis objects,
checker program identifiers and parameters,
quantization policies,
convention fingerprints,
and any bound objects required for deterministic verification.Disclosed objects are referenced by hash in the receipt or by a linked disclosure bundle object whose hash is committed by the receipt.
13.4.3(c) Redaction boundaries.If the receipt commits to redaction policies, then any verifier must respect those boundaries: verification proceeds only if method policies support verification under redaction; otherwise the receipt remains a commitment without public verifiability. This boundary is explicit in the receipt metadata.
13.4.3(d) Dependency proofs under selective disclosure.When composing receipts, dependency proofs may require revealing membership proofs and the relevant pin-record encodings, but need not reveal underlying private payload bytes. This supports compositional verification while keeping payloads private by commitment.
13.4.4 Receipt pinning and indexing
13.4.4(a) Receipt pinning.Receipts may be pinned as commitments:[\mathsf{RECEIPT}/\langle h(\mathsf{Rcpt})\rangle \to h(\mathsf{Rcpt}),]subject to (\Omega) scope and write budgets. Receipt pinning requires a commitment witness and is contradiction-controlled for namespaces that enforce uniqueness.
13.4.4(b) Receipt indexing.The runtime may maintain canonical indices:
by plan hash: (\mathsf{RECEIPT}/\mathsf{BYPLAN}/\langle h(\mathsf{Plan})\rangle),
by final state hash: (\mathsf{RECEIPT}/\mathsf{BYSTATE}/\langle h_{\mathsf{final}}\rangle),
by artifact pin id: (\mathsf{RECEIPT}/\mathsf{BYOUTPUT}/\langle \mathsf{pin_id}\rangle).Indices are implemented as pinned alias objects or pinned index objects, each subject to contradiction policy and consistency proofs on update.
13.4.4(c) Receipt supersession.Receipts may be superseded by stronger receipts (refinement ordering) only through explicit supersession records that bind:
old receipt hash,
new receipt hash,
refinement proof hash (showing stronger invariant set and/or tighter tolerances and/or higher verification level),
and compatibility of policy hashes.Supersession is never implicit.
13.4.4(d) Receipt audit closure.Receipt indexing must preserve auditability:
every index update is logged by a step record,
every receipt is traceable to its ledger header and plan hash,
every dependency on a receipt is representable by dependency proofs,
and all such objects are content-addressed and replay-verifiable.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 13 — EXECUTION PROTOCOL: RUN, LOG, VERIFY
CHAPTER 14 — RECEIPT ALGEBRA: COMPOSITION, MERGE, REFINEMENT
This chapter defines receipts as algebraic objects and specifies the operations that make verification compositional: validity, strength ordering, sequential composition by dependency proofs, parallel merge by co-verification, and a formal reuse economy grounded in anti-equivocation constraints. Receipts are the portable verification unit: they allow downstream computations to depend on upstream verified artifacts without re-executing upstream internal payloads, subject to explicit compatibility constraints and locally verifiable dependency proofs.
14.1 Receipt validity and strength
14.1.1 Validity predicate
14.1.1(a) Receipt object recap.A receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}) is a COS object:[\mathsf{Rcpt}:=(h(\mathsf{Plan}),h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),h_{\mathsf{final}},r_{\mathsf{pins}},r_{\mathsf{wits}},r_{\mathsf{ev}}?,h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{mode}),]with all fields canonically encoded and hashed.
14.1.1(b) Validity predicate.A receipt is valid iff all conditions hold:
Ledger validity: the ledger identified by (h(\mathsf{LedHdr})) verifies under replay with the verifier set (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}) and canonicalization policy (h_{\mathsf{Canon}}).
Final state match: the ledger terminal state hash equals (h_{\mathsf{final}}).
Output commitment match: the Merkle root computed from the extracted output pin records for the plan’s targets equals (r_{\mathsf{pins}}).
Witness commitment match: the Merkle root computed from the witnesses used in the executed plan segment equals (r_{\mathsf{wits}}).
Policy binding match: the policy hashes embedded in (\mathsf{Rcpt}) match the ledger header and the runtime header used for replay verification.
If any condition fails, the receipt is invalid.
14.1.1(c) Verifiability vs validity.Validity is a property of the receipt relative to the existence and accessibility of the referenced ledger and required objects. Verifiability is the ability of a given verifier to check validity given available disclosures. A receipt may be valid while not verifiable by a party lacking required proof objects; this boundary is encoded by (\mathsf{mode}) and associated disclosure policy objects.
14.1.1(d) Receipt identity.The receipt identity is its object hash (h(\mathsf{Rcpt})). Two receipts are identical iff their canonical bytes are identical. Any semantic notion of “same result” across different receipts must be expressed via refinement or equivalence relations defined in this chapter.
14.1.2 Strength preorder and refinement
14.1.2(a) Strength components.Receipt strength is determined by:
the invariant set and tolerance profile bound by the plan and its proof objects,
the verification level(s) used in required proofs,
the corridor policy strictness (budgets, determinism, contradiction policy, evidence retention),
the canonicalization and verifier set versions.
Strength is not inferred; it is derived from pinned hashes referenced by the receipt and the plan.
14.1.2(b) Strength preorder.Define a preorder (\preceq) over receipts: (\mathsf{Rcpt}_2\preceq \mathsf{Rcpt}_1) (“(\mathsf{Rcpt}_2) is at least as strong as (\mathsf{Rcpt}_1)”) iff:
Artifact equivalence: the output artifacts of (\mathsf{Rcpt}_2) are equal to or refine those of (\mathsf{Rcpt}_1) under an explicit equivalence/refinement proof object (a proof witness binding the two output pin sets and their values under declared invariants).
Tolerance tightening: for each invariant required by (\mathsf{Rcpt}_1), the tolerances in (\mathsf{Rcpt}_2)’s bound claims are (\le) those in (\mathsf{Rcpt}_1).
Invariant superset: (\mathsf{Rcpt}_2) supports an invariant set that contains the invariant set required by (\mathsf{Rcpt}_1).
Verification level dominance: (\mathsf{Rcpt}_2)’s required proof verification levels are (\ge) those of (\mathsf{Rcpt}_1) for the relevant claims.
Policy strictness dominance: (\Omega_2) is at least as strict as (\Omega_1) under a pinned policy-preorder; canonicalization and verifier set hashes match or are bridged by explicit policy-bridge receipts if the framework permits such bridging.
This preorder is used to formalize refinement and supersession.
14.1.2(c) Refinement object.A refinement proof is a COS object:[\mathsf{RefProof}:=(h(\mathsf{Rcpt}_1),h(\mathsf{Rcpt}_2),h(\mathsf{OutEq}),h(\mathsf{ClaimCompare}),h(\mathsf{PolicyCompare})),]where:
(\mathsf{OutEq}) is an output equivalence/refinement proof object,
(\mathsf{ClaimCompare}) binds invariant/tolerance/verification-level comparisons,
(\mathsf{PolicyCompare}) binds corridor strictness comparison and policy hash compatibility.A refinement is admissible only if the referenced proof objects verify deterministically under the verifier set of the receipts.
14.1.2(d) Refinement closure under composition.If (\mathsf{Rcpt}_2\preceq \mathsf{Rcpt}_1) and a downstream computation accepts (\mathsf{Rcpt}_1) as an upstream dependency, it may accept (\mathsf{Rcpt}_2) instead, provided dependency proofs bind the consumed artifacts and policy compatibility holds. Thus refinement supports substitution of stronger upstream results without downstream changes.
14.1.3 Supersession and replacement
14.1.3(a) Supersession record.Supersession is an explicit commitment:[\mathsf{Sup}:=(h(\mathsf{Rcpt}{\mathrm{old}}),h(\mathsf{Rcpt}{\mathrm{new}}),h(\mathsf{RefProof}),\mathsf{reason_code}),]pinned in a contradiction-controlled namespace. (\mathsf{reason_code}) is a pinned enum (tightened tolerances, higher verification level, corrected defect, policy tightening, deprecation).
14.1.3(b) Supersession admissibility.Supersession is admissible only if:
(\mathsf{RefProof}) verifies and establishes (\mathsf{Rcpt}{\mathrm{new}}\preceq \mathsf{Rcpt}{\mathrm{old}}),
policy hash compatibility is satisfied or bridged explicitly,
supersession does not create contradictions in receipt indices (Section 13.4.4 and this chapter’s compatibility constraints).
14.1.3(c) Replacement vs supersession.Replacement is a weaker notion than supersession: a replacement receipt may exist that produces different outputs or operates under different policies. Replacement does not imply refinement; it is merely an alternative. Supersession implies refinement and thus safe substitution for downstream dependencies.
14.1.3(d) Supersession auditability.Every supersession record must be traceable to:
the old receipt hash,
the new receipt hash,
the refinement proof hash,
and the ledger headers for both receipts.This enables independent auditors to verify supersession mechanically.
14.1.4 Compatibility constraints
14.1.4(a) Policy compatibility.Two receipts are policy-compatible for composition or merge only if:
canonicalization hashes match,
object standard hashes match,
verifier set hashes match,
and corridor policy hashes are compatible under a pinned compatibility rule:
either equal, or
the downstream corridor is stricter and explicitly permits consumption of upstream artifacts produced under a weaker corridor, or
a policy-bridge mechanism exists and is used, producing a policy-bridge receipt that establishes compatibility.
Absent these conditions, receipts cannot compose.
14.1.4(b) Convention compatibility.When downstream consumption depends on upstream artifacts with convention fingerprints, the downstream plan must either:
require the same convention fingerprints, or
include explicit bridge transforms aligning conventions.Receipt composition cannot assume convention alignment; it must be proven by plan structure and dependency proofs.
14.1.4(c) Claim compatibility.Downstream requirements for invariants, tolerances, and verification levels must be satisfied by upstream claim strength. If the upstream receipt is weaker, it cannot be used unless a refinement or proof regeneration exists that strengthens the upstream artifact.
14.1.4(d) Disclosure compatibility.If the downstream verifier requires public verification, upstream receipts must be in a disclosure mode that provides the required local-check data (selective reveal) or must provide a mechanism for the verifier to access required objects. Otherwise, composition is possible for private execution but may be unverifiable publicly; this boundary must be explicit.
14.2 Sequential composition
14.2.1 Dependency conditions
14.2.1(a) Consumption model.Sequential composition models the case where a downstream plan consumes one or more artifacts produced by an upstream receipt. Consumption is represented by including the hashes of the consumed pin records (or consumed object hashes) in the downstream step provenance source roots (r_{\mathsf{src}}) (Chapter 5).
14.2.1(b) Dependency admissibility predicate.Let (\mathsf{Rcpt}_u) be an upstream receipt, and let (\mathsf{Plan}_d) be a downstream plan. The dependency is admissible iff:
policy compatibility holds between (\mathsf{Rcpt}_u) and (\mathsf{Plan}_d),
the downstream plan’s required invariants and tolerances are satisfied by the upstream artifact’s claim strength (or the downstream plan includes explicit bridges and proof steps to derive what it needs),
the downstream plan’s provenance bindings include the consumed artifact hashes,
membership of the consumed artifacts in (\mathsf{Rcpt}_u)’s pin root can be proven by Merkle proofs.
14.2.1(c) Consumed artifact identification.Consumed artifacts are identified by canonical pin record encodings (not by alias names). The canonical consumed pin record hash is:[h(\mathsf{PinRecord})=H(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{PinRecord})).]Downstream dependency must reference these hashes, ensuring unambiguous consumption.
14.2.1(d) No implicit dependency.Downstream computations may not claim dependency on an upstream receipt unless they provide explicit dependency proofs and provenance bindings. Any narrative “uses result X” without hash-committed consumption is meaningless within the framework.
14.2.2 Dependency proof objects
14.2.2(a) Dependency proof schema.A dependency proof (\mathsf{ProofDep}) is a COS object (Chapter 6) that binds:
downstream plan hash (h(\mathsf{Plan}_d)),
a canonical list of upstream receipt hashes ([h(\mathsf{Rcpt}_u)]),
a canonical list of consumed pin record hashes ([h(\mathsf{PinRec}_j)]),
Merkle membership proofs ([\mathsf{MP}{u,j}]) showing each consumed pin record is included in each referenced upstream receipt’s (r{\mathsf{pins}}) as appropriate,
the downstream provenance root (r_{\mathsf{src}}) (or a commitment to the set of provenance roots across steps) that includes the consumed pin record hashes,
and the policy hash compatibility commitments.
14.2.2(b) Dependency proof verification.A verifier accepts (\mathsf{ProofDep}) iff:
each upstream receipt is valid or is accepted as valid under a pinned trust policy,
each Merkle membership proof verifies against the upstream receipt’s pin root,
the downstream provenance roots commit to the consumed pin record hashes,
compatibility constraints hold.
14.2.2(c) Granularity.Dependency proofs are pin-level: they prove dependence on specific output records, not on informal “results.” This granularity prevents equivocation and over-claiming.
14.2.2(d) Step-local linkage.The dependency proof must specify which downstream steps consume which upstream artifacts, or must provide a pinned derivation rule that allows deterministic reconstruction of that mapping from the downstream ledger. This prevents ambiguous claims of consumption.
14.2.3 Composed receipt construction
14.2.3(a) Sequential composition operator.Given an upstream receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}_u) and a downstream receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}_d) whose execution consumed artifacts from (\mathsf{Rcpt}u) with a verified dependency proof, define a composed receipt:[\mathsf{Rcpt}{d\circ u}:=\mathsf{Compose}(\mathsf{Rcpt}_u,\mathsf{Rcpt}_d,\mathsf{ProofDep}),]which is a receipt object that:
retains (h(\mathsf{LedHdr}d)) and (h{\mathsf{final},d}) as its ledger and final state commitments,
extends the pin root to commit both the downstream outputs and the referenced upstream consumed pin records as dependencies under a canonical dependency-root construction,
extends the witness root to include the dependency proof hash and any required upstream receipt hashes (as dependencies),
commits to the dependency proof object hash explicitly.
14.2.3(b) Dependency-root construction.Define a dependency root (r_{\mathsf{dep}}) as a Merkle root over canonical encodings of dependency references:[\mathsf{DepRec}:=(h(\mathsf{Rcpt}u),h(\mathsf{PinRec}),\mathsf{MP}),]sorted canonically. The composed receipt commits to ((r{\mathsf{pins},d},r_{\mathsf{dep}})) as a pair; it does not conflate upstream and downstream outputs.
14.2.3(c) Correctness boundary.The composed receipt asserts:
downstream execution is valid,
dependencies are correctly referenced and provably included in upstream receipts,
policy compatibility holds as declared.It does not assert any new semantic theorem beyond what downstream proofs and upstream proofs already certify.
14.2.3(d) Minimal disclosure preservation.Composed receipts preserve privacy: dependency references require only hashes and membership proofs. Upstream private payload bytes need not be revealed if the upstream receipt and proof methods support selective reveal or if verification is performed in a trusted environment. Disclosure boundaries remain explicit.
14.2.4 Associativity constraints
14.2.4(a) Associativity statement (up to exact receipt equality).Sequential receipt composition is associative up to canonical equality when compatibility conditions hold and dependency proofs are correctly constructed:[(\mathsf{Rcpt}_3\circ \mathsf{Rcpt}_2)\circ \mathsf{Rcpt}_1 \equiv\mathsf{Rcpt}_3\circ(\mathsf{Rcpt}_2\circ \mathsf{Rcpt}_1),]provided the dependency roots and ordering policies are pinned and identical.
14.2.4(b) Conditions for associativity.Associativity requires:
consistent policy hashes across all receipts (or explicit policy-bridges that compose),
consistent Merkle policy and ordering rules,
deterministic dependency-root encoding,
no ambiguity in which artifacts are consumed and how they are referenced.
If any condition fails, associativity is not assumed; different composition orders yield different receipts and must be related, if desired, by explicit equivalence proofs.
14.2.4(c) Associativity proof objects.If a system needs to treat two differently grouped compositions as equivalent, it must provide a certified rewrite/equivalence proof binding the two composed receipt hashes and proving equality of their dependency roots under canonical ordering. No informal argument is admissible.
14.2.4(d) Replay associativity coupling.Receipt associativity is distinct from transform replay associativity (Chapter 8). Receipt associativity concerns verification artifacts and dependency commitments; transform replay associativity concerns execution semantics. Both are enforced by pinned canonicalization and ordering rules.
14.3 Parallel merge
14.3.1 Merge admissibility
14.3.1(a) Parallel merge intent.Parallel merge combines independent receipts into a single co-verification artifact without asserting semantic interaction. Merge is used to aggregate verified results for indexing, publication, or bundled reuse.
14.3.1(b) Merge admissibility predicate.Two receipts (\mathsf{Rcpt}_a,\mathsf{Rcpt}_b) are merge-admissible iff:
policy compatibility holds (canonicalization/object/verifier hashes match or are bridged explicitly),
their committed outputs are non-contradictory under the applicable contradiction policy for the merge namespace,
their witness sets are well-formed under the same verifier set,
their disclosure modes are compatible with the merge’s intended verification audience (hash-only vs selective reveal).
14.3.1(c) Independence does not require disjointness.Receipts may share upstream dependencies or even share outputs by identical pin records. Merge admissibility is not disjointness; it is non-contradiction and policy compatibility.
14.3.1(d) Merge failure handling.If merge would introduce a contradiction in a contradiction-controlled namespace (e.g., two receipts both claim incompatible pinned truths for the same pin id), merge is inadmissible unless an explicit consistency/merge resolution is performed as a separate execution producing its own receipt.
14.3.2 Non-contradiction requirements
14.3.2(a) Contradiction predicate for merged pinsets.Let (\mathcal{P}_a,\mathcal{P}b) be the pin record sets committed by the receipts. Define (\mathcal{P}{a\cup b}=\mathcal{P}a\cup\mathcal{P}b) under canonical equality of pin record bytes. Merge non-contradiction requires:[\mathsf{Contradict}{\Omega}(\mathcal{P}{a\cup b})=\mathsf{false}]under the contradiction policy governing the merge namespace.
14.3.2(b) Conflict detection and evidence.If conflicts exist, a merge attempt must emit a typed conflict evidence object enumerating conflicting pin ids and record hashes. Conflict evidence can be used to compile a certified contradiction resolution plan; it cannot be ignored.
14.3.2(c) Non-contradiction vs semantic consistency.Non-contradiction is about explicit commitments, not about informal semantic compatibility. Two receipts may be non-contradictory yet semantically related; the merge does not claim semantic relations unless accompanied by explicit relation edges and proofs.
14.3.2(d) Merge namespace discipline.Merged receipts are pinned under a dedicated namespace whose contradiction policy is pinned. This prevents a merge artifact from being mistaken for an update to the primary truth namespace without explicit consistency proofs.
14.3.3 Union Merkle roots and ordering
14.3.3(a) Union pin root.The merged receipt constructs a union pin root:[r_{\mathsf{pins},a\cup b}:=\mathsf{MerkleRoot}(\mathrm{Sort}(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathcal{P}_{a\cup b}))).]Sorting is lexicographic on canonical pin record encodings.
14.3.3(b) Union witness root.Similarly, define:[r_{\mathsf{wits},a\cup b}:=\mathsf{MerkleRoot}(\mathrm{Sort}(\mathcal{W}_a\cup \mathcal{W}_b)),]where (\mathcal{W}_a) and (\mathcal{W}_b) are sets of witness hashes referenced by the receipts (and, if required by policy, include dependency proof hashes). Union is set union under byte equality.
14.3.3(c) Canonical merge encoding.The merged receipt object includes:
the ordered list of constituent receipt hashes,
the merged pin root,
the merged witness root,
and the policy hashes.The constituent receipt hash list is set-like and is canonically sorted lexicographically.
14.3.3(d) Stability under repeated merges.Repeated merges are stable: merging (\mathsf{Rcpt}_a) with (\mathsf{Rcpt}_b) and later merging with (\mathsf{Rcpt}_c) yields the same final merged roots as merging all three at once, provided ordering policies and contradiction policies are unchanged and no conflicts exist. This is a consequence of deterministic set union and canonical Merkle roots.
14.3.4 Merge receipts as co-verification
14.3.4(a) Merge receipt semantics.A merged receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}_{a\cup b}) asserts only:
each constituent receipt is valid under its ledger and policies,
the merged pin set is the union of constituent pin sets,
the merged witness set is the union of constituent witness sets,
and the union is non-contradictory under the merge namespace policy.
It does not assert any dependency or causal relation between constituent receipts.
14.3.4(b) Verification procedure.To verify a merged receipt, a verifier:
verifies each constituent receipt (or accepts them under a trust policy),
verifies union Merkle root construction deterministically,
verifies non-contradiction under the merge policy,
verifies policy compatibility constraints.
14.3.4(c) Use in indexing and publication.Merged receipts serve as stable indices: they allow a publisher to commit to a set of verified results as a single hash. Downstream consumers can verify membership of a specific result in the merged set via Merkle proofs without reading all receipts.
14.3.4(d) Merge and refinement.Merge does not imply refinement. If a later merged receipt replaces an earlier one, that is not supersession unless an explicit refinement proof exists showing that the new merged receipt includes all prior results (or refined versions) and does not weaken policies or claim strength.
14.4 Proof reuse economy (formal)
14.4.1 Reuse as dependency references
14.4.1(a) Reuse primitive.Reuse is achieved by:
referencing upstream receipts,
providing dependency proofs for consumed pin records,
binding consumption into downstream provenance source roots,
and verifying compatibility constraints.
This turns verified outputs into reusable building blocks without re-executing their internal derivations.
14.4.1(b) Reuse closure condition.Reuse is admissible only if the upstream receipt’s claims satisfy downstream needs under the strength preorder, or if downstream includes explicit bridging/strengthening steps. Reuse cannot bypass missing invariants or insufficient verification levels.
14.4.1(c) Cached hub reuse.For frequently used library entries (hubs), the system maintains cached receipts indexed by spec and parameter hashes. Reuse selection is deterministic: choose the strongest admissible cached receipt.
14.4.1(d) Reuse correctness boundary.Reuse proves that a downstream result depends on a particular upstream artifact, not that the upstream artifact is “true in all senses.” Truth remains bounded by the upstream claim object: invariant set, tolerance profile, verification level, and conventions.
14.4.2 Cost reduction vs trust boundaries
14.4.2(a) Cost model.Reuse reduces verification and execution cost by:
avoiding recomputation of upstream derivations,
avoiding re-verification of upstream proofs beyond receipt validity checks,
replacing expensive transforms with receipt membership proofs.
The cost reduction is explicit: the route compiler can include reuse edges with known costs (Merkle proofs and receipt verification) instead of re-derivation edges.
14.4.2(b) Trust boundary definition.A verifier may choose to trust some receipts without replay-verifying their ledgers. Such trust must be represented as a pinned trust policy object and must not be conflated with formal validity. Public verification workflows should assume no trust beyond pinned policies unless explicitly stated.
14.4.2(c) Verification modes and reuse.Reuse is compatible with:
full replay verification (strongest),
receipt-only verification with cached ledger validation (intermediate),
membership-proof verification against trusted receipts (weakest).The selected verification mode must be explicit and committed by hash in any published verification artifact.
14.4.2(d) Boundary enforcement.If a downstream result is presented as publicly verifiable, the verifier must have sufficient disclosed local-check data to validate required proofs. Reuse does not remove this requirement; it shifts where the required proofs reside.
14.4.3 Anti-equivocation constraints
