14.4.3(a) Equivocation definition.Equivocation occurs when a party claims a downstream result depends on an upstream artifact without actually consuming it, or claims inconsistent upstream artifacts under the same identifier. The framework prevents this by:
requiring membership proofs for consumed pin records,
requiring provenance source roots to include consumed hashes,
enforcing contradiction policies for pinned identifiers,
and binding dependencies by hash.
14.4.3(b) Anti-equivocation theorem (receipt level).Given collision-resistant hashing and correct Merkle proofs, a party cannot produce a valid dependency proof asserting consumption of a pin record not included in the upstream receipt’s pin root. Therefore dependency claims are non-equivocable at the pin-record level.
14.4.3(c) Policy smuggling prevention.A party cannot smuggle weaker policies by reusing receipts under incompatible runtime headers or corridor policies: compatibility constraints require matching policy hashes or explicit policy-bridge receipts. Absent such compatibility, composition is ill-typed and rejected.
14.4.3(d) Branch and convention smuggling prevention.Because convention fingerprints are committed and bridge insertions are explicit, a party cannot claim to have used a result under one convention while actually using it under another without producing explicit bridges and their proofs. Convention mismatches trigger drift detectors and are repairable only by certified bridges.
14.4.4 Public verification workflows
14.4.4(a) Publication artifact set.A public verification package for a result consists of:
the receipt hash and receipt object bytes,
membership proofs for the claimed outputs under (r_{\mathsf{pins}}),
the verifier set hash and method registry commitments,
disclosed local-check data required to verify any proof objects needed for the claimed outputs (selective disclosure mode),
and dependency proofs for any upstream receipts relied upon.
14.4.4(b) Public verification procedure.A public verifier:
verifies receipt structure and policy hashes,
verifies membership proofs for output pin records,
verifies dependency proofs and membership of consumed artifacts in upstream receipts,
verifies proof objects referenced by outputs using disclosed local-check data and pinned checker programs,
optionally replay-verifies ledgers if full transparency is required.
14.4.4(c) Result aggregation and indexing.For publishing multiple results, a publisher may publish a merged receipt committing to a set of receipts. Consumers verify membership via Merkle proofs. Aggregation does not increase claim strength; it only provides compact commitment.
14.4.4(d) Audit and challenge workflow.If a public verifier detects inconsistency (invalid receipt, invalid membership proof, invalid proof witness), the verifier produces a challenge artifact: a typed evidence object referencing the failing hash and the exact verification step that failed. Challenges are replay-verifiable and can deterministically trigger repair or deprecation procedures in the library’s governance layer.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 14 — RECEIPT ALGEBRA: COMPOSITION, MERGE, REFINEMENT
CHAPTER 15 — MACRO SYSTEM: HUMAN-READABLE PROGRAMS WITH RIGOR
This chapter specifies a macro system that provides a human-readable assembly layer over executable programs while preserving all guarantees of the framework: typed legality, explicit contracts, explicit convention fingerprints, witness discipline, corridor feasibility, and deterministic replay. A macro is not syntactic sugar that can change meaning; it is a typed object whose canonical expansion is a deterministic program in the execution kernel’s instruction model, accompanied by an explicit witness plan and test basis commitments. The macro system enables reusable operator definitions, certified library entries, and proof-carrying optimization without reintroducing informal reasoning.
15.1 Macro object model
15.1.1 Macro schema fields
15.1.1(a) Macro object.A macro is a COS object (\mathsf{Macro}) with canonical fields:[\mathsf{Macro}:=(\mathsf{macro_id},\ \mathsf{name},\ \mathsf{version},\ \mathsf{kind},\ h(\mathsf{Bind}),\ h(\mathsf{Asm}),\ h(\mathsf{ParamSchema}),\ h(\mathsf{CanonReq}),\ h(\mathsf{WEmit}),\ h(\mathsf{Tests}),\ h(\mathsf{Deps}),\ h(\mathsf{Admit})).]
(\mathsf{macro_id}:=h(\mathsf{Macro})) is the canonical identifier (hash of canonical bytes).
(\mathsf{name}) is a canonical key string.
(\mathsf{version}) is a canonical version tuple.
(\mathsf{kind}\in{\mathsf{HUB},\mathsf{BRIDGE},\mathsf{REPAIR},\mathsf{COMPOSITE},\mathsf{NORMALIZER}}) is a pinned enum.
(h(\mathsf{Bind})) binds the macro to a legal transform contract and library entry semantics (Section 15.1.2).
(h(\mathsf{Asm})) is the canonical macro assembly program template (Section 15.2.1).
(h(\mathsf{ParamSchema})) is the required parameter schema for macro instantiation.
(h(\mathsf{CanonReq})) pins canonical expansion and normalization requirements (Section 15.1.3).
(h(\mathsf{WEmit})) is the witness emission plan (Section 15.2.3).
(h(\mathsf{Tests})) is the embedded test basis commitment bundle (Section 15.1.4).
(h(\mathsf{Deps})) is the dependency set (submacros, entries, methods, specs).
(h(\mathsf{Admit})) is an admission certificate bundle anchoring the macro to verified receipts (Section 15.4.4).
15.1.1(b) Macro instantiation object.A macro instantiation is a COS object (\mathsf{MacroInst}):[\mathsf{MacroInst}:=(h(\mathsf{Macro}),\ h(P),\ h(\mathsf{Context}),\ h(\mathsf{SpecBind}),\ h(\mathsf{ExpandPolicy})),]where (h(P)) is the parameter hash conforming to (h(\mathsf{ParamSchema})), (h(\mathsf{SpecBind})) binds required convention fingerprints, and (h(\mathsf{ExpandPolicy})) selects permitted expansion depth and any policy-gated options (all pinned and deterministic).
15.1.1(c) Macro product.The product of a macro instantiation is a compiled program object:[\mathsf{Prog}(\mathsf{MacroInst}) := \mathsf{Expand}(\mathsf{Macro},P,\mathsf{Context},\mathsf{ExpandPolicy}),]a COS program in canonical program encoding (Chapter 8), together with a derived witness plan and ledger plan segments consistent with the macro’s bindings.
15.1.1(d) Macro well-formedness predicate.A macro is well-formed iff:
all referenced hashes resolve to well-formed COS objects,
(\mathsf{name}) satisfies canonical naming rules,
(\mathsf{Asm}) parses under the pinned macro grammar,
(\mathsf{ParamSchema}) is well-formed,
(\mathsf{CanonReq}) is well-formed and deterministic,
(\mathsf{WEmit}) references only witness types and proof methods present in the verifier set,
(\mathsf{Tests}) is consistent with (\mathsf{Bind}) and with claimed verification levels,
dependencies are acyclic under the macro expansion graph (unless explicitly permitted by a pinned recursion policy that is bounded and deterministic).
15.1.2 Contract binding to transform entries
15.1.2(a) Binding object.Macro binding is a COS object (\mathsf{Bind}):[\mathsf{Bind}:=(h(\mathsf{LT}),\ \mathsf{entry_ref}?,\ \mathsf{SpecIn},\ \mathsf{SpecOut},\ h(\mathcal I),\ h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}),\ \mathsf{VL}_{\min},\ h(\mathsf{LossProfile})?).]
(h(\mathsf{LT})) is the legal transform contract hash.
(\mathsf{entry_ref}) optionally references a library entry id if the macro is an implementation of a catalog entry.
(\mathsf{SpecIn},\mathsf{SpecOut}) are convention bundles (Chapter 7).
(h(\mathcal I)), (h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})) bind the invariant set and tolerance profile to be satisfied by macro execution.
(\mathsf{VL}_{\min}) is the minimum verification level required for any proof claims emitted by the macro.
(h(\mathsf{LossProfile})) is present iff the macro implements a quotient-bearing or lossy transform (Chapter 8).
15.1.2(b) Binding correctness conditions.A macro instantiation is admissible only if:
its parameter map conforms to (\mathsf{LT})’s parameter schema and to (\mathsf{Macro})’s parameter schema (these may be identical or the macro schema may strictly extend the transform schema with auxiliary compile-time parameters),
the compiled program produced by expansion has endpoints matching the bound lenses of (h(\mathsf{LT})),
all intermediate spec transitions are compatible (or bridged explicitly within the expanded program),
the macro’s required invariants and tolerances are not stronger than those of the bound transform contract unless the macro includes explicit strengthening steps with their own proofs.
15.1.2(c) Entry-binding modes.A macro can bind to a library entry in one of two modes:
Entry implementation mode: (\mathsf{entry_ref}) points to an entry id and the macro provides the canonical program (\Pi) for that entry. The macro hash becomes the authoritative implementation reference for the entry.
Composite route mode: (\mathsf{entry_ref}) is absent; the macro composes multiple entries to implement a higher-level operation, producing a new transform contract (h(\mathsf{LT})) admitted separately.
In both modes, binding is explicit; no macro may be used as a transform unless it binds to a legal transform contract hash.
15.1.2(d) Binding and replay profile alignment.The macro must bind the replay profile hash (h(\mathcal R)) either through (h(\mathsf{LT})) or by explicit inclusion in (\mathsf{Bind}). If the macro expands into steps with different replay profiles, it is inadmissible unless explicit bridges or normalization steps reconcile the differences and the resulting composed replay profile is pinned and admitted.
15.1.3 Canonical expansion rules
15.1.3(a) Expansion function.Expansion is a deterministic function:[\mathsf{Expand}:\ (\mathsf{Macro},P,\mathsf{Context},\mathsf{ExpandPolicy})\to \Pi,]producing a canonical program (\Pi) (Chapter 8) and a canonical step-parameter sequence ((h(P_t))_{t=1}^n). Expansion is required to be referentially transparent: identical inputs yield identical output bytes.
15.1.3(b) Submacro expansion ordering.If a macro references submacros, expansion proceeds by a fixed ordering rule pinned by (h(\mathsf{CanonReq})):
submacros are expanded in program order,
if a line expands to multiple submacros, submacro expansion order is lexicographic by submacro id unless the assembly order is explicitly sequence-like and must be preserved,
submacro expansion depth is bounded by a pinned maximum depth (d_{\max}) included in (\mathsf{ExpandPolicy}).
No dynamic selection among submacros is permitted unless the selector is a pinned constant included in parameters.
15.1.3(c) Parameter substitution.Parameter substitution is purely functional. Each instruction line defines a parameter-binding map (P_t) constructed deterministically from:
the macro instantiation parameters (P),
explicitly provided line-local constants,
and explicitly referenced context bindings by key.All parameter keys are canonical, and value serialization is canonical; the step parameter hash (h(P_t)) is computed under canonical encoding. No implicit variable capture is permitted.
15.1.3(d) Canonical normalization insertion during expansion.Expansion must insert any required normalization steps mandated by:
convention compatibility rules,
(\mathsf{CanonReq}) insertion policy (e.g., mandatory normalizers after bridges, before commitments),
and corridor near-threshold mandated mitigations when such mitigations are expressible as compile-time requirements.
Inserted normalizers are explicit transform steps and appear as edges in the expanded program; omission is illegal if required by pinned policies.
15.1.4 Embedded test basis commitments
15.1.4(a) Tests bundle object.A macro’s (\mathsf{Tests}) object is:[\mathsf{Tests}:=(\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{TB}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(\mathsf{Claim}),h(\mathsf{Coverage})?,h(\mathsf{MethodSet})).]
(\mathsf{VL}) is the verification level.
(h(\mathsf{TB})) is the test basis object hash.
(h(\mathsf{ChkProg})) is the checker program identity hash.
(h(\mathsf{Claim})) binds invariants and tolerances.
(h(\mathsf{MethodSet})) lists required proof method ids.
15.1.4(b) Test adequacy and claim admissibility.If the macro claims equivalence or correctness beyond trivial schema validation, the tests bundle must be present and must be consistent with (\mathsf{Bind}). Claims are admissible only if:
the method id checker supports the declared verification level,
the test basis is valid for the required object schemas,
and the claim object does not exceed the method’s admissible claim class at that verification level.
15.1.4(c) Embedded tests as admission prerequisites.Library admission of a macro as an entry implementation requires an admission receipt that includes successful verification of the tests bundle. The tests bundle ensures that “human-readable” definitions are still mechanically grounded.
15.1.4(d) Test object immutability.Any modification to the tests bundle changes the macro hash. Therefore strengthening tests (higher verification level or broader invariant set) produces a distinct macro id, enabling explicit supersession by refinement proofs rather than silent upgrades.
15.2 Macro assembly language
15.2.1 Instruction line grammar
15.2.1(a) Lexical conventions.Macro assembly is a canonical text form that is itself encoded as a COS object. Canonicalization rules include:
UTF-8 encoding,
normalized line endings,
no significance of whitespace beyond token separation,
canonical ordering of key-value pairs where order is not semantically significant (applies to parameter maps within a line).
15.2.1(b) Instruction line structure.Each instruction line has the canonical abstract syntax:[\mathsf{Line}:=(\mathsf{opref},\ h(\mathsf{ParamExpr}),\ h(\mathsf{EmitSpec})?,\ h(\mathsf{AssertSpec})?),]where:
(\mathsf{opref}) references either a transform entry id, a submacro id, or a kernel primitive opcode id,
(\mathsf{ParamExpr}) is a parameter-expression object producing a concrete parameter map at expansion,
(\mathsf{EmitSpec}) describes witness emission annotations,
(\mathsf{AssertSpec}) optionally describes compile-time assertions (e.g., spec bundle equality, schema requirements) that must hold deterministically; failed assertions make expansion inadmissible.
15.2.1(c) Formal grammar (EBNF).Let (\langle\cdot\rangle) denote nonterminals and terminal tokens be quoted:
(\langle\mathsf{Program}\rangle ::= \langle\mathsf{Line}\rangle^\ast)
(\langle\mathsf{Line}\rangle ::= \langle\mathsf{OpRef}\rangle\ \langle\mathsf{Params}\rangle\ [\langle\mathsf{Emit}\rangle]\ [\langle\mathsf{Assert}\rangle])
(\langle\mathsf{OpRef}\rangle ::= "ENTRY:"\langle\mathsf{EntryId}\rangle\ |\ "MACRO:"\langle\mathsf{MacroId}\rangle\ |\ "KERNEL:"\langle\mathsf{OpcodeId}\rangle)
(\langle\mathsf{Params}\rangle ::= "params"\ "{"\ \langle\mathsf{KVList}\rangle\ "}")
(\langle\mathsf{Emit}\rangle ::= "emit"\ "{"\ \langle\mathsf{EmitList}\rangle\ "}")
(\langle\mathsf{Assert}\rangle ::= "assert"\ "{"\ \langle\mathsf{AssertList}\rangle\ "}")
(\langle\mathsf{KVList}\rangle) is a list of (\mathsf{key}=\mathsf{value}) pairs whose canonical encoding sorts keys lexicographically unless the schema declares sequence-like order for those pairs. (\langle\mathsf{EmitList}\rangle) and (\langle\mathsf{AssertList}\rangle) are set-like and sorted canonically.
15.2.1(d) Typed parsing and canonical AST encoding.Assembly text is parsed deterministically into a canonical AST object (h(\mathsf{Asm})). Equality of assembly programs for the macro system is definitional equality of canonical AST bytes, not of raw text, ensuring whitespace and formatting do not affect macro identity.
15.2.2 Parameter binding and hashing
15.2.2(a) Parameter expression evaluation.A parameter expression is a typed object:[\mathsf{ParamExpr}:=(h(\mathsf{Schema}),h(\mathsf{Bindings}),h(\mathsf{Derivations})),]where (\mathsf{Bindings}) maps parameter keys to one of:
literal typed values (quantized numerics, canonical strings),
object hash references,
context key references (resolved from (\mathsf{Context})),
or derived values computed by pinned deterministic derivation rules in (\mathsf{Derivations}).
15.2.2(b) Canonical parameter map construction.For each line (t), evaluate (\mathsf{ParamExpr}_t) to produce a concrete parameter map (P_t) that:
conforms to the line’s schema and the referenced entry or opcode parameter schema,
has keys sorted lexicographically,
has values encoded canonically (hash references for objects; quantized integers for numerics; fixed encoding for enums),
includes explicit spec hashes where required by compatibility protocol.
The step parameter hash is (h(P_t)).
15.2.2(c) No ambient parameter inheritance.Parameter inheritance from prior lines is illegal unless expressed explicitly as:
referencing prior line output hashes by name through a pinned binding mechanism, or
using context bindings updated by explicit commitment steps.Macros may not rely on “register state” that is not represented by committed artifacts and provenance roots.
15.2.2(d) Parameter determinism and replay.All parameter maps emitted by macro expansion are committed in the expanded program and thus in the plan hash. Replay determinism requires that parameter map construction uses only pinned derivation rules and committed inputs. Any dependence on system environment invalidates macro admissibility.
15.2.3 Witness emission annotations
15.2.3(a) Witness emission specification.The witness emission annotation (\mathsf{EmitSpec}) is a typed object:[\mathsf{EmitSpec}:=(\mathsf{req},\mathsf{emit},\mathsf{methods},\mathsf{lcd},\mathsf{severity}),]where:
(\mathsf{req}) is a bitset of witness types required before projection,
(\mathsf{emit}) is a bitset of witness types expected to be emitted by apply,
(\mathsf{methods}) is a canonical set of proof method ids expected/required (for proof witnesses),
(\mathsf{lcd}) is a canonical set of required local-check data hashes,
(\mathsf{severity}) is a pinned enum describing whether missing emissions are hard failures or only evidence-worthy (hard failure for all required witnesses).
15.2.3(b) Consistency with entry witness profiles.For a line referencing an entry, the line’s (\mathsf{EmitSpec}) must be consistent with the entry’s witness profile:
it cannot require fewer witnesses than the entry requires,
it may require additional witnesses only if those witnesses are required by the corridor policy or by macro-level strengthening steps,
it cannot request witness types unsupported by the runtime verifier set.
15.2.3(c) Binding of emitted witnesses.Emitted witnesses are step-local; macro annotations cannot relax binding requirements. Every emitted witness must bind to the step context (\mathsf{Ctx}_t). Macro annotations may specify which evidence hashes are required to be included in source roots for binding chains (e.g., repair-trigger evidence, dependency proof hashes).
15.2.3(d) Witness plan derivation.The macro object includes (h(\mathsf{WEmit})), a full witness plan template that is expanded into a step-indexed (\mathsf{WPlan}) segment for the expanded program. The derived witness plan is committed in the compiled plan and is enforced by the execution protocol.
15.2.4 Deterministic compilation to opcode stream
15.2.4(a) Compilation output.Macro compilation produces a canonical program:[\Pi^\star := [(\mathsf{op_id}_1,h(P_1)),\ldots,(\mathsf{op_id}_n,h(P_n))],]where each (\mathsf{op_id}_t) is:
a library entry id (for transform steps),
or a kernel opcode id (for kernel primitives),
never a raw code pointer.
15.2.4(b) Canonical expansion to entry steps.If a line references a macro, compilation replaces it by that macro’s canonical expansion, with parameter substitution applied deterministically. The expanded sequence is inserted into the parent sequence with no reordering beyond what is explicitly permitted by pinned canonicalization rules and certified rewrite proofs.
15.2.4(c) Canonical step descriptors and plan coupling.The compiled program can be mapped to step descriptors by the execution protocol (Chapter 13). Macro compilation must therefore ensure that each step’s parameters include:
required spec hashes,
required schema hashes,
required method ids and local-check data references as needed to satisfy witness plan and detector schedule.
15.2.4(d) Compilation determinism theorem (macro level).For a fixed macro hash (h(\mathsf{Macro})), fixed instantiation parameters (h(P)), fixed context hash (h(\mathsf{Context})), and fixed expansion policy hash, compilation yields a unique program hash (h(\Pi^\star)). Any deviation in expansion order, parameter hashing, or instruction selection is a determinism violation.
15.3 Macro legality theorem
15.3.1 Step-level legality implies macro legality
15.3.1(a) Macro legality predicate.A macro instantiation is legal under corridor policy (\Omega) iff executing its expanded program (\Pi^\star) from an admissible pre-state checkpoint yields a ledger segment that verifies and produces a post-state satisfying the macro’s binding contract (h(\mathsf{LT})) and claim object (h(\mathsf{Claim})).
15.3.1(b) Theorem (macro legality by step legality).Let (\Pi^\star) be the canonical expansion of a macro instantiation. Suppose that for each step (t) of (\Pi^\star):
the referenced entry/primitive is admissible and its contract is well-formed,
the step is feasible under corridor budgets and scope,
all required witnesses verify and bind to (\mathsf{Ctx}_t),
all mandatory detectors return non-failing results under (\Omega),
the kernel projector returns verdict (\mathsf{ACCEPT}) or (\mathsf{PARTIAL}) and applies deltas deterministically.
Then the macro execution is legal: the ledger segment verifies under replay, and the resulting macro-level state transition is a legal execution in the framework.
15.3.1(c) Macro-level failure.If any step fails hard legality and yields (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}), the macro execution does not produce the macro-level target artifacts. The macro legality predicate fails. Partial acceptance steps are permitted only as allowed by (\Omega) and by the macro’s binding; if partial acceptance discards any required effects for the macro’s contract, the macro execution is invalid for that contract.
15.3.1(d) Proof of theorem.The theorem follows from:
the execution kernel’s determinism and legality projector (Chapter 4),
corridor feasibility and witness enforcement (Chapters 3 and 5),
the fact that macro expansion yields a concrete program in the same execution model.Macro legality is therefore not a separate semantic layer; it is inherited from step legality.
15.3.2 Invariant preservation via witness plan
15.3.2(a) Macro-level invariant set.The macro binding declares an invariant set (h(\mathcal I)) and tolerance profile (h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})). The macro is permitted to expand into steps that preserve a superset of invariants or tighter tolerances, but it must not claim macro-level invariants stronger than can be justified by the expanded steps’ proof witnesses and tolerance accounting.
15.3.2(b) Witness plan sufficiency.The macro witness plan (\mathsf{WPlan}) is sufficient iff for each invariant (I\in\mathcal I), the expanded program contains:
either a direct transform contract that declares preservation of (I),
or a proof witness step whose claim object includes (I) and whose verification level meets requirements,and cumulative tolerance usage remains within (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) and corridor budgets.
15.3.2(c) Composition of invariant claims.Macro-level invariant preservation is derived by composition rules (Chapter 8): invariants preserved by the macro are those preserved by every required step in the chain (intersection, with explicit quotient handling). Any lossy step requires the macro’s binding to declare an explicit loss profile; otherwise the macro is ill-typed.
15.3.2(d) Evidence and audit.Macro execution must record evidence objects for near-threshold invariant drift and tolerance accumulation as required by (\Omega). Therefore macro-level invariants are not merely declared; their satisfaction is auditably supported by proof witnesses and evidence in the ledger segment.
15.3.3 Replay determinism under expansion
15.3.3(a) Replay determinism conditions.Replay determinism for macros requires:
canonical expansion is deterministic and produces fixed program bytes,
all step parameter hashes are deterministic,
all step deltas and witness lists are canonical,
all required proofs are verified deterministically under pinned methods,
any permitted pseudo-randomness is derived from pinned seeds and committed in source roots.
15.3.3(b) Expansion determinism implies replay determinism (conditional).If expansion determinism holds and each expanded step satisfies replay determinism, then the macro execution ledger segment is replay-deterministic: independent re-execution from the same pre-state checkpoint yields identical post-state hash and identical step record hashes.
15.3.3(c) Deterministic macro identifiers.Because macro ids are content-addressed, any change in assembly AST, parameter schema, canonicalization rules, witness plan, or tests bundle produces a different macro id. Therefore replay determinism is stable under macro identification: the macro id uniquely determines the expansion behavior.
15.3.3(d) Prohibition of runtime-dependent macro behavior.Macros may not branch on runtime state in a way that alters expansion. Any conditional behavior must be expressed as:
explicit route selection performed by the compiler (Chapter 12) producing a different plan hash, or
explicit candidate exploration in execution (Chapter 17) whose outputs are not commitments unless certified.Macro expansion is therefore static with respect to the plan; dynamic behavior is handled by other layers.
15.3.4 Composition closure in the library
15.3.4(a) Macro admission as library entries.A macro can be admitted into the transform library as an entry implementation iff:
its binding references a valid transform contract,
its expansion yields a program satisfying the contract under the library’s canonicalization and policy hashes,
its tests bundle verifies under the required verification level and claim class,
an admission receipt exists proving execution and verification under the reference runtime header.
15.3.4(b) Closure under composition.If macros are admitted as entries, they compose like entries: composition is legal iff spec bundles match or are bridged explicitly, witness profiles are supported, corridor budgets permit execution, and replay associativity constraints hold (Chapter 8). Macros do not introduce new composition semantics; they are programs over entries and kernel primitives.
15.3.4(c) Replay associativity for macros.A macro that is intended to serve as a canonical library entry must satisfy replay associativity for compositions where it appears, or must require explicit normalization/bridge boundaries that restore replay associativity. Otherwise, the macro is admissible only as a plan-local artifact, not as a canonical library entry.
15.3.4(d) Macro equivalence and rewrite proofs.Two macros may implement equivalent behavior while having different macro ids. Any claim of macro equivalence must be established by a certified rewrite proof between their expanded programs under declared invariants and verification level. Such equivalences enable optimization and substitution without informal reasoning.
15.4 Standard macro catalog
15.4.1 Hub macro templates
15.4.1(a) Hub macro requirements.Hub macros are canonical entry implementations for designated hub transforms. Each hub macro template must include:
strict SpecIn/SpecOut bundles,
strong invariant sets and explicit tolerances,
a minimum verification level (\mathsf{VL}_{\min}) pinned by library policy,
embedded test bundles suitable to detect the hub’s critical degrees of freedom,
and cached receipt hooks enabling reuse.
15.4.1(b) Hub macro structure.A hub macro template is typically a short sequence:
optional prerequisite normalization steps,
a core transform step,
optional proof emission step (if proof objects are separate from operator objects),
commitment steps pinning the hub operator artifact and alias (subject to corridor scope).
All of these are explicit; no hub macro relies on implicit state.
15.4.1(c) Hub macro parameterization.Hub macros must separate:
convention parameters (which must be bound into Spec bundles),
computational parameters (which may vary under the contract’s parameter schema).Any parameter that affects conventions must not be left as a free parameter; it must be committed by spec hash.
15.4.1(d) Hub macro admissibility.A hub macro is admissible for library entry status only if its admission receipt exists and its proof method ids are in the verifier registry with the required verification level support.
15.4.2 Bridge macro templates
15.4.2(a) Bridge macro role.Bridge macros implement convention bridge transforms (Chapter 10) as canonical programs. Bridge macros must declare:
the mapping between SpecIn and SpecOut convention bundles,
the explicit mapping rule object (permutation/scaling/sign/unit/domain),
the proof method id validating the mapping,
and mandatory post-bridge normalization steps.
15.4.2(b) Bridge macro minimality.Bridge macros must be minimal in scope: they should write only the proof store (for mapping proofs/evidence) and, when needed, commit only the bridge artifact itself and any canonical alias records. Any broader writes require explicit justification and are typically inadmissible for bridge class macros.
15.4.2(c) Bridge macro test adequacy.Bridge macros must embed test bases that detect the exact convention degrees of freedom they adjust. A bridge macro whose tests cannot detect its own mapping errors is inadmissible.
15.4.2(d) Bridge macro composition.Bridge macros may be chained by the compiler. Chaining must be deterministic and must respect post-bridge normalization insertion points. The chain’s cumulative tolerance usage is accounted in the tolerance ledger.
15.4.3 Repair macro templates
15.4.3(a) Repair macro role.Repair macros implement certified repair families (Chapter 10) and must bind:
defect evidence hashes in provenance source roots,
a repair goal predicate and completion proof,
strict scope isolation sets,
and any required bridge/normalizer steps.
15.4.3(b) Repair macro structure.A repair macro template includes:
evidence ingestion (dependency proof references and source-root binding),
repair execution steps (bridge/regrid/renormalize/proof regeneration),
completion proof emission (repair completion proof object),
optional commitment of a repair receipt pointer (if the system pins repair receipts for indexing).
15.4.3(c) Repair macro determinism.Repair macros must not choose actions dynamically at runtime. Repair selection is a compile-time decision produced by the repair planner from defect evidence and pinned policies. If branching among repair options is permitted, the branching must be represented as explicit candidate exploration under caps, with commitment only after proof selection.
15.4.3(d) Repair macro auditability.Repair macros must emit evidence objects linking:
defect evidence (\to) repair plan (\to) repair completion proof (\to) repair receipt.This evidence chain is required for audit and for prevention of “silent repair” narratives.
15.4.4 Testing and admission procedures
15.4.4(a) Macro admission bundle.A macro admission bundle (\mathsf{Admit}) must include:
a reference plan that instantiates the macro under canonical parameters,
the reference receipt verifying successful execution,
proof objects verifying all embedded tests,
and a binding to the runtime header hashes under which admission holds.
15.4.4(b) Admission predicates.A macro is admitted into the standard macro catalog iff:
its expansion is deterministic and bounded,
its expanded program is feasible under default corridor class constraints,
required proof methods are present and support the macro’s declared verification level,
its admission receipt verifies under replay,
it satisfies replay associativity constraints when designated as a compositional library entry, or else is explicitly marked as non-associative and restricted to plan-local use.
15.4.4(c) Regression and supersession.Any change to a macro (assembly, witness plan, tests, bindings) produces a new macro id. Supersession of an older macro by a newer one requires an explicit refinement proof at the macro/program level showing that the new macro’s expanded program refines or equals the old macro’s claims under the strength preorder. Silent replacement is forbidden.
15.4.4(d) Catalog pinning and indexing.Standard macros are pinned under canonical namespaces:
(\mathsf{MACRO}/\langle \mathsf{macro_id}\rangle \to h(\mathsf{Macro})),
(\mathsf{MACRO}/\mathsf{HUB}/\langle \mathsf{name}\rangle),
(\mathsf{MACRO}/\mathsf{BRIDGE}/\langle \mathsf{name}\rangle),
(\mathsf{MACRO}/\mathsf{REPAIR}/\langle \mathsf{name}\rangle),with alias records explicit and contradiction-controlled. Index updates require commitment witnesses and, where necessary, consistency proofs.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 15 — MACRO SYSTEM: HUMAN-READABLE PROGRAMS WITH RIGOR
CHAPTER 16 — OPTIMIZATION: PROOF-CARRYING REWRITES
This chapter specifies optimization as a certified program transformation discipline. An optimizer is not permitted to “improve performance” by changing semantics informally. Every optimization is a rewrite that must be (i) admissible under a safety contract, (ii) convention-preserving (or explicitly bridged), (iii) non-weakening with respect to corridor policy and required verification strength, and (iv) accompanied by proof objects that are locally verifiable under the witness system. Optimization outputs are executable programs whose equivalence to the inputs is either exact or bounded under explicitly declared invariants and tolerances.
16.1 Optimization safety contract
16.1.1 Rewrite admissibility conditions
16.1.1(a) Rewrite as legal transform on programs.A rewrite is a legal transform (\mathsf{RW}) whose carrier is the program space:[\mathsf{RW}:\ \Pi \mapsto \Pi',]where (\Pi,\Pi') are COS program objects. (\mathsf{RW}) is admissible only if it is represented by a well-formed rewrite rule object (Section 16.2) and is executed under a corridor policy (\Omega) that permits optimization steps (scope and budgets for proof-store writes, detector evidence, and optional commitment of optimization receipts).
16.1.1(b) Admissibility predicate.A proposed rewrite application at program position interval ([i,j]) is admissible only if:
the input program is in canonical program normal form (\mathsf{PNF}(\Pi)=\Pi) under the pinned normal-form policy (Chapter 8),
the pattern matches deterministically at ([i,j]) under the pinned matcher,
all rewrite guards evaluate to true deterministically (Section 16.2.2),
convention compatibility holds after replacement (Section 16.1.3),
the corridor non-weakening requirement holds (Section 16.1.4),
required equivalence proofs are produced or referenced and verify under the verifier set (Section 16.1.2).
Failure of any admissibility component makes the rewrite illegal.
16.1.1(c) Rewrite scope discipline.Rewrite steps may write only to:
the proof store (to emit rewrite proofs and evidence),
and optionally to a plan store or macro store (to record optimized program artifacts),
and to commitment store only if explicitly authorized (e.g., pin optimized plan or program).A rewrite may not mutate unrelated commitments or relations. Any scope expansion requires witness-gated authorization under (\Omega).
16.1.1(d) Deterministic stability of admissibility.Admissibility must be replay-deterministic. Therefore:
pattern matching and guard evaluation are purely functions of (\Pi), the rule object hash, and pinned policy hashes,
tie-break choices are pinned,
and any cost model used for selection is pinned by (\Omega) or by the optimization policy object bound into parameters.
16.1.2 Required equivalence proofs
16.1.2(a) Rewrite proof requirement.Every admissible rewrite must yield a rewrite proof object (\mathsf{ProofRw}) (Chapter 6) binding:[\mathsf{ProofRw}:=(h(\Pi),h(\Pi'),h(\mathcal I),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}),\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{SpecSet}),h(\mathsf{TB}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),\mathsf{rule_id},h(\mathsf{MatchEvidence}),h(\mathsf{GuardEvidence})).]The proof binds:
input and output program hashes,
invariant set and tolerance profile under which equivalence is claimed,
verification level (\mathsf{VL}),
convention spec set,
the rule id hash,
evidence demonstrating where the pattern matched and that guards held.
16.1.2(b) Equivalence claim boundary.A rewrite can claim:
exact equivalence when (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}=\mathbf{0}) and invariants include all observables required by downstream goals, or
bounded equivalence when (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\neq\mathbf{0}), in which case tolerance accounting must be integrated into corridor tolerance ledgers and must not violate required exactness constraints.
Equivalence is always relative to explicit invariants, tolerances, and verification level; no rewrite may claim stronger equivalence than what its checker supports.
16.1.2(c) Proof reuse.A rewrite may reuse an existing proof object only if:
the proof binds the same input program hash and output program hash,
the rule id and match interval are the same (or provably equivalent under canonical program normal form),
the proof’s policy hashes are compatible with the current runtime header,
the proof’s binding can be step-locally re-bound (Chapter 5 provenance binding).Otherwise, reuse is certificate laundering and is prohibited.
16.1.2(d) Multi-rule composed proofs.If an optimizer applies multiple rewrites, it may produce:
one proof per rewrite step, or
a composite proof object certifying the entire (\Pi\to\Pi') transformation, provided the composite proof is locally verifiable and binds the full rule sequence and intermediate program hashes. Composition must preserve proof soundness under pinned claim boundaries.
16.1.3 Convention preservation constraints
16.1.3(a) Spec preservation predicate.Let (\mathsf{Spec}(\Pi)) denote the convention bundle sequence induced by the program’s steps (from entry SpecIn/SpecOut and explicit bridges). A rewrite is convention-preserving iff:
it preserves boundary spec bundles at the replacement boundaries, and
it does not introduce any spec change that is not accounted for by explicit bridge steps in (\Pi').
Formally, if the matched interval ([i,j]) is replaced by ([i',j']), then:[\mathsf{SpecOut}(\Pi_{i-1}) = \mathsf{SpecIn}(\Pi'{i'})\quad\text{and}\quad\mathsf{SpecOut}(\Pi'{j'}) = \mathsf{SpecIn}(\Pi_{j+1}),]with the understanding that boundary indices must exist (or be the program boundary) and all equalities are byte-equalities of convention bundle hashes.
16.1.3(b) Bridge insertion requirement.If a replacement would change boundary specs, then the rewrite must explicitly insert certified bridge steps and include their proof obligations. The rewrite proof must bind the bridge entry ids and prove that the net effect preserves required invariants and does not violate corridor tolerance budgets. Implicit bridge insertion is prohibited; it must appear as explicit steps in (\Pi').
16.1.3(c) Canonical normalization insertion.If a rewrite inserts bridges, it must also insert mandatory normalization steps at the policy-required insertion points (Chapter 7). The optimizer may not omit these steps; omission would render (\Pi') infeasible or illegal.
16.1.3(d) Convention fingerprint invariants.Optimization cannot alter convention fingerprints of committed artifacts unless the optimization’s purpose is explicitly a convention bridge (which is then a different class of operation and must be represented as such). In general, optimization is semantic-preserving; convention changes are not optimizations but transports.
16.1.4 Corridor non-weakening requirement
16.1.4(a) Non-weakening definition.Let (\Omega) be the corridor policy under which (\Pi) is intended to execute. A rewrite (\Pi\to\Pi') is corridor-non-weakening iff for every execution of (\Pi') under (\Omega) satisfying the plan’s witness and detector obligations:
(\Pi') does not require weaker determinism settings than (\Pi),
(\Pi') does not require skipping detectors required by (\Omega) or the ledger plan,
(\Pi') does not expand scope to forbidden targets or require additional scope extensions not already permitted,
(\Pi') does not increase tolerance usage beyond what the goal and (\Omega) permit (unless the goal explicitly allows it and the rewrite proof’s claim object records this change as a refinement in the opposite direction, which would not be admissible when exactness is required).
16.1.4(b) Cost non-weakening vs safety.Non-weakening does not require that (\Pi') have lower cost; it requires that (\Pi') not weaken safety guarantees. Cost reduction is a goal of optimization, but safety constraints dominate.
16.1.4(c) Budget feasibility constraint.The optimizer may produce a program that is infeasible under current corridor budgets; such a rewrite is not admissible for execution under (\Omega), though it may be admissible as a hypothetical program if explicitly labeled as such and not committed as an executable plan. For executable optimization, feasibility under (\Omega) is required.
16.1.4(d) Non-weakening proof obligation.The rewrite proof must include a corridor compliance proof component:[\mathsf{ProofCorr}:=(h(\Omega),h(\Pi),h(\Pi'),h(\mathsf{DetReq}),h(\mathsf{Detectors}),h(\mathsf{ScopeReq}),h(\mathsf{TolReq})),]verified deterministically by checking that (\Pi')’s required policy components are (\preceq) (\Omega) (i.e., do not weaken constraints) under pinned policy-preorder rules.
16.2 Rewrite rule format
16.2.1 Pattern matching in program normal form
16.2.1(a) Pattern object.A rewrite rule includes a pattern (\mathsf{Pat}) that matches a contiguous subprogram in canonical normal form. (\mathsf{Pat}) is a typed object:[\mathsf{Pat}:=(\mathsf{len},\ [\mathsf{StepPat}1,\ldots,\mathsf{StepPat}{\mathsf{len}}],\ h(\mathsf{Vars}),\ h(\mathsf{SpecReq}),\ h(\mathsf{SchemaReq})),]where each (\mathsf{StepPat}) specifies:
required op kind (entry class or specific entry id),
parameter constraints (exact matches on some keys, variable bindings on others),
required spec bundles on boundaries or within steps.
16.2.1(b) Variable binding and canonical substitution.Pattern matching produces a substitution map (\theta) mapping pattern variables to concrete values (hashes or quantized integers). Substitution is canonical: variables are ordered lexicographically, and (\theta) is encoded as a canonical map. The match evidence object must commit to (\theta) and the match interval ([i,j]).
16.2.1(c) Deterministic matcher.The matcher is pinned by hash and must be deterministic:
it scans the program in increasing index order,
it matches the first admissible location under pinned tie-break rules,
it emits match evidence for each match candidate if required by (\Omega), but selection is deterministic.
16.2.1(d) Normal form requirement.Patterns are defined over canonical program normal form (\mathsf{PNF}). If (\Pi) is not in (\mathsf{PNF}), optimization must first apply (\mathsf{PNF}) (as a legal transform on programs) and produce a proof (or rely on the definitional property) that (\Pi) and (\mathsf{PNF}(\Pi)) are equivalent under the required invariants.
16.2.2 Guards and deterministic predicates
16.2.2(a) Guard object.Guards are a set-like list of deterministic predicates:[\mathsf{Guards}:=[g_1,\ldots,g_m],]each guard (g_k) referencing a pinned predicate id with parameters derived from (\theta) and from the surrounding program context. Guards enforce conditions such as:
domain restrictions for transforms,
invariants required by downstream steps,
cost feasibility thresholds,
absence of forbidden side effects (e.g., no writes to contradiction-controlled namespaces in the matched interval),
absence of lossy steps when exactness is required.
16.2.2(b) Guard evaluation.Guard evaluation is deterministic and produces a guard evidence object:[\mathsf{GuardEvidence}:=(h(\mathsf{rule}),[g_k\mapsto \mathsf{true/false}]_{k=1}^m,h(\theta),\mathsf{interval}),]encoded canonically. Any false guard makes the rewrite inadmissible at that interval.
16.2.2(c) Guard dependency resolution.Guards may inspect:
the matched steps’ contracts and spec bundles,
the corridor policy (\Omega),
and any pinned library metadata (e.g., invertibility tiers, loss profiles).Guards may not inspect uncommitted runtime state; optimization is a compile-time operation driven by pinned artifacts.
16.2.2(d) Guard monotonicity.Guards that rely on corridor constraints must be monotone with respect to policy strengthening: if a rewrite is admissible under (\Omega), it remains admissible under a stricter corridor (\Omega') unless (\Omega') forbids the rewrite class outright. This ensures stable behavior under tightened safety policies.
16.2.3 Replacement forms and bridge insertion
16.2.3(a) Replacement template.A rewrite rule includes a replacement template (\mathsf{Repl}):[\mathsf{Repl}:=(\mathsf{len}',[\mathsf{StepTpl}1,\ldots,\mathsf{StepTpl}{\mathsf{len}'}],h(\mathsf{SpecOutReq}),h(\mathsf{PostNormReq})).]Each (\mathsf{StepTpl}) specifies:
an opref (entry id or kernel opcode),
parameter construction rules from (\theta),
required witness emission annotations,
required spec requirements.
16.2.3(b) Bridge insertion within replacement.If the replacement changes conventions internally, it must include explicit bridge steps. The rule must specify which bridge class and which entry ids are admissible. Bridge selection is deterministic via pinned tie-breaks (e.g., choose lexicographically earliest minimal-cost bridge entry satisfying required verification level and tolerance constraints).
16.2.3(c) Post-replacement normalization.Replacement templates must include any mandatory normalization steps required by Chapter 7 and by the corridor’s mitigation policies. Post-normalization requirements are specified by (h(\mathsf{PostNormReq})) and enforced during rewrite application.
16.2.3(d) Replacement correctness boundary.Replacement must preserve:
boundary spec bundles,
required invariants and tolerances,
corridor non-weakening properties,as certified by the rewrite proof. Any replacement that would weaken these properties is inadmissible.
16.2.4 Rule versioning and hashing
16.2.4(a) Rewrite rule object.A rewrite rule is a COS object:[\mathsf{Rule}:=(\mathsf{rule_id},\mathsf{version},h(\mathsf{Pat}),h(\mathsf{Guards}),h(\mathsf{Repl}),h(\mathsf{Claims}),h(\mathsf{PolicyReq}),h(\mathsf{Tests})).](\mathsf{rule_id}=h(\mathsf{Rule})) is the hash of canonical bytes. Any change yields a new rule id.
16.2.4(b) Claims object.(\mathsf{Claims}) binds:
invariants and tolerance profile under which equivalence is claimed,
minimum verification level for rewrite proof,
whether the rewrite is exact or bounded,
any required loss/quotient profiles (typically absent; if present, the rule is not an optimization but a semantic change and must be classified accordingly and constrained by goals).
16.2.4(c) Policy requirement object.(\mathsf{PolicyReq}) binds the minimum corridor class and determinism requirements under which the rule is admissible. A rule requiring weaker determinism settings than the runtime header provides is inadmissible.
16.2.4(d) Rule admission and deprecation.Rules are admitted into the optimizer rule set only with admission receipts verifying:
deterministic matcher correctness,
correctness of replacement under declared claims on representative test bases,
and non-weakening constraints.Rules may be deprecated by explicit deprecation records; no silent removal is permitted for canonical optimization catalogs.
16.3 Optimization classes
16.3.1 Peephole simplification
16.3.1(a) Definition.Peephole simplification replaces a small local pattern with a simpler one, typically:
eliminating redundant normalizers when already in normal form,
canceling inverse pairs (where exact inverses are certified),
removing dead steps whose outputs are unused and whose side effects are provably absent,
merging adjacent steps when a certified composite exists.
16.3.1(b) Safety conditions.Peephole rules are admissible only if they preserve:
required invariants under exact or bounded equivalence,
convention bundles at boundaries,
and do not introduce additional proof obligations that cannot be met.
16.3.1(c) Proof obligations.Each peephole application emits a rewrite proof binding:
the matched interval,
the rule id,
the input and output program hashes,
and the invariant/tolerance claims.
16.3.1(d) Deterministic match selection.The optimizer applies peephole rules in deterministic order: scan from left to right, apply the first admissible rule under pinned rule ordering and tie-break policies, then continue scanning according to a pinned rescan strategy.
16.3.2 Common subroute elimination
16.3.2(a) Definition.Common subroute elimination identifies duplicated subprogram segments that compute identical intermediate artifacts and replaces duplicate computations by reuse of a single computed artifact, expressed by:
factoring repeated subroutes into a shared macro or shared plan segment,
inserting explicit dependency consumption from the shared segment,
and adjusting provenance roots accordingly.
16.3.2(b) Correctness requirements.Elimination is admissible only if:
the duplicated subroutes are certified equivalent under required invariants and conventions,
the shared computation is executed before all consumers (topological order),
and the new dependency references are explicit and verifiable by dependency proofs.
16.3.2(c) Proof obligations.The optimizer must produce:
equivalence proofs for each eliminated duplicate subroute,
dependency proof objects showing that consumers now consume the shared artifact,
and a proof that the transformation preserves corridor non-weakening constraints.
16.3.2(d) Impact on receipts.Common subroute elimination may reduce compute and proof-store writes but may increase dependency proof burden. The optimization cost model accounts for both. The output plan’s receipt commits to the new dependency roots and witness roots.
16.3.3 Hub shortcuts (certified)
16.3.3(a) Definition.A hub shortcut replaces a longer route segment with a single hub transform or hub macro that is certified to be equivalent to the segment under declared invariants and conventions. Hubs are designated library entries with strong contracts and reusable receipts (Chapter 11).
16.3.3(b) Admissibility.A hub shortcut is admissible only if:
the hub entry’s SpecIn/SpecOut match the boundary specs of the replaced segment (or explicit bridges are inserted),
the hub’s invariant set and tolerance profile refine or match the replaced segment’s claims,
and the hub’s verification level meets or exceeds required levels.
16.3.3(c) Proof obligations.A hub shortcut requires a rewrite proof certifying equivalence between:
the original segment program hash,
and the hub program hash (with inserted bridges/normalizers as needed),under declared invariants and tolerances. Proof may reference cached hub receipts when permitted and compatible.
16.3.3(d) Shortcut selection determinism.If multiple hubs can replace a segment, the optimizer selects deterministically by:
exactness preference,
highest verification level,
minimal tolerance usage,
minimal hop count,
minimal witness burden,
lexicographic tie-break on hub entry id.
16.3.4 Witness minimization and tolerance tightening
16.3.4(a) Witness minimization.Witness minimization seeks to reduce proof-store writes and verification burden without weakening guarantees by:
selecting transforms whose correctness is supported by stronger built-in contracts requiring fewer auxiliary proofs,
reusing existing receipts and proofs via dependency proofs,
replacing multi-proof sequences with a single stronger proof method where admissible.
Witness minimization is admissible only under corridor non-weakening constraints.
16.3.4(b) Tolerance tightening as optimization.Tolerance tightening is a correctness optimization: it strengthens claim boundaries (smaller (\varepsilon), higher verification level) to reduce long-chain drift risk and enable more aggressive reuse. Tightening is permitted only if feasible under corridor budgets and explicitly requested or required by policy (e.g., near-threshold detectors).
16.3.4(c) Tightening and rewrite proofs.Any rewrite that tightens tolerances must produce:
a rewrite proof for behavioral equivalence under the tighter tolerance profile,
and (if necessary) proof regeneration steps that raise verification level or strengthen test basis coverage.
16.3.4(d) Optimization objective hierarchy.Safety dominates: an optimizer may choose to increase cost to tighten tolerances and reduce drift risk when required. The objective function is pinned and typically lexicographic: preserve exactness, preserve invariants, preserve conventions, then reduce cost.
16.4 Optimizer algorithm
16.4.1 Deterministic application order
16.4.1(a) Rule ordering.The optimizer maintains a pinned ordered list of rewrite rule ids ([\mathsf{rule_id}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{rule_id}_m]). Ordering is canonical and part of the optimization policy object. The optimizer must iterate rules in this order.
16.4.1(b) Scan strategy.The optimizer uses a pinned scan strategy:
left-to-right scan for the earliest admissible match,
apply at most one rule per scan pass or apply maximal non-overlapping matches depending on pinned policy,
rescan window policy after applying a rewrite (e.g., rescan from (i-\delta) for a pinned (\delta)).
All scan behaviors are deterministic functions of the current program bytes.
16.4.1(c) Termination.Optimization terminates when:
no rule matches admissibly, or
a pinned maximum number of rewrite applications is reached, or
a pinned budget for optimization compute cost is exhausted.Termination conditions are deterministic and recorded as evidence.
16.4.1(d) Normal form stabilization.After each rewrite (or at the end), the optimizer applies (\mathsf{PNF}) to restore canonical program normal form if required by policy, and records the resulting program hash. (\mathsf{PNF}) application is deterministic.
16.4.2 Cost model and selection
16.4.2(a) Optimization cost functional.The optimizer evaluates candidate rewrites by a pinned cost functional:[\mathrm{OptCost}(\Pi)=(\varepsilon\text{-priority},\ C_{\varepsilon},\ C_{\mathsf{risk}},\ C_{\mathsf{wit}},\ C_{\mathsf{budget}},\ C_{\mathsf{write}},\ \mathsf{LexKey}(\Pi)),]where:
(\varepsilon\text{-priority}) and (C_{\varepsilon}) enforce exactness/tolerance preference,
(C_{\mathsf{risk}}) penalizes near-threshold tolerance accumulation and drift risk,
witness/budget/write costs reflect corridor consumption,
(\mathsf{LexKey}) is the lexicographic tie-break on program hash and rule application trace.
The optimizer selects rewrites that strictly improve (\mathrm{OptCost}) under lexicographic comparison, subject to non-weakening constraints.
16.4.2(b) Feasibility gating.A rewrite candidate is considered only if the resulting program is feasible under corridor budgets and scope for the intended execution environment. If feasibility cannot be established statically, the candidate is rejected unless a policy permits emitting a “hypothetical” optimized plan marked non-executable without additional budgets.
16.4.2(c) Exactness preservation priority.Any rewrite that would convert an exact route into an approximate route is inadmissible unless the goal explicitly permits approximation and the optimization policy allows trading exactness for cost. Even then, the rewrite must record the changed claim boundary explicitly and must not be used when downstream requires exactness.
16.4.2(d) Deterministic tie-breaks.If multiple rewrite candidates yield identical (\mathrm{OptCost}), choose the one whose resulting program hash is lexicographically smallest. If still tied, choose the smallest rule id sequence (lexicographically). This ensures canonical optimized programs.
16.4.3 Proof emission pipeline
16.4.3(a) Proof objects for each rewrite.For each applied rewrite, emit:
match evidence object,
guard evidence object,
rewrite proof object (\mathsf{ProofRw}),
corridor non-weakening proof component (or evidence object) binding (\Omega) and policy requirements.
All emitted objects are stored in the proof store and referenced by the optimization ledger segment or plan artifact.
16.4.3(b) Proof chaining.The optimizer may construct a proof chain:[\Pi_0 \xrightarrow{\mathsf{ProofRw}_1} \Pi_1 \xrightarrow{\mathsf{ProofRw}_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{\mathsf{ProofRw}_k} \Pi_k,]and optionally produce a composite proof that (\Pi_0\sim \Pi_k). Each proof binds to its immediate predecessor/successor program hashes. This prevents proof gaps.
16.4.3(c) Proof retention policy.The corridor policy determines which proofs must be retained as commitments and which may remain in the proof store without pinning. However, any downstream receipt that claims the optimized program is equivalent to the original must be able to provide the necessary rewrite proofs for verification. Therefore proof retention must be consistent with intended reuse and disclosure mode.
16.4.3(d) Proof verifier integration.All proofs emitted by the optimizer are verified under the same pinned verifier set. Any proof verification failure invalidates the rewrite and must cause optimization to revert to the prior program state, with evidence emitted.
16.4.4 Replay preservation and receipts
16.4.4(a) Optimization as an executed plan segment.Optimization itself can be executed as a plan segment in the framework, producing:
a ledger segment recording each rewrite,
an optimized program object pinned in the proof store,
and optionally a commitment pin to the optimized plan/program.
This execution is replay-verifiable.
16.4.4(b) Optimization receipt.An optimization receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}_{\mathsf{opt}}) commits to:
input program hash (h(\Pi_{\mathrm{in}})),
output program hash (h(\Pi_{\mathrm{out}})),
the set of rewrite proof hashes (witness root),
the optimization policy hash (rule set, matcher, cost model),
and the runtime header/corridor policy hashes under which optimization was verified.
16.4.4(c) Use of optimized programs.A downstream execution may use the optimized program in place of the original only if:
the optimization receipt verifies,
the rewrite proofs establish equivalence under required invariants and conventions,
corridor non-weakening holds for the downstream environment,
and the optimized program is feasible under (\Omega).
16.4.4(d) Preservation of replay identity.Optimization may change execution traces and thus ledger hashes, but it must preserve claimed outputs under declared equivalence. Any claim of identical outputs must be supported by proofs and receipts; otherwise the system treats the optimized run as a different computation with distinct receipts, even if numerically similar.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 16 — OPTIMIZATION: PROOF-CARRYING REWRITES
CHAPTER 17 — SEARCH AND EXPLORATION UNDER CONSTRAINT
This chapter specifies constrained exploration: how the framework generates hypotheses, scores them, truncates deterministically, and transitions from exploration to commitment only by certified proof. Exploration is treated as first-class computation but is strictly budgeted, scope-limited, replay-deterministic, and auditable. The goal is to allow necessary search (routing, model selection, candidate derivations, ambiguity handling) without permitting uncontrolled branching, silent approximation, or uncitable “best guess” commitments.
17.1 Candidate generation semantics
17.1.1 Hypothesis spaces and canonical enumeration
17.1.1(a) Hypothesis space object.A hypothesis space is a typed COS object:[\mathsf{HypSpace}:=(\mathsf{hs_id},\ \mathsf{kind},\ h(\mathsf{GenRule}),\ h(\mathsf{Domain}),\ h(\mathsf{CanonOrder}),\ h(\mathsf{DedupRule}),\ \mathsf{bounds}).]
(\mathsf{kind}) identifies the hypothesis family (route candidates, parameter candidates, proof method candidates, model candidates, repair candidates).
(\mathsf{GenRule}) is a pinned deterministic generator rule defining how candidates are produced.
(\mathsf{Domain}) binds the admissible candidate domain (constraints, allowable parameter ranges).
(\mathsf{CanonOrder}) defines canonical enumeration order.
(\mathsf{DedupRule}) defines candidate equivalence for deduplication.
(\mathsf{bounds}) includes explicit caps on enumeration length, depth, or complexity.
17.1.1(b) Canonical enumeration function.Candidate enumeration is a deterministic function:[\mathsf{Enum}:\ (\mathsf{HypSpace},\mathsf{seed}?)\to (c_1,c_2,\ldots),]returning a (conceptual) infinite stream of candidates in canonical order, truncated by corridor caps. If a seed is permitted, it must be a pinned seed object hash included in source roots. If seeds are disallowed by (\Omega), (\mathsf{Enum}) must be seedless and purely deterministic from (\mathsf{HypSpace}).
17.1.1(c) Canonical order and stable identifiers.Each candidate (c) is a typed object with stable hash (h(c)). Canonical enumeration order is defined by a pinned comparator that depends only on canonical candidate bytes (or on canonical derived keys computed deterministically). Enumeration must not depend on hash-table iteration, concurrency, timing, or platform behavior.
17.1.1(d) Deduplication rule.Deduplication is deterministic: a candidate is retained iff it is not equivalent to any earlier retained candidate under (\mathsf{DedupRule}). The deduplication rule is pinned and may be:
strict hash equality, or
equivalence under a declared invariant/tolerance profile certified by equivalence proofs (expensive and therefore corridor-budgeted).Any deduplication beyond hash equality requires explicit proof methods and is treated as a proof-bearing action.
17.1.2 Scoring and quantization
17.1.2(a) Score function object.Candidate scoring is defined by a pinned score function object:[\mathsf{ScoreFn}:=(\mathsf{sf_id},\ h(\mathsf{Inputs}),\ h(\mathsf{Rule}),\ h(Q_{\mathsf{score}}),\ \mathsf{range},\ \mathsf{tie_break}).]
(\mathsf{Rule}) references a deterministic scoring algorithm (e.g., route cost functional, proof burden cost, feasibility penalty).
(h(Q_{\mathsf{score}})) pins score quantization.
17.1.2(b) Quantized scoring.Scores are integers (s_q\in\mathbb{Z}) under a pinned quantization policy (Q_{\mathsf{score}}). The scoring function computes a real-valued score internally only if allowed by numeric policy, but the committed score is the quantized integer. Canonical encoding of candidate sets uses these quantized scores and therefore is stable under replay.
17.1.2(c) Multi-objective scoring.If scoring is multi-objective, it is encoded as a lexicographic score vector:[\mathbf{s}_q = (s^{(0)}_q,s^{(1)}_q,\ldots,s^{(k)}_q)\in\mathbb{Z}^{k+1},]canonically encoded in fixed dimension, with pinned interpretation rules. Candidate comparison uses lexicographic order; ties are resolved by a pinned tie-break rule such as candidate hash ordering.
17.1.2(d) Score evidence.If (\Omega) requires auditability for exploration, a scoring evidence object may be emitted for each retained candidate (or for the top-K candidates), containing:
candidate hash,
score vector,
scoring rule id hash,
any key feature values used in scoring (quantized),
binding to step context.Evidence emission is corridor-budgeted and may be required for repair triggers or disambiguation audits.
17.1.3 Deterministic truncation
17.1.3(a) Truncation operator.Given a set of scored candidates (S={(c_i,\mathbf{s}i)}{i=1}^M) and a branch cap (b), truncation returns a deterministic retained subset (S') of size (\le b) by:
sorting by canonical comparator (\prec) induced by score vectors and tie-break rule,
retaining the first (b) candidates,
emitting a truncation mask (m) mapping candidate hashes to keep/drop decisions.
The comparator and tie-break rule are pinned by policy.
17.1.3(b) Stability of truncation under replay.Truncation must be replay-deterministic: for identical inputs and identical policies, the retained set and truncation mask are identical. Any nondeterminism in candidate ordering or tie-break violates determinism and triggers replay divergence defects.
17.1.3(c) Truncation evidence.When truncation occurs, evidence must be emitted (if required by (\Omega)) including:
branch cap (b),
number of candidates before truncation,
hash of sorted candidate list (or its Merkle root),
truncation mask hash,
and the comparator id hash.
This evidence supports repair analysis and tuning of caps.
17.1.3(d) Interaction with deduplication.Deduplication precedes truncation under canonical ordering rules: the retained set is computed from the deduplicated sequence. If deduplication is proof-based, its cost and evidence requirements are accounted before truncation.
17.1.4 Branch caps and bounded exploration
17.1.4(a) Branch cap semantics.The corridor policy (\Omega) defines a branch cap (b_{\mathsf{branch}}) and possibly per-class caps (b_{\mathsf{branch}}(\mathsf{kind})). Exploration steps must not cause the candidate store to exceed these caps after projection; any excess is deterministically truncated. If (\Omega) treats branch cap violations as hard failures for certain classes, the step is rejected rather than truncated.
17.1.4(b) Expansion cap and recursion depth.Exploration may involve progressive deepening (Section 17.4.2). Each step declares expansion depth (\lambda); (\Omega) enforces ( \lambda\le b_\lambda). Any attempt to exceed (b_\lambda) without admissible escalation is illegal.
17.1.4(c) Exploration budget accounting.Exploration consumes:
compute budget for enumeration and scoring,
write budget for candidate store mutations and evidence objects,
tolerance budgets if approximate scoring or approximate feasibility checks are used.All consumption is committed in the step cost vector and thus is replay-verifiable.
17.1.4(d) No uncontrolled search.Any exploration that would require unbounded search must be represented as either:
a bounded enumeration under caps, or
a trace-proof-producing procedure (Chapter 6) that records the search transcript and allows local verification.Unbounded search without transcript is inadmissible.
17.2 Exploratory transforms
17.2.1 Exploration-class steps and scope limits
17.2.1(a) Exploration-class transform definition.An exploration-class transform is a legal transform contract whose primary target is the candidate store (\mathsf{Cand}) (Chapter 4) and whose output commitments are not permitted by default. Such transforms have (\mathsf{kind}=\mathsf{EXPLORATION}) in their contract metadata and must declare:
strict scope: allowed writes are limited to candidate store and proof store (for evidence),
no direct writes to contradiction-controlled commitments unless explicitly authorized by a transition-to-commitment step (Section 17.2.4),
explicit branch caps and truncation policies.
17.2.1(b) Scope barrier between exploration and commitment.Exploration-class steps are forbidden from:
pinning new committed truths,
overwriting contradiction-controlled pins,
declaring merges of identities,unless accompanied by certified commitment-transition proofs and explicit scope-extension witnesses under (\Omega). By default, exploration steps produce candidates, not commitments.
17.2.1(c) Exploration artifacts.Exploration produces:
candidate set objects (\mathsf{CandSet}),
routing evidence objects (optional but policy-gated),
guard and feasibility evidence objects,
and possibly trace proofs if the exploration includes constrained search whose transcript is required.
All such artifacts are stored in the proof store and referenced by candidate store entries.
17.2.1(d) Feasibility under (\Omega).Exploration-class transforms must be feasible under corridor budgets. If exploration is likely to exceed budgets, it must be split into multiple bounded steps (progressive deepening) or be rejected. Budget escalation is permitted only by explicit budget witnesses and only if (\Omega) allows escalation for exploration class.
17.2.2 Required routing witnesses
17.2.2(a) Routing witness requirement.Any step that mutates candidate store must emit a routing/candidate witness (W_{\mathsf{ROUTE}}) (Chapter 5) binding:
the step context,
the candidate list and scores,
the comparator id and tie-break rule id,
the branch cap applied,
and any truncation mask if truncation occurred.
This makes candidate generation auditable and replay-verifiable.
17.2.2(b) Feasibility witness.If exploration includes feasibility pruning under (\Omega) (e.g., removing infeasible routes), the step must emit a feasibility evidence object or trace proof showing:
which feasibility predicates were applied,
which candidates were discarded due to which predicates,
and the pinned policy hashes for those predicates.This can be compact (counts and hashes) or explicit (lists) depending on (\Omega).
17.2.2(c) Scoring witness.If candidate scoring impacts downstream commitment selection, the step must emit scoring evidence binding:
score function id hash,
inputs used (by hash),
and the resulting score vector per selected candidate (at least for the top-K that survive truncation).
17.2.2(d) Witness completeness and discardability.Exploration outputs are not admissible as inputs to commitment selection unless their routing/scoring witnesses verify. If witness obligations fail, exploration steps are rejected or their candidate outputs are discarded deterministically; the policy controlling this behavior is pinned by (\Omega).
17.2.3 Provenance requirements for exploration
17.2.3(a) Source-root binding.Exploration must bind to a source root (r_{\mathsf{src}}) containing:
hashes of all input artifacts consulted (transform library root hash, relevant entries, relevant pins, policy objects),
hashes of any seed objects used (if allowed),
hashes of any prior candidate sets consumed.This ensures exploration results cannot be detached from their inputs.
17.2.3(b) No hidden library state.Because routing and candidate generation depend on the transform library, exploration provenance must commit to a library-root hash (h(\mathsf{LibRoot})) (Chapter 11). A candidate route is meaningful only relative to a specific library state; committing (\mathsf{LibRoot}) ensures determinism and auditability.
17.2.3(c) Candidate lineage.When exploration consumes prior candidates, it must record lineage:[\mathsf{Lineage}:=(h(\mathsf{CandSet}{\mathrm{in}}),h(\mathsf{CandSet}{\mathrm{out}}),h(\mathsf{Rule}),h(\mathsf{Evidence})),]stored as a proof object. This enables deterministic reconstruction of the exploration chain.
17.2.3(d) Exploration receipts (optional).If (\Omega) requires, exploration segments may produce receipts. An exploration receipt commits to:
plan hash,
final candidate store hash,
witness root for routing/scoring witnesses,
evidence root for pruning traces.Exploration receipts do not claim correctness beyond candidate-generation semantics; they certify that the exploration was performed deterministically under policy and is replay-verifiable.
17.2.4 Transition to commitment via proof
17.2.4(a) Commitment transition principle.No candidate becomes a commitment by selection alone. Transition from exploration to commitment requires a certified selection step:
choose candidate(s) deterministically under a pinned selection policy,
produce a proof witness justifying selection under declared criteria,
commit the selected artifact(s) via commitment primitives with proper commitment witnesses.
Selection is therefore a proof-carrying transform, not a heuristic decision.
17.2.4(b) Selection criteria object.Selection criteria are a typed object:[\mathsf{SelectCrit}:=(h(\mathsf{CandSet}),h(\mathsf{ScoreFn}),h(\mathsf{Policy}),h(\mathsf{ReqProof}),h(\mathcal I),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}),\mathsf{VL}_{\min}),]binding the candidate set hash, scoring function hash, selection policy hash, required proof obligations, invariant set and tolerance profile, and minimum verification level.
17.2.4(c) Certified selection witness.The selection step must emit a proof/equivalence witness that certifies either:
the selected candidate is feasible and satisfies required invariants (e.g., route feasibility under (\Omega); no lossy steps when exactness required),
or, when selection is among multiple equivalent candidates, that the selected candidate is the lexicographically minimal admissible choice under pinned tie-break rules.
The selection witness binds to the step context and to the candidate set and score evidence, preventing post hoc manipulation.
17.2.4(d) Commitment step coupling.Immediately after certified selection, the plan must execute commitment steps that pin:
the selected artifact hash (route plan, proof, operator, etc.),
and any required supporting evidence (selection proof, feasibility proof).If commitment is deferred, the system must maintain explicit dependency links ensuring that the later commitment binds to the same candidate set and selection proof; otherwise, the commitment is invalid.
17.3 Disambiguation and multi-hypothesis handling
17.3.1 Ambiguity sets as first-class objects
17.3.1(a) AmbiguitySet object.Ambiguity is represented explicitly by a typed object:[\mathsf{AmbSet}:=(\mathsf{amb_id},h(\mathsf{AltSet}),h(\mathsf{Obs}),h(\mathsf{Constraints}),h(\mathsf{Context}),\mathsf{status}),]where:
(\mathsf{AltSet}) is a candidate set of alternative interpretations/hypotheses,
(\mathsf{Obs}) specifies observables and invariants that distinguish alternatives,
(\mathsf{Constraints}) binds permitted selection policies and minimum verification levels,
(\mathsf{status}\in{\mathsf{UNRESOLVED},\mathsf{RESOLVED},\mathsf{DEFERRED}}).
Ambiguity sets are stored and can be pinned; they are not treated as errors unless the goal requires resolution.
17.3.1(b) Ambiguity as structured state.An ambiguity set is part of the candidate/proof substrates, not a narrative note. Therefore it is searchable, auditable, and can trigger deterministic repair or disambiguation steps when required by (\Omega) or by goal constraints.
17.3.1(c) Ambiguity equivalence and clustering.Alternatives may be clustered by equivalence proofs under certain invariants. Clustering must be certified: if two alternatives are treated as equivalent for the purpose of selection, an explicit equivalence proof must exist binding those alternatives under declared invariants and tolerances.
17.3.1(d) Ambiguity persistence policy.(\Omega) may require unresolved ambiguity sets to be carried forward explicitly and may forbid committing results that depend on unresolved ambiguity in contradiction-controlled namespaces. This policy is explicit and enforced by detectors.
17.3.2 Resolution policies
17.3.2(a) ResolutionPolicy object.Resolution is governed by a pinned policy object:[\mathsf{ResPol}:=(\mathsf{res_kind},h(\mathsf{ScoreFn}),h(\mathsf{TieBreak}),h(\mathsf{ProofReq}),\mathsf{VL}_{\min},\mathsf{allow_defer}),]where (\mathsf{res_kind}\in{\mathsf{SELECT},\mathsf{SPLIT},\mathsf{MERGE},\mathsf{DEFER}}).
17.3.2(b) Deterministic resolution.Resolution must be deterministic:
(\mathsf{SELECT}) chooses the top candidate under score and tie-break rules,
(\mathsf{SPLIT}) creates refined ambiguity sets by partitioning alternatives deterministically,
(\mathsf{MERGE}) merges alternatives only when an equivalence proof exists,
(\mathsf{DEFER}) leaves ambiguity unresolved and records explicit deferral evidence.
17.3.2(c) Proof requirements.If the goal requires resolution, (\mathsf{SELECT}) must include a proof witness that the selected hypothesis satisfies feasibility and required invariants at the required verification level. If (\mathsf{MERGE}), an equivalence proof is required. If (\mathsf{SPLIT}), a partition correctness proof may be required by policy. If (\mathsf{DEFER}), deferral evidence is required.
17.3.2(d) Resolution compatibility with corridor.Resolution must respect corridor constraints: selection cannot choose a candidate whose execution would be infeasible under (\Omega). Therefore feasibility predicates are part of resolution scoring or guard checks.
17.3.3 Partial acceptance in ambiguous contexts
17.3.3(a) Partial acceptance semantics.When ambiguity exists, steps may produce (\mathsf{PARTIAL}) verdicts that:
retain ambiguity sets and candidate alternatives,
discard uncertified commitments that depend on unresolved ambiguity,
commit only those artifacts that are independent of ambiguity or that are common to all admissible alternatives (common-core commitments).
17.3.3(b) Common-core proof requirement.Committing common-core artifacts requires a proof that the artifact is invariant across all retained alternatives under declared invariants and tolerances. This is a universal quantification over candidates; in practice, it must be certified by either:
a proof method that can establish the property structurally, or
a trace proof that exhaustively checks all retained candidates within caps.Absent such a proof, no common-core commitment is permitted.
17.3.3(c) Ambiguity-sensitive masking.The projector’s acceptance mask may discard operations that would commit ambiguity-dependent artifacts without sufficient proofs. Such masking is deterministic and recorded; it ensures legal partial progress in the presence of ambiguity without corrupting commitments.
17.3.3(d) Ambiguity evidence and audit.Any partial acceptance due to ambiguity must emit evidence:
ambiguity set hash,
reason code (unresolved ambiguity),
discarded operation identifiers,
required next steps (resolution policy id).This evidence supports deterministic continuation strategies.
17.3.4 Certified selection criteria
17.3.4(a) Selection proof obligations.Selection among hypotheses must be certified. Selection proofs must bind:
candidate set hash,
score function id and tie-break id,
selection policy id,
feasibility and invariant checks required,
and the selected candidate hash.
17.3.4(b) Minimality proofs.When policy requires choosing the lexicographically minimal admissible candidate among equals, the selection proof must include evidence that:
no candidate with strictly better score exists in the retained set,
and among equal-score candidates, no candidate with smaller tie-break key exists.This can be verified deterministically from the sorted candidate list committed by the routing witness.
17.3.4(c) Feasibility inclusion proofs.If the selection claim includes feasibility under (\Omega), the proof must reference feasibility evidence objects or trace proofs produced during exploration that certify why infeasible candidates were pruned or why the selected candidate is feasible.
17.3.4(d) Certification level.Selection proofs must meet the minimum verification level required by the goal and corridor. Selecting a candidate based on a lower verification level is inadmissible when the goal requires a higher level.
17.4 Termination and liveness under budgets
17.4.1 Halting conditions
17.4.1(a) Halting conditions.Exploration halts when:
branch cap is reached and no further refinement is permitted under (\Omega),
compute or write budgets are exhausted,
tolerance budgets are exhausted for required invariants,
a hard defect is detected (determinism failure, replay divergence, forbidden convention drift),
no feasible candidate remains after pruning.
Halting conditions are deterministic and produce typed halt evidence objects.
17.4.1(b) Halt artifact.Exploration halt emits (\mathsf{Halt}) containing:
exploration kind,
step index and checkpoint hash,
branch cap and budget state snapshots,
candidate set hash at halt,
reason code and evidence references.This artifact is suitable for triggering repair planning or fallback routing.
17.4.1(c) Halt vs failure.Halting is not necessarily a failure of correctness; it is a liveness boundary under budgets.
