A goal may permit returning partial exploration results (e.g., candidate route plan) without committing a final selection. Such outcomes must be explicitly encoded as goal satisfaction or as goal incompleteness with evidence.
17.4.1(d) Deterministic reproducibility.Given the same inputs, library root, policies, and caps, exploration halting behavior (including the final candidate set and evidence) is deterministic. This ensures that resource limitations do not introduce nondeterminism.
17.4.2 Progressive deepening under caps
17.4.2(a) Progressive deepening schedule.Progressive deepening is a deterministic sequence of exploration passes with increasing depth parameter (\lambda) and/or increasing branch cap within corridor maxima:[\lambda_1 < \lambda_2 < \cdots < \lambda_k,\quad b_1 \le b_2 \le \cdots \le b_k,]with schedule parameters pinned by policy and/or derived deterministically from near-threshold evidence. Each pass produces a candidate set and evidence, and later passes may consume earlier candidate sets as lineage inputs.
17.4.2(b) Deepening admissibility.Deepening is admissible only if:
each pass is feasible under budgets,
each pass is replay-deterministic,
and the deepening schedule is committed (either as part of the plan or as a deterministic policy response to evidence).
17.4.2(c) Monotone refinement property.If policy requires monotone refinement, then each deepening pass must not discard a candidate that is strictly better under the pinned score ordering unless it is rendered infeasible by newly applied constraints or unless the discard is justified by a proof (e.g., dominance proof). This property prevents oscillatory exploration.
17.4.2(d) Deepening evidence.Each pass emits evidence binding:
pass index,
(\lambda) and branch cap used,
candidate set hash output,
lineage input candidate set hash (if any),
and reason codes for deepening continuation.
17.4.3 Failure reporting and evidence
17.4.3(a) Failure classification.Exploration failures are classified by pinned enums, at minimum:
(\mathsf{F_BUDGET}): compute/write budget exhaustion,
(\mathsf{F_CAP}): branch cap prevents further exploration,
(\mathsf{F_NOFEAS}): no feasible candidates remain,
(\mathsf{F_DET}): determinism/replay failure,
(\mathsf{F_SPEC}): convention mismatch prevents exploration continuation,
(\mathsf{F_PROOF}): required proof method unavailable or verification failure.
17.4.3(b) Failure evidence schema.Failure evidence objects bind:
step context,
classification code,
budget snapshots and cap values,
candidate hashes and counts,
implicated policy hashes and method ids.Evidence is canonical and stored in the proof store.
17.4.3(c) Use of failure evidence.Failure evidence is used to:
trigger certified repair (Chapter 10) when repairable (e.g., missing bridges, convention mismatch),
trigger rerouting compilation (Chapter 12) when alternative feasible routes exist,
or declare non-repairable halt when no repair path exists under (\Omega).
17.4.3(d) No silent fallback.Exploration may not silently substitute a weaker candidate when the best candidate is infeasible. Any fallback must be explicit, certified by selection policy, and recorded in evidence.
17.4.4 Safe fallback routes
17.4.4(a) Fallback policy object.Fallback is governed by a pinned policy:[\mathsf{FallbackPol}:=(h(\mathsf{Goal}),h(\mathsf{Constraints}),\mathsf{tiers},h(\mathsf{SelectCrit}),h(\mathsf{ProofReq})),]where (\mathsf{tiers}) is an ordered list of permissible relaxations (e.g., allow bounded tolerance, allow lower verification level, allow different lens route) with explicit limits.
17.4.4(b) Fallback admissibility.Fallback is admissible only if:
the goal explicitly permits fallback tiers,
each tier’s relaxation is explicitly encoded as a modified constraints object (new hash),
a new plan is compiled under the relaxed constraints and is feasible under (\Omega),
and the resulting receipt records the relaxed claim boundaries (invariants/tolerances/verification levels).
Fallback cannot be invoked implicitly or retroactively.
17.4.4(c) Certified fallback selection.Selecting a fallback tier requires a certified selection step (as in 17.2.4), binding:
the failure evidence hash that triggered fallback,
the chosen tier id,
the new constraints hash,
and the compiled fallback plan hash.
17.4.4(d) Fallback auditability and user-facing boundaries.Fallback outputs are auditable: receipts must commit to the relaxed constraints and the triggering failure evidence. Any consumer can determine that a fallback occurred and exactly which guarantees were relaxed. Fallback is therefore safe: it does not hide degraded guarantees.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 17 — SEARCH AND EXPLORATION UNDER CONSTRAINT
CHAPTER 18 — DETERMINISTIC NAVIGATION GRAPH: THE METRO MAP COMPILER
This chapter specifies the deterministic navigation graph (“metro map”) and the compiler that produces it. The metro map is a canonical, hash-addressed routing substrate that integrates: transform library entries, convention fingerprints, feasibility predicates under corridor policy (\Omega), and deterministic scoring/tie-breaking rules. The metro map is not a visualization artifact; it is a formal object whose hash commits to the exact navigational universe used by compilation, repair planning, optimization, and explanation. Routes are computed by deterministic shortest-legal path selection over this graph; route artifacts are emitted as typed objects with receipts and auditable explainability.
18.1 Graph semantics
18.1.1 Node identity and pin references
18.1.1(a) Node types.The metro map node set (\mathcal{V}) is partitioned into canonical node classes:
Lens nodes (\mathsf{V}_{\mathsf{L}}): nodes are lens identifiers (v=h(L)) for admitted lenses.
Artifact nodes (\mathsf{V}_{\mathsf{A}}): nodes represent pinned artifacts and artifact classes, encoded as:[v=(\mathsf{kind},\mathsf{id},h(\mathsf{Schema})?),]where (\mathsf{kind}\in{\mathsf{PIN},\mathsf{PROOF},\mathsf{PLAN},\mathsf{RECEIPT}}) and (\mathsf{id}) is a canonical identifier.
Spec nodes (\mathsf{V}_{\mathsf{S}}): nodes represent convention bundles (\mathsf{Spec}) (Chapter 7) by hash (h(\mathsf{Spec})), used for explicit spec-transition modeling when needed.
Policy nodes (\mathsf{V}_{\Omega}): optional nodes representing corridor classes or policy constraints by hash when policy-bridging is permitted (otherwise policies are fixed and do not appear as nodes).
Each node is a COS object with a canonical encoding. The node id is its hash.
18.1.1(b) Pin reference semantics.A pin reference node corresponds to a commitment store key (\mathsf{pin_id}). Its meaning is:
existence and identity depend on the state commitment store,
the node’s outgoing edges represent transforms that can consume or update that pin under admissible scope and contradiction policies.
Pin references in the metro map are abstracted as identifiers; actual existence is checked by feasibility predicates using a specific pre-state checkpoint hash.
18.1.1(c) Node namespace discipline.Node identifiers are canonical and namespace-partitioned:
lens node ids are 256-bit hashes of lens objects,
artifact node ids are hashes of canonical node records,
spec node ids are hashes of convention bundle objects.No two node types share the same canonical type tag; node typing is enforced by COS schemas.
18.1.1(d) Node resolvability.A metro map instance is parameterized by:
a library root hash (h(\mathsf{LibRoot})),
a corridor policy hash (h_\Omega),
and optionally a checkpoint hash (h(\Psi)) for pin existence/availability checks.Node resolvability is a deterministic predicate: a node is “active” iff its referenced hash resolves and (if state-dependent) its referenced pin exists in the specified checkpoint.
18.1.2 Edge identity and contract references
18.1.2(a) Edge objects.Each directed edge (e\in\mathcal{E}) is a COS object:[\mathsf{Edge}:=(\mathsf{edge_id},\ \mathsf{src},\ \mathsf{dst},\ \mathsf{etype},\ h(\mathsf{Entry}),\ h(\mathsf{LT}),\ h(\Pi),\ h(\mathsf{Ann})),]where:
(\mathsf{edge_id}=h(\mathsf{Edge})),
(\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst}\in\mathcal{V}) are node ids,
(\mathsf{etype}\in{\mathsf{TRANSFORM},\mathsf{BRIDGE},\mathsf{NORMALIZER},\mathsf{REPAIR},\mathsf{REUSE},\mathsf{COMMIT}}) is a pinned enum,
(h(\mathsf{Entry})) references a library entry (Chapter 11),
(h(\mathsf{LT})) references the legal transform contract (Chapter 2),
(h(\Pi)) references the canonical program (Chapter 8),
(h(\mathsf{Ann})) references edge annotation schema fields (Section 18.1.3).
18.1.2(b) Edge typing and endpoints.Edges connect either:
lens nodes to lens nodes (pure transport),
lens nodes to artifact nodes (emit plan/proof/receipt artifacts),
artifact nodes to lens nodes (consume pinned artifacts to enter a lens domain),
spec nodes to spec nodes (explicit convention mapping edges, if the graph includes spec layer explicitly).
Edge typing determines admissible feasibility predicates and scoring terms.
18.1.2(c) Contract reference correctness.An edge is admissible in the metro map only if:
(h(\mathsf{Entry})) is pinned in the library root and admitted by receipts,
(h(\mathsf{LT})) matches the contract referenced by the entry,
the entry’s SpecIn/SpecOut hashes match the ones declared in the edge annotation,
and all witness methods required by the contract exist in the verifier set.
If any mismatch exists, the edge is excluded.
18.1.2(d) Edge versioning.Edge identity is content-addressed; any change in contract, program, annotation, or endpoints produces a new edge id. Therefore the metro map is immutable for a fixed library root and policy hash: updates yield a new metro map hash.
18.1.3 Edge annotation schemas
18.1.3(a) Annotation object.Edge annotations are a COS object:[\mathsf{Ann}:=(h(\mathsf{SpecIn}),h(\mathsf{SpecOut}),h(\mathcal I),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}),h(\mathcal{B}),h(\mathcal{S}),h(\mathcal{W}),h(\mathcal{R}),h(\mathsf{Loss})?,\mathsf{invTier}?,\mathsf{flags}),]where:
(\mathsf{SpecIn},\mathsf{SpecOut}) are convention bundles (Chapter 7),
(h(\mathcal I)) is the invariant set,
(h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})) is the tolerance profile,
(h(\mathcal{B})) budget profile,
(h(\mathcal{S})) scope profile,
(h(\mathcal{W})) witness profile,
(h(\mathcal{R})) replay profile,
(h(\mathsf{Loss})) is present if the transform is quotient-bearing/lossy (Chapter 8),
(\mathsf{invTier}) is an invertibility tier when applicable (exact inverse, approximate inverse, quotient-only, non-invertible),
(\mathsf{flags}) includes pinned categorical flags (hub designation, bridge class, normalizer class, exploration class).
18.1.3(b) Cost feature vector.For deterministic route scoring, each edge includes or derives a cost feature vector:[\mathsf{Feat}(e):=(\mathsf{exactFlag},c_{\mathsf{hop}},c_{\mathsf{budget}},c_{\mathsf{wit}},c_{\mathsf{write}},\mathbf{tols}),]where (\mathbf{tols}) is the per-invariant tolerance usage vector (quantized). Feature extraction is deterministic from the annotation objects and pinned scoring policy.
18.1.3(c) Feasibility feature bundle.Annotations include feasibility metadata used for pruning:
required verifier method ids and minimum verification level,
hard scope restrictions (forbidden namespaces),
hard domain constraints (parameter domain predicates),
and whether bridges/normalizers are mandatory at boundaries.These are represented as hashes to pinned predicate objects and policy ids.
18.1.3(d) Annotation canonicalization.Annotations are canonical COS objects; any ambiguity in ordering of included sets (invariants, witness method ids) is forbidden. Therefore identical edge semantics produce identical annotation bytes and identical hashes.
18.1.4 Graph hashing and versioning
18.1.4(a) Graph object.The metro map graph is a COS object:[\mathsf{Metro}:=(\mathsf{metro_ver},h(\mathsf{LibRoot}),h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h(\mathsf{Nodes}),h(\mathsf{Edges}),h(\mathsf{Policies})),]where:
(\mathsf{Nodes}) is a set-like list of node objects (or their ids) in canonical order,
(\mathsf{Edges}) is a set-like list of edge objects (or their ids) in canonical order,
(\mathsf{Policies}) pins route scoring weights, feasibility predicate ids, tie-break rules, and any metro-map-specific constraints.
The metro map hash is (h(\mathsf{Metro})).
18.1.4(b) Canonical ordering for hashing.Nodes are ordered lexicographically by their canonical node bytes. Edges are ordered lexicographically by their canonical edge bytes. This ensures graph hashing is stable across implementations and independent of insertion order.
18.1.4(c) Versioning.(\mathsf{metro_ver}) is incremented only when the graph schema or hashing rules change. Changes to the library root, policy hash, or verifier set produce a new (\mathsf{Metro}) object even if (\mathsf{metro_ver}) is unchanged.
18.1.4(d) Subgraph commitments.Optionally, the metro map may expose Merkle roots:
(r_{\mathsf{nodes}}) over node encodings,
(r_{\mathsf{edges}}) over edge encodings,enabling membership proofs for edges used in route artifacts and explainability packages. Merkle policy is pinned.
18.2 Route feasibility predicates
Route feasibility is evaluated by deterministic predicates whose identifiers are pinned in (\mathsf{Metro.Policies}). Feasibility is always relative to (\Omega) and, when state-dependent, relative to a checkpoint hash.
18.2.1 Convention compatibility feasibility
18.2.1(a) Compatibility predicate.For consecutive edges (e_i,e_{i+1}) in a candidate route, convention compatibility requires:[h(\mathsf{SpecOut}(e_i)) = h(\mathsf{SpecIn}(e_{i+1})),]unless there exists an explicit bridge edge (b) between them such that:[h(\mathsf{SpecOut}(e_i)) \xrightarrow{b} h(\mathsf{SpecIn}(e_{i+1})).]Bridges are edges with (\mathsf{etype}=\mathsf{BRIDGE}) and must be included explicitly in the route.
18.2.1(b) Bridge feasibility.A bridge edge is feasible only if:
its proof method ids are supported by the verifier set,
its tolerance usage is admissible under corridor budgets and required constraints,
and its scope does not require forbidden writes.If no feasible bridge chain exists, the route is infeasible.
18.2.1(c) Spec boundary feasibility for artifacts.When consuming or emitting artifact nodes, the spec bundle must match the artifact’s required conventions, or the route must include explicit bridges and normalizers ensuring artifacts are in canonical conventions. Artifact compatibility is enforced by the same spec matching rules; artifacts are treated as having their own convention bundle requirements.
18.2.1(d) No implicit compatibility.Convention compatibility is categorical and enforced structurally. Any route that would require implicit sign flips, scaling changes, index permutations, unit conversions, or domain/branch changes is infeasible unless explicit bridges exist and are included.
18.2.2 Budget feasibility
18.2.2(a) Edge-level budget feasibility.An edge (e) is budget-feasible under corridor policy (\Omega) iff its declared budget profile (\mathcal{B}_e) does not exceed corridor maxima and the edge is executable within corridor per-step budgets without forbidden escalation.
18.2.2(b) Route-level budget feasibility.Route-level feasibility depends on cumulative budgets:[\mathbf{c}{\mathrm{route}}=\sum_i \mathbf{c}(e_i),]where (\mathbf{c}(e_i)) is a deterministic cost feature vector derived from (e_i)’s annotation and policy weights. A route is feasible iff (\mathbf{c}{\mathrm{route}}) is within corridor global or segment budgets as defined by (\Omega). If (\Omega) budgets are step-local only, feasibility is checked per edge; if (\Omega) includes segment-level caps, feasibility is checked cumulatively.
18.2.2(c) Budget escalation feasibility.If budget escalation is permitted, feasibility may include explicit escalation edges or planned escalation witnesses. Escalation is never implicit; a route that relies on escalation must include an explicit escalation plan artifact or must be marked infeasible.
18.2.2(d) Budget evidence and pruning.Budget infeasibility pruning must be deterministic. When pruning occurs, the compiler emits a feasibility evidence object specifying the failing predicate, the edge or prefix where failure occurs, and the required budget component. This evidence is used by repair planning and fallback routing.
18.2.3 Tolerance feasibility
18.2.3(a) Tolerance accumulation.Each edge (e) has a tolerance usage vector (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}e). Route cumulative tolerance usage is:[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathrm{route}} := \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{e_1}\oplus \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{e_2}\oplus \cdots \oplus \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{e_k},]with (\oplus) per-invariant accumulation operators pinned by (\Omega).
18.2.3(b) Feasibility predicate.Given required maximum tolerances (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\max}) from goal constraints and corridor tolerance budgets (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^\Omega), a route is tolerance-feasible iff:[\forall I:\ \varepsilon{\mathrm{route},I}\le \min(\varepsilon_{\max,I},\varepsilon^\Omega_I)]for all required invariants (I). Exactness constraints are encoded by (\varepsilon_{\max,I}=0).
18.2.3(c) Near-threshold mitigation requirements.If (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathrm{route}}) is near-threshold under (\Omega), the route is feasible only if it includes mandatory normalization checkpoints or reroute steps required by mitigation policies. These are explicit edges; feasibility includes their presence.
18.2.3(d) Quotient-bearing steps.Edges with loss/quotient profiles are feasible only if the goal constraints permit the corresponding information loss and if required invariants are preserved in the quotient sense. Routes that require dropped invariants are infeasible.
18.2.4 Verifier support feasibility
18.2.4(a) Method support predicate.An edge is verifier-feasible iff all witness types and proof method ids required by its witness profile (\mathcal{W}e) are present in the runtime verifier set (h{\mathsf{VerSet}}) and satisfy minimum verification level requirements.
18.2.4(b) Local-check data availability.If verification requires local-check data objects (test bases, checker programs, quantization policies), those objects must be resolvable by hash and admissible under disclosure mode requirements if public verification is required. If required objects cannot be resolved or disclosed as required, the edge is infeasible for the requested verification mode.
18.2.4(c) Polynomial-time guarantee.Verifier feasibility includes a complexity constraint: each required verifier must run within the corridor compute cap for verification steps. If a verifier method is too expensive for the corridor, it is infeasible unless an admissible budget escalation exists.
18.2.4(d) Evidence retention feasibility.If (\Omega) mandates evidence retention for certain detectors, the route must be feasible with respect to proof-store write budgets. Edges that generate mandatory evidence beyond allowed write budgets are infeasible under the current (\Omega).
18.3 Route scoring and selection
18.3.1 Cost functional terms
18.3.1(a) Edge cost features.Each edge (e) supplies deterministic feature components:
hop cost (1),
budget cost contribution,
witness burden contribution,
protected-write penalty,
tolerance usage vector,
categorical flags (exact vs approximate; hub vs non-hub; bridge class; invertibility tier).
18.3.1(b) Route cost tuple.Route scoring uses a lexicographic cost tuple:[\mathsf{RCost}(r):=(\varepsilon\text{-priority},C_{\varepsilon},k,C_{\mathsf{budget}},C_{\mathsf{wit}},C_{\mathsf{write}},C_{\mathsf{risk}},\mathsf{LexKey}(r)),]with:
(k) hop count,
(C_{\mathsf{budget}}) aggregate budget cost,
(C_{\mathsf{wit}}) aggregate witness burden cost,
(C_{\mathsf{write}}) aggregate protected-write penalty,
(C_{\mathsf{risk}}) risk penalty (e.g., near-threshold tolerance and drift risk) computed deterministically from cumulative tolerances and policy thresholds,
(\mathsf{LexKey}(r)) deterministic tie-break key.
All weights and penalty functions are pinned in (\mathsf{Metro.Policies}).
18.3.1(c) Risk penalty.Risk penalty encodes long-chain stability preferences: routes that approach tolerance budgets or that include many approximate steps may be penalized even when feasible. This is policy-controlled and deterministic.
18.3.1(d) Reuse edges and cost.If reuse via receipts is permitted, reuse edges represent “consume upstream artifact” operations with costs reflecting membership proof verification and dependency proof generation rather than re-execution. The cost model treats reuse as low compute and low tolerance usage but includes trust boundary penalties if public verification requires additional disclosure.
18.3.2 Exactness preference rules
18.3.2(a) Categorical precedence.Exactness is lexicographically dominant: any feasible route with zero tolerance usage on required invariants is strictly preferred over any route with nonzero tolerance usage, unless the goal explicitly allows approximation and exact routes are absent.
18.3.2(b) Exactness under quotient semantics.A route may be “exact” in a quotient sense only if the goal’s invariants and observables are declared in the quotient space and the route’s loss profiles are compatible. This is encoded by goal constraints; exactness is not inferred.
18.3.2(c) Bridge selection under exactness.When exactness is required, any bridge chain that consumes nonzero tolerance usage is infeasible unless no exact bridge exists and the goal explicitly permits bounded approximation. Otherwise, the compiler must refuse the route rather than silently degrade.
18.3.2(d) Invertibility preference.When goals require reversible transport, the scoring policy may prefer edges with higher invertibility tier (exact inverse over approximate inverse over quotient). This preference is pinned and applied deterministically.
18.3.3 Witness load weighting
18.3.3(a) Witness burden computation.Witness burden is a deterministic sum:[C_{\mathsf{wit}}(r)=\sum_{e\in r}\sum_{\tau}\omega_\tau n_\tau(e),]with witness type weights (\omega_\tau) pinned. Proof and consistency witnesses typically have higher weights than provenance witnesses.
18.3.3(b) Verification level interaction.Higher verification levels may increase witness burden and compute cost. Scoring policy may prefer minimal verification level that satisfies the goal unless long-chain stability policies require stronger proofs. This preference is pinned; it is not heuristic.
18.3.3(c) Cached receipt reuse weighting.Routes that reuse cached hub receipts may reduce witness burden by replacing re-derivation proofs with membership proofs. Scoring policy may penalize reliance on weaker trust modes; this is encoded in the reuse edge’s feature vector and the risk penalty.
18.3.3(d) Witness feasibility gating.Witness burden is considered only among feasible routes. Routes requiring unsupported witness types or method ids are eliminated before scoring.
18.3.4 Deterministic tie-breaks
18.3.4(a) Route lexicographic key.(\mathsf{LexKey}(r)) is the canonical encoding of the route’s edge id sequence and any deterministic parameter-binding placeholders:[\mathsf{LexKey}(r):=\mathsf{Enc}([\mathsf{edge_id}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{edge_id}_k]).]If parameter hashes are determined during routing, include ((\mathsf{edge_id}_i,h(P_i))) pairs. The rule is pinned: either edge-only or edge+param; it must be consistent across implementations.
18.3.4(b) Stable shortest-path algorithm.Route selection uses a deterministic shortest-legal path algorithm whose priority queue ordering is (\mathsf{RCost}). When costs tie, the algorithm selects the predecessor with smaller (\mathsf{LexKey}). This ensures canonical routes and stable plan hashes.
18.3.4(c) Deterministic failure reporting.If no feasible route exists, the compiler produces a canonical infeasibility certificate by selecting the lexicographically minimal failing predicate record among candidate prefixes under a pinned ordering of defect classes and boundary locations.
18.3.4(d) Deterministic explainability.Tie-break decisions are recorded as deterministic artifacts (Section 18.4.4): the compiler emits evidence objects indicating which candidates tied and why the chosen route won under (\mathsf{LexKey}).
18.4 Route artifacts
18.4.1 Route plans as typed objects
18.4.1(a) RoutePlan object.A route plan is a COS object:[\mathsf{RoutePlan}:=(h(\mathsf{Metro}),h(\mathsf{Goal}),h(\mathsf{Context}),h(\mathsf{Constraints}),h(\mathsf{Start}),h(\mathsf{End}),h(\mathsf{Route}),h(\mathsf{Costs}),h(\mathsf{FeasCert})).]
(h(\mathsf{Route})) references the canonical route edge-id sequence object.
(h(\mathsf{Costs})) references computed cost tuple and feature summaries.
(h(\mathsf{FeasCert})) references the feasibility certificate object containing predicate confirmations.
18.4.1(b) Route object.(\mathsf{Route}) is a sequence-like list:[\mathsf{Route}:=[\mathsf{edge_id}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{edge_id}_k],]canonically encoded. Each (\mathsf{edge_id}) must have a membership proof in the metro map edge root if the metro map is published with Merkle commitments.
18.4.1(c) Route feasibility certificate.(\mathsf{FeasCert}) is an evidence object that binds:
the set of feasibility predicates checked,
their outcomes,
the policy hash set used,
and any bridge/normalizer obligations satisfied by inclusion in the route.It is required for audit and for deterministic repair planning.
18.4.1(d) RoutePlan admissibility.A route plan is admissible only if it references a metro map hash and policy hashes that match the runtime header and corridor policy under which it will be executed. Otherwise, the route plan is ill-typed and execution must refuse it.
18.4.2 Witness and ledger plans as typed objects
18.4.2(a) WitnessPlan object.Given a route, the compiler emits a witness plan:[\mathsf{WPlan}:=[\mathsf{WReq}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{WReq}_n],]where each (\mathsf{WReq}_t) is derived deterministically from the edge witness profiles and corridor requirements. The witness plan binds method ids and required local-check data hashes.
18.4.2(b) LedgerPlan object.Similarly, the compiler emits:[\mathsf{LPlan}:=[\mathsf{LStep}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{LStep}_n],]binding detector schedules, partial acceptance policies, and per-step budget caps. Both plans are pinned in the final execution plan object (Chapter 12).
18.4.2(c) Plan linkage to metro map.Both (\mathsf{WPlan}) and (\mathsf{LPlan}) reference (h(\mathsf{Metro})) and (h_\Omega) implicitly via the containing plan. This ensures that witness obligations are interpreted relative to the exact library/policy environment that produced the route.
18.4.2(d) Deterministic derivation.Witness and ledger plans are deterministic functions of:
the selected route (edge sequence),
the corridor policy hash,
and pinned plan-derivation rules.Therefore, identical inputs yield identical plan component hashes.
18.4.3 Plan receipts for routing decisions
18.4.3(a) Routing receipt object.A routing decision can itself be receipted. A routing receipt is a COS receipt-like object:[\mathsf{Rcpt}{\mathsf{route}}:=(h(\mathsf{Metro}),h(\mathsf{Goal}),h(\mathsf{Context}),h(\mathsf{Constraints}),h(\mathsf{RoutePlan}),h(\mathsf{Proofs})?,h(\mathsf{Evidence}),h\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}).]It commits to the chosen route and the feasibility/scoring evidence.
18.4.3(b) Purpose of routing receipts.Routing receipts provide:
auditability of route selection,
reproducibility of compilation decisions,
and stable dependencies for downstream artifacts that require referencing the exact compilation environment (e.g., repair chains, optimization logs).
18.4.3(c) Verification procedure.Verifying a routing receipt consists of:
verifying metro map membership of all edges in the route,
verifying feasibility certificate contents against metro map annotations and corridor policy,
verifying cost computation deterministically under pinned scoring policy,
verifying tie-break evidence when costs tie.
18.4.3(d) Routing receipt boundaries.A routing receipt does not guarantee that executing the route will succeed; execution can still fail due to state-dependent factors (missing pins, contradictions, runtime policy mismatches) or due to defects detected at runtime. Routing receipts guarantee only that, under the metro map and corridor assumptions encoded, the route was the canonical best feasible route at compilation time.
18.4.4 Explainability as deterministic extraction
18.4.4(a) Explainability object.Explainability is a deterministic extraction artifact:[\mathsf{Explain}:=(h(\mathsf{RoutePlan}),h(\mathsf{Trace}),h(\mathsf{AltSet})?,h(\mathsf{TieSet})?,h(\mathsf{Policy})),]where:
(\mathsf{Trace}) is a compact proof/trace of the shortest-path computation (optional but permitted),
(\mathsf{AltSet}) is the set of top-K alternative routes with cost tuples (optional, policy-gated),
(\mathsf{TieSet}) records ties and the tie-break decision evidence,
(\mathsf{Policy}) binds scoring weights and predicate ids.
18.4.4(b) Deterministic extraction rules.Explainability must not depend on narrative or heuristics. It is produced by deterministic extraction from:
the metro map object,
feasibility certificate,
cost tuple and scoring policy,
and tie-break records.Extraction yields a structured artifact, not prose.
18.4.4(c) Minimal explanation guarantee.At minimum, explainability must provide:
the selected route edge ids,
the cost tuple for the selected route,
the first failing feasibility predicate for the best infeasible alternative (if any) when required,
and the tie-break basis when ties occurred.This ensures that route choice is mechanically defensible.
18.4.4(d) Audit-ready packaging.An explainability package may include membership proofs for the route edges in the metro map’s edge root and may include a routing receipt. This allows third parties to verify the route choice independently without access to the full library content beyond the committed hashes.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 18 — DETERMINISTIC NAVIGATION GRAPH: THE METRO MAP COMPILER
CHAPTER 19 — STABILITY PROJECTION AND LONG-CHAIN CONTROL
This chapter specifies long-chain stability: how the framework maintains legality and meaning over extended compositions of transforms. The central mechanisms are (i) stability anchors and checkpointing as fixed points of the legality projector, (ii) tolerance ledgers that track cumulative approximation budgets per invariant, (iii) loop diagnostics that detect representation holonomy (nontrivial defect on closed paths), and (iv) certified tunneling: representation jumps executed as programs that route through checkpoint anchors with minimal bridges and mandatory post-jump normalization. The chapter formalizes safety theorems that bound drift and guarantee replay-consistent control of long compositions.
19.1 Stability anchor and checkpointing
19.1.1 Fixed-point stability conditions
19.1.1(a) Stability projector fixed point.Let (\mathsf{Project}) be the legality projector (Chapter 4) under corridor policy (\Omega). A state (\Psi) is a stability anchor iff it is a fixed point of the projector under the empty transition:[\mathsf{Project}(\Psi,\Delta=\varnothing,W=\varnothing,\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{0},\Omega,\mathsf{StepDesc}=\varnothing)=(\Psi,\mathsf{ACCEPT},m^\star,E^\star),]where (m^\star) is the all-accept mask and (E^\star) contains no failing evidence. This defines stability as a structural property: canonicalization is satisfied, contradiction policy is satisfied, and no mandatory detectors signal failure.
19.1.1(b) Stability invariants.A stability anchor must satisfy the following invariants:
Canonical normal form: all committed objects referenced by the commitment store are in canonical normal form per Chapter 7.
Contradiction consistency: contradiction predicate (\mathsf{Contradict}_\Omega(\mathsf{CommitStore})=\mathsf{false}) for all contradiction-controlled namespaces.
Policy coherence: runtime header hashes referenced by the state match the ledger header and corridor policy hashes.
Ledger coherence: step index and chain continuity are consistent with the ledger header and the last step record hash.
Tolerance ledger coherence: cumulative tolerance usage vector (Section 19.2.1) is within corridor budgets and in canonical encoding.
19.1.1(c) Stability proof object.A stability anchor may be certified by a proof object (\mathsf{ProofStab}) binding:[\mathsf{ProofStab}:=(h(\Psi),h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h(\mathsf{AuditSet}),\mathsf{VL}),]where (\mathsf{AuditSet}) enumerates the detector predicates checked to establish stability. This proof is locally verifiable: it reduces to deterministic checks of store hashes, contradiction scans, and canonical normal form checks.
19.1.1(d) Stability as a route boundary.Stability anchors are mandatory boundaries for long-chain control: any long route segment must begin and end at a stability anchor (or produce one as a postcondition) when required by (\Omega) or by goal constraints. This allows long computations to be broken into replay-verifiable segments.
19.1.2 Checkpoint hash semantics
19.1.2(a) Checkpoint object.A checkpoint (\mathsf{Chk}) is a COS object committing:[\mathsf{Chk}:=(h(\Psi),h_{\mathsf{commit}},h_{\mathsf{rel}},h_{\mathsf{cand}},h_{\mathsf{proof}},\mathsf{step_index},h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),h(\mathsf{RuntimeHdr}),h(\mathsf{TolLedger})).]The checkpoint hash (h(\mathsf{Chk})) is content-addressed and uniquely identifies the complete state boundary.
19.1.2(b) Semantics of checkpoint equality.Two checkpoints are equal iff their canonical bytes are equal. Equality implies identity of:
full state hash (h(\Psi)),
all per-store hashes,
ledger header and runtime header hashes,
tolerance ledger state.
Therefore a checkpoint is a complete, replay-stable boundary; it is not merely a pointer.
19.1.2(c) Checkpoints as replay roots.A checkpoint defines a replay root: subsequent steps bind their provenance to (h(\Psi)) of the checkpoint and their ledger position to (\mathsf{step_index}). Any attempt to execute steps that claim to follow a checkpoint but whose pre-state hash does not match is illegal and is detected as replay divergence.
19.1.2(d) Checkpoint inclusion in receipts.Receipts must commit to the final checkpoint hash (via (h_{\mathsf{final}}=h(\Psi_n)) and optionally (h(\mathsf{Chk}_n))). When a plan is executed, the receipt thereby commits to a stable boundary that can be used as a starting point for future plans.
19.1.3 Checkpoint-bound transforms
19.1.3(a) Definition.A checkpoint-bound transform is a legal transform whose contract requires:
input state to be at a checkpoint satisfying a specified predicate,
and output state to be at a checkpoint satisfying a specified postcondition predicate.Such transforms are used for segment transitions that must preserve global invariants tightly (e.g., end-of-segment normalization, proof regeneration stabilization).
19.1.3(b) Boundary predicates.Boundary predicates are pinned deterministic predicates (g_{\mathrm{in}},g_{\mathrm{out}}) defined over checkpoint objects:[g_{\mathrm{in}}(\mathsf{Chk}{t})=\mathsf{true},\quad g{\mathrm{out}}(\mathsf{Chk}_{t'})=\mathsf{true}.]Predicates may include:
canonical normal form enforcement,
contradiction-free constraints,
tolerance ledger constraints,
required evidence clearance (e.g., no unresolved near-threshold mitigation obligations).
19.1.3(c) Contract implications.Checkpoint-bound transforms are required at specific insertion points:
before committing critical results,
after bridge chains that alter conventions,
after long approximate segments,
and before tunneling jumps (Section 19.4).Omission of required checkpoint-bound transforms is detected by long-chain control policies and causes plan infeasibility or runtime rejection.
19.1.3(d) Witness discipline for boundary transforms.Checkpoint-bound transforms require proof witnesses that certify the boundary predicate holds. These proofs bind the checkpoint hash and the policy hashes, preventing boundary claims from being detached from actual state.
19.1.4 Restart and replay boundaries
19.1.4(a) Restart boundary definition.A restart boundary is a checkpoint chosen as a stable starting point for re-execution:
after failure,
after repair,
or as part of planned segmentation.Restart boundaries are determined deterministically by:
the most recent stable anchor checkpoint satisfying a pinned predicate,
or the last successful checkpoint in the ledger (if stability predicate is satisfied by default).
19.1.4(b) Replay boundary definition.A replay boundary is any checkpoint after which replay verification may start. Replay boundary validity depends on availability of:
the checkpoint object,
the ledger header,
and all required proof store objects referenced by subsequent step records.Replay boundaries are explicit via checkpoint hashes; implicit “resume from here” is not permitted.
19.1.4(c) Failure restart rule.On hard failure, the execution protocol reverts to the pre-step checkpoint. Long-chain control specifies additional rules: repeated failure at the same boundary triggers certified repair (Chapter 10) or halting (Chapter 9), with the restart boundary being the last stable anchor preceding the earliest failing step.
19.1.4(d) Segment receipts.Long computations may be represented as a chain of segment receipts, each ending at a checkpoint. Segment receipts compose via dependency proofs (Chapter 14), and segmentation enables partial verification and partial reuse without requiring full replay of the entire long chain.
19.2 Drift bounds and tolerance ledgers
19.2.1 Per-invariant tolerance vectors
19.2.1(a) Tolerance ledger object.The tolerance ledger is a COS object:[\mathsf{TolLedger}:=(h(\mathsf{InvIndex}),h(\oplus),\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega},\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}},\mathsf{near_params},\mathsf{history_root}?),]where:
(\mathsf{InvIndex}) canonically orders invariant identifiers,
(h(\oplus)) commits accumulation operators per invariant,
(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega}) is the corridor tolerance budget vector (quantized),
(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}}) is the cumulative usage vector (quantized),
(\mathsf{near_params}) commits near-threshold fractions (\alpha_I) and mitigation policy ids,
(\mathsf{history_root}) optionally commits a Merkle root of per-step tolerance contributions for audit.
19.2.1(b) Cumulative update rule.After each step (t), update:[\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}{I,t} = \varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}{I,t-1}\ \oplus_I\ \varepsilon_{I,t},]where (\varepsilon_{I,t}) is the step’s declared tolerance consumption for invariant (I), derived deterministically from the executed transform contracts and any approximate bridges/regrids/normalizers used. For exact steps, (\varepsilon_{I,t}=0).
19.2.1(c) Interpretation boundary.Tolerance usage is not an observed drift measurement; it is a declared and verified accounting of approximation allowances. Observed drift measurements (Chapter 9) may be checked against tolerances, but the ledger tracks budget consumption independent of measurements.
19.2.1(d) Canonical encoding and identity.(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}}) is encoded as a fixed-length vector aligned to (\mathsf{InvIndex}) and stored in canonical integer encoding. Therefore the tolerance ledger state is stable across replay and is committed in checkpoint hashes.
19.2.2 Composition operators for tolerances
19.2.2(a) Accumulation operator bundle.For each invariant (I), (\oplus_I) is a pinned deterministic operator. The bundle (h(\oplus)) commits the full set ({\oplus_I}). Operators must be total, associative (for deterministic accumulation), and monotone in each argument.
19.2.2(b) Operator classes.Permitted operator classes include:
worst-case additive: (\varepsilon\oplus \varepsilon'=\varepsilon+\varepsilon'),
bounded-sum under caps: (\min(\varepsilon+\varepsilon',\varepsilon^{\Omega}_I)),
norm-composed bounds: ((\varepsilon^p + (\varepsilon')^p)^{1/p}) represented in quantized form where permitted,
hybrid operators pinned explicitly.
Operators are not inferred; they are selected by policy and committed by hash.
19.2.2(c) Operator compatibility with proofs.If a proof claim uses a tolerance profile, that profile must be consistent with the ledger operators. A proof cannot claim a tolerance that would require a different accumulation model than (h(\oplus)). Such mismatches are detected as defects.
19.2.2(d) Associativity and replay.Associativity of (\oplus_I) ensures that cumulative tolerance usage is independent of grouping. This is required for stable long-chain accounting and for deterministic route scoring and selection.
19.2.3 Forced renormalization triggers
19.2.3(a) Near-threshold trigger.Define near-threshold for invariant (I) by:[\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_{I,t} > \alpha_I \varepsilon^{\Omega}_I,]where (\alpha_I) is pinned. When near-threshold is reached for any hard invariant (I), the corridor mandates mitigation actions.
19.2.3(b) Forced renormalization policy.A forced renormalization trigger maps to a required action set:[\mathsf{Mitigate}(I) := {\mathsf{NormStepIds},\mathsf{ReroutePolicies},\mathsf{ProofTightenPolicies}},]represented as pinned ids. The next plan segment must include one of the admissible mitigation actions. If no mitigation action is feasible, the system must halt with non-repairable evidence.
19.2.3(c) Renormalization as certified transform.Renormalization steps are explicit transforms (Chapter 10) that project states or artifacts back into canonical normal forms or into a constrained feasible set. Renormalization may not “reset” tolerance usage unless explicitly certified by a proof that the projection reduces error bounds in a way permitted by (\Omega). Otherwise tolerance only accumulates.
19.2.3(d) Trigger evidence and coupling.Trigger evidence objects must bind:
invariant id,
current cumulative tolerance,
corridor budget,
near-threshold fraction,
required mitigation policy id(s),
and checkpoint hash.Repair plans and mitigation steps must include this evidence hash in their provenance source roots.
19.2.4 Long-chain safety theorems
19.2.4(a) Safety theorem (budgeted drift).Let a chain of transforms (e_1,\ldots,e_k) execute under corridor policy (\Omega). If:
each step’s declared tolerance usage (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{e_i}) is correctly accounted and verified,
cumulative tolerance ledger satisfies (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}}_{k}\le \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega}),
all convention fingerprints are compatible or bridged explicitly,
all required invariants are checked at declared checkpoints (as mandated),then the chain remains within the corridor’s declared approximation envelope for the tracked invariants. This does not assert physical correctness; it asserts compliance with the declared, audited bound system.
19.2.4(b) Stability theorem (checkpoint segmentation).If a long chain is segmented by stability anchors (\mathsf{Chk}{t_0},\mathsf{Chk}{t_1},\ldots,\mathsf{Chk}_{t_m}) such that each segment begins and ends at a stability anchor and all post-commit audits pass, then:
replay verification can be performed segment-wise,
defects are localizable to a segment,
repairs can revert to the segment boundary deterministically.
19.2.4(c) Safety under refinement.If a segment receipt is superseded by a refinement receipt that strengthens tolerances or verification level while preserving outputs under required invariants, then any downstream computation that depended on the weaker receipt remains valid under the refined receipt, subject to compatibility constraints. This supports gradual strengthening of long chains without redoing downstream work.
19.2.4(d) Long-chain failure theorem.If cumulative tolerance budgets for any hard invariant are exceeded and no admissible repair or mitigation exists under (\Omega), then no legal execution can proceed to a state satisfying the corridor’s required invariants. The correct outcome is a deterministic halt artifact referencing the first exceeded invariant and the earliest checkpoint where the violation becomes inevitable.
19.3 Loop diagnostics (holonomy)
Loop diagnostics detect nontrivial “holonomy” on closed routes: a route that returns to the same lens and conventions but accumulates a defect (drift, convention mismatch, or nontrivial transformation) that is not identity under declared invariants.
19.3.1 Closed route tests
19.3.1(a) Closed route definition.A route (r) is closed if it starts and ends at the same lens node and has matching boundary convention bundles:[h(L_{\mathrm{start}})=h(L_{\mathrm{end}}),\quad h(\mathsf{SpecIn}_r)=h(\mathsf{SpecOut}_r).]
19.3.1(b) Closed route test object.A closed route test is a COS object:[\mathsf{LoopTest}:=(h(\mathsf{RoutePlan}),h(\mathsf{TB}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(\mathcal I),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}),\mathsf{VL}),]binding the route, test basis, checker program, invariant set, tolerance profile, and verification level used to evaluate loop identity.
19.3.1(c) Identity claim.A closed route may claim one of:
exact identity under invariants: (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}=\mathbf{0}),
bounded identity: (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\neq \mathbf{0}),
quotient identity: identity only after applying a quotient map (must be explicit).
The claim type is a mandatory field in the loop test object.
19.3.1(d) Test execution discipline.Executing a loop test is a program execution producing a receipt. The receipt commits to measured drift values on the test basis and thus yields an auditable loop diagnostic.
19.3.2 Holonomy defect measurement
19.3.2(a) Holonomy measure.For a closed route (r) acting on input payload (x), define holonomy defect under invariant (I) by:[h_I(r;x) := \big| I(x) - I(r(x)) \big|_I,]computed deterministically on quantized measurements under pinned policies. A holonomy defect exists if any required (h_I(r;x)) exceeds the declared tolerance (\varepsilon_I).
19.3.2(b) Multi-input holonomy measurement.Holonomy is evaluated on a test basis (TB) of inputs ({x_j}). The diagnostic summary includes:
max defect per invariant,
mean defect per invariant (optional),
and a canonical signature hash of the defect vector across the basis.
All statistics are computed deterministically in quantized arithmetic.
19.3.2(c) Holonomy classification.Holonomy outcomes are classified by pinned enums:
(\mathsf{LOOP_OK}),
(\mathsf{LOOP_NEAR}),
(\mathsf{LOOP_FAIL}),with near thresholds pinned similarly to other drift detectors. Loop failure triggers repair routing or halting depending on policy.
19.3.2(d) Evidence and audit.Loop diagnostics emit evidence objects binding:
route hash,
test basis hash,
invariant/tolerance profile hashes,
defect statistics,
and the checkpoint hashes used to execute the loop test.
19.3.3 Certified loop-equivalence proofs
19.3.3(a) Loop-equivalence proof object.A loop-equivalence proof is a proof object:[\mathsf{ProofLoop}:=(h(\mathsf{Route}),h(\mathsf{id}),h(\mathcal I),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}),\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{TB}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(\mathsf{Evidence})?),]certifying that executing the route is equivalent to identity under declared invariants, tolerances, and verification level.
19.3.3(b) Proof verification.Verification reduces to:
executing the checker program on the route’s effect over the test basis,
verifying convention compatibility (spec bundles match),
verifying no hidden bridges or implicit coercions,
verifying tolerance ledger constraints for the loop.
19.3.3(c) Use in routing and optimization.Certified loop-equivalence proofs allow the optimizer and router to:
simplify routes by removing loops,
justify commuting transformations,
and certify replay associativity for route regroupings (Chapter 8 and 16).
19.3.3(d) Loop proof strength.Loop proofs are bounded by their verification level and test basis. If stronger loop identity guarantees are required, proof regeneration under stricter checks is necessary (Chapter 10).
19.3.4 Repair routing via minimal bridges
19.3.4(a) Holonomy repair classes.If a loop defect is detected, repair planning determines whether it is due to:
convention mismatch hidden by incomplete normalization (requires bridge insertion + normalization),
accumulation of approximate tolerances (requires reroute to exact transforms or insertion of stronger normalization checkpoints),
genuine non-identity due to lossy transforms (requires acknowledging quotient semantics or forbidding the loop).
19.3.4(b) Minimal bridge repair.If the defect is convention-related, the repair plan must:
revert to the last stable checkpoint,
insert the minimal certified bridge chain needed to restore exact convention alignment,
insert required post-bridge normalizers,
and rerun the loop test to certify loop-equivalence.
Minimality is deterministic by the route cost functional.
19.3.4(c) Evidence coupling.Repair plans must include the loop diagnostic evidence hash in their provenance source roots. Repair completion must emit evidence that the loop diagnostic now passes under the same or stronger claim boundary.
19.3.4(d) Non-repairable loop defects.If the loop includes an unavoidable lossy step or if required invariants cannot be preserved under any feasible route within (\Omega), the loop is non-repairable and must be marked inadmissible; the system must halt or reroute around the loop depending on goal constraints.
19.4 Tunneling (certified representation jumps)
Tunneling is a certified representation jump: a program that transitions between two distant regions of the metro map by routing through stability anchors and using minimal bridges and mandatory normalization, while preserving declared invariants and maintaining replay determinism.
19.4.1 Jump definition as a program
19.4.1(a) Tunnel program object.A tunnel is a program (\Pi_{\mathsf{tun}}) with a dedicated contract:[\mathsf{LT}_{\mathsf{tun}}: (L_a,\mathsf{Spec}_a)\Rightarrow(L_b,\mathsf{Spec}_b),]implemented as:
a checkpoint selection step (select anchor checkpoint(s)),
a bridge chain aligning conventions and lenses as needed,
mandatory normalization steps,
optional proof regeneration steps if required by the target’s verification level,
commitment of the tunnel receipt and any required alias/index artifacts.
The tunnel program is compiled deterministically; it is not a heuristic jump.
19.4.1(b) Tunnel objective.The tunnel objective is to minimize:
number of bridge steps,
tolerance usage,
witness burden,subject to preserving required invariants and meeting exactness/verification level requirements. This objective is pinned and yields deterministic tunnel route selection.
19.4.1(c) Tunnel legality.A tunnel is legal only if it has:
explicit spec bundles (\mathsf{Spec}_a,\mathsf{Spec}_b),
explicit bridge steps for any spec mismatch,
explicit normalizers at required insertion points,
and proof witnesses certifying correctness of each bridge and any approximate steps.
19.4.1(d) Tunnel receipts.Tunnel execution yields a tunnel receipt committing:
the source checkpoint hash,
the destination checkpoint hash (or destination state hash),
the bridge chain edge ids,
witness root and evidence root,
and the preserved invariants/tolerance profile.
19.4.2 Required checkpoints and bindings
19.4.2(a) Checkpoint requirements.Tunneling requires:
a source stability anchor checkpoint (\mathsf{Chk}_a) satisfying (g_a(\mathsf{Chk}_a)=\mathsf{true}),
a destination stability anchor checkpoint (\mathsf{Chk}_b) satisfying (g_b(\mathsf{Chk}_b)=\mathsf{true}),where (g_a,g_b) are pinned predicates. If the current state is not a stability anchor, a checkpoint-bound stabilization transform must be executed first.
19.4.2(b) Binding requirements.All tunnel witnesses bind:
source checkpoint hash,
destination state hash,
the tunnel program hash,
and the bridge chain delta root.This ensures the tunnel cannot be reinterpreted as a different jump without invalidating witnesses.
19.4.2(c) Dependency inclusion.Tunnel provenance source roots must include:
the metro map hash (h(\mathsf{Metro})),
the library root hash,
the corridor policy hash,
and any upstream receipts consumed.This ensures the tunnel is auditable and environment-specific.
19.4.2(d) Replay boundary guarantee.A completed tunnel yields a post-tunnel checkpoint that is a stability anchor. This ensures the tunnel output is a valid restart and replay boundary for subsequent computations.
19.4.3 Minimal-bridge property
19.4.3(a) Minimality statement.A tunnel route satisfies the minimal-bridge property if among all feasible tunnel routes meeting the invariant and policy constraints, it minimizes a lexicographic objective:[(\varepsilon\text{-priority},C_{\varepsilon},#\text{bridges},C_{\mathsf{wit}},C_{\mathsf{budget}},\mathsf{LexKey}).]This ensures preference for exact bridges, then minimal tolerance, then minimal bridge count, then witness and budget costs.
19.4.3(b) Minimality evidence.The tunnel plan includes an explainability artifact (Chapter 18) showing:
the selected bridge chain,
the cost tuple,
the first infeasibility reasons for the best alternative chains (optional by policy),
and tie-break evidence if needed.This binds minimality as an auditable claim.
19.4.3(c) Bridge closure requirements.Minimality depends on bridge catalog closure (Chapter 11). If the catalog lacks an exact bridge, the tunnel may require an approximate bridge; this must be explicitly recorded in tolerance usage and may be infeasible under exactness constraints.
19.4.3(d) Minimality under corridor tightening.If (\Omega) tightens budgets or tolerances, the minimal feasible tunnel route may change. Therefore tunnel plans are parameterized by (\Omega) and are not assumed portable across corridor policies without explicit policy-bridge receipts.
19.4.4 Post-jump normalization and commitments
19.4.4(a) Mandatory post-jump normalization.After executing the bridge chain, the tunnel must execute normalization steps that:
enforce canonical normal forms under the target convention bundle,
enforce contradiction consistency for any commitments introduced by the tunnel (e.g., tunnel receipt pins),
update tolerance ledgers and ensure they remain within budgets,
satisfy any checkpoint-bound postconditions required by stability anchors.
19.4.4(b) Commitment discipline.Tunnels may commit:
the tunnel receipt,
optional alias/index records for navigation,
and any necessary proof objects (stored in proof store, optionally pinned).Any additional commitments are forbidden unless explicitly required by the tunnel contract and authorized by scope and corridor budgets.
19.4.4(c) Post-jump evidence and audit chain.The tunnel must emit evidence objects binding:
pre and post spec bundles,
applied bridge ids and proof hashes,
normalization ids,
and the resulting stability anchor proof hash (if produced).This evidence forms a complete audit chain for the jump.
19.4.4(d) Post-jump correctness boundary.The tunnel guarantees only:
the declared invariants are preserved (exact or bounded),
the destination state is stable and replay-verifiable,
and any committed navigation artifacts are correct under the policy hashes.It does not guarantee that future routes from the destination are feasible; that depends on subsequent compilation under the metro map and corridor constraints.
Primary source artifact:
CHAPTER 19 — STABILITY PROJECTION AND LONG-CHAIN CONTROL
CHAPTER 20 — SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE: MODULES, INTERFACES, REFERENCE IMPLEMENTATION
This chapter specifies a concrete, modular system architecture for implementing the framework. The architecture is defined as a collection of deterministic modules with explicit interfaces, pinned policies, and replay-verifiable behavior. The reference implementation is defined at the level of module contracts and interface semantics: each module’s responsibilities, inputs/outputs, canonical encodings, failure modes, and trust boundaries are specified so that independent implementations can interoperate by hash-committed artifacts and produce identical ledger and receipt commitments under identical policies.
20.1 Module decomposition
20.1.1 Object layer (schemas, codecs, store)
20.1.1(a) Responsibilities.The object layer is responsible for:
enforcing the Canonical Object Standard (COS) encodings and validation rules,
providing schema registries and type tags,
providing canonical codecs (encoders/decoders),
providing content-addressable storage (CAS) for object bytes,
providing canonical indexing structures used by the execution kernel (pin records, edge records, candidate records, proof objects),
providing Merkle utilities for roots and membership proofs used by receipts.
The object layer is pure with respect to semantics: it does not interpret invariants; it guarantees only canonical bytes, hashes, and schema correctness.
20.1.1(b) Core data types.The object layer exposes the following typed primitives:
(\mathsf{TypeId}): canonical type identifier.
(\mathsf{SchemaHash}): hash of a schema definition.
(\mathsf{ObjBytes}\in{0,1}^{8\ast}): canonical serialized bytes for an object payload.
(\mathsf{ObjHash}\in{0,1}^{256}): content hash (H(\mathsf{Tag}(t)|\mathbf{b})).
(\mathsf{ObjRef}:=(\mathsf{TypeId},\mathsf{ObjHash})): typed reference.
All fields are encoded under COS (Chapter 6). Every object written to any store must have a valid (\mathsf{ObjRef}) and must validate against its schema hash.
20.1.1(c) Interfaces.The object layer exposes deterministic interfaces:
Schema registry[\mathsf{SchemaGet}(\mathsf{TypeId})\to \mathsf{SchemaHash}\cup \mathsf{Err},\quad\mathsf{SchemaBytes}(\mathsf{SchemaHash})\to \mathsf{ObjBytes}\cup\mathsf{Err}.]
Codec[\mathsf{Encode}(\mathsf{TypeId},x)\to \mathsf{ObjBytes}\cup \mathsf{Err},\quad\mathsf{Decode}(\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b})\to x\cup \mathsf{Err}.]
Validation[\mathsf{Validate}(\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b})\to {\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}},\quad\mathsf{Hash}(\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b})\to \mathsf{ObjHash}.]
CAS[\mathsf{CASPut}(\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b})\to \mathsf{ObjHash}\cup\mathsf{Err},\quad\mathsf{CASGet}(\mathsf{ObjHash})\to \mathsf{ObjBytes}\cup\mathsf{Err}.]
Merkle[\mathsf{MerkleRoot}([\mathbf{b}_1,\ldots,\mathbf{b}_n])\to \mathsf{ObjHash},\quad\mathsf{MerkleProof}({\mathbf{b}_i},i)\to \mathsf{ProofBytes},\quad\mathsf{VerifyMerkle}(\mathsf{root},\mathbf{b},\mathsf{proof})\to {\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}}.]
All of these are deterministic under pinned policies. Any deviation in canonical encoding, hashing, or Merkle construction is a determinism failure at the system level.
20.1.1(d) Error semantics.Object-layer errors are typed COS error objects (Chapter 5 evidence/failure codes) and must include:
error code enum value,
referenced (\mathsf{TypeId}) and (\mathsf{SchemaHash}) where applicable,
the offending hash or bytes hash where applicable,
and deterministic diagnostic payload fields.
Object-layer errors never mutate state; they are safe to cache and replay.
20.1.2 Verifier layer (witness rules, method registry)
20.1.2(a) Responsibilities.The verifier layer is responsible for:
defining witness schemas and required binding fields,
defining a method registry for proof/equivalence/trace/rewrite/dependency proof methods,
providing deterministic verification procedures for each witness type and each method id,
enforcing verification-level constraints and claim strength limits,
enforcing provenance binding to step contexts and anti-laundering conditions.
The verifier layer is part of the trusted computing base: it defines what constitutes a valid proof and how it is checked.
20.1.2(b) Method registry.A method registry entry is a COS object:[\mathsf{Method}:=(\mathsf{method_id},\mathsf{ver_digest},h(\mathsf{InSchema}),h(\mathsf{OutSchema}),h(\mathsf{LCDSchema}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(\mathsf{VLPolicy}),h(\mathsf{DisclosurePolicy}),h(\mathsf{CostClass})).]
(\mathsf{ver_digest}) is the implementation digest hash pinned in the runtime header.
(\mathsf{ChkProg}) references a deterministic checker program descriptor.
(\mathsf{VLPolicy}) specifies admissible claim classes per verification level.
(\mathsf{DisclosurePolicy}) specifies required local-check disclosures for public verification modes.
(\mathsf{CostClass}) specifies cost accounting parameters for corridor compute budgets.
A verifier set hash (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}) commits to the entire method registry state.
20.1.2(c) Verifier interfaces.The verifier layer exposes:
Witness verification[\mathsf{VerifyWitness}(\mathsf{wtype},h(W),\mathsf{Ctx},\mathsf{Resolve})\to {\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}}\times h(\mathsf{Evidence})?,]where (\mathsf{Resolve}) is a deterministic resolver into CAS bytes and schemas.
Proof method verification[\mathsf{VerifyMethod}(\mathsf{method_id},h(\mathsf{Proof}),\mathsf{Ctx},\mathsf{Resolve})\to {\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}}\times h(\mathsf{Evidence})?.]
Claim admissibility check[\mathsf{CheckClaim}(\mathsf{method_id},h(\mathsf{Claim}),\mathsf{VL})\to {\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}}.]
These interfaces must be deterministic and polynomial-time in the size of disclosed local-check data, per Chapter 5.
20.1.2(d) Binding and anti-laundering enforcement.Verifier procedures must enforce:
exact match of binding record (\mathsf{Ctx}) fields,
exact match (or permitted reference) to the provenance witness hash for the step,
inclusion of delta root and post-state hash in bindings,
inclusion of policy hashes (at minimum (h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}})) in bindings.
Failure to enforce binding is a verifier defect and invalidates the security model; therefore binding checks are mandatory and executed before any semantic verification.
20.1.3 Execution layer (kernel, projector, detectors)
20.1.3(a) Responsibilities.The execution layer is responsible for:
maintaining runtime state stores (commitment, relation, candidate, proof),
executing step semantics: expand → apply → project (Chapter 4),
enforcing corridor policy (\Omega) (Chapter 3) via the legality projector,
running drift detectors and producing evidence objects (Chapter 9),
producing ledger records and checkpoints (Chapter 13),
enforcing normalization insertion points as mandated (Chapter 7),
invoking certified repair workflows when required (Chapter 10), strictly as explicit plans.
20.1.3(b) Kernel interface.The kernel exposes:[\mathsf{Step}(\Psi,\mathsf{StepDesc},\Omega)\to (\Psi',\mathsf{StepRec},\mathsf{Chk}',h(\mathsf{EvidenceList})),]implemented internally as:
(\mathsf{Expand}),
(\mathsf{Apply}) (opcode execution against library entries or kernel primitives),
(\mathsf{Project}) (feasibility checks, witness verification, detector schedule, acceptance mask),
state update by applying accepted deltas,
deterministic logging.
The step interface is the only state-transition interface; any module that mutates state must do so through the kernel.
20.1.3(c) Projector and detector submodules.Execution decomposes into:
Projector[\mathsf{Project}(\Psi,\Delta,W,\mathbf{c},\Omega,\mathsf{StepDesc})\to (\Psi',\mathsf{verdict},m,h(\mathsf{EvidenceList})).]
Detectors[\mathsf{DetectPhase}_p(\Psi,\mathsf{StepDesc},\Delta,W,\mathbf{c},\Omega)\to (\mathsf{OK/NEAR/FAIL},h(\mathsf{Evidence})),]for (p\in{\mathsf{PREAPPLY},\mathsf{PREPROJECT},\mathsf{PROJECT},\mathsf{POSTCOMMIT}}).
Detector ordering and evidence retention are pinned by (\Omega) and by the ledger plan. Detector implementations are pinned by digest hashes in the runtime header and are part of the trusted base for determinism.
20.1.3(d) Execution invariants and failure behavior.Execution must preserve:
replay determinism,
canonical ordering of deltas, witnesses, and evidence,
contradiction-free commitment constraints under (\Omega),
and stable checkpoint semantics.
Hard failures yield (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}) with deterministic evidence objects. Partial acceptance is permitted only per the ledger plan and corridor classification; it is enforced by deterministic acceptance masks and recorded in the step record.
20.1.4 Compilation layer (parser, router, assembler, optimizer)
20.1.4(a) Responsibilities.The compilation layer is responsible for:
parsing and normalizing intents into canonical goal objects (Chapter 12),
extracting context and constraints as hash-addressed objects,
constructing or loading the metro map graph object (Chapter 18),
routing: selecting deterministic shortest-legal routes under (\Omega),
assembling route plans into executable micro-programs with parameter hashes,
emitting witness plans and ledger plans,
emitting explainability artifacts for routing and tie-break decisions,
performing proof-carrying optimization (Chapter 16), producing rewrite proofs and optimized program objects when permitted.
Compilation is deterministic and produces plan hashes that uniquely identify the intended execution.
20.1.4(b) Interfaces.Compilation exposes:
Parse and normalize[\mathsf{ParseIntent}(\text{Text})\to h(\mathsf{Goal})\cup\mathsf{Err},\quad\mathsf{ExtractContext}(\text{Text})\to h(\mathsf{Context})\cup\mathsf{Err}.]
Metro map build/load[\mathsf{BuildMetro}(h(\mathsf{LibRoot}),h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}})\to h(\mathsf{Metro})\cup\mathsf{Err}.]
Route selection[\mathsf{Route}(h(\mathsf{Metro}),h(\mathsf{Goal}),h(\mathsf{Context}),h(\mathsf{Constraints}),h(\mathsf{Checkpoint})?)\to h(\mathsf{RoutePlan})\cup\mathsf{Err}.]
Plan assembly[\mathsf{Assemble}(h(\mathsf{RoutePlan}))\to h(\mathsf{Plan})\cup\mathsf{Err}.]
Optimize (optional, policy-gated)[\mathsf{Optimize}(h(\mathsf{Plan}),h(\mathsf{OptPolicy}))\to h(\mathsf{Plan}')\times h(\mathsf{OptProofs})\cup\mathsf{Err}.]
All outputs are COS objects stored in the proof store; compilation may pin plans depending on scope.
20.1.4(c) Deterministic constraints.Compilation determinism requires:
pinned parser and normalization dictionaries,
pinned cost functional weights and tie-break rules,
pinned feasibility predicates and method registry,
canonical serialization of plans.
Compilation must not consult ambient state beyond the explicitly resolved checkpoint (if routing is state-dependent). Any state dependence must be expressed by including the checkpoint hash as an input to routing and plan hashing.
20.1.4(d) Compilation failure artifacts.Compilation failure produces typed error artifacts with deterministic evidence including:
failing feasibility predicates,
the earliest failing boundary,
missing bridge classes or unsupported verifier methods,
and the policy hashes in effect.
Failure artifacts are suitable inputs for certified repair planning (e.g., add missing bridges to library, tighten corridor, adjust constraints).
20.2 Determinism engineering
20.2.1 Canonical byte formats
20.2.1(a) Canonical encoding invariants.All modules must treat COS canonical encoding as the sole source of truth for object identity. Canonical encoding invariants include:
fixed field ordering per schema,
canonical integer encoding (CVI),
canonical map and set ordering,
fixed endianness and layout policies for arrays,
explicit schema hashes embedded in type tags or headers.
Any module that accepts non-canonical bytes is ill-formed; any module that emits non-canonical bytes produces invalid hashes and breaks replay.
20.2.1(b) Canonicalization boundaries.Canonicalization must occur:
at object ingestion (decoder refuses ill-formed bytes),
before hashing,
before committing objects to CAS,
before committing any delta or witness lists,
before computing Merkle roots for receipts.
Canonicalization is not optional; it is enforced by the object layer and re-checked by the execution projector.
20.2.1(c) Canonical equality discipline.Equality checks are always performed on canonical bytes or hashes. Semantic equality that could map distinct bytes to equal semantics is prohibited by COS: canonical encoders must select unique representatives, ensuring semantic equality implies definitional equality for encodable values.
20.2.1(d) Cross-implementation conformance.A reference conformance suite must test:
byte-equality of encoded objects across implementations,
hash equality for identical semantic inputs,
Merkle root equality for identical leaf sets,
failure determinism for malformed objects.Conformance test artifacts are plans and receipts, not informal tests.
20.2.2 Deterministic numeric policies
20.2.2(a) Numeric policy objects.Numeric determinism is enforced by pinned policy objects:
quantization policies (Q) for all committed numeric values,
float policies (F) only when explicitly allowed, including NaN and signed-zero rules,
overflow and rounding policies.
Policies are referenced by hash in all objects that depend on numeric interpretation.
20.2.2(b) Quantization-first rule.Any numeric value that affects hashing, ordering, or verification outcomes must be quantized to integers under a pinned policy. Floating values may exist in transient computation but must not be committed unless their exact byte representation is pinned by a float policy.
20.2.2(c) Numeric reproducibility.Execution and verification must produce identical quantized outputs on replay. Therefore:
numeric operations that affect committed artifacts must be deterministic under pinned policies,
any use of approximate floating must be either prohibited or explicitly bounded and recorded as tolerance usage,
numeric drift beyond declared tolerances triggers defect detection and repair.
20.2.2(d) Numeric audit objects.When required by (\Omega), numeric computations produce evidence objects:
pre/post quantized values,
drift calculations,
threshold comparisons,
policy hashes used.These evidence objects are replay-verifiable and eliminate hidden numeric divergence.
20.2.3 Deterministic ordering policies
20.2.3(a) Global ordering commitments.Determinism requires pinned ordering policies for:
map key ordering,
set element ordering,
witness ordering,
delta operation ordering,
candidate sorting and truncation,
route tie-breaks in compilation and routing,
rewrite rule ordering in optimization.
Ordering policies are explicit objects whose hashes are committed in the runtime header and in plans.
20.2.3(b) Tie-break rules.All ties must be resolved deterministically using pinned rules, typically:
lexicographic order on canonical bytes,
lexicographic order on hashes,
or lexicographic order on (score vector, hash) pairs.No randomness or platform iteration order is permitted.
20.2.3(c) Canonical iteration prohibition.Implementations must not depend on:
hash-table iteration order,
non-deterministic scheduling,
concurrency races,
unspecified container ordering.Any such dependence is a determinism defect and is detected by replay divergence checks.
20.2.3(d) Ordering evidence for explainability.Routing and optimization modules must be able to emit deterministic tie-break evidence objects:
competing candidates’ cost tuples,
selected candidate’s lexicographic key,
rule id and selection rationale encoded as structured fields.This provides audit-ready explainability without narrative.
20.2.4 Reproducible pseudo-randomness (if allowed)
20.2.4(a) Policy-gated randomness.Pseudo-randomness is permitted only if (\Omega) explicitly allows it. If allowed, it must be represented as a deterministic function of:
a pinned seed object hash,
a pinned generator algorithm id hash,
and a pinned usage protocol (how many draws, where, in what order).
Randomness is therefore replayable and auditable.
20.2.4(b) Seed objects.A seed is a typed object:[\mathsf{Seed}:=(\mathsf{seed_kind},\mathbf{b}_{\mathsf{seed}},h(\mathsf{GenAlg})),]stored in CAS and referenced by hash. Seed use must be committed in provenance source roots and parameter hashes.
20.2.4(c) Randomness scope restriction.Randomness must not influence:
canonical encoding or ordering directly,
commitment decisions without certified selection proofs,
detector decisions without explicit policy.Randomness may be used for optional test generation at specified verification levels only if the verification level semantics allow seed-based deterministic generation and the seed is committed.
20.2.4(d) Randomness audit trail.When randomness is used, the system must emit evidence:
seed hash,
generator id hash,
draw-count commitments (or transcript hash),
and derived test basis hash.This ensures replay verification can reproduce the same generated artifacts.
20.3 Storage and indexing
20.3.1 Content-addressable stores
20.3.1(a) CAS architecture.All artifacts are stored in CAS keyed by (\mathsf{ObjHash}). CAS must support:
idempotent put: (\mathsf{CASPut}) returns existing hash for identical bytes,
get by hash,
optional existence checks,
optional batch retrieval and batch validation.
CAS is the backbone for deduplication and for privacy by commitment.
20.3.1(b) Store partitioning.Implementations may partition CAS by object family (proof objects, plans, receipts, witnesses) for performance, but partitioning must not affect canonical hashes. Partition boundaries must be invisible to consumers: the hash is the single address.
20.3.1(c) Integrity requirements.CAS must enforce:
hash correctness (bytes must match key),
type correctness (type tag validation),
schema correctness (validation against schema).Any mismatch is a hard error and must not be silently repaired.
20.3.1(d) Merkleized store snapshots.For receipts and audit, stores may expose Merkle roots over subsets (pins, witnesses, evidence). Merkle commitments must use pinned ordering and pinned hash functions; membership proofs are used for dependency proofs and public verification packages.
20.3.2 Pin indices and prefix scans
20.3.2(a) Commitment store indexing.The commitment store supports canonical key lookup and canonical prefix scans:[\mathsf{PinGet}(\mathsf{pin_id})\to \mathsf{PinRecord}\cup\mathsf{None},\quad\mathsf{PinScan}(\mathsf{prefix})\to [\mathsf{PinRecord}]^\ast.]Scan results must be ordered canonically by pin id.
20.3.2(b) Index objects.For large key spaces, indices are explicit objects pinned by hash:[\mathsf{Index}:=(\mathsf{index_kind},\mathsf{prefix},r_{\mathsf{pins}},h(\mathsf{BuildPlan}),h(\mathsf{Rcpt})),]binding the index to a build plan and receipt. Index updates are commitment mutations and require witnesses and contradiction checks.
20.3.2(c) Prefix scan determinism.Prefix scans must be deterministic across implementations:
canonical collation rules are pinned,
no locale-dependent ordering is permitted,
range boundaries are canonical byte boundaries.
20.3.2(d) Receipt and plan indices.Dedicated indices may exist:
by plan hash,
by final state hash,
by output pin id,
by library entry id (cached hub receipts).All such indices are explicit objects with receipts or with admission proofs to ensure they are reproducible and audit-ready.
20.3.3 Proof retrieval and dependency DAGs
20.3.3(a) Link index.Proof objects include explicit link lists; the proof store maintains a link index:[\mathsf{Links}(h_o)\to [h_{r_1},\ldots,h_{r_k}],]canonically ordered. Link lists are used to reconstruct dependency closures for verification and garbage collection.
20.3.3(b) Dependency DAG object.A dependency graph for a plan or receipt is represented as a COS object:[\mathsf{DepDAG}:=(h(\mathsf{Root}),h(\mathsf{Nodes}),h(\mathsf{Edges}),h(\mathsf{Topo})?),]where nodes are object hashes and edges are “consumes” relations. The DAG can be derived deterministically from link indices and step provenance source roots.
20.3.3(c) Retrieval protocols.Retrieval must support:
closure retrieval for a receipt: fetch all objects needed to verify receipt validity,
closure retrieval for a proof witness: fetch local-check data and referenced objects,
closure retrieval for a plan: fetch referenced entries, contracts, specs, policies, and macro expansions.
Closure retrieval is deterministic given the root hash and pinned policies.
20.3.3(d) Verification caching.Verification results may be cached as explicit cached-verification objects binding:
object hash,
verifier set hash,
policy hashes,
outcome and evidence hash.Caching is safe only when cache keys include policy hashes; otherwise it can cause invalid acceptance under changed verifiers.
20.3.4 Garbage collection under proofs
20.3.4(a) GC safety principle.Garbage collection must not delete any object that is reachable from:
a pinned commitment,
a pinned receipt,
a pinned plan,
or a pinned proof/witness required by any of the above.
Reachability is determined by link indices, Merkle membership proofs, and dependency DAGs.
20.3.4(b) Root set definition.The GC root set is a pinned object:[\mathsf{GCRoots}:=(h(\mathsf{Pins}),h(\mathsf{Receipts}),h(\mathsf{Plans}),h(\mathsf{ProofRoots}),h(\mathsf{Policies})),]constructed deterministically from the commitment store and configured retention policies (also pinned). Any change to retention policy yields a new root set hash.
20.3.4(c) Mark-and-sweep with deterministic traversal.GC uses deterministic mark traversal:
traverse roots in canonical order,
traverse link lists in canonical order,
mark reachable hashes.Sweep deletes unmarked objects. Deletion order does not affect semantics, but must not violate determinism of observable state. Deletion events may be logged as evidence objects if (\Omega) requires audit.
20.3.4(d) Proof retention constraints.If a receipt is published or used as a dependency, all objects required for its verification must be retained at least until its retention policy expires (policy-defined). If objects are deleted, the receipt becomes unverifiable for parties lacking caches; therefore retention policy must be explicit and reflected in publication workflows.
20.4 Security and trust boundaries
20.4.1 Trusted computing base definition
20.4.1(a) TCB components.The trusted computing base (TCB) is the minimal set of components whose correctness is required for system soundness and determinism:
Hash function (H) implementation and binding identifier.
Canonical encoders/decoders and schema registry (COS).
Merkle construction and membership proof verifier.
Verifier set implementations and method registry digests.
Execution kernel projector (\mathsf{Project}) and its enforcement of (\Omega).
Deterministic ordering and tie-break policy implementations.
Ledger encoding and hashing implementations.
Any component outside this set may be replaced without breaking the formal guarantees if it does not affect canonical bytes, hashes, or verifier outcomes.
20.4.1(b) TCB version pinning.All TCB components are pinned by digest hashes in:
runtime header,
ledger header,
and receipts.Therefore any TCB change produces different header hashes and breaks compatibility unless explicitly bridged by policy migration mechanisms (if permitted). No silent TCB upgrades are permitted.
20.4.1(c) TCB conformance.TCB conformance is established by conformance suites expressed as plans and receipts that:
test canonical encoding equivalence,
test verifier correctness on known proof objects,
test projector behavior on constructed steps,
test replay determinism across implementations.
20.4.1(d) TCB failure semantics.If replay divergence occurs under identical header hashes, this indicates a TCB fault or hash collision. The correct system behavior is deterministic halting with replay divergence evidence and refusal to continue execution under the same runtime header.
20.4.2 Witness laundering defenses
20.4.2(a) Step-local binding enforcement.Witness laundering is prevented by mandatory binding of each witness to:
op id,
parameter hash,
pre-state hash,
delta root,
source root,
post-state hash,
policy hashes.Binding is verified by deterministic verifiers and is included in evidence on failure.
20.4.2(b) Provenance witness primacy.A provenance witness (PROV) is required for any non-revert step and acts as the binding root. Other witnesses may reference the provenance witness hash, but may not omit binding fields required by policy. This prevents partial bindings that could be replayed in other contexts.
20.4.2(c) Non-transplantability as enforced property.Because (\mathsf{Ctx}) includes post-state hash and delta root, a witness authorizing one delta cannot authorize another.
