Any attempt to reuse witnesses in a different step produces verifier failure deterministically. This is an enforcement property, not an assumption.
20.4.2(d) Proof reuse boundaries.Proof objects (equivalence proofs, rewrite proofs, trace proofs) may be reused only as referenced artifacts, not as step-local witnesses, unless they are re-bound by step-local provenance witnesses and the method policies allow such reuse. This separates “proof object identity” from “authorization to commit” and prevents laundering via detached proof references.
20.4.3 Convention smuggling defenses
20.4.3(a) Convention fingerprints as hard boundaries.All objects that participate in composition or commitment carry or reference convention bundles by hash. Spec mismatches are detected deterministically and either repaired by explicit bridges or rejected. This prevents hidden sign flips, scaling changes, index shifts, unit changes, and branch changes.
20.4.3(b) Bridge-only convention change rule.Any convention change must occur via explicit bridge transforms whose contracts declare:
SpecIn and SpecOut,
mapping rule objects,
proofs of correctness under declared invariants and tolerances.Implicit coercions are prohibited and detected by drift detectors.
20.4.3(c) Normalization insertion enforcement.Post-bridge normalization is mandatory. Without normalization, representational slack can conceal convention drift. The system enforces normalization insertion points via plan compilation and projector checks.
20.4.3(d) Smuggling detection evidence.Detected convention smuggling produces evidence objects that bind:
expected and observed spec hashes,
boundary location (step index, transform id),
missing bridge classes,
and required repair class.Evidence is replay-verifiable and suitable for certified repair planning.
20.4.4 Denial-of-service and governance controls
20.4.4(a) DoS surfaces.Potential denial-of-service surfaces include:
uncontrolled candidate explosion,
excessive proof-store writes,
expensive verifier methods invoked repeatedly,
unbounded trace proofs,
large Merkle sets for receipts,
repeated contradiction resolution attempts.
All such surfaces are controlled by corridor budgets: compute caps, write budgets, branch caps, expansion caps, and rate limits.
20.4.4(b) Governance controls.The system includes explicit governance objects (pinned by hash) defining:
which transforms are admitted into the library,
which proof methods are allowed,
which corridor policies are permitted,
who may pin commitments in protected namespaces (modeled as authorization predicates and required witnesses),
deprecation and supersession procedures for entries, macros, and rules.
Governance is executed as plans with receipts; it is not informal administration.
20.4.4(c) Throttling and rate limiting.Rate limits and throttling policies (Chapter 3) are enforced by detectors and projector rules. Triggered throttles cause deterministic omission of soft outputs or hard failure depending on policy. Throttle events are recorded as evidence and can trigger repair (e.g., switching to reuse routes).
20.4.4(d) Safe degradation and explicit fallback.If goals allow fallback, the system may compile fallback plans under relaxed constraints, but only by explicit constraint objects and certified selection steps (Chapter 17). Any degradation of guarantees (lower verification level, nonzero tolerances) must be committed in plan and receipt hashes. Silent weakening is forbidden.
CHAPTER 20 — SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE: MODULES, INTERFACES, REFERENCE IMPLEMENTATION
CHAPTER 21 — FORMAL COMPLETION STATEMENT AND CLOSURE THEOREMS
This chapter states precisely what the framework completes, what it does not claim, and the closure theorems that characterize its formal power. The completion is not a claim that mathematics is “finished.” It is a claim that a missing operational layer—lawful, auditable representation transport—has been made explicit, executable, and replay-verifiable. The framework thereby converts mathematical practice into a proof-carrying program calculus with deterministic verification receipts.
21.1 What is completed
21.1.1 Missing layer identified (representation transport)
21.1.1(a) The missing layer.Let (\mathcal{O}) denote the class of mathematical objects as used in practice. Standard mathematical texts and implementations primarily specify:
internal rules within a chosen representation (coordinates, basis, encoding, convention),
derivations performed inside that representation,
occasional informal justification that a change of representation “preserves meaning.”
The missing layer is the absence of a first-class, rule-governed object that specifies representation change as:
a typed carrier map between representations,
an explicit invariant set that is preserved (exactly or within tolerance),
an explicit convention fingerprint mapping,
explicit scope and budget rules,
a verifiable witness binding to provenance and replay.
Without this layer, representation changes are typically informal, implicit, or implementation-defined, and therefore cannot be audited, replayed, or composed safely at scale.
21.1.1(b) Representation transport as a typed transport problem.Define a lens (L=(D,\mathcal{A},\mathcal{I})) (Chapter 1) as a representation carrier. A representation transport is a typed morphism[\mathsf{LT}: L_{\mathrm{in}}\Rightarrow L_{\mathrm{out}}]with an executable carrier map (\widetilde{T}:D_{\mathrm{in}}\to D_{\mathrm{out}}) and a contract specifying preserved invariants (\mathcal{I}{\mathsf{LT}}), tolerances (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathsf{LT}}), budgets (\mathcal{B}{\mathsf{LT}}), scope (\mathcal{S}{\mathsf{LT}}), witnesses (\mathcal{W}{\mathsf{LT}}), and replay profile (\mathcal{R}{\mathsf{LT}}) (Chapter 2). The framework treats this morphism as the primitive unit of “meaning-preserving change,” rather than treating it as an informal or external operation.
21.1.1(c) The corrected primitive notion of “same meaning.”“Same meaning” is not defined by symbol similarity or by a narrative interpretation. It is defined by explicit invariants and observables:[x \sim_{\mathcal{I},\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}} y\iff\forall I\in\mathcal{I}:\ |I(x)-I(y)|\le \varepsilon_I,]with all evaluations performed deterministically and canonically. This corrects the common practice failure where equivalence is asserted without stating what is preserved, under what measurement regime, and under what conventions.
21.1.1(d) Completion statement for the missing layer.The framework completes the missing layer by providing:
typed lenses and canonical object identities,
legal transform contracts for representation transport,
corridor policy (\Omega) enforcing budgets, scope, determinism, and contradiction discipline,
witness system preventing certificate laundering via step-local provenance binding,
execution kernel producing replay-verifiable ledgers and receipts,
deterministic compilation to certified routes and repair/optimization as certified transforms.
21.1.2 Explicit constructs added (contracts, policies, witnesses, replay)
21.1.2(a) Contract construct (LTC).The legal transform contract (\mathsf{LT}) is the canonical object that replaces informal “this transform preserves meaning” statements. It commits by hash to:
input/output lenses,
executable carrier map specification,
invariant clauses and tolerances,
budgets and scope,
witness profile and verifier method ids,
replay profile (canonical parameterization and canonicalization requirements).
A transform is not admitted unless these elements are explicit and verifiable.
21.1.2(b) Policy construct (corridor (\Omega)).(\Omega) is a pinned legality environment that:
budgets compute/write/branch/tolerance,
enforces scope discipline between exploration and commitment,
pins determinism requirements and contradiction policy,
defines detector schedules and mitigation obligations,
defines admissible escalations and their proof obligations.
This replaces implicit assumptions about “reasonable runtime” and “safe outputs” with a machine-checkable legality boundary.
21.1.2(c) Witness construct (step-local certificates).Witnesses are typed objects whose validity reduces to deterministic verification and whose binding includes:[(\mathsf{op_id},h_P,h_{\mathsf{pre}},r_\Delta,r_{\mathsf{src}},h_{\mathsf{post}},h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{hdr}}).]This binding prevents transplanting proofs, authorizations, or resolutions into contexts other than the one in which they were issued. The witness system therefore formalizes “why this step is allowed” as a verifiable artifact, eliminating unaudited authority.
21.1.2(d) Replay construct (ledger and receipts).The ledger is an append-only chain of canonically encoded step records; receipts are compact commitments to ledger validity, final state, outputs, witnesses, and policy hashes. Replay verification is therefore a deterministic function of the ledger and referenced objects; correctness claims are portable as receipts and composable by dependency proofs.
21.1.3 Reduction of practice to executable programs
21.1.3(a) Practice primitives.Mathematical practice, as executed in research and engineering, consists primarily of:
defining objects and representations,
converting between representations,
computing derived objects,
proving equivalence or bounds (often informally),
checking consistency and resolving contradictions,
optimizing or simplifying derivations,
reusing results.
21.1.3(b) Program reduction theorem (structural).For each practice primitive above, the framework provides a program-level reduction:
Definitions → COS objects with canonical encoding and hashes; commitments pin them.
Representation conversions → legal transforms (\mathsf{LT}) with explicit contracts.
Computations → execution kernel steps applying (\mathsf{LT}) programs.
Proofs/bounds → proof objects with local-check verification and verification levels.
Consistency/contradiction resolution → consistency witnesses and post-resolution audits.
Optimization → proof-carrying rewrites with rewrite proofs and non-weakening constraints.
Reuse → receipts + dependency proofs binding consumed artifacts.
Thus practice is reduced to execution of a program (\Pi) with a ledger and receipt.
21.1.3(c) Operational semantics as the authoritative meaning.Let (\mathrm{Exec}(\Pi,\Psi_0,\Omega)) denote executing program (\Pi) from checkpoint state (\Psi_0) under corridor (\Omega), yielding ledger (\mathsf{Ledger}), final state (\Psi_n), and receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}). The authoritative meaning of the practice act is:
the committed artifacts in (\Psi_n),
the verifiable trace (\mathsf{Ledger}),
and the claim boundaries committed by (\mathsf{Rcpt}) (invariants/tolerances/verification levels/conventions).
21.1.3(d) Elimination of untracked degrees of freedom.The reduction is meaningful only because degrees of freedom that historically cause drift are eliminated or forced explicit:
conventions and normal forms are hashed and enforced,
tolerances are declared and ledgered,
witness methods and checkers are pinned,
routing and tie-break decisions are deterministic and receipted.
21.1.4 Irreducibility arguments for components
21.1.4(a) Canonicalization irreducibility.Without canonical encoding and hash-addressed identity:
equality is implementation-dependent,
receipts are not stable,
replay verification cannot be well-defined.Therefore canonicalization is necessary for deterministic identity and interoperable verification.
21.1.4(b) Witness irreducibility.Without step-local binding of witnesses to ((h_{\mathsf{pre}},r_\Delta,h_{\mathsf{post}})):
valid proofs can be transplanted into invalid contexts,
authorization can be laundered,
contradiction resolution can be replayed incorrectly.Therefore provenance-bound witnesses are necessary for anti-laundering and for auditable legality.
21.1.4(c) Corridor irreducibility.Without (\Omega):
exploration can explode without bound,
implicit writes can corrupt commitments,
contradiction control becomes ad hoc,
determinism becomes unenforceable.Therefore a pinned corridor policy is necessary for resource safety and global coherence.
21.1.4(d) Ledger/receipt irreducibility.Without a canonically encoded ledger and replay verifier:
verification is not portable,
results cannot be reused safely,
“proof-carrying computation” collapses into informal trust.Therefore ledger + receipt are necessary for compositional verification and auditability.
21.2 Soundness (bounded) and its exact meaning
21.2.1 Soundness relative to invariants and tolerances
21.2.1(a) Bounded soundness definition.A claim in this framework is a tuple:[C := (h(\mathcal I),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}),\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{Spec}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(\mathsf{TB}),h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{hdr}}),]binding invariants, tolerances, verification level, conventions, checker program, test basis, and policy hashes. A receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}) is sound for claim (C) iff:
(\mathsf{Rcpt}) is valid (Chapter 13–14),
every proof object required by the plan verifies under the method registry and checker programs committed by (h_{\mathsf{hdr}}),
every invariant check required by (C) passes under the declared (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) on the declared test basis regime (as required by (\mathsf{VL})),
convention fingerprints match the claim’s (\mathsf{Spec}) (or are bridged explicitly and the claim accounts for it),
tolerance ledger constraints for the executed segment do not exceed (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) where the claim requires bounded accumulation.
21.2.1(b) What soundness does and does not assert.Soundness asserts that:
the executed artifacts satisfy the declared checks under the declared regime,
and that verification is replay-deterministic under pinned policies.Soundness does not assert that:
the invariant set is complete for some external notion of “truth,”
the test basis is exhaustive beyond its declared verification level semantics,
the world conforms to the model beyond what is encoded in the invariants.
21.2.1(c) Soundness as a verifier theorem.Let (\mathsf{Verify}(\mathsf{Ledger},\mathsf{LedHdr})) be the replay verifier. If (\mathsf{Verify}) returns (\mathsf{OK}) and all required proof verifiers return (\mathsf{true}), then every committed output claimed by (\mathsf{Rcpt}) satisfies its declared claim boundary (C). This is a property of the framework’s verification semantics; it is independent of informal interpretation.
21.2.1(d) Soundness failure semantics.If (\mathsf{Verify}) fails or any required proof verifier fails, the receipt is invalid for the claim boundary. Failure yields deterministic evidence objects identifying the earliest failing step and the failure class. No “partial trust” is implied unless explicitly encoded by a weaker claim boundary and a separate receipt that certifies that weaker boundary.
21.2.2 Local-check boundaries and claim strength
21.2.2(a) Local-check principle.Proof verification is constrained to polynomial-time deterministic checks over disclosed local-check data. Therefore every proof method defines a claim boundary: what it can certify at each verification level (\mathsf{VL}). The claim boundary is part of the method registry and is enforced by proof verifiers rejecting over-claims.
21.2.2(b) Verification level semantics.Verification levels (\mathsf{VL0..VL4}) (Chapter 5) define increasing strength: finite tests, deterministic expanded tests, bounded coverage regimes, and mechanized certificates where permitted. A claim is valid only at its declared (\mathsf{VL}). Interpreting a (\mathsf{VL0}) proof as a global theorem is illegal within the framework.
21.2.2(c) Strength preorder.Claim strength ordering is defined by:
invariant superset inclusion,
tolerance tightening,
verification level dominance,
convention bundle specificity.Receipts and proofs compose under this preorder; substitution requires refinement proofs.
21.2.2(d) Explicitness of non-exhaustiveness.Where a method cannot certify global properties, the method registry and claim objects must encode this limitation. The framework thereby prevents accidental escalation of “tested under regime (R)” into “true universally,” unless an explicit mechanized proof method supports it and is pinned.
21.2.3 Determinism assumptions
21.2.3(a) Determinism as a pinned requirement.Determinism is not assumed; it is enforced by:
canonical encodings,
pinned numeric policies,
pinned ordering and tie-break rules,
pinned verifier set digests,
and replay verification.
A computation is admissible only if it is replay-deterministic under the runtime header hashes.
21.2.3(b) Allowed pseudo-randomness.If pseudo-randomness is allowed by (\Omega), it is treated as deterministic given pinned seeds and pinned generators. Seed hashes must appear in provenance source roots. Any randomness not committed by hash is illegal.
21.2.3(c) Determinism boundary for external dependencies.External data may be consumed only via hash-committed artifacts (external commitments). If an external source changes without a new commitment hash, the computation cannot legally depend on it. This ensures determinism in the sense relevant to verification: all dependencies are hash-addressed and replay-resolvable.
21.2.3(d) Determinism failure classification.Replay divergence is a hard failure. The framework treats it as a TCB fault or a dependency violation. The correct response is halting or certified repair at the system layer (not semantic reinterpretation).
21.2.4 Explicit non-goals and limits
21.2.4(a) No claim of absolute mathematical completeness.The framework does not claim to decide all mathematical statements. It claims to formalize and verify the process of doing mathematics and computation within declared claim boundaries, not to provide an oracle for undecidable problems.
21.2.4(b) No hidden semantics beyond declared invariants.The framework does not infer meaning beyond declared invariants and observables. If a user requires a property not declared, it must be added as an invariant with a verifier regime; otherwise it is out of scope.
21.2.4(c) No guarantee beyond local-check regimes without mechanized proofs.Where verification relies on local-check regimes, the guarantees are bounded. Stronger guarantees require stronger proof methods and explicit mechanized certificates. The framework supports such methods but does not presume their existence.
21.2.4(d) No bypass of corridor and governance.The framework does not allow unbounded search, silent policy weakening, implicit convention changes, or informal commitment of contradictory truths. All such attempts are blocked by (\Omega), witnesses, detectors, and contradiction policy.
21.3 Completeness (representational)
21.3.1 Expressibility of mathematical acts
21.3.1(a) Representational completeness statement.A framework instance (\mathcal{F}) is representationally complete for a practice domain (\mathcal{D}) if every act in (\mathcal{D}) can be expressed as a program (\Pi) over legal transforms and primitives such that executing (\Pi) under some corridor (\Omega) produces:
the intended artifacts as COS objects,
and verifiable receipts establishing the intended claim boundaries.
This is a statement about expressibility in the program calculus, not about solving all problems in (\mathcal{D}) efficiently.
21.3.1(b) Act-to-program mapping.For each act type:
define objects → COS objects and pins,
compute → legal transform programs,
verify → proof objects with local-check verifiers,
compare/merge → equivalence proofs and contradiction resolutions,
optimize → rewrite proofs and optimization receipts,
reuse → receipts + dependency proofs,
repair → certified repair programs.
Therefore every act has a canonical artifact type and a canonical program-level representation.
21.3.1(c) Conditions for expressibility.Expressibility requires:
appropriate lenses and object schemas for the domain,
a transform library containing the required transports and computations,
witness methods capable of certifying the required claim boundaries,
corridor policies permitting the required budgets and scopes.
These are explicit and can be expanded by domain plugins (Chapter 11).
21.3.1(d) Expressibility boundary.If an act relies on an implicit convention or an informal equivalence, it is not representationally complete until that convention and equivalence are formalized as spec fingerprints and invariants with verifiers. The framework therefore converts “informal practice debt” into explicit missing constructs.
21.3.2 Closure under certified composition
21.3.2(a) Closure theorem (contracts).Given legal transforms (\mathsf{LT}_1,\mathsf{LT}_2) that are composable under compatibility protocol, their composition (\mathsf{LT}_2\circ \mathsf{LT}_1) is representable as a program and inherits:
invariant contract intersection,
tolerance composition by pinned operators,
scope intersection (conservative),
witness union (step-local),
replay profile compatibility enforced by normalization/bridge boundaries.
Thus the system is closed under composition at the program level.
21.3.2(b) Closure theorem (receipts).Given receipts (\mathsf{Rcpt}_u) and (\mathsf{Rcpt}_d) with verified dependency proofs and compatibility constraints, their sequential composition yields a composed verification artifact that is itself verifiable under the receipt algebra. Parallel merge yields a co-verification artifact when non-contradictory. Therefore verification is closed under composition and aggregation.
21.3.2(c) Closure theorem (repair).Every repair is itself a legal program. Repair receipts can be composed into longer chains and can be depended upon by subsequent computations via dependency proofs. Therefore the system is closed under self-correction without leaving the formal execution model.
21.3.2(d) Closure theorem (optimization).Proof-carrying rewrites transform programs into programs, producing rewrite proofs and optimization receipts. Optimized programs can substitute original programs where rewrite proofs establish equivalence under required claim boundaries. Thus the system is closed under certified optimization.
21.3.3 Self-hosting and recursion bounds
21.3.3(a) Self-hosting statement.The framework is self-hosting when:
plans, macros, rewrite rules, and even portions of the verifier registry are represented as COS objects,
compilation outputs are themselves pinned and receipted,
optimization and repair are represented as legal transforms over these objects.
Self-hosting means the system can reason about and transform its own programs and artifacts within the same verification discipline.
21.3.3(b) Recursion and boundedness.Self-hosting does not imply unbounded recursion. Recursion is governed by:
corridor expansion caps,
branch caps,
compute and write budgets,
and explicit recursion policies for macro expansion or exploration.Any recursive procedure must be represented as a bounded program or as progressive deepening under caps with explicit evidence and halting conditions.
21.3.3(c) Fixed-point stabilization.Stability anchors (Chapter 19) provide fixed points for long-chain control and for recursive workflows: a self-hosting transformation must end in a stability anchor to be admissible as a reusable artifact for subsequent passes. This converts recursion into a sequence of stable segments, each with receipts.
21.3.3(d) Non-self-contradiction boundary.Because commitments are contradiction-controlled and proof strengths are ordered by refinement, self-hosting updates must respect supersession and replacement rules. The system forbids silent overwrites; it requires explicit refinement proofs or contradiction resolution proofs, preventing self-hosting from degenerating into uncontrolled self-modification.
21.3.4 Library expansion as future work
21.3.4(a) Expansion is explicit and receipted.Adding new lenses, transforms, bridges, invariants, or proof methods is not an informal act. It requires:
COS schema admission,
transform contract admission,
proof method registry admission,
and admission receipts demonstrating deterministic execution and verification under pinned policies.
21.3.4(b) Expansion preserves invariants of the framework instance.Library expansion must preserve:
determinism (canonical encodings and pinned methods),
witness anti-laundering properties,
corridor feasibility discipline,
compatibility protocol closure where claimed.
If expansion would weaken these, it must occur under a new runtime header hash and be treated as a new instance, not a silent change.
21.3.4(c) Extension mechanism.Domain plugins (Chapter 11) provide the formal extension mechanism: they introduce new lens families, invariants, transforms, and proof methods with admission tests and receipts. Extension is therefore structured, auditable, and compatible with the metro map compiler.
21.3.4(d) Future completeness is catalog completeness.The primary “future work” dimension is not adding new meta-rules; it is expanding the catalog of admissible, certified transforms and proof methods so that more domains and more acts become representable under stronger claim boundaries and higher verification levels.
21.4 One-page founding specification
21.4.1 Minimal axioms and objects
A0 (Hash and canonical encoding).There exists a fixed hash function (H) and a canonical object standard (COS) such that every typed object (o) has canonical bytes (\mathbf{b}) and identity (h(o)=H(\mathsf{Tag}(t)|\mathbf{b})). Canonical encoding is deterministic and cross-platform stable.
A1 (Lens).A lens is a typed object (L=(D,\mathcal{A},\mathcal{I})) with admissible payload predicate (\mathsf{Adm}_L), canonical addressing (\mathcal{A}), and invariant schema (\mathcal{I}).
A2 (Legal transform contract).A legal transform contract is a typed object (\mathsf{LT}) binding:[(L_{\mathrm{in}},L_{\mathrm{out}},\widetilde{T},\mathcal{I}{\mathsf{LT}},\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathsf{LT}},\mathcal{B}{\mathsf{LT}},\mathcal{S}{\mathsf{LT}},\mathcal{W}{\mathsf{LT}},\mathcal{R}{\mathsf{LT}}),]where (\widetilde{T}) is deterministic and all components are hash-pinned.
A3 (Corridor policy).A corridor policy (\Omega) is a pinned object defining feasibility constraints and mandatory checks: budgets, scope, determinism, contradiction policy, detector schedule, and admissible escalations.
A4 (Witness system).Witnesses are typed objects verified by a pinned verifier set; validity includes binding to step context:[(\mathsf{op_id},h_P,h_{\mathsf{pre}},r_\Delta,r_{\mathsf{src}},h_{\mathsf{post}},h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{hdr}}).]
A5 (State and kernel).State (\Psi) consists of four stores (commitment, relation, candidate, proof) with canonical hashes. The kernel executes steps by expand → apply → project, producing verdicts and deterministic post-state hashes.
21.4.2 Execution rule and legality predicate
21.4.2(a) Step execution rule.Given pre-state (\Psi), step descriptor (\mathsf{StepDesc}), and corridor (\Omega), execute:[\mathsf{Expand}\to \mathsf{Apply}\to \mathsf{Project},]producing ((\Psi',\mathsf{verdict},m,E)).
21.4.2(b) Legality predicate.A step is legal iff:
(\mathsf{Project}) returns (\mathsf{ACCEPT}) or (\mathsf{PARTIAL}),
all required witnesses verify and bind to the reconstructed step context,
all mandatory detectors satisfy (\Omega)’s pass conditions,
contradiction policy holds in the post-state,
canonical normal form invariants hold at commitment boundaries.
Illegal steps revert to pre-state with deterministic evidence.
21.4.2(c) Plan execution rule.A plan is a program (\Pi) with witness plan (\mathsf{WPlan}) and ledger plan (\mathsf{LPlan}). Executing a plan is sequential execution of its steps under (\Omega), logging each step record and producing a terminal checkpoint.
21.4.2(d) Replay rule.Replay verification recomputes post-state hashes by deterministic re-application of accepted deltas under (\Omega) and verifies all required witnesses. Any mismatch is a determinism failure.
21.4.3 Verification receipt definition
21.4.3(a) Receipt definition.A receipt is a typed object committing:
plan hash,
ledger header hash,
final state hash,
Merkle roots for output pins and witnesses (and evidence if required),
corridor and runtime header policy hashes,
disclosure mode.
21.4.3(b) Receipt validity.A receipt is valid iff:
the referenced ledger verifies under replay,
final state hash matches,
Merkle roots match extracted outputs and used witnesses,
policy hashes match.
21.4.3(c) Receipt composition.Receipts compose sequentially via dependency proofs and merge in parallel via union roots subject to non-contradiction. Refinement is defined by a strength preorder and requires explicit refinement proofs.
21.4.3(d) Receipt boundary.A receipt certifies only the declared claim boundary of its plan: invariants, tolerances, verification levels, conventions, and policies. No additional interpretation is permitted.
21.4.4 Interop conformance checklist
Hash function binding: identical (H) identifier hash; identical Merkle policy hash.
COS conformance: canonical encoders/decoders produce byte-identical encodings for all supported types; schema hashes match.
Numeric policy conformance: quantization and float policies (if allowed) match by hash; committed numerics are identical under replay.
Ordering conformance: map/set ordering, delta ordering, witness ordering, candidate ordering, routing tie-breaks match by hash.
Verifier set conformance: method registry and verifier digests match; proof verifiers accept/reject identically; verification levels interpreted identically.
Kernel conformance: expand/apply/project semantics match; mask computation and verdict rules match; post-state hashes match under replay.
Corridor conformance: (\Omega) hashes match; detector schedules and thresholds match; contradiction policy matches.
Ledger conformance: step record schema hashes match; chain hashing matches; receipts computed match.
Library conformance: library root hash matches; entry admission receipts verify; metro map hash matches when built from the same inputs.
Repair/optimization conformance: repair plans and rewrite rules are pinned; proof-carrying rewrites verify; resulting plan hashes are stable under the same policies.
CHAPTER 21 — FORMAL COMPLETION STATEMENT AND CLOSURE THEOREMS
APPENDIX A — ADDRESSING, CANONICAL NAMING, AND HASHING
This appendix specifies the addressing and naming substrate required for deterministic identity, deterministic traversal, and replay-stable verification. It defines (i) address algebras as typed objects with canonical encodings and total orders, (ii) canonical naming rules and namespace partitioning for commitments and indices, (iii) canonical serialization rules sufficient for cross-platform byte stability, and (iv) hash commitments and Merkle constructions used to bind objects, pinsets, witnesses, and outputs to verifiable roots.
A.1 Address algebra formalism
An address algebra formalizes the index space used to address components of structured objects (arrays, tensors, coefficient tables, residue classes, graph vertices/edges, plan steps, proof fields). It is the sole admissible basis for deterministic enumeration.
A.1.1 Index sets and encodings
A.1.1(a) Index set object.An index set is a typed object (\mathsf{IdxSet}) with canonical fields:[\mathsf{IdxSet}:=(\mathsf{kind},\ h(\mathsf{Param}),\ h(\mathsf{Enc}),\ h(\mathsf{Dec}),\ h(\mathsf{Bounds})?,\ h(\mathsf{Eq})?),]where:
(\mathsf{kind}) is a pinned enum identifying the index family (e.g., (\mathsf{NAT}), (\mathsf{INT}), (\mathsf{TUPLE}), (\mathsf{GRID}), (\mathsf{RESIDUE}), (\mathsf{PATH}), (\mathsf{EDGEID}), (\mathsf{STEPID})).
(\mathsf{Param}) is a typed parameter object (e.g., dimension (d), modulus (m), shape vector (\mathbf{n}), coordinate bounds).
(\mathsf{Enc}) is a deterministic encoding rule object.
(\mathsf{Dec}) is a deterministic decoding rule object.
(\mathsf{Bounds}) optionally constrains the set to a finite subset (e.g., (0\le i < n)).
(\mathsf{Eq}) optionally defines an equality predicate for semantic equivalence beyond byte equality (default is exact equality of canonical encodings; any weaker equality must be explicit).
The semantic carrier of (\mathsf{IdxSet}) is denoted (\llbracket \mathsf{IdxSet}\rrbracket), the set of admissible indices.
A.1.1(b) Canonical index encoding.Each (\mathsf{IdxSet}) induces a canonical encoding function:[\mathsf{enc}:\ \llbracket \mathsf{IdxSet}\rrbracket \to {0,1}^{8\ast},]and a canonical decoding function:[\mathsf{dec}:\ {0,1}^{8\ast}\to \llbracket \mathsf{IdxSet}\rrbracket \cup {\bot}.]The following are required:
Determinism: (\mathsf{enc}(i)) is uniquely determined for any (i) in the index set.
Sound decode: if (\mathsf{dec}(\mathbf{b})=i\neq\bot), then (\mathsf{enc}(i)=\mathbf{b}).
Completeness: for any (i), (\mathsf{dec}(\mathsf{enc}(i))=i).
Well-formedness: any (\mathbf{b}) that is not in the image of (\mathsf{enc}) decodes to (\bot).
These requirements imply a bijection between indices and their canonical bytes.
A.1.1(c) Standard index kinds.The following index kinds are normative templates (notationally, “(\cong)” denotes definitional equality of the template’s canonical schema):
Natural range (\mathsf{IdxSet}_{\mathsf{Nat}(n)}\cong(\mathsf{kind}=\mathsf{NAT},\ \mathsf{Param}=(n),\ \mathsf{Bounds}: 0\le i < n)).Encoding: canonical unsigned varint of (i). Decoding rejects (i\ge n).
Integer range (\mathsf{IdxSet}_{\mathsf{Int}(a,b)}) with (a\le i < b).Encoding: ZigZag varint of (i-a). Decoding rejects outside bounds.
Fixed-length tuple (\mathsf{IdxSet}_{\mathsf{Tuple}(\mathsf{IdxSet}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{IdxSet}_d)}).Encoding: concatenate encodings (\mathsf{enc}_1(i_1)|\cdots|\mathsf{enc}_d(i_d)) with length-prefixes per component. Decoding splits deterministically using length prefixes and applies component decoders.
Grid coordinate (\mathsf{IdxSet}_{\mathsf{Grid}(\mathbf{n})}) where (\mathbf{n}\in\mathbb{N}^d).Semantic indices are (d)-tuples ((i_1,\ldots,i_d)) with (0\le i_k<n_k). Encoding uses tuple encoding of natural ranges.
Residue class (\mathsf{IdxSet}{\mathsf{Res}(m)}) where (m\in\mathbb{N}{\ge 1}).Semantic indices are residues in ({0,\ldots,m-1}). Encoding uses unsigned varint; decoding rejects (\ge m). Any alternative residue representative is illegal unless an explicit equivalence predicate is provided and a canonical representative rule is pinned.
A.1.1(d) Address record schema.An address is a typed record:[\mathsf{Addr}:=(h(\mathsf{IdxSet}),\ \mathbf{b}{\mathsf{idx}}),]where (\mathbf{b}{\mathsf{idx}}) is the canonical encoding of an index in the referenced index set. Address well-formedness requires (\mathsf{dec}(\mathbf{b}_{\mathsf{idx}})\neq \bot). The address identity hash is computed from canonical bytes of (\mathsf{Addr}).
A.1.2 Canonical orderings
A.1.2(a) Canonical total order.Every address algebra must define a canonical total order (\preceq) on indices. The order is required to be:
Total: for all (i,j), (i\preceq j) or (j\preceq i).
Antisymmetric: (i\preceq j) and (j\preceq i) implies (i=j).
Transitive: (i\preceq j\preceq k\Rightarrow i\preceq k).
Deterministic: depends only on the canonical bytes (or pinned derivations from them).
A.1.2(b) Default ordering by canonical bytes.The default ordering is byte-lexicographic order on canonical encodings:[i\preceq j \iff \mathsf{enc}(i)\le_{\mathrm{lex}} \mathsf{enc}(j),]where (\le_{\mathrm{lex}}) is lexicographic order on byte strings under a pinned byte collation (bytewise comparison). This yields a total order because (\mathsf{enc}) is injective.
A.1.2(c) Structured lexicographic orders.For product index sets (\mathsf{Tuple}(\mathsf{IdxSet}1,\ldots,\mathsf{IdxSet}d)), an alternative canonical order may be declared:[(i_1,\ldots,i_d)\preceq (j_1,\ldots,j_d)\iff\exists k:\ i_1=j_1,\ldots,i{k-1}=j{k-1}\ \land\ i_k\preceq_k j_k,]where (\preceq_k) are the component canonical orders. This order is admissible only if it is pinned as part of the address algebra and yields the same order as byte-lexicographic order under the chosen tuple encoding; otherwise it is a different order and must be explicitly declared and used consistently.
A.1.2(d) Enumeration function.An address algebra provides a deterministic enumeration function for finite bounded sets:[\mathsf{Enum}(\mathsf{IdxSet})\to [i_1,\ldots,i_N],]returning all indices in increasing order. For finite (\mathsf{IdxSet}) with bounds, (\mathsf{Enum}) must return the complete set without omission or duplication. For infinite sets, (\mathsf{Enum}) is a stream operator and must be used only under explicit truncation caps; any truncation must be deterministic by the declared order.
A.1.3 Isomorphisms between address algebras
Address-algebra isomorphisms formalize “same index space, different encoding/order” and support certified bridges.
A.1.3(a) Address algebra object.An address algebra is a typed object:[\mathcal{A}:=(h(\mathsf{IdxSet}),\ h(\preceq),\ h(\mathsf{EncRule}),\ h(\mathsf{DecRule})),]where the (\mathsf{IdxSet}) fixes admissible indices and (\preceq) fixes the canonical order. The encoding/decoding rule hashes must match those of the index set; any mismatch is ill-formed.
A.1.3(b) Isomorphism definition.An isomorphism between two address algebras (\mathcal{A}) and (\mathcal{A}') is a bijection:[\pi:\ \llbracket \mathsf{IdxSet}\rrbracket \to \llbracket \mathsf{IdxSet}'\rrbracket]such that:
Bijective: (\pi) is one-to-one and onto.
Order transport: for all (i,j),[i\preceq j \iff \pi(i)\preceq' \pi(j),]if the isomorphism is declared order-preserving. If not order-preserving, the isomorphism must declare the order change explicitly and is treated as a transport that requires explicit bridge steps.
Encoding transport: there exists a deterministic encoding map (\phi) such that[\mathsf{enc}'(\pi(i)) = \phi(\mathsf{enc}(i)),]and (\phi) is pinned by hash.
A.1.3(c) Isomorphism certificate object.An isomorphism is represented by a typed certificate:[\mathsf{IsoCert}:=(h(\mathcal{A}),h(\mathcal{A}'),h(\mathsf{PiRule}),h(\mathsf{PiInvRule}),h(\mathsf{OrderClaim}),h(\mathsf{TestBasis}),\mathsf{VL}),]where:
(\mathsf{PiRule}) pins a deterministic procedure computing (\pi(i)),
(\mathsf{PiInvRule}) pins a deterministic procedure computing (\pi^{-1}(j)),
(\mathsf{OrderClaim}) declares whether order is preserved,
(\mathsf{TestBasis}) binds a finite basis for local verification when full proof is not mechanized,
(\mathsf{VL}) is the verification level for the certificate.
For finite bounded index sets, (\mathsf{IsoCert}) may require full enumeration proof (trace proof) establishing bijection; for large sets, (\mathsf{VL}) must explicitly bound the claim.
A.1.3(d) Composition and identity.Isomorphism certificates compose: if (\pi_{12}:\mathcal{A}_1\to\mathcal{A}2) and (\pi{23}:\mathcal{A}2\to\mathcal{A}3), then (\pi{23}\circ \pi{12}) yields (\mathcal{A}_1\to\mathcal{A}_3) with composed rule objects pinned by hash. Identity isomorphism is represented by the identity rule and is always admissible.
A.1.4 Address compatibility predicates
Compatibility predicates determine whether two address algebras can be composed directly, require an explicit bridge, or are incompatible.
A.1.4(a) Compatibility outcome type.Define the compatibility outcome enum:[\mathsf{AddrCompat}\in{\mathsf{MATCH},\mathsf{ISO_NEEDED},\mathsf{BRIDGE_NEEDED},\mathsf{INCOMPATIBLE}}.]A compatibility decision is accompanied by a typed evidence object binding the compared hashes and the reason codes.
A.1.4(b) Match predicate.(\mathsf{MATCH}(\mathcal{A},\mathcal{A}')) holds iff:
(h(\mathsf{IdxSet})=h(\mathsf{IdxSet}')),
(h(\preceq)=h(\preceq')),
and encoding/decoding rule hashes match.
This is definitional equality of address algebras; no bridge is needed.
A.1.4(c) Isomorphism-needed predicate.(\mathsf{ISO_NEEDED}) holds iff:
the index sets are isomorphic and the algebras are intended to be equivalent representations (e.g., same coordinate set with different encoding),
an (\mathsf{IsoCert}) exists (by hash) admissible under the verifier set and corridor policy,
and the intended composition context permits substitution by isomorphism (e.g., purely addressing-level alignment without semantic changes).
In this case, an explicit address-bridge transform must be inserted whose correctness is justified by the (\mathsf{IsoCert}).
A.1.4(d) Bridge-needed vs incompatible.(\mathsf{BRIDGE_NEEDED}) holds iff:
a deterministic mapping exists but is not an isomorphism in the required sense (e.g., non-bijective projection, coarsening, quotienting),
or the order is not preserved and the consumer requires a specific canonical order,
or the mapping changes semantics beyond mere addressing.
(\mathsf{INCOMPATIBLE}) holds iff no admissible bridge exists under the current library and corridor constraints, or if the required mapping would violate forbidden domain or contradiction policies. The compatibility predicate must be deterministic and must output evidence binding the missing bridge class or the violated constraint.
A.2 Canonical naming rules
Canonical naming defines the only admissible keys for commitment pins, indices, aliases, library entries, plans, receipts, and published artifacts. Canonical naming is a determinism boundary: any non-canonical name is ill-formed.
A.2.1 Allowed alphabets and normalization
A.2.1(a) Canonical key type.A canonical key is a UTF-8 byte string (\mathbf{k}) satisfying a pinned grammar (\mathsf{KeyGrammar}). Canonical keys are compared bytewise; no locale-dependent collation is permitted.
A.2.1(b) Grammar constraints.The grammar is specified as a deterministic predicate (\mathsf{KeyOK}(\mathbf{k})) with at minimum:
Allowed characters: ASCII subset ({a!-!z, A!-!Z, 0!-!9, _, -, /, .}) plus an optional pinned extension set. Any Unicode beyond ASCII is forbidden unless explicitly permitted by policy and normalized by a pinned normalization rule.
No leading/trailing whitespace. No internal whitespace.
No empty segments: for slash-separated namespaces, “//” is forbidden.
Max length constraints: a pinned integer bound (L_{\max}).
Reserved prefixes: a pinned list of reserved prefixes; keys must not collide with reserved system keys unless they are system-generated objects.
A.2.1(c) Normalization function.A normalization function (\mathsf{NormKey}) is a deterministic function mapping arbitrary text inputs to canonical keys. Within the system, a key is admissible iff (\mathsf{NormKey}(\mathbf{k})=\mathbf{k}). (\mathsf{NormKey}) is pinned by hash; changing normalization rules changes key admissibility and therefore requires a new policy hash.
A.2.1(d) Key identity.Key identity is definitional: two keys are identical iff their canonical bytes are equal. No case-folding equivalence or Unicode normalization equivalence is permitted unless explicitly implemented by (\mathsf{NormKey}) and thus baked into canonical bytes.
A.2.2 Namespace partitioning
A.2.2(a) Namespace object.A namespace is a typed object:[\mathsf{NS}:=(\mathsf{prefix},h(\mathsf{Schema}),h(\mathsf{Policy}),h(\mathsf{ContrPol})?,h(\mathsf{AuthPol})?),]where:
(\mathsf{prefix}) is a canonical key prefix (e.g., “PLAN/”, “RECEIPT/”, “TL/LT/”, “TL/BRIDGE/”),
(\mathsf{Schema}) specifies the value schema that may be pinned under this namespace,
(\mathsf{Policy}) specifies allowed operations (add/update/supersede), evidence retention, and whether aliasing is permitted,
(\mathsf{ContrPol}) optionally specifies contradiction control rules,
(\mathsf{AuthPol}) optionally specifies required authorization witnesses to write in this namespace.
A.2.2(b) Namespace well-formedness.A namespace is well-formed iff:
(\mathsf{prefix}) is canonical and ends with “/”.
(\mathsf{prefix}) is disjoint from other namespace prefixes under a pinned non-overlap policy, or else overlap is explicitly declared and ordered by longest-prefix match.
(\mathsf{Schema}) resolves and is immutable by hash.
Policies resolve and are pinned.
A.2.2(c) Key-to-namespace resolution.Key resolution to a namespace is deterministic by longest-prefix match over declared namespaces. If no namespace matches, the key is invalid. If multiple matches exist with equal longest-prefix length, the configuration is invalid and must be rejected as a policy error.
A.2.2(d) Namespace-based constraints.Namespace policies define:
whether updates are allowed,
whether supersession requires refinement proofs,
whether multiple values under a key are permitted (typically forbidden unless disambiguation is explicit),
and whether the namespace is contradiction-controlled.
These constraints are enforced by commitment witnesses and the corridor contradiction policy.
A.2.3 Alias objects and alias legality
Aliases provide stable naming indirections without changing object identity. Alias mutation is a commitment mutation and is contradiction-controlled.
A.2.3(a) Alias object schema.An alias is a typed object:[\mathsf{Alias}:=(\mathsf{alias_key},\mathsf{target_kind},\mathsf{target_id},h(\mathsf{Target})?,h(\mathsf{PolicyBind}),h(\mathsf{Prov})),]where:
(\mathsf{alias_key}) is a canonical key in an alias namespace,
(\mathsf{target_kind}\in{\mathsf{PIN},\mathsf{OBJHASH},\mathsf{ENTRY},\mathsf{PLAN},\mathsf{RECEIPT}}),
(\mathsf{target_id}) is the referenced identifier (pin id bytes or object hash bytes),
(h(\mathsf{Target})) optionally binds additional target metadata (schema hash, spec bundle hash),
(h(\mathsf{PolicyBind})) binds policy hashes under which the alias is intended to be interpreted,
(h(\mathsf{Prov})) binds provenance to the step context that created/updated the alias.
A.2.3(b) Alias resolution algorithm.Alias resolution is deterministic:
Given an alias key, retrieve the alias object by key from the commitment store.
If (\mathsf{target_kind}=\mathsf{PIN}), resolve (\mathsf{target_id}) by pin lookup.
If (\mathsf{target_kind}=\mathsf{OBJHASH}), resolve from CAS.
If (\mathsf{target_kind}) references a library entry/plan/receipt, resolve by the appropriate namespace lookup.
Resolution must enforce policy binding compatibility: if the current runtime header hashes do not match (h(\mathsf{PolicyBind})) and no policy-bridge is permitted, resolution is illegal for execution contexts and must produce a typed error.
A.2.3(c) Alias legality constraints.An alias is legal iff:
(\mathsf{alias_key}) is canonical and in an alias namespace.
The target resolves under the declared (\mathsf{target_kind}).
The alias does not create a cycle under a pinned cycle policy. If alias-to-alias is permitted, cycles are forbidden; cycle detection must be deterministic and bounded (e.g., maximum alias-depth (d_{\max}) pinned).
If the alias namespace is contradiction-controlled, any update must be accompanied by a consistency witness justifying overwrite/supersession.
A.2.3(d) Alias supersession and refinement.Alias updates that change target must be represented as:
supersession records with refinement proofs when the alias is intended to denote “best available” under a strength preorder, or
disambiguation (new alias keys) when targets are not refinement-related.
Silent replacement is forbidden.
A.2.4 Collision and ambiguity rules
Naming collisions and ambiguous references are handled only through explicit disambiguation, merge proofs, or supersession under refinement.
A.2.4(a) Collision definition.A collision is one of:
Two distinct values attempt to occupy the same contradiction-controlled key without an admissible resolution kind.
Two distinct aliases in the same alias namespace attempt to claim the same canonical name with incompatible targets under the namespace’s semantics.
Two schema-incompatible values are pinned under the same namespace prefix where the schema requires uniqueness.
Collisions are detected by the contradiction predicate pinned in the corridor policy and by namespace-specific validators.
A.2.4(b) Ambiguity definition.Ambiguity occurs when a key or alias resolves to multiple admissible targets under a namespace that permits multiplicity (rare), or when external inputs contain a non-canonical name that can be normalized in more than one way. Ambiguity is not resolved by heuristic; it yields an explicit ambiguity artifact (candidate set) or a hard failure depending on (\Omega).
A.2.4(c) Disambiguation mechanisms.Permitted disambiguation mechanisms are:
Key refinement: append a deterministic disambiguator suffix derived from a hash prefix of the target (e.g., “/h:ABCD1234”), with suffix formatting pinned by policy.
Namespace split: move one of the colliding items into a different namespace, with explicit bridge or dependency links if needed.
Merge: if two items are equivalent under an explicit equivalence proof, insert a merge record and update references, subject to contradiction policy.
Supersession: replace an item by a stronger refinement item, with explicit refinement proof and supersession record.
All mechanisms are explicit and receipted.
A.2.4(d) Deterministic tie-break rules.When multiple disambiguation options are admissible, selection is deterministic:
Prefer merge if equivalence proof exists and namespace permits merge.
Else prefer supersession if refinement proof exists and namespace permits supersession.
Else prefer key refinement with deterministic suffix.
Else fail with ambiguity evidence.
Tie-break decisions must be recorded as evidence objects binding the competing targets and the chosen rule id.
A.3 Canonical serialization
Canonical serialization ensures that objects have unique byte representations and therefore stable hashes. This appendix section specifies the minimal serialization rules required for interoperability.
A.3.1 Field ordering and schema hashing
A.3.1(a) Schema object and canonical schema hash.A schema is a typed object:[\mathsf{Schema}:=(\mathsf{schema_id},\mathsf{type_tag},[\mathsf{Field}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{Field}_m],h(\mathsf{Rules}),\mathsf{ver}),]where each field is:[\mathsf{Field}:=(\mathsf{name},\mathsf{ftype},\mathsf{reqFlag},h(\mathsf{Subtype})?,h(\mathsf{Constraints})?).]Field order is the sequence order in the schema object. The schema hash (h(\mathsf{Schema})) is computed from canonical bytes of the schema; it is used as an immutable schema identifier.
A.3.1(b) Canonical field ordering rule.Encoding must write fields in the exact schema order. Maps inside objects are ordered by canonical key bytes; set-like fields are ordered by canonical element bytes; sequence-like fields preserve order. Optional fields use canonical presence markers.
A.3.1(c) Schema evolution and compatibility.Any schema change produces a different schema hash. Compatibility across schema hashes is never assumed. If a migration is required, it must be represented as an explicit transform with a contract declaring how fields map and which invariants are preserved.
A.3.1(d) Schema validity predicate.A schema is valid iff:
field names are canonical keys under a pinned grammar for field names,
field types are known (primitive, composite, map, set, sequence),
constraints and subtype rules resolve by hash,
and no duplicate field names exist.
Schema invalidity is a policy error and forbids object decoding/encoding under that schema hash.
A.3.2 Deterministic numeric encoding
A.3.2(a) Canonical varint.All integers are encoded using a single pinned integer encoding scheme:
Unsigned varint for (\mathbb{N}) with minimal byte length.
Signed integers encoded by a pinned bijection (\mathsf{ZigZag}:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{N}) followed by unsigned varint.
Any alternative integer encoding is illegal.
A.3.2(b) Quantized numeric objects.Any numeric value that affects hashing, ordering, or verification must be represented as:[(,h(Q),\ q,),]where (Q) is a pinned quantization policy object and (q\in\mathbb{Z}) is the quantized integer. Quantization policy (Q) includes scaling, rounding, overflow behavior, and dtype tags. Numeric equality for quantized values is byte equality of ((h(Q),q)).
A.3.2(c) Floating values (when permitted).Floating values may appear in committed objects only when a pinned float policy object (F) exists. Floats are then encoded as raw IEEE-754 bytes under policy (F), with any required canonicalization (NaN normalization, signed zero normalization) applied deterministically prior to encoding. If float policy is absent, floats are illegal in committed objects.
A.3.2(d) Numeric comparison rules.All comparisons that affect ordering or feasibility are performed on canonical encodings:
quantized integers compare by integer order,
float bytes compare only when explicitly permitted and pinned; otherwise floats must be quantized before any comparison used in ordering or hashing.
Tolerance comparisons use quantized norms and pinned metric objects; no mixed numeric mode comparisons are permitted.
A.3.3 Canonical set/list encoding
A.3.3(a) Sequence-like encoding.A sequence-like collection ([x_1,\ldots,x_n]) is encoded as:
varint length (n),
element encodings in index order.
Sequence semantics treat order as meaning.
A.3.3(b) Set-like encoding.A set-like collection ({x_1,\ldots,x_n}) is encoded as:
varint cardinality (n),
canonical bytes of each element,
sort the element-byte strings lexicographically,
verify strict uniqueness of element bytes,
emit in sorted order.
Set semantics treat order as irrelevant and forbid duplicates.
A.3.3(c) Map encoding.A map (M:K\to V) is encoded as:
varint cardinality,
sort keys by canonical key bytes,
for each key, emit key bytes then value bytes.
Maps are deterministic under canonical key encoding; duplicate keys are illegal.
A.3.3(d) Canonical ordering invariants.Canonical encodings must ensure:
the same semantic set produces the same bytes regardless of insertion order,
iteration over encoded collections is deterministic,
the lexicographic order used is pinned and platform-independent.
Any implementation-dependent ordering breaks determinism and is invalid.
A.3.4 Cross-platform byte stability proof sketch
A.3.4(a) Uniqueness of canonical encoding.Assume:
schemas define fixed field order,
integer encodings are pinned and minimal,
set/map ordering is pinned lexicographic on canonical bytes,
numeric policies are pinned and applied deterministically,
decoders reject non-canonical forms.
Then for any semantic object value (x) representable under a schema, there exists exactly one byte string (\mathbf{b}) such that (\mathsf{Decode}(\mathbf{b})=x) and (\mathbf{b}) is canonical.
A.3.4(b) Platform-independence lemma.If two implementations share:
identical schema hashes,
identical canonical encoding rules,
identical numeric policies,
identical ordering policies,then for any representable (x), both implementations compute identical (\mathbf{b}=\mathsf{Encode}(x)). Therefore object hash identity is implementation-independent.
A.3.4(c) Deterministic hash identity lemma.Given a fixed hash function (H), if (\mathbf{b}) is canonical and identical across implementations, then (H(\mathbf{b})) is identical across implementations. Therefore references by hash are portable.
A.3.4(d) Error determinism lemma.Because decoders and validators are deterministic and reject non-canonical encodings, error outcomes are deterministic: the same malformed bytes produce the same failure code and evidence payload hash (under pinned error schema), enabling replay-stable failure behavior.
A.4 Hash commitments and Merkle structures
Hash commitments bind objects and sets of objects to compact roots. Merkle structures enable membership proofs needed for receipts, dependency proofs, and publication bundles.
A.4.1 Object hash definition
A.4.1(a) Domain-separated hashing.Define a domain-separated hash:[h(o) := H(\mathsf{DS}(t)\ |\ \mathbf{b}),]where:
(t) is the type tag,
(\mathsf{DS}(t)) is a fixed domain-separation prefix derived deterministically from (t) (e.g., (\mathsf{DS}(t)=H(\mathsf{Tag}(t))) or a pinned encoding of the type tag),
(\mathbf{b}) is canonical object bytes (excluding the type tag if the type tag is already in (\mathsf{DS}(t)); the exact convention is pinned and must be consistent).
Domain separation prevents cross-type collisions from being interpreted as equal objects.
A.4.1(b) Hash correctness predicate.An object reference ((t,h)) is valid iff:
(\mathbf{b}) resolves in CAS at key (h),
(\mathbf{b}) validates under schema for (t),
recomputed hash equals (h).
CAS must reject any attempt to store bytes under an incorrect hash.
A.4.1(c) Structural hashes.For composite structures (plans, ledgers, receipts), hash identity is computed from canonical bytes that include component hashes. Therefore large objects can be referenced by root hashes without duplicating payloads; identity is preserved by hash composition.
A.4.1(d) Hash assumptions.The framework’s security uses standard assumptions:
collision resistance of (H),
second-preimage resistance,
and Merkle construction security under (H).These assumptions are explicit and bounded: validity and soundness are conditional on them.
A.4.2 Pinset hash definition
Pinsets commit to sets of pin records (commitment store entries) and are used to define output roots for receipts and indices.
A.4.2(a) Pin record encoding.A pin record (\mathsf{PinRecord}) is canonically encoded bytes (\mathbf{p}) including:
pin id bytes,
pin type enum bytes,
value hash bytes,
provenance hash bytes,
metadata hash bytes (or empty marker).
Pin record equality is byte equality of (\mathbf{p}).
A.4.2(b) Pinset root.Given a finite set of pin records (\mathcal{P}), define:
canonical leaf list (L=\mathrm{Sort}([\mathbf{p}_1,\ldots,\mathbf{p}_n])) by lexicographic order on (\mathbf{p}_i),
compute the Merkle root (r_{\mathcal{P}}:=\mathsf{MerkleRoot}(L)) under a pinned Merkle policy.
The resulting root commits to the set (order-insensitive) because sorting is deterministic.
A.4.2(c) Pinset identity and canonicality.Two pinsets are identical iff their sorted leaf lists are identical bytewise, hence their roots are identical. Any insertion order differences do not affect identity.
A.4.2(d) Pinset membership proofs.A membership proof for a pin record (\mathbf{p}) in pinset root (r_{\mathcal{P}}) consists of:
the leaf (\mathbf{p}),
its position index in the sorted leaf list,
and the Merkle authentication path (sibling hashes).Verification reconstructs the root deterministically.
A.4.3 Witness root and output root
Witness and output roots bind the verification boundary of an execution.
A.4.3(a) Witness root.Let (\mathcal{W}) be the set of witness hashes used by an execution segment. Define leaves as canonical fixed-length encodings of witness hashes. Compute:[r_{\mathsf{wits}}:=\mathsf{MerkleRoot}(\mathrm{Sort}(\mathcal{W})).]Sorting is lexicographic on hash bytes. The witness root commits to the witness set independent of discovery order.
A.4.3(b) Output root.The output root is the pinset root for the execution’s declared outputs:[r_{\mathsf{out}} := r_{\mathcal{P}{\mathrm{targets}}},]where (\mathcal{P}{\mathrm{targets}}) is the set of pin records corresponding to plan targets at the terminal checkpoint. If the system also commits evidence roots, define (r_{\mathsf{ev}}) analogously as a Merkle root over evidence hashes.
A.4.3(c) Binding to execution context.Receipts bind:
plan hash,
ledger header hash,
final state hash,
(r_{\mathsf{out}}),
(r_{\mathsf{wits}}),
and policy hashes.
This binding is the compact certificate of verifiable execution: it prevents swapping witnesses or outputs without changing the receipt hash.
A.4.3(d) Root recomputation and verification.Verification recomputes roots deterministically from:
terminal commitment store (for output root),
step records’ witness lists (for witness root),
retained evidence lists (for evidence root when required),and checks equality with receipt-committed roots. Any mismatch is an invalid receipt.
A.4.4 Membership proofs and verification
A.4.4(a) Merkle policy object.The Merkle construction is pinned by a policy object (\mathsf{MerklePol}) including:
hash function id hash,
leaf hashing rule,
internal node hashing rule,
handling of odd-length levels (duplication vs explicit empty node),
canonical encoding of node concatenation.
All Merkle roots and proofs are defined relative to (\mathsf{MerklePol}).
A.4.4(b) Merkle proof object schema.A membership proof is a typed object:[\mathsf{MP}:=(h(\mathsf{MerklePol}),\mathsf{root},\mathsf{leaf},\mathsf{index},[\mathsf{sib}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{sib}_d]),]where (\mathsf{sib}_k) are sibling hashes along the path and (\mathsf{index}) is the leaf position in the sorted leaf list. Proofs are canonical: sibling lists are sequence-like and must be in path order.
A.4.4(c) Verification algorithm.Given (\mathsf{MP}), verification computes the root deterministically:
Compute leaf hash under (\mathsf{MerklePol}) from (\mathsf{leaf}).
For (k=1) to (d), combine current hash with (\mathsf{sib}_k) as left/right child determined by the corresponding bit of (\mathsf{index}) (under pinned left/right convention), producing the parent hash.
Accept iff the final computed hash equals (\mathsf{root}) and (\mathsf{root}) matches the committed root in the receipt or index object.
The algorithm is deterministic and runs in time (O(d)), where (d) is the proof depth.
A.4.4(d) Membership proof admissibility constraints.Membership proofs are admissible only if:
(\mathsf{MerklePol}) hash matches the policy hash committed by the receipt or index,
(\mathsf{leaf}) is canonically encoded (for pin records, exact canonical pin record bytes; for witness hashes, exact fixed-length hash bytes),
and (\mathsf{index}) is consistent with the sorted order of leaves (if required by policy; when sorted order is not available, proofs may be accepted only in contexts where the sorted leaf list is itself committed and retrievable).
Failure to satisfy admissibility yields deterministic failure evidence.
APPENDIX A — ADDRESSING, CANONICAL NAMING, AND HASHING
APPENDIX B — WITNESS SCHEMAS AND UNIVERSAL VERIFIER RULES
This appendix specifies the witness universe and the universal verification rules that determine legality of all state transitions. Witnesses are typed, hash-addressed objects whose validity reduces to deterministic verification under a pinned verifier set. Every admissible witness binds to a step-local context consisting of parameter hashes, state hashes, delta commitments, source commitments, and policy hashes. This binding prevents witness reuse outside its intended execution context and enforces auditability and replay determinism.
B.1 Witness universe
B.1.1 Type set and tags
B.1.1(a) Witness type universe.Let (\mathsf{WType}) be a pinned finite enum of witness types. The minimal required set is:[\mathsf{WType}\in{\mathsf{PROV},\mathsf{COMMIT},\mathsf{REL},\mathsf{ROUTE},\mathsf{PROOF},\mathsf{CONSIST},\mathsf{BUDGET},\mathsf{SCOPE},\mathsf{DETECT}}.]Additional witness types are permitted only via explicit verifier registry extension; any extension changes (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}) and therefore changes the runtime header compatibility class.
B.1.1(b) Witness tag encoding and domain separation.Each witness object is a COS object with a type tag (t_{\mathsf{W}}) and a witness type field (\mathsf{wtype}\in\mathsf{WType}). Its identity hash is:[h(W)=H(\mathsf{DS}(t_{\mathsf{W}})\ |\ \mathsf{Enc}(W)),]with domain separation (\mathsf{DS}(t_{\mathsf{W}})) pinned. No two witness types share a tag encoding. A witness whose tag does not match the expected witness schema is ill-formed and must be rejected.
B.1.1(c) Canonical witness envelope.Every witness is an instance of a canonical envelope schema:[\mathsf{Witness}:=(\mathsf{wtype},\ \mathsf{bind},\ \mathsf{payload},\ \mathsf{meta}?),]where:
(\mathsf{bind}) is a binding record (Section B.1.2),
(\mathsf{payload}) is a type-dependent payload object whose schema is determined by (\mathsf{wtype}),
(\mathsf{meta}) is optional and may include method ids, cost class tags, or disclosure flags when required by policy; any such metadata must be schema-validated and canonical.
B.1.1(d) Witness schema registry.Witness schemas are registered in the verifier registry as schema hashes:[\mathsf{WitSchema}(\tau)\mapsto h(\mathsf{Schema}_\tau),]for each (\tau\in\mathsf{WType}). A verifier must reject any witness whose schema hash does not match the registry entry for its (\mathsf{wtype}). This prevents “schema spoofing” and ensures cross-implementation interoperability.
B.1.2 Required bindings
B.1.2(a) Step context binding record.Define the canonical step context record:[\mathsf{Ctx}:=(\mathsf{op_id},,h_P,,h_{\mathsf{pre}},,r_\Delta,,r_{\mathsf{src}},,h_{\mathsf{post}},,h_\Omega,,h_{\mathsf{hdr}}),]where:
(\mathsf{op_id}) is the canonical opcode identifier for the step,
(h_P) is the canonical parameter hash,
(h_{\mathsf{pre}}) is the pre-state hash,
(r_\Delta) is the delta root (Merkle root over per-store delta hashes),
(r_{\mathsf{src}}) is the source root (Merkle root over consumed input hashes, including required policy objects when mandated),
(h_{\mathsf{post}}) is the post-state hash,
(h_\Omega) is the corridor policy hash,
(h_{\mathsf{hdr}}) is the runtime header hash committing canonicalization, object standard, verifier set, and cost model hashes.
The binding record (\mathsf{bind}) in every witness is either (\mathsf{Ctx}) itself or a record that references a provenance witness hash (h(W_{\mathsf{PROV}})) and includes no conflicting fields.
B.1.2(b) Binding correctness predicate.A witness (W) is bound to a step context (\mathsf{Ctx}) iff:
(\mathsf{bind}=\mathsf{Ctx}) bytewise, or
(\mathsf{bind}) contains a field (\mathsf{prov_ref}=h(W_{\mathsf{PROV}})) and the referenced provenance witness verifies to the same (\mathsf{Ctx}).
This predicate is mandatory and must be checked before any payload-level verification. Failure yields witness invalidity.
B.1.2(c) Source root completeness requirement.For any witness verifier that requires access to an object hash (h(x)), the verifier must ensure that (h(x)) is included in (r_{\mathsf{src}}) or is introduced in the same step’s accepted proof delta under a scope rule permitted by (\Omega). Missing inputs are a hard verifier failure. This prevents “hidden inputs” to verification.
B.1.2(d) Policy binding requirement.All witnesses bind to (h_\Omega) and (h_{\mathsf{hdr}}). Therefore witnesses are not portable across policy changes or verifier set changes unless a policy-bridge mechanism exists and yields a separate receipt that the downstream process consumes explicitly. Absent such policy-bridging, any attempt to reuse witnesses across different policy hashes is invalid by binding mismatch.
B.1.3 Ordering rules
B.1.3(a) Canonical witness list order.A step’s witness list is encoded as a set-like collection. Canonical ordering is:[W_i \prec W_j \iff (\mathsf{wtype}_i < \mathsf{wtype}_j)\ \lor\ (\mathsf{wtype}_i=\mathsf{wtype}_j\ \land\ h(W_i) < h(W_j)),]where the witness type ordering is the enum order pinned by the verifier registry and hash comparison is lexicographic on bytes. The witness list hash (h(W)) is computed from the canonical encoding of this ordered list.
B.1.3(b) Canonical ordering inside witness payloads.Witness payloads may contain lists, maps, and sets. Canonicalization must follow COS rules:
set-like collections are sorted lexicographically by canonical element bytes,
maps are sorted by canonical key bytes,
sequence-like collections preserve order only when the schema declares order-semantic significance; otherwise order is not permitted and must be represented as a set-like encoding.
Witnesses with non-canonical internal ordering are ill-formed.
B.1.3(c) Ordering and replay identity.Because witness list hashes are committed in step records and receipts, ordering is part of replay identity. Two executions that differ only in witness ordering are not replay-equivalent. Therefore ordering rules are non-negotiable, and verifiers must reject witnesses that attempt to exploit ordering ambiguity.
B.1.3(d) Deterministic omission and ordering stability.If (\Omega) permits omission of certain optional evidence witnesses, omission must be deterministic and must preserve canonical ordering among present witnesses. The omission decision must be recorded by evidence objects when required by policy so that replay can deterministically reproduce the same witness set.
B.1.4 Anti-reuse constraints
B.1.4(a) Non-transplantability principle.Witnesses are step-local authorizations. Because binding includes ((h_{\mathsf{pre}},r_\Delta,h_{\mathsf{post}})), a witness that authorizes one state transition cannot authorize a different transition. Formally, if (\mathsf{Ctx}\neq \mathsf{Ctx}'), then a witness bound to (\mathsf{Ctx}) is not bound to (\mathsf{Ctx}'). This is enforced by binding verification.
B.1.4(b) Anti-reuse rule for authorization witnesses.Witness types (\mathsf{COMMIT},\mathsf{REL},\mathsf{SCOPE},\mathsf{CONSIST},\mathsf{BUDGET}) are authorization witnesses. An authorization witness is admissible only if its authorized operation set is a superset of the step’s accepted operation set under the acceptance mask and is consistent with the committed delta root. Therefore even if binding matches, reusing an authorization witness in a step with different deltas fails operation-set consistency checks.
B.1.4(c) Proof object reuse vs witness reuse.A proof object (e.g., equivalence proof bytes stored in the proof store) may be referenced by multiple steps as a dependency, but it does not itself authorize writes. Authorization requires a step-local proof witness that binds the proof object hash into the step context. This separation prevents laundering a globally valid proof object into an unauthorized write without producing the correct step-local bindings and scope authorizations.
B.1.4(d) Rebinding prohibition.Rebinding a witness without producing a new witness object is prohibited because binding is inside the witness bytes and therefore inside the witness hash. Any rebinding produces a different witness hash and must be issued as a new witness object via a legal step. Attempts to “reinterpret” an existing witness under a new context are invalid by definitional identity.
B.2 Provenance verifier
The provenance verifier enforces step-local binding for all witness families and anchors the witness set to a single context.
B.2.1 Required fields and checks
B.2.1(a) Provenance witness schema.A provenance witness is a witness with (\mathsf{wtype}=\mathsf{PROV}) and payload:[\mathsf{ProvPayload}:=(\mathsf{Ctx},\ h(\mathsf{StepDesc}),\ \mathsf{step_index},\ h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),\ \mathsf{mode}?),]where:
(\mathsf{Ctx}) is the binding record,
(h(\mathsf{StepDesc})) commits the step descriptor fields used to compute (\mathsf{op_id}), (h_P), and any expansion depth parameters,
(\mathsf{step_index}) binds the ledger position,
(h(\mathsf{LedHdr})) binds the ledger header identity,
(\mathsf{mode}) optionally binds disclosure mode requirements for evidence and proof objects if required.
B.2.1(b) Provenance verification algorithm.Given a candidate provenance witness (W_{\mathsf{PROV}}) and a step record, verification checks:
schema validity of (W_{\mathsf{PROV}}),
canonical encoding validity of all fields,
equality of (\mathsf{Ctx}) reconstructed from the step record with (\mathsf{Ctx}) in the witness payload,
equality of (h(\mathsf{LedHdr})) with the ledger header hash for the ledger being verified,
consistency of (\mathsf{step_index}) with the step record index.
All checks are deterministic. Any failure yields (\mathsf{false}) and a deterministic evidence object of type (\mathsf{E_PROV}).
B.2.1(c) Single-context constraint.The provenance verifier defines the canonical step context for the step. All other witnesses must bind to the same context. A step containing two different valid provenance witnesses with different contexts is invalid. If multiple provenance witnesses appear, they must be byte-identical (hence hash-identical) or the step fails.
B.2.1(d) Provenance primacy.Provenance witness verification must be performed before verifying any other witness types. Other verifiers may accept bindings that reference the provenance witness hash only if the provenance witness verifies first, ensuring that bindings are not circular or ambiguous.
B.2.2 Source root commitments
B.2.2(a) Source root definition.The source root (r_{\mathsf{src}}) is a Merkle root over a set of consumed input hashes. The consumed set includes, at minimum:
hashes of payload objects read,
hashes of referenced pin records read (or their value hashes depending on policy),
hashes of proof objects used for verification,
hashes of policy objects when required (e.g., (h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}})),
hashes of seeds used for any permitted deterministic pseudo-random generation,
hashes of upstream receipts and dependency proofs when reuse occurs.
The exact inclusion rule is pinned by (\Omega) and by verifier method disclosure policies.
B.2.2(b) Source inclusion verification.The provenance verifier must ensure that any object hash required by any witness verifier is included in (r_{\mathsf{src}}) under the pinned inclusion rule, unless the object is introduced in the same step’s accepted proof delta. If not included, the step is invalid due to missing dependency commitments.
B.2.2(c) Minimality and completeness.The framework permits source roots to be supersets; minimality is not required. Completeness is required. Therefore the provenance verifier checks completeness (required inclusion) but does not reject additional source inputs unless (\Omega) forbids them (e.g., privacy or scope constraints on reading certain namespaces).
B.2.2(d) Source root evidence.If source root completeness fails, the verifier emits evidence binding:
missing hash(es),
required-by verifier method id or witness type,
and the step context.This evidence is deterministic and supports repair planning (e.g., correcting provenance source construction rules or adding missing references).
B.2.3 Delta root commitments
B.2.3(a) Delta root definition.The delta root (r_\Delta) is the Merkle root over the ordered list of per-store delta hashes:[r_\Delta:=\mathsf{MerkleRoot}([h(\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{rel}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{cand}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}})]),]under a pinned Merkle policy and a pinned leaf ordering.
B.2.3(b) Delta root verification.The provenance verifier checks that:
each per-store delta hash in the step record matches the hash of the canonical per-store delta encoding,
the delta root recomputed from per-store delta hashes equals the delta root in the step record and in the provenance witness binding.
Any mismatch is a hard determinism failure and yields evidence of type (\mathsf{E_DET}) or (\mathsf{E_REPLAY}) depending on phase.
B.2.3(c) Delta admissibility coupling.Delta root verification is necessary but not sufficient. Authorization witnesses must additionally authorize the operations committed by the deltas. However, without delta root binding, authorization witnesses could authorize different operations. Therefore delta root inclusion in bindings is mandatory and is enforced at provenance level.
B.2.3(d) Canonical delta encoding requirement.If a delta encoding is not canonical, then its hash is ill-defined across implementations. Therefore the provenance verifier rejects any delta whose canonicalization predicate fails. This ensures that delta roots are stable and replayable.
B.2.4 Post-state binding
B.2.4(a) Post-state hash definition.The post-state hash (h_{\mathsf{post}}) is computed from post-store hashes and header commitments (Chapter 4 and Chapter 13). It is committed in the step record and appears in the step context binding.
B.2.4(b) Post-state binding verification.The provenance verifier checks that the witness binding includes a post-state hash equal to the step record post hash. For (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}) steps, (h_{\mathsf{post}}=h_{\mathsf{pre}}) must hold. A witness that binds to a placeholder post hash is invalid unless the framework explicitly supports a pinned placeholder-finalization mechanism; if such mechanism exists, the finalization rule must itself be committed and replay-verifiable, and the final witness hash must be the one included in the step record.
B.2.4(c) Post-state binding necessity.Without post-state binding, a witness could authorize a delta without committing to its result, enabling post hoc substitution of outcomes. Post-state binding prevents this by making every witness a commitment to the unique outcome of the authorized delta under the pinned projector semantics.
B.2.4(d) Post-state binding and contradiction control.Contradiction resolution witnesses must bind to the post-state hash because contradiction policy is a global postcondition. Verifying a contradiction resolution witness without post-state binding is insufficient; the contradiction predicate must hold in the post-state committed by the witness.
B.3 Commitment/relation verifiers
This section specifies the universal verification rules for witnesses authorizing commitment store and relation store mutations, including consistency/merge operations and budget justification artifacts.
B.3.1 Commitment verifier rules
B.3.1(a) Commitment witness schema.A commitment witness has (\mathsf{wtype}=\mathsf{COMMIT}) and payload:[\mathsf{CommitPayload}:=(h(\mathsf{AuthSet}),h(\mathsf{NSBind}),h(\mathsf{Just})?),]where:
(\mathsf{AuthSet}) is a canonically ordered list of authorized commitment operations (PinAdd/PinUpdate) with fields excluding provenance hash (provenance is supplied by the binding itself),
(\mathsf{NSBind}) binds applicable namespace policies and contradiction policy ids for the targeted keys,
(\mathsf{Just}) optionally references a consistency witness hash when required.
Each authorized operation record includes:[\mathsf{AuthOp}:=(\mathsf{op_kind},\mathsf{pin_id},\mathsf{pin_type}?,h_{\mathsf{value}},h_{\mathsf{meta}}?),]with canonical ordering by ((\mathsf{pin_id},\mathsf{op_kind})).
B.3.1(b) Commitment verification algorithm.Given (W_{\mathsf{COMMIT}}) and the step record, verify deterministically:
provenance binding holds (via B.2),
schema validity of the witness payload and authorized operation set,
for every accepted commitment operation in (\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}}^{(m)}), there exists a matching (\mathsf{AuthOp}) in (\mathsf{AuthSet}) authorizing it exactly (same pin id and value hash and metadata hash),
every (\mathsf{AuthOp}) targets a key whose namespace policy permits the operation kind,
contradiction predicate and resolution requirements are satisfied for any affected contradiction-controlled namespaces:
if the operation creates a collision, (\mathsf{Just}) must be present and must reference a valid consistency witness that binds the same step context and enumerates the conflict set,
the referenced value hashes resolve to well-formed COS objects and satisfy any namespace-required schema hashes.
Any failure yields (\mathsf{false}) and deterministic evidence of type (\mathsf{E_SCOPE}), (\mathsf{E_CONTR}), or (\mathsf{E_VERIFY}).
B.3.1(c) Primary vs secondary commitment writes.If (\Omega) distinguishes primary vs secondary writes, then:
commitment writes in primary namespaces may be authorized by (\mathsf{COMMIT}) witness alone (plus consistency witness when required),
commitment writes in secondary namespaces additionally require a scope-extension witness (\mathsf{SCOPE}) authorizing that namespace for the step.The commitment verifier must enforce this by consulting (\mathsf{NSBind}) and the corridor scope policy.
B.3.1(d) Supersession enforcement.If a commitment update is intended as supersession under refinement, then the commitment verifier must require:
a refinement proof object binding old and new receipts or artifacts,
and a supersession record schema that links them.Absent refinement proofs, overwrite is permitted only when the namespace policy allows overwrite and a consistency witness justifies it. Therefore “replacement without proof” is forbidden in refinement-controlled namespaces.
B.3.2 Relation verifier rules
