B.3.2(a) Relation witness schema.A relation witness has (\mathsf{wtype}=\mathsf{REL}) and payload:[\mathsf{RelPayload}:=(h(\mathsf{EdgeAuthSet}),h(\mathsf{RelPolBind}),h(\mathsf{Just})?),]where:
(\mathsf{EdgeAuthSet}) is a canonically ordered list of authorized relation operations (EdgeAdd/EdgeUpdate),
(\mathsf{RelPolBind}) binds edge type policies, merge-capability flags, and weight bounds by hash,
(\mathsf{Just}) optionally references merge/consistency witnesses when required by edge type policy.
Each authorized edge op includes:[\mathsf{EdgeAuthOp}:=(\mathsf{op_kind},\mathsf{edge_id}?,\mathsf{src}?,\mathsf{dst}?,\mathsf{etype},w_q),]with canonical ordering by ((\mathsf{etype},\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst},\mathsf{edge_id},\mathsf{op_kind})).
B.3.2(b) Relation verification algorithm.Verify deterministically:
provenance binding holds,
schema validity and canonical ordering of (\mathsf{EdgeAuthSet}),
for each accepted relation operation in (\Delta_{\mathsf{rel}}^{(m)}), a matching authorized op exists,
weight bounds hold for each operation under the pinned quantization policy and the edge type policy in (\mathsf{RelPolBind}),
merge-capability enforcement: if an operation modifies a merge-capable edge type in a way that changes cluster identity or equivalence class structure, (\mathsf{Just}) must reference a merge authorization witness that verifies (Section B.3.3),
relation store invariants required by (\Omega) hold post-commit (cycle constraints, uniqueness constraints, if pinned).
B.3.2(c) Relation scope constraints.If (\Omega) restricts relation writes to certain edge types or namespaces (e.g., forbidding identity edges outside governance), the verifier must enforce these restrictions by consulting (\mathsf{RelPolBind}) and the corridor scope policy. Unauthorized relation updates are hard failures.
B.3.2(d) Relation canonicalization.Relation operations must reference canonical node identifiers and canonical edge identifiers. Any mismatch in canonical encoding is a schema failure. Relation verifiers reject non-canonical ids because they break replay determinism and graph hashing.
B.3.3 Merge/consistency verifier rules
B.3.3(a) Consistency witness schema.A consistency witness has (\mathsf{wtype}=\mathsf{CONSIST}) and payload:[\mathsf{ConsistPayload}:=(\mathsf{res_kind},h(\mathsf{ConflictSet}),h(\mathsf{Outputs}),h(\mathsf{Proof}),h(\mathsf{PolBind})),]where:
(\mathsf{res_kind}\in{\mathsf{DISAMBIGUATE},\mathsf{MERGE},\mathsf{OVERWRITE}}),
(\mathsf{ConflictSet}) enumerates conflicting commitment keys/records or merge cluster ids,
(\mathsf{Outputs}) enumerates intended post-resolution artifacts (new pin records, alias records, cluster ids),
(\mathsf{Proof}) references a proof object whose method id is permitted for this resolution kind and namespace,
(\mathsf{PolBind}) binds contradiction predicate ids and resolution policy ids.
B.3.3(b) Consistency verification algorithm.Verify deterministically:
provenance binding holds,
(\mathsf{res_kind}) is allowed by corridor contradiction policy for the affected namespaces,
the conflict set matches the contradiction evidence derivable from pre-state and proposed deltas under the contradiction predicate,
the proof object verifies under its method id and local-check data, and its claim object matches the resolution kind requirements (e.g., merge requires equivalence claim; overwrite requires refinement/correction claim; disambiguate requires partition correctness claim),
the outputs are canonical and consistent with post-state: after applying accepted deltas, the contradiction predicate holds and the output records exist with hashes matching those in (\mathsf{Outputs}).
Any failure yields (\mathsf{false}) and deterministic evidence of type (\mathsf{E_CONTR}) or (\mathsf{E_VERIFY}).
B.3.3(c) Merge authorization and cluster integrity.For merge operations affecting relation clusters or identity classes, the consistency witness must additionally bind:
the set of items to merge,
the resulting representative id,
and a cluster integrity policy id.The verifier must enforce that merges do not implicitly apply transitive closure beyond what is explicitly authorized, unless the policy explicitly declares a closure rule and the proof verifies that closure result.
B.3.3(d) Post-resolution audit requirement.Consistency verification is incomplete without post-resolution audits:
contradiction scan of affected namespaces,
canonical normal-form checks on updated/pinned objects,
and dependency integrity checks if the resolution changes identifiers referenced elsewhere.These audits are deterministic and mandatory under (\Omega).
B.3.4 Budget justification verifier rules
B.3.4(a) Budget witness schema.A budget justification witness has (\mathsf{wtype}=\mathsf{BUDGET}) and payload:[\mathsf{BudgetPayload}:=(h(\mathsf{Req}),\mathsf{reason_code},h(\mathsf{Proof}),h(\mathsf{PolBind})),]where:
(\mathsf{Req}) specifies requested overrides to compute/write/branch/tolerance components in quantized units,
(\mathsf{reason_code}) is a pinned enum permitted by (\Omega),
(\mathsf{Proof}) references a proof object whose method id is permitted for escalations,
(\mathsf{PolBind}) binds the corridor maxima and escalation rules by hash.
B.3.4(b) Budget verification algorithm.Verify deterministically:
provenance binding holds,
the requested overrides do not exceed corridor maxima in (\mathsf{PolBind}),
the reason code is permitted for the step class and target namespaces,
the proof object verifies and its claim class corresponds to an admissible escalation justification class,
the requested budgets are sufficient to cover the observed or committed cost vector (\mathbf{c}) of the step as recorded in the step record.
Budget witnesses may not authorize scope expansion; scope is verified independently.
B.3.4(c) Windowed escalations.If (\Omega) permits escalation for a window of steps, the budget witness must include explicit window bounds (step index range) and bind them in the payload. The verifier must ensure the witness is applied only within the declared window and that the ledger records the substitution deterministically.
B.3.4(d) Escalation audit record.Every accepted budget witness must produce evidence:
original budgets,
requested budgets,
reason code,
proof hash,
affected step indices,all bound to (\mathsf{Ctx}). This evidence is required to prevent silent budget inflation and to support post hoc audit.
B.4 Proof verifier registry
The proof verifier registry defines method ids, local-check requirements, verification levels, and deterministic failure semantics. It is the authoritative source for what “proof” means within the framework.
B.4.1 Method id governance
B.4.1(a) Method id object.A proof method is identified by a method id (\mathsf{method_id}) and represented by a COS object:[\mathsf{Method}:=(\mathsf{method_id},\mathsf{ver},h(\mathsf{Digest}),h(\mathsf{InSchema}),h(\mathsf{OutSchema}),h(\mathsf{LCDSchema}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(\mathsf{VLPolicy}),h(\mathsf{ClaimClass}),h(\mathsf{DisclosurePol}),h(\mathsf{CostClass})).](\mathsf{Digest}) is the implementation digest hash; changing it produces a new method object and therefore a new verifier set hash.
B.4.1(b) Registry object and verifier set hash.The registry is a COS object:[\mathsf{Reg}:=(\mathsf{reg_ver},[\mathsf{Method}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{Method}M],h(\mathsf{GlobalRules})),]with methods sorted canonically by (\mathsf{method_id}). The verifier set hash (h{\mathsf{VerSet}}:=h(\mathsf{Reg})) is included in runtime headers, ledger headers, and receipts.
B.4.1(c) Admission and deprecation.Adding, updating, or deprecating a method id requires:
a registry update producing a new (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}),
conformance test plans and receipts verifying deterministic behavior and claim boundary enforcement,
explicit compatibility declarations for which prior receipts remain verifiable under the new verifier set (if policy permits cross-verifier-set compatibility; otherwise, compatibility is not assumed).
B.4.1(d) Policy binding.Method ids bind to:
numeric policy requirements,
convention fingerprint requirements,
and allowed disclosure modes.Therefore method verification is always interpreted relative to the runtime header and corridor policy; it cannot be “ported” informally.
B.4.2 Local-check data requirements
B.4.2(a) Local-check data object (LCD).For each method id, local-check data is a COS object:[\mathsf{LCD}:=(\mathsf{method_id},h(\mathsf{Claim}),h(\mathsf{TB}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(\mathsf{NumPol}),h(\mathsf{SpecBind}),h(\mathsf{Aux})?),]where:
(h(\mathsf{Claim})) binds invariants, tolerances, quotient/loss profiles if applicable, and verification level,
(h(\mathsf{TB})) is the test basis hash,
(h(\mathsf{ChkProg})) is the checker program id hash and digest binding,
(h(\mathsf{NumPol})) binds quantization/float policies required by the method,
(h(\mathsf{SpecBind})) binds required convention fingerprints,
(\mathsf{Aux}) optionally binds additional pinned parameters (coverage grids, structural side-condition proofs).
B.4.2(b) LCD admissibility predicate.LCD is admissible for a method id iff:
(\mathsf{method_id}) matches,
LCD schema hash matches (h(\mathsf{LCDSchema})) in the method registry,
required components are present and resolve,
claim object is admissible under (\mathsf{VLPolicy}) and (\mathsf{ClaimClass}),
numeric and spec bindings are compatible with runtime header and corridor policy.
B.4.2(c) Test basis determinism.Test bases must be either explicitly listed or deterministically generated from pinned rules. If a test basis uses a seed, the seed hash must be in the step’s source root and the generation procedure must be pinned. Otherwise, seed-based generation is prohibited.
B.4.2(d) LCD disclosure policy.LCD must declare which components are required for public verification. If a receipt is in selective-disclosure mode, the disclosed bundle must include all required LCD components; otherwise, the proof is unverifiable publicly and must be marked accordingly by receipt mode metadata.
B.4.3 Verification levels and admissible claims
B.4.3(a) Verification level enum and partial order.Verification levels (\mathsf{VL}) are a pinned enum:[\mathsf{VL}\in{\mathsf{VL0},\mathsf{VL1},\mathsf{VL2},\mathsf{VL3},\mathsf{VL4}},]with total order (\mathsf{VL0}<\cdots<\mathsf{VL4}). The method registry binds, for each method id, which (\mathsf{VL}) are supported and what claim classes are admissible at each level.
B.4.3(b) Claim object schema.A claim is a COS object:[\mathsf{Claim}:=(h(\mathcal I),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}),\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{Spec}),h(\mathsf{Loss})?,h(\mathsf{Assumptions})?),]where:
(h(\mathcal I)) identifies invariants,
(h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})) identifies tolerances,
(\mathsf{VL}) identifies verification level,
(h(\mathsf{Spec})) binds convention fingerprints,
(h(\mathsf{Loss})) is present for quotient/loss semantics,
(h(\mathsf{Assumptions})) optionally binds side assumptions (domain restrictions, bounded norms) required by the claim.
Claims must not exceed what the method id supports at the declared (\mathsf{VL}); over-claims are rejected deterministically.
B.4.3(c) Claim admissibility predicate.For method (\mathsf{method_id}), define (\mathsf{AdmitClaim}(\mathsf{method_id},\mathsf{Claim})) to be true iff:
(\mathsf{VL}) is supported,
invariant identifiers are supported by the checker predicate or its declared extension interface,
tolerances are representable under the pinned numeric policies required by the method,
convention fingerprint requirements are satisfied,
quotient/loss claims are permitted and coherent with invariants (invariants must be quotient-invariant when loss is present).
B.4.3(d) Strength preorder enforcement.A verifier must enforce strength boundaries: if a downstream requirement asks for a claim (C_{\mathrm{req}}), an upstream proof satisfies it only if the upstream claim (C_{\mathrm{up}}) refines it under the pinned strength preorder:
invariant superset,
tolerance tightening,
verification level dominance,
and matching or bridged convention fingerprints.Any mismatch requires proof regeneration or additional certified steps; it cannot be bypassed.
B.4.4 Failure modes and diagnostics
B.4.4(a) Failure code enum.Verifiers emit deterministic failure evidence with codes drawn from a pinned enum, at minimum:[{\mathsf{E_WF},\mathsf{E_BIND},\mathsf{E_SCHEMA},\mathsf{E_MISSING},\mathsf{E_ORDER},\mathsf{E_CLAIM},\mathsf{E_CHECK},\mathsf{E_COST},\mathsf{E_DISCLOSE},\mathsf{E_POLICY}}.]Each code has a required payload schema.
B.4.4(b) Deterministic diagnostic payloads.Diagnostic payloads must be structured and canonical, including:
implicated witness hash,
implicated method id hash (if applicable),
implicated LCD hash and missing component hashes (if missing),
binding mismatch fields (expected vs observed),
schema mismatch hashes,
ordering mismatch evidence (expected sort key vs observed),
claim mismatch (required vs provided claim hashes),
checker predicate outcome (quantized values and drift) when a check fails.
Diagnostics must not include informal text; they are machine-parsable artifacts.
B.4.4(c) Verifier cost accounting integration.Verifier executions contribute to corridor compute cost. If a verifier exceeds corridor compute cap without admissible budget justification, the step fails with evidence code (\mathsf{E_COST}) and is rejected. Therefore “verification too expensive” is an admissible deterministic failure mode with explicit evidence.
B.4.4(d) Failure determinism theorem (verifier).Given fixed:
witness bytes,
resolved object bytes,
method registry hash,
checker program digest hash,
numeric and convention policy hashes,
and step context,the verifier output (accept/reject) and failure evidence hash are deterministic. This property is required for replay verification. Any implementation that produces nondeterministic verifier outcomes is invalid under the framework’s determinism contract.
APPENDIX B — WITNESS SCHEMAS AND UNIVERSAL VERIFIER RULES
APPENDIX C — LENS FAMILIES AND INVARIANT CATALOG
This appendix specifies canonical lens families and a catalog of invariant families sufficient to express, certify, and compose the dominant representation types used across computation and mathematics. It also specifies tolerance models and the canonical measurement/quantization discipline required for deterministic, replay-verifiable invariant evaluation. All constructs are defined as typed, hash-addressed COS objects whose validity is enforceable by deterministic verifiers.
C.1 Lens definitions
A lens is a typed object[L := (D,\mathcal{A},\mathcal{I},\mathsf{Adm},\llbracket\cdot\rrbracket,\mathsf{ObsAlg},\mathsf{NF},\mathsf{Spec}),]where:
(D) is a carrier domain (payload semantic carrier),
(\mathcal{A}) is an address algebra (Appendix A),
(\mathcal{I}) is an invariant schema (Section C.2),
(\mathsf{Adm}:D\to{\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}}) is the admissibility predicate,
(\llbracket\cdot\rrbracket:D\to \mathsf{Sem}[\mathsf{PayloadType}(L)]) is the interpretation map,
(\mathsf{ObsAlg}) is the set of admissible observables for invariant evaluation,
(\mathsf{NF}) is the canonical normal-form operator for payloads in this lens,
(\mathsf{Spec}) is the convention bundle (Sign/Scale/Index/Unit/Domain, Appendix K).
All components are hash-committed in the lens object and must be resolvable under COS.
C.1.1 Discrete grids
C.1.1(a) Discrete grid lens schema.A discrete grid lens is parameterized by a grid specification object (\mathsf{GridSpec}) and a sample array schema (\mathsf{ArrSchema}). Its carrier domain is:[D_{\mathsf{grid}} := {(h(\mathsf{GridSpec}),h(\mathsf{Arr}),h(\mathsf{Spec}))},]where (\mathsf{Arr}) is a canonical array object (Appendix/Chapter 6) whose shape must match (\mathsf{GridSpec.shape}). A discrete grid lens is:[L_{\mathsf{grid}} := (D_{\mathsf{grid}},\mathcal{A}{\mathsf{grid}},\mathcal{I}{\mathsf{grid}},\mathsf{Adm}{\mathsf{grid}},\llbracket\cdot\rrbracket{\mathsf{grid}},\mathsf{ObsAlg}{\mathsf{grid}},\mathsf{NF}{\mathsf{grid}},\mathsf{Spec}_{\mathsf{grid}}).]
C.1.1(b) Address algebra for grids.For (\mathsf{GridSpec}) with dimension (d) and shape (\mathbf{n}=(n_1,\dots,n_d)), define:[\mathsf{IdxSet}{\mathsf{grid}} := \mathsf{IdxSet}{\mathsf{Grid}(\mathbf{n})},]with indices (i=(i_1,\dots,i_d)) satisfying (0\le i_k<n_k). The canonical order is the pinned IndexSpec order; absent a special order, byte-lex order of tuple encodings is used (Appendix A). The address algebra (\mathcal{A}_{\mathsf{grid}}) binds this (\mathsf{IdxSet}) and its canonical order.
C.1.1(c) Admissibility predicate.(\mathsf{Adm}_{\mathsf{grid}}) returns true iff:
(h(\mathsf{GridSpec})) resolves and is canonical,
(h(\mathsf{Arr})) resolves and validates against array schema,
array shape equals (\mathbf{n}) and layout matches IndexSpec layout policy,
numeric policy referenced by the array (quantization (Q) or float policy (F)) matches the lens’s declared numeric admissibility constraints,
the payload is in normal form: (\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{grid}}(p)\equiv p).
C.1.1(d) Interpretation and observables.The interpretation map (\llbracket\cdot\rrbracket_{\mathsf{grid}}) decodes a payload into a semantic signal object (\mathsf{Sig}) consisting of the grid and sample values. The admissible observables include, at minimum:
point evaluation (\phi_i(x)=x[i]) for indices (i),
aggregate sums and means over canonical index enumeration,
canonical norms computed in quantized arithmetic (where permitted),
spectral or transform-based observables only if transform conventions are bound by (\mathsf{Spec}) and implemented by admissible operators.
The observable set is pinned by hash; invariants can only depend on observables in (\mathsf{ObsAlg}_{\mathsf{grid}}).
C.1.2 Continuous carriers (symbolic)
Continuous lenses represent analytic objects whose primary representation is symbolic or functional rather than sampled arrays. Continuous lenses are still executable by representing symbolic expressions and evaluation rules as COS objects.
C.1.2(a) Continuous lens schema.A continuous lens is parameterized by:
a function signature object (\mathsf{SigSpec}) (domain/codomain types and arity),
an expression language schema (\mathsf{ExprLang}) (syntax, typing rules, canonical AST encoding),
an evaluation policy (\mathsf{EvalPol}) (allowed reductions, determinism rules, numeric policies),
and a domain specification (\mathsf{DomainSpec}) defining admissible parameter domains and branch policies.
Its carrier domain is:[D_{\mathsf{cont}} := {\mathsf{Expr}},]where (\mathsf{Expr}) is a typed canonical AST object in (\mathsf{ExprLang}).
C.1.2(b) Address algebra for continuous objects.Continuous objects are addressed by structural addresses (AST paths). Define:[\mathsf{IdxSet}{\mathsf{path}} := \mathsf{IdxSet}{\mathsf{Path}(\mathsf{PathGrammar})},]where an index is a finite sequence of child selectors (e.g., integers selecting children of AST nodes) encoded canonically. The canonical order is lex order on path encodings. Addressing enables deterministic referencing of subexpressions, parameters, and rewrite sites.
C.1.2(c) Admissibility predicate.(\mathsf{Adm}_{\mathsf{cont}}(\mathsf{Expr})) returns true iff:
the AST validates under (\mathsf{ExprLang}) typing rules,
all free variables and parameters are declared and typed,
branch policies for multi-valued operators are explicit and pinned,
canonicalization holds: the expression is in canonical normal form under (\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{cont}}), which includes canonical ordering of commutative nodes, canonical constant folding rules, and canonical alpha-renaming rules when applicable.
C.1.2(d) Interpretation and observables.Interpretation (\llbracket\cdot\rrbracket_{\mathsf{cont}}) maps the expression object to a semantic function object. Observables include:
evaluation at a canonical test basis of points in the domain (points represented as quantized vectors under pinned numeric policy),
symbolic invariants (degree, structural constraints, syntactic normal-form properties),
derivative and transform observables only when their operator conventions are explicit and pinned.Continuous invariants are enforced either structurally (proofs of identities in the expression language) or by deterministic evaluation on declared test bases at declared verification levels.
C.1.3 Graph and combinatorial carriers
Graph lenses represent discrete structures where semantics are encoded in nodes, edges, and labeled attributes with canonical ordering.
C.1.3(a) Graph lens schema.A graph lens is parameterized by:
node identifier schema (\mathsf{NodeSchema}),
edge identifier schema (\mathsf{EdgeSchema}),
a labeled multigraph schema with type tags for edge types,
and an address algebra describing canonical node/edge ordering.
Carrier domain:[D_{\mathsf{graph}} := {\mathsf{GraphObj}},]where (\mathsf{GraphObj}) encodes:[\mathsf{GraphObj}:=(\mathsf{Nodes},\mathsf{Edges},\mathsf{Attr},h(\mathsf{Spec})).]
C.1.3(b) Address algebra for graphs.Define two index sets:
(\mathsf{IdxSet}_{V}) for nodes, typically a set of canonical node ids ordered lexicographically by canonical bytes;
(\mathsf{IdxSet}{E}) for edges, ordered by ((\mathsf{etype},\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst},\mathsf{edge_id})) where each component is canonical bytes and ordering is lexicographic.The address algebra (\mathcal{A}{\mathsf{graph}}) is the disjoint union algebra:[\mathsf{IdxSet}_{\mathsf{graph}} := \mathsf{TaggedUnion}({\mathsf{NODE}}\times \mathsf{IdxSet}_V,\ {\mathsf{EDGE}}\times \mathsf{IdxSet}_E),]with canonical ordering by tag then component order.
C.1.3(c) Admissibility predicate.(\mathsf{Adm}_{\mathsf{graph}}) requires:
node ids are unique and canonical,
edges reference existing nodes (total endpoint validity),
edge types are in a pinned enum and satisfy edge-type policies (bounds, merge-capability) where relevant,
attribute maps are canonical (sorted keys, canonical value encodings),
the graph object is in canonical normal form (\mathsf{NF}_{\mathsf{graph}}), which includes canonical ordering of node and edge sets and canonical attribute normalization.
C.1.3(d) Interpretation and observables.Interpretation (\llbracket\cdot\rrbracket_{\mathsf{graph}}) yields an abstract labeled graph. Observables include:
degree sequences (canonical ordering),
adjacency signatures,
cycle counts for bounded lengths (when permitted by budget),
connectivity properties evaluated by deterministic algorithms with pinned tie-breaks (e.g., BFS with canonical neighbor ordering),
invariants related to merge/cluster structures (equivalence classes) only when those equivalences are explicitly represented by edges and proofs.
C.1.4 Modular/arithmetic carriers
Modular lenses represent arithmetic objects (integers mod (m), residue tables, congruence-class indexed arrays, sieves) where addressing is by residues and admissibility includes modular constraints.
C.1.4(a) Modular lens schema.A modular lens is parameterized by:
modulus (m\in\mathbb{N}_{\ge 1}) (pinned in (\mathsf{Param})),
residue index set (\mathsf{IdxSet}_{\mathsf{Res}(m)}),
carrier payload schema (e.g., residue-labeled vectors, tables, constraint sets),
and a DomainSpec specifying admissible parameter domains (e.g., allowed moduli ranges, coprimality constraints).
Carrier domain is one of:
residue vectors (D_{\mathsf{resvec}}=\mathbb{Z}_m^n) represented as quantized integer arrays with explicit modulus binding,
residue tables (D_{\mathsf{restab}}=\mathbb{Z}_m^{n\times k}),
constraint sets (D_{\mathsf{constr}}) (sets of congruence constraints) encoded as canonical lists of tuples ((a,b,m)) with explicit normalization.
C.1.4(b) Address algebra for residue carriers.The address algebra includes:
residue indices (r\in{0,\ldots,m-1}) encoded canonically (Appendix A),
optionally product indices ((r,i)) for residue tables and vectors with secondary indices (i) (natural range index sets),
canonical ordering by residue then secondary indices or by byte-lex order of tuple encodings as pinned by IndexSpec.
C.1.4(c) Admissibility predicate.(\mathsf{Adm}_{\mathsf{mod}}) requires:
modulus binding is explicit and consistent across payload fields,
residues are in canonical representative range (0..m-1),
any constraint sets are normalized (e.g., gcd-normalization if required by schema; explicit equivalence classes must be declared if normalization changes meaning),
numeric encodings are quantized and canonical,
payload is in normal form with canonical ordering and deduplication rules.
C.1.4(d) Interpretation and observables.Interpretation yields modular arithmetic semantics. Observables include:
residue frequency distributions,
satisfaction indicators for constraint sets,
canonical hashes of normalized constraint representations,
invariants involving gcd, valuation, modular consistency computed deterministically with pinned algorithms.
C.2 Invariant families
An invariant is a typed clause:[\mathsf{InvClause}:=(\mathsf{inv_id},h(\mathsf{Eval}),h(|\cdot|),h(Q_{\mathsf{meas}}),\varepsilon,\mathsf{applicability},\mathsf{hardness}?,h(\mathsf{ObsReq}),h(\mathsf{SpecReq})),]where:
(\mathsf{Eval}) references a deterministic evaluation rule producing a quantized vector,
(|\cdot|) references a pinned norm/metric id,
(Q_{\mathsf{meas}}) pins quantization for measured values,
(\varepsilon) is the tolerance bound in quantized units,
(\mathsf{ObsReq}) binds required observables (subset of lens observable algebra),
(\mathsf{SpecReq}) binds required conventions for interpretation,
applicability and hardness are pinned predicates/enums interpreted under (\Omega).
Invariant evaluation is defined only when (\mathsf{ObsReq}\subseteq \mathsf{ObsAlg}(L)) and (\mathsf{SpecReq}) is satisfied (or bridged explicitly).
C.2.1 Algebraic invariants
C.2.1(a) Structural identity invariants.Algebraic invariants include equalities and identities that can be verified deterministically by evaluating a finite identity predicate on a canonical test basis of inputs or by structural normalization:
Homomorphism invariant: for a map (f),[f(x\circ y)=f(x)\circ' f(y)]checked on a pinned finite basis ({(x_i,y_i)}) or proven structurally.
Group action invariant: (g\cdot(h\cdot x)=(gh)\cdot x) under explicit action encoding.
Ring identity invariants: distributivity, associativity, identity element preservation, encoded as checkers over canonical ASTs or canonical algebraic normal forms.
C.2.1(b) Canonical algebraic normal forms as invariants.For symbolic lenses, canonical normal form equality is an algebraic invariant:[\mathsf{NF}(x)=\mathsf{NF}(y),]where (\mathsf{NF}) is pinned and deterministic. This invariant is exact when normal form is exact; otherwise it is a bounded claim at an explicit verification level.
C.2.1(c) Modular algebra invariants.For modular carriers:
congruence preservation (x\equiv y\ (\mathrm{mod}\ m)),
residue class consistency across transforms,
gcd/valuation preservation invariants (when transforms claim to preserve prime-power structure).
All such invariants must bind modulus and normalization rules explicitly; any modulus change is a convention change requiring bridges.
C.2.1(d) Algebraic invariant checkers.Algebraic invariants are checked by:
deterministic evaluation on a pinned basis (finite),
or deterministic structural checks (pattern equality under canonical normal form),
or trace proofs for search-derived algebraic properties (e.g., exhaustive verification over a bounded finite set).
The admissible checker method and verification level are part of the invariant clause.
C.2.2 Analytic norms and energies
C.2.2(a) Norm invariants on discrete grids.For grid lenses, analytic invariants include:
(\ell_p) norms of sample arrays under pinned evaluation order,
energy functionals (E(x)=\sum_i w_i \cdot \phi(x[i])) with pinned weights and nonlinearities,
inner products and orthogonality checks (when bound to conventions and quantization).
All evaluations are performed in quantized arithmetic under pinned (Q_{\mathsf{meas}}), with explicit overflow handling.
C.2.2(b) Operator norm and stability invariants.For operator objects, analytic invariants include:
bounds on operator norms (spectral, induced norms) certified by bounded checkers,
Lipschitz bounds or contractivity bounds for transforms:[|T(x)-T(y)|\le K|x-y|]verified on a pinned test basis or by explicit bound certificates (trace proofs or mechanized proofs where available).
C.2.2(c) Continuous analytic invariants.For continuous lenses, analytic invariants include:
boundedness over a domain subset (verified on a deterministic coverage net at a declared verification level),
derivative constraints (e.g., monotonicity or convexity) verified by symbolic checkers or by bounded sampling regimes with explicit bounds,
integral constraints when integrals are defined by pinned quadrature schemes and quantized outputs.
C.2.2(d) Energy-preservation under convention.Energy invariants are convention-sensitive: scaling and unit conventions affect norm values. Therefore every analytic invariant clause must bind required ScaleSpec/UnitSpec hashes. Any transform that changes ScaleSpec or UnitSpec must either:
preserve energy by compensating scaling (certified), or
declare that the invariant is not preserved (loss profile).
C.2.3 Probabilistic mass/entropy constraints
C.2.3(a) Mass and normalization invariants.For discrete distributions (\mathsf{Dist}), mass invariants include:
nonnegativity (p_i\ge 0),
normalization (\sum_i p_i = 1) in quantized units,
bounded deviation from normalization (|\sum_i p_i - 1|\le \varepsilon) when approximation is allowed.
These invariants require a pinned probability quantization policy and pinned handling of rounding.
C.2.3(b) Entropy and divergence invariants.Entropy (H(p)=-\sum_i p_i\log p_i) and divergences (KL, JS, TV) may be invariants only if:
log conventions and branch policies are explicit (DomainSpec),
evaluation is performed deterministically with pinned approximations or rational bounds,
tolerances are explicit and accounted.
Because such functionals can be expensive and sensitive, admissibility requires explicit compute budgets and verification levels.
C.2.3(c) Stationarity and consistency invariants.For probabilistic transforms (Markov kernels, updates), invariants include:
stationarity residual norms,
detailed balance checks (if applicable),
conservation constraints over partitions of the support.
These invariants bind to canonical support ordering and require deterministic evaluation order.
C.2.3(d) Probabilistic invariants under privacy modes.When payloads are private, probabilistic invariants may be verified via selective reveal of local-check data and committed hashes. Any method that allows verification without revealing the distribution must declare its disclosure policy; otherwise public verification requires revealing the necessary values in quantized form.
C.2.4 Structural invariants (symmetry/commutation)
C.2.4(a) Symmetry invariants.Symmetry invariants assert invariance under a group of transformations (G) encoded as operator objects:[I(g\cdot x)=I(x)\quad \forall g\in G_{\mathrm{test}},]where (G_{\mathrm{test}}) is a pinned finite subset or deterministic generator regime. Symmetry checks require:
explicit group action encoding,
explicit convention bindings for action representation,
explicit test basis over both group elements and inputs.
C.2.4(b) Commutation invariants.Commutation invariants assert:[T_1\circ T_2 \sim T_2\circ T_1]under declared invariants and tolerances, verified by rewrite proofs (program equivalence) or by evaluating both compositions on a pinned test basis. Commutation is not assumed; it is certified by proof objects. These invariants are central for optimization and for route regrouping.
C.2.4(c) Holonomy/loop invariants.Loop invariants (Chapter 19) assert that a closed route is equivalent to identity under invariants. This is a structural invariant of the transform algebra and is certified by loop-equivalence proofs and holonomy defect measurements.
C.2.4(d) Convention fingerprint invariants.A structural invariant may include convention preservation as an explicit invariant:[h(\mathsf{SpecOut})=h(\mathsf{SpecIn})]for transforms that claim to preserve conventions, or an explicit mapping invariant for bridges. Convention drift detection relies on these invariants being explicit and enforced.
C.3 Tolerance models
Tolerance models define how bounded drift is represented, compared, and accumulated across chains. Tolerances are first-class objects; they are never implicit.
C.3.1 Scalar vs vector tolerances
C.3.1(a) Scalar tolerance.A scalar tolerance is a quantized nonnegative integer (q_\varepsilon) under a pinned quantization policy (Q_{\varepsilon}), interpreted in a declared metric space. Scalar tolerances apply to scalar invariant outputs or to norms reducing vectors to scalars.
C.3.1(b) Vector tolerance.A vector tolerance is a fixed-length vector (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}^k) aligned to an invariant output vector of length (k). Vector tolerances are used when componentwise bounds are required:[|I_j(x)-I_j(y)| \le \varepsilon_j.]Componentwise comparison uses canonical integer arithmetic.
C.3.1(c) Mixed models.An invariant clause may specify:
a vector output with a scalar tolerance bound on a norm of the difference,
or a scalar output with a vector tolerance for multi-case constraints (e.g., piecewise domains) only when the interpretation is explicit and pinned.
Any mixed interpretation must be a pinned tolerance model object referenced by the invariant clause.
C.3.1(d) Tolerance object schema.A tolerance profile is a COS object:[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}:=(h(\mathsf{InvIndex}),[q_{\varepsilon,I}]_{I\in\mathsf{InvIndex}}),]where (\mathsf{InvIndex}) is a canonical ordering of invariant ids. This ensures that tolerance vectors are comparable and composable deterministically.
C.3.2 Composition operators
C.3.2(a) Operator family.For each invariant (I), define a pinned accumulation operator:[\oplus_I:\ \mathbb{Z}{\ge 0}\times \mathbb{Z}{\ge 0}\to \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0},]required to be associative and monotone. The operator is represented by a method id and parameter object hash so its behavior is fixed and verifiable.
C.3.2(b) Permitted operators.Permitted operators include:
additive: (\varepsilon\oplus \varepsilon'=\varepsilon+\varepsilon'),
capped additive: (\min(\varepsilon+\varepsilon',\varepsilon^{\Omega}_I)),
(p)-norm accumulation in quantized regime when representable (must be explicit and pinned),
hybrid operators encoded as deterministic programs whose correctness is certified by admission receipts.
C.3.2(c) Composition across a chain.For a chain of steps with per-step tolerance usage (\varepsilon_{I,t}), the cumulative is:[\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}{I,n}=\varepsilon{I,1}\oplus_I \cdots \oplus_I \varepsilon_{I,n}.]Cumulative tolerances are committed in tolerance ledgers and checkpoint objects; therefore they are replay-verifiable.
C.3.2(d) Compatibility constraints.A proof claim or contract that assumes a particular accumulation model must match the pinned (\oplus_I) in the corridor policy. Mismatch is a defect: it implies claims are being interpreted under different accumulation semantics.
C.3.3 Budget coupling to tolerances
C.3.3(a) Corridor tolerance budgets.The corridor policy (\Omega) defines a budget vector (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega}) over invariants. Any route or execution segment is feasible only if cumulative tolerance usage is within budget:[\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_{I}\le \varepsilon^{\Omega}_I \quad \forall I\ \text{required}.]
C.3.3(b) Transform-level vs corridor-level tolerances.Transform contracts declare per-step bounds (\varepsilon^{(t)}_I). The corridor budget is a global cap. A transform can be admissible and yet infeasible in a long route if cumulative usage exceeds corridor budget. This coupling is enforced by compilation feasibility pruning and by post-commit tolerance-ledger detectors.
C.3.3(c) Tightening and escalation.Tolerances may be tightened (stronger claims) only via explicit proof regeneration or rerouting to exact steps. Loosening tolerances (weaker claims) is permitted only if the goal explicitly allows it and the corridor policy permits it via a budget escalation witness. Any loosening must be explicit in plan/receipt hashes.
C.3.3(d) Tolerance-aware routing and optimization.Routing and optimization treat tolerance usage as a primary cost term. Exactness is lexicographically preferred. Approximate steps are permitted only when goal constraints allow them and when corridor budgets support them.
C.3.4 Near-violation semantics
C.3.4(a) Near-threshold parameters.For each invariant (I), define a pinned near threshold fraction (\alpha_I\in(0,1)). Near threshold occurs when:[\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_I > \alpha_I\varepsilon^{\Omega}_I.]
C.3.4(b) Near verdict and mitigation.Near states produce evidence objects and may trigger mandatory mitigation:
forced normalization checkpoints,
reroute to exact transforms,
proof strengthening,
reduction in exploration caps.Mitigation obligations are represented as pinned policy ids and must be satisfied by subsequent plan segments or the execution must halt if obligations are hard.
C.3.4(c) Near semantics for measured drift.Separately from tolerance usage, measured drift (d_I) (Chapter 9) has its own near/ok/fail classification:
(\mathsf{OK}): (d_I\le \alpha_I\varepsilon_I),
(\mathsf{NEAR}): (\alpha_I\varepsilon_I<d_I\le \varepsilon_I),
(\mathsf{FAIL}): (d_I>\varepsilon_I).Both tolerance-ledger near and measured-drift near are supported; they are distinct but can trigger the same mitigation classes.
C.3.4(d) Mandatory evidence.Near conditions must emit canonical evidence objects binding:
invariant id,
current cumulative tolerance and budget,
near threshold,
and required mitigation policy ids.Evidence objects are stored in the proof store and referenced by step records when retention is mandatory.
C.4 Canonical measurement and quantization
Canonical measurement converts invariant evaluation into deterministic quantized values suitable for hashing, comparison, and replay verification.
C.4.1 Quantization step pinning
C.4.1(a) Measurement quantization policy.Each invariant clause references a pinned measurement quantization policy (Q_{\mathsf{meas}}). This policy specifies:
integer scaling factors for measurement outputs,
rounding mode,
overflow behavior,
and canonical encoding of measurement vectors.
C.4.1(b) Measurement function interface.An invariant evaluator computes a real or symbolic intermediate and then applies quantization:[\mathsf{Meas}I(x) := \mathsf{Quant}{Q_{\mathsf{meas}}}\big(\mathsf{Eval}_I(x)\big)\in \mathbb{Z}^{k_I}.]The evaluation (\mathsf{Eval}_I) must be deterministic given the object bytes and pinned policies; if (\mathsf{Eval}_I) depends on numeric approximations, those approximations must be pinned and their tolerance usage accounted.
C.4.1(c) Measurement domain binding.Measurement is well-defined only when the required convention bundle (\mathsf{SpecReq}) matches the object’s spec (or is bridged explicitly). For example, measuring spectral energy requires a known scaling convention; measuring phase requires a known phase policy. Measurements without correct spec binding are illegal.
C.4.1(d) Quantization policy identity.Quantization policy hashes are part of invariant clause hashes and therefore part of contracts and receipts. Any change in quantization policy changes measurement values and invalidates prior proofs unless explicitly bridged.
C.4.2 Deterministic rounding
C.4.2(a) Rounding modes.Rounding modes are a pinned enum (e.g., toward zero, to nearest ties-to-even, toward (-\infty), toward (+\infty)). The rounding mode must be part of (Q_{\mathsf{meas}}) and is applied deterministically.
C.4.2(b) Exactness criteria.If (\mathsf{Eval}_I(x)) is exactly representable in the quantized domain, then quantization introduces no error. Exactness is a property of the evaluator and numeric policy; it must not be inferred from floating computations. Exact invariants require (\varepsilon=0) and must be evaluated in a domain where determinism is provable.
C.4.2(c) Overflow and saturation.Overflow behavior is pinned: either error-on-overflow (preferred for verification) or saturating arithmetic. Saturation changes semantics and must be declared if used. Error-on-overflow yields deterministic failure evidence and is preferable for correctness-critical invariants.
C.4.2(d) Canonical vector rounding.For vector outputs, rounding is applied elementwise in a fixed canonical order. Any dependence on parallelism or non-deterministic reduction order is forbidden. Reductions (sums, norms) must be performed in deterministic integer arithmetic in canonical index order.
C.4.3 Stable comparison under tolerances
C.4.3(a) Quantized drift computation.Given quantized measurement vectors (\mathbf{u}=\mathsf{Meas}_I(x)) and (\mathbf{v}=\mathsf{Meas}_I(y)), compute drift:[\mathbf{d}=\mathbf{u}-\mathbf{v}\in\mathbb{Z}^{k_I}.]The invariant’s norm id (h(|\cdot|)) specifies a deterministic norm computation (|\mathbf{d}|_I) in integer arithmetic (possibly after converting to a scalar via pinned reduction rules).
C.4.3(b) Tolerance comparison.An invariant clause provides tolerance (\varepsilon_I) in the same quantized units as (|\mathbf{d}|_I). The pass predicate is:[|\mathbf{d}|I \le \varepsilon_I.]For componentwise tolerances, the predicate is elementwise:[|d_j|\le \varepsilon{I,j}\quad \forall j.]All comparisons are integer comparisons and therefore deterministic.
C.4.3(c) Near thresholds.Near thresholds for measured drift use pinned (\alpha_I):
(\mathsf{OK}) if (|\mathbf{d}|_I\le \lfloor \alpha_I\varepsilon_I\rfloor),
(\mathsf{NEAR}) if (\lfloor \alpha_I\varepsilon_I\rfloor < |\mathbf{d}|_I \le \varepsilon_I),
(\mathsf{FAIL}) if (|\mathbf{d}|_I > \varepsilon_I),with rounding rules pinned for computing (\lfloor \alpha_I\varepsilon_I\rfloor) in quantized arithmetic.
C.4.3(d) Stability across representations.Stable comparison requires that measurement values are computed under compatible conventions. If two objects are in different specs, a certified bridge must be applied to align specs before measurement comparison, or the invariant must explicitly be defined in a quotient space where the mismatch is irrelevant. Otherwise comparisons are illegal.
C.4.4 Measurement proof objects
C.4.4(a) Measurement evidence object.A measurement evidence object is a COS object:[\mathsf{MeasEv}:=(\mathsf{inv_id},h(\mathsf{ObjLHS}),h(\mathsf{ObjRHS})?,h(Q_{\mathsf{meas}}),\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}?,\mathbf{d}?,|\mathbf{d}|_I?,\varepsilon_I,\mathsf{verdict},\mathsf{bind}),]binding measured objects, quantization policy, measurement vectors, drift values, tolerance, verdict, and step context binding. Fields with “?” are omitted when the evidence is a one-sided measurement (e.g., recording a single invariant value) rather than a comparison.
C.4.4(b) Measurement proof object.When a step’s legality requires proving that an invariant check passed, the step must either:
include (\mathsf{MeasEv}) objects for each required invariant comparison as evidence, or
include a proof object (\mathsf{ProofMeas}) that commits to a Merkle root over the set of (\mathsf{MeasEv}) objects and provides a method id whose verifier can validate the root and the verdicts.
(\mathsf{ProofMeas}) supports compact receipts without embedding all measurement vectors directly in step records.
C.4.4(c) Verification of measurement proofs.A measurement proof verifier checks:
correct binding to step context,
presence and validity of required measurement evidence objects (by membership proofs under the measurement root),
deterministic recomputation of measurements when required by method policy (full recomputation vs evidence-trust mode),
and consistency with contract-declared invariant ids, norms, and tolerances.
C.4.4(d) Measurement disclosure boundaries.Measurement vectors may be sensitive. Disclosure policy determines whether (\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}) are revealed publicly or remain private while their hashes are committed. If public verification requires them, they must be provided in selective disclosure bundles. If a method supports verification without revealing measurements, that capability must be explicit in the method’s disclosure policy and claim class.
APPENDIX C — LENS FAMILIES AND INVARIANT CATALOG
APPENDIX D — LEDGER FORMAT, REPLAY VERIFICATION, AND RECEIPTS
This appendix specifies (i) the canonical ledger format, (ii) the replay verification algorithm, and (iii) the receipt extraction and verification procedures. The ledger is the unique append-only trace of execution under pinned policies; replay verification is the unique deterministic procedure that checks ledger correctness; receipts are compact, portable commitments to verified outcomes, witnesses, and policy hashes.
D.1 Ledger header and identities
D.1.1 Version hash commitments
D.1.1(a) Ledger format version object.Define a ledger format version object:[\mathsf{LedFmt}:=(\mathsf{fmt_id},\mathsf{fmt_ver},h(\mathsf{HdrSchema}),h(\mathsf{StepSchema}),h(\mathsf{MerklePol}),h(\mathsf{HashPol})),]where:
(\mathsf{fmt_id}=h(\mathsf{LedFmt})),
(\mathsf{fmt_ver}) is a pinned version tuple,
(h(\mathsf{HdrSchema})) and (h(\mathsf{StepSchema})) are schema hashes for ledger header and step records,
(h(\mathsf{MerklePol})) pins Merkle construction rules,
(h(\mathsf{HashPol})) pins domain separation rules for ledger hashing.
A ledger instance is admissible only if it commits to a valid (\mathsf{LedFmt}) object.
D.1.1(b) Ledger header object.The ledger header is a COS object:[\mathsf{LedHdr}:=(\mathsf{fmt_id},\ h(\mathsf{Plan})?,\ h_\Omega,\ h_{\mathsf{Canon}},\ h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},\ h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\ h_{\mathsf{CostModel}},\ h(\mathsf{DetPol}),\ h(\mathsf{ContrPol}),\ h_{\mathsf{Genesis}},\ \mathsf{hdr_meta}?).]Fields:
(h(\mathsf{Plan})) is optional and present when the ledger is tied to a specific plan; otherwise the ledger is a general-purpose execution trace and plan linkage is provided by receipts.
(h(\mathsf{DetPol})) commits deterministic ordering rules and pseudo-randomness policy (if allowed).
(h(\mathsf{ContrPol})) commits contradiction predicate ids and resolution policies used by the projector.
(h_{\mathsf{Genesis}}) commits the genesis object (Section D.1.4).
D.1.1(c) Ledger identity.The ledger identity is:[h(\mathsf{LedHdr}) := H(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{LedHdr})),]with (\mathsf{Enc}) the canonical COS encoder. Two ledgers are the same ledger iff their (\mathsf{LedHdr}) bytes are identical.
D.1.1(d) Ledger header immutability.All step records are interpreted relative to the ledger header. No step record is admissible unless its embedded header reference equals (h(\mathsf{LedHdr})) bytewise. Any change in corridor policy, verifier set, canonicalization, object standard, Merkle policy, or hash policy requires a new ledger header and therefore a different ledger.
D.1.2 Policy hash commitments
D.1.2(a) Policy binding completeness.A ledger header must commit to all policy hashes that influence legality or replay outcomes:
corridor policy (h_\Omega),
canonicalization policy (h_{\mathsf{Canon}}),
object standard (h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}}),
verifier set (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}),
cost model (h_{\mathsf{CostModel}}) when cost is part of replay identity,
determinism policy (h(\mathsf{DetPol})),
contradiction policy (h(\mathsf{ContrPol})),
Merkle policy (h(\mathsf{MerklePol})) via (\mathsf{fmt_id}),
hash policy (h(\mathsf{HashPol})) via (\mathsf{fmt_id}).
Any computation-relevant policy not committed by hash is illegal.
D.1.2(b) Policy compatibility predicate.Given two ledger headers (\mathsf{LedHdr}a,\mathsf{LedHdr}b), define compatibility:[\mathsf{Compat}(\mathsf{LedHdr}a,\mathsf{LedHdr}b)=\mathsf{true}\iffh{\mathsf{Canon},a}=h{\mathsf{Canon},b}\ \land\ h{\mathsf{ObjStd},a}=h{\mathsf{ObjStd},b}\ \land\ h_{\mathsf{VerSet},a}=h_{\mathsf{VerSet},b}\ \land\ \mathsf{fmt_id}_a=\mathsf{fmt_id}b\ \land\ h\Omega{}a=h\Omega{}_b,]unless an explicit policy-bridge mechanism is defined and executed under its own ledger and receipt. Absent such mechanism, compatibility is strict equality of the committed hashes.
D.1.2(c) Policy resolution rule.All verifiers must resolve policy hashes to policy objects by hash. If a required policy object cannot be resolved, replay verification fails with a deterministic missing-policy evidence object.
D.1.2(d) Policy integrity and governance.Any update to policy objects requires producing new policy hashes and therefore a new ledger header for any execution under the updated policy. Policy mutation without changing hashes is outside the framework and is not admissible.
D.1.3 Canonicalization commitments
D.1.3(a) Canonical encoding requirement.Ledger header and step records must be COS objects and must be encoded canonically under (h_{\mathsf{Canon}}). Canonicalization includes:
fixed field ordering,
canonical varint encoding,
canonical map/set ordering,
canonical numeric encoding (quantization policies referenced by hash),
pinned byte collation for lexicographic ordering.
Any non-canonical ledger object is invalid.
D.1.3(b) Deterministic ordering commitments.The ledger commits to ordering rules that affect replay:
delta operation ordering,
witness list ordering,
evidence list ordering,
candidate ordering and truncation comparators,
route tie-break and rewrite tie-break policies when such artifacts are emitted as part of execution.
Ordering rules are bound by (h(\mathsf{DetPol})) and must be resolved by verifiers.
D.1.3(c) Canonical record chaining.Each step record includes a pointer hash to the previous record, creating a hash chain. Chain validity requires:[\mathsf{prevHash}_1 = h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),\quad\mathsf{prevHash}t = h(\mathsf{StepRec}{t-1})\ \text{for }t\ge 2.]Any deviation is a ledger integrity failure.
D.1.3(d) Canonical failure reporting.Failures and rejection events are represented as evidence objects stored in the proof store and referenced by step records. Evidence objects are canonical COS objects; omission of mandatory evidence is a determinism violation and invalidates replay.
D.1.4 Genesis and chain linkage
D.1.4(a) Genesis object.The genesis object is a COS object:[\mathsf{Genesis}:=(h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),\ h(\Psi_0),\ h_{\mathsf{commit},0},\ h_{\mathsf{rel},0},\ h_{\mathsf{cand},0},\ h_{\mathsf{proof},0},\ h(\mathsf{Chk}_0),\ h(\mathsf{TolLedger}_0)?),]binding:
the ledger header hash,
the initial full state hash,
per-store initial hashes,
initial checkpoint hash,
and optionally initial tolerance ledger hash when the tolerance ledger is tracked as an explicit object.
The ledger header includes (h_{\mathsf{Genesis}}:=h(\mathsf{Genesis})).
D.1.4(b) Chain linkage invariant.For step (t), the step record must satisfy:[h_{\mathsf{pre},1}=h(\Psi_0),\quadh_{\mathsf{pre},t}=h_{\mathsf{post},t-1}\ \text{for }t\ge 2.]This ensures the step chain is contiguous and defines a unique execution path.
D.1.4(c) Checkpoint linkage.Each successful step produces a post-step checkpoint (\mathsf{Chk}t) whose committed state hash equals (h{\mathsf{post},t}). A checkpoint referenced by later steps must match the ledger chain; mismatched checkpoints are invalid dependencies.
D.1.4(d) Ledger segment boundaries.A ledger may be segmented for long-chain control. Segment boundaries are explicit checkpoints whose hashes are committed by receipts. Segment composition uses dependency proofs; segment boundaries do not alter ledger semantics.
D.2 Step record schema
D.2.1 Pre/post hashes
D.2.1(a) Step record object.A step record (\mathsf{StepRec}t) is a COS object:[\mathsf{StepRec}t :=(h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),\ \mathsf{step_index},\ \mathsf{op_id},\ h_P,\ h\Gamma,\ \lambda,\ h{\mathsf{pre}},\ h_{\mathsf{post}},\ h_{\mathsf{commit}}^{-},h_{\mathsf{rel}}^{-},h_{\mathsf{cand}}^{-},h_{\mathsf{proof}}^{-},\ h_{\mathsf{commit}}^{+},h_{\mathsf{rel}}^{+},h_{\mathsf{cand}}^{+},h_{\mathsf{proof}}^{+},\ \ldots),]followed by delta commitments, witness/evidence commitments, cost vector, verdict and mask, and the previous record hash (Sections D.2.2–D.2.4). All hashes are fixed-length byte strings.
D.2.1(b) Pre/post state coherence.The verifier must check:
(h_{\mathsf{pre}}=h(\Psi_{t-1})),
(h_{\mathsf{post}}=h(\Psi_{t})),and chain linkage holds as in D.1.4(b). For rejection with revert, (h_{\mathsf{post}}=h_{\mathsf{pre}}).
D.2.1(c) Per-store hash coherence.Per-store hashes must be coherent with full state hash composition rules. Specifically, the verifier recomputes (h(\Psi_t)) from per-store post hashes and header bindings; mismatch implies replay divergence. Store hashes are committed both pre and post to enable localization of divergence.
D.2.1(d) Step descriptor binding.(\mathsf{op_id}, h_P, h_\Gamma,\lambda) define the step descriptor. These fields are part of the step context binding used by witnesses. Any mismatch between the descriptor used by (\mathsf{Apply}) and the descriptor in the step record is a determinism failure; the framework treats the step record as authoritative.
D.2.2 Delta commitments
D.2.2(a) Per-store deltas and hashes.Each step record commits to per-store delta hashes:[h(\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}}),\ h(\Delta_{\mathsf{rel}}),\ h(\Delta_{\mathsf{cand}}),\ h(\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}}),]where each delta is canonically encoded as a sequence-like list of primitive operations in canonical within-delta order.
D.2.2(b) Delta root.The step record commits to delta root:[r_\Delta := \mathsf{MerkleRoot}([h(\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{rel}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{cand}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}})]),]under the pinned Merkle policy. This root is included in witness bindings and is used to ensure witnesses authorize the exact delta bundle.
D.2.2(c) Accepted delta semantics.The step record commits to an acceptance mask (m) (Section D.2.4). Replay verification recomputes the accepted deltas (\Delta^{(m)}) by applying (m) to (\Delta) under pinned filtering rules. Any attempt to interpret delta commitments without mask filtering is invalid; the mask is part of the step’s meaning.
D.2.2(d) Delta resolvability and scope.Deltas may reference object hashes in the proof store. Any referenced hash must be resolvable by the verifier:
either pre-existing in the proof store,
or introduced in the same step’s accepted proof delta.If resolvability fails, the step is invalid. Scope violations in deltas are detected by the projector and must be reflected in verdict (reject+revert) or in deterministic omission under permitted partial acceptance rules.
D.2.3 Witness references
D.2.3(a) Witness list commitment.The step record commits to:
the set of witness hashes used for the step (W_t={h(W_1),\ldots,h(W_k)}),
the witness list hash (h(W_t)) computed from canonical ordering,
and optionally a witness Merkle root (r_{\mathsf{wits},t}) for per-step witness membership proofs.
Witness list ordering is canonical: by (wtype, hash) under pinned enum order.
D.2.3(b) Required witness classes.The step record may include an optional field (h(\mathsf{WReq}_t)) referencing the witness requirement record from the plan’s witness plan. If present, replay verification checks that:
all required witness types are present,
required proof method ids are present where applicable,
and all required witnesses verify successfully under the pinned verifier set.
If (\mathsf{WReq}_t) is not present, required witness types are determined by the transform contract and corridor policy referenced by the ledger header.
D.2.3(c) Witness binding reconstruction.Replay verifier reconstructs step context:[\mathsf{Ctx}t := (\mathsf{op_id},h_P,h{\mathsf{pre}},r_\Delta,r_{\mathsf{src}},h_{\mathsf{post}},h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{hdr}}),]and verifies each witness binds to (\mathsf{Ctx}_t). Missing or mismatched bindings are hard failures.
D.2.3(d) Witness-to-delta consistency.Authorization witnesses must be consistent with the accepted delta bundle. Verifiers must check that authorized operation sets (e.g., authorized pin updates) cover the accepted operations. For partial acceptance, the accepted operations must be a subset of authorized operations and must be explicitly determined by the acceptance mask.
D.2.4 Verdicts and masks
D.2.4(a) Verdict enum.The step record commits to a verdict:[\mathsf{verdict}\in{\mathsf{ACCEPT},\mathsf{PARTIAL},\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}},]encoded as a pinned enum. Verdict meaning is defined by the execution kernel and corridor policy.
D.2.4(b) Acceptance mask object.The acceptance mask is a COS object:[m := (m_{\mathsf{commit}},m_{\mathsf{rel}},m_{\mathsf{cand}},m_{\mathsf{proof}},m_{\mathsf{op}}),]where:
(m_{\mathsf{store}}\in{0,1}) indicate store-level acceptance,
(m_{\mathsf{op}}) encodes within-delta filtering decisions (candidate truncation masks, omission of unauthorized secondary writes when permitted, selection among alternative candidate entries when deterministic truncation applies).
Mask encoding is canonical and pinned by policy.
D.2.4(c) Evidence list commitment.The step record commits to an evidence list hash (h(E_t)) computed from canonical ordering of evidence object hashes. If (\Omega) mandates evidence retention for detectors or failure cases, missing evidence objects invalidate the step record by determinism violation.
D.2.4(d) Cost vector commitment.The step record commits to a cost vector (\mathbf{c}_t) in canonical quantized units. If the corridor policy mandates deterministic cost accounting, replay verification recomputes (\mathbf{c}_t) (or checks its derivation) and requires equality; otherwise (\mathbf{c}_t) is treated as an audited report field that must satisfy feasibility constraints but need not be recomputed exactly.
D.3 Replay algorithm
D.3.1 Deterministic delta application
D.3.1(a) Replay input.Replay verification consumes:
(\mathsf{LedHdr}) and (\mathsf{Genesis}),
the step record sequence ({\mathsf{StepRec}_t}),
a deterministic resolver into CAS and commitment store snapshots,
the pinned verifier set and detector set referenced by header hashes.
D.3.1(b) Deterministic replay state.Replay maintains a state (\widehat{\Psi}_t) initialized to (\Psi_0) from (\mathsf{Genesis}). For each step record (t):
check chain linkage (h(\widehat{\Psi}{t-1})=h{\mathsf{pre},t}),
reconstruct (\Delta_t) by resolving delta objects or by interpreting delta commitments as canonical delta bytes,
reconstruct (m_t) by reading the committed mask and verifying its legality (or recomputing mask if the policy demands mask recomputation; the rule is pinned),
compute accepted deltas (\Delta_t^{(m)}),
apply accepted deltas in canonical store order and canonical within-delta order to produce (\widehat{\Psi}_t),
recompute per-store post hashes and full post-state hash (\widehat{h}_{\mathsf{post},t}),
require (\widehat{h}{\mathsf{post},t}=h{\mathsf{post},t}) and per-store hashes match.
D.3.1(c) Canonical application order.Canonical application order is fixed:
apply commitment deltas,
apply relation deltas,
apply candidate deltas (including deterministic truncation),
apply proof deltas.Any other order is illegal. Within each store, operations apply in canonical delta order. When operations conflict, conflict resolution is governed by corridor policy (contradiction policy for commitments; edge policies for relations; truncation policies for candidates; schema and hash correctness for proofs).
D.3.1(d) Post-commit audits in replay.If (\Omega) mandates post-commit audits (contradiction scans, normal-form checks, tolerance ledger updates), replay must execute these audits deterministically and require outcomes consistent with the recorded verdict and evidence commitments. Audit failures imply invalid ledger even if hashes match, because legality requires detector outcomes.
D.3.2 Witness verification ordering
D.3.2(a) Step context reconstruction.For step (t), reconstruct:[\mathsf{Ctx}t := (\mathsf{op_id},h_P,h{\mathsf{pre}},r_\Delta,r_{\mathsf{src}},h_{\mathsf{post}},h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{hdr}}),]where (r_{\mathsf{src}}) is either committed explicitly in the step record or derivable by pinned rules from the step record’s referenced objects and plan requirements. If (r_{\mathsf{src}}) is not reconstructible deterministically, replay fails.
D.3.2(b) Verification order.Witness verification proceeds in a pinned order:
verify provenance witness (\mathsf{PROV}),
verify budget witness (\mathsf{BUDGET}) (if present),
verify scope extension witness (\mathsf{SCOPE}) (if present),
verify proof witnesses (\mathsf{PROOF}) (including method verification and local-check),
verify consistency witnesses (\mathsf{CONSIST}) (if present),
verify commitment witnesses (\mathsf{COMMIT}) and relation witnesses (\mathsf{REL}),
verify routing witnesses (\mathsf{ROUTE}) and detector witnesses (\mathsf{DETECT}) if policy requires them explicitly.
This order ensures binding and prerequisites are verified before dependent authorizations.
D.3.2(c) Witness-to-step record consistency.Verifiers must check:
witness list hash (h(W_t)) matches canonical ordering of the witness set referenced,
each witness binds to (\mathsf{Ctx}_t),
required witness types are present and verify,
proof witnesses reference valid method ids and local-check data and satisfy claim admissibility.
Any mismatch is a replay failure.
D.3.2(d) Witness-to-delta and witness-to-mask consistency.Authorization witnesses must authorize at least the accepted delta operations. If a witness authorizes more than what is accepted, that is permissible only if the accepted operations are a subset and the acceptance mask explains the discards deterministically under policy. If a witness authorizes fewer or different operations than those accepted, the step is invalid.
D.3.3 Divergence localization
D.3.3(a) Divergence detection predicate.A divergence occurs at the smallest (t) for which any of the following fails:
chain linkage (h_{\mathsf{pre},t}=h(\widehat{\Psi}_{t-1})),
delta root or per-store delta hashes mismatch recomputation,
witness list hash mismatch,
required witness fails verification,
post-state hash mismatch,
required detector outcome inconsistency under (\Omega),
evidence retention mismatch when mandated.
D.3.3(b) Localization procedure.Upon divergence at step (t), localize by comparing in canonical order:
header/policy mismatch,
commitment store post hash mismatch,
relation store post hash mismatch,
candidate store post hash mismatch,
proof store post hash mismatch,
mask mismatch,
witness mismatch,
detector/evidence mismatch.The first mismatch encountered determines the divergence class.
D.3.3(c) Divergence evidence object.Replay produces a divergence evidence object:[\mathsf{DivEv}:=(h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),\mathsf{step_index},\mathsf{div_class},h_{\mathsf{expected}},h_{\mathsf{observed}},h(\mathsf{Details}),\mathsf{bind'}),]where (\mathsf{bind'}) is a replay-verifier binding record identifying the ledger and step and policy hashes (distinct from execution step context, but canonical and pinned). The details field includes structured subfields such as expected vs observed per-store hashes and implicated witness/method ids.
D.3.3(d) Earliest fault semantics.Replay verification result is defined by the earliest divergence step. Later steps are not examined for correctness once divergence is found because their meaning depends on the preceding state. This ensures deterministic failure localization.
D.3.4 Replay soundness statement
D.3.4(a) Soundness statement (ledger).Assume collision resistance of (H) and correctness of canonical encoders/decoders and verifiers under the committed header hashes. If replay verification returns (\mathsf{OK}), then:
every step record corresponds to a deterministic application of accepted deltas under (\Omega),
every required witness verifies and binds to its step context,
all mandatory detector constraints and contradiction policies hold at all committed checkpoints,
the terminal state hash (h_{\mathsf{final}}) corresponds to the deterministic execution of the full ledger from genesis.
This establishes ledger correctness relative to pinned policies.
D.3.4(b) Bounded claim boundary.Replay soundness does not claim any semantic property beyond what is stated by the proofs and invariants referenced by the plan and its witnesses. It asserts only that the ledger is a correct trace under the pinned system semantics and that all required verification predicates passed.
D.3.4(c) Failure completeness (deterministic).If replay verification returns (\mathsf{FAIL}), it must provide a deterministic earliest divergence index and a divergence evidence object. Thus failure is not an absence of proof; it is a structured, replay-verifiable diagnostic artifact.
D.3.4(d) Replay equivalence of implementations.Two independent implementations are interoperable for a ledger iff, given the same (\mathsf{LedHdr}), (\mathsf{Genesis}), step records, and referenced objects, both either:
verify the ledger and produce identical intermediate and final state hashes at every step, or
fail at the same earliest step with divergence evidence in the same divergence class under pinned failure schemas.
D.4 Receipt extraction
D.4.1 Output Merkle roots
D.4.1(a) Target pinset definition.Let (\mathsf{Targets}) be the plan’s target set (or the receipt’s declared output selection set if the ledger is not plan-bound). Define the output pinset (\mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{out}}) as the set of pin records in the terminal commitment store whose keys match (\mathsf{Targets}), with each pin record encoded canonically.
D.4.1(b) Output root computation.Compute:[r_{\mathsf{out}} := \mathsf{MerkleRoot}\big(\mathrm{Sort}([\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{PinRecord})\mid \mathsf{PinRecord}\in \mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{out}}])\big),]where sorting is lexicographic on canonical pin record bytes and MerkleRoot uses the pinned Merkle policy.
D.4.1(c) Output extraction determinism.Output extraction is deterministic because:
pin lookup and prefix scans are canonical and ordered,
target selection is a canonical set (sorted by canonical key bytes),
pin record encoding is canonical.
Any ambiguity in target resolution yields an explicit ambiguity artifact or failure depending on (\Omega).
D.4.1(d) Output membership proofs.For any output pin record, a membership proof under (r_{\mathsf{out}}) consists of the pin record bytes, its index in the sorted leaf list, and the Merkle authentication path. These proofs are used for dependency proofs and public verification packages.
D.4.2 Witness Merkle roots
D.4.2(a) Witness set definition.Define the witness set (\mathcal{W}) as the union over all steps in the executed segment of the step’s witness hashes:[\mathcal{W}:=\bigcup_{t=1}^n {h(W)\in W_t}.]If policy requires only required witnesses be included, the inclusion rule is pinned; otherwise include all witnesses referenced in step records.
D.4.2(b) Witness root computation.Compute:[r_{\mathsf{wits}} := \mathsf{MerkleRoot}\big(\mathrm{Sort}([\mathsf{EncHash}(h)\mid h\in \mathcal{W}])\big),]where (\mathsf{EncHash}) is the canonical fixed-length encoding of hash bytes and sorting is lexicographic.
D.4.2(c) Evidence root (policy-gated).If (\Omega) requires evidence-root commitments, define evidence set (\mathcal{E}) as retained evidence hashes and compute:[r_{\mathsf{ev}} := \mathsf{MerkleRoot}(\mathrm{Sort}([\mathsf{EncHash}(h)\mid h\in \mathcal{E}])).]Evidence inclusion is pinned by policy; missing mandatory evidence invalidates the receipt.
D.4.2(d) Witness membership proofs.Witness membership proofs under (r_{\mathsf{wits}}) allow downstream verifiers to confirm that specific proof authorizations and method verifications were part of the verified execution without re-reading the entire ledger, provided the ledger validity is established or trusted under an explicit trust policy.
D.4.3 Minimal disclosure receipts
D.4.3(a) Receipt schema.A receipt is a COS object:[\mathsf{Rcpt}:=(h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),h(\mathsf{Plan})?,h_{\mathsf{final}},r_{\mathsf{out}},r_{\mathsf{wits}},r_{\mathsf{ev}}?,h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{mode},h(\mathsf{Disclosure})?),]where (\mathsf{mode}\in{\mathsf{HASH_ONLY},\mathsf{SELECTIVE}}) and (h(\mathsf{Disclosure})) is present in selective mode.
D.4.3(b) Hash-only mode semantics.In (\mathsf{HASH_ONLY}) mode, the receipt is a commitment to outputs and witnesses and a pointer to a ledger. A third party can verify membership proofs for outputs and witnesses under the committed roots, but full validity requires access to ledger records and required proof objects. This mode supports private verification and public commitment without revealing payloads.
D.4.3(c) Selective disclosure mode semantics.In (\mathsf{SELECTIVE}) mode, the receipt additionally commits to a disclosure bundle object that includes:
required local-check data objects,
test basis objects,
checker program identities,
quantization and convention policy objects required to verify claimed proofs,
and any membership proofs needed for dependency claims.Selective disclosure does not require revealing all payload objects; it reveals the minimal set required by method disclosure policies.
D.4.3(d) Disclosure policy enforcement.Disclosure requirements are method-dependent. For any proof witness to be publicly verifiable, its method id disclosure policy must be satisfied by the disclosed bundle. If disclosure is insufficient, the receipt remains a commitment but is not publicly verifiable; this boundary is explicit in (\mathsf{mode}) and disclosure metadata.
D.4.4 Receipt verification procedure
D.4.4(a) Verification inputs.Receipt verification consumes:
receipt object bytes,
a resolver for ledger header and step record objects (or a trusted ledger cache),
a resolver for disclosed objects (selective mode) or a resolver for private objects (hash-only mode within a trusted environment),
the verifier set referenced by (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}),
and the Merkle policy referenced by (\mathsf{fmt_id}).
D.4.4(b) Minimal verification procedure.The minimal procedure is:
Validate receipt schema and canonical encoding.
Verify policy hash consistency: receipt policy hashes match ledger header hashes.
Verify ledger validity:
either by full replay verification, or
by a pinned trust policy indicating the ledger header is already verified and cached.
Confirm ledger terminal state hash equals (h_{\mathsf{final}}).
Recompute output root (r_{\mathsf{out}}) from extracted target pin records at terminal state; compare to receipt.
Recompute witness root (r_{\mathsf{wits}}) from step record witness lists; compare to receipt.
If evidence root is present, recompute or verify evidence inclusion under the pinned evidence inclusion rule; compare to receipt.
Any mismatch yields deterministic failure evidence.
D.4.4(c) Public verification in selective mode.In selective mode, additionally:
Validate disclosure bundle schema and canonical encoding.
Verify that all required method disclosure policies for claimed proofs are satisfied.
Verify required proof objects using disclosed local-check data and pinned checker programs.
Verify membership proofs for outputs and dependencies as needed.This procedure yields public verifiability without requiring disclosure of private payload bytes beyond what method policies demand.
D.4.4(d) Verification result object.Receipt verification outputs a COS object:[\mathsf{RcptVerify}:=(h(\mathsf{Rcpt}),\mathsf{status},h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),h(\mathsf{Evidence})?,h_{\mathsf{final}}?),]where (\mathsf{status}\in{\mathsf{OK},\mathsf{FAIL}}). On failure, (h(\mathsf{Evidence})) references a deterministic failure evidence object describing the first failing verification stage and implicated hashes.
APPENDIX D — LEDGER FORMAT, REPLAY VERIFICATION, AND RECEIPTS
APPENDIX E — COMPILER SCHEMAS: GOALS, PLANS, ROUTES, AND REPAIRS
This appendix specifies the canonical data schemas produced and consumed by compilation and repair planning. It formalizes (i) goal specifications, (ii) plans (programs with witness and ledger plans), (iii) deterministic routing policy artifacts (route plans and feasibility certificates), and (iv) repair plan schemas derived from defect evidence. All objects are COS objects: canonically encoded, hash-addressed, policy-bound, and replay-auditable. No compilation result is meaningful unless it is fully determined by the committed input hashes and pinned policies.
E.1 Goal specification schema
E.1.1 Goal types and targets
E.1.1(a) GoalType enum.Define a pinned enum (\mathsf{GoalType}) with canonical tag bytes and total order:[\mathsf{GoalType}\in{\mathsf{PIN},\mathsf{EDGE},\mathsf{PROOF},\mathsf{ROUTE},\mathsf{EVAL},\mathsf{REPAIR}}.]
(\mathsf{PIN}): produce and commit one or more pin records in the commitment store.
(\mathsf{EDGE}): produce and (if permitted) commit relation edges and accompanying artifacts.
(\mathsf{PROOF}): produce proof objects; optionally pin them.
(\mathsf{ROUTE}): compile a certified route plan artifact without execution.
(\mathsf{EVAL}): execute a plan to produce requested outputs and a receipt.
(\mathsf{REPAIR}): compile a repair plan in response to defect evidence.
The goal type determines admissible target kinds, required witness classes, and admissible output receipts.
E.1.1(b) Target record schema.Targets are expressed as a set-like collection of target records:[\mathsf{Targets}:={\mathsf{TargetRec}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{TargetRec}_m},]each target record:[\mathsf{TargetRec}:=(\mathsf{tgt_kind},\mathsf{tgt_id},h(\mathsf{SchemaReq})?,h(\mathsf{SpecReq})?,h(\mathsf{NSReq})?,h(\mathsf{StrengthReq})?).]Fields:
(\mathsf{tgt_kind}\in{\mathsf{PIN},\mathsf{EDGE},\mathsf{PROOF},\mathsf{PLAN},\mathsf{RECEIPT},\mathsf{EVIDENCE}}).
(\mathsf{tgt_id}) is canonical bytes for the identifier:
for (\mathsf{PIN}): canonical key bytes,
for (\mathsf{EDGE}): canonical edge-type and endpoint identifiers or an edge id format,
for (\mathsf{PROOF}): proof object hash bytes or a canonical proof name key,
for (\mathsf{PLAN}): plan hash bytes,
for (\mathsf{RECEIPT}): receipt hash bytes (when goal is to index or merge receipts),
for (\mathsf{EVIDENCE}): evidence object hash bytes (when goal is repair or audit packaging).
(h(\mathsf{SchemaReq})) optionally binds required schema hashes.
(h(\mathsf{SpecReq})) optionally binds required convention bundle hashes.
(h(\mathsf{NSReq})) optionally binds namespace policy objects governing where targets may be pinned.
(h(\mathsf{StrengthReq})) optionally binds required claim strength (invariant set, tolerance profile, verification level).
(\mathsf{Targets}) is encoded canonically as a set-like collection ordered lexicographically by ((\mathsf{tgt_kind},\mathsf{tgt_id})).
E.1.1(c) Target admissibility predicate.A target set (\mathsf{Targets}) is admissible for (\mathsf{GoalType}\ g) iff:
every (\mathsf{tgt_kind}) is permitted by (g) (e.g., (\mathsf{ROUTE}) goals may target (\mathsf{PLAN}) but may not require (\mathsf{PIN}) commitments),
every (\mathsf{tgt_id}) is canonical by Appendix A naming rules when it is a key,
each namespace requirement (h(\mathsf{NSReq})) is compatible with corridor scope constraints and contradiction policies,
any schema/spec/strength requirements are resolvable and consistent with the object type to be produced.
Violation yields a typed compile failure object with deterministic evidence.
E.1.1(d) Target closure requirements.Targets may reference other targets (e.g., a receipt target implies pin targets). Target closure is computed deterministically by a pinned closure rule:[\mathsf{CloseTargets}(g,\mathsf{Targets})\to \mathsf{Targets}^\star,]which expands implied targets (e.g., (\mathsf{EVAL}) implies (\mathsf{RECEIPT}) unless explicitly disabled by policy). Closure must be deterministic, canonical, and committed by hash in the goal object.
E.1.2 Context and constraints
E.1.2(a) Context object schema.Context is a COS object:[\mathsf{Context}:=(h(\mathsf{Bindings}),h(\mathsf{Refs}),h(\mathsf{Specs}),h(\mathsf{Defaults}),h(\mathsf{StateRef})?).]
(\mathsf{Bindings}) is a canonical map from canonical keys to typed values or object hashes.
(\mathsf{Refs}) is a canonical set of referenced artifacts:
pin ids,
object hashes,
library root hash,
optional checkpoint hash for state-dependent compilation.
(\mathsf{Specs}) binds required or implied convention bundles (SpecIn/SpecOut requirements) as hashes.
(\mathsf{Defaults}) lists default objects applied explicitly by the compiler.
(\mathsf{StateRef}) optionally binds a checkpoint hash (h(\mathsf{Chk})) when feasibility depends on current state (e.g., pin existence, cached receipts).
All components are canonical COS objects and are hashed; compilation decisions depend only on their hashes and pinned policies.
E.1.2(b) Constraints object schema.Constraints are a COS object:[\mathsf{Constraints}:=(h(\mathcal I_{\mathrm{req}}),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\max}),\mathsf{VL}{\min},h(\mathsf{DetReq}),h(\mathsf{CorrReq}),h(\mathsf{PrivReq}),h(\mathsf{Overrides})?).]
(h(\mathcal I_{\mathrm{req}})): required invariant set.
(h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max})): maximum tolerances permitted for required invariants.
(\mathsf{VL}_{\min}): minimum verification level required for required proof claims.
(h(\mathsf{DetReq})): determinism strictness requirements (e.g., forbid seed-based generation).
(h(\mathsf{CorrReq})): corridor requirements (e.g., required scope class, cap classes) potentially stricter than default (\Omega).
(h(\mathsf{PrivReq})): privacy/disclosure requirements (hash-only vs selective reveal, required disclosure bundles).
(h(\mathsf{Overrides})): optional explicit override permissions, constrained by (\Omega).
Constraints are canonicalized by sorting invariant ids, quantizing tolerances, and canonical ordering of override permissions.
E.1.2(c) Default insertion discipline.Any missing constraint field is filled by an explicit default object from a pinned default registry (\mathsf{DefReg}):[\mathsf{DefReg}:\ (\mathsf{GoalType},\mathsf{ObjType})\mapsto (h(\mathsf{ContextDefault}),h(\mathsf{ConstraintsDefault})).]Defaults are recorded in (\mathsf{Context.Defaults}) and thus affect goal and plan hashes. No implicit defaults are permitted.
E.1.2(d) Compatibility and satisfiability precheck.Before routing, the compiler must perform deterministic satisfiability checks:
required proof methods supporting (\mathsf{VL}{\min}) exist in (h{\mathsf{VerSet}}),
(\mathcal I_{\mathrm{req}}) is evaluable in at least one reachable lens chain (or can be made evaluable via explicit bridges),
(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max}) is compatible with corridor tolerance budgets and accumulation operators,
privacy requirements are compatible with required proof methods’ disclosure policies.Failure yields a compile infeasibility certificate.
E.1.3 Required invariants and tolerances
E.1.3(a) Invariant set object.The required invariant set is a COS object:[\mathcal I_{\mathrm{req}}:= (\mathsf{InvIndex},{\mathsf{inv_id}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{inv_id}_k}),]encoded as a set-like list sorted by canonical invariant id bytes. Each invariant id resolves to an invariant clause definition in the invariant registry (Appendix C).
E.1.3(b) Tolerance profile object.The tolerance profile is a COS object:[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\max}:=(h(\mathsf{InvIndex}),[q{\varepsilon,I}]{I\in\mathsf{InvIndex}}),]a fixed-length vector aligned to the invariant index. Exactness requirements are encoded by (q{\varepsilon,I}=0) for the relevant invariants. All tolerance components are quantized integers under pinned quantization policies.
E.1.3(c) Claim boundary object.For goals requiring proofs or receipts, the compiler may produce an explicit claim boundary object:[\mathsf{StrengthReq}:=(h(\mathcal I_{\mathrm{req}}),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\max}),\mathsf{VL}{\min},h(\mathsf{SpecReq}),h(\mathsf{LossAllow})?),]where (h(\mathsf{LossAllow})) optionally specifies permitted quotient semantics. Loss is forbidden unless explicitly allowed; any route containing lossy edges is infeasible when loss is not allowed.
