E.1.3(d) Propagation to routing.The compiler must propagate invariant and tolerance requirements into the metro map scoring and feasibility filters:
prune edges that drop required invariants without recovery,
prune edges that consume tolerances incompatible with (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max}),
enforce exactness preference lexicographically,
enforce required verification levels for proof methods.This propagation is deterministic and committed in route feasibility certificates.
E.1.4 Corridor bindings
E.1.4(a) Corridor binding object.A goal binds to corridor policy through:[\mathsf{CorrBind}:=(h_\Omega,h(\mathsf{CorrReq}),h(\mathsf{ScopeReq}),h(\mathsf{DetReq}),h(\mathsf{ContrReq}),h(\mathsf{DetectorsReq})),]where:
(h_\Omega) is the runtime corridor policy hash,
(\mathsf{CorrReq}) may request stricter caps/budgets than (\Omega) (never weaker),
(\mathsf{ScopeReq}) requests allowed write targets and protected namespaces,
(\mathsf{ContrReq}) binds contradiction policy requirements,
(\mathsf{DetectorsReq}) binds mandatory detector schedule requirements.
Bindings are admissible only if they do not weaken (\Omega). Any strengthening must be feasible under (\Omega) or require an explicit policy migration mechanism (if supported).
E.1.4(b) State-dependent compilation binding.When routing depends on availability of pinned artifacts (existing pins, cached receipts), the goal must bind a checkpoint hash (h(\mathsf{Chk})) in context. This makes compilation a function of state snapshot:[h(\mathsf{Goal}) = H(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{GoalType},\mathsf{Targets},h(\mathsf{Context}),h(\mathsf{Constraints}),h(\mathsf{Chk})?)).]Absent a checkpoint binding, compilation must treat such availability as unknown and either restrict to state-independent routes or produce a plan that includes prerequisite steps to construct required artifacts.
E.1.4(c) Budget escalation permissions.A goal may permit budget escalation only by explicit override permissions in (\mathsf{Overrides}), bounded by (\Omega). Escalation cannot be assumed. If a route requires escalation, the plan must include explicit budget witness requirements and the execution must supply such witnesses; otherwise the plan is infeasible.
E.1.4(d) Contradiction-controlled namespaces.If a goal targets writes into contradiction-controlled namespaces, the goal must include:
explicit resolution kind permissions (merge/overwrite/disambiguate),
minimum proof method ids for resolution,
and required evidence retention for contradiction events.These constraints are encoded in (\mathsf{CorrBind}) and are enforced during plan assembly and execution.
E.2 Plan schema
A plan is a fully specified execution artifact: route + program + witness plan + ledger plan + policy bindings, all content-addressed by hash.
E.2.1 Route list and edge ids
E.2.1(a) Route object schema.A route is a COS object:[\mathsf{Route}:=[\mathsf{edge_id}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{edge_id}_k],]sequence-like and order-significant. Each (\mathsf{edge_id}) is a 256-bit hash referencing a metro map edge object (Chapter 18). Route edges must be membership-provable in the metro map edge root when metro map is Merkle-committed.
E.2.1(b) Route boundary fields.A route is accompanied by a boundary record:[\mathsf{RouteBounds}:=(\mathsf{start_node},\mathsf{end_node},h(\mathsf{SpecStart}),h(\mathsf{SpecEnd}),h(\mathsf{LensStart})?,h(\mathsf{LensEnd})?),]binding start/end nodes and convention bundles. The route is admissible only if these boundaries match the goal’s required spec and lens expectations or include explicit bridges in the edge sequence.
E.2.1(c) Route feasibility certificate.Plans must include a feasibility certificate object hash (h(\mathsf{FeasCert})) committing:
feasibility predicate ids applied,
outcomes,
detected required bridges and normalizers,
cumulative tolerance usage,
and any required witness method availability checks.
(\mathsf{FeasCert}) is replay-derivable from metro map annotations and policy hashes; it is included for deterministic explainability and repair planning.
E.2.1(d) Route explainability artifact (optional, policy-gated).When required, plans include an explainability artifact (h(\mathsf{Explain})) containing:
selected route cost tuple,
tie-break evidence when alternatives tie,
best infeasible alternative and its first failing predicate (optional).Explainability is structured and deterministic and must not include narrative content.
E.2.2 Program encoding
E.2.2(a) Program object schema.A program is a COS object:[\Pi := [\mathsf{Step}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{Step}_n],]where each step is:[\mathsf{Step}_t:=(\mathsf{op_id}_t,h(P_t),h(\Gamma_t),\lambda_t,h(\mathsf{EntryRef})?).]
(\mathsf{op_id}_t) is either a library entry id, a macro id, or a kernel opcode id, all canonical.
(h(P_t)) is the parameter hash (parameters are COS objects).
(h(\Gamma_t)) is the context hash (either shared or step-specific).
(\lambda_t) is expansion depth, bounded by corridor policy.
(h(\mathsf{EntryRef})) optionally binds a resolved entry object hash to guard against alias drift; if present, it must match the library entry id and the library root hash.
Programs are canonically encoded; program hash is (h(\Pi)).
E.2.2(b) Parameter object schema.Each step parameter object (P_t) is a COS object conforming to the parameter schema required by the referenced op. Parameter maps are canonical maps: keys sorted lexicographically, values canonically encoded. Any default parameters must be explicit references to default objects by hash.
E.2.2(c) Program normal form binding.Plans must bind the program normal form policy hash (h(\mathsf{PNF})) when optimization or canonical expansion depends on it. If the plan claims the program is in normal form, it must include evidence or rely on definitional properties of (\mathsf{PNF}) under pinned policy. Programs not in normal form may be executed; however, any rewrite-based optimization assumes normal form.
E.2.2(d) Program-to-route consistency.Program steps must correspond to route edges deterministically: there exists a pinned derivation rule mapping each route edge to one or more program steps (e.g., macro expansion). The plan includes (h(\mathsf{Route2Prog})) specifying this mapping policy. Any mismatch between route and program under this policy invalidates the plan.
E.2.3 Witness plan encoding
E.2.3(a) Witness requirement record.A witness plan is a sequence-like COS object:[\mathsf{WPlan}:=[\mathsf{WReq}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{WReq}_n],]where each record:[\mathsf{WReq}_t:=(\mathsf{step_index},\mathsf{op_id}_t,h(P_t),\mathsf{reqTypes},h(\mathsf{ReqMethods}),h(\mathsf{ReqLCD}),h(\mathsf{ReqPolicies}),h(\mathsf{Cond})?).]
(\mathsf{reqTypes}) is a bitset over witness types.
(\mathsf{ReqMethods}) is a canonical set of method ids required (for proof witnesses).
(\mathsf{ReqLCD}) is a canonical set of required local-check data object hashes.
(\mathsf{ReqPolicies}) binds any policy ids required (e.g., contradiction resolution rules).
(\mathsf{Cond}) is optional and binds conditional requirements (e.g., consistency witness required only if contradiction predicate triggers).
All sets are encoded set-like and sorted canonically.
E.2.3(b) Derivation rule.Witness plan derivation is deterministic from:
route edge witness profiles,
corridor policy witness requirements,
and any additional macro-level requirements.The plan includes a derivation policy hash (h(\mathsf{WPlanPol})) so independent compilers produce identical witness plans.
E.2.3(c) Conditional witness predicates.Conditional requirements must be pinned predicates computable deterministically during projection (e.g., contradiction predicate outcome). If a conditional predicate is not deterministic or depends on hidden state, it is inadmissible. Conditional witness requirements must be listed explicitly to preserve replay determinism.
E.2.3(d) Witness-plan completeness predicate.A plan is witness-complete iff for every step (t), all required witness types and method ids are supported by the runtime verifier set (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}). This is checked at compile time and recorded in feasibility certificates.
E.2.4 Ledger plan encoding
E.2.4(a) Ledger step policy record.A ledger plan is a sequence-like COS object:[\mathsf{LPlan}:=[\mathsf{LStep}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{LStep}_n],]where each record:[\mathsf{LStep}_t:=(\mathsf{step_index},\mathsf{op_id}_t,h(P_t),\mathsf{primaryTarget},h(\mathsf{DetSched}),h(\mathsf{PartialPol}),h(\mathsf{BudgetCaps}),h(\mathsf{ScopeCaps}),h(\mathsf{EvidenceReq})).]
(\mathsf{primaryTarget}) identifies primary store target (commit/rel/cand/proof) per the transform contract.
(\mathsf{DetSched}) binds detector schedule ids and their phase order (pre-apply, pre-project, project-time, post-commit).
(\mathsf{PartialPol}) binds whether partial acceptance is permitted and which truncation comparators and mask encodings apply.
(\mathsf{BudgetCaps}) binds per-step budget caps derived from (\Omega) and entry budgets.
(\mathsf{ScopeCaps}) binds per-step scope limits and required scope-extension witness ids.
(\mathsf{EvidenceReq}) binds evidence retention requirements for this step.
E.2.4(b) Detector schedule schema.A detector schedule is a COS object:[\mathsf{DetSched}:=(h(\mathsf{PreApply}),h(\mathsf{PreProject}),h(\mathsf{Project}),h(\mathsf{PostCommit}),h(\mathsf{OrderPol})),]each phase being a canonical ordered list of detector ids. Detector ids resolve to detector implementations pinned by the runtime header and (\Omega). The order policy is pinned and must be consistent across implementations.
E.2.4(c) Partial acceptance policy.A partial policy is a COS object:[\mathsf{PartialPol}:=(\mathsf{allowPartial},h(\mathsf{Discardable}),h(\mathsf{TruncComp}),h(\mathsf{MaskSchema}),h(\mathsf{CommitHardness})),]specifying which outputs may be discarded deterministically and which outputs are hard (never discardable). Contradiction-controlled commitments are hard by default; any relaxation must be explicit and is typically forbidden.
E.2.4(d) Ledger-plan feasibility.Ledger plan feasibility requires that:
detectors and evidence requirements fit within corridor compute/write budgets,
mask computations are deterministic and bounded,
any required audits (post-commit contradiction scans, normal-form checks) are included.Feasibility is checked at compile time using cost classes of verifiers and detectors and recorded in feasibility certificates.
E.3 Deterministic routing policy
Routing policy defines feasibility pruning, cost functional computation, tie-break rules, and bridge insertion rules. Routing is a deterministic computation producing route plans and certificates.
E.3.1 Feasibility filters
E.3.1(a) Filter schema.Feasibility filters are pinned predicate ids grouped as:[\mathsf{FeasFilt}:=(h(\mathsf{SpecFilt}),h(\mathsf{BudgetFilt}),h(\mathsf{TolFilt}),h(\mathsf{VerFilt}),h(\mathsf{DomainFilt}),h(\mathsf{ScopeFilt}),h(\mathsf{LossFilt})).]Each component is a canonical ordered list of predicate ids whose semantics are pinned by (\Omega) and the metro map policies.
E.3.1(b) Convention compatibility filter.(\mathsf{SpecFilt}) rejects any route boundary where (\mathsf{SpecOut}\neq \mathsf{SpecIn}) without explicit bridge edges. It also enforces mandatory normalizers after certain bridge classes. This filter is categorical: mismatch is not “near”; it is infeasible.
E.3.1(c) Budget and tolerance filters.(\mathsf{BudgetFilt}) rejects edges whose budget profiles exceed corridor maxima and rejects routes whose cumulative budgets exceed segment caps (when present).(\mathsf{TolFilt}) rejects routes whose cumulative tolerance usage exceeds (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max}) or corridor tolerance budgets for required invariants. It enforces near-threshold mitigation insertion when required by (\Omega).
E.3.1(d) Verifier and scope filters.(\mathsf{VerFilt}) rejects edges requiring unsupported method ids or verification levels.(\mathsf{ScopeFilt}) rejects edges whose scope requires forbidden writes for the goal type and corridor class. Scope feasibility is checked with respect to the plan’s intended commitment targets and contradiction policies.
E.3.2 Cost functional terms
E.3.2(a) Edge feature extraction.Each metro map edge has annotation features:[\mathsf{Feat}(e):=(\mathsf{exactFlag},\mathbf{tols},c_{\mathsf{budget}},c_{\mathsf{wit}},c_{\mathsf{write}},\mathsf{invTier},\mathsf{hubFlag},\mathsf{bridgeClass}?),]derived deterministically from edge annotations and pinned cost-weight policies.
E.3.2(b) Route cost tuple.Route cost is computed as a lexicographic tuple:[\mathsf{RCost}(r):=(\varepsilon\text{-priority},C_{\varepsilon},k,C_{\mathsf{budget}},C_{\mathsf{wit}},C_{\mathsf{write}},C_{\mathsf{risk}},\mathsf{LexKey}(r)),]where:
(\varepsilon\text{-priority}=0) iff cumulative tolerance usage is zero on required invariants (exact), else (1),
(C_{\varepsilon}) is a deterministic penalty computed from cumulative tolerance usage relative to (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max}),
(k) is hop count,
(C_{\mathsf{budget}},C_{\mathsf{wit}},C_{\mathsf{write}}) are sums of edge contributions,
(C_{\mathsf{risk}}) penalizes near-threshold usage and long-chain instability risks as pinned,
(\mathsf{LexKey}(r)) is a deterministic tie-break key.
All weights and penalty functions are pinned by the routing policy object.
E.3.2(c) Exactness preference.Exact routes dominate approximate routes whenever exactness is required (i.e., (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max}=\mathbf{0}) on required invariants). If approximation is permitted, exactness still dominates lexicographically unless routing policy explicitly disables it for specific goal types.
E.3.2(d) Reuse cost terms.If reuse edges (receipt consumption) are permitted, routing policy includes:
a reuse verification cost (membership proofs + dependency proofs),
a trust boundary penalty when public verification requires additional disclosures,
and a preference rule selecting strongest admissible cached receipts by refinement preorder.These terms are deterministic and committed in routing feasibility/scoring evidence.
E.3.3 Tie-break rules
E.3.3(a) LexKey definition.(\mathsf{LexKey}(r)) is the canonical encoding of the route’s edge id sequence:[\mathsf{LexKey}(r):=\mathsf{Enc}([\mathsf{edge_id}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{edge_id}_k]),]or, if parameter-binding is determined during routing, the encoding of pairs ((\mathsf{edge_id}_i,h(P_i))). The choice is pinned; implementations must agree.
E.3.3(b) Deterministic shortest-path selection.Routing uses a deterministic shortest-legal path algorithm whose comparator is (\mathsf{RCost}). For equal cost tuples, the chosen route is the one with lexicographically smallest (\mathsf{LexKey}). This ensures plan hashes are stable across independent compilers.
E.3.3(c) Deterministic failure selection.If no feasible route exists, the compiler produces an infeasibility certificate selecting the lexicographically minimal failing predicate record among candidate prefixes under a pinned order:
missing verifier methods,
forbidden scope,
missing bridges,
budget infeasibility,
tolerance infeasibility,
domain infeasibility.This yields reproducible failure artifacts.
E.3.3(d) Tie-break evidence schema.When ties occur among feasible routes with identical cost tuples except (\mathsf{LexKey}), the compiler may emit tie-break evidence:[\mathsf{TieEv}:=(h(\mathsf{RouteA}),h(\mathsf{RouteB}),\mathsf{RCost},\mathsf{LexKeyA},\mathsf{LexKeyB},\mathsf{chosen}),]binding the competing routes and the deterministic selection basis. Emission is policy-gated; if emitted, it is canonical and hashed.
E.3.4 Bridge insertion rules
E.3.4(a) Spec mismatch detection.Bridge insertion is triggered whenever adjacent edges have mismatched convention bundles:[h(\mathsf{SpecOut}(e_i))\neq h(\mathsf{SpecIn}(e_{i+1})).]No implicit coercion is permitted. The compiler must either insert a bridge chain or reject the route.
E.3.4(b) Bridge chain selection.Bridge selection is a deterministic subrouting problem on the bridge subgraph, minimizing a pinned objective:[(\varepsilon\text{-priority},C_{\varepsilon},#\text{bridges},C_{\mathsf{wit}},C_{\mathsf{budget}},\mathsf{LexKey}),]subject to feasibility under (\Omega) and (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max}). Exact bridges are strictly preferred. If multiple chains tie, lexicographically smallest chain is chosen.
E.3.4(c) Mandatory post-bridge normalization.After any bridge chain, the compiler must insert required normalizers mandated by:
convention discipline,
bridge class policies,
and corridor near-threshold mitigation rules.Normalizer insertion is deterministic and committed in route and program. Missing required normalizers makes the plan infeasible.
E.3.4(d) Bridge proof requirements.Inserted bridges impose proof witness requirements. The compiler must ensure that:
required bridge proof method ids are supported by (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}),
required verification level constraints are satisfied,
tolerance usage is accounted.Bridge insertion is therefore coupled to witness plan generation and ledger plan detector schedules.
E.4 Repair plan schema
Repair planning compiles defect evidence into explicit repair programs (Chapter 10) with deterministic minimality and auditability.
E.4.1 Defect→repair mapping
E.4.1(a) Repair goal object.A repair goal is a COS object:[\mathsf{RepGoal}:=(h(\mathsf{DefEvidence}),\mathsf{defect_class},h(\mathsf{RepTargetPred}),h(\mathsf{RepConstraints}),h(\mathsf{RepPolicy})),]where:
(h(\mathsf{DefEvidence})) is the triggering defect evidence hash (Chapter 9),
(\mathsf{defect_class}) is a pinned enum (convention drift, invariant drift, accumulation, replay divergence, contradiction),
(\mathsf{RepTargetPred}) is a deterministic postcondition predicate describing “defect resolved,”
(\mathsf{RepConstraints}) binds allowed repair classes and budgets under (\Omega),
(\mathsf{RepPolicy}) binds minimality objective and tie-break rules.
E.4.1(b) Repair class mapping table.Repair planning uses a pinned mapping table:[\mathsf{RepMap}:\ (\mathsf{defect_class},\mathsf{subcode})\mapsto [\mathsf{RepairClass}]^\ast,]where (\mathsf{RepairClass}\in{\mathsf{BRIDGE},\mathsf{REGRID},\mathsf{RENORM},\mathsf{PROOFREGEN},\mathsf{REROUTE},\mathsf{HALT}}) and the list is ordered by deterministic preference. For each class, the table binds required transforms, required witness methods, and required postchecks.
E.4.1(c) Defect evidence interface.Repair planning requires defect evidence to provide:
boundary location (step index, edge id, spec mismatch location),
implicated policy hashes,
missing bridge class or failing invariant id(s),
earliest divergence point for replay divergence,
and feasibility reasons for alternative repairs (optional).Evidence schemas must therefore be sufficiently rich for deterministic repair compilation.
E.4.1(d) Non-repairable classification.Certain defects are non-repairable under a given (\Omega) and library state:
replay divergence under fixed headers,
missing bridge classes with no admissible bridge entries,
hard invariant failures with no alternative route,
tolerance budget exhaustion with no permissible tightening or reroute.Repair planning must classify these deterministically and produce a halt plan or halt artifact rather than speculative repairs.
E.4.2 Minimal repair sequences
E.4.2(a) Repair plan object.A repair plan is a COS plan object with (\mathsf{GoalType}=\mathsf{REPAIR}) whose route and program implement the repair. It includes:
the repair goal (\mathsf{RepGoal}),
a route plan through repair transforms (bridges/normalizers/regrids/proof regeneration),
a completion proof step that verifies (\mathsf{RepTargetPred}),
and a repair receipt target.
E.4.2(b) Minimality objective.Repairs are selected by a pinned lexicographic objective:[(\varepsilon\text{-priority},C_{\varepsilon},#\text{repair steps},C_{\mathsf{wit}},C_{\mathsf{budget}},C_{\mathsf{write}},\mathsf{LexKey}).]Exact repairs (no tolerance increase) dominate. Repairs that tighten tolerances or strengthen proofs may increase cost but are preferred when required by (\Omega) or by goal constraints.
E.4.2(c) Repair isolation set.Repair plans must include an explicit isolation set object:[\mathsf{IsoSet}:=(h(\mathsf{PinsAllowed}),h(\mathsf{EdgesAllowed}),h(\mathsf{ObjsAllowed}),h(\mathsf{NamespacesAllowed})),]bounding the allowed writes. Isolation is enforced by scope constraints and scope witnesses. Writing outside isolation is illegal.
E.4.2(d) Repair completion evidence.Repairs must emit a completion proof object:[\mathsf{ProofRep}:=(h(\mathsf{RepGoal}),h(\mathsf{Plan}),h_{\mathsf{preChk}},h_{\mathsf{postChk}},h(\mathsf{DefEvidence}),h(\mathsf{DetOKSet}),\mathsf{VL}),]certifying that the triggering defect class is resolved and that mandated detectors now pass at the repaired boundary. This proof is required to produce a repair receipt.
E.4.3 Reroute triggers
E.4.3(a) Reroute predicate.Reroute is selected when:
defect evidence indicates invariant drift or tolerance accumulation rather than convention mismatch,
or convention mismatch is repairable but would require an approximate bridge incompatible with exactness constraints,
or feasibility filters fail due to unsupported verifier methods or insufficient budgets for current route.
Reroute triggers are deterministic functions of defect evidence fields and pinned repair policy.
E.4.3(b) Reroute plan schema.A reroute repair plan includes:
a new route plan computed under updated constraints (e.g., enforce exactness, raise verification level, restrict to invertible edges),
mandatory normalization checkpoints,
proof regeneration steps if required to satisfy (\mathsf{VL}_{\min}),
and a completion proof verifying that the defect is resolved.
The reroute plan commits to the original failing route hash and the defect evidence hash to preserve auditability.
E.4.3(c) Tightening vs loosening.Reroute typically tightens constraints (prefer exact transforms, stronger proofs). Loosening (fallback) is permitted only if the goal explicitly allows it and is encoded as a new constraints object hash. Any loosened reroute must produce receipts explicitly binding the weakened claim boundary.
E.4.3(d) Deterministic boundary selection for rerun.Reroute repairs must specify a rerun boundary checkpoint. Boundary selection is deterministic:
choose the last stable anchor checkpoint preceding the earliest failing step index in defect evidence,
or the pre-step checkpoint for a single-step defect.The selected boundary checkpoint hash is committed in the repair plan and used for replay stability.
E.4.4 Halt conditions
E.4.4(a) Halt plan vs halt artifact.If a defect is non-repairable under the current policies, the compiler must output:
either a halt plan (a plan whose execution emits a halt receipt and pins a halt artifact), if (\Omega) permits committing halt artifacts,
or a halt artifact object as a compile output (not executed), when commitment is not permitted.
Halt outputs are deterministic and bind to defect evidence.
E.4.4(b) Halt artifact schema.A halt artifact is a COS object:[\mathsf{Halt}:=(h(\mathsf{DefEvidence}),\mathsf{halt_code},\mathsf{earliest_step},h(\mathsf{PolicySet}),h(\mathsf{LibRoot}),h(\mathsf{Chk}),h(\mathsf{Explain})?),]where:
(\mathsf{halt_code}) is a pinned enum (replay divergence, missing bridge, infeasible under budgets, unsupported method ids, contradiction non-resolvable, tolerance exhaustion),
(\mathsf{PolicySet}) binds relevant policy hashes,
(h(\mathsf{Chk})) binds the checkpoint where execution remains safe (last stable anchor),
(h(\mathsf{Explain})) optionally provides deterministic explainability.
E.4.4(c) Halt determinism.Halt selection is deterministic by repair policy:
choose the earliest failing defect evidence,
choose the minimal non-repairable classification,
commit to the exact missing prerequisite (bridge class, method id, budget component).No heuristic halts are permitted.
E.4.4(d) Halt as a governance input.Halt artifacts are designed to be actionable inputs to governance and library expansion:
missing bridge class triggers admission of a new bridge entry,
missing method id triggers verifier registry extension,
infeasible budgets trigger corridor policy adjustment (if permitted),
contradiction non-resolvable triggers refinement of resolution policies or constraint clarification.Any governance action must be represented as a new plan under updated policies and yields new receipts.
APPENDIX E — COMPILER SCHEMAS: GOALS, PLANS, ROUTES, AND REPAIRS
APPENDIX F — LEGAL TRANSFORM CONTRACT TEMPLATES AND ADMISSION PACKETS
This appendix specifies canonical templates for legal transform contracts and the admission packet discipline that binds each contract to executable implementations, verifier support, and verified reference receipts. A legal transform contract is the sole admissible unit of representation transport and computation within the system; admission packets are the sole admissible mechanism by which a contract becomes part of an executable, compositional library.
F.1 Contract core schema
F.1.1 Field set and canonical encoding
F.1.1(a) Contract object.A legal transform contract is a COS object (\mathsf{LT}) with canonical fields:[\mathsf{LT}:=(\mathsf{lt_id},\ \mathsf{kind},\ \mathsf{ver},\ h(L_{\mathrm{in}}),\ h(L_{\mathrm{out}}),\ h(\mathsf{SpecIn}),\ h(\mathsf{SpecOut}),\ h(\mathsf{Impl}),\ h(\mathcal I_{\mathsf{LT}}),\ h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}{\mathsf{LT}}),\ h(\mathcal{B}{\mathsf{LT}}),\ h(\mathcal{S}{\mathsf{LT}}),\ h(\mathcal{W}{\mathsf{LT}}),\ h(\mathcal{R}_{\mathsf{LT}}),\ h(\mathsf{Loss})?,\ h(\mathsf{Admit})?,\ \mathsf{meta}?).]
(\mathsf{lt_id}:=h(\mathsf{LT})) is the contract identity hash.
(\mathsf{kind}) is a pinned enum (Section F.1.3).
(\mathsf{ver}) is a version tuple (major, minor, patch) and is part of the canonical bytes.
(h(L_{\mathrm{in}}),h(L_{\mathrm{out}})) are lens hashes (Appendix C).
(h(\mathsf{SpecIn}),h(\mathsf{SpecOut})) are convention bundle hashes (Appendix K).
(h(\mathsf{Impl})) is the implementation descriptor hash (program/rule id bundle).
(h(\mathcal I_{\mathsf{LT}})) is invariant clause set hash; (h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathsf{LT}})) is tolerance profile hash.
(h(\mathcal{B}{\mathsf{LT}})) is budget profile hash; (h(\mathcal{S}{\mathsf{LT}})) is scope profile hash.
(h(\mathcal{W}{\mathsf{LT}})) is witness profile hash; (h(\mathcal{R}{\mathsf{LT}})) is replay profile hash.
(h(\mathsf{Loss})) is present iff the transform is quotient-bearing or intentionally lossy.
(h(\mathsf{Admit})) is present iff the contract is admitted into a library catalog; absence indicates a local/unadmitted contract artifact.
All fields are encoded under COS rules: fixed field order, canonical integer encodings, canonical map/set encodings, fixed endianness and layout for embedded arrays, and canonical byte collation for sorting.
F.1.1(b) Implementation descriptor (\mathsf{Impl}).(\mathsf{Impl}) is a COS object:[\mathsf{Impl}:=(\mathsf{impl_kind},h(\Pi)?,h(\mathsf{Rule})?,h(\mathsf{ProgNF})?,h(\mathsf{Deps})?,h(\mathsf{CostClass}),h(\mathsf{DetReq})),]where:
(\mathsf{impl_kind}\in{\mathsf{PROGRAM},\mathsf{RULE},\mathsf{MACRO}}),
(h(\Pi)) is the canonical program hash for the implementation when (\mathsf{PROGRAM}),
(h(\mathsf{Rule})) is a rule identifier (e.g., for kernel-primitive implementations) when (\mathsf{RULE}),
(h(\mathsf{ProgNF})) pins program-normal-form policy when required,
(h(\mathsf{Deps})) binds dependency entries, required bridges, and required normalizers,
(h(\mathsf{CostClass})) binds cost accounting metadata,
(h(\mathsf{DetReq})) binds determinism requirements (ordering, pseudo-randomness policy, numeric policy requirements).
F.1.1(c) Canonical contract id and immutability.Contract identity is content-addressed:[\mathsf{lt_id} = H(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{LT})).]Therefore any change to any field yields a distinct (\mathsf{lt_id}). Contracts are immutable; “updating a contract” is a new contract with a new (\mathsf{lt_id}), optionally linked by supersession metadata in admitted catalogs.
F.1.1(d) Contract schema hash binding.Each (\mathsf{LT}) includes an implicit type tag whose schema hash (h(\mathsf{Schema}_{\mathsf{LT}})) is pinned by the object standard. Verifiers must reject any (\mathsf{LT}) object whose schema hash does not match the expected contract schema for the runtime header’s object standard hash.
F.1.2 Required hash commitments and references
F.1.2(a) Required references (minimum set).A well-formed (\mathsf{LT}) must commit by hash to the following referenced objects, each of which must be resolvable and well-formed:
(h(L_{\mathrm{in}})), (h(L_{\mathrm{out}})) (lens objects).
(h(\mathsf{SpecIn})), (h(\mathsf{SpecOut})) (convention bundle objects).
(h(\mathsf{Impl})) (implementation descriptor).
(h(\mathcal I_{\mathsf{LT}})) (invariant clause set).
(h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathsf{LT}})) (tolerance profile aligned to invariant index).
(h(\mathcal{B}_{\mathsf{LT}})) (budget profile).
(h(\mathcal{S}_{\mathsf{LT}})) (scope profile).
(h(\mathcal{W}_{\mathsf{LT}})) (witness profile).
(h(\mathcal{R}_{\mathsf{LT}})) (replay profile).
(h(\mathsf{Loss})) if lossy or quotient-bearing.
F.1.2(b) Reference closure and resolvability.A contract is resolvable only if the transitive closure of its required references is resolvable under the resolver used for execution and verification:[\mathsf{Resolve}^\ast(h(\mathsf{LT}))\downarrow.]If any referenced object is missing, the contract is not executable and cannot be admitted. If admission is attempted, admission must fail deterministically with a missing-dependency evidence object.
F.1.2(c) Policy compatibility commitments.Contracts intended for library use must additionally bind a compatibility vector:[\mathsf{CompatVec}:=(h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h_{\mathsf{CostModel}},h(\mathsf{MerklePol}),h(\mathsf{DetPol}),h(\Omega_{\min})),]stored either in (\mathsf{meta}) or in the admission packet. This vector constrains which runtime headers and corridor classes may execute the contract.
F.1.2(d) Explicit non-preservation commitments.If the transform changes conventions or drops invariants, it must declare so explicitly:
convention changes appear in (\mathsf{SpecIn}\neq \mathsf{SpecOut}) and are treated as bridges or composites with explicit bridges,
invariant drops appear as absence from (\mathcal I_{\mathsf{LT}}) and/or presence in a loss profile’s dropped invariants list,
quotient semantics appear in (h(\mathsf{Loss})).Any implicit change is illegal and must be rejected by drift detectors and verifiers.
F.1.3 Contract kinds (atomic/bridge/normalizer/composite/repair)
F.1.3(a) Kind enum and constraints.(\mathsf{kind}) is a pinned enum:[\mathsf{kind}\in{\mathsf{ATOMIC},\mathsf{BRIDGE},\mathsf{NORMALIZER},\mathsf{COMPOSITE},\mathsf{REPAIR}}.]Kind determines required structure and admissible semantics:
(\mathsf{ATOMIC}): single-purpose transform with (\mathsf{ImplKind}\in{\mathsf{PROGRAM},\mathsf{RULE}}); may preserve conventions or change conventions only if it is explicitly a bridge (thus (\mathsf{ATOMIC}) with (\mathsf{SpecIn}\neq\mathsf{SpecOut}) is invalid unless also (\mathsf{BRIDGE})).
(\mathsf{BRIDGE}): convention-mapping transform; must satisfy (\mathsf{SpecIn}\neq\mathsf{SpecOut}) in at least one convention component and must bind a bridge class indicator in metadata.
(\mathsf{NORMALIZER}): canonicalization transform; must satisfy (\mathsf{SpecIn}=\mathsf{SpecOut}) and must be idempotent under declared invariants at its declared verification level.
(\mathsf{COMPOSITE}): program-defined composition of multiple entries/steps; must bind the composed route/program hashes in (\mathsf{Impl}) and satisfy replay-profile coherence conditions.
(\mathsf{REPAIR}): defect-driven transform; must bind defect evidence hashes and a completion predicate in metadata or in a bound repair-goal object referenced by (h(\mathsf{Admit})) or (h(\mathsf{Impl})).
F.1.3(b) Bridge kind requirements.If (\mathsf{kind}=\mathsf{BRIDGE}), then:
At least one convention fingerprint component differs between (\mathsf{SpecIn}) and (\mathsf{SpecOut}).
The contract must include a mapping rule object reference (h(\mathsf{MapRule})) either within (\mathsf{Impl}) or within (\mathsf{meta}).
The invariant contract must include at least the invariants required to justify “meaning preservation” across the convention change (as declared), and tolerances must be explicit.
Mandatory post-bridge normalization requirement must be encoded either as a dependency in (\mathsf{Impl.Deps}) or as a policy-bound insertion requirement in (\mathcal{R}_{\mathsf{LT}}).
F.1.3(c) Normalizer kind requirements.If (\mathsf{kind}=\mathsf{NORMALIZER}), then:
(\mathsf{SpecIn}=\mathsf{SpecOut}).
The contract must declare idempotence as a claim boundary (exact or bounded) and must bind a proof method that can certify idempotence at the declared (\mathsf{VL}).
The contract must declare which invariants are preserved exactly and which are preserved within tolerance, and must not silently change semantics; if it changes semantics, it is not a normalizer but a lossy transform and must be marked as such with (h(\mathsf{Loss})).
The scope profile must be narrow: normalizers typically write to proof store and produce normalized objects; any commitment writes must be explicit and policy-gated.
F.1.3(d) Repair kind requirements.If (\mathsf{kind}=\mathsf{REPAIR}), then:
The contract must bind a repair goal object (h(\mathsf{RepGoal})) containing triggering evidence hash(es), defect class, and completion predicate.
The program must include a completion proof emission step whose verifier can check that the completion predicate holds at the post-state.
The scope profile must include an isolation set restricting writes to affected namespaces; violation is illegal.
Repair minimality objective and tie-break rules must be pinned either in the repair goal object or in the corridor repair policy.
F.1.4 Well-formedness predicates and validators
F.1.4(a) Contract well-formedness predicate.Define (\mathsf{WF}(\mathsf{LT})) to be true iff:
Lens hashes resolve and lenses are well-formed.
Convention bundles resolve and are well-formed.
Implementation descriptor resolves and is well-formed, and its program/rule references resolve.
Invariant clause set resolves and each clause is well-formed (Section F.2.1).
Tolerance profile resolves and is aligned to invariant index; tolerances are quantized and nonnegative.
Budget profile resolves and is well-formed (Section F.3.1).
Scope profile resolves and is well-formed (Section F.3.2).
Witness profile resolves and references only supported witness types and method ids (Appendix B).
Replay profile resolves and pins required determinism constraints (Section F.4).
If loss profile is present, it is well-formed and coherent with invariants and invertibility claims (Section F.2.4).
Kind-specific constraints hold (Section F.1.3).
Failure must yield deterministic evidence objects identifying the first failing component under a pinned validation order.
F.1.4(b) Validator algorithm (deterministic).A contract validator (\mathsf{ValidateLT}) is a deterministic algorithm:[\mathsf{ValidateLT}(h(\mathsf{LT}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\mathsf{true/false},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?),]that:
resolves contract bytes and checks schema hash,
resolves required references and checks each referenced object schema hash,
checks invariant/tolerance alignment and admissibility,
checks witness method availability under (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}) when admission is intended,
checks compatibility vector constraints when present.
Validation order is pinned to guarantee deterministic “first error” evidence.
F.1.4(c) Invariant-subset sanity checks.The validator must check that (\mathcal I_{\mathsf{LT}}) is compatible with the involved lenses:
each invariant’s required observables subset is contained in both lens observable algebras (or explicit measurement transports exist and are included in (\mathsf{Impl.Deps}) for composite implementations),
each invariant’s required convention fingerprints are compatible with (\mathsf{SpecIn}) and (\mathsf{SpecOut}) in the manner declared (preserve vs map vs quotient),
any invariant clause declared “hard” is consistent with corridor policy hardness rules when used under a particular (\Omega) (hardness is ultimately a corridor classification, but contracts may specify default hardness class).
F.1.4(d) Honesty checks (invertibility and loss).The validator must enforce honesty constraints:
if loss profile is present, the contract must not claim exact invertibility,
if inverse certificates are claimed (by metadata), corresponding inverse contracts and proofs must be referenced by hash,
if (\mathsf{kind}=\mathsf{NORMALIZER}), loss profile must be absent unless the normalizer is explicitly a quotient projection and is marked accordingly; such marking changes its kind semantics and must be explicit.
F.2 Invariant clauses and tolerance profiles
F.2.1 Clause schema (id, evaluator, norm, tolerance, applicability)
F.2.1(a) Invariant clause object.An invariant clause is a COS object:[\mathsf{InvClause}:=(\mathsf{inv_id},h(\mathsf{Eval}),h(\mathsf{Norm}),h(Q_{\mathsf{meas}}),h(\mathsf{ObsReq}),h(\mathsf{SpecReq}),h(\mathsf{App}),\mathsf{tol},h(\oplus_{\mathsf{inv}}),\mathsf{flags}?).]Fields:
(\mathsf{inv_id}) is a canonical identifier.
(\mathsf{Eval}) references a deterministic evaluator producing quantized vectors.
(\mathsf{Norm}) references a pinned metric/norm id.
(Q_{\mathsf{meas}}) pins measurement quantization.
(\mathsf{ObsReq}) binds required observables.
(\mathsf{SpecReq}) binds required convention components for valid evaluation.
(\mathsf{App}) is applicability predicate id (deterministic).
(\mathsf{tol}) is tolerance bound in quantized units (scalar or vector).
(\oplus_{\mathsf{inv}}) is the accumulation operator id for tolerance ledgers.
F.2.1(b) Invariant clause well-formedness.A clause is well-formed iff:
evaluator id resolves and is deterministic under pinned numeric and ordering policies,
norm id resolves and norm computation is deterministic in integer arithmetic under pinned reduction rules,
quantization policy resolves and is consistent with evaluator outputs,
applicability predicate resolves and is deterministic,
tolerance encoding is canonical and nonnegative,
accumulation operator resolves and is associative and monotone.
F.2.1(c) Invariant clause evaluation interface.Each clause defines a measurement function:[\mathsf{Meas}{\mathsf{inv}}(x):=\mathsf{Quant}{Q_{\mathsf{meas}}}(\mathsf{Eval}(x))\in\mathbb{Z}^{k},]and a drift predicate for comparing input/output:[|\mathsf{Meas}{\mathsf{inv}}(x)-\mathsf{Meas}{\mathsf{inv}}(y)|_{\mathsf{Norm}} \le \mathsf{tol}.]All computations are deterministic and quantized.
F.2.1(d) Clause admission to contracts.A contract may include a clause only if:
the clause’s required observables are available in the involved lenses (or explicit measurement transforms are included in composite implementations),
the clause’s required spec components match the contract’s (\mathsf{SpecIn}) and (\mathsf{SpecOut}) in the declared way (preserved or mapped by explicit bridge semantics),
and the clause’s tolerance is compatible with the contract’s tolerance profile vector and corridor tolerance budgets when used under a given (\Omega).
F.2.2 Applicability predicates and domain coupling
F.2.2(a) Applicability predicate object.Applicability is a deterministic predicate object:[\mathsf{AppPred}:=(\mathsf{pred_id},h(\mathsf{Inputs}),h(\mathsf{Rule}),h(\mathsf{DomainSpec})?,h(\mathsf{SpecReq})?),]where (\mathsf{Rule}) references a deterministic predicate program. Applicability may depend on:
object schema fields,
parameter values (by hash),
domain constraints (DomainSpec),
or spec fingerprints.
F.2.2(b) Domain coupling constraints.If applicability depends on domain properties (e.g., “only for normalized distributions,” “only for bandlimited signals,” “only for modulus prime”), then the domain predicate must be explicit and pinned. Any transform relying on domain coupling must either:
require the domain predicate as a guard in the contract (precondition), or
include a domain-enforcing projection step (explicit transform) in composite implementations.
F.2.2(c) Applicability determinism.Applicability predicates must be referentially transparent. They may depend only on:
the canonical bytes of objects and parameters referenced by hash,
pinned policy objects,
and deterministic computations.Dependence on ambient state (time, randomness, unordered iteration) is illegal.
F.2.2(d) Applicability evidence (optional).When required by (\Omega), steps may emit applicability evidence objects binding:
which invariants were applicable,
which invariants were skipped,
and the predicate ids used.This evidence supports auditability and prevents silent omission of required invariants.
F.2.3 Tolerance operator references and accumulation metadata
F.2.3(a) Tolerance profile alignment.Each contract includes a tolerance profile (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathsf{LT}}) aligned to its invariant index (\mathsf{InvIndex}). Alignment requires:
same invariant index object hash for both (\mathcal I_{\mathsf{LT}}) and (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathsf{LT}}),
tolerance component ordering exactly matches the invariant index ordering,
each tolerance component corresponds to an invariant clause id.
F.2.3(b) Accumulation operator binding.Each invariant clause binds an accumulation operator id (h(\oplus_{\mathsf{inv}})). The contract must also bind a tolerance ledger policy hash (h(\mathsf{TolPol})) in its replay profile or metadata, ensuring that:
accumulation operators used by execution and by routing are identical,
near-threshold parameters (\alpha_I) are pinned and consistent,
any per-invariant coupling to corridor budgets is explicit.
F.2.3(c) Per-step tolerance usage metadata.A contract must declare the step’s tolerance usage vector (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathsf{use}}), which may be:
equal to (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathsf{LT}}) (maximum allowed drift consumption),
or a strictly smaller vector representing actual declared consumption for this transform under default parameters,
or parameter-dependent via pinned deterministic rules (parameter hash determines tolerance usage exactly).
This distinction is necessary for long-chain budgeting: “allowed tolerance” differs from “consumed tolerance” unless the policy explicitly equates them.
F.2.3(d) Consistency checks with measured drift.When required by (\Omega), a transform must emit evidence comparing measured drift (d_I) to tolerance bounds (\varepsilon_I). A mismatch between declared tolerance usage and observed drift (e.g., observed drift exceeding declared tolerance) is a defect. Conversely, observed drift below tolerance does not reduce cumulative tolerance usage unless a certified projection or bound reduction rule is explicitly provided; tolerance usage is not automatically refundable.
F.2.4 Loss/quotient clause integration and honesty fields
F.2.4(a) Loss profile object.A lossy or quotient-bearing contract must include (h(\mathsf{Loss})) referencing:[\mathsf{Loss}:=(h(\mathsf{EqRel}),h(\mathsf{QuotMap}),h(\mathsf{ObsKeep}),h(\mathsf{InvKeep}),h(\mathsf{InvDrop}),\mathsf{invTier},h(\mathsf{CanonRep}),h(\mathsf{ProofReq})?).]
(\mathsf{EqRel}) defines the equivalence relation ((\approx)) in the quotient.
(\mathsf{QuotMap}) defines the quotient map (q).
(\mathsf{ObsKeep}) declares preserved observables.
(\mathsf{InvKeep}) and (\mathsf{InvDrop}) partition invariants.
(\mathsf{invTier}) is invertibility tier (exact/approx/quotient-only/non-invertible).
(\mathsf{CanonRep}) defines canonical representative selection rules in the quotient.
(\mathsf{ProofReq}) optionally binds required proofs for honesty and quotient-correctness.
F.2.4(b) Honesty constraints.If (h(\mathsf{Loss})) is present:
The contract must not claim invertibility beyond its (\mathsf{invTier}).
The contract must not claim preservation of invariants in (\mathsf{InvDrop}).
Any proof claims must be interpreted in the quotient space where appropriate; claims in the original space must be explicitly forbidden unless a recovery transform exists and is certified.
F.2.4(c) Composition coherence fields.Loss profiles must include composition metadata sufficient for routing and repair:
how quotient relations compose,
whether quotient equivalence is transitive under the declared checker regime (verification level bound),
how canonical representatives are chosen under composition.If composition coherence cannot be certified, the contract must restrict composability by declaring a composition guard predicate that prevents unsafe chaining.
F.2.4(d) Recovery hooks (optional).If the contract claims partial recovery (e.g., invertible on a subspace), it must bind:
a domain restriction predicate (h(\mathsf{Dom}^\star)),
a recovery transform contract id (h(\mathsf{RecLT})),
and proof objects certifying recovery bounds.Recovery is never assumed; it must be explicitly present or omitted.
F.3 Budget and scope profiles
F.3.1 Budget vector schema (compute/write/branch/tolerance maxima)
F.3.1(a) Budget profile object.A budget profile is a COS object:[\mathcal{B}{\mathsf{LT}}:=(h(\mathsf{CostClass}),b{\mathsf{comp}},h(\mathsf{WriteBud}),b_{\mathsf{branch}},b_{\lambda},h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\max}),h(\mathsf{RateLim})?).]Fields:
(b_{\mathsf{comp}}) is compute cap in quantized compute units.
(\mathsf{WriteBud}) maps target classes to quantized write caps.
(b_{\mathsf{branch}}) is branch cap for candidate generation within the transform.
(b_{\lambda}) is expansion cap for internal deepening.
(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\max}) is maximum tolerance usage vector permitted for this transform (may match (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathsf{LT}}) or be looser; any looseness must be justified by contract semantics and corridor constraints).
(\mathsf{RateLim}) optionally binds rate-limit rules for repeated invocation contexts.
F.3.1(b) Write budget taxonomy.Write budgets are keyed by the target class enum:[\mathsf{Target}\in{\mathsf{COMMIT},\mathsf{REL},\mathsf{CAND},\mathsf{PROOF},\mathsf{PLAN},\mathsf{RECEIPT},\mathsf{INDEX}}.]Each target class budget is a quantized scalar in a pinned unit (bytes, records, or weighted write units) specified by the write budget policy object.
F.3.1(c) Budget feasibility predicate.Budget feasibility is evaluated by the projector using the pinned cost model:[\mathbf{c}\preceq \mathcal{B}_{\mathsf{LT}} \ \land\ \mathbf{c}\preceq \Omega,]where (\mathbf{c}) is the observed per-step cost vector (compute/write/branch/tolerance). Any exceedance is illegal unless a budget justification witness is present and admissible under both the contract and (\Omega).
F.3.1(d) Budget determinism binding.The budget profile binds to:
cost class id hash (h(\mathsf{CostClass})),
determinism requirements for how costs are computed (ordering, measurement),ensuring replay verification can recompute costs where mandated. Any mismatch in cost model hashes invalidates budget evidence and may invalidate receipts when deterministic cost accounting is part of the claim boundary.
F.3.2 Scope target taxonomy and namespace controls
F.3.2(a) Scope profile object.A scope profile is a COS object:[\mathcal{S}_{\mathsf{LT}}:=(\mathsf{primTarget},h(\mathsf{Allow}),h(\mathsf{Deny}),h(\mathsf{NSAllow}),h(\mathsf{NSDeny}),h(\mathsf{SecRules}),h(\mathsf{AuthReq})).]Fields:
(\mathsf{primTarget}\in\mathsf{Target}) is the primary target class.
(\mathsf{Allow}) and (\mathsf{Deny}) are sets of allowed/denied target classes.
(\mathsf{NSAllow}) and (\mathsf{NSDeny}) specify allowed/denied namespace prefixes for commitment keys and index keys.
(\mathsf{SecRules}) defines secondary write rules (Section F.3.3).
(\mathsf{AuthReq}) binds required authorization witness types/methods for scope extensions.
F.3.2(b) Namespace controls.Namespace controls are evaluated on commitment keys and alias keys. Controls require:
keys resolve to namespaces under canonical namespace partitioning (Appendix A),
the namespace’s policy permits the requested operation (add/update/supersede),
contradiction policy requirements are met for contradiction-controlled namespaces,
authorization requirements (if any) are satisfied by witnesses.
The scope verifier must reject any write to a denied namespace.
F.3.2(c) Read vs write scope.Scope profiles may optionally include read constraints for privacy or governance. Read constraints are enforced by source root inclusion rules and by policy restrictions: if a transform reads from a forbidden namespace, the step is illegal. Read scope is optional and must be explicit if used.
F.3.2(d) Isolation sets (repair and governance).For repair and governance transforms, scope profiles must include explicit isolation sets restricting permissible writes to a bounded subset of keys/edges. Isolation sets are typed objects and are enforced by projector scope checks. Any attempt to write outside isolation is a hard failure.
F.3.3 Primary vs secondary write declarations and gating fields
F.3.3(a) Primary write semantics.Primary writes are writes to (\mathsf{primTarget}) and to allowed primary namespaces in (\mathsf{NSAllow}). Primary writes are permitted when:
the contract’s witness profile includes the required primary authorization witness type (e.g., (\mathsf{COMMIT}) for commitment writes),
and the corridor policy permits the write under budgets and contradiction policies.
F.3.3(b) Secondary write rules.Secondary writes are any writes to targets or namespaces not designated primary. Secondary writes are permitted only when:
(\mathsf{SecRules}) permits the target/namespace as secondary,
a scope-extension witness (\mathsf{SCOPE}) is present authorizing the specific secondary write set,
the authorization binds to the same step context and enumerates the authorized key/address range or explicit list.
Secondary write authorization must be granular; blanket “allow all secondary writes” is illegal unless the corridor policy explicitly permits a fixed wildcard class pinned by hash.
F.3.3(c) Gating by witness methods.(\mathsf{AuthReq}) binds which witness types/method ids authorize secondary writes (e.g., consistency proofs for overwriting contradiction-controlled keys, budget proofs for escalation). The contract must not weaken (\Omega): even if the contract allows a secondary write, (\Omega) may still forbid it.
F.3.3(d) Deterministic omission policy.If (\Omega) permits partial acceptance by omitting unauthorized secondary writes, the contract must declare whether such omissions are permissible for this transform kind. If omissions are permitted, the acceptance mask must record omitted operations deterministically. If omissions are not permitted (default for commitment writes), unauthorized secondary write attempts cause reject+revert.
F.3.4 Escalation hooks (budget justification bindings)
F.3.4(a) Escalation allowance fields.A contract may permit escalation only by explicit fields:[\mathsf{Esc}:=(h(\mathsf{EscPol}),h(\mathsf{Reasons}),h(\mathsf{MaxOverrides}),h(\mathsf{ProofReq})).]
(\mathsf{EscPol}) binds the escalation policy (step-local or windowed).
(\mathsf{Reasons}) is a set of permitted reason codes.
(\mathsf{MaxOverrides}) defines maxima for compute/write/branch/tolerance overrides.
(\mathsf{ProofReq}) binds required proof methods for budget justification.
If (\mathsf{Esc}) is absent, the transform forbids escalation beyond its declared budget profile regardless of corridor allowance.
F.3.4(b) Budget witness binding requirement.Any escalation must be accompanied by a (\mathsf{BUDGET}) witness whose payload:
specifies requested overrides,
specifies reason code,
references a proof object whose method id is permitted,
binds to the step context and delta root.
Verifiers must check that the requested override covers the step’s cost vector and does not exceed maxima.
F.3.4(c) Escalation non-scope rule.Budget escalation never expands scope. A budget witness cannot authorize new write targets or namespaces; scope is controlled only by scope witnesses and scope profile rules. Any contract that attempts to conflate budget and scope authorization is invalid.
F.3.4(d) Escalation audit artifacts.If escalation occurs, the step must emit escalation evidence binding:
original budget profile hash,
requested override object hash,
proof hash and reason code,
affected step indices if windowed.This evidence is required for audit and for deterministic repair planning when escalations become frequent.
F.4 Replay profiles and admission packets
F.4.1 Canonical parameter schema binding and parameter hash rules
F.4.1(a) Parameter schema object.A replay profile binds a parameter schema hash:[\mathsf{ParamSchema}:=(h(\mathsf{Schema}),h(\mathsf{Defaults})?,h(\mathsf{DeriveRules})?,h(\mathsf{CompatRules})?),]specifying required parameter fields, default object references, deterministic derivations, and compatibility rules.
F.4.1(b) Parameter hashing rule.Parameter hashing is defined by a pinned rule (\mathsf{ParamHashPol}) requiring:
canonical key ordering (lexicographic),
canonical value encoding (hash references for objects; quantized integers for numerics; pinned enum encodings),
explicit inclusion of any policy-dependent values (spec hashes, quantization policy hashes) that affect execution,
prohibition of implicit defaults (defaults must be explicit references and must appear in the canonical parameter bytes).
The parameter hash is:[h(P)=H(\mathsf{Enc}(P)).]
F.4.1(c) Parameter domain enforcement.Replay profiles may bind DomainSpec hashes indicating admissible parameter domains. Parameter domain violations must produce deterministic typed errors and are not permitted to be silently clamped unless an explicit projection transform is included and certified.
F.4.1(d) Parameter determinism obligations.Replay requires that identical (h(P)) yields identical behavior. Therefore the replay profile must forbid any parameter-dependent behavior not captured by (h(P)). Any hidden parameter (ambient setting) is illegal and must be treated as a determinism defect.
F.4.2 Deterministic ordering commitments (delta/witness/evidence)
F.4.2(a) Ordering policy object.A replay profile binds an ordering policy object:[\mathsf{OrderPol}:=(h(\mathsf{DeltaOrder}),h(\mathsf{WitnessOrder}),h(\mathsf{EvidenceOrder}),h(\mathsf{CandComp}),h(\mathsf{TieBreak}),h(\mathsf{MerkleLeafOrder})).]Each component references pinned ordering rules:
delta operation ordering within each store,
witness ordering,
evidence ordering,
candidate comparator and truncation tie-break,
route/optimization tie-breaks when applicable,
Merkle leaf ordering (sorted vs sequence-like, pinned).
F.4.2(b) Canonical delta order commitments.Delta ordering rules must specify:
primary sort keys (operation kind, target key/address),
secondary sort keys (field values),
and normalization rules (e.g., canonical encoding of keys prior to comparison).Order must be total and deterministic. Any transform producing deltas must canonicalize deltas before hashing; replay verification relies on these commitments.
F.4.2(c) Canonical witness and evidence order commitments.Witness ordering and evidence ordering are set-like orderings by pinned comparator rules (typically (type, hash)). Evidence ordering must be deterministic to ensure evidence roots and evidence list hashes are stable across implementations.
F.4.2(d) Deterministic selection policies.Any selection step (candidate truncation, route tie-break, rewrite tie-break) must be determined by pinned comparators and pinned tie-break rules. Replay profiles must include these rules; otherwise selection behavior is not reproducible and the transform is inadmissible for committed execution.
F.4.3 Compatibility hashes (canon/objstd/verset/policy class)
F.4.3(a) Runtime header compatibility.A contract intended for admission must bind required runtime header hashes:[\mathsf{ReqHdr}:=(h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h_{\mathsf{CostModel}},h(\mathsf{MerklePol}),h(\mathsf{HashPol})).]Execution is permitted only when the runtime header matches these hashes exactly, unless a policy migration mechanism exists and is executed as a separate plan producing a receipt that the execution consumes.
F.4.3(b) Corridor class compatibility.Contracts may bind a minimal corridor class constraint (h(\Omega_{\min})) defining minimal strictness required (e.g., required determinism mode, required contradiction policy, required evidence retention class). A runtime corridor (\Omega) is compatible iff (\Omega\succeq \Omega_{\min}) under a pinned corridor preorder. Weaker corridors are incompatible.
F.4.3(c) Verifier support compatibility.Contracts bind witness profile method ids; compatibility requires those method ids exist in (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}) and support required verification levels. If method ids are absent or deprecated, the contract cannot execute under that runtime header and is infeasible.
F.4.3(d) Convention compatibility commitments.Compatibility requires that (\mathsf{SpecIn}) and (\mathsf{SpecOut}) be satisfiable in the library environment: required bridge classes must exist when (\mathsf{SpecIn}/\mathsf{SpecOut}) imply convention changes, and required normalizers must exist when mandatory insertion points are specified. Admission packets must bind these dependencies.
F.4.4 Admission packet schema (ref plan, ref receipt, tests, claims)
F.4.4(a) Admission packet object.An admission packet is a COS object:[\mathsf{Admit}:=(h(\mathsf{LT}),\mathsf{entry_id}?,\mathsf{admit_ver},h(\mathsf{CompatVec}),h(\mathsf{RefPlan}),h(\mathsf{RefRcpt}),h(\mathsf{Tests}),h(\mathsf{Claims}),h(\mathsf{Deps}),h(\mathsf{Govern})?,h(\mathsf{Supersede})?,h(\mathsf{Deprecate})?).]Fields:
(h(\mathsf{LT})) binds the contract.
(\mathsf{entry_id}) optionally binds a library entry id that this contract occupies.
(h(\mathsf{CompatVec})) binds required runtime header and corridor class compatibility.
(h(\mathsf{RefPlan})) is a reference plan that instantiates and/or validates the contract under canonical parameters.
(h(\mathsf{RefRcpt})) is a verified receipt for executing (\mathsf{RefPlan}).
(h(\mathsf{Tests})) binds test bases, checker programs, and disclosure requirements used for admission checks.
(h(\mathsf{Claims})) binds invariant set, tolerance profile, verification level, and loss profile (if any) that define the contract’s claim boundary.
(h(\mathsf{Deps})) binds dependency closure: referenced lenses, specs, bridges, normalizers, proof methods, macros, and auxiliary policies.
Governance/supersession/deprecation fields are optional and used by library management.
F.4.4(b) Admission validation predicate.An admission packet is valid iff:
(h(\mathsf{LT})) resolves and (\mathsf{WF}(\mathsf{LT})=\mathsf{true}),
compatibility vector is consistent with the contract’s replay profile and witness profile,
reference plan resolves and is well-formed and targets validation of the contract (e.g., executes the transform on canonical test inputs and emits required proofs),
reference receipt verifies and binds to the reference plan hash, ledger header hash, and required policy hashes,
tests bundle is consistent with claims and with method registry (proof methods exist and verification levels supported),
dependency closure resolves and is internally consistent.
Admission validation must be deterministic; failures produce deterministic evidence objects.
F.4.4(c) Admission guarantees and boundaries.Admission guarantees only:
well-formedness under COS,
executability under the kernel and corridor class,
verifier support for required witnesses,
correctness of admission tests at declared verification level and claim boundary,
compatibility commitments enabling routing inclusion.
Admission does not guarantee universal truth beyond claim boundaries. Any stronger guarantees require stronger proof methods and explicit mechanized certificates.
F.4.4(d) Supersession and deprecation in admission packets.Admission packets may include:
supersession record (h(\mathsf{Supersede})) binding old contract/entry ids and refinement proofs,
deprecation record (h(\mathsf{Deprecate})) binding deprecation reason codes and replacement suggestions,
governance binding (h(\mathsf{Govern})) specifying authorization constraints for pinning the entry into protected namespaces.
Supersession requires refinement proofs; deprecation does not imply refinement. Both are explicit and auditable.
APPENDIX F — LEGAL TRANSFORM CONTRACT TEMPLATES AND ADMISSION PACKETS
APPENDIX G — KERNEL PRIMITIVES, DELTA ALGEBRA, AND STATE HASHING
This appendix specifies the kernel-level primitives, the delta algebra used to represent state transitions, and the canonical hashing rules that define state identity, checkpoint identity, and replay equivalence. All definitions are given as typed, canonically encoded objects; all semantics are deterministic under pinned policies. The kernel’s observable behavior is entirely determined by (i) the pre-state hash, (ii) the canonical delta commitments, (iii) the acceptance mask, (iv) the witness set, and (v) the pinned corridor and verifier policies.
G.1 Primitive operation taxonomy
Let the runtime state be a tuple of stores:[\Psi := (\mathsf{CommitStore},\mathsf{RelStore},\mathsf{CandStore},\mathsf{ProofStore},\mathsf{LinkIndex},\mathsf{Hdr},\mathsf{LedHdr},\mathsf{TolLedger},\mathsf{step_index}),]with (\mathsf{Hdr}) committing (h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h_{\mathsf{CostModel}}). Each primitive operation is a typed record whose canonical encoding is fixed by schema. Primitives are partitioned by target store.
G.1.1 Commitment primitives (pin/add/update/check/conflict)
G.1.1(a) Commitment store record types.A commitment key (pin id) is a canonical key (\mathsf{PinId}) (Appendix A). A pin record is:[\mathsf{PinRecord}:=(\mathsf{pin_id},\mathsf{pin_type},h_{\mathsf{value}},h_{\mathsf{prov}},h_{\mathsf{meta}}?),]where:
(\mathsf{pin_type}) is a pinned enum identifying the record class,
(h_{\mathsf{value}}) is a hash of a COS object stored in the proof store,
(h_{\mathsf{prov}}) is the provenance witness hash for the step that created/updated the record,
(h_{\mathsf{meta}}) is an optional metadata object hash.
The commitment store is a total map from (\mathsf{PinId}) to (\mathsf{PinRecord}) for existing pins.
G.1.1(b) Primitive operation records.Commitment primitives are represented as operation records with (\mathsf{op_kind}\in{\mathsf{PIN_ADD},\mathsf{PIN_UPDATE}}) plus read predicates used by verifiers ((\mathsf{PIN_CHECK},\mathsf{PIN_CONFLICT})). Write primitives appear in deltas; read predicates do not.
PinAdd[\mathsf{PinAdd}:=(\mathsf{PIN_ADD},\mathsf{pin_id},\mathsf{pin_type},h_{\mathsf{value}},h_{\mathsf{meta}}?).]
PinUpdate[\mathsf{PinUpdate}:=(\mathsf{PIN_UPDATE},\mathsf{pin_id},h'{\mathsf{value}},h'{\mathsf{meta}}?).]
PinCheck (read predicate)[\mathsf{PinCheck}(\mathsf{pin_id})\to (\mathsf{exists},\mathsf{PinRecord}?).]
PinConflict (read predicate)[\mathsf{PinConflict}(\mathsf{pin_id},\mathsf{candidate})\to{\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}},]where (\mathsf{candidate}) is a canonical candidate pin record payload (pin type, value hash, metadata hash) without provenance.
G.1.1(c) Applicability and well-formedness.A commitment write operation is well-formed iff:
(\mathsf{pin_id}) is canonical and resolves to a namespace whose policy permits the operation kind,
(h_{\mathsf{value}}) (or (h'_{\mathsf{value}})) resolves to a well-formed COS object, and its schema matches any namespace-required schema,
optional metadata hash resolves if present and matches required metadata schema,
all fields are canonically encoded.
Applicability predicates:
(\mathsf{PIN_ADD}) is applicable only when (\mathsf{pin_id}\notin\mathsf{CommitStore}) unless a contradiction policy explicitly permits add-as-disambiguation (which must be represented as a separate disambiguation transform producing a different key).
(\mathsf{PIN_UPDATE}) is applicable only when (\mathsf{pin_id}\in\mathsf{CommitStore}).
G.1.1(d) Deterministic semantics.Let (\mathsf{ApplyCommitOp}(\mathsf{CommitStore},\mathsf{op},h_{\mathsf{prov}})\to \mathsf{CommitStore}'\cup\mathsf{Err}) be the semantic function used by the projector when applying accepted deltas. Semantics:
For (\mathsf{PIN_ADD}), create (\mathsf{PinRecord}) with the step’s provenance witness hash (h_{\mathsf{prov}}) and insert at key (\mathsf{pin_id}). If key exists, return (\mathsf{Err}) unless policy and accepted consistency witness authorize disambiguation/overwrite; overwrite is handled by (\mathsf{PIN_UPDATE}) plus an explicit consistency witness.
For (\mathsf{PIN_UPDATE}), replace the record’s value/meta fields and set provenance hash to (h_{\mathsf{prov}}). If key absent, return (\mathsf{Err}).
No delete primitive exists. Any “removal” is represented by supersession or deprecation records as pinned artifacts under explicit namespace policies.
G.1.2 Relation primitives (edge/add/update/query/type policy)
G.1.2(a) Relation store record types.A relation store is a labeled directed multigraph. A node id (\mathsf{NodeId}) is canonical bytes. An edge record is:[\mathsf{EdgeRec}:=(\mathsf{edge_id},\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst},\mathsf{etype},w_q,h_{\mathsf{prov}},h_{\mathsf{meta}}?),]where (\mathsf{etype}) is a pinned enum, (w_q) is a quantized integer weight, and (h_{\mathsf{prov}}) is step provenance witness hash.
G.1.2(b) Primitive operation records.Relation write primitives:
EdgeAdd[\mathsf{EdgeAdd}:=(\mathsf{EDGE_ADD},\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst},\mathsf{etype},w_q,h_{\mathsf{meta}}?).]
EdgeUpdate[\mathsf{EdgeUpdate}:=(\mathsf{EDGE_UPDATE},\mathsf{edge_id},w'q,h'{\mathsf{meta}}?).]
Read predicates:
EdgeQuery[\mathsf{EdgeQuery}(\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst},\mathsf{etype})\to [\mathsf{edge_id}]^\ast,]returned in canonical order.
EdgeTypePolicy[\mathsf{EdgeTypePolicy}(\mathsf{etype})\to (\mathsf{bounds},\mathsf{mergeCapable},\mathsf{cycleRules},h(\mathsf{ReqProof})?),]pinned by policy hash.
G.1.2(c) Applicability and well-formedness.An edge op is well-formed iff:
node ids are canonical,
edge type is valid,
weight satisfies quantization and bounds for the edge type,
metadata schema matches edge type policy if present,
endpoints exist when policy requires total endpoint validity (default: endpoints must exist; otherwise edge add is illegal unless a node-creation mechanism is explicit and authorized).
Edge update is applicable only when (\mathsf{edge_id}) exists.
G.1.2(d) Deterministic semantics.(\mathsf{ApplyRelOp}(\mathsf{RelStore},\mathsf{op},h_{\mathsf{prov}})\to \mathsf{RelStore}'\cup\mathsf{Err}):
(\mathsf{EDGE_ADD}): allocate a deterministic edge id (\mathsf{edge_id}:=H(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst},\mathsf{etype},w_q,h_{\mathsf{meta}}?)|h_{\mathsf{prov}})) under a pinned edge-id policy, create (\mathsf{EdgeRec}), insert. Allocation policy must be pinned; alternatively, (\mathsf{edge_id}) may be computed without (h_{\mathsf{prov}}) if edges are intended to be content-addressed independent of provenance; the choice is pinned by (\Omega) and the relation schema.
(\mathsf{EDGE_UPDATE}): update weight/meta and set provenance hash.
Merge-capable edge types require additional proofs; absent valid merge/consistency witnesses, (\mathsf{ApplyRelOp}) must return (\mathsf{Err}) (projector rejects).
G.1.3 Candidate primitives (add/prune/sort/truncate/lineage)
G.1.3(a) Candidate store record types.A candidate record is:[\mathsf{CandRec}:=(h_c,\mathbf{s}q,h{\mathsf{prov}},h(\mathsf{Lineage})?),]where:
(h_c) is the candidate object hash,
(\mathbf{s}_q\in\mathbb{Z}^{k}) is a quantized score vector (fixed dimension pinned by scoring policy),
(h_{\mathsf{prov}}) is step provenance witness hash,
lineage is optional and binds derivation from prior candidate sets.
Candidate store semantics treat candidates as an ordered multiset under a pinned comparator.
G.1.3(b) Primitive operation records.Candidate write primitives:
CandAdd[\mathsf{CandAdd}:=(\mathsf{CAND_ADD},h_c,\mathbf{s}_q,h(\mathsf{Lineage})?).]
CandPrune[\mathsf{CandPrune}:=(\mathsf{CAND_PRUNE},h_c,h(\mathsf{Reason})?),]where (\mathsf{Reason}) is an optional evidence/proof reference.
CandSort[\mathsf{CandSort}:=(\mathsf{CAND_SORT},h(\mathsf{Comparator})).]
CandTruncate[\mathsf{CandTruncate}:=(\mathsf{CAND_TRUNCATE},b_{\mathsf{branch}},h(\mathsf{Comparator}),h(\mathsf{TieBreak})).]
CandLineage (auxiliary)[\mathsf{Lineage}:=(h(\mathsf{CandSetIn})?,h(\mathsf{Rule}),h(\mathsf{Inputs}),h(\mathsf{Evidence})?),]stored in the proof store and referenced by CandAdd.
G.1.3(c) Comparator and tie-break rule.The candidate comparator is pinned:[(h_c,\mathbf{s}_q)\prec(h'_c,\mathbf{s}'_q)\iff \mathbf{s}q \succ{\mathrm{lex}} \mathbf{s}'_q\ \lor\ (\mathbf{s}_q=\mathbf{s}'_q\ \land\ h_c < h'_c),]unless a different comparator is specified by policy hash. Comparator identity is an object hash and must be included in any sort/truncate operations.
G.1.3(d) Deterministic semantics.(\mathsf{ApplyCandDelta}) applies operations in canonical delta order (Section G.2) and enforces:
candidate admissibility: candidate hash resolves to a well-formed object of an allowed candidate type class,
score vector dimension and quantization policy correctness,
deterministic sorting and truncation by pinned comparator,
branch cap enforcement: after applying all adds/prunes, if size exceeds cap, apply deterministic truncation; truncation mask must be computed and committed in the acceptance mask/evidence when required.
Candidate pruning is permitted only if policy permits and required proofs exist when pruning affects ambiguity sets or required alternatives.
G.1.4 Proof primitives (add/link/verify/prune/redact marker)
G.1.4(a) Proof store record types.The proof store is a content-addressable map from object hash to canonical object bytes:[\mathsf{ProofStore}:\ \mathsf{ObjHash}\to (\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b}).]A link index maps object hash to a canonical list of referenced object hashes:[\mathsf{LinkIndex}:\ \mathsf{ObjHash}\to [\mathsf{ObjHash}]^\ast.]
G.1.4(b) Primitive operation records.Proof write primitives:
ObjAdd[\mathsf{ObjAdd}:=(\mathsf{OBJ_ADD},\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b}),]with implied object hash (h=H(\mathsf{DS}(\mathsf{TypeId})|\mathbf{b})).
ObjLink[\mathsf{ObjLink}:=(\mathsf{OBJ_LINK},h,[h_1,\ldots,h_k]),]where the list is canonically ordered and each referenced hash must resolve or be introduced in the same step under accepted proof delta.
ObjVerifyLocal[\mathsf{ObjVerifyLocal}:=(\mathsf{OBJ_VERIFY},h,\mathsf{method_id},h(\mathsf{LCD})).]
ObjPrune[\mathsf{ObjPrune}:=(\mathsf{OBJ_PRUNE},h,h(\mathsf{Just})?),]permitted only if explicitly allowed by (\Omega) and justified by an admissible dominance/equivalence proof.
RedactMarker[\mathsf{RedactMarker}:=(\mathsf{REDACT},h,\mathsf{TypeId},h(\mathsf{RedactPol})),]used to publish typed commitments to withheld bytes under explicit disclosure policies.
G.1.4(c) Applicability and well-formedness.ObjAdd is well-formed iff:
(\mathbf{b}) validates under (\mathsf{TypeId}),
hash recomputation matches implied hash (the operation is rejected if the implied hash does not match stored key policy).
ObjLink is well-formed iff:
(h) exists or is introduced in this step,
all references exist or are introduced in this step,
reference list is canonical (sorted or sequence-like as schema requires).
ObjVerifyLocal is well-formed iff method id exists in verifier set and LCD resolves and is schema-valid.
ObjPrune is well-formed only with justification and policy permission.
G.1.4(d) Deterministic semantics.(\mathsf{ApplyProofOp}) updates the proof store and link index deterministically:
ObjAdd inserts object bytes by hash; if object already exists with identical bytes, insertion is idempotent; if object exists with different bytes under same hash, this violates hash correctness and is illegal.
ObjLink sets the link list for (h) to the canonical list; updates require that link index policy allows updates; otherwise it is illegal.
ObjVerifyLocal produces a verification evidence object (or verifier witness) but does not mutate proof store unless verification logs are explicitly written as proof objects; any such write must be represented as an additional ObjAdd of an evidence object.
ObjPrune may remove objects only if doing so does not break reachability from pinned commitments and receipts under the pinned GC policy; otherwise it is illegal.
G.2 Delta encoding and ordering
A delta is a finite sequence of primitive write operations for a specific store. A delta bundle is the 4-tuple:[\Delta := (\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}},\Delta_{\mathsf{rel}},\Delta_{\mathsf{cand}},\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}}).]Each component delta is encoded canonically and hashed; the delta root is a Merkle commitment to the per-store delta hashes.
G.2.1 Delta record schemas per substrate
G.2.1(a) Delta objects.Each per-store delta is a COS object:[\Delta_{\bullet}:=(\mathsf{delta_ver},\ [\mathsf{Op}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{Op}_m],\ h(\mathsf{OrderPol}),\ h(\mathsf{Schema})?),]where:
(\mathsf{delta_ver}) is a pinned version tuple for delta schemas,
the op list is sequence-like but must be in canonical within-delta order,
(h(\mathsf{OrderPol})) binds the ordering policy used to canonicalize the op list,
(h(\mathsf{Schema})) is optional and binds store-specific delta schema hash when multiple variants exist.
G.2.1(b) Commitment delta schema.(\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}}) op list contains only (\mathsf{PinAdd}) and (\mathsf{PinUpdate}) records.
G.2.1(c) Relation delta schema.(\Delta_{\mathsf{rel}}) op list contains only (\mathsf{EdgeAdd}) and (\mathsf{EdgeUpdate}) records.
G.2.1(d) Candidate and proof delta schemas.(\Delta_{\mathsf{cand}}) op list contains (\mathsf{CandAdd},\mathsf{CandPrune},\mathsf{CandSort},\mathsf{CandTruncate}).(\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}}) op list contains (\mathsf{ObjAdd},\mathsf{ObjLink}) and optional audit objects (RedactMarker) as permitted by policy.
Verification operations ((\mathsf{OBJ_VERIFY})) do not mutate state and thus do not appear as deltas; their outcomes appear as evidence objects stored via ObjAdd.
G.2.2 Canonical within-delta ordering rules
G.2.2(a) Ordering policy.An ordering policy object (\mathsf{DeltaOrderPol}\bullet) defines a deterministic total order for operations in (\Delta\bullet). The order is computed from canonical op encodings and pinned sort keys.
G.2.2(b) Commitment delta ordering.Commitment operations are sorted by:
(\mathsf{pin_id}) lexicographic on canonical key bytes,
operation kind order (\mathsf{PIN_ADD}<\mathsf{PIN_UPDATE}),
(h_{\mathsf{value}}) bytes lexicographic,
metadata hash bytes (empty marker sorts before non-empty),
full canonical op bytes tie-break.
This ordering is required to make delta hashes stable.
G.2.2(c) Relation delta ordering.Relation operations are sorted by:
(\mathsf{etype}) enum order,
(\mathsf{src}) bytes, then (\mathsf{dst}) bytes,
operation kind (\mathsf{EDGE_ADD}<\mathsf{EDGE_UPDATE}),
(\mathsf{edge_id}) for updates (empty marker sorts before non-empty for adds),
weight bytes,
full op bytes tie-break.
G.2.2(d) Candidate and proof delta ordering.Candidate operations are sorted by:
operation kind order (\mathsf{CAND_ADD}<\mathsf{CAND_PRUNE}<\mathsf{CAND_SORT}<\mathsf{CAND_TRUNCATE}),
candidate hash bytes for add/prune,
score vector bytes for add,
comparator hash bytes and cap value for sort/truncate,
full op bytes tie-break.
Proof operations are sorted by:
operation kind (\mathsf{OBJ_ADD}<\mathsf{OBJ_LINK}<\mathsf{REDACT}),
object hash bytes (implied for ObjAdd; explicit for ObjLink/Redact),
type id bytes for ObjAdd/Redact,
full op bytes tie-break.
G.2.3 Delta root construction and per-store delta hashes
G.2.3(a) Per-store delta hash.For each store delta (\Delta_\bullet), define:[h(\Delta_\bullet):=H(\mathsf{Enc}(\Delta_\bullet)),]where (\mathsf{Enc}) is COS canonical encoding.
G.2.3(b) Delta root.Define the delta root:[r_\Delta := \mathsf{MerkleRoot}([h(\Delta_{\mathsf{commit}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{rel}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{cand}}),h(\Delta_{\mathsf{proof}})]),]where the leaf order is fixed and pinned. This root is bound into witness step contexts.
G.2.3(c) Empty delta conventions.The empty delta (\varnothing_\bullet) is a canonical delta object with (\mathsf{delta_ver}) present and an empty op list. Its hash is fixed and serves as the canonical identity element for delta root computation. Any omission of a delta component is illegal; all four delta hashes must be committed per step.
G.2.3(d) Delta hash correctness verification.The replay verifier recomputes (h(\Delta_\bullet)) from delta bytes and requires equality with step record commitments. Any mismatch is a determinism failure and is classified as a delta divergence defect.
G.2.4 Acceptance mask encoding and filtering semantics
G.2.4(a) Mask object schema.The acceptance mask is a COS object:[m := (m_{\mathsf{commit}},m_{\mathsf{rel}},m_{\mathsf{cand}},m_{\mathsf{proof}},m_{\mathsf{op}}),]where (m_{\mathsf{store}}\in{0,1}) indicates store-level acceptance, and (m_{\mathsf{op}}) is a store-indexed optional structure encoding within-delta selection.
G.2.4(b) Filtering function.Define deterministic filtering:[\mathsf{Filter}\bullet(\Delta\bullet,m_{\mathsf{op}},\Omega)\to \Delta_\bullet^{(m)},]applied as:
if (m_{\bullet}=0), then (\Delta_\bullet^{(m)}=\varnothing_\bullet),
if (m_{\bullet}=1), then (\Delta_\bullet^{(m)}) is obtained by selecting a subset of operations in canonical order according to (m_{\mathsf{op}}) and policy rules.
G.2.4(c) Candidate truncation mask.If candidate store size exceeds (b_{\mathsf{branch}}), then (m_{\mathsf{op}}) must include a truncation mask binding the retained candidate hashes and their canonical order. The truncation mask is computed deterministically by the pinned comparator; it must be committed in the step record and is replay-derivable.
G.2.4(d) Legality interaction.Masking may discard only those operations classified as discardable by (\Omega) and the ledger plan. Commitment operations in contradiction-controlled namespaces are non-discardable by default; any attempt to mask them without explicit policy permission is illegal. If a hard constraint fails, masking is not used; the verdict is reject+revert.
G.3 State hashing and checkpoint objects
State identity is defined by canonical store encodings and pinned header commitments. Checkpoints are explicit, hash-addressed boundaries of state.
G.3.1 Per-store canonical encodings and hashes
G.3.1(a) Commitment store encoding and hash.Encode (\mathsf{CommitStore}) as a sequence-like list of ((\mathsf{pin_id},\mathsf{PinRecord})) pairs sorted lexicographically by (\mathsf{pin_id}) canonical bytes. Define:[h_{\mathsf{commit}}:=H(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{CommitStore})).]Pin record encodings include provenance hashes; therefore commitment identity incorporates the authorization history as recorded by the ledger.
G.3.1(b) Relation store encoding and hash.Encode (\mathsf{RelStore}) as:
a canonical node set encoding (sorted node ids),
a canonical edge set encoding (sorted by ((\mathsf{etype},\mathsf{src},\mathsf{dst},\mathsf{edge_id}))),
canonical attribute encodings where present.Define:[h_{\mathsf{rel}}:=H(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{RelStore})).]
G.3.1(c) Candidate store encoding and hash.Encode (\mathsf{CandStore}) as the candidate list in canonical comparator order with deduplication rules applied as pinned:[h_{\mathsf{cand}}:=H(\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{CandStore})).]Candidate encoding includes score vectors and provenance hashes.
G.3.1(d) Proof store encoding and hash.The proof store may be large; its canonical commitment is defined as a Merkle root over object entries:
leaf for each stored object is (\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{ObjHash},\mathsf{TypeId},H(\mathbf{b}))) or (\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{ObjHash},H(\mathsf{TypeId}|\mathbf{b}))), under a pinned policy.
