sort leaves by (\mathsf{ObjHash}) bytes, compute Merkle root (r_{\mathsf{proof}}).The proof store hash is:[h_{\mathsf{proof}}:=r_{\mathsf{proof}}.]Link index commitments are included either:
by including a separate Merkle root over ((\mathsf{ObjHash},\mathrm{LinkListHash})) pairs, or
by requiring link lists be derivable from object bytes under a pinned policy.The chosen mechanism is pinned by (h_{\mathsf{Canon}}) and must be consistent across implementations.
G.3.2 Full state hash composition and header binding
G.3.2(a) Runtime header hash.Define runtime header object:[\mathsf{Hdr}:=(h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h_{\mathsf{CostModel}},h(\mathsf{LedFmt})).]Its hash (h(\mathsf{Hdr})) is included in state identity.
G.3.2(b) Full state hash.Define the full state hash:[h(\Psi):=H(\mathsf{Enc}(h(\mathsf{Hdr}),h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),\mathsf{step_index},h_{\mathsf{commit}},h_{\mathsf{rel}},h_{\mathsf{cand}},h_{\mathsf{proof}},h(\mathsf{TolLedger})?)).]If a tolerance ledger is tracked as a first-class object, include its hash; otherwise, include the tolerance ledger state commitment in a fixed field of (\mathsf{Hdr}) or (\mathsf{LedHdr}) under a pinned policy.
G.3.2(c) State hash determinism.Given identical store contents (as canonical encodings) and identical header commitments, independent implementations compute identical store hashes and thus identical full state hashes. Any difference indicates either non-canonical encoding, policy mismatch, or implementation non-conformance.
G.3.2(d) State hash boundaries.State hash commits to:
policy hashes (determinism boundary),
ledger header hash (trace boundary),
store hashes (content boundary),
step index (position boundary).Therefore two states with identical store contents but different ledger headers or step indices are distinct states; this prevents “state reuse” across ledgers without explicit dependency proofs.
G.3.3 Checkpoint schema (store hashes, ledger header, tol-ledger)
G.3.3(a) Checkpoint object.A checkpoint is a COS object:[\mathsf{Chk}:=(h(\mathsf{Hdr}),h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),\mathsf{step_index},h(\Psi),h_{\mathsf{commit}},h_{\mathsf{rel}},h_{\mathsf{cand}},h_{\mathsf{proof}},h(\mathsf{TolLedger})?,h(\mathsf{Meta})?).]
G.3.3(b) Checkpoint well-formedness.A checkpoint is well-formed iff:
(h(\Psi)) recomputes to the committed value from the committed store hashes and header hashes,
the ledger header hash matches the ledger in which the checkpoint appears,
step index matches the ledger position,
tolerance ledger hash matches the tolerance ledger state at that position (when tracked).
G.3.3(c) Checkpoint as restart boundary.Any subsequent plan that begins execution from a checkpoint must bind the checkpoint hash in its context. Execution is legal only if the runtime can load a state whose full hash equals (h(\Psi)) of the checkpoint under the same runtime header hashes.
G.3.3(d) Checkpoint evidence.Policies may require checkpoint evidence objects that prove stability conditions (canonical normal forms, contradiction-free commitments). Such proofs are stored in proof store and may be referenced by checkpoint metadata. These are optional unless (\Omega) mandates stability anchors.
G.3.4 Revert/idempotence conditions and proofs
G.3.4(a) Reject+revert condition.A step verdict (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}) requires:[h_{\mathsf{post}}=h_{\mathsf{pre}},\quad h_{\mathsf{commit}}^{+}=h_{\mathsf{commit}}^{-},\quad h_{\mathsf{rel}}^{+}=h_{\mathsf{rel}}^{-},\quad h_{\mathsf{cand}}^{+}=h_{\mathsf{cand}}^{-},\quad h_{\mathsf{proof}}^{+}=h_{\mathsf{proof}}^{-}.]Replay verification enforces these equalities. Any deviation indicates illegal partial mutation on failure.
G.3.4(b) Projector idempotence on empty transitions.For any state (\Psi), applying empty deltas with no witnesses and zero cost must produce either:
(\mathsf{ACCEPT}) with unchanged state if (\Psi) is stable under (\Omega), or
(\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}) with unchanged state and evidence indicating stability violations (e.g., contradiction present, non-canonical committed object) if policy requires strict stability.This defines stability anchors as fixed points.
G.3.4(c) Normalizer idempotence obligations.Idempotence of normalization transforms is not a kernel property; it is a contract property that must be certified by proof objects. The kernel enforces idempotence only indirectly by:
requiring that normalizers be declared as such and admitted by tests,
verifying their proof witnesses when used in committed plans.
G.3.4(d) Deterministic revert proof obligations.If (\Omega) requires, rejected steps must emit a deterministic failure object whose evidence includes:
earliest failing predicate id,
implicated witness/proof method id (if applicable),
offending delta hash or mask field,
and a binding to the pre-state hash and delta root.This makes revert behavior audit-ready and replay-verifiable.
G.4 Step trace and failure objects
G.4.1 Step descriptor canonical encoding
G.4.1(a) Step descriptor object.A step descriptor is a COS object:[\mathsf{StepDesc}:=(\mathsf{op_id},h(\mathsf{OpRef}),h_P,h_\Gamma,\lambda,h(\mathsf{Plan})?,h(\mathsf{Entry})?),]where:
(\mathsf{op_id}) is a canonical opcode token,
(h(\mathsf{OpRef})) optionally references an entry id, macro id, or kernel opcode descriptor,
(h_P) and (h_\Gamma) bind parameters and context,
(\lambda) is expansion depth,
optional plan and entry references bind the step to a specific plan and specific admitted entry object to prevent alias drift.
G.4.1(b) Step id derivation.A canonical step id may be derived deterministically as:[\mathsf{step_id}:=H(\mathsf{Enc}(h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),\mathsf{step_index},h_{\mathsf{pre}},\mathsf{Enc}(\mathsf{StepDesc}))).]The derivation rule is pinned. Step id may be included in evidence and witnesses to provide a compact identifier for binding.
G.4.1(c) Descriptor validity.A descriptor is valid iff:
all referenced hashes resolve and are well-formed,
(\lambda\le b_\lambda) under (\Omega),
the op reference is admissible under the runtime header (method ids supported, entry admitted).
Invalid descriptors cause deterministic pre-apply failure.
G.4.1(d) Descriptor-to-contract resolution.If (\mathsf{op_id}) references a transform, the descriptor must resolve to exactly one legal transform contract hash under a pinned resolution rule:
either direct by entry id (\to h(\mathsf{LT})),
or via macro expansion (\to) composed program with a bound contract hash.Ambiguous resolution is illegal.
G.4.2 Cost vector schema and accounting commitments
G.4.2(a) Cost vector object.A step cost vector is a COS object:[\mathbf{c}:=(c_{\mathsf{comp}},h(\mathsf{WriteCost}),c_{\mathsf{branch}},h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathsf{use}}),h(\mathsf{CostMeta})?),]where:
(c_{\mathsf{comp}}) is compute cost in quantized units,
(\mathsf{WriteCost}) maps target classes to quantized write costs,
(c_{\mathsf{branch}}) is branch usage count,
(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathsf{use}}) is tolerance usage vector for the step (aligned to invariant index),
optional (\mathsf{CostMeta}) binds cost class ids for detectors and verifiers invoked.
G.4.2(b) Deterministic cost computation rule.Cost computation is pinned by (h_{\mathsf{CostModel}}) and depends deterministically on:
counts and sizes of applied primitive operations,
counts and cost classes of verifier invocations,
candidate generation operations and truncation work,
evidence emission work required by (\Omega).If (h_{\mathsf{CostModel}}) mandates recomputation under replay, the replay verifier recomputes and requires equality with committed cost vector.
G.4.2(c) Budget feasibility coupling.The projector checks feasibility:[\mathbf{c}\preceq \Omega\ \land\ \mathbf{c}\preceq \mathcal{B}_{\mathsf{LT}},]with explicit handling of admissible budget justification witnesses. Any budget justification must bind requested overrides and must be verified before accepting the step.
G.4.2(d) Cost evidence.When a step is near budget thresholds or exceeds budgets (without admissible escalation), deterministic cost evidence objects are emitted binding:
cost vector,
relevant budget caps,
near thresholds,
implicated operations/verifiers contributing most to cost (optional, policy-gated but deterministic if emitted).
G.4.3 Evidence list schema and canonical ordering
G.4.3(a) Evidence object reference list.A step’s evidence list is a set-like list of evidence object hashes:[E_t := {h(E_1),\ldots,h(E_m)}.]The step record commits (h(E_t)), the hash of the canonical encoding of the sorted evidence hash list.
G.4.3(b) Canonical ordering.Evidence hashes are sorted lexicographically by:
evidence class enum order (when evidence hashes are expanded to include type tags; otherwise by hash bytes alone),
evidence code enum order (if included in a structured evidence index object),
hash bytes lexicographic.The ordering policy is pinned; implementations must agree.
G.4.3(c) Evidence retention rules.Evidence retention is enforced by (\Omega). If evidence is mandatory for a detector outcome, omission is a determinism failure. If evidence is optional, omission must be deterministic under pinned rules; otherwise replay diverges.
G.4.3(d) Evidence-to-mask coupling.If partial acceptance occurs due to truncation or omission of discardable operations, evidence must link:
the mask decisions,
the cap or scope rule that triggered omission,
and the discarded operation identifiers.This makes partial acceptance auditable and prevents silent discard.
G.4.4 Deterministic failure object schemas and codes
G.4.4(a) Failure code enum.Define a pinned failure code enum (\mathsf{ErrCode}) containing at minimum:
(\mathsf{E_SCHEMA}): schema invalid / canonical encoding violation,
(\mathsf{E_TYPE}): type mismatch,
(\mathsf{E_MISSING}): missing required referenced hash,
(\mathsf{E_SCOPE}): forbidden write target/namespace or unauthorized secondary write,
(\mathsf{E_BUDGET}): budget infeasible without admissible escalation,
(\mathsf{E_BRANCH}): branch cap violation when truncation not permitted,
(\mathsf{E_DET}): determinism violation (ordering, non-canonical output, policy mismatch),
(\mathsf{E_PROV}): provenance binding failure,
(\mathsf{E_VERIFY}): witness/proof verification failure,
(\mathsf{E_CONTR}): contradiction without admissible resolution,
(\mathsf{E_REPLAY}): replay divergence.
Each code has a required payload schema.
G.4.4(b) Failure object schema.A failure object is a COS object:[\mathsf{Fail}:=(\mathsf{code},h(\mathsf{Ctx}),\mathsf{phase},h(\mathsf{Details}),h(\mathsf{Refs}),h(\mathsf{PolicySet})),]where:
(\mathsf{phase}\in{\mathsf{PREAPPLY},\mathsf{PREPROJECT},\mathsf{PROJECT},\mathsf{POSTCOMMIT},\mathsf{REPLAY}}),
(\mathsf{Details}) contains structured fields specific to the failure code (expected vs observed hashes, missing hashes list, forbidden namespace key, failing predicate id),
(\mathsf{Refs}) is a canonical list of implicated object hashes (delta hashes, witness hashes, method ids, policy hashes),
(\mathsf{PolicySet}) binds relevant policy hashes.
G.4.4(c) First-failure determinism.Failure emission must be deterministic by a pinned predicate evaluation order. When multiple failures could be reported, the system must choose the earliest under:
schema validity failures,
binding failures,
scope failures,
budget failures,
contradiction failures,
proof verification failures,
determinism/replay failures,unless (\Omega) defines a different order. This ensures identical failures yield identical failure objects and failure evidence hashes across implementations.
G.4.4(d) Failure recording in step records.Rejected steps may still emit failure objects into the proof store (subject to scope/budget constraints for evidence writes). The step record must include the evidence hash list when evidence retention is mandatory. The failure object binds to the step context (or a pinned pre-apply binding variant when post-state hash is unavailable due to early failure); the binding variant rules must be pinned and deterministic.
APPENDIX G — KERNEL PRIMITIVES, DELTA ALGEBRA, AND STATE HASHING
APPENDIX H — DETECTOR CATALOG, DEFECT CODES, AND EVIDENCE PAYLOADS
This appendix specifies the detector registry, defect code taxonomy, and the canonical evidence schemas used across execution, replay verification, repair planning, and audit. Detectors are deterministic functions run under the corridor policy (\Omega) at pinned phases; their outputs are evidence objects bound to step contexts. Evidence objects are first-class, hash-addressed artifacts: they are the sole admissible mechanism for reporting defects, near-threshold signals, mandatory mitigations, and repair completion.
H.1 Detector registry schema
H.1.1 Detector id governance and version pinning
H.1.1(a) Detector id object.A detector is identified by a detector id (\mathsf{det_id}) and represented by a COS object:[\mathsf{Det}:=(\mathsf{det_id},\mathsf{ver},h(\mathsf{Digest}),h(\mathsf{InSchema}),h(\mathsf{OutSchema}),h(\mathsf{ReqRefs}),h(\mathsf{PhaseClass}),h(\mathsf{SevPol}),h(\mathsf{CostClass}),h(\mathsf{DisclosurePol})?).]
(\mathsf{Digest}) is the implementation digest hash; any change produces a new (\mathsf{Det}) object and therefore a new registry hash.
(\mathsf{InSchema}) and (\mathsf{OutSchema}) are schema hashes for detector inputs and outputs.
(\mathsf{ReqRefs}) binds the resolver requirements (what hashes must be available in the step’s source root or in the proof delta).
(\mathsf{PhaseClass}) binds which phases the detector may run in.
(\mathsf{SevPol}) binds severity thresholds and required mitigation policy ids.
(\mathsf{CostClass}) binds cost accounting metadata.
(\mathsf{DisclosurePol}) optionally binds evidence redaction requirements if sensitive.
H.1.1(b) Detector registry object.The detector registry is a COS object:[\mathsf{DetReg}:=(\mathsf{reg_ver},[\mathsf{Det}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{Det}_M],h(\mathsf{GlobalDetRules})).]Detectors are sorted canonically by (\mathsf{det_id}). The registry hash (h(\mathsf{DetReg})) is committed in:
corridor policy (\Omega),
runtime headers,
ledger headers (when detector schedules are enforced at replay).
H.1.1(c) Governance constraints.Adding/removing/updating detectors requires producing a new detector registry hash and, if detectors influence legality, a new corridor policy hash. Therefore detector governance is an explicit policy update that must be receipted and cannot be silent.
H.1.1(d) Admission requirements.A detector is admissible in a registry only if:
its implementation digest is pinned,
it is deterministic under its declared resolver requirements,
it has conformance plans/receipts demonstrating stable outputs for canonical test cases,
its severity policy is pinned and coherent with corridor semantics (hard vs soft constraints),
its evidence payload schema is canonical and adequate for repair mapping.
H.1.2 Detector interface (inputs, outputs, resolver requirements)
H.1.2(a) Detector input object.A detector input is a COS object:[\mathsf{DetIn}:=(h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),\mathsf{phase},\mathsf{step_index},h(\mathsf{Ctx}),h_{\mathsf{pre}},r_\Delta,h_{\mathsf{post}}?,h(\mathsf{DeltaRefs}),h(\mathsf{WitRefs}),h(\mathsf{EvidRefs})?,h(\mathsf{StateRefs})?,h(\mathsf{PolRefs})).]
(\mathsf{phase}\in{\mathsf{PREAPPLY},\mathsf{PREPROJECT},\mathsf{PROJECT},\mathsf{POSTCOMMIT},\mathsf{REPLAY}}).
(h(\mathsf{Ctx})) is the step context binding (Appendix B); for early phases where post-state is unknown, a pinned binding variant is used and must be explicitly declared by (\Omega).
(\mathsf{DeltaRefs}) references per-store delta hashes and delta root.
(\mathsf{WitRefs}) references witness hashes.
(\mathsf{StateRefs}) optionally references per-store hashes and checkpoint hashes where needed.
(\mathsf{PolRefs}) references relevant policy hashes (at minimum (h_\Omega), (h_{\mathsf{Canon}}), (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}), and detector registry hash).
H.1.2(b) Detector output object.A detector output is a COS object:[\mathsf{DetOut}:=(\mathsf{det_id},\mathsf{severity},\mathsf{code},h(\mathsf{Evidence}),h(\mathsf{Mitigate})?,h(\mathsf{Cost})?).]
(\mathsf{severity}\in{\mathsf{OK},\mathsf{NEAR},\mathsf{FAIL}}) or ({\mathsf{OK},\mathsf{FAIL}}) for categorical detectors.
(\mathsf{code}) is a pinned enum code within the detector’s defect family.
(h(\mathsf{Evidence})) is an evidence object hash (Section H.3).
(h(\mathsf{Mitigate})) is present when severity triggers mandatory mitigations.
(h(\mathsf{Cost})) optionally records detector cost accounting metadata.
H.1.2(c) Resolver requirements.A detector declares a resolver requirement object:[\mathsf{ReqRefs}:=(h(\mathsf{NeedFromSrc}),h(\mathsf{NeedFromDelta}),h(\mathsf{NeedFromPolicy}),h(\mathsf{NeedOptional})),]specifying which referenced hashes must be present in the step’s source root, which may be introduced by the proof delta, and which are optional. Detectors must fail deterministically with (\mathsf{E_MISSING})-class evidence when required references are absent.
H.1.2(d) Determinism constraints.Detectors must not read ambient state (time, randomness, concurrency order). Any pseudo-random behavior is prohibited unless (\Omega) permits it and the seed hash is committed and included in (\mathsf{DetIn}). Detector outputs must be deterministic functions of (\mathsf{DetIn}) and resolved referenced objects.
H.1.3 Phase schedules and ordering commitments
H.1.3(a) Phase schedule object.A detector schedule is a COS object:[\mathsf{DetSched}:=(h(\mathsf{PreApply}),h(\mathsf{PreProject}),h(\mathsf{Project}),h(\mathsf{PostCommit}),h(\mathsf{Replay}),h(\mathsf{OrderPol}),h(\mathsf{RetentionPol})).]Each phase component is a sequence-like list of detector ids in execution order. Ordering is not advisory; it is part of determinism and is pinned by (h(\mathsf{OrderPol})).
H.1.3(b) Ordering commitments.Ordering policy specifies:
total order for detectors within each phase,
tie-break rules for evidence ordering when multiple detectors emit evidence,
rule for how detector evidence hashes are aggregated into the step evidence list (union vs concatenation) and how duplicates are handled.
All such rules are pinned and enforced by the kernel projector and replay verifier.
H.1.3(c) Phase admissibility constraints.Each detector declares which phases it may run in via (\mathsf{PhaseClass}). The schedule must respect these constraints; a detector scheduled in a forbidden phase invalidates the plan or causes deterministic compilation failure.
H.1.3(d) Schedule hashing and plan binding.The schedule hash (h(\mathsf{DetSched})) is committed in the ledger plan and optionally in the corridor policy. This ensures that execution and replay use the same detector schedule; otherwise replay equivalence is ill-defined.
H.1.4 Evidence retention policy bindings and caps
H.1.4(a) Retention policy object.Evidence retention is governed by a COS object:[\mathsf{RetentionPol}:=(h(\mathsf{RequiredDetectors}),h(\mathsf{RequiredOnNear}),h(\mathsf{RequiredOnFail}),h(\mathsf{OptionalDetectors}),h(\mathsf{Caps}),h(\mathsf{RedactionPol})?).]
(\mathsf{RequiredDetectors}) lists detectors whose evidence must always be retained.
(\mathsf{RequiredOnNear}) lists detectors requiring evidence retention when severity is (\mathsf{NEAR}).
(\mathsf{RequiredOnFail}) lists detectors requiring evidence retention on (\mathsf{FAIL}).
(\mathsf{OptionalDetectors}) lists detectors whose evidence may be omitted deterministically under pinned omission rules.
(\mathsf{Caps}) binds per-step and per-window evidence count/size caps and throttling actions.
(\mathsf{RedactionPol}) binds redaction requirements for sensitive evidence.
H.1.4(b) Retention feasibility.Retention feasibility must be checked against corridor write budgets. If mandatory evidence retention would exceed proof-store write budgets, compilation must reject the plan or require explicit budget escalation witnesses (if permitted). Runtime cannot silently omit mandatory evidence.
H.1.4(c) Deterministic omission rules.When evidence is optional, omission must be deterministic and policy-pinned. For example, retain only:
the lexicographically minimal (K) evidence hashes by (detector class, code, hash),
or all evidence of certain classes and omit the rest.Omission decisions must be reproducible and, if required, logged as a summary evidence object.
H.1.4(d) Evidence caps and throttling.If caps are reached, throttling actions are determined by policy:
omit optional evidence first,
truncate candidate evidence by deterministic selection,
escalate budgets only if explicitly permitted and justified,
or halt when mandatory evidence cannot be retained.These actions must be recorded as evidence objects and are replay-verifiable.
H.2 Defect code taxonomy
Defect codes classify detector outcomes and determine repair triggers. Codes are pinned enums; their payload schemas are fixed.
H.2.1 Convention drift codes and boundary markers
H.2.1(a) Convention drift code enum.Convention drift codes include at minimum:
(\mathsf{CD_SPEC_IN_MISMATCH}): observed input spec differs from required SpecIn and no bridge present,
(\mathsf{CD_SPEC_OUT_MISMATCH}): produced output spec differs from declared SpecOut,
(\mathsf{CD_MISSING_BRIDGE_CLASS}): required bridge class absent from library,
(\mathsf{CD_MISSING_NORMALIZER}): required post-bridge normalizer absent or omitted,
(\mathsf{CD_IMPLICIT_COERCION}): detected semantic coercion without explicit bridge operations.
H.2.1(b) Boundary marker schema.Convention drift evidence must include a boundary marker:[\mathsf{Boundary}:=(\mathsf{step_index},\mathsf{edge_id}?,\mathsf{boundary_kind},h(\mathsf{SpecExpected}),h(\mathsf{SpecObserved}),h(\mathsf{Loc})?),]where (\mathsf{boundary_kind}\in{\mathsf{EDGE_BOUNDARY},\mathsf{BRIDGE_BOUNDARY},\mathsf{COMMIT_BOUNDARY},\mathsf{CHECKPOINT_BOUNDARY}}) and (h(\mathsf{Loc})) optionally references a structural location (e.g., program interval, object field path).
H.2.1(c) Severity semantics.Convention drift is categorical: (\mathsf{OK}) or (\mathsf{FAIL}). No (\mathsf{NEAR}) state exists because spec mismatch is discrete. Any (\mathsf{FAIL}) is a hard failure unless the corridor explicitly allows discarding non-committed exploratory artifacts, which must be explicitly encoded; commitment boundaries are always hard.
H.2.1(d) Repair linkage.Each convention drift code maps to repair class (\mathsf{BRIDGE}) or (\mathsf{RENORM}) or (\mathsf{HALT}) depending on whether bridges exist and are feasible under (\Omega). Mapping is defined in the repair-trigger table (Section H.4).
H.2.2 Invariant drift codes (OK/NEAR/FAIL thresholds)
H.2.2(a) Invariant drift code enum.Invariant drift codes include:
(\mathsf{ID_PASS}): all required invariant checks pass within near threshold,
(\mathsf{ID_NEAR}): at least one invariant is near threshold,
(\mathsf{ID_FAIL}): at least one invariant exceeds tolerance,
(\mathsf{ID_NOT_EVALUABLE}): invariant required but not evaluable in current lens/spec regime,
(\mathsf{ID_MISSING_MEAS_DEP}): required measurement dependencies missing.
H.2.2(b) Threshold semantics.Each invariant clause binds tolerance (\varepsilon_I) and near fraction (\alpha_I). Drift is computed deterministically from quantized measurements:
(\mathsf{OK}) if (d_I\le \lfloor \alpha_I\varepsilon_I\rfloor),
(\mathsf{NEAR}) if (\lfloor \alpha_I\varepsilon_I\rfloor < d_I \le \varepsilon_I),
(\mathsf{FAIL}) if (d_I>\varepsilon_I).All values are integers under pinned quantization.
H.2.2(c) Hardness coupling.Invariant drift (\mathsf{FAIL}) is hard if the invariant is classified hard by (\Omega) or by the target namespace constraints. If the invariant is classified soft, the projector may permit partial acceptance by discarding dependent secondary outputs only when policy allows and only when commitments do not depend on the failing invariant.
H.2.2(d) Repair linkage.Invariant drift (\mathsf{NEAR}) triggers mandatory mitigation actions when required by (\Omega): normalization insertion, reroute to exact transforms, or proof strengthening. (\mathsf{FAIL}) triggers reroute or renormalization or halt depending on feasibility; mapping is in Section H.4.
H.2.3 Accumulation codes (tolerance ledger violations)
H.2.3(a) Accumulation code enum.Tolerance accumulation codes include:
(\mathsf{AC_OK}): cumulative tolerance within safe region,
(\mathsf{AC_NEAR}): cumulative tolerance near corridor budget,
(\mathsf{AC_FAIL}): cumulative tolerance exceeds corridor budget,
(\mathsf{AC_OP_MISMATCH}): accumulation operator mismatch between contract and corridor,
(\mathsf{AC_UNACCOUNTED}): detected approximate step without declared tolerance usage.
H.2.3(b) Ledger semantics.Accumulation uses pinned operators (\oplus_I) and corridor budgets (\varepsilon^\Omega_I). Near fraction (\alpha_I) determines (\mathsf{NEAR}). Accumulation is computed deterministically from declared tolerance usage vectors committed in step records (or derived deterministically from contracts and parameter hashes when usage is not explicitly committed but derivable).
H.2.3(c) Unaccounted approximation as defect.If a detector observes that an operation uses an approximate mechanism (e.g., an approximate bridge or regrid) but declared tolerance usage for relevant invariants is zero, this is a defect ((\mathsf{AC_UNACCOUNTED})) and is treated as determinism/contract violation. Correctness depends on explicit accounting.
H.2.3(d) Repair linkage.(\mathsf{AC_NEAR}) triggers forced renormalization or reroute, and possibly proof tightening. (\mathsf{AC_FAIL}) triggers reroute or halt if no feasible reroute exists. Operator mismatches trigger halting or policy migration plans.
H.2.4 Replay divergence codes (localization classes)
H.2.4(a) Replay divergence code enum.Replay divergence codes include:
(\mathsf{RD_PREHASH_MISMATCH}): pre-state hash mismatch in chain linkage,
(\mathsf{RD_DELTAHASH_MISMATCH}): delta hash mismatch,
(\mathsf{RD_MASK_MISMATCH}): acceptance mask mismatch (or mask recomputation mismatch when required),
(\mathsf{RD_WITNESSHASH_MISMATCH}): witness list hash mismatch,
(\mathsf{RD_POSTHASH_MISMATCH}): post-state hash mismatch,
(\mathsf{RD_STOREHASH_MISMATCH}): per-store hash mismatch (commit/rel/cand/proof),
(\mathsf{RD_DETECTOR_MISMATCH}): detector/evidence mismatch under mandatory retention,
(\mathsf{RD_POLICY_MISMATCH}): header policy hashes mismatch.
H.2.4(b) Localization class enum.Replay divergence evidence must include a localization class:[\mathsf{LocClass}\in{\mathsf{HDR},\mathsf{COMMIT},\mathsf{REL},\mathsf{CAND},\mathsf{PROOF},\mathsf{MASK},\mathsf{WITNESS},\mathsf{DETECT},\mathsf{DELTA},\mathsf{GENESIS}}.]
H.2.4(c) Hardness.Replay divergence is always hard. It indicates either:
TCB non-conformance under committed header hashes,
missing objects required for replay,
or tampering of ledger records.Repair inside the same runtime header is not permitted; correct action is halt or explicit policy migration.
H.2.4(d) Repair linkage.Replay divergence maps to (\mathsf{HALT}) by default, or to a policy-migration plan if the framework supports such migration and the ledger explicitly indicates permitted migration procedures.
H.3 Evidence schemas (expanded)
H.3.1 Evidence common header (bind, code, refs, policy hashes)
H.3.1(a) Evidence object schema.Evidence is a COS object:[\mathsf{Evidence}:=(\mathsf{ev_id},\mathsf{ev_class},\mathsf{severity},\mathsf{code},\mathsf{phase},h(\mathsf{Bind}),h(\mathsf{Refs}),h(\mathsf{Payload}),h(\mathsf{PolicySet}),h(\mathsf{Disclosure})?).]
(\mathsf{ev_id}=h(\mathsf{Evidence})).
(\mathsf{ev_class}\in{\mathsf{CONVENTION},\mathsf{INVARIANT},\mathsf{ACCUMULATION},\mathsf{REPLAY},\mathsf{BUDGET},\mathsf{SCOPE},\mathsf{CONTRADICTION},\mathsf{DETECTOR}}).
(\mathsf{severity}\in{\mathsf{OK},\mathsf{NEAR},\mathsf{FAIL}}) or categorical subset.
(\mathsf{code}) is defect subtype code (from H.2 or detector-specific).
(\mathsf{phase}) identifies execution phase.
(h(\mathsf{Bind})) binds to step context (Appendix B) or to a pinned replay-binding variant for replay-verifier outputs.
(h(\mathsf{Refs})) is a canonical set of referenced hashes (witnesses, deltas, method ids, specs, pins).
(h(\mathsf{Payload})) is class-specific payload (Sections H.3.2).
(h(\mathsf{PolicySet})) binds policy hashes relevant to interpreting the evidence.
(h(\mathsf{Disclosure})) optionally binds redaction/disclosure requirements.
H.3.1(b) Bind object.The bind object is:[\mathsf{Bind}:=(\mathsf{bind_kind},\mathsf{Ctx})\quad \text{or}\quad (\mathsf{bind_kind},h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),\mathsf{step_index},h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{hdr}}),]where (\mathsf{bind_kind}) identifies whether the binding is a normal step context binding or a replay verifier binding. The binding must be deterministic and sufficient to prevent transplantation.
H.3.1(c) Refs canonicalization.(\mathsf{Refs}) is set-like and canonical. References include:
implicated invariant ids,
implicated spec hashes,
implicated delta hashes and delta root,
implicated witness hashes and method ids,
implicated pin ids,
implicated checkpoint hashes,
implicated metro map or library root hashes when relevant.Ref ordering is lexicographic on canonical encoding of each reference record.
H.3.1(d) PolicySet object.(\mathsf{PolicySet}) includes:[\mathsf{PolicySet}:=(h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h_{\mathsf{CostModel}},h(\mathsf{DetReg}),h(\mathsf{DetSched}),h(\mathsf{MerklePol})).]This binds evidence interpretation to the exact policy universe used to produce it.
H.3.2 Payload schemas by defect class
H.3.2(a) Convention drift payload.[\mathsf{Payload}_{\mathsf{CONVENTION}}:=(h(\mathsf{Boundary}),h(\mathsf{SpecExpected}),h(\mathsf{SpecObserved}),h(\mathsf{MissingBridgeClasses})?,h(\mathsf{MissingNormalizers})?,h(\mathsf{RouteCtx})?).]
(\mathsf{Boundary}) is boundary marker (H.2.1).
(\mathsf{MissingBridgeClasses}) is a canonical set of bridge class tokens.
(\mathsf{RouteCtx}) optionally binds the route edge ids at the boundary.
H.3.2(b) Invariant drift payload.[\mathsf{Payload}_{\mathsf{INVARIANT}}:=(h(\mathsf{InvList}),h(\mathsf{MeasRoot})?,h(\mathsf{MeasEvs})?,h(\mathsf{Thresholds}),h(\mathsf{Applicability})?).]
(\mathsf{InvList}) is the canonical list of invariants evaluated with per-invariant verdict and drift magnitudes.
(\mathsf{MeasRoot}) optionally commits to a Merkle root of measurement evidence objects.
(\mathsf{Thresholds}) binds (\varepsilon_I) and near thresholds (\alpha_I\varepsilon_I).
(\mathsf{Applicability}) binds which invariants were applicable.
H.3.2(c) Accumulation payload.[\mathsf{Payload}_{\mathsf{ACCUMULATION}}:=(h(\mathsf{TolLedger}),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}}),h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega}),h(\oplus),h(\mathsf{NearParams}),h(\mathsf{Window})?).]Includes current ledger state, budgets, accumulation operators, near parameters, and optional bounded window evidence (e.g., per-step contributions Merkle root).
H.3.2(d) Replay divergence payload.[\mathsf{Payload}{\mathsf{REPLAY}}:=(\mathsf{earliest_step},\mathsf{locClass},h{\mathsf{expected}},h_{\mathsf{observed}},h(\mathsf{StoreExpected})?,h(\mathsf{StoreObserved})?,h(\mathsf{Diff})?).]Includes earliest divergence step, localization class, expected/observed hashes and optional per-store expected/observed hashes for localization.
H.3.3 Merkle commitments for evidence bundles
H.3.3(a) Evidence bundle root.When evidence retention requires compact commitments, define an evidence root:[r_{\mathsf{ev}}:=\mathsf{MerkleRoot}(\mathrm{Sort}([\mathsf{EncHash}(h(\mathsf{Evidence}_i))])),]where leaves are fixed-length encodings of evidence hashes sorted lexicographically. This root may be committed in step records, checkpoints, and receipts.
H.3.3(b) Evidence membership proof.A membership proof for evidence hash (h(\mathsf{Evidence})) under (r_{\mathsf{ev}}) is a Merkle proof object under pinned Merkle policy (Appendix A). Membership proofs allow downstream audits to confirm inclusion of specific defect or mitigation evidence without reading all evidence objects.
H.3.3(c) Evidence list hash vs evidence root.A step record may commit:
evidence list hash (h(E_t)) for the ordered list of evidence hashes,
and optionally evidence Merkle root (r_{\mathsf{ev},t}).Both are deterministic; list hash commits exact ordering; root commits set membership. Policy may require one or both.
H.3.3(d) Canonical evidence aggregation.Aggregation of evidence across steps into receipts or merged receipts uses union roots and canonical ordering rules. Any aggregation must preserve non-contradiction requirements for evidence types where contradictions matter (e.g., mutually exclusive mitigation claims), enforced by governance policies if needed.
H.3.4 Redaction and disclosure fields for evidence
H.3.4(a) Redaction policy binding.Evidence may contain sensitive payloads. Redaction is governed by a pinned redaction policy object (h(\mathsf{RedactPol})) specifying:
which payload fields may be redacted,
how redacted fields are replaced (by hashes or markers),
what verification remains possible under redaction.
Evidence objects include (h(\mathsf{Disclosure})) when redaction applies.
H.3.4(b) Redaction marker objects.For redacted sub-objects, publish a marker:[\mathsf{RedactMarker}:=(h(\mathsf{Obj}),\mathsf{TypeId},h(\mathsf{RedactPol}),h(\mathsf{Bind})),]binding the hidden object hash and its type, and the policy authorizing redaction. This allows audit of what was withheld without revealing contents.
H.3.4(c) Public verifiability boundary.If evidence is required for public verification (e.g., to justify a repair completion), its disclosure policy must be satisfied. If evidence is redacted beyond what the verifier method allows, the evidence remains a commitment but is not publicly verifiable; this boundary must be explicit in the receipt’s disclosure mode.
H.3.4(d) Deterministic redaction.Redaction must be deterministic: given the same evidence and redaction policy, the redacted evidence bytes are identical. Redaction cannot be a discretionary narrative process; it is a transformation with canonical rules and hashes.
H.4 Repair-trigger mapping table
Repair-trigger mapping defines deterministic repair classes and required mitigation action ids in response to defect evidence.
H.4.1 Defect→repair class mapping (bridge/regrid/renorm/reroute/halt)
H.4.1(a) Mapping object.The mapping table is a pinned COS object:[\mathsf{RepMap}:=[\mathsf{MapRow}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{MapRow}_N],]each row:[\mathsf{MapRow}:=(\mathsf{ev_class},\mathsf{code},\mathsf{severity},[\mathsf{RepairClass}]^\ast,h(\mathsf{ReqActions}),h(\mathsf{ReqProofs}),h(\mathsf{TieBreak})).]
(\mathsf{RepairClass}\in{\mathsf{BRIDGE},\mathsf{REGRID},\mathsf{RENORM},\mathsf{REROUTE},\mathsf{PROOFREGEN},\mathsf{HALT}}).
(\mathsf{ReqActions}) binds mandatory mitigation action ids.
(\mathsf{ReqProofs}) binds required proof method ids for repair.
(\mathsf{TieBreak}) pins deterministic preference order among repair classes.
H.4.1(b) Canonical mapping precedence.If multiple rows match an evidence object, choose the row with:
most specific code match,
most restrictive severity match,
smallest lexicographic mapping row id,under pinned policy. This ensures deterministic repair selection.
H.4.1(c) Repair feasibility gating.Mapping does not imply feasibility. If the preferred repair class is infeasible under (\Omega) (budgets/scope/method support), the repair planner must attempt the next class in the ordered list, emitting feasibility evidence. If none are feasible, choose (\mathsf{HALT}).
H.4.1(d) Explicit non-repairable classifications.Certain codes map directly to (\mathsf{HALT}) regardless of feasibility (e.g., replay divergence under fixed header hashes, missing policy objects, verifier registry mismatch). Such direct mappings are explicit in the table.
H.4.2 Mandatory mitigation action ids and insertion points
H.4.2(a) Mitigation action object.Mitigation actions are pinned ids referencing canonical action templates:[\mathsf{MitAct}:=(\mathsf{act_id},\mathsf{act_kind},h(\mathsf{Template}),h(\mathsf{InsertPol}),h(\mathsf{ReqProofs}),h(\mathsf{ScopeReq}),h(\mathsf{BudgetReq})).]
(\mathsf{act_kind}\in{\mathsf{INSERT_NORMALIZER},\mathsf{INSERT_BRIDGE},\mathsf{REROUTE_EXACT},\mathsf{TIGHTEN_PROOFS},\mathsf{SEGMENT_CHECKPOINT},\mathsf{HALT}}).
(\mathsf{InsertPol}) pins insertion points (before commitments, after bridges, at checkpoints, under near thresholds).
H.4.2(b) Insertion point semantics.Insertion points are canonical boundary kinds:[\mathsf{InsertPoint}\in{\mathsf{BEFORE_COMMIT},\mathsf{AFTER_BRIDGE},\mathsf{CHECKPOINT_BOUNDARY},\mathsf{LOOP_BOUNDARY},\mathsf{NEXT_STEP}}.]The planner must insert mitigation steps deterministically at the specified insertion point relative to the defect evidence boundary marker.
H.4.2(c) Binding to defect evidence.Mitigation actions must include the triggering evidence hash in their provenance source roots. This creates an auditable chain:[h(\mathsf{Evidence}) \to \mathsf{MitigationPlan} \to \mathsf{MitigationReceipt}.]
H.4.2(d) Mitigation compliance enforcement.If (\Omega) declares mitigation actions mandatory for a near-threshold condition, any plan segment executed after the defect boundary must include the required mitigation action ids; otherwise execution is illegal and must reject+revert. This enforcement is checked by detectors and by ledger plan rules.
H.4.3 Non-repairable classification rules
H.4.3(a) Non-repairable predicate object.Non-repairable classification is determined by a pinned predicate:[\mathsf{NonRepPred}:=(h(\mathsf{Rules}),h(\mathsf{PolicyReq}),h(\mathsf{LibReq})).]Rules include deterministic conditions such as:
replay divergence with policy mismatch or missing objects,
missing bridge class with no admissible bridge entries in library root,
missing proof method ids under verifier set,
hard invariant fail with no alternate feasible route in metro map under required invariants,
tolerance ledger overflow with no admissible reroute/tightening actions under (\Omega).
H.4.3(b) Evidence requirements for non-repairable classification.The detector or repair planner must emit non-repairable evidence including:
the failing rule id,
the missing prerequisite hash (bridge entry id, method id, policy hash),
the earliest boundary and checkpoint hash at which safe halt occurs.
This evidence is required for governance actions and for explicit failure receipts.
H.4.3(c) Halt action selection.When non-repairable, the system must produce either:
a halt artifact (compile-time), or
a halt receipt (execution-time), depending on scope and corridor permission for committing halt artifacts.Halt selection is deterministic and binds to non-repairable evidence.
H.4.3(d) Governance hooks.Non-repairable evidence objects are structured inputs to governance:
missing bridges → add bridge entries and admit them with receipts,
missing methods → extend verifier registry with conformance receipts,
budget infeasibility → adjust corridor policy class or require budget escalation mechanisms,
contradiction non-resolvable → refine resolution policies or clarify constraints.Any governance response must itself be a plan and yield receipts.
H.4.4 Repair completion evidence schemas
H.4.4(a) Repair completion evidence object.A repair completion evidence object is:[\mathsf{RepDone}:=(h(\mathsf{DefEvidence}),h(\mathsf{RepPlan}),h(\mathsf{RepRcpt}),h_{\mathsf{preChk}},h_{\mathsf{postChk}},h(\mathsf{DetSet}),h(\mathsf{PostChecks}),\mathsf{status}),]where:
(h(\mathsf{DefEvidence})) is the triggering evidence hash,
(h(\mathsf{RepPlan})) is the repair plan hash,
(h(\mathsf{RepRcpt})) is the repair receipt hash,
(h_{\mathsf{preChk}},h_{\mathsf{postChk}}) are checkpoint hashes,
(\mathsf{DetSet}) lists detectors that must now report (\mathsf{OK}),
(\mathsf{PostChecks}) lists post-commit audits run,
(\mathsf{status}\in{\mathsf{OK},\mathsf{FAIL}}).
H.4.4(b) Completion verification predicate.Completion is verified deterministically by:
verifying the repair receipt and its ledger validity,
confirming that the defect boundary condition is eliminated (e.g., spec hashes now match, invariant drift below threshold, tolerance ledger within budget),
confirming mandatory detectors now pass at the repaired boundary checkpoint,
confirming any required normalization insertion points were satisfied.
All checks must be replay-verifiable.
H.4.4(c) Completion chaining.Repair completion evidence must be consumable as an input to subsequent plans via dependency proofs. Therefore completion evidence must be stored in proof store and its hash must be included in source roots of subsequent steps that rely on the repair.
H.4.4(d) Repair supersession updates.When repair produces a stronger artifact replacing a weaker one, the repair plan may produce supersession records and refinement proofs. These are separate commitment actions governed by contradiction policies; their completion is part of repair completion when the repair goal includes updating indices or alias records.
APPENDIX H — DETECTOR CATALOG, DEFECT CODES, AND EVIDENCE PAYLOADS
APPENDIX I — CORRIDOR POLICY ((\Omega)) SCHEMA, PREORDER, AND GOVERNANCE HOOKS
This appendix specifies the corridor policy (\Omega) as a pinned legality environment. (\Omega) defines budgets, scope discipline, determinism requirements, contradiction resolution rules, mandatory detector schedules, throttling controls, and governance hooks for admissions, supersession, and publication. (\Omega) is not configuration; it is a content-addressed contract that all execution, compilation, verification, repair, and optimization steps must satisfy. Any change to (\Omega) changes its hash and therefore changes ledger and receipt compatibility.
I.1 Corridor object schema
A corridor policy is a COS object:[\Omega := (\omega_id,\omega_ver,h(\mathsf{BudgetPol}),h(\mathsf{ScopePol}),h(\mathsf{DetPol}),h(\mathsf{ContrPol}),h(\mathsf{DetSched}),h(\mathsf{RetentionPol}),h(\mathsf{ThrottlePol}),h(\mathsf{GovPol}),h(\mathsf{Compat})?,\omega_{\mathsf{meta}}?),]with (\omega_id=h(\Omega)). All referenced policy objects are required to resolve by hash.
I.1.1 Budget fields and cost-model bindings
I.1.1(a) Budget policy object.[\mathsf{BudgetPol}:=(h(\mathsf{CostModel}),h(\mathsf{Units}),b_{\mathsf{comp}},h(\mathsf{WriteBud}),b_{\mathsf{branch}},b_{\lambda},h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega}),h(\oplus),h(\mathsf{NearParams}),h(\mathsf{EscPol})?).]
(h(\mathsf{CostModel})) pins cost accounting procedures and cost classes.
(\mathsf{Units}) pins quantization for compute and write units.
(b_{\mathsf{comp}}) is the per-step compute cap (or a per-window cap when used with windows).
(\mathsf{WriteBud}) maps target classes to per-step write caps.
(b_{\mathsf{branch}}) caps exploration branching and candidate store size.
(b_{\lambda}) caps expansion depth.
(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega}) is corridor tolerance budget vector aligned to invariant index.
(h(\oplus)) pins per-invariant tolerance accumulation operators.
(\mathsf{NearParams}) pins near fractions (\alpha_I) and mitigation policies.
(\mathsf{EscPol}) is optional and pins admissible escalation reasons, maxima, and proof requirements.
I.1.1(b) Budget feasibility predicate.Given a step cost vector (\mathbf{c}), budget feasibility is:[\mathsf{BudgetOK}(\mathbf{c},\Omega)\iff c_{\mathsf{comp}}\le b_{\mathsf{comp}}\ \land\ \forall t:\ c_{\mathsf{write}}(t)\le b_{\mathsf{write}}(t)\ \land\ c_{\mathsf{branch}}\le b_{\mathsf{branch}}\ \land\ \lambda\le b_{\lambda}\ \land\ \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}}\le \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega},]subject to admissible budget escalation witnesses when escalation is permitted. Accumulated tolerance is tracked in tolerance ledgers and checkpoint objects; feasibility must be checked deterministically at the required phases.
I.1.1(c) Cost model determinism.If (\Omega) declares cost determinism mandatory, replay verification must recompute costs and require equality. If cost determinism is not mandatory, costs are still feasibility-constrained but may be treated as audited reports; this classification is a pinned field in (\mathsf{CostModel}).
I.1.1(d) Budget evidence obligations.(\Omega) may require evidence emission:
on near-threshold budgets,
on budget escalation,
on budget infeasibility.Required evidence codes and payload schemas are pinned by detector registry and retention policy; omission is illegal.
I.1.2 Scope fields and primary/secondary write policies
I.1.2(a) Scope policy object.[\mathsf{ScopePol}:=(h(\mathsf{Targets}),h(\mathsf{NSAllow}),h(\mathsf{NSDeny}),h(\mathsf{PrimRules}),h(\mathsf{SecRules}),h(\mathsf{AuthReq}),h(\mathsf{ReadPol})?).]
(\mathsf{Targets}) enumerates allowed target classes.
(\mathsf{NSAllow}/\mathsf{NSDeny}) are namespace prefix sets controlling commitment keys and index keys.
(\mathsf{PrimRules}) defines primary target semantics by step class (e.g., exploration vs commitment).
(\mathsf{SecRules}) defines secondary write allowances and required witnesses.
(\mathsf{AuthReq}) binds required witness types/methods for scope extensions.
(\mathsf{ReadPol}) optionally restricts reads (privacy/governance).
I.1.2(b) Primary/secondary discipline.For each step, (\Omega) classifies its primary target (commit/rel/cand/proof/plan/receipt/index). Secondary writes are any writes outside primary. Secondary writes require explicit (\mathsf{SCOPE}) witnesses with granular authorization; unauthorized secondary writes are hard failures unless (\Omega) explicitly classifies the affected operations as discardable under partial acceptance.
I.1.2(c) Exploration vs commitment scope.(\Omega) enforces a default separation:
exploration steps may write to candidate and proof stores, and to limited relation stores; they may not write to contradiction-controlled commitments,
commitment steps may write to commitment store but require commitment witnesses and contradiction-resolution witnesses when needed,
repair and governance steps have restricted isolation sets and may write to protected namespaces only with stronger proof requirements.
This classification is pinned and must be enforced deterministically.
I.1.2(d) Scope evidence and auditing.Scope violations produce evidence objects with codes indicating:
forbidden namespace,
unauthorized secondary write,
missing scope witness,
attempted mutation of protected namespace.Evidence is required when retention policy mandates; scope checks are part of projector legality.
I.1.3 Determinism profile (ordering, numeric, randomness)
I.1.3(a) Determinism policy object.[\mathsf{DetPol}:=(h(\mathsf{OrderPol}),h(\mathsf{NumPol}),h(\mathsf{RandPol}),h(\mathsf{CanonReq}),h(\mathsf{ReplayReq}),h(\mathsf{TieBreakPol})).]
(\mathsf{OrderPol}) pins ordering rules for deltas, witnesses, evidence, candidates, routing and rewriting tie-breaks.
(\mathsf{NumPol}) pins quantization and float policies allowed for committed artifacts and invariant evaluation.
(\mathsf{RandPol}) pins whether pseudo-randomness is allowed and how it must be seeded and committed.
(\mathsf{CanonReq}) pins mandatory canonicalization and normalization insertion requirements.
(\mathsf{ReplayReq}) pins replay strictness (e.g., must recompute mask, must recompute cost).
(\mathsf{TieBreakPol}) pins deterministic tie-break rules for ambiguous choices.
I.1.3(b) Determinism enforcement.A step is illegal if any determinism requirement is violated:
non-canonical encoding,
non-deterministic iteration order,
unpinned randomness,
floating behavior not pinned or used where forbidden,
tie-break not pinned.Determinism violations are hard failures and are recorded by defect evidence.
I.1.3(c) Randomness constraints.If randomness is allowed, seeds must be typed objects referenced by hash and included in source roots. Randomness may not influence commitments unless mediated by certified selection steps and proofs; otherwise it becomes non-auditable and is forbidden.
I.1.3(d) Determinism in compilation.(\Omega) may constrain compilation determinism, requiring:
deterministic routing without heuristic fallback,
deterministic optimization rule ordering,
deterministic bridge insertion.Such constraints are pinned and thus affect plan hashes and receipts.
I.1.4 Contradiction profile (predicate ids, resolution kinds)
I.1.4(a) Contradiction policy object.[\mathsf{ContrPol}:=(h(\mathsf{ContrPred}),h(\mathsf{Namespaces}),h(\mathsf{ResKinds}),h(\mathsf{ResProofReq}),h(\mathsf{PostChecks}),h(\mathsf{MergePol})?).]
(\mathsf{ContrPred}) is the contradiction predicate id, deterministic on commitment store and proposed deltas.
(\mathsf{Namespaces}) lists contradiction-controlled namespaces.
(\mathsf{ResKinds}) lists permitted resolution kinds (DISAMBIGUATE/MERGE/OVERWRITE).
(\mathsf{ResProofReq}) binds required proof methods for each resolution kind per namespace.
(\mathsf{PostChecks}) binds post-resolution audits.
(\mathsf{MergePol}) optionally binds identity merge rules and cluster integrity constraints.
I.1.4(b) Resolution legality.Contradictions in contradiction-controlled namespaces are hard failures unless resolved by admissible consistency witnesses whose proof obligations satisfy (\mathsf{ResProofReq}). Resolution must be step-local, provenance-bound, and post-checked; silent overwrite is forbidden unless explicitly permitted with proof.
I.1.4(c) Contradiction evidence requirements.Whenever contradiction predicate triggers, evidence must bind:
conflicting keys/hashes,
predicate id,
resolution kind attempted,
proof hashes and verifier method ids used.If resolution fails, the step must reject+revert and evidence must be retained when retention policy demands.
I.1.4(d) Contradiction policy stability.Because contradiction policy determines admissible commitment evolution, changing contradiction policy changes (\Omega) hash and thus produces incompatible ledgers/receipts unless explicit policy migration is defined and executed.
I.2 Policy preorder and compatibility
I.2.1 Dominance relation on policy strictness
I.2.1(a) Policy strictness preorder.Define a pinned preorder (\succeq) over corridor policies:[\Omega_2 \succeq \Omega_1]iff (\Omega_2) is at least as strict as (\Omega_1) in each component:
budgets: (b_{\mathsf{comp},2}\le b_{\mathsf{comp},1}), (b_{\mathsf{write},2}\le b_{\mathsf{write},1}), (b_{\mathsf{branch},2}\le b_{\mathsf{branch},1}), (b_{\lambda,2}\le b_{\lambda,1}), (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega_2}\le \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega_1}) componentwise (tighter tolerance budgets are stricter),
scope: allowed targets/namespaces in (\Omega_2) are subsets of those in (\Omega_1),
determinism: (\Omega_2) forbids at least as many nondeterministic behaviors (or equal),
contradiction: (\Omega_2) permits no additional resolution kinds without stronger proof requirements,
evidence retention: (\Omega_2) requires at least as much mandatory evidence,
verification: (\Omega_2) requires at least as high verification levels for protected actions (or equal).
This preorder is pinned; any alternative strictness semantics must be explicit.
I.2.1(b) Use in refinement and substitution.If an artifact is produced under (\Omega_2\succeq \Omega_1), it may be accepted in contexts requiring (\Omega_1) only if:
policy compatibility constraints are satisfied by explicit rules (below),
and claim boundaries (invariants/tolerances/verification levels) are at least as strong.
I.2.1(c) Strictness does not imply compatibility.Even if (\Omega_2\succeq \Omega_1), policy hashes differ, so ledgers and receipts are not identical. Compatibility requires explicit compatibility rules or policy-bridge receipts. The preorder supports reasoning about relative strength, not about hash equality.
I.2.1(d) Deterministic encoding of preorder checks.Preorder checks are executed by deterministic comparator functions whose code is pinned by policy. Any use of the preorder in compilation, receipt algebra, or governance must commit the comparator id hash to ensure reproducibility.
I.2.2 Policy-bridge admissibility conditions
I.2.2(a) Policy bridge definition.A policy bridge is a certified transform program:[\mathsf{PB}:\Omega_{\mathrm{src}}\Rightarrow \Omega_{\mathrm{dst}},]producing a policy-bridge receipt that certifies compatibility of artifacts across policy hashes. Policy bridging is optional; if not supported, policy compatibility is strict equality.
I.2.2(b) Admissibility constraints.A policy bridge is admissible only if:
both policies are resolvable and well-formed,
the bridge is authorized by governance policy (policy updates are protected),
the bridge produces explicit proof objects demonstrating that the destination policy’s requirements are satisfied by the relevant artifacts or by additional mitigation steps (e.g., re-verification under stricter verifiers, additional evidence retention),
the bridge does not weaken determinism or allow forbidden scope expansions.
I.2.2(c) Bridge outputs.A policy bridge yields:
a policy-bridge receipt binding ((h(\Omega_{\mathrm{src}}),h(\Omega_{\mathrm{dst}}))),
a set of derived compatibility claims (which artifacts remain valid, which must be regenerated),
and any required re-verification steps as receipts.
I.2.2(d) Policy-bridge limitations.Policy bridges cannot retroactively change ledgers; they can only certify relationships between artifacts produced under different policies and require explicit new receipts for any strengthened obligations. Any claim that old receipts are valid under a new policy without re-verification is prohibited unless explicitly proven by the bridge.
I.2.3 Evidence retention classes and mandatory detectors
I.2.3(a) Retention classes.(\Omega) may classify evidence retention into pinned classes:[\mathsf{RetClass}\in{\mathsf{MIN},\mathsf{STD},\mathsf{AUDIT},\mathsf{FORENSIC}},]each class binding:
which detectors are mandatory,
which severities require evidence retention,
whether evidence roots must be committed in receipts,
whether detector traces must be retained as trace proofs.
I.2.3(b) Mandatory detector binding.Mandatory detectors are pinned by detector id list in (\Omega). The execution kernel must run them at the specified phases and must retain evidence as required. Replay verification must check that the ledger’s evidence commitments satisfy these requirements.
I.2.3(c) Retention feasibility under budgets.Retention requirements must be feasible under write budgets; otherwise execution cannot proceed without explicit budget escalation permitted by (\Omega). If (\Omega) forbids escalation for evidence retention, infeasible retention causes deterministic halting.
I.2.3(d) Retention compatibility.Artifacts produced under a policy with lower retention class may not satisfy a downstream requirement for higher retention class unless:
the necessary evidence can be reconstructed deterministically from the ledger and referenced objects, or
a re-execution under the higher retention policy is performed and receipted, or
a policy bridge certifies equivalence and reconstructibility.
I.2.4 Verification-mode constraints (hash-only vs selective)
I.2.4(a) Verification mode object.(\Omega) binds permitted verification modes:[\mathsf{VerModePol}:=(\mathsf{allow_hashOnly},\mathsf{allow_selective},h(\mathsf{DisclosureReq}),h(\mathsf{RedactPol})?).]
(\mathsf{DisclosureReq}) binds required disclosure bundles for publicly verifiable receipts.
(\mathsf{RedactPol}) binds permissible redaction policies for evidence and proofs.
I.2.4(b) Mode feasibility.If a goal requires public verifiability, (\Omega) must permit selective disclosure and the plan must include disclosure bundle generation. If a method’s disclosure policy requires revealing certain local-check data, the plan must satisfy it; otherwise the receipt may be valid but not publicly verifiable, which must be explicit in receipt metadata.
I.2.4(c) Mode restrictions on execution.(\Omega) may forbid use of methods that cannot be verified under the required disclosure mode. Such restrictions are enforced by compilation feasibility filters (Appendix E) and by admission constraints for library entries (Appendix F).
I.2.4(d) Verification-mode compatibility in receipt algebra.Receipt composition and merge must respect mode constraints:
a public bundle must ensure each composed receipt has sufficient disclosures or provides dependency proofs in a trusted environment,
mixed-mode aggregation must be explicitly labeled and must not claim public verifiability unless disclosures are present.
I.3 Throttling, rate limits, and windows
I.3.1 Window definitions and canonical counters
I.3.1(a) Window object.A window is a COS object:[\mathsf{Window}:=(\mathsf{win_kind},\mathsf{span},\mathsf{anchor},h(\mathsf{Derive})).]
(\mathsf{win_kind}\in{\mathsf{STEPS},\mathsf{SEGMENT}}) (time-based windows are forbidden unless time is a pinned input).
(\mathsf{span}\in\mathbb{N}) is the number of steps or segments.
(\mathsf{anchor}) defines window alignment (e.g., start at genesis, start at last checkpoint).
(\mathsf{Derive}) pins deterministic derivation rules mapping step indices to window ids.
I.3.1(b) Canonical counters.Counters are deterministic functions of the ledger prefix. For each window id, maintain counters:
number of commitment writes by namespace class,
number of proof-store additions,
number of candidate additions,
number of detector evidences retained,
verifier invocations by method class.
Counters are computed from step records and are thus replay-derivable.
I.3.1(c) Counter identity and hashing.If (\Omega) requires, window counter snapshots are committed as COS objects with hashes. Otherwise counters are recomputed on demand. Either way, counter computation rules are pinned.
I.3.1(d) Window boundary determinism.Window boundaries must be deterministically computable from step indices and checkpoint indices. No real-time clocks may be used unless time is treated as a pinned input hash and committed in the ledger, which is disallowed by default.
I.3.2 Rate-limit objects and accounting rules
I.3.2(a) Rate limit schema.A rate limit is a COS object:[\mathsf{RateLim}:=(\mathsf{lim_id},h(\mathsf{Window}),h(\mathsf{Metric}),\mathsf{max},h(\mathsf{OnHit}),h(\mathsf{EvidenceReq})).]
(\mathsf{Metric}) identifies what is counted (e.g., commit writes to protected namespaces, proof-store bytes, candidate churn).
(\mathsf{max}) is the cap in quantized units.
(\mathsf{OnHit}) defines throttling action (omit/truncate/halt/escalate).
(\mathsf{EvidenceReq}) defines required evidence on hit/near.
I.3.2(b) Accounting rules.Metrics are computed deterministically from step records:
commit writes counted by applied (\mathsf{PinAdd}/\mathsf{PinUpdate}) operations in accepted deltas,
proof additions counted by accepted (\mathsf{ObjAdd}) operations,
candidate churn counted by accepted (\mathsf{CandAdd}/\mathsf{CandPrune}),
detector evidence counted by evidence hashes retained under retention policy.
I.3.2(c) Near thresholds.Rate limits may define near fractions (\alpha). Near-hit occurs when metric exceeds (\alpha\cdot \mathsf{max}). Near-hit can trigger mandatory mitigations (reduce exploration, switch to reuse routes, delay commitments). Near-hit evidence emission is policy-driven and deterministic.
I.3.2(d) Rate-limit admissibility.Rate limits must be feasible under corridor budgets. Unachievable rate limits cause deterministic compile failure for plans that would necessarily violate them. Rate-limit policies are therefore integrated into routing feasibility filters.
I.3.3 Throttle actions (omit/truncate/halt/escalate)
I.3.3(a) Throttle action object.A throttling action is a COS object:[\mathsf{ThrottleAct}:=(\mathsf{act_id},\mathsf{act_kind},h(\mathsf{Params}),h(\mathsf{MaskPol})?,h(\mathsf{ProofReq})?).]
(\mathsf{act_kind}\in{\mathsf{OMIT_OPTIONAL},\mathsf{TRUNCATE_CAND},\mathsf{HALT},\mathsf{REQUIRE_ESC}}).
(\mathsf{MaskPol}) pins deterministic mask construction rules for omission/truncation.
(\mathsf{ProofReq}) binds required proofs if escalation is required.
I.3.3(b) Omission semantics.Omission is permitted only for operations classified discardable under (\Omega) and ledger plan. Omission is implemented via acceptance masks and must be deterministic and auditable. Omission cannot remove required commitments or required witnesses.
I.3.3(c) Truncation semantics.Truncation applies to candidate stores and optionally to optional evidence lists. Truncation must use pinned comparators and tie-break rules and must emit truncation evidence when required.
I.3.3(d) Escalation requirement semantics.If throttling action is (\mathsf{REQUIRE_ESC}), execution must not proceed unless an admissible budget escalation witness is provided. Absence yields reject+revert. Escalation does not change scope; scope remains enforced.
I.3.4 Throttle evidence schema and audit rules
I.3.4(a) Throttle evidence object.Throttle events produce evidence:[\mathsf{ThrottleEv}:=(h(\mathsf{RateLim}),h(\mathsf{WindowId}),\mathsf{metric_value},\mathsf{max},\mathsf{severity},h(\mathsf{Act}),h(\mathsf{Bind}),h(\mathsf{Details})?).]Includes current metric value, cap, severity, action selected, and binding.
I.3.4(b) Audit chain.Throttle evidence must be included in the step’s evidence list and in subsequent mitigation steps’ source roots when mitigation is required. This ensures an auditable chain from rate-limit pressure to action.
I.3.4(c) Deterministic action selection.If multiple throttle actions could apply, selection is deterministic by pinned precedence: omit optional first, then truncate candidates, then require escalation, then halt (unless policy specifies direct halt). Selection evidence must include the rule id or precedence id used.
I.3.4(d) Throttle replay requirements.Replay verification must reproduce throttle evidence and mask outcomes. Any divergence indicates nondeterministic throttling and invalidates the ledger.
I.4 Governance controls
I.4.1 Admission permissions and protected namespaces
I.4.1(a) Governance policy object.[\mathsf{GovPol}:=(h(\mathsf{AdminNS}),h(\mathsf{ProtectedNS}),h(\mathsf{AdmitReq}),h(\mathsf{AuthPred}),h(\mathsf{PublishReq}),h(\mathsf{AuditReq})).]
(\mathsf{AdminNS}) lists namespaces reserved for governance records (library entries, verifier registries, policy updates).
(\mathsf{ProtectedNS}) lists protected commitment namespaces requiring higher proof and authorization.
(\mathsf{AdmitReq}) binds admission packet requirements (Appendix F).
(\mathsf{AuthPred}) binds authorization predicate ids for who/what may write governance keys (modeled as required witness types and method ids).
(\mathsf{PublishReq}) binds requirements for public publication of receipts and proofs.
(\mathsf{AuditReq}) binds mandatory audit evidence for governance actions.
I.4.1(b) Protected write gating.Writes to protected namespaces require:
explicit authorization witnesses (e.g., governance witness types),
consistency witnesses for contradiction-controlled keys,
and proof objects verifying admission/refinement/deprecation claims as required.Unauthorized writes are hard failures.
I.4.1(c) Admission records.Admitting a transform or macro into the library requires pinning an admission packet object hash under a canonical library namespace. Governance policy requires that admission packets be validated and reference receipts verifying reference plans. This ensures library entries are executable and verifiable under pinned policies.
I.4.1(d) Governance as plans.All governance actions are executed as plans producing ledgers and receipts. There is no out-of-band mutation of the library or policy objects. This makes governance itself replay-verifiable and auditable.
I.4.2 Supersession policy and refinement requirements
I.4.2(a) Supersession policy object.[\mathsf{SupPol}:=(h(\mathsf{RefOrder}),h(\mathsf{SupSchema}),h(\mathsf{ReqProofs}),h(\mathsf{IndexRules})).]
(\mathsf{RefOrder}) defines refinement preorder on claim strength (invariants/tolerances/verification levels/policy strictness).
(\mathsf{SupSchema}) defines supersession record schema.
(\mathsf{ReqProofs}) binds required proof method ids for supersession (refinement proof).
(\mathsf{IndexRules}) defines how indices are updated when supersession occurs.
I.4.2(b) Supersession legality.Supersession of a pinned artifact is legal only if:
a refinement proof object verifies and establishes new artifact is at least as strong as old under (\mathsf{RefOrder}),
supersession record binds old/new hashes and refinement proof hash,
contradiction policy permits overwrite/supersession in that namespace.
I.4.2(c) Replacement vs supersession.Replacement without refinement proof is not supersession and may be forbidden in protected namespaces. If replacement is permitted, it must be disambiguated under a different key or recorded as an explicit non-refinement replacement with incompatibility markers.
I.4.2(d) Audit obligations for supersession.Supersession steps must emit evidence binding:
old/new hashes,
refinement proof hash,
policy hashes,
affected indices.This evidence is retained according to retention class and is required for public audit of library evolution.
I.4.3 Deprecation policy and incompatibility markers
I.4.3(a) Deprecation policy object.[\mathsf{DepPol}:=(h(\mathsf{DepSchema}),h(\mathsf{ReasonCodes}),h(\mathsf{CompatMarkers}),h(\mathsf{ReplacementRules})).]Deprecation records may declare:
deprecated artifact hash,
reason code (security, incorrectness, replaced by stronger, unsupported),
replacement suggestions (hashes),
compatibility markers (e.g., “incompatible with verifier set vX,” “requires policy bridge,” “no longer admitted”).
I.4.3(b) Incompatibility marker schema.An incompatibility marker is a COS object:[\mathsf{Incompat}:=(h(\mathsf{Obj}),\mathsf{kind},h(\mathsf{PolicyReq}),h(\mathsf{Reason})?,h(\mathsf{Since})?).]Markers are used by compilers to prune infeasible routes and by verifiers to report why an artifact cannot be accepted under current policies.
I.4.3(c) Deprecation enforcement in compilation.Compilers must consult deprecation and incompatibility markers when constructing metro map edges. Deprecated entries may be excluded or assigned high cost depending on policy. Incompatibility markers must cause hard pruning: using incompatible artifacts is ill-typed.
I.4.3(d) Deprecation audit.Deprecation actions are governance steps and must produce receipts. Deprecation evidence must bind:
deprecated artifact hash,
reason code,
associated defect evidence hash (if deprecation is due to detected defects),
replacement hash(es),
and affected policy hashes.
I.4.4 Audit obligations and publication constraints
I.4.4(a) Audit requirement object.[\mathsf{AuditReq}:=(h(\mathsf{DetReq}),h(\mathsf{RetentionReq}),h(\mathsf{DisclosureReq}),h(\mathsf{PublishSchema}),h(\mathsf{ChallengeSchema})).]Audit requirements specify:
which detectors must be enabled for publishable receipts,
which evidence must be retained and included in evidence roots,
which disclosure bundles must accompany public receipts,
the schema for publication packages,
the schema for challenge artifacts (dispute workflows).
I.4.4(b) Publication constraints.To publish a receipt as publicly verifiable, governance policy requires:
receipt mode is selective disclosure,
all required local-check data and checker identities are disclosed according to method disclosure policies,
evidence roots include required audit evidence where mandated,
receipt and publication package are pinned in designated publication namespaces with contradiction control.
I.4.4(c) Challenge workflow objects.A challenge is a COS object:[\mathsf{Challenge}:=(h(\mathsf{Rcpt}),h(\mathsf{Claim}),h(\mathsf{FailEvidence}),h(\mathsf{VerifierCtx}),\mathsf{status}),]binding the receipt, the claim boundary being challenged, deterministic failure evidence, and verifier context hashes. Challenges are verifiable artifacts and can trigger certified repair or deprecation plans.
I.4.4(d) Audit closure theorem (governance).If governance actions (admissions, supersessions, deprecations, policy updates) are executed solely as plans under (\Omega) with required detectors and evidence retention, then the evolution of the library and policies is replay-verifiable and audit-ready. This ensures that trust does not rest on informal administrator authority but on receipts and proof-carrying governance traces.
APPENDIX I — CORRIDOR POLICY ((\Omega)) SCHEMA, PREORDER, AND GOVERNANCE HOOKS
APPENDIX J — NUMERIC POLICIES, QUANTIZATION, AND METRIC CATALOG
This appendix specifies numeric determinism as a first-class policy layer. It defines (i) quantization policies (Q) for integer-coded real values, (ii) floating policies (F) for the restricted cases where IEEE-754 bytes are permitted in committed artifacts, (iii) a pinned catalog of norms/metrics/divergences used by invariant evaluation and drift detection, and (iv) reproducibility test plans ensuring cross-platform byte stability and replay-consistent numeric behavior. All numeric semantics that affect hashing, ordering, legality, or proof verification must be pinned by hash through this appendix’s policy objects.
J.1 Quantization policy schema ((Q))
A quantization policy defines a deterministic mapping between semantic numeric values and canonical integer representations used by COS objects and verifiers.
J.1.1 Fixed-point representation and scaling fields
J.1.1(a) Quantization policy object.A quantization policy is a COS object:[Q := (q_id,q_ver,\mathsf{dtype},\mathsf{scale},\mathsf{offset}?,\mathsf{range},\mathsf{round},\mathsf{overflow},h(\mathsf{LayoutPol})?,h(\mathsf{Adm})?,\mathsf{meta}?),]with (q_id=h(Q)). Fields:
(\mathsf{dtype}) is a pinned enum of integer carrier types (e.g., (\mathsf{I16},\mathsf{I32},\mathsf{I64},\mathsf{Z}) unbounded integer; unbounded is permitted only where storage bounds are enforced elsewhere).
(\mathsf{scale}\in\mathbb{N}_{\ge 1}) is the fixed-point denominator; semantic value is (v = (q+\mathsf{offset})/\mathsf{scale}) or (v=q/\mathsf{scale}) if offset absent.
(\mathsf{offset}\in\mathbb{Z}) is optional and used for biased encodings; default is zero.
(\mathsf{range}=(q_{\min},q_{\max})) defines admissible integer range; it must be consistent with (\mathsf{dtype}) bounds if (\mathsf{dtype}) is bounded.
(\mathsf{round}) is a pinned rounding mode (Section J.1.2).
(\mathsf{overflow}) is pinned overflow behavior (Section J.1.3).
(\mathsf{LayoutPol}) is required when quantizing vectors/tensors whose serialization depends on layout coupling (Section J.1.4).
(\mathsf{Adm}) is an admissibility predicate over semantic inputs (optional; when present, quantization rejects non-admissible semantic values deterministically).
J.1.1(b) Quantize and dequantize functions.Quantization defines deterministic functions:[\mathsf{Quant}_Q:\ \mathbb{R}\to \mathbb{Z}\cup{\bot},\qquad\mathsf{Dequant}_Q:\ \mathbb{Z}\to \mathbb{Q},]with:[\mathsf{Dequant}_Q(q)=\frac{q+\mathsf{offset}}{\mathsf{scale}}.](\mathsf{Quant}_Q) computes the integer (q) that approximates (v\cdot \mathsf{scale}-\mathsf{offset}) under the pinned rounding and overflow rules. If (\mathsf{Adm}) is present and fails, or overflow is (\mathsf{ERROR}) and range is violated, return (\bot).
J.1.1(c) Canonical numeric field encoding.A quantized scalar field in any COS object is encoded as:[(h(Q),q),]where (h(Q)) is the policy hash and (q) is the canonical varint encoding of the signed integer under COS rules. Equality and ordering are defined on this pair; no implicit reinterpretation is permitted.
J.1.1(d) Exactness classification.Given a semantic value (v), quantization is exact iff:[v\cdot \mathsf{scale}-\mathsf{offset}\in\mathbb{Z}\ \text{and}\ q\in[q_{\min},q_{\max}],]and (\mathsf{Quant}_Q(v)\neq \bot). Exactness is not inferred from floating computations; it is determined by the policy and the representability condition.
J.1.2 Rounding modes and canonicalization rules
J.1.2(a) Rounding mode enum.Define a pinned enum (\mathsf{RoundMode}) with canonical tags:[\mathsf{RoundMode}\in{\mathsf{TOWARD_ZERO},\mathsf{FLOOR},\mathsf{CEIL},\mathsf{NEAREST_TIES_EVEN},\mathsf{NEAREST_TIES_AWAY}}.]The quantization policy binds exactly one rounding mode.
J.1.2(b) Deterministic rounding definition.Let (x=v\cdot \mathsf{scale}-\mathsf{offset}). Rounding produces (q=\mathsf{Round}(x)) deterministically:
(\mathsf{TOWARD_ZERO}): (q=\mathrm{sgn}(x)\lfloor |x|\rfloor).
(\mathsf{FLOOR}): (q=\lfloor x\rfloor).
(\mathsf{CEIL}): (q=\lceil x\rceil).
(\mathsf{NEAREST_TIES_EVEN}): choose nearest integer; if tie at half-integer, choose even integer.
(\mathsf{NEAREST_TIES_AWAY}): choose nearest integer; if tie, choose the integer with greater absolute value.
All rounding is defined on rationals derived from pinned computations. If (x) is represented internally via finite precision arithmetic, the representation must be pinned and deterministic; otherwise (x) must be provided already as a rational object.
J.1.2(c) Canonicalization of derived quantizations.If a quantized value is produced by arithmetic on other quantized values, canonicalization requires:
perform arithmetic in exact integer/rational space consistent with the policy (see J.1.4),
apply pinned rounding exactly once at the final quantization step,
record any intermediate overflow events deterministically if overflow policy requires evidence.
Re-quantizing the same semantic value through different computational paths must yield identical (q) when all pinned rules are followed.
J.1.2(d) Tie determinism and forbidden ambiguity.Tie cases must be resolved only by the pinned rounding mode. Any implementation that relies on platform floating rounding or unspecified half-way behavior is invalid. Therefore whenever tie can occur, computations must be performed either in exact rational space or in pinned deterministic higher precision sufficient to determine the tie outcome unambiguously.
J.1.3 Overflow behavior and admissibility predicates
J.1.3(a) Overflow mode enum.Define pinned enum (\mathsf{OverflowMode}):[\mathsf{OverflowMode}\in{\mathsf{ERROR},\mathsf{SATURATE},\mathsf{WRAP}}.](\mathsf{WRAP}) is forbidden by default for legality-critical quantities unless explicitly permitted by corridor policy; it changes semantics and can hide defects.
J.1.3(b) Overflow semantics.After computing candidate (q), if (q\notin[q_{\min},q_{\max}]):
(\mathsf{ERROR}): return (\bot) and emit deterministic failure evidence when used in execution.
(\mathsf{SATURATE}): clamp (q) to (q_{\min}) or (q_{\max}).
(\mathsf{WRAP}): reduce (q) modulo (q_{\max}-q_{\min}+1) into the range (semantics must be explicit and is generally reserved for modular carriers).
Overflow behavior is part of (Q) and therefore part of object identity.
J.1.3(c) Admissibility predicate coupling.(\mathsf{Adm}) predicates may require:
bounded semantic domain (e.g., probabilities in ([0,1])),
finiteness constraints,
monotone constraints,
domain-specific restrictions (e.g., log-domain arguments).Admissibility failures must be deterministic and yield typed evidence codes when quantization is used in required legality checks.
J.1.3(d) Overflow and invariants.If overflow mode is (\mathsf{SATURATE}) or (\mathsf{WRAP}), any invariant computed from such quantized values must explicitly account for the semantic consequences. In particular, invariants claiming exactness ((\varepsilon=0)) are generally incompatible with saturating quantization unless the invariant is explicitly defined in the saturated domain. Such compatibility constraints must be enforced by claim admissibility rules and verifiers.
J.1.4 Vector/tensor quantization and layout coupling
J.1.4(a) Quantized vector object.A quantized vector is a COS object:[\mathsf{QVec}:=(h(Q),\mathsf{len},[q_1,\ldots,q_{\mathsf{len}}]),]with sequence-like encoding preserving index order. Index order is defined by a pinned IndexSpec/LayoutPol. Vectors used in set-like contexts must be canonicalized via explicit ordering rules; otherwise they are sequence-like and order matters.
J.1.4(b) Quantized tensor object.A quantized tensor is a COS object:[\mathsf{QTen}:=(h(Q),\mathsf{dtype},\mathsf{shape},h(\mathsf{LayoutPol}),\mathbf{data}),]where (\mathbf{data}) is a canonical contiguous serialization of the integer array under the pinned layout policy. Layout policy pins:
dimension order,
row-major/column-major rules,
stride policy,
and any padding rules (padding is forbidden unless explicitly declared).
J.1.4(c) Layout-coupled quantization constraints.For certain domains, quantization depends on layout (e.g., block quantization, per-channel scaling). Such policies must be explicit:[Q_{\mathrm{block}} := (Q,\ h(\mathsf{BlockSpec}),\ h(\mathsf{ChannelSpec}),\ h(\mathsf{BlockOrder})).]Block boundaries and channel axes are part of the policy and thus part of object identity. Any mismatch in block partitioning yields different bytes and is a determinism defect.
J.1.4(d) Deterministic reductions.Norm computations and drift measurements often reduce vectors/tensors. Reductions must be deterministic:
fixed traversal order determined by layout policy,
integer arithmetic in a pinned widened accumulator type (e.g., widen to (\mathsf{I128}) or unbounded integer) to prevent overflow ambiguity,
explicit overflow behavior for accumulators (prefer (\mathsf{ERROR}) for legality-critical checks).Reduction policies are part of norm/metric objects (Section J.3).
J.2 Floating policy schema ((F))
Floating is permitted only when explicitly allowed by policy and when raw IEEE-754 bytes are treated as the canonical committed representation under a pinned float policy.
J.2.1 Allowed precisions and rounding mode binding
J.2.1(a) Float policy object.A float policy is a COS object:[F := (f_id,f_ver,\mathsf{precision},\mathsf{round},\mathsf{nanPol},\mathsf{zeroPol},\mathsf{subnormPol},\mathsf{infPol},h(\mathsf{SerPol}),h(\mathsf{Adm})?,\mathsf{meta}?),]with (f_id=h(F)). Fields:
(\mathsf{precision}\in{\mathsf{BIN32},\mathsf{BIN64}}),
(\mathsf{round}\in\mathsf{RoundMode}) (pinned),
(\mathsf{SerPol}) pins serialization rules (endianness, byte order, array packing).
J.2.1(b) Rounding binding scope.Floating rounding mode is relevant only when floating computations are performed and then committed. Any committed float bytes must be produced under the pinned rounding mode, or else must be treated as opaque bytes that cannot be recomputed. If the framework requires recomputation under replay, then floating computations must be fully pinned and deterministic; otherwise float usage must be restricted to contexts where bytes are treated as primary and not recomputed.
J.2.1(c) Float field encoding.A float scalar field is encoded as:[(h(F),\mathbf{b}),]where (\mathbf{b}) are raw IEEE-754 bytes in pinned endianness. Arrays of floats are encoded as canonical byte sequences under pinned layout and serialization policy.
J.2.1(d) Float admissibility.Floating values may be forbidden in certain namespaces (e.g., commitments requiring exactness). Such restrictions are encoded by corridor and namespace policies. If forbidden, any attempt to commit float bytes yields deterministic scope/encoding failure.
J.2.2 NaN canonicalization and forbidden-value rules
J.2.2(a) NaN policy enum.[\mathsf{NaNPol}\in{\mathsf{FORBID},\mathsf{CANONICALIZE}}.]
(\mathsf{FORBID}): any NaN input is invalid; committing NaNs yields deterministic failure.
(\mathsf{CANONICALIZE}): NaNs are permitted but must be normalized to a single pinned canonical NaN bit pattern.
J.2.2(b) NaN canonicalization rule.If (\mathsf{CANONICALIZE}), the policy binds a canonical NaN pattern (\mathbf{b}{\mathsf{NaN}}). Any NaN encountered in a value to be committed must be replaced with (\mathbf{b}{\mathsf{NaN}}) deterministically before hashing and serialization. This eliminates multiple NaN encodings causing hash divergence.
J.2.2(c) Forbidden-value rules.Float policy may forbid:
NaN,
infinities,
subnormals,
signed zero,depending on (\mathsf{infPol},\mathsf{subnormPol},\mathsf{zeroPol}). Forbidden values yield deterministic failure evidence with explicit offending field location when required by (\Omega).
