J.2.2(d) Interaction with metrics.Metrics and invariants cannot rely on unspecified NaN propagation. If floats are used in metric evaluation, NaN handling must be pinned and consistent; otherwise measurement becomes nondeterministic. Preferred practice is quantized integer measurement for all legality-critical metrics.
J.2.3 Signed-zero and subnormal policies
J.2.3(a) Signed-zero policy.[\mathsf{ZeroPol}\in{\mathsf{PRESERVE},\mathsf{NORMALIZE_PLUS}}.]
(\mathsf{PRESERVE}): committed bytes preserve the sign bit of zero.
(\mathsf{NORMALIZE_PLUS}): any (-0) is replaced with (+0) deterministically.
J.2.3(b) Subnormal policy.[\mathsf{SubnormPol}\in{\mathsf{ALLOW},\mathsf{FLUSH_TO_ZERO},\mathsf{FORBID}}.]If (\mathsf{FLUSH_TO_ZERO}), subnormals are deterministically mapped to zero under (\mathsf{zeroPol}). If (\mathsf{FORBID}), any subnormal yields deterministic failure.
J.2.3(c) Inf policy.[\mathsf{InfPol}\in{\mathsf{ALLOW},\mathsf{FORBID}}.]If (\mathsf{FORBID}), any infinity yields deterministic failure. Allowing infinity requires explicit metric semantics for how infinities are treated, which is discouraged for legality-critical checks.
J.2.3(d) Canonicalization order.When multiple canonicalizations apply, apply in deterministic order pinned by (F):
subnormal handling,
signed-zero handling,
NaN handling,
infinity checks,then serialization. This ensures consistent bytes across implementations.
J.2.4 Cross-platform constraints and serializer rules
J.2.4(a) Serializer policy.[\mathsf{SerPol}:=(\mathsf{endianness},\mathsf{arrayPacking},\mathsf{alignment},\mathsf{padPol}).]
(\mathsf{endianness}) is fixed (default little-endian).
(\mathsf{arrayPacking}) pins contiguous layout.
(\mathsf{alignment}) is informational; committed bytes cannot depend on platform alignment.
(\mathsf{padPol}) must forbid padding unless explicitly declared and identical across implementations.
J.2.4(b) Cross-platform determinism constraints.If floating is used in committed artifacts, implementations must:
serialize raw IEEE-754 bytes without using platform-dependent conversions,
avoid extended precision intermediates affecting committed results (or pin intermediate rounding through deterministic procedures),
enforce canonicalization of NaN, signed zero, subnormals.
J.2.4(c) Prohibition of non-IEEE formats.Non-IEEE floating formats are forbidden for committed artifacts. If hardware uses extended precision internally, results must be rounded to the pinned precision deterministically before serialization.
J.2.4(d) Float use boundary.Where exact reproducibility is required, quantized policies (Q) are preferred. Floating policies (F) exist for compatibility with certain domains but must be treated as high-risk for determinism unless strictly pinned and tested.
J.3 Norms, metrics, and divergences
This section defines the canonical metric catalog used for invariant evaluation, drift detection, tolerance comparisons, and optimization risk scoring. Each metric is a pinned object with deterministic evaluation rules on quantized inputs.
J.3.1 Scalar norms and absolute/relative error rules
J.3.1(a) Scalar metric object.A scalar metric is a COS object:[\mathsf{Met1}:=(\mathsf{met_id},\mathsf{kind},h(Q_{\mathsf{meas}}),h(\mathsf{RedPol}),h(\mathsf{OverflowPol}),h(\mathsf{Params})?),]where (\mathsf{kind}\in{\mathsf{ABS},\mathsf{REL},\mathsf{ABS_REL}}) defines error computation:
(\mathsf{ABS}): (|x-y|),
(\mathsf{REL}): (|x-y|/\max(|x|,|y|,\delta)) with (\delta) pinned as an integer floor in quantized units,
(\mathsf{ABS_REL}): lexicographic pair or weighted combination pinned by params.
All computations are performed on quantized integers using pinned scaling rules; division must be defined as a pinned rational comparison or as a pinned fixed-point division policy.
J.3.1(b) Deterministic division policy.Relative errors require division. Division is pinned by a division policy object specifying:
numerator/denominator scaling,
rounding mode,
overflow behavior,
minimum denominator floor (\delta).Verifiers must not use platform floating division for relative error unless explicitly permitted and pinned.
J.3.1(c) Comparison to tolerances.Metrics must output quantized integers in the same units as tolerance bounds, or must output pairs with a pinned comparison rule. The comparison rule is part of the invariant clause; otherwise legality is ill-defined.
J.3.1(d) Forbidden undefined cases.Relative errors with zero denominators are handled by pinned (\delta). Any other undefined behavior (division by zero, NaN propagation) is forbidden in legality-critical metrics.
J.3.2 Vector norms ((\ell_1,\ell_2,\ell_\infty)) and quantized evaluation
J.3.2(a) Vector norm object.A vector norm is a COS object:[\mathsf{Norm}:=(\mathsf{norm_id},\mathsf{kind},h(Q_{\mathsf{meas}}),h(\mathsf{AccPol}),h(\mathsf{RedPol}),h(\mathsf{Params})?),]where (\mathsf{kind}\in{\mathsf{L1},\mathsf{L2},\mathsf{LINF},\mathsf{Lp}}). (\mathsf{AccPol}) pins accumulator widening and overflow behavior.
J.3.2(b) Deterministic reductions.Given quantized difference vector (\mathbf{d}\in\mathbb{Z}^n):
(\ell_1): compute (\sum_i |d_i|) using widened accumulator and fixed traversal order.
(\ell_\infty): compute (\max_i |d_i|) deterministically.
(\ell_2): compute (\sum_i d_i^2) in widened accumulator, then apply a pinned square-root comparison rule:
either compare squared norm to squared tolerance (preferred, avoids sqrt),
or compute integer sqrt under a pinned integer sqrt policy.The chosen rule is pinned in (\mathsf{Params}) of the norm object.
J.3.2(c) General (\ell_p).For (\ell_p) with (p) rational, evaluation must be pinned and typically uses comparisons in powered space to avoid irrational operations. If (p) requires root operations, those operations must be pinned and deterministic; otherwise (\ell_p) is inadmissible for legality checks.
J.3.2(d) Quantized evaluation and scaling.Norm outputs are quantized integers. If squared norms are used, tolerances must be represented in compatible squared units or with explicit conversion rules pinned by policy. Any implicit unit conversion is forbidden.
J.3.3 Operator norms and spectral metrics (bounded checks)
J.3.3(a) Operator metric object.An operator metric is a COS object:[\mathsf{OpMet}:=(\mathsf{opmet_id},\mathsf{kind},h(\mathsf{TB}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(Q_{\mathsf{meas}}),h(\mathsf{Bounds})?,\mathsf{VL}),]where (\mathsf{kind}\in{\mathsf{INDUCED},\mathsf{SPECTRAL_BOUND},\mathsf{LIPSCHITZ_BOUND}}). Operator metrics are verified by bounded checks:
evaluate operator on a pinned test basis,
compute induced norm estimates in quantized arithmetic,
compare to pinned bound.
J.3.3(b) Bounded check semantics.Operator norms are not computed exactly in general. Therefore the metric object must declare:
what is being bounded (upper bound, lower bound, interval),
verification level semantics describing whether the bound is empirical on a finite basis or a certified bound with mechanized certificate.Claims must match verification level; otherwise over-claim is rejected.
J.3.3(c) Spectral metrics.Spectral estimates (e.g., largest singular value) require pinned deterministic algorithms and bounded iteration counts. Iterative methods are permitted only if:
iteration count is pinned,
initialization is deterministic,
rounding/quantization is pinned at each step,
tolerance budgets account for approximation,
and the verifier can reproduce or validate the result under a trace proof regime if required.
J.3.3(d) Use in legality.Operator metrics may be used in legality predicates only when:
the metric method is admitted and locally verifiable,
and the corridor budgets permit it.Otherwise they may be used as advisory scores in routing/optimization but not as hard legality checks.
J.3.4 Probability divergences (KL/TV/JS) in quantized regimes
J.3.4(a) Divergence object.A divergence is a COS object:[\mathsf{Div}:=(\mathsf{div_id},\mathsf{kind},h(Q_{\mathsf{prob}}),h(\mathsf{LogPol}),h(\mathsf{ZeroPol}),h(\mathsf{AccPol}),h(\mathsf{Params})?,\mathsf{VL}),]where (\mathsf{kind}\in{\mathsf{TV},\mathsf{KL},\mathsf{JS}}). Policies bind:
probability quantization,
log evaluation policy (for KL/JS),
handling of zeros,
deterministic accumulation.
J.3.4(b) Total variation (TV).For quantized distributions (p,q) aligned on support:[\mathrm{TV}(p,q)=\tfrac12 \sum_i |p_i-q_i|.]Compute in integer arithmetic; the factor (1/2) is handled by a pinned rational comparison policy (compare ( \sum |p_i-q_i| \le 2\varepsilon)).
J.3.4(c) KL and JS.KL requires (\sum p_i \log(p_i/q_i)). Logs are problematic for determinism; therefore:
log policy must define quantized log approximation or rational bounds,
zero-handling must be explicit (forbid zeros, add smoothing with explicit policy, or treat terms with (p_i=0) as zero),
any smoothing is a semantic change and must be explicit in the policy and accounted in invariants/tolerances.
JS can be computed from KL with intermediate mixture; intermediate quantization must be pinned.
J.3.4(d) Divergences as legality checks.Divergence checks may be legality-critical only when:
the divergence method is admitted with a verifier that can validate the computed value deterministically under pinned approximations,
verification level is sufficient for the claim class,
and corridor budgets allow the computation.Otherwise divergences are restricted to diagnostic evidence or scoring.
J.4 Numeric reproducibility test plans
This section specifies canonical test plans and proof templates that validate numeric determinism across implementations and across platforms.
J.4.1 Deterministic arithmetic conformance tests
J.4.1(a) Conformance plan schema.A numeric conformance plan is a COS plan object whose targets include:
pinning a set of canonical numeric policy objects,
executing deterministic arithmetic kernels over canonical inputs,
emitting receipts and evidence objects committing outputs.
Conformance plans are admitted into the conformance suite (Appendix O) and are used to validate implementation compliance.
J.4.1(b) Required test families.Minimum test families:
varint encoding/decoding round trips and minimal-length checks,
quantization exactness tests for representable rationals,
rounding tie-case tests for each rounding mode,
overflow behavior tests for each overflow mode,
reduction determinism tests for sums and norms under canonical traversal order.
J.4.1(c) Cross-implementation equality criterion.Conformance passes only if:
outputs (quantized integers, vectors, tensors) are byte-identical across implementations,
evidence hashes for drift and comparisons match,
receipts match by hash given identical policies.Any mismatch indicates non-conformance.
J.4.1(d) Failure evidence.Conformance failures must produce deterministic evidence objects localizing:
which policy object hash,
which test vector index,
expected vs observed output bytes hash,
which rounding/overflow/reduction rule is violated.
J.4.2 Quantization equivalence proof templates
J.4.2(a) Proof template object.A quantization equivalence proof template is a COS object defining the structure of proofs that:
a quantized computation matches a declared semantic computation within tolerance,
or two different quantization policies are equivalent on a declared domain subset.
J.4.2(b) Policy equivalence conditions.Two quantization policies (Q_1,Q_2) may be declared equivalent on a domain if there exists a deterministic proof method showing:[\forall v\in \mathsf{Dom}:\ \mathsf{Quant}{Q_1}(v)=\mathsf{Quant}{Q_2}(v),]or bounded difference under a pinned metric. Such proofs require explicit domain predicates and are typically feasible only for restricted domains (finite enumerations or simple affine relationships).
J.4.2(c) Template fields.Template binds:
the two policy hashes,
the domain predicate id hash,
the checker program id hash,
the test basis or enumeration scheme hash,
the verification level.Templates ensure proofs are structured and interoperable.
J.4.2(d) Use in migrations.Quantization equivalence proofs are used for policy migration and for safe reuse of artifacts across numeric policy changes when permitted. Absent such proofs, numeric policy changes produce incompatible receipts and must be treated as distinct claim universes.
J.4.3 Drift measurement and threshold tests
J.4.3(a) Drift test plan.A drift test plan constructs pairs of objects (x,y) with known expected drift under pinned metrics and verifies:
measured drift values are deterministic,
near/ok/fail classification matches pinned thresholds,
evidence payloads are canonical and match across implementations.
J.4.3(b) Threshold rounding tests.Near thresholds use (\lfloor \alpha\varepsilon\rfloor) or equivalent pinned rule. Tests must cover:
boundary cases where (d) equals the near threshold,
cases where (d) equals tolerance,
off-by-one cases induced by quantized threshold computation.
J.4.3(c) Norm correctness tests.Norm test plans validate:
(\ell_1,\ell_\infty) exactness under integer arithmetic,
(\ell_2) squared-norm comparison semantics,
accumulator widening rules preventing overflow ambiguity.
J.4.3(d) Evidence reproducibility.Tests must assert not only numeric outputs but also evidence object hashes and receipt hashes, ensuring complete replay stability for legality-critical measurement evidence.
J.4.4 Floating exception handling and canonical NaN tests
J.4.4(a) Float conformance plan.A float conformance plan is admitted only if floating is permitted by corridor policy. It tests:
serialization endianness,
NaN canonicalization behavior (if allowed),
signed zero normalization,
subnormal handling,
infinity handling.
J.4.4(b) Canonical NaN tests.If (\mathsf{NaNPol}=\mathsf{CANONICALIZE}), the plan must construct multiple NaN payload patterns and verify they all serialize to the single pinned canonical NaN bit pattern. Evidence objects must bind the original bit patterns and the normalized output bytes.
J.4.4(c) Forbidden-value tests.If NaNs/infinities/subnormals are forbidden, the plan must ensure the system rejects them deterministically with correct failure codes and structured diagnostics.
J.4.4(d) Float determinism boundary.The float conformance suite must explicitly delineate which floating operations are allowed to be recomputed under replay and which must be treated as opaque committed bytes. If recomputation is required, the suite must demonstrate identical recomputation across implementations under pinned rounding and canonicalization. If not possible, floating must be restricted to non-recomputed contexts or forbidden in legality-critical claims.
APPENDIX J — NUMERIC POLICIES, QUANTIZATION, AND METRIC CATALOG
APPENDIX K — CONVENTION SPEC CATALOG (SIGN/SCALE/INDEX/UNIT/DOMAIN)
This appendix defines the convention specification objects that eliminate silent representational degrees of freedom. Convention specs are typed, hash-addressed objects; they are the sole admissible mechanism for expressing sign conventions, scaling/normalization conventions, index/centering conventions, unit systems, and parameter/branch domains. Compatibility is decided by equality of convention hashes or by explicit certified bridges whose contracts map one convention spec to another. No implicit coercions are permitted.
A convention bundle is the tuple:[\mathsf{Spec}:=(h(\mathsf{SignSpec}),h(\mathsf{ScaleSpec}),h(\mathsf{IndexSpec}),h(\mathsf{UnitSpec}),h(\mathsf{DomainSpec})),]canonically encoded and hashed. The bundle hash is used for compatibility checks at transform boundaries and commitment boundaries.
K.1 SignSpec schema
K.1.1 Orientation and handedness fields
K.1.1(a) SignSpec object.[\mathsf{SignSpec}:=(s_id,s_ver,\mathsf{domain_tag},\mathsf{handedness},\mathsf{axis_orient},\mathsf{sign_choices},h(\mathsf{PhasePol}),h(\mathsf{ConjPol}),h(\mathsf{CompatPol}),\mathsf{meta}?),]with (s_id=h(\mathsf{SignSpec})). Fields:
(\mathsf{domain_tag}) is a pinned enum identifying the convention domain (e.g., transform kernels, coordinate orientation, residue ordering, operator composition direction).
(\mathsf{handedness}\in{\mathsf{RIGHT},\mathsf{LEFT}}) for coordinate frames where applicable.
(\mathsf{axis_orient}) is a fixed-length vector of axis direction flags (increasing vs decreasing orientation) aligned to a pinned axis index set; absent for domains without axes.
(\mathsf{sign_choices}) is a canonical map of named sign degrees of freedom (e.g., forward exponent sign, inverse exponent sign, kernel sign, action direction sign). Keys are canonical and domain-tagged; values are (\pm 1) encoded as canonical integers.
(h(\mathsf{PhasePol})) binds phase quotient policy (Section K.1.3).
(h(\mathsf{ConjPol})) binds conjugation/adjoint insertion policy (Section K.1.2).
(h(\mathsf{CompatPol})) binds compatibility predicates and bridge mapping class (Section K.1.4).
K.1.1(b) Canonicalization constraints.A SignSpec is well-formed iff:
all map keys are canonical and belong to the domain tag’s allowed key set (domain governance),
all sign values are exactly (-1) or (+1),
axis orientation vector length matches the domain’s required dimension when present,
all referenced policies resolve by hash.
Any sign DOF not explicitly listed is treated as nonexistent; defaults are forbidden unless represented as explicit default policy objects referenced by hash.
K.1.1(c) SignSpec equality.Two sign conventions are identical iff their canonical bytes are equal. No “equivalence up to phase” is inferred at the sign spec level; phase equivalence is handled by PhasePol explicitly.
K.1.1(d) SignSpec scope.SignSpec governs only sign/orientation degrees of freedom. It does not govern scaling, index order, units, or parameter domains; those are separate specs in the convention bundle.
K.1.2 Conjugation/adjoint insertion policy fields
K.1.2(a) ConjPol object.[\mathsf{ConjPol}:=(c_id,c_ver,\mathsf{allow_conj},\mathsf{allow_adjoint},\mathsf{implicit_forbidden},h(\mathsf{ConjRule}),h(\mathsf{AdjRule}),h(\mathsf{ProofReq}),\mathsf{meta}?),]with (c_id=h(\mathsf{ConjPol})).
(\mathsf{implicit_forbidden}=\mathsf{true}) is required by default: any conjugation or adjoint operation must be explicit in the program unless explicitly declared as an internal step whose effect is reflected in deltas and proofs.
(\mathsf{ConjRule}) and (\mathsf{AdjRule}) reference deterministic rule objects specifying how conjugation/adjoint is represented at the object level (e.g., explicit operator insertion, explicit AST rewrite).
(\mathsf{ProofReq}) binds required proof methods when conjugation/adjoint insertion is claimed to preserve specific invariants.
K.1.2(b) Explicitness requirement.If a transform requires conjugation or adjoint insertion to align conventions, then that alignment must be realized by an explicit bridge transform step (or an explicit internal substep declared by contract and surfaced through committed artifacts). Any “implicit conjugation by convention” is illegal.
K.1.2(c) Interaction with invertibility.Conjugation/adjoint insertion can change invertibility tier depending on domain. ConjPol must declare whether conjugation is treated as:
an exact involution (preferred), or
a quotient operation in domains where conjugation changes observables.This must be consistent with loss profiles and invariant clauses.
K.1.2(d) Verification requirements.When conjugation/adjoint insertion affects invariants, the bridge or transform must provide proof witnesses certifying the invariant preservation under the declared ConjPol. Proofs must bind to the ConjPol hash to prevent cross-policy laundering.
K.1.3 Phase quotient options and invariants coupling
K.1.3(a) PhasePol object.[\mathsf{PhasePol}:=(p_id,p_ver,\mathsf{phase_mode},h(\mathsf{EqRel})?,h(\mathsf{CanonRep})?,h(\mathsf{InvCouple}),h(\mathsf{ProofReq}),\mathsf{meta}?),]with (p_id=h(\mathsf{PhasePol})).
(\mathsf{phase_mode}\in{\mathsf{EXACT},\mathsf{MOD_2PI},\mathsf{GLOBAL_PHASE_QUOTIENT}}).
If phase is quotiented, (h(\mathsf{EqRel})) defines the equivalence relation and (h(\mathsf{CanonRep})) defines canonical representative selection.
(h(\mathsf{InvCouple})) binds which invariants are phase-invariant and therefore preserved under quotient semantics.
(h(\mathsf{ProofReq})) binds required proof methods to justify quotient correctness.
K.1.3(b) Invariant coupling rule.An invariant clause that depends on phase must explicitly declare whether it is evaluated:
in exact phase space, or
in quotient phase space under PhasePol.If an invariant is not quotient-invariant, then a transform operating under a quotient phase mode cannot claim to preserve that invariant.
K.1.3(c) Canonical representative selection.If phase is quotiented, canonical representative selection must be deterministic and pinned. Examples include:
choose principal value in a pinned branch range,
choose minimal absolute phase in quantized units with lex tie-break,
choose representative determined by a pinned normalization transform.Representative selection affects bytes and hashes and therefore must be explicit.
K.1.3(d) Phase bridges.Mapping between different phase modes requires explicit phase bridges:
exact-to-quotient: projection transform with loss profile,
quotient-to-exact: recovery transform only if side information exists and is pinned; otherwise impossible.Phase bridges must be admitted and proof-carrying.
K.1.4 Compatibility predicate and bridge class mapping
K.1.4(a) Sign compatibility predicate.Sign compatibility between SignSpec hashes is defined by:[\mathsf{SignCompat}(h(S_1),h(S_2))=\mathsf{true}\iff h(S_1)=h(S_2).]No other compatibility is assumed. If unequal, a bridge is required.
K.1.4(b) Bridge class mapping.A SignSpec includes a bridge-class mapping object:[\mathsf{CompatPol}:=(h(\mathsf{BridgeClassMap}),h(\mathsf{BridgePref}),h(\mathsf{BridgeProofReq})).]
(\mathsf{BridgeClassMap}) maps differences in sign DOFs to required bridge classes (e.g., orientation flip, conjugation insertion, kernel sign flip).
(\mathsf{BridgePref}) binds deterministic preference among candidate bridges (exact over approximate; minimal witness burden).
(\mathsf{BridgeProofReq}) binds required proof methods and minimum verification level for bridges.
K.1.4(c) Difference signature.Define a deterministic difference signature between two SignSpecs:[\Delta_{\mathsf{Sign}}(S_1,S_2):=\mathsf{DiffMap}(\mathsf{sign_choices}_1,\mathsf{sign_choices}_2,\mathsf{handedness},\mathsf{axis_orient},\mathsf{PhasePol},\mathsf{ConjPol}),]encoded canonically. This signature selects bridge templates deterministically. Diff computation must not depend on external state.
K.1.4(d) Compatibility evidence.If sign mismatch is detected, the system emits evidence binding:
(h(S_1)), (h(S_2)),
difference signature hash,
required bridge class token(s),
whether a feasible bridge exists under (\Omega),
and the selected bridge entry id when feasible.
K.2 ScaleSpec schema
K.2.1 Unitary tags and norm-preservation declarations
K.2.1(a) ScaleSpec object.[\mathsf{ScaleSpec}:=(k_id,k_ver,\mathsf{domain_tag},\mathsf{scale_mode},\mathsf{unitary_tag},h(\mathsf{Factors}),h(Q_{\mathsf{scale}}),h(\mathsf{NormBind}),h(\mathsf{CompatPol}),\mathsf{meta}?),]with (k_id=h(\mathsf{ScaleSpec})).
(\mathsf{scale_mode}\in{\mathsf{UNITARY},\mathsf{FWD_SCALE},\mathsf{INV_SCALE},\mathsf{SYMMETRIC},\mathsf{NONE}}).
(\mathsf{unitary_tag}\in{\mathsf{CLAIM_UNITARY},\mathsf{NO_CLAIM}}) declares whether transforms under this spec are intended to preserve a specified norm/energy.
(h(\mathsf{Factors})) binds scaling constants.
(Q_{\mathsf{scale}}) binds quantization policy for representing scale factors (prefer exact rational/fixed-point).
K.2.1(b) NormBind coupling.(\mathsf{NormBind}) binds which norm/energy invariants are intended to be preserved under (\mathsf{UNITARY}) mode and how they are measured (metric id hashes). Without (\mathsf{NormBind}), “unitary” is meaningless and forbidden.
K.2.1(c) Admissibility and honesty.If (\mathsf{unitary_tag}=\mathsf{CLAIM_UNITARY}), then any transform contract using this ScaleSpec must include the corresponding norm invariants in its invariant clause set (or explicitly declare why the claim is local/approximate at a given verification level). Otherwise the unitary tag is an over-claim and must be rejected at admission time.
K.2.1(d) ScaleSpec scope.ScaleSpec governs normalization constants and scale factors; it does not determine units (UnitSpec) and does not determine index ordering (IndexSpec). Cross-coupling must be explicit via DomainSpec constraints and bridge chains.
K.2.2 Scaling factor representation (exact vs bounded)
K.2.2(a) Factors object.[\mathsf{Factors}:=(h(\mathsf{KeySet}),\mathsf{map},\mathsf{exactFlag},h(\mathsf{TolUse})?,h(\mathsf{ProofReq})?).]
(\mathsf{map}) is a canonical map from factor keys (canonical strings) to quantized scalar representations ((h(Q),q)) or to exact rational objects under pinned representation.
(\mathsf{exactFlag}\in{\mathsf{EXACT},\mathsf{BOUNDED}}).
If bounded, (h(\mathsf{TolUse})) declares tolerance usage vector and (h(\mathsf{ProofReq})) binds required proof methods to justify the bound.
K.2.2(b) Exactness constraints.Exact factors must be representable exactly in the pinned numeric domain. If factors are represented as floats, exactness cannot be claimed unless float bytes are treated as exact primary representations and the domain semantics accept them as exact (generally discouraged). Preferred exact factor representations are integers and rationals.
K.2.2(c) Bounded factor semantics.If factors are bounded approximations, the ScaleSpec must declare:
which invariants are affected,
tolerance usage contribution,
and near-threshold mitigation rules for long chains.Using bounded scale factors without tolerance accounting is prohibited and triggers accumulation defects.
K.2.2(d) Factor ordering and identity.Factor maps are canonical maps sorted by key bytes. Therefore factor identity is stable across implementations. Any factor omission is meaningful: absent keys are treated as nonexistent; defaults are forbidden unless explicitly referenced.
K.2.3 Normalization family identifiers and parameterization
K.2.3(a) Normalization family id.ScaleSpec includes a normalization family identifier:[\mathsf{NormFam}:=(\mathsf{fam_id},\mathsf{fam_kind},h(\mathsf{ParamsSchema}),h(\mathsf{Defaults})?,h(\mathsf{ReqProof})?),]where (\mathsf{fam_kind}) identifies the normalization class (e.g., unitary normalization, probability normalization, energy normalization). This allows deterministic selection of normalizers and bridges.
K.2.3(b) Parameterization.Normalization parameters (e.g., dimensionality-dependent scale constants) must be explicit:
either as factor entries keyed by dimension and committed as exact values,
or as deterministic derivation rules pinned by hash with outputs representable exactly or bounded with explicit tolerance usage.
K.2.3(c) Interaction with operators.Operator objects that claim a ScaleSpec must reference the ScaleSpec hash explicitly. Any operator that internally assumes a different scaling without declaring it is ill-formed and will be detected by convention drift detectors.
K.2.3(d) Normalizer insertion points.ScaleSpec may require mandatory normalization insertion points (e.g., before commitments, after bridges). These requirements are encoded in compatibility policy objects and enforced by compilation; omission is illegal.
K.2.4 Compatibility predicate and scale-bridge templates
K.2.4(a) Scale compatibility.[\mathsf{ScaleCompat}(h(K_1),h(K_2))=\mathsf{true}\iff h(K_1)=h(K_2).]If unequal, a scale bridge is required.
K.2.4(b) Scale bridge templates.ScaleSpec binds a scale-bridge template registry:[\mathsf{CompatPol}:=(h(\mathsf{ScaleBridgeMap}),h(\mathsf{Pref}),h(\mathsf{ProofReq})).](\mathsf{ScaleBridgeMap}) maps differences in factors and normalization families to required bridge templates (e.g., multiply by (\alpha), divide by (\alpha), apply unitary normalization). The templates reference:
mapping factor objects,
required invariants,
tolerance usage vectors when bounded,
required proof methods.
K.2.4(c) Exactness preference.Scale bridge selection is deterministic: exact bridges dominate approximate bridges. If exactness is required by goal constraints, approximate scale bridges are infeasible.
K.2.4(d) Evidence coupling.Scale bridge execution must emit evidence binding:
input and output ScaleSpec hashes,
factor difference signature,
applied factor object hash,
proof hash and verification level,
tolerance usage contribution when bounded.
K.3 IndexSpec schema
K.3.1 Index modes and address algebra references
K.3.1(a) IndexSpec object.[\mathsf{IndexSpec}:=(i_id,i_ver,\mathsf{domain_tag},h(\mathcal{A}),\mathsf{index_mode},h(\mathsf{OriginPol}),h(\mathsf{PermPol}),h(\mathsf{LayoutPol}),h(\mathsf{CompatPol}),\mathsf{meta}?),]with (i_id=h(\mathsf{IndexSpec})).
(h(\mathcal{A})) is the address algebra hash (Appendix A).
(\mathsf{index_mode}) specifies the canonical index set mode (0..N-1, centered, residue, path, etc.).
(\mathsf{OriginPol}) specifies origin/centering rules.
(\mathsf{PermPol}) specifies permitted permutations and how they are represented.
(\mathsf{LayoutPol}) pins array layout and traversal order.
(\mathsf{CompatPol}) binds compatibility and bridge mapping.
K.3.1(b) IndexSpec well-formedness.IndexSpec is well-formed iff:
address algebra resolves and is well-formed,
index mode is consistent with the address algebra’s index set (e.g., centered mode must include centering metadata),
layout policy is consistent with the object standard’s array encoding rules,
permutation policy resolves and includes bijectivity proof requirements where applicable.
K.3.1(c) Index mode semantics.Index mode is a pinned enum defining semantic interpretation of indices:
(\mathsf{ZERO_BASED}): indices are (0..N-1),
(\mathsf{CENTERED}): indices represent shifted coordinates; requires origin policy,
(\mathsf{RESIDUE}): indices represent residues mod (m),
(\mathsf{PATH}): structural addresses in AST or graph.
Any use of index mode without required supporting policies is invalid.
K.3.1(d) IndexSpec identity.IndexSpec hashes are used to decide compatibility. No implicit permutation or centering shift is permitted when IndexSpec mismatches; explicit index bridges are required.
K.3.2 Origin/centering rules and grid coupling
K.3.2(a) OriginPol object.[\mathsf{OriginPol}:=(o_id,o_ver,\mathsf{origin_kind},h(\mathsf{Params}),h(\mathsf{GridCouple})?,h(\mathsf{ProofReq})?),]with (\mathsf{origin_kind}\in{\mathsf{AT_ZERO},\mathsf{CENTER},\mathsf{USER_DEFINED}}).
(\mathsf{Params}) includes deterministic rules for computing the origin index (e.g., (\lfloor N/2\rfloor) per axis).
(\mathsf{GridCouple}) binds how origin interacts with grid specs (spacing, boundary conditions).
(\mathsf{ProofReq}) binds required proofs when origin shifts claim invariant preservation (e.g., phase corrections).
K.3.2(b) Centering semantics.Centering changes index interpretation and often couples to phase/scaling conventions. Therefore centering shifts are not “layout changes”; they are semantic convention changes and require explicit bridge steps when mapping between centered and uncentered IndexSpecs.
K.3.2(c) Grid coupling constraints.If IndexSpec is coupled to GridSpec (discrete grids), origin must be consistent with:
grid shape,
boundary conditions,
and any periodicity constraints.These constraints are encoded as deterministic predicates. Violations produce deterministic failures.
K.3.2(d) Origin evidence.When origin/centering is applied or bridged, evidence must bind:
input/output IndexSpec hashes,
origin parameters,
any induced permutation rule ids,
and proof hashes for invariant preservation when required.
K.3.3 Permutation rule objects and bijectivity proofs
K.3.3(a) PermPol object.[\mathsf{PermPol}:=(p_id,p_ver,h(\mathsf{PermKinds}),h(\mathsf{PermRules}),h(\mathsf{BijProofReq}),h(\mathsf{InvProofReq}),h(\mathsf{CompatPol})).]
(\mathsf{PermKinds}) enumerates permitted permutation classes (axis swap, reversal, shift, residue reindexing, AST child reorder under commutativity).
(\mathsf{PermRules}) binds deterministic rule objects for generating permutation maps.
(\mathsf{BijProofReq}) binds required proof methods for bijectivity when permutations are not trivially bijective.
(\mathsf{InvProofReq}) binds required proof methods for invariants preserved by permutations.
K.3.3(b) Permutation object.A permutation is a COS object:[\mathsf{Perm}:=(h(\mathcal{A}{\mathrm{in}}),h(\mathcal{A}{\mathrm{out}}),\mathsf{perm_kind},h(\mathsf{Rule}),h(\mathsf{Params}),h(\mathsf{Proof})?).]
(\mathsf{Rule}) computes (\pi(i)) deterministically.
(\mathsf{Proof}) certifies bijectivity and any required invariant preservation.
K.3.3(c) Bijectivity proof requirements.Bijectivity proofs must be:
trace proofs for finite bounded index sets (full enumeration),
or structural proofs for rule-defined permutations (e.g., affine bijection mod (m) with gcd constraint),
or bounded-check proofs at declared verification levels for large sets.Any claim of bijectivity must match its verification level.
K.3.3(d) Composition and canonicalization.Permutation composition is explicit: composed permutations are represented as composed permutation objects with composed rule references and composed proofs. Implicit composition through repeated application is allowed only when the repeated application appears as explicit bridge steps in the program.
K.3.4 Layout policy binding and canonical traversal rules
K.3.4(a) LayoutPol object.[\mathsf{LayoutPol}:=(l_id,l_ver,\mathsf{layout_kind},\mathsf{endianness},\mathsf{stride_pol},h(\mathsf{AxisOrder}),h(\mathsf{PadPol}),h(\mathsf{CompatPol})).]
(\mathsf{layout_kind}\in{\mathsf{ROW_MAJOR},\mathsf{COLUMN_MAJOR},\mathsf{EXPLICIT}}).
(\mathsf{stride_pol}) pins whether only contiguous layouts are allowed (default: contiguous).
(\mathsf{AxisOrder}) pins axis ordering for serialization.
(\mathsf{PadPol}) forbids padding unless explicitly declared.
K.3.4(b) Canonical traversal.Traversal order for serialization, norm evaluation, and deterministic reductions is derived from LayoutPol. Any deviation is a determinism defect. Layout changes are not permitted implicitly; if LayoutPol differs, either:
the object is not admissible for the required lens, or
an explicit layout-normalizer transform must be inserted (a normalizer, not a bridge, if specs remain same; otherwise a bridge if layout change changes spec).
K.3.4(c) Layout compatibility.Layout compatibility is strict equality of LayoutPol hashes. Layout conversions require explicit normalizers or bridges (depending on whether spec changes). Layout conversions must be admitted and proof-carrying when they claim invariants are preserved.
K.3.4(d) Layout evidence and audit.Any layout conversion must emit evidence binding:
input/output LayoutPol hashes,
conversion transform id,
proof hashes when invariants are claimed,
and any tolerance usage when conversion is approximate (e.g., due to re-quantization).
K.4 UnitSpec and DomainSpec schemas
K.4.1 Unit systems, conversion maps, and exactness flags
K.4.1(a) UnitSpec object.[\mathsf{UnitSpec}:=(u_id,u_ver,\mathsf{unit_system},h(\mathsf{BaseUnits}),h(\mathsf{DerivedUnits})?,h(\mathsf{ConvMap}),\mathsf{exactFlag},h(Q_{\mathsf{unit}})?,h(\mathsf{CompatPol}),\mathsf{meta}?),]with (u_id=h(\mathsf{UnitSpec})).
(\mathsf{unit_system}) is a pinned enum (e.g., SI, canonical abstract units, domain-specific unit systems).
(\mathsf{BaseUnits}) defines base dimensions and their canonical symbols.
(\mathsf{DerivedUnits}) optionally defines derived unit expressions.
(\mathsf{ConvMap}) binds deterministic conversion maps to/from other unit specs when available.
(\mathsf{exactFlag}\in{\mathsf{EXACT},\mathsf{BOUNDED}}).
(Q_{\mathsf{unit}}) pins numeric policy for conversion factors.
K.4.1(b) Conversion map object.A conversion map is a COS object:[\mathsf{ConvMap}:=(h(\mathsf{FromUnit}),h(\mathsf{ToUnit}),h(\mathsf{Rule}),h(\mathsf{Factors}),h(\mathsf{ProofReq})?,h(\mathsf{TolUse})?).]Exact conversions must be representable exactly; bounded conversions must include tolerance usage and required proofs.
K.4.1(c) Unit compatibility.Unit compatibility is strict equality of UnitSpec hashes. Mapping between different unit specs requires explicit unit bridges (transforms) using conversion maps; no implicit unit conversion is permitted.
K.4.1(d) Unit evidence.Unit bridges must emit evidence binding:
input/output UnitSpec hashes,
conversion map hash,
factor objects and exact/bounded flags,
proofs and tolerance usage.
K.4.2 Parameter constraint encoding and boundary policies
K.4.2(a) DomainSpec object.[\mathsf{DomainSpec}:=(d_id,d_ver,h(\mathsf{ParamSchema}),h(\mathsf{Constraints}),h(\mathsf{BoundaryPol}),h(\mathsf{BranchPol})?,h(\mathsf{CompatPol}),\mathsf{meta}?),]with (d_id=h(\mathsf{DomainSpec})).
(\mathsf{ParamSchema}) binds the parameter schema hash.
(\mathsf{Constraints}) is a canonical set of constraint predicate objects.
(\mathsf{BoundaryPol}) specifies what happens at boundary cases (reject, project, clamp) and must be explicit.
(\mathsf{BranchPol}) binds branch policies for multi-valued functions (Section K.4.3).
(\mathsf{CompatPol}) binds compatibility and domain bridge templates.
K.4.2(b) Constraint object schema.Constraints are represented as canonical predicate objects:[\mathsf{Constr}:=(\mathsf{constr_id},\mathsf{kind},h(\mathsf{Var}),h(\mathsf{Rule}),h(\mathsf{Params}),h(\mathsf{ProofReq})?).]
(\mathsf{kind}) includes inequality constraints, discrete membership, modular constraints, gcd/coprimality constraints, boundedness constraints, and structural constraints.
Any constraint requiring proof (e.g., gcd property) must specify proof requirements; otherwise it is evaluated deterministically by computation and recorded as evidence.
K.4.2(c) Boundary policy semantics.Boundary policy defines:
whether out-of-domain inputs cause deterministic failure,
or are mapped into domain by an explicit projection (which is a semantic change and must be represented as a transform; boundary policy cannot silently clamp unless clamping is represented as a declared projection with proofs).Boundary policy is therefore coupled to scope and transform contracts.
K.4.2(d) Domain evidence requirements.Domain violations must produce evidence objects binding:
domain spec hash,
failing constraint id and failing parameter key,
observed parameter bytes hash,
boundary policy action (reject/project) when applicable.This evidence is used by repair planners and by deterministic reroute compilation.
K.4.3 Branch policies for multi-valued functions
K.4.3(a) BranchPol object.[\mathsf{BranchPol}:=(b_id,b_ver,\mathsf{branch_kind},h(\mathsf{Range}),h(\mathsf{Rule}),h(\mathsf{ProofReq})?,h(\mathsf{CompatPol}),\mathsf{meta}?),]with (\mathsf{branch_kind}\in{\mathsf{LOG},\mathsf{ARG},\mathsf{ROOT},\mathsf{CONTINUATION}}).
(\mathsf{Range}) pins the principal branch interval (e.g., ((-\pi,\pi])) in quantized units.
(\mathsf{Rule}) pins deterministic branch selection procedure.
(\mathsf{ProofReq}) binds proof obligations when branch changes are claimed to preserve invariants.
K.4.3(b) Branch selection determinism.Branch selection must not depend on floating noise or implementation-specific comparisons. It must be computed in pinned quantized arithmetic and must be stable under replay.
K.4.3(c) Branch changes as domain bridges.Mapping between different BranchPol hashes is a domain bridge and must be explicit and certified. Branch changes are not “optimizations”; they are representation changes and must be treated as bridges. Any implicit branch change is convention smuggling and is illegal.
K.4.3(d) Branch coupling to invariants.Invariants sensitive to branch choices must bind the BranchPol hash in their SpecReq or DomainSpec requirements. If branch is quotiented (e.g., global phase), that quotient must be explicit (PhasePol) and must be coherent with invariant claims.
K.4.4 Compatibility predicate and domain-bridge templates
K.4.4(a) Domain compatibility.DomainSpec compatibility is strict equality of DomainSpec hashes. If unequal, a domain bridge is required. Domain bridges may be:
restriction bridges (tighten domain),
extension bridges (widen domain) only when explicitly permitted (generally discouraged),
projection bridges (map out-of-domain values into domain) only when explicitly declared and certified.
K.4.4(b) Domain bridge template registry.DomainSpec binds:[\mathsf{CompatPol}:=(h(\mathsf{DomBridgeMap}),h(\mathsf{Pref}),h(\mathsf{ProofReq})).](\mathsf{DomBridgeMap}) maps differences in constraints and branch policies to required domain bridge templates and required proofs.
K.4.4(c) Minimal domain bridge property.If multiple domain bridges could align domains, selection is deterministic and prefers:
restriction (tightening) over projection,
exact constraints enforcement over bounded approximations,
minimal witness burden,
lexicographic tie-break.
This ensures domain alignment does not silently weaken correctness.
K.4.4(d) Domain bridge evidence.Domain bridges must emit evidence binding:
input/output DomainSpec hashes,
failing constraints in the input domain,
bridge template id,
proof hashes and verification level,
and any tolerance usage changes if projection is approximate.
APPENDIX K — CONVENTION SPEC CATALOG (SIGN/SCALE/INDEX/UNIT/DOMAIN)
APPENDIX L — PROOF METHOD LIBRARY: CHECKERS, LEVELS, AND PROOF SCHEMAS
This appendix specifies the proof method library: the canonical method registry, verification levels, checker program contracts, and proof object schemas. Proof in this framework means: a locally verifiable, deterministic artifact whose claim boundary is explicitly declared by invariants, tolerances, conventions, and verification level, and whose verifier is pinned by implementation digest hash. No proof is valid outside its declared boundary; no claim is admissible unless the method registry declares it admissible at the specified verification level.
L.1 Method registry schema
L.1.1 Method id structure and version pinning
L.1.1(a) Method object.A proof method is a COS object:[\mathsf{Method}:=(\mathsf{method_id},\mathsf{ver},h(\mathsf{Digest}),\mathsf{kind},h(\mathsf{InSpec}),h(\mathsf{OutSpec}),h(\mathsf{LCDSpec}),h(\mathsf{ClaimClass}),h(\mathsf{VLPolicy}),h(\mathsf{DiscPol}),h(\mathsf{CostClass}),h(\mathsf{DetReq}),\mathsf{meta}?),]with (\mathsf{method_id}=h(\mathsf{Method})). Fields:
(\mathsf{ver}) is version tuple (major/minor/patch).
(h(\mathsf{Digest})) is the implementation digest hash; it pins the verifier/checker implementation.
(\mathsf{kind}\in{\mathsf{EQ},\mathsf{REWRITE},\mathsf{TRACE},\mathsf{DEP}}) selects the proof family.
(h(\mathsf{InSpec}),h(\mathsf{OutSpec})) bind required input/output object schemas and convention requirements.
(h(\mathsf{LCDSpec})) binds local-check data schema (Section L.1.3).
(h(\mathsf{ClaimClass})) binds admissible claim templates for the method.
(h(\mathsf{VLPolicy})) binds admissible verification levels and their semantics.
(h(\mathsf{DiscPol})) binds disclosure requirements (Section L.1.4).
(h(\mathsf{CostClass})) binds cost accounting caps and complexity metadata.
(h(\mathsf{DetReq})) binds determinism constraints required for this method (numeric policies, ordering policies, seed policies).
L.1.1(b) Method registry object.The method registry is a COS object:[\mathsf{MethReg}:=(\mathsf{reg_ver},[\mathsf{Method}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{Method}M],h(\mathsf{GlobalRules})),]with methods sorted lexicographically by (\mathsf{method_id}). The verifier set hash is:[h{\mathsf{VerSet}}:=h(\mathsf{MethReg}).]This hash is committed in runtime headers, ledger headers, and receipts.
L.1.1(c) Governance and immutability.Method identity is content-addressed. Any change in verifier logic or allowed claims produces a new (\mathsf{method_id}) and thus a new (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}). Therefore method updates are policy changes and must be governed and receipted; silent upgrades are forbidden.
L.1.1(d) Admission predicate.A method is admissible in the registry only if:
its digest is pinned,
its schemas resolve and are valid under COS,
its verification procedure is deterministic and polynomial-time in declared input size (bounded by cost class),
its claim class and verification level policy are coherent (no over-claim),
it has conformance plans/receipts demonstrating deterministic accept/reject behavior on canonical test suites.
L.1.2 Input/output schema requirements and resolvers
L.1.2(a) InSpec/OutSpec objects.Input/output requirements are specified by:[\mathsf{InSpec}:=(h(\mathsf{TypeSetIn}),h(\mathsf{SchemaSetIn}),h(\mathsf{SpecReqIn}),h(\mathsf{DomainReqIn})?),][\mathsf{OutSpec}:=(h(\mathsf{TypeSetOut}),h(\mathsf{SchemaSetOut}),h(\mathsf{SpecReqOut}),h(\mathsf{DomainReqOut})?).]
(\mathsf{TypeSet}) lists admissible COS types for inputs/outputs.
(\mathsf{SchemaSet}) lists admissible schema hashes.
(\mathsf{SpecReq}) binds required convention bundles or required convention components (Sign/Scale/Index/Unit/Domain).
(\mathsf{DomainReq}) optionally binds required domain predicates.
L.1.2(b) Resolver contract.Method verification depends on a deterministic resolver:[\mathsf{Resolve}:\ \mathsf{ObjHash}\to (\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b})\cup{\bot}.]Resolver behavior is constrained:
it must be deterministic,
it must validate returned bytes under schema,
it must reject bytes not matching the requested hash,
it must enforce disclosure policy constraints for public verification modes.
L.1.2(c) Required references and source-root coupling.For each method, the registry may specify a required reference set schema:[\mathsf{ReqRefs}:=(h(\mathsf{NeedLCD}),h(\mathsf{NeedTB}),h(\mathsf{NeedChkProg}),h(\mathsf{NeedInputs}),h(\mathsf{NeedPolicies})),]that must be present in the step’s source root or disclosed bundle. Missing required references cause deterministic method failure.
L.1.2(d) Schema validation at verification time.Before evaluating a proof predicate, a method verifier must validate:
input object types and schema hashes match (\mathsf{InSpec}),
output object types and schema hashes match (\mathsf{OutSpec}),
convention and domain requirements are satisfied or explicitly bridged in the program whose equivalence is being proven.Any mismatch is a (\mathsf{E_SCHEMA}/\mathsf{E_TYPE}/\mathsf{E_POLICY}) failure.
L.1.3 Local-check data schema and required components
L.1.3(a) Local-check data object.Local-check data (LCD) is a COS object:[\mathsf{LCD}:=(h(\mathsf{Method}),h(\mathsf{Claim}),\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{TB}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(\mathsf{NumPol}),h(\mathsf{SpecBind}),h(\mathsf{Aux})?,h(\mathsf{Disclosure})?),]where:
(h(\mathsf{Claim})) binds invariant set, tolerances, loss/quotient semantics, and assumptions (Section L.2),
(\mathsf{VL}) is the verification level (must be allowed by the method’s VL policy),
(h(\mathsf{TB})) is the test basis hash,
(h(\mathsf{ChkProg})) is the checker program descriptor hash,
(h(\mathsf{NumPol})) binds numeric policy hashes required (Appendix J),
(h(\mathsf{SpecBind})) binds convention bundle hashes required (Appendix K),
(\mathsf{Aux}) optionally binds coverage parameters, structural side-condition proofs, or trace transcript policies,
(\mathsf{Disclosure}) optionally binds disclosure requirements (for selective reveal).
L.1.3(b) LCD validity predicate.(\mathsf{LCD}) is valid iff:
(h(\mathsf{Method})) resolves and matches the method being used,
claim object is admissible for the method at (\mathsf{VL}),
test basis resolves and is valid under the method’s test-basis schema,
checker program resolves and matches required digest and semantics,
numeric and convention bindings satisfy method determinism requirements,
auxiliary fields, if present, are schema-valid and coherent.
LCD is the “proof environment”; it prevents proofs from silently changing test regimes, rounding policies, or conventions.
L.1.3(c) Test basis object.A test basis is a COS object:[\mathsf{TB}:=(\mathsf{tb_id},\mathsf{tb_kind},h(\mathsf{Gen}),h(\mathsf{Elems})?,h(\mathsf{Seed})?,\mathsf{size},h(\mathsf{OrderPol}),h(\mathsf{DedupPol})?).]
(\mathsf{tb_kind}) identifies basis type: explicit list, deterministic generator, deterministic coverage grid, structural basis (AST patterns), route basis (edge sequences).
If generated, (h(\mathsf{Gen})) is a deterministic generator rule; if seeded, (h(\mathsf{Seed})) must be pinned and committed in provenance.
(\mathsf{OrderPol}) pins basis ordering (determinism).
(\mathsf{DedupPol}) pins deduplication (optional).
L.1.3(d) Claim boundary coupling.LCD’s claim object must bind:
the invariants and tolerances being proven,
convention bundle requirements,
loss/quotient semantics if applicable,
and assumptions required for the claim.No proof is valid without an explicit claim object; the method verifier must reject any proof with missing or mismatched claim binding.
L.1.4 Disclosure requirements and verification-mode flags
L.1.4(a) Disclosure policy object.A method includes a disclosure policy:[\mathsf{DiscPol}:=(\mathsf{modeFlags},h(\mathsf{ReqReveal}),h(\mathsf{OptReveal}),h(\mathsf{AllowOpaque}),h(\mathsf{RedactPol})?).]
(\mathsf{modeFlags}) indicates which verification modes the method supports: (\mathsf{HASH_ONLY}), (\mathsf{SELECTIVE}), or both.
(\mathsf{ReqReveal}) is the set of object classes that must be revealed for public verification (e.g., TB, ChkProg descriptor, numeric policy, convention specs).
(\mathsf{OptReveal}) is optional reveal set (for richer audit).
(\mathsf{AllowOpaque}) indicates whether the method can verify claims without revealing the underlying payload objects (rare; must be explicit and usually requires additional cryptographic mechanisms, otherwise disallowed).
L.1.4(b) Verification-mode flags.If a goal requires public verifiability, the compiler must choose methods whose disclosure policies are compatible with selective disclosure, and must include the required revealed artifacts in a disclosure bundle. Using a method incompatible with required verification mode is infeasible.
L.1.4(c) Redaction policy coupling.If evidence or LCD can be redacted, the method must declare which fields may be redacted without invalidating verification. Redaction must be deterministic and must preserve the ability to verify claim boundaries where required; otherwise the receipt cannot claim public verifiability.
L.1.4(d) Disclosure completeness check.A verifier validating a selectively disclosed proof must check:
all required revealed objects are present and match the hashes committed by LCD and method registry,
any redaction markers follow the pinned redaction policy,
any missing required reveal yields deterministic failure (\mathsf{E_DISCLOSE}).
L.2 Verification levels and admissible claim classes
Verification levels define the strength and boundaries of what a method can certify. Levels are a pinned enum with method-specific admissibility rules.
L.2.1 VL0 finite-basis regime
L.2.1(a) Semantics.(\mathsf{VL0}) certifies that a predicate holds on a finite, explicitly specified test basis. It does not claim coverage beyond that basis.
L.2.1(b) Required LCD components.At (\mathsf{VL0}), LCD must include:
explicit TB (or deterministic generator with fully disclosed basis elements),
checker program id/digest,
numeric policies,
convention bundle bindings,
claim object with invariants and tolerances.
L.2.1(c) Admissible claim classes.Admissible claims at (\mathsf{VL0}) include:
equality/bounded drift on the test basis,
structural side conditions that can be checked deterministically,
feasibility predicates on enumerated candidates.Claims of global equivalence or universal properties are not admissible at (\mathsf{VL0}).
L.2.1(d) Deterministic evaluation rule.The checker must evaluate the predicate on TB in canonical order and must aggregate results deterministically. Any failure yields deterministic evidence including the first failing test element under pinned ordering.
L.2.2 VL1 finite-basis + structural side-conditions
L.2.2(a) Semantics.(\mathsf{VL1}) extends (\mathsf{VL0}) by including deterministic structural side-conditions that narrow the claim space, such as:
syntactic normal form equality,
commutation relations verified structurally,
type and schema invariants,
algebraic constraints on parameters.
L.2.2(b) Required components.In addition to (\mathsf{VL0}) components, LCD includes:
side-condition objects (proofs or deterministic checkers) bound by hash,
structural reduction rules (canonical normalization policy hashes).
L.2.2(c) Admissible claim classes.VL1 may certify stronger claims than VL0 when side conditions justify them, but the claim class must be explicitly declared in the method’s claim class policy. For example:
program equivalence under canonical program normal form + finite-basis checks,
invertibility of a permutation rule given bijectivity proof.
L.2.2(d) Over-claim rejection.A method verifier must reject any claim at VL1 that implies global coverage unless the method’s claim class policy explicitly defines the structural side-conditions sufficient for that implication and the verifier can check them deterministically.
L.2.3 VL2 deterministic expanded test generation regime
L.2.3(a) Semantics.(\mathsf{VL2}) certifies predicates over an expanded test suite derived deterministically from pinned generation rules and pinned seeds (if allowed). The regime aims at broader coverage than VL0/VL1, still bounded and replayable.
L.2.3(b) Deterministic generation.VL2 requires that:
generation rule id is pinned,
seed object hash is pinned and committed if seeds are used,
generator produces a deterministic ordered test list,
generation size bounds are pinned and respected.
L.2.3(c) Admissible claim classes.VL2 may certify:
bounded coverage over a structured test space (e.g., parameter grids),
probabilistic-like coverage only if generation is deterministic and the claim class describes the coverage precisely (e.g., “covers all grid points in a deterministic lattice”).It cannot claim true randomness unless randomness is treated as pinned input and claim semantics are explicit.
L.2.3(d) Cost caps.VL2 verification cost can be high; method cost class must specify caps and the corridor must permit it. If verification exceeds caps, the verifier must fail deterministically with cost evidence.
L.2.4 VL3 bounded coverage + VL4 mechanized certificate hooks
L.2.4(a) VL3 semantics.VL3 certifies bounded coverage guarantees defined by a pinned coverage policy, such as:
deterministic (\varepsilon)-net coverage over a domain,
exhaustive enumeration over a finite bounded set,
deterministic partition coverage with complete representatives.
VL3 claims must explicitly state the coverage model and its bounds; the verifier must be able to check that the coverage regime was executed fully within caps.
L.2.4(b) VL4 semantics.VL4 supports mechanized certificate hooks: the proof includes a mechanized certificate object verified by a pinned proof-checker digest. VL4 does not assume a specific external theorem prover; it assumes a pinned checker whose correctness is part of the TCB for that method id.
L.2.4(c) Mechanized certificate object.A mechanized certificate is a COS object:[\mathsf{MechCert}:=(h(\mathsf{Checker}),h(\mathsf{CertBytes}),h(\mathsf{Claim}),h(\mathsf{Refs}),h(\mathsf{Env})),]where:
(h(\mathsf{Checker})) pins the proof-checker digest,
(\mathsf{CertBytes}) is the certificate payload (opaque bytes),
refs bind required objects and assumptions,
env binds policy hashes and any required encoding rules.
The checker must validate the certificate deterministically.
L.2.4(d) Claim admissibility under VL3/VL4.VL3/VL4 claims can be strong but must still be within method claim class policy. Over-claims are rejected: “mechanized certificate present” does not imply admissibility unless the checker semantics correspond to the claim object and the method registry allows it.
L.3 Checker program contract
Checker programs implement predicate evaluation and drift computation. They are pinned by digest hashes and governed as part of the verifier set.
L.3.1 Checker identity hash and implementation digest binding
L.3.1(a) Checker program descriptor.[\mathsf{ChkProg}:=(\mathsf{chk_id},\mathsf{ver},h(\mathsf{Digest}),h(\mathsf{IO}),h(\mathsf{NumReq}),h(\mathsf{SpecReq}),h(\mathsf{CostClass}),h(\mathsf{DetReq}),\mathsf{meta}?),]with (\mathsf{chk_id}=h(\mathsf{ChkProg})). The digest pins implementation. Any change yields a new checker id and invalidates proofs that reference the old id unless superseded by explicit proof regeneration.
L.3.1(b) IO schema binding.(h(\mathsf{IO})) binds expected input/output object schemas and required LCD schema. Checker must reject inputs that do not conform, yielding deterministic schema failure evidence.
L.3.1(c) Stability under policy hashes.Checker programs must bind to numeric policy and convention requirements and must behave deterministically under those policies. Any checker that depends on unspecified floating behavior or unordered iteration is invalid for legality-critical proofs.
L.3.1(d) Checker admission.Checkers are admitted through method registry governance and conformance tests demonstrating deterministic behavior and correct rejection of over-claims under known test suites.
L.3.2 Input resolution, quantization, and convention binding
L.3.2(a) Resolution protocol.Checker resolves required objects by hash:
input objects,
output objects,
LCD components (TB, claim object, numeric policies, spec hashes),
any auxiliary objects referenced by LCD.
Resolution must be deterministic and must verify hash correctness and schema validity.
L.3.2(b) Quantization and numeric policies.All numeric computations relevant to proof outcomes must operate on quantized representations under pinned policies (Appendix J). If checker requires floating, float policy must be pinned and conformance-tested; otherwise checker must convert floats to quantized integers deterministically before comparisons.
L.3.2(c) Convention binding.Checker must verify that inputs/outputs conform to required convention bundles. If spec mismatch exists, checker must reject unless the proof explicitly includes bridges and the claim semantics permit evaluating in a quotient space. Implicit convention coercion is forbidden.
L.3.2(d) Domain and branch constraints.If checker semantics involve multi-valued functions or domain restrictions, checker must enforce DomainSpec and BranchPol requirements as pinned by LCD. Any branch ambiguity must yield deterministic failure unless explicitly quotiented.
L.3.3 Predicate semantics and drift computation rules
L.3.3(a) Predicate result schema.Checker outputs a deterministic result object:[\mathsf{ChkRes}:=(\mathsf{pass},\mathsf{firstFail}?,h(\mathsf{MeasRoot})?,h(\mathsf{Summary}),h(\mathsf{Evidence})?),]where:
(\mathsf{pass}\in{\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}}),
(\mathsf{firstFail}) identifies the first failing test element under pinned ordering when applicable,
(\mathsf{MeasRoot}) optionally commits to measurement evidence,
(\mathsf{Summary}) includes quantized drift summaries,
and optional evidence objects for diagnostics.
L.3.3(b) Drift computation.Drift is computed from quantized measurements:[d := |\mathsf{Meas}(x)-\mathsf{Meas}(y)|_{\mathsf{Norm}},]with deterministic reductions and accumulator policies pinned. Threshold comparison uses quantized tolerances. Near thresholds are computed deterministically using pinned rounding rules.
L.3.3(c) Structural side-condition semantics.If checker includes structural side-conditions (VL1), it must verify canonical normal forms and structural predicates deterministically, producing explicit failure codes when structural conditions fail.
L.3.3(d) Proof outputs as artifacts.If policy requires, checker may emit measurement evidence objects into the proof store (via proof deltas) and commit their Merkle root in the proof object. Emission must be deterministic and budgeted; otherwise checker must rely on compact summaries only.
L.3.4 Complexity class, cost accounting, and caps
L.3.4(a) Complexity classification.Each checker binds a cost class specifying:
expected asymptotic complexity in terms of input sizes and test basis size,
maximum allowed test basis size for this checker under typical corridors,
per-invocation compute unit estimates.
This metadata is used by compilation to prune infeasible proofs and by execution to enforce budgets.
L.3.4(b) Cost accounting rules.Checkers contribute to step cost vectors. If (\Omega) requires deterministic cost recomputation, checkers must expose deterministic cost accounting counters (e.g., number of operations, number of tests executed) in a canonical form that can be recomputed under replay.
L.3.4(c) Caps and early termination.Checkers may terminate early on the first failing test element only if the method registry policy permits it and defines the exact ordering for “first fail.” Early termination must be deterministic; otherwise it introduces nondeterminism in which failure evidence is produced.
L.3.4(d) Failure on cap exceedance.If checker exceeds its cap (test basis size too large, computation too expensive), it must fail deterministically with a cost failure evidence object. It must not silently reduce coverage or skip tests; any reduction must be explicit and would change verification level semantics, requiring a different claim boundary.
L.4 Proof object schema catalog
Each proof family has a canonical schema. Proof objects are COS objects stored in the proof store and referenced by proof witnesses. Proof objects do not authorize writes; authorization is provided by step-local witnesses (Appendix B).
L.4.1 Equivalence proof schemas
L.4.1(a) Equivalence proof object.[\mathsf{ProofEq}:=(\mathsf{proof_id},\mathsf{method_id},h(\mathsf{Claim}),\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{LCD}),h(\mathsf{LHS}),h(\mathsf{RHS}),h(\mathsf{SpecBind}),h(\mathsf{TB}),h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(\mathsf{Result}),h(\mathsf{Refs})?,\mathsf{meta}?),]with (\mathsf{proof_id}=h(\mathsf{ProofEq})). Fields bind claim boundary, LCD, both sides being compared, spec bindings, test basis, checker id, and checker result.
L.4.1(b) Semantics.(\mathsf{ProofEq}) certifies equivalence of LHS and RHS under the claim boundary at the declared verification level. It does not certify more. Any downstream requirement stronger than the claim boundary requires a refinement proof or proof regeneration.
L.4.1(c) Admissibility constraints.ProofEq is admissible only if method registry allows (\mathsf{kind}=\mathsf{EQ}) and the claim class is admissible at (\mathsf{VL}). Spec bindings must match or be explicitly bridged; otherwise proof is ill-typed.
L.4.1(d) Evidence and disclosure.ProofEq may include measurement evidence roots and disclosure bundle references. If selective disclosure is required, the proof must reference a disclosure bundle satisfying method disclosure policy.
L.4.2 Rewrite proof schemas
L.4.2(a) Rewrite proof object.[\mathsf{ProofRw}:=(\mathsf{proof_id},\mathsf{method_id},h(\mathsf{Claim}),\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{LCD}),h(\Pi),h(\Pi'),h(\mathsf{Rule}),h(\mathsf{MatchEv}),h(\mathsf{GuardEv}),h(\mathsf{SpecBind}),h(\mathsf{Result}),h(\mathsf{Refs})?,\mathsf{meta}?).]Binds input/output program hashes, rewrite rule id, match and guard evidence, and checker results.
L.4.2(b) Semantics.ProofRw certifies that applying rule at the specified match location under specified guards transforms (\Pi) to (\Pi') while preserving the claim boundary. It also supports non-weakening proofs when required by optimization policy.
L.4.2(c) Match and guard evidence.Match evidence includes:
interval indices,
variable substitution map (\theta),
matched step descriptors and parameter hashes.Guard evidence includes predicate ids and deterministic outcomes. Both are canonical objects.
L.4.2(d) Composition.Multiple rewrite proofs can be chained. A composite rewrite proof may be defined as a proof object that binds the sequence of intermediate program hashes and proofs; this is admissible only if verifier supports it and claim class policy allows composition without loss of soundness.
L.4.3 Trace proof schemas
L.4.3(a) Trace proof object.[\mathsf{ProofTr}:=(\mathsf{proof_id},\mathsf{method_id},h(\mathsf{Claim}),\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{LCD}),\mathsf{trace_kind},h(\mathsf{InputSpec}),h(\mathsf{Transcript}),h(\mathsf{Bound})?,h(\mathsf{Result}),h(\mathsf{Refs})?,\mathsf{meta}?).]Trace proofs certify outcomes of bounded searches or procedures by providing a transcript that can be verified deterministically.
L.4.3(b) Transcript requirements.Transcripts must be:
canonical objects or canonical byte strings with schema hashes,
ordered deterministically,
sufficient for verification without external oracles,
bounded in size or committed in Merkleized segments with membership proofs.If transcript is too large, it must be chunked deterministically with chunk roots committed.
L.4.3(c) Bound objects.Trace proofs may include bound objects describing error bounds, coverage bounds, or certificate bounds. Bounds must be pinned by hash and verifiable from the transcript or from mechanized certificate hooks.
L.4.3(d) Use cases.Trace proofs are used for:
verifying candidate pruning steps,
verifying sieve or constraint satisfaction transcripts,
verifying bounded optimization passes,
verifying bounded enumeration properties (bijectivity for finite index sets).
L.4.4 Dependency proof schemas
L.4.4(a) Dependency proof object.[\mathsf{ProofDep}:=(\mathsf{proof_id},\mathsf{method_id},h(\mathsf{Claim})?,\mathsf{VL},h(\mathsf{LCD})?,h(\mathsf{DownPlan}),[\ h(\mathsf{UpRcpt}i)\ ]{i=1}^k,[\ h(\mathsf{PinRec}j)\ ]{j=1}^m,[\mathsf{MP}_{i,j}],h(\mathsf{SrcBind}),h(\mathsf{Compat}),h(\mathsf{Result}),\mathsf{meta}?).]Dependency proofs certify that downstream computations consume specific upstream artifacts by membership proofs under upstream receipt pin roots and by binding those hashes into downstream provenance roots.
L.4.4(b) Compatibility binding.Dependency proof includes compatibility object (h(\mathsf{Compat})) binding:
required policy hash equality or policy bridge receipts,
required convention compatibility or explicit bridges,
required claim strength preorder checks (upstream artifacts strong enough for downstream needs).
L.4.4(c) Result semantics.ProofDep certifies anti-equivocation: a downstream plan cannot claim to depend on an upstream artifact without a valid membership proof and source-root binding. It does not certify the semantic truth of upstream artifact beyond the upstream receipt’s claim boundary.
L.4.4(d) Verification requirements.Verifier checks:
upstream receipts validity or trusted cache policy,
Merkle membership proofs correctness,
downstream provenance commitments include consumed hashes,
policy and convention compatibility constraints satisfied.Any failure yields deterministic evidence suitable for repair or governance.
APPENDIX L — PROOF METHOD LIBRARY: CHECKERS, LEVELS, AND PROOF SCHEMAS
APPENDIX M — RECEIPT FORMATS, DEPENDENCY PROOFS, AND PUBLICATION PACKAGES
This appendix specifies receipt formats, dependency proof variants, and publication packages enabling compositional verification with explicit disclosure boundaries. Receipts are the portable verification unit; dependency proofs are the formal mechanism for proof reuse without equivocation; publication packages define how receipts and supporting artifacts are made publicly verifiable under selective disclosure and redaction policies. Indices and supersession graphs provide deterministic navigation and governance over evolving receipts.
M.1 Receipt schema variants
A receipt is a COS object committing policy hashes, outcome hashes, and Merkle roots. Variants differ by how much of the ledger and supporting data is included or referenced.
M.1.1 Full receipts (ledger-linked)
M.1.1(a) Full receipt schema.[\mathsf{RcptFull}:=(h(\mathsf{RcptBase}),h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),h(\mathsf{LedgerRef}),h(\mathsf{Plan})?,h_{\mathsf{final}},r_{\mathsf{pins}},r_{\mathsf{wits}},r_{\mathsf{ev}}?,h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{mode},h(\mathsf{DiscBundle})?).]
(h(\mathsf{LedgerRef})) is a content-addressed reference to ledger step records:
either a Merkle root over step record hashes,
or a CAS root hash of a ledger object that includes all step records,
the ledger reference type is pinned by the ledger format policy.
(h(\mathsf{RcptBase})) binds the receipt base schema (field set and ordering), enabling strict validation.
M.1.1(b) Verification semantics.A full receipt is sufficient for complete replay verification given access to:
the ledger object or step record set committed by (h(\mathsf{LedgerRef})),
proof store objects referenced by witnesses and LCDs,
pinned policy objects referenced by hashes.
M.1.1(c) Use cases.Full receipts are used for:
archival certification,
audits requiring full trace availability,
dispute resolution where independent parties must replay from genesis or from committed checkpoint roots.
M.1.1(d) Size control.To keep size bounded, full receipts do not embed step records inline; they embed a ledger reference root. Step records themselves remain in CAS and can be fetched by membership proofs under the ledger reference root.
M.1.2 Minimal receipts (membership-proof oriented)
M.1.2(a) Minimal receipt schema.[\mathsf{RcptMin}:=(h(\mathsf{RcptBase}),h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),h_{\mathsf{final}},r_{\mathsf{pins}},r_{\mathsf{wits}},r_{\mathsf{ev}}?,h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{mode},h(\mathsf{DiscBundle})?).]Minimal receipts omit (h(\mathsf{LedgerRef})) and (h(\mathsf{Plan})) unless required. They commit to roots only, enabling membership-proof verification when the ledger header is already trusted/verified.
M.1.2(b) Verification semantics.Minimal receipt validity requires that a verifier either:
replay-verify the ledger separately and trust (h(\mathsf{LedHdr})) as verified, or
accept a pinned trust policy stating that the ledger header is in a trusted set.
Then membership proofs validate inclusion of specific output pins or witnesses under the committed roots.
M.1.2(c) Use cases.Minimal receipts are used for:
high-volume publication where full ledgers are not distributed,
caching hub results,
dependency linking where upstream ledger validity is assumed by trust policy or cached verification.
M.1.2(d) Boundaries.Minimal receipts do not by themselves enable independent replay unless the ledger is obtainable elsewhere. This is not a weakness; it is an explicit verification-mode boundary.
M.1.3 Hash-only receipts (commitment-only)
M.1.3(a) Hash-only receipt schema.[\mathsf{RcptHash}:=(h(\mathsf{RcptBase}),h(\mathsf{LedHdr})?,h(\mathsf{Plan})?,h_{\mathsf{final}}?,r_{\mathsf{pins}}?,r_{\mathsf{wits}}?,r_{\mathsf{ev}}?,h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{mode}=\mathsf{HASH_ONLY},h(\mathsf{Note})?).]Hash-only receipts are commitments that may omit some roots when publication policy requires minimal leakage; omitted fields must be indicated explicitly by presence markers in the schema. Any omitted fields reduce what can be verified publicly; this boundary is explicit.
M.1.3(b) Semantics.Hash-only receipts assert existence of an execution and commitment of certain hashes, but do not guarantee that any party can verify them without additional access. They are used where confidentiality prevents disclosure of ledger references or output roots.
M.1.3(c) Admissibility constraints.Hash-only receipts are admissible only when:
the corridor policy permits hash-only mode for the goal class,
method disclosure policies for required proofs do not require public disclosure,
and publication policy accepts “commitment-only” status.
M.1.3(d) Upgrade path.Hash-only receipts may be upgraded to selective disclosure receipts by publishing a disclosure bundle that reveals enough local-check data and membership proofs to allow public verification. Upgrade is done by issuing a new receipt that binds the old receipt hash and includes the newly disclosed roots; this is not mutation of the old receipt.
M.1.4 Selective-disclosure receipts and bundle objects
M.1.4(a) Selective receipt schema.Selective disclosure receipts are (\mathsf{RcptFull}) or (\mathsf{RcptMin}) with (\mathsf{mode}=\mathsf{SELECTIVE}) and an attached disclosure bundle hash:[h(\mathsf{DiscBundle})\neq \varnothing.]
M.1.4(b) Disclosure bundle object.[\mathsf{DiscBundle}:=(h(\mathsf{BundleSpec}),h(\mathsf{Objs}),h(\mathsf{MerkleProofs})?,h(\mathsf{RedactMarkers})?,h(\mathsf{PolicySet}),\mathsf{meta}?),]where:
(\mathsf{BundleSpec}) lists required disclosed object classes and schemas by method disclosure policies,
(\mathsf{Objs}) is a canonical set of disclosed objects (or their hashes plus bytes),
(\mathsf{MerkleProofs}) optionally contains membership proofs for outputs/witnesses under the receipt roots,
(\mathsf{RedactMarkers}) includes deterministic redaction markers for withheld sub-objects,
(\mathsf{PolicySet}) binds policy hashes ensuring disclosed objects are interpreted correctly.
M.1.4(c) Disclosure completeness predicate.A selective receipt is publicly verifiable iff the disclosure bundle satisfies:
every method id’s required reveal set for proofs implicated by the receipt,
required numeric and convention policies,
required test bases and checker identities,
and any required membership proofs for claimed outputs and dependencies.
M.1.4(d) Minimality principle.Disclosure bundles should reveal only what is required. Minimality is policy-defined and deterministic where enforced: if multiple equivalent disclosure options exist, choose the lexicographically minimal bundle hash among those that satisfy disclosure requirements, to stabilize publication artifacts.
M.2 Dependency proof schema variants
Dependency proofs certify that downstream computations consume specific upstream artifacts and that policy/convention compatibility holds.
M.2.1 Pin membership proofs and consumption binding
M.2.1(a) Pin dependency proof.[\mathsf{DepPins}:=(h(\mathsf{DownPlan}),h(\mathsf{DownRcpt})?,[\ h(\mathsf{UpRcpt}i)\ ]{i=1}^k,[\ h(\mathsf{PinRec}j)\ ]{j=1}^m,[\mathsf{MP}_{i,j}],h(\mathsf{SrcBind}),h(\mathsf{Compat}),h(\mathsf{Result})).]
(\mathsf{MP}_{i,j}) proves (h(\mathsf{PinRec}j)) is included in upstream receipt (i)’s pin root (r{\mathsf{pins}}).
(\mathsf{SrcBind}) commits that downstream provenance roots include the consumed pin record hashes (or their value hashes, per pinned provenance inclusion policy).
M.2.1(b) Consumption binding variants.Two permitted consumption binding modes (must be explicit in (\mathsf{SrcBind})):
Pin-record binding: downstream source roots include hashes of full pin records.
Value-hash binding: downstream source roots include hashes of values referenced by pin records; pin records are still referenced via dependency proofs.Pin-record binding is stronger; value-hash binding is allowed only when the system’s provenance policy permits it and contradiction risks are controlled.
M.2.1(c) Verification procedure.Verify:
upstream receipt validity or trusted cache policy,
Merkle membership proofs for each consumed pin record hash,
downstream provenance contains required consumed hashes,
policy compatibility holds.
M.2.1(d) Anti-equivocation guarantee.If Merkle proofs verify and provenance binding includes consumed hashes, downstream cannot equivocate about consumption: it is cryptographically committed and replay-verifiable.
M.2.2 Witness membership proofs and method binding
M.2.2(a) Witness dependency proof.Certain workflows require proving that a downstream claim relied on upstream witnesses or proof methods. Define:[\mathsf{DepWits}:=(h(\mathsf{DownPlan}),[\ h(\mathsf{UpRcpt}_i)\ ],[\ h(\mathsf{Wit}j)\ ],[\mathsf{MP}^{\mathsf{wit}}{i,j}],h(\mathsf{MethodBind}),h(\mathsf{Compat}),h(\mathsf{Result})).]
(\mathsf{MP}^{\mathsf{wit}}_{i,j}) proves witness hash (h(\mathsf{Wit}j)) is included in upstream witness root (r{\mathsf{wits}}).
(\mathsf{MethodBind}) binds required verifier set hash and method registry entries relevant to interpreting those witnesses.
M.2.2(b) Use cases.Witness dependency proofs support:
audit that a result was produced under a particular verification regime,
compliance workflows requiring proof that certain detectors or proofs were executed,
linking repair completion evidence to prior defect evidence via witness roots.
M.2.2(c) Verification procedure.Verify membership proofs under witness roots and verify method registry compatibility. If verifier set hashes differ, a policy-bridge receipt is required; otherwise dependency is invalid.
M.2.2(d) Privacy boundary.Witness membership proofs do not reveal payloads; they reveal only witness hashes. Any deeper verification that requires local-check data is handled by disclosure bundles and method disclosure policies.
M.2.3 Multi-receipt dependency composition objects
M.2.3(a) Dependency composition object.To compose many upstream receipts, define:[\mathsf{DepPack}:=(h(\mathsf{DownPlan}),[\ h(\mathsf{UpRcpt}i)\ ]{i=1}^k,h(\mathsf{DepPins})?,h(\mathsf{DepWits})?,h(\mathsf{CompatPack}),h(\mathsf{Summary}),h(\mathsf{Result})).]This object bundles multiple dependency proofs and a single compatibility pack.
M.2.3(b) Compatibility pack.[\mathsf{CompatPack}:=(h(\mathsf{HdrCompat}),h(\mathsf{OmegaCompat}),h(\mathsf{SpecCompat}),h(\mathsf{StrengthCompat}),h(\mathsf{ModeCompat})).]Each component is either:
a deterministic proof object, or
a canonical “checked OK” record derived deterministically from comparing hashes, when proof is not required.
M.2.3(c) Summary object.Summary includes:
list of consumed pin ids,
mapping from consumed pins to upstream receipts,
minimal set of membership proofs included,
any required proof-strength checks performed.Summary is canonical and used for deterministic explainability.
M.2.3(d) Verification.Verify the pack by verifying its component dependency proofs and compatibility checks. Packs reduce redundancy in publication packages and improve auditability.
M.2.4 Policy compatibility proofs and bridge receipts
M.2.4(a) Policy compatibility proof.When policies differ, compatibility requires explicit proofs or bridge receipts:[\mathsf{PolCompat}:=(h(\Omega_{\mathrm{src}}),h(\Omega_{\mathrm{dst}}),h(\mathsf{BridgeRcpt})?,h(\mathsf{Rules}),h(\mathsf{Result})).]If (\Omega) bridging is not supported, policy compatibility is strict equality; in that case, (\mathsf{BridgeRcpt}) must be absent and (\mathsf{Rules}) must specify “equal-by-hash.”
M.2.4(b) Bridge receipts.A policy bridge receipt commits:
source policy hash and destination policy hash,
the re-verification or migration steps executed,
and which artifacts are certified compatible.Bridge receipts are first-class receipts and are consumable as dependencies.
M.2.4(c) Convention compatibility proofs.Similarly, if upstream artifacts have different convention bundles than downstream requires, convention compatibility is established either by:
explicit bridge transforms in the downstream plan, or
an upstream bridge receipt that produced artifacts already aligned to downstream specs.No implicit alignment is permitted.
M.2.4(d) Strength compatibility proofs.If downstream requires stronger claims (higher verification levels, tighter tolerances), compatibility requires refinement proofs or proof regeneration receipts. Otherwise the dependency is inadmissible.
M.3 Publication packaging
Publication packages define the exact artifacts required to make a receipt and its dependencies publicly verifiable, subject to disclosure and redaction constraints.
M.3.1 Public verification bundle schema
M.3.1(a) Public package object.[\mathsf{PubPack}:=(h(\mathsf{PackSpec}),h(\mathsf{RootRcpt}),h(\mathsf{UpRcpts})?,h(\mathsf{DepPack})?,h(\mathsf{DiscBundles})?,h(\mathsf{MerkleProofs})?,h(\mathsf{PolicySet}),h(\mathsf{IndexHints})?,\mathsf{meta}?).]
(\mathsf{PackSpec}) pins what is included (receipt variant, required proofs, required disclosures).
(\mathsf{RootRcpt}) is the primary receipt being published.
