(\mathsf{UpRcpts}) lists referenced upstream receipts (optional; may be replaced by a merged receipt).
(\mathsf{DepPack}) bundles dependency proofs.
(\mathsf{DiscBundles}) contains the disclosure bundles required for public verification.
(\mathsf{MerkleProofs}) contains membership proofs for claimed outputs and witnesses.
(\mathsf{PolicySet}) binds policy hashes required to interpret all artifacts.
(\mathsf{IndexHints}) optionally provides deterministic lookup pointers (e.g., plan hash, output pin ids) without altering verifiability.
M.3.1(b) Package completeness predicate.A public package is complete iff a verifier can:
validate receipt schemas and policy hashes,
validate membership proofs for outputs,
validate dependency proofs for consumed artifacts,
validate all proof objects required by the claim boundary using disclosed LCD and checker identities,
validate any required evidence roots and included evidence proofs if audit class requires them.
Completeness is determined by pinned method disclosure policies and corridor publication requirements.
M.3.1(c) Minimal package option.A package may be minimal if it:
includes only receipts and membership proofs,
excludes full ledgers,
relies on external availability of ledgers under a trust policy or cached verification.Minimal packages must declare their verification assumptions explicitly in (\mathsf{PackSpec}).
M.3.1(d) Deterministic construction.Given a receipt hash and a pinned publication policy, constructing a publication package is deterministic: choose the lexicographically minimal set of required objects and proofs that satisfies completeness. This stabilizes publication artifacts.
M.3.2 Redaction markers and redaction policy objects
M.3.2(a) Redaction policy.[\mathsf{RedactPol}:=(h(\mathsf{AllowedFields}),h(\mathsf{ForbiddenFields}),h(\mathsf{SubstRule}),h(\mathsf{ProofReq}),h(\mathsf{DiscCompat})).]Defines which fields may be removed, how they are substituted (hash-only markers), and what additional proofs are required to preserve verifiability under redaction.
M.3.2(b) Redaction markers.[\mathsf{RedactMarker}:=(h(\mathsf{Obj}),\mathsf{TypeId},h(\mathsf{RedactPol}),h(\mathsf{Bind})?).]Markers commit to hidden object hashes and types. Binding is optional; if present, it binds the marker to a specific publication package or step context.
M.3.2(c) Verifiability under redaction.Redaction is permitted only if the proof methods used support verification without the redacted bytes, as declared by method disclosure policies. If a method requires the redacted bytes, then either:
redaction is forbidden, or
the receipt is not publicly verifiable and must be labeled as such.
M.3.2(d) Redaction determinism.Redaction must be deterministic and policy-pinned. Identical inputs under identical redaction policy produce identical redacted bytes and identical hashes. Discretionary redaction is prohibited.
M.3.3 Challenge artifacts and dispute workflow objects
M.3.3(a) Challenge object.[\mathsf{Challenge}:=(h(\mathsf{TargetRcpt}),h(\mathsf{Claim}),h(\mathsf{VerifierCtx}),h(\mathsf{FailEv}),h(\mathsf{Steps})?,\mathsf{status}).]
(\mathsf{TargetRcpt}) is the receipt hash being challenged.
(\mathsf{Claim}) is the claim boundary being challenged.
(\mathsf{VerifierCtx}) binds the verifier environment (policy hashes, verifier set hash).
(\mathsf{FailEv}) is deterministic failure evidence: the first failing verification stage and implicated hashes.
(\mathsf{Steps}) optionally references a minimal reproducer: subset of ledger steps and required objects, Merkle-committed.
(\mathsf{status}\in{\mathsf{OPEN},\mathsf{RESOLVED},\mathsf{REJECTED}}).
M.3.3(b) Dispute workflow.A dispute workflow is a sequence of plans and receipts:
reproduce verification failure deterministically,
if failure due to missing disclosure, produce an upgrade package,
if failure due to defect, produce certified repair plan and repair receipt,
supersede or deprecate affected receipts via governance steps.
All actions are replay-verifiable and auditable.
M.3.3(c) Challenge verification.A challenge is valid iff:
the failure evidence is reproducible under the pinned verifier context,
the challenged receipt and claim boundary hashes match those in the publication package,
and the failure evidence references verifiable mismatch (hash mismatch, missing object, failed checker, policy mismatch).
M.3.3(d) Resolution artifacts.Resolution produces:
a repair completion evidence object (if repaired),
a supersession record (if refined),
or a deprecation record (if invalidated).Resolution is not narrative; it is a set of linked, hash-addressed artifacts.
M.3.4 Aggregation via merged receipts and union roots
M.3.4(a) Merged receipt object.A merged receipt aggregates multiple receipts:[\mathsf{RcptMerge}:=(h(\mathsf{MergeBase}),[\ h(\mathsf{Rcpt}i)\ ]{i=1}^k,r_{\mathsf{pins}},r_{\mathsf{wits}},r_{\mathsf{ev}}?,h(\mathsf{ContrCheck}),h(\mathsf{PolicySet}),\mathsf{mode},h(\mathsf{DiscBundle})?).]
constituent receipt list is set-like and canonically sorted,
union roots are computed deterministically from constituent roots,
(\mathsf{ContrCheck}) binds non-contradiction evidence under merge namespace policy.
M.3.4(b) Non-contradiction requirement.Merged receipts must satisfy non-contradiction rules for merged pinsets under a pinned merge namespace. If conflicts exist, merge is inadmissible unless a separate contradiction resolution plan produces a new receipt that explicitly resolves conflicts.
M.3.4(c) Membership proofs in merged receipts.Membership proofs can show:
a given receipt hash is included in the merged receipt,
a given output pin record is included in the union pin root.This supports scalable publication and indexing.
M.3.4(d) Strength and supersession.Merge does not imply refinement. A later merged receipt supersedes an earlier one only if it is proven to contain all prior results (or refined replacements) and to satisfy non-weakening policy requirements. Supersession requires explicit refinement proofs.
M.4 Receipt indices and supersession graphs
Indices provide deterministic lookup. Supersession graphs represent evolution of receipts under refinement preorder.
M.4.1 Index by plan hash
M.4.1(a) Plan-index record.[\mathsf{IdxByPlan}:=(h(\mathsf{Plan}),[\ h(\mathsf{Rcpt}_i)\ ]^\ast,h(\mathsf{SelPol}),h(\mathsf{PolicySet})).]
list of receipts for a plan is set-like and sorted,
(\mathsf{SelPol}) defines deterministic selection of preferred receipt (strongest by preorder),
(\mathsf{PolicySet}) binds which policies the index pertains to (indices are policy-scoped).
M.4.1(b) Preferred receipt selection.Preferred receipt for a plan is selected deterministically:
highest verification level,
tightest tolerances,
largest invariant set,
strictest corridor class,
lexicographically smallest receipt hash as tie-break.
Selection policy is pinned; index records must include the selected receipt hash or be derivable deterministically.
M.4.1(c) Update discipline.Updating indices is a commitment write under protected namespaces. Updates require:
authorization witnesses,
consistency witnesses if contradiction-controlled,
and refinement proofs if the update claims supersession.
M.4.1(d) Audit evidence.Index updates must emit evidence binding:
old index state hash,
new index state hash,
receipts added/removed,
refinement proofs used.
M.4.2 Index by final state hash
M.4.2(a) State-index record.[\mathsf{IdxByState}:=(h_{\mathsf{final}},[\ h(\mathsf{Rcpt}_i)\ ]^\ast,h(\mathsf{PolicySet})).]Used to locate receipts that produce a specific final state hash. Useful for caching and reuse.
M.4.2(b) Compatibility constraints.Because state hash includes policy hashes, state-index keys are policy-scoped. Receipts from different policy hashes cannot share the same state hash unless a policy bridge exists that certifies compatibility and produces a new state hash under the bridged policy.
M.4.2(c) Update discipline.Same as plan index: protected updates and contradiction control.
M.4.2(d) Use in replay and repair.State index supports:
locating the last stable checkpoint for repairs,
retrieving cached segments for replay,
detecting repeated failure patterns by state hash.
M.4.3 Index by output pin id
M.4.3(a) Output-index record.[\mathsf{IdxByOutput}:=(\mathsf{pin_id},[\ h(\mathsf{Rcpt}_i)\ ]^\ast,h(\mathsf{PinRecHash})?^\ast,h(\mathsf{PolicySet})).]Optionally includes hashes of pin records corresponding to the pin id to support membership proof caching.
M.4.3(b) Output index semantics.An output index indicates which receipts commit a given pin id (not necessarily the same value). For contradiction-controlled namespaces, the output index may require that only the current canonical value receipt be indexed; this is governed by governance policy.
M.4.3(c) Non-contradiction and disambiguation.If multiple receipts output the same pin id with different values, the index must either:
store all receipts and require disambiguation at query time, or
store only the superseding receipt with refinement proofs, under a pinned index rule.This choice is pinned and auditable.
M.4.3(d) Use in publication.Output indices support discovery and aggregation: build merged receipts for all outputs in a namespace, or publish “latest result” pointers under alias keys with supersession proofs.
M.4.4 Supersession DAG schema and refinement proofs
M.4.4(a) Supersession edge object.[\mathsf{SupEdge}:=(h(\mathsf{RcptOld}),h(\mathsf{RcptNew}),h(\mathsf{RefProof}),\mathsf{reason_code},h(\mathsf{PolicySet}),h(\mathsf{TimeIndex})?).]Time fields are not real-time; (\mathsf{TimeIndex}) is optional and represents ledger position or governance step index, pinned and deterministic.
M.4.4(b) Supersession DAG object.[\mathsf{SupDAG}:=(h(\mathsf{Nodes}),h(\mathsf{Edges}),h(\mathsf{Topo})?,h(\mathsf{PolicySet})).]
nodes are receipt hashes,
edges are supersession edges,
topological ordering is optional but if present must be deterministic and verifiable from edges.
M.4.4(c) Refinement proof requirements.(\mathsf{RefProof}) must certify that (\mathsf{RcptNew}) refines (\mathsf{RcptOld}) under the strength preorder: invariants superset, tolerances tightened, verification level no lower, policies compatible or bridged. Proof verification uses method registry and disclosure policies. Over-claim is rejected.
M.4.4(d) Query semantics.Given a receipt hash, “current” receipt under supersession is determined by:
following supersession edges to maximal elements under the DAG,
choosing the lexicographically minimal maximal receipt hash if multiple maxima exist (unless policy forbids multiple maxima, requiring contradiction resolution).This yields deterministic “latest/strongest” pointers without informal time dependence.
APPENDIX M — RECEIPT FORMATS, DEPENDENCY PROOFS, AND PUBLICATION PACKAGES
APPENDIX N — REFERENCE APIS: MODULE INTERFACES AND ERROR CONTRACTS
This appendix specifies reference APIs for an interoperable implementation. Each API is defined as a deterministic interface over COS objects and hashes. All inputs/outputs are canonically encoded; all errors are typed COS error objects with deterministic codes and payload schemas. No interface may rely on ambient configuration; all policy and registry dependencies are passed explicitly by hash or are committed in runtime headers and ledgers.
N.1 Object layer API
The object layer provides schema registry, canonical codecs, validation, content-addressable storage, and Merkle utilities.
N.1.1 Schema registry endpoints
N.1.1(a) SchemaGet.[\mathsf{SchemaGet}(\mathsf{TypeId})\to (\mathsf{SchemaHash}\ \cup\ \mathsf{Err})]Returns the schema hash for the given type id under the active object standard (h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}}). Errors:
(\mathsf{E_SCHEMA_UNKNOWN}): type id not registered in object standard.
N.1.1(b) SchemaBytes.[\mathsf{SchemaBytes}(\mathsf{SchemaHash})\to (\mathbf{b}\ \cup\ \mathsf{Err})]Returns canonical bytes of the schema object. Errors:
(\mathsf{E_MISSING_OBJ}): schema bytes not present in CAS.
N.1.1(c) SchemaValidate.[\mathsf{SchemaValidate}(\mathbf{b})\to (\mathsf{true/false},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Deterministically validates that (\mathbf{b}) is a well-formed schema under COS. If false, evidence object includes failure code and location.
N.1.1(d) SchemaList (optional).[\mathsf{SchemaList}()\to [(\mathsf{TypeId},\mathsf{SchemaHash})]^\ast]Returns canonical list of registered type ids and schema hashes under (h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}}), sorted lexicographically by type id bytes.
N.1.2 Codec endpoints and validation endpoints
N.1.2(a) Encode.[\mathsf{Encode}(\mathsf{TypeId},x)\to (\mathbf{b}\ \cup\ \mathsf{Err})]Encodes semantic value (x) into canonical bytes (\mathbf{b}) under the schema for (\mathsf{TypeId}). Errors:
(\mathsf{E_SCHEMA_MISMATCH}): (x) violates schema constraints.
(\mathsf{E_NUMPOL}): numeric value cannot be represented under pinned policy (e.g., overflow under (\mathsf{ERROR})).
(\mathsf{E_CANON}): canonicalization failure (non-canonical encoding attempted).
N.1.2(b) Decode.[\mathsf{Decode}(\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b})\to (x\ \cup\ \mathsf{Err})]Decodes canonical bytes to a semantic value. Errors:
(\mathsf{E_SCHEMA_INVALID}): bytes do not validate under schema.
(\mathsf{E_CANON_NONCAN}): bytes decode but are not canonical under pinned canonicalization rules (must be rejected).
N.1.2(c) Validate.[\mathsf{Validate}(\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b})\to (\mathsf{true/false},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Validates schema and canonical form. When false, evidence includes:
error code,
offending field path (structural address),
expected vs observed constraints where applicable.
N.1.2(d) Canonicalize (optional, restricted).[\mathsf{Canonicalize}(\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b})\to (\mathbf{b}^\star\ \cup\ \mathsf{Err})]Produces canonical bytes (\mathbf{b}^\star) if the object admits canonicalization without semantic change per schema policy. Default policy forbids canonicalizing non-canonical bytes unless explicitly permitted; otherwise must return error (\mathsf{E_CANON_FORBID}).
N.1.3 Content-addressable store endpoints
N.1.3(a) CASPut.[\mathsf{CASPut}(\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b})\to (\mathsf{ObjHash}\ \cup\ \mathsf{Err})]Requires (\mathbf{b}) to be canonical and schema-valid. Computes:[h:=H(\mathsf{DS}(\mathsf{TypeId})|\mathbf{b})]and stores ((\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b})) under key (h). Idempotent if already present with identical bytes. Errors:
(\mathsf{E_CANON_NONCAN}): non-canonical bytes.
(\mathsf{E_HASH_MISMATCH}): provided key (if any) disagrees with computed hash.
(\mathsf{E_STORE}): storage failure (must be deterministic category; details may be opaque but error code fixed).
N.1.3(b) CASGet.[\mathsf{CASGet}(\mathsf{ObjHash})\to ((\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b})\ \cup\ \mathsf{Err})]Returns stored bytes and type id. Errors:
(\mathsf{E_MISSING_OBJ}): object not found.
N.1.3(c) CASHas.[\mathsf{CASHas}(\mathsf{ObjHash})\to {\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}}]
N.1.3(d) CASBatchGet (optional).[\mathsf{CASBatchGet}([\mathsf{ObjHash}]^\ast)\to [(\mathsf{ObjHash},\mathsf{TypeId},\mathbf{b})\cup(\mathsf{ObjHash},\mathsf{Err})]^\ast]Returns results in the same order as requested list; deterministically signals missing objects per hash.
N.1.4 Merkle endpoints and membership proof endpoints
N.1.4(a) MerkleRoot.[\mathsf{MerkleRoot}(h(\mathsf{MerklePol}),[\mathbf{leaf}_1,\ldots,\mathbf{leaf}_n])\to (\mathsf{ObjHash}\ \cup\ \mathsf{Err})]Computes root under pinned Merkle policy. Leaves must be canonical according to policy leaf rules (e.g., fixed-length hash encodings). Errors:
(\mathsf{E_MERKLE_POL}): policy mismatch/unknown.
(\mathsf{E_MERKLE_LEAF}): leaf invalid under policy.
N.1.4(b) MerkleProof.[\mathsf{MerkleProof}(h(\mathsf{MerklePol}),[\mathbf{leaf}_i],i)\to (\mathsf{MP}\ \cup\ \mathsf{Err})]Produces membership proof object (\mathsf{MP}) (Appendix A). Requires deterministic leaf ordering (sorted or sequence-like) per policy.
N.1.4(c) VerifyMerkle.[\mathsf{VerifyMerkle}(\mathsf{MP})\to (\mathsf{true/false},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Returns true iff proof reconstructs committed root under policy. On false, returns deterministic evidence code (\mathsf{E_MERKLE_FAIL}).
N.1.4(d) MerkleLeafSort (utility).[\mathsf{MerkleLeafSort}(h(\mathsf{MerklePol}),[\mathbf{leaf}]^\ast)\to [\mathbf{leaf}]^\ast]Sorts leaves deterministically when policy requires sorted roots.
N.2 Verifier layer API
The verifier layer validates witnesses and proof objects under a pinned verifier set hash.
N.2.1 Witness verification endpoints
N.2.1(a) VerifyWitness.[\mathsf{VerifyWitness}(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{wtype},h(W),h(\mathsf{Ctx}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\mathsf{true/false},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Steps:
resolve witness bytes by (h(W)),
validate schema and canonical encoding for (\mathsf{wtype}),
validate binding to (\mathsf{Ctx}) (Appendix B),
validate payload-level rules for the witness type.On failure, returns evidence hash with deterministic code.
N.2.1(b) VerifyWitnessSet.[\mathsf{VerifyWitnessSet}(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},[\ h(W_i)\ ]^\ast,h(\mathsf{Ctx}),\mathsf{WReq}?,\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\mathsf{true/false},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Verifies presence, canonical ordering, and all required witness types/method ids per (\mathsf{WReq}) when provided.
N.2.1(c) BindContext (utility).[\mathsf{BindContext}(\mathsf{StepRec},\mathsf{LedHdr},h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{hdr}})\to h(\mathsf{Ctx})]Deterministically reconstructs step context binding record used for witness verification.
N.2.1(d) WitnessSchemaGet.[\mathsf{WitnessSchemaGet}(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{wtype})\to (h(\mathsf{Schema})\cup \mathsf{Err})]
N.2.2 Method verification endpoints
N.2.2(a) VerifyProofMethod.[\mathsf{VerifyProofMethod}(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{method_id},h(\mathsf{Proof}),h(\mathsf{Ctx}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\mathsf{true/false},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Validates:
method registry entry exists,
proof object schema matches method kind,
LCD exists and is valid,
checker program id/digest matches,
claim admissibility holds at declared verification level,
checker result passes.
N.2.2(b) VerifyChecker.[\mathsf{VerifyChecker}(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h(\mathsf{ChkProg}),h(\mathsf{LCD}),h(\mathsf{Inputs}),h(\mathsf{Outputs})?,\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\mathsf{ChkRes}\ \cup\ \mathsf{Err})]Returns canonical checker result object; errors are deterministic.
N.2.2(c) MethodGet.[\mathsf{MethodGet}(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{method_id})\to (\mathsf{Method}\ \cup\ \mathsf{Err})]
N.2.2(d) ProofSchemaGet.[\mathsf{ProofSchemaGet}(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{method_id})\to (h(\mathsf{Schema})\cup\mathsf{Err})]
N.2.3 Claim admissibility endpoints and level checks
N.2.3(a) CheckClaimAdmissible.[\mathsf{CheckClaimAdmissible}(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{method_id},h(\mathsf{Claim}),\mathsf{VL})\to (\mathsf{true/false},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Rejects over-claims deterministically.
N.2.3(b) CheckVLSupported.[\mathsf{CheckVLSupported}(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{method_id},\mathsf{VL})\to {\mathsf{true},\mathsf{false}}]
N.2.3(c) CompareStrength (utility).[\mathsf{CompareStrength}(h(\mathsf{StrengthReq}),h(\mathsf{StrengthHave}),h(\mathsf{PreorderPol}))\to (\mathsf{ok/fail},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Determines whether an upstream claim boundary suffices for a downstream requirement.
N.2.3(d) ClaimNormalize.[\mathsf{ClaimNormalize}(h(\mathsf{Claim}))\to (h(\mathsf{Claim}^\star)\cup\mathsf{Err})]Canonicalizes claim representations (sort invariant ids, quantize tolerances, normalize spec bindings). Used to ensure claim identity stability.
N.2.4 Disclosure requirement endpoints and proof dependencies
N.2.4(a) DisclosureRequirements.[\mathsf{DisclosureRequirements}(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{method_id},\mathsf{mode})\to (h(\mathsf{ReqReveal})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Returns required reveal classes for public verification under the selected mode.
N.2.4(b) ProofDependencies.[\mathsf{ProofDependencies}(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h(\mathsf{Proof}))\to (h(\mathsf{DepList})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Returns canonical dependency list object containing:
required LCD hashes,
required TB hashes,
required checker program hashes,
required policy hashes,
required referenced input/output object hashes,
required membership proofs if proof is intended to be verified via Merkle commitments.
N.2.4(c) VerifyDisclosureBundle.[\mathsf{VerifyDisclosureBundle}(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h(\mathsf{DiscBundle}),h(\mathsf{Rcpt}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\mathsf{true/false},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Checks that the bundle includes all required revealed objects for all proofs implicated by the receipt’s claim boundary.
N.2.4(d) RedactionCheck.[\mathsf{RedactionCheck}(h(\mathsf{RedactPol}),h(\mathsf{DiscBundle}))\to (\mathsf{true/false},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Ensures deterministic redaction markers follow policy and do not omit required verification material.
N.3 Execution layer API
The execution layer runs programs under the kernel, produces ledgers and checkpoints, and supports replay verification and plan-gated repairs.
N.3.1 Step execution endpoint (expand/apply/project)
N.3.1(a) Step.[\mathsf{Step}(h(\mathsf{Hdr}),\Psi,\mathsf{StepDesc},h(\mathsf{WReq})?,h(\mathsf{LStep})?,\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\Psi',h(\mathsf{StepRec}),h(\mathsf{Chk}),h(\mathsf{EvidenceList}))]Performs expand → apply → project under (\Omega) from runtime header. Must be deterministic. Output includes:
updated state (\Psi'),
step record hash,
post-step checkpoint hash,
evidence list hash.
N.3.1(b) ApplyOnly (optional, non-committing).[\mathsf{ApplyOnly}(\Psi,\mathsf{StepDesc},\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\Delta,W,\mathbf{c},h(\mathsf{EvidenceList}))]Produces deltas and witnesses without committing, for compilation-time simulation or planning. Use is policy-gated; outputs cannot be committed unless passed through (\mathsf{Project}) and logged.
N.3.1(c) ProjectOnly (optional).[\mathsf{ProjectOnly}(\Psi,\Delta,W,\mathbf{c},\mathsf{StepDesc},h(\Omega))\to (\mathsf{verdict},m,h(\mathsf{EvidenceList}))]Computes verdict and mask deterministically; used in replay and auditing.
N.3.1(d) Error contract.Step errors are not exceptions; they are verdicts and evidence. The endpoint must not crash; it must return reject+revert with deterministic failure evidence unless the failure is unrecoverable (e.g., missing runtime header policy object), in which case it must return a typed execution error object (\mathsf{E_RUNTIME}) with deterministic payload.
N.3.2 Ledger emission endpoint and step record construction
N.3.2(a) LedgerInit.[\mathsf{LedgerInit}(h(\mathsf{Hdr}),h(\mathsf{Plan})?,h(\mathsf{Chk}_0),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (h(\mathsf{LedHdr})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Creates ledger header and genesis commitments deterministically.
N.3.2(b) LedgerAppend.[\mathsf{LedgerAppend}(h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),h(\mathsf{StepRec}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (h(\mathsf{LedgerRef})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Appends step record hash to ledger chain under pinned ledger format. Must enforce chain linkage and previous-hash correctness.
N.3.2(c) StepRecBuild.[\mathsf{StepRecBuild}(\Psi,\Psi',\mathsf{StepDesc},\Delta,W,\mathbf{c},m,\mathsf{verdict},h(\mathsf{EvidenceList}),h(\mathsf{LedHdr}))\to (h(\mathsf{StepRec})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Builds canonical step record bytes and returns its hash.
N.3.2(d) ReceiptExtract.[\mathsf{ReceiptExtract}(h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),h(\mathsf{Chk}_n),h(\mathsf{Targets})?,\mathsf{mode},h(\mathsf{DiscBundle})?)\to (h(\mathsf{Rcpt})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Extracts receipt roots deterministically and produces receipt object.
N.3.3 Checkpoint management endpoints (create/load/validate)
N.3.3(a) CheckpointCreate.[\mathsf{CheckpointCreate}(h(\mathsf{Hdr}),h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),\Psi)\to (h(\mathsf{Chk})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Creates canonical checkpoint object binding store hashes and state hash.
N.3.3(b) CheckpointLoad.[\mathsf{CheckpointLoad}(h(\mathsf{Chk}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\Psi\cup\mathsf{Err})]Loads state snapshot or reconstructs it from committed stores; reconstruction policy must be pinned. Errors:
missing required store snapshot objects,
policy mismatch with current runtime header.
N.3.3(c) CheckpointValidate.[\mathsf{CheckpointValidate}(h(\mathsf{Chk}),h(\mathsf{Hdr}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\mathsf{true/false},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Validates that checkpoint hashes recompute correctly, policies match, and store hashes are coherent.
N.3.3(d) CheckpointStabilityCheck (optional).[\mathsf{CheckpointStabilityCheck}(h(\mathsf{Chk}),h(\Omega),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\mathsf{OK/FAIL},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Runs stability anchor checks (canonical normal forms, contradiction scan, tolerance ledger within budget) when required.
N.3.4 Repair invocation endpoints (plan-gated)
N.3.4(a) RepairCompile.[\mathsf{RepairCompile}(h(\mathsf{DefEvidence}),h(\Omega),h(\mathsf{LibRoot}),h(\mathsf{Hdr}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (h(\mathsf{RepPlan})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Compiles a repair plan deterministically from defect evidence and policy.
N.3.4(b) RepairRun.[\mathsf{RepairRun}(h(\mathsf{RepPlan}),h(\mathsf{Chk}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (h(\mathsf{RepRcpt}),h(\mathsf{Chk}')\cup\mathsf{Err})]Executes repair plan from checkpoint, yields repair receipt and post-repair checkpoint.
N.3.4(c) RepairValidate.[\mathsf{RepairValidate}(h(\mathsf{DefEvidence}),h(\mathsf{RepRcpt}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\mathsf{OK/FAIL},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Validates that repair completion predicate holds and mandatory detectors now pass.
N.3.4(d) Policy constraints.Repair endpoints must enforce governance permissions and isolation sets. Repair cannot write outside authorized namespaces even if it would “fix” defects; such writes violate scope policy.
N.4 Compilation layer API
The compilation layer parses intent, constructs metro maps, selects routes, assembles plans, and optionally optimizes them.
N.4.1 Intent parse and normalization endpoints
N.4.1(a) ParseIntent.[\mathsf{ParseIntent}(\text{Text},h(\mathsf{ParsePol}))\to (h(\mathsf{Goal})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Produces canonical goal object hash. Parse policy pins normalization dictionaries and tokenization rules.
N.4.1(b) ExtractContext.[\mathsf{ExtractContext}(\text{Text},h(\mathsf{ParsePol}),h(\mathsf{Chk})?)\to (h(\mathsf{Context})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Optionally binds a checkpoint hash for state-dependent compilation.
N.4.1(c) NormalizeConstraints.[\mathsf{NormalizeConstraints}(h(\mathsf{Goal}),h(\mathsf{Context}),h(\mathsf{DefReg}),h(\Omega))\to (h(\mathsf{Constraints})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Applies explicit defaults deterministically and produces constraints hash.
N.4.1(d) CompileRequest.[\mathsf{CompileRequest}(h(\mathsf{Goal}),h(\mathsf{Context}),h(\mathsf{Constraints}),h(\mathsf{Hdr}),h(\mathsf{LibRoot}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (h(\mathsf{Plan})\cup\mathsf{Err})]One-shot compile pipeline.
N.4.2 Metro map build/load endpoints
N.4.2(a) BuildMetro.[\mathsf{BuildMetro}(h(\mathsf{LibRoot}),h(\Omega),h(\mathsf{Hdr}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (h(\mathsf{Metro})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Builds deterministic navigation graph object with node/edge sets and policy bindings.
N.4.2(b) LoadMetro.[\mathsf{LoadMetro}(h(\mathsf{Metro}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\mathsf{Metro}\cup\mathsf{Err})]
N.4.2(c) MetroValidate.[\mathsf{MetroValidate}(h(\mathsf{Metro}),h(\mathsf{LibRoot}),h(\Omega),h(\mathsf{Hdr}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\mathsf{true/false},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Ensures edge annotations are coherent, entry admission packets verify, and policy hashes match.
N.4.2(d) MetroEdgeMembershipProof (optional).[\mathsf{MetroEdgeMP}(h(\mathsf{Metro}),\mathsf{edge_id})\to (\mathsf{MP}\cup\mathsf{Err})]Produces membership proof for route publication.
N.4.3 Route selection and plan assembly endpoints
N.4.3(a) RouteSelect.[\mathsf{RouteSelect}(h(\mathsf{Metro}),h(\mathsf{Goal}),h(\mathsf{Context}),h(\mathsf{Constraints}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (h(\mathsf{RoutePlan})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Selects deterministic shortest-legal route under feasibility filters and cost functional.
N.4.3(b) AssemblePlan.[\mathsf{AssemblePlan}(h(\mathsf{RoutePlan}),h(\mathsf{Hdr}),h(\Omega),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (h(\mathsf{Plan})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Emits program, witness plan, ledger plan, and plan bindings.
N.4.3(c) ExplainRoute (optional).[\mathsf{ExplainRoute}(h(\mathsf{RoutePlan}),h(\mathsf{ExplainPol}))\to (h(\mathsf{Explain})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Produces deterministic explainability artifact.
N.4.3(d) FeasibilityCertificate.[\mathsf{FeasCert}(h(\mathsf{RoutePlan}))\to h(\mathsf{FeasCert})]Returns route feasibility certificate hash; certificate must be verifiable from metro map annotations and constraints.
N.4.4 Optimization endpoints (rewrite policy gated)
N.4.4(a) OptimizePlan.[\mathsf{OptimizePlan}(h(\mathsf{Plan}),h(\mathsf{OptPol}),h(\mathsf{Hdr}),h(\Omega),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (h(\mathsf{Plan}')\times h(\mathsf{OptRcpt})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Applies proof-carrying rewrites deterministically, emitting rewrite proofs and an optimization receipt or evidence root per policy.
N.4.4(b) NormalizeProgram.[\mathsf{NormalizeProgram}(h(\Pi),h(\mathsf{PNF}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (h(\Pi^\star)\cup\mathsf{Err})]Applies canonical program normal form deterministically.
N.4.4(c) ApplyRewriteOnce.[\mathsf{ApplyRewriteOnce}(h(\Pi),h(\mathsf{Rule}),h(\mathsf{OptPol}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (h(\Pi')\times h(\mathsf{ProofRw})\cup\mathsf{Err})]Applies a single admissible rewrite at the earliest admissible match (or at a specified match interval if explicitly provided and policy permits), emitting rewrite proof.
N.4.4(d) VerifyOptimizationReceipt.[\mathsf{VerifyOptRcpt}(h(\mathsf{OptRcpt}),\mathsf{Resolve})\to (\mathsf{OK/FAIL},h(\mathsf{Evidence})?)]Verifies that the optimized program refines or equals the original under the required claim boundary and non-weakening constraints.
Error contracts (shared across APIs)
All endpoints return either a valid output or a typed error object hash with deterministic schema:[\mathsf{Err}:=(\mathsf{err_code},\mathsf{phase},h(\mathsf{Bind})?,h(\mathsf{Refs}),h(\mathsf{Details}),h(\mathsf{PolicySet})).]
(\mathsf{err_code}) is a pinned enum (schema/type/missing/policy/budget/scope/verify/determinism).
(\mathsf{phase}) identifies where failure occurred (compile, execute, replay, publish).
(\mathsf{Bind}) binds to step context when applicable.
(\mathsf{Refs}) lists implicated hashes.
(\mathsf{Details}) is structured, canonical payload.
(\mathsf{PolicySet}) binds policy hashes for interpretation.
No API returns informal text; any human-readable diagnostics must be derived from these structured error objects by separate presentation layers and must not affect hashes.
APPENDIX N — REFERENCE APIS: MODULE INTERFACES AND ERROR CONTRACTS
APPENDIX O — CONFORMANCE SUITE AND INTEROPERABILITY TESTS
This appendix specifies the conformance suite: the required test plans and interoperability checks that establish cross-implementation determinism and correctness. Conformance is not informal testing; it is a collection of executable plans that produce receipts. Passing conformance means that independent implementations produce identical canonical bytes, identical hashes, identical ledgers, identical receipts, and identical failure evidence under identical policy hashes and inputs. Any divergence is localized by deterministic diagnostics and classified by failure codes.
O.1 Canonical encoding conformance
O.1.1 Type encoder/decoder test plans
O.1.1(a) Encoder/decoder round-trip plan template.For each registered (\mathsf{TypeId}) under (h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}}), define a test plan:[\mathsf{PlanEncDec}(\mathsf{TypeId}):=\text{generate canonical semantic instances }x_1,\ldots,x_n \to \mathbf{b}_i=\mathsf{Encode}(x_i)\to x'_i=\mathsf{Decode}(\mathbf{b}_i).]The plan must commit:
(\mathbf{b}_i) hashes,
decoded values (x'_i) re-encoded bytes hashes,
and a proof object that (\mathbf{b}_i) are canonical (i.e., (\mathsf{Canonicalize}(\mathbf{b}_i)=\mathbf{b}_i)).
O.1.1(b) Required properties.For each instance:
(\mathsf{Decode}(\mathsf{Encode}(x_i))=x_i),
(\mathsf{Encode}(\mathsf{Decode}(\mathbf{b}_i))=\mathbf{b}_i),
(\mathsf{Validate}(\mathbf{b}_i)=\mathsf{true}),
(h(\mathbf{b}_i)) matches the committed object hash.
Any failure yields deterministic evidence referencing the field path and schema hash.
O.1.1(c) Negative tests.For each type, include malformed byte cases:
non-canonical encodings (same semantic but non-minimal varints, unsorted sets),
schema-violating encodings (wrong field types, missing required fields),
invalid hashes (bytes that do not match committed hash).Decoder must reject deterministically with correct error codes.
O.1.1(d) Conformance receipt.Each plan produces a receipt committing:
object standard hash,
schema hashes involved,
list of tested instance hashes,
pass/fail evidence roots.Passing requires byte-identical outputs and receipts across implementations.
O.1.2 Map/set ordering and canonical ordering tests
O.1.2(a) Map ordering test.Construct maps with keys inserted in different orders but semantically identical. Encode each map; assert encoded bytes identical. Verify:
keys are sorted lexicographically by canonical key bytes,
duplicates are rejected deterministically,
decoding preserves map semantics.
O.1.2(b) Set ordering test.Construct sets with identical elements in different insertion orders. Encode each set; assert identical bytes. Verify:
element bytes are sorted lexicographically,
duplicates are rejected deterministically (unless schema explicitly allows multisets, in which case encoding must reflect multiset semantics explicitly),
membership proofs for Merkle roots built from sets are stable.
O.1.2(c) Mixed nested collections.Test nested structures: maps whose values are sets/lists and sets whose elements are maps. Ensure canonical ordering applies recursively and deterministically.
O.1.2(d) Ordering policy hash binding.Tests must bind ordering policy hashes (h(\mathsf{OrderPol})). If ordering policy changes, tests must be rerun; receipts are policy-scoped.
O.1.3 Numeric policy conformance tests (Q/F)
O.1.3(a) Quantization conformance suite.For each admitted quantization policy (Q):
test representable rationals and their exact quantizations,
test rounding tie cases for each rounding mode,
test overflow cases for each overflow mode,
test vector/tensor layout-coupled quantization,
test deterministic reduction under norms.
Outputs must be byte-identical across implementations; evidence must localize mismatch (expected vs observed (q), rounding mode, scale, offset).
O.1.3(b) Float policy conformance suite (if allowed).For each admitted float policy (F):
test endianness and serialization,
test canonical NaN behavior when allowed,
test forbidden value rejection when configured,
test signed-zero and subnormal handling.
Floating tests are admissible only when corridor and object standard permit float commitments.
O.1.3(c) Numeric drift measurement tests.For each metric/norm id:
compute drift on constructed pairs with known expected results,
test near-threshold boundary behavior,
test squared-norm comparisons for (\ell_2) to avoid sqrt ambiguity.
O.1.3(d) Numeric conformance receipts.Receipts commit:
numeric policy hashes,
metric catalog hashes,
test basis hashes,
measured outputs and evidence roots.Passing requires identical receipts across implementations.
O.1.4 Cross-platform byte stability test batteries
O.1.4(a) Stability battery definition.A stability battery is a set of conformance plans run across:
different OS/hardware architectures,
different language implementations,
different compiler/toolchain builds,all under identical policy hashes.
O.1.4(b) Deterministic serialization invariants.Battery asserts:
byte-identical encoded objects for all test instances,
byte-identical Merkle roots for identical leaf sets,
byte-identical hashes for all stored objects,
byte-identical failure evidence for all negative tests.
O.1.4(c) Handling of platform pitfalls.Battery includes targeted cases:
integer boundary values (varint minimality),
Unicode normalization and forbidden characters (key normalization),
floating canonicalization (if permitted),
large nested structures (ordering recursion),
endianness-sensitive structures.
O.1.4(d) Battery pass artifact.Battery pass produces a merged receipt aggregating all conformance receipts. Membership proofs allow auditors to verify inclusion of each component test result.
O.2 Verifier conformance
O.2.1 Provenance binding tests and anti-laundering tests
O.2.1(a) Binding correctness tests.Construct steps with:
correct (\mathsf{Ctx}) binding,
mismatched pre hash,
mismatched delta root,
mismatched post hash,
mismatched policy hashes.Ensure provenance verifier:
accepts only correct bindings,
rejects others deterministically with correct evidence codes.
O.2.1(b) Transplantability negative tests.Attempt to reuse a witness from step (t) in step (t') with a different context. Verifier must reject due to binding mismatch. Evidence must reference both contexts and the mismatched field.
O.2.1(c) Authorization laundering tests.Create a commitment delta that differs from what a commitment witness authorizes. Verifier must reject (operation-set mismatch), even if binding matches. This tests the “authorize accepted delta subset” rule.
O.2.1(d) Provenance primacy tests.Ensure witness verification ordering matters:
missing provenance witness should invalidate all other witnesses,
multiple provenance witnesses must be identical or step fails.Test deterministic ordering and error precedence.
O.2.2 Proof method checker tests and regressions
O.2.2(a) Method correctness tests.For each method id:
provide known passing proofs and known failing proofs,
ensure checker results are deterministic,
ensure claim admissibility is enforced.
O.2.2(b) Regression suite.Maintain a pinned corpus of proof objects (hashes) and expected verifier outcomes. Re-running the suite under the same verifier set hash must yield identical outcomes. Any change requires a new verifier set hash and explicit migration.
O.2.2(c) LCD completeness tests.Remove required LCD components (TB, checker id, numeric policies, spec bindings) and ensure verifier rejects with (\mathsf{E_MISSING}) or (\mathsf{E_DISCLOSE}) depending on mode.
O.2.2(d) Complexity cap tests.Construct proofs that exceed cost caps for methods. Verifier must fail deterministically with cost evidence. This ensures methods remain within corridor constraints.
O.2.3 Verification level boundary tests (over-claim rejection)
O.2.3(a) Over-claim cases.For each method, attempt to assert claim classes not permitted at a given verification level (e.g., universal claim at VL0). Verifier must reject deterministically with (\mathsf{E_CLAIM}).
O.2.3(b) Level mismatch cases.Provide LCD declaring VL2 but supplying TB that violates VL2 generation rules; reject deterministically. Provide proof object declaring VL3 without required coverage evidence; reject.
O.2.3(c) Strength preorder checks.Test that downstream requirements for stronger claims reject weaker proofs:
higher verification level required,
tighter tolerances required,
larger invariant set required.Verifier must enforce strength compatibility or require refinement proofs.
O.2.3(d) Claim canonicalization tests.Ensure that semantically identical claim objects encode identically. Any non-canonical claim encoding must be rejected or canonicalized per policy. Claim identity must be stable.
O.2.4 Disclosure policy conformance tests
O.2.4(a) Selective disclosure completeness.For selective mode receipts, verify that disclosure bundles include all required objects per method disclosure policies. Missing required reveals must fail deterministically.
O.2.4(b) Redaction boundary tests.Attempt to redact required fields for public verification. Verifier must reject. Attempt to redact optional fields; verifier must accept and preserve verifiability. Redaction must be deterministic.
O.2.4(c) Mode mismatch tests.Attempt to publicly verify a hash-only receipt without required ledger/proof access; verifier must produce a structured “not publicly verifiable under mode” outcome consistent with policy.
O.2.4(d) Disclosure equivalence tests.If multiple disclosure bundles satisfy requirements, policy may require selecting the lexicographically minimal bundle. Test deterministic selection to stabilize publication artifacts.
O.3 Kernel and projector conformance
O.3.1 Delta application and store-hash tests
O.3.1(a) Delta ordering tests.Construct deltas with operations inserted in non-canonical order; ensure canonicalization produces identical delta hashes. Ensure replay recomputation matches.
O.3.1(b) Per-store application tests.For each store:
apply a known delta sequence,
verify post-store hash equals expected,
verify full state hash composition equals expected,
verify idempotence on replay.
O.3.1(c) Cross-store ordering tests.Ensure the kernel applies store deltas in fixed order (commit → rel → cand → proof). Construct cases where order would matter; ensure the mandated order is used.
O.3.1(d) Link index tests.Verify that ObjAdd/ObjLink results in deterministic link index commitments and that link retrieval yields canonical order.
O.3.2 Mask determinism and truncation tests
O.3.2(a) Partial acceptance mask tests.Construct steps that trigger partial acceptance (e.g., candidate cap exceeded). Verify that:
truncation mask is deterministic,
accepted delta subset is deterministic,
step record commits correct mask,
replay reproduces identical mask and store hashes.
O.3.2(b) Scope-based masking tests.When (\Omega) permits discarding unauthorized secondary outputs, ensure masking discards exactly those outputs and records evidence; otherwise ensure step rejects.
O.3.2(c) Tie-break tests.Construct candidate sets with equal scores. Ensure comparator tie-break uses hash ordering deterministically and truncation retains lexicographically smallest candidates.
O.3.2(d) Mask schema conformance.Ensure mask encoding is canonical and that any non-canonical mask bytes are rejected.
O.3.3 Reject+revert semantics and evidence emission tests
O.3.3(a) Hard failure tests.Trigger each hard failure class:
schema invalid,
witness binding invalid,
missing required witness,
forbidden scope write,
contradiction without resolution,
budget infeasible without escalation.Ensure kernel:
produces (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}),
keeps post-state equal to pre-state,
emits deterministic evidence with correct failure code precedence.
O.3.3(b) Evidence retention tests.When retention is mandatory, ensure evidence objects are emitted and referenced in step record. When optional, ensure omission follows deterministic rule and does not break replay.
O.3.3(c) Failure precedence tests.When multiple failures are present, ensure the system reports the deterministic first failure under the pinned precedence order.
O.3.3(d) Repair-trigger evidence tests.Ensure failure evidence includes sufficient structured fields for repair planner (boundary markers, missing bridge classes, failing invariant ids).
O.3.4 Detector schedule conformance and mandatory evidence tests
O.3.4(a) Detector ordering tests.Verify detector schedule runs in pinned order and yields identical evidence ordering and evidence roots. Reordering must change hashes and is therefore detectable.
O.3.4(b) Mandatory detector enforcement tests.Disable mandatory detector in a plan; compilation must fail or execution must reject depending on policy. Ensure mandatory detectors cannot be skipped silently.
O.3.4(c) Near-threshold mitigation tests.Trigger (\mathsf{NEAR}) conditions for tolerance ledger and drift. Ensure required mitigation action ids are emitted as evidence and required in subsequent plans; ensure ignoring mitigation causes rejection.
O.3.4(d) Detector conformance receipts.Detector tests must produce receipts committing evidence roots and detector ids used, enabling independent verification of detector behavior.
O.4 End-to-end interoperability
O.4.1 Plan hashing stability across implementations
O.4.1(a) Deterministic compilation tests.Given identical:
goal text (or canonical goal object),
context and constraints hashes,
metro map hash,
corridor policy hash,all implementations must produce identical plan hash (h(\mathsf{Plan})). This validates:
parsing/normalization determinism (if text-based),
routing determinism,
parameter hashing determinism,
witness/ledger plan derivation determinism.
O.4.1(b) Tie-break stability tests.Construct cases with multiple equal-cost routes. Ensure all implementations select the same route by lexicographic tie-break. Emit explainability evidence and ensure its hash matches.
O.4.1(c) Bridge insertion stability tests.Construct cases with required bridge chains. Ensure identical bridge chain selection, post-bridge normalizer insertion, and thus identical plan hash.
O.4.1(d) Optimization stability tests (policy-gated).If optimization is enabled, ensure proof-carrying rewrites yield identical optimized plan hash given identical optimizer policy hash.
O.4.2 Ledger replay equivalence across implementations
O.4.2(a) Execution trace equivalence.Execute the same plan from the same checkpoint under the same runtime header hashes. All implementations must produce:
identical step records (hashes and bytes),
identical ledger header hash,
identical final state hash,
identical receipts (hashes and bytes).Any divergence indicates non-conformance.
O.4.2(b) Replay verifier equivalence.Replay verify the produced ledger on each implementation. All must return (\mathsf{OK}). For negative cases (tampered record, missing object), all must fail at the same earliest step with the same divergence class and deterministically structured evidence.
O.4.2(c) Partial acceptance equivalence.Run plans that cause partial acceptance. Ensure identical masks, truncation outcomes, and store hashes across implementations.
O.4.2(d) Long-chain segmentation equivalence.Segmented execution with checkpoints must be stable: segment receipts compose and replay independently in all implementations with identical results.
O.4.3 Receipt validity and membership-proof verification tests
O.4.3(a) Receipt extraction equivalence.From identical terminal state and targets, all implementations must compute identical:
output pin root,
witness root,
evidence root (if present),
receipt bytes and hash.
O.4.3(b) Membership proof equivalence.Membership proofs for outputs and witnesses must verify identically across implementations under the pinned Merkle policy. Proof bytes must be canonical and stable.
O.4.3(c) Dependency proof equivalence.Construct dependency proofs consuming upstream outputs. Verify:
membership proofs validate,
downstream provenance binding validates,
compatibility proofs validate.All implementations must accept/reject identically.
O.4.3(d) Publication package equivalence.For selective disclosure receipts, verify public packages:
include all required disclosed objects,
satisfy method disclosure requirements,
verify proofs deterministically.Any missing disclosure must produce deterministic error evidence.
O.4.4 Cross-implementation matching checklist and failure diagnostics
O.4.4(a) Matching checklist.An implementation matches the reference if it matches, by hash:
(h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}}) and all schema hashes,
(h_{\mathsf{Canon}}) and canonical ordering rules,
(h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}) and all method digests,
(h_\Omega) and detector schedules,
(h(\mathsf{LedFmt})), Merkle policy hash, and hash policy hash,
plan hashes for conformance plans,
ledger header and step record hashes,
receipt hashes and membership proof verification outputs.
O.4.4(b) Failure diagnostic object.On mismatch, produce a diagnostic artifact:[\mathsf{Diag}:=(\mathsf{diag_kind},h(\mathsf{Expected}),h(\mathsf{Observed}),h(\mathsf{Loc}),h(\mathsf{PolicySet}),h(\mathsf{Evidence})?),]where (\mathsf{Loc}) indicates which component diverged (schema, encoding, ordering, verifier, projector, detector, Merkle, routing). Diagnostics must be deterministic and sufficient for remediation.
O.4.4(c) Deterministic remediation steps.Remediation is not narrative. Given (\mathsf{Diag}), the suite defines deterministic next actions:
schema mismatch → update object standard or fix codec,
ordering mismatch → fix ordering policy implementation,
verifier digest mismatch → align verifier implementations or update registry and policy hashes,
projector mismatch → fix kernel semantics or detector schedule,
Merkle mismatch → fix Merkle policy,
routing mismatch → fix tie-break or feasibility filters.
O.4.4(d) Conformance closure artifact.The conformance suite produces a single merged receipt committing to the set of all conformance receipts. Auditors verify membership proofs for required tests to confirm a particular implementation’s conformance coverage.
APPENDIX O — CONFORMANCE SUITE AND INTEROPERABILITY TESTS
APPENDIX P — WORKED EXAMPLES AND CANONICAL WORKFLOWS
This appendix provides canonical, executable workflows expressed entirely in the framework’s object vocabulary: typed objects, legal transform contracts, plans, ledgers, witnesses, evidence, receipts, dependency proofs, supersession records, and stability checkpoints. Each workflow is specified as a deterministic procedure with explicit inputs, outputs, artifacts, and correctness predicates. No step relies on informal interpretation; every transition is mediated by explicit contracts and verifiable witnesses.
P.1 Minimal end-to-end workflow
P.1.1 Define typed objects and pin commitments
P.1.1(a) Inputs.
Runtime header hash (h(\mathsf{Hdr})) binding (h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h_{\mathsf{CostModel}},h(\mathsf{LedFmt})).
Corridor policy hash (h_\Omega).
Library root hash (h(\mathsf{LibRoot})) (may be empty for local-only workflows).
Canonical object standard (h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}}) with schemas for: (\mathsf{Lens},\mathsf{Spec},\mathsf{LT},\mathsf{Plan},\mathsf{PinRecord},\mathsf{Receipt}).
P.1.1(b) Construct canonical payload object.Define a payload (X) as a COS object of some admitted type (e.g., a discrete grid sample object):[X := (\text{type tag},\ \text{canonical bytes } \mathbf{b}_X),\qquad h(X)=H(\mathsf{DS}(\text{type})|\mathbf{b}X).]The payload must validate under its schema and be canonical under (h{\mathsf{Canon}}).
P.1.1(c) Define lens and convention bundle.Define a lens object (L) and convention bundle (\mathsf{Spec}):[L=(D,\mathcal{A},\mathcal{I},\mathsf{Adm},\llbracket\cdot\rrbracket,\mathsf{ObsAlg},\mathsf{NF},\mathsf{Spec}),\quad\mathsf{Spec}=(h(\mathsf{SignSpec}),h(\mathsf{ScaleSpec}),h(\mathsf{IndexSpec}),h(\mathsf{UnitSpec}),h(\mathsf{DomainSpec})).]Commit:
(h(L)) into the proof store,
(h(\mathsf{Spec})) into the proof store,
any dependent policy/spec objects referenced by (L) into the proof store.
P.1.1(d) Pin commitments.Choose canonical pin keys:
(\mathsf{PIN}/\mathsf{INPUT}/X),
(\mathsf{PIN}/\mathsf{LENS}/L),
(\mathsf{PIN}/\mathsf{SPEC}/S).Create commitment delta operations:
(\mathsf{PIN_ADD}(\mathsf{PIN}/\mathsf{INPUT}/X, h(X))),
(\mathsf{PIN_ADD}(\mathsf{PIN}/\mathsf{LENS}/L, h(L))),
(\mathsf{PIN_ADD}(\mathsf{PIN}/\mathsf{SPEC}/S, h(\mathsf{Spec}))),authorized by a commitment witness (\mathsf{COMMIT}) bound to the step context and permitted namespaces. The step must produce a ledger record and checkpoint (Appendix D, Appendix G).
P.1.2 Compile route and assemble plan
P.1.2(a) Goal object.Construct a canonical goal:[\mathsf{Goal}:=(\mathsf{goal_type}=\mathsf{EVAL},\ \mathsf{obj_type},\ h(\mathsf{Targets}),\ h(\mathsf{Context}),\ h(\mathsf{Constraints})).]Targets include:
output pin key(s) to be produced (e.g., (\mathsf{PIN}/\mathsf{OUTPUT}/Y)),
a receipt target (\mathsf{RECEIPT}/\langle h(\mathsf{Rcpt})\rangle) if the corridor requires receipt pinning.
P.1.2(b) Context and constraints.Context binds:
input pin id (\mathsf{PIN}/\mathsf{INPUT}/X),
required lens hash (h(L)),
convention bundle hash (h(\mathsf{Spec})),
checkpoint hash (h(\mathsf{Chk}_0)) when state-dependent compilation is required.Constraints bind:
required invariants (h(\mathcal I_{\mathrm{req}})),
tolerance maxima (h(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max})),
minimum verification level (\mathsf{VL}_{\min}),
determinism requirements and disclosure mode.
P.1.2(c) Route selection.Using the metro map compiler, select a deterministic shortest-legal route:[\mathsf{RoutePlan}:=(h(\mathsf{Metro}),h(\mathsf{Goal}),h(\mathsf{Context}),h(\mathsf{Constraints}),h(\mathsf{Route}),h(\mathsf{FeasCert}),h(\mathsf{Costs})?).]Feasibility requires:
convention compatibility across edges (explicit bridges inserted if needed),
budget feasibility under (\Omega),
tolerance feasibility under (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\max}) and (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega}),
verifier support feasibility (methods available under (h_{\mathsf{VerSet}})).
P.1.2(d) Plan assembly.Assemble:
micro-program (\Pi=[(\mathsf{op_id}t,h(P_t))]{t=1}^n),
witness plan (\mathsf{WPlan}),
ledger plan (\mathsf{LPlan}),into:[\mathsf{Plan}:=(h(\mathsf{Goal}),h(\mathsf{Context}),h(\mathsf{Constraints}),h(\mathsf{RoutePlan}),h(\Pi),h(\mathsf{WPlan}),h(\mathsf{LPlan}),h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}).]Plan hash (h(\mathsf{Plan})) is the canonical execution intent.
P.1.3 Execute plan, produce ledger and receipt
P.1.3(a) Ledger initialization.Create ledger header (\mathsf{LedHdr}) binding:
(h(\mathsf{Plan})),
policy hashes (h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},h_{\mathsf{CostModel}}),
genesis object (h(\mathsf{Genesis})) referencing (h(\mathsf{Chk}_0)) and store hashes.
P.1.3(b) Step execution loop.For each step (t) in (\Pi):
Expand: construct scratch workspace deterministically.
Apply: compute deltas (\Delta_t), witness candidates, cost vector (\mathbf{c}_t).
Project: run mandatory detectors, verify required witnesses, compute verdict and acceptance mask (m_t).
Update state: apply accepted deltas in canonical order.
Log: emit (\mathsf{StepRec}t) with hashes (h{\mathsf{pre}},h_{\mathsf{post}},r_\Delta,h(W_t),h(E_t),\mathbf{c}_t,\mathsf{verdict}_t,m_t).
Emit checkpoint (\mathsf{Chk}_t).
Any hard failure yields (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}) and deterministic evidence; the post-state equals pre-state for that step.
P.1.3(c) Receipt extraction.At terminal checkpoint (\mathsf{Chk}_n), extract:
output pin root (r_{\mathsf{pins}}) over target pin records,
witness root (r_{\mathsf{wits}}) over step witness hashes,
evidence root (r_{\mathsf{ev}}) if retention requires it.Create receipt:[\mathsf{Rcpt}:=(h(\mathsf{LedHdr}),h(\mathsf{Plan}),h_{\mathsf{final}},r_{\mathsf{pins}},r_{\mathsf{wits}},r_{\mathsf{ev}}?,h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}},\mathsf{mode},h(\mathsf{DiscBundle})?).]
P.1.3(d) Correctness predicate (end-to-end).The run is correct for its claim boundary iff:
ledger replay verifies under the pinned header hashes,
receipt roots match recomputed roots from the ledger terminal state,
all required proof witnesses verify at required verification levels,
contradiction policies hold in contradiction-controlled namespaces,
tolerance ledger is within (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\Omega}) and any mandatory mitigations were satisfied.
P.1.4 Reuse via dependency proofs and composed receipts
P.1.4(a) Downstream plan consumption.A downstream plan (\mathsf{Plan}_d) consumes an upstream output pin record (\mathsf{PinRecord}_u) by including:
membership proof (\mathsf{MP}_{u}) that (\mathsf{PinRecord}u\in r{\mathsf{pins}}(\mathsf{Rcpt}_u)),
provenance binding that includes (h(\mathsf{PinRecord}u)) (or its value hash under permitted provenance policy) in (r{\mathsf{src}}).
P.1.4(b) Dependency proof object.[\mathsf{ProofDep}:=(h(\mathsf{DownPlan}),[h(\mathsf{Rcpt}_u)], [h(\mathsf{PinRecord}_u)], [\mathsf{MP}_u], h(\mathsf{SrcBind}), h(\mathsf{Compat}), h(\mathsf{Result})).]Compatibility requires policy hash compatibility (or bridge receipts) and sufficient claim strength (refinement preorder).
P.1.4(c) Sequential composition of receipts.Given a downstream receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}_d) produced by executing (\mathsf{Plan}_d), define composed receipt artifact by binding dependency proofs and preserving downstream terminal state. The composed artifact commits:
downstream terminal receipt components,
dependency root over upstream receipt hash and consumed pin record membership proofs.
P.1.4(d) Anti-equivocation condition.Reuse is non-equivocable iff:
membership proofs verify against upstream roots,
consumed hashes are bound into downstream provenance roots,
and policy compatibility proofs verify (or hashes match).Absent these, “reuse” is invalid and must be rejected.
P.2 Convention mismatch repair workflow
P.2.1 Detect spec mismatch and emit defect evidence
P.2.1(a) Detection trigger.During execution or compilation feasibility checks, detect:[h(\mathsf{SpecOut}(e_i))\neq h(\mathsf{SpecIn}(e_{i+1}))]at a boundary where no explicit bridge chain exists or where required post-bridge normalizers are missing.
P.2.1(b) Defect evidence object.Emit a convention defect evidence:[\mathsf{Evidence}{\mathsf{CONVENTION}}:=(\mathsf{severity}=\mathsf{FAIL},\mathsf{code}=\mathsf{CD_SPEC_IN_MISMATCH}\ \text{or subtype},h(\mathsf{Boundary}),h(\mathsf{SpecExpected}),h(\mathsf{SpecObserved}),h(\mathsf{MissingBridgeClasses})?,h(\mathsf{MissingNormalizers})?,h(\mathsf{Bind}),h(\mathsf{PolicySet})).]Boundary binds step index and edge ids; policy set binds (h\Omega), verifier set, canon, and metro map hash if applicable.
P.2.1(c) Verdict semantics.If the mismatch occurs at a commitment boundary or other hard boundary, verdict must be (\mathsf{REJECT_REVERT}). If mismatch occurs in an exploratory, discardable context and (\Omega) permits partial acceptance, the acceptance mask may drop the affected outputs, but evidence must be retained as required.
P.2.1(d) Repair trigger mapping.Use the pinned defect→repair mapping to select repair class:
(\mathsf{BRIDGE}) if required bridge class exists and is feasible,
(\mathsf{RENORM}) if post-bridge normalization is missing,
(\mathsf{HALT}) if bridge class does not exist or is infeasible under (\Omega).
P.2.2 Insert certified bridge chain and post-bridge normalizers
P.2.2(a) Repair goal object.[\mathsf{RepGoal}:=(h(\mathsf{DefEvidence}),\mathsf{defect_class}=\mathsf{CONVENTION},h(\mathsf{RepTargetPred}),h(\mathsf{RepConstraints}),h(\mathsf{RepPolicy})).](\mathsf{RepTargetPred}) requires postcondition:[h(\mathsf{SpecOut}(e_i))=h(\mathsf{SpecIn}(b_1)),\ \ldots,\ h(\mathsf{SpecOut}(b_k))=h(\mathsf{SpecIn}(e_{i+1}))]and mandatory normalizers applied at required insertion points.
P.2.2(b) Bridge chain compilation.Compile a minimal bridge chain (B=[b_1,\ldots,b_k]) minimizing:[(\varepsilon\text{-priority},C_{\varepsilon},#B,C_{\mathsf{wit}},C_{\mathsf{budget}},\mathsf{LexKey}),]subject to feasibility under (\Omega) and constraints. Exact bridges dominate bounded bridges unless approximation is explicitly permitted.
P.2.2(c) Normalizer insertion.Insert required normalizers immediately after bridges as dictated by convention discipline and corridor mitigation policies. Normalizers must be explicit steps with their own contracts and witness requirements.
P.2.2(d) Repair plan assembly.Assemble repair plan (\mathsf{Plan}_{\mathsf{rep}}) consisting of:
revert to stable checkpoint prior to failure boundary,
execute bridge chain,
execute post-bridge normalizers,
optionally rerun failed segment prefix to re-establish route continuity,
emit repair completion proof and repair receipt.
P.2.3 Rerun segment and produce repair receipt
P.2.3(a) Rerun boundary selection.Choose deterministic rerun boundary checkpoint:
last stable anchor checkpoint preceding earliest failing step index,
or pre-step checkpoint for single boundary mismatch.
Bind this checkpoint hash in the repair plan context.
P.2.3(b) Execution and logging.Execute repair plan under the kernel, producing:
repair ledger segment,
post-repair checkpoint (\mathsf{Chk}_{\mathsf{rep}}),
repair receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}_{\mathsf{rep}}) committing bridge and normalizer outputs and evidence.
P.2.3(c) Completion proof.Emit repair completion proof object:[\mathsf{ProofRep}:=(h(\mathsf{RepGoal}),h(\mathsf{Plan}{\mathsf{rep}}),h{\mathsf{preChk}},h_{\mathsf{postChk}},h(\mathsf{DefEvidence}),h(\mathsf{DetOKSet}),\mathsf{VL}).]Verifiers confirm that the defect evidence class no longer triggers at the boundary and mandatory detectors pass.
P.2.3(d) Post-repair feasibility recheck.Recompile or revalidate the original route segment under the same metro map hash and (\Omega) to confirm that the repaired conventions permit a feasible continuation without additional bridges. Any new mismatch yields new defect evidence; repeated mismatch triggers library governance or halt per policy.
P.2.4 Supersession update and audit chain construction
P.2.4(a) Supersession record.If the repair produces a corrected pinned artifact that should replace an earlier one (e.g., corrected operator, corrected plan, corrected alias), emit supersession record:[\mathsf{Sup}:=(h(\mathsf{Old}),h(\mathsf{New}),h(\mathsf{RefProof}),\mathsf{reason_code}),]where (\mathsf{RefProof}) certifies refinement or correction and binds to (\mathsf{Rcpt}_{\mathsf{rep}}).
P.2.4(b) Index updates.Update indices (by plan hash, by output pin id) under protected namespaces only with:
authorization witnesses,
contradiction resolution witnesses if required,
evidence retention as mandated by (\Omega).
P.2.4(c) Audit chain.Construct explicit dependency chain:[h(\mathsf{DefEvidence})\to h(\mathsf{Plan}{\mathsf{rep}})\to h(\mathsf{Rcpt}{\mathsf{rep}})\to h(\mathsf{ProofRep})\to h(\mathsf{Sup})\to (\text{updated indices}).]Each arrow is a hash reference in provenance source roots and/or dependency proofs.
P.2.4(d) Repair closure condition.Repair is considered closed iff:
repaired boundary passes convention compatibility checks,
downstream plans consuming repaired artifacts reference repair receipt as dependency,
indices and aliases reflect the superseding artifact where required by governance policy.
P.3 Optimization workflow
P.3.1 Convert to canonical program normal form
P.3.1(a) Inputs.
Original plan (\mathsf{Plan}) with program (\Pi).
Normal-form policy hash (h(\mathsf{PNF})).
Optimization policy hash (h(\mathsf{OptPol})) binding rewrite rule set, ordering, and non-weakening requirements.
P.3.1(b) Normalization transform.Apply program normal form operator (\mathsf{PNF}) deterministically:[\Pi^\star := \mathsf{PNF}(\Pi),]where (\mathsf{PNF}) is idempotent and pinned. If (\Pi\neq \Pi^\star), emit a proof artifact that (\Pi) and (\Pi^\star) are equivalent under the plan’s claim boundary, either:
definitional (if (\mathsf{PNF}) is part of canonical identity semantics for programs), or
a rewrite proof at a permitted verification level.
P.3.1(c) Boundary preservation check.Verify that (\Pi^\star) preserves boundary convention bundles, required invariants, and does not weaken corridor constraints. Any deviation requires explicit bridges/normalizers or renders optimization infeasible.
P.3.1(d) Artifact commitment.Store (\Pi^\star) in proof store and optionally pin under:(\mathsf{PLAN}/\mathsf{PNF}/\langle h(\Pi)\rangle \to h(\Pi^\star)),subject to governance and scope.
P.3.2 Apply proof-carrying rewrites under policy
P.3.2(a) Rewrite rule objects.Each rewrite rule (R) is a pinned object:[\mathsf{Rule}:=(\mathsf{rule_id},h(\mathsf{Pat}),h(\mathsf{Guards}),h(\mathsf{Repl}),h(\mathsf{Claims}),h(\mathsf{PolicyReq}),h(\mathsf{Tests})).]
P.3.2(b) Deterministic rewrite application order.Apply rules in pinned rule order; scan program left-to-right; apply earliest admissible match under pinned tie-break. Maintain rewrite trace:[\Pi_0=\Pi^\star \xrightarrow{R_1} \Pi_1 \xrightarrow{R_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{R_m} \Pi_m.]Termination when no admissible matches remain or when optimization caps are reached (pinned).
P.3.2(c) Non-weakening constraint.Reject any rewrite that:
introduces additional tolerance usage when exactness is required,
changes conventions without explicit bridges and proofs,
weakens witness requirements or detector schedules,
expands scope to forbidden namespaces,
requires unsupported verifier methods.
P.3.2(d) Feasibility under (\Omega).If the optimized program (\Pi_m) is infeasible under corridor budgets or scope, it is not admissible as an executable optimization; it may only be emitted as a hypothetical artifact explicitly marked non-executable, which is typically forbidden unless (\Omega) permits such artifacts.
P.3.3 Emit rewrite proofs and optimization receipt
P.3.3(a) Rewrite proof per step.For each rewrite (\Pi_{k-1}\to \Pi_k), emit (\mathsf{ProofRw}_k) binding:
input/output program hashes,
rule id,
match evidence and guard evidence,
claim boundary (invariants/tolerances/spec),
verification level and LCD.
P.3.3(b) Optimization trace and summary.Emit a summary object:[\mathsf{OptSummary}:=(h(\Pi_0),h(\Pi_m),[\mathsf{rule_id}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{rule_id}_m],h(\mathsf{ProofSetRoot}),h(\mathsf{CostDelta}),h(\mathsf{NonWeakEv})?).]
P.3.3(c) Optimization receipt.Produce optimization receipt:[\mathsf{Rcpt}{\mathsf{opt}}:=(h(\Pi_0),h(\Pi_m),h(\mathsf{OptPol}),h(\mathsf{ProofSetRoot}),h\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}}).]This receipt certifies that (\Pi_m) is equivalent to (\Pi_0) under the claim boundary and respects non-weakening constraints.
P.3.3(d) Admission of optimized plan.If the optimization is to replace the original plan in a catalog, it must be introduced by supersession under refinement rules with explicit refinement proof binding (\mathsf{Rcpt}_{\mathsf{opt}}).
P.3.4 Execute optimized plan and validate equivalence by receipts
P.3.4(a) Execution.Execute the optimized plan (\mathsf{Plan}') (same goal/context/constraints, updated program and/or route) from the same checkpoint, producing (\mathsf{Rcpt}').
P.3.4(b) Equivalence validation.Validate equivalence between outputs of original and optimized executions by:
using (\mathsf{Rcpt}_{\mathsf{opt}}) to justify program equivalence,
or emitting an explicit output equivalence proof object (\mathsf{ProofEqOut}) comparing output pin records under required invariants and tolerances when outputs are not byte-identical but are equivalent in the claim boundary.
P.3.4(c) Output identity vs claim equivalence.If the optimization changes internal trace but preserves claimed outputs, receipts differ. If the goal requires identical output pin record bytes (not merely equivalent semantics), then optimization must preserve byte identity of outputs; otherwise it is not admissible for that goal class.
P.3.4(d) Supersession and indexing.If (\mathsf{Plan}') is intended to replace (\mathsf{Plan}) as canonical, update plan→receipt indices and alias pointers only with:
refinement proofs,
governance authorization,
and audit evidence.
P.4 Long-chain control workflow
P.4.1 Segment computation into stability anchors and checkpoints
P.4.1(a) Segmentation policy.Define segmentation policy binding:
maximum steps per segment,
required stability anchor predicate (g(\mathsf{Chk})),
mandatory post-commit audits per segment boundary.
P.4.1(b) Segment execution.Execute plan in segments:[(\Pi^{(1)},\mathsf{Chk}0)\to \mathsf{Chk}{t_1}\to (\Pi^{(2)},\mathsf{Chk}{t_1})\to \mathsf{Chk}{t_2}\to \cdots]Each segment ends at a checkpoint satisfying the stability predicate and yields a segment receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}^{(i)}).
P.4.1(c) Stability anchor checks.At segment boundaries, enforce:
contradiction-free commitment state for contradiction-controlled namespaces,
canonical normal forms for committed objects,
tolerance ledger within budgets,
mandatory detector evidence retained as required.
P.4.1(d) Segment composition.Compose segment receipts via dependency proofs, enabling replay verification per segment and localized repair without replaying the entire chain.
P.4.2 Tolerance ledger near-trigger and mandatory mitigation
P.4.2(a) Tolerance ledger update.Maintain (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\mathrm{cum}}) at each step and checkpoint using pinned accumulation operators (\oplus_I). Commit the tolerance ledger state hash in checkpoints.
P.4.2(b) Near-trigger evidence.When (\varepsilon^{\mathrm{cum}}_I > \alpha_I \varepsilon^\Omega_I) for any hard invariant (I), emit accumulation evidence with (\mathsf{NEAR}) severity and required mitigation action ids.
P.4.2(c) Mandatory mitigation actions.Mitigation actions include one or more of:
insert canonical normalization checkpoint(s),
reroute next segment to exact transforms (or tighter tolerances),
strengthen proof verification levels by proof regeneration,
reduce exploration caps to prevent additional drift.Selection among mitigations is deterministic under pinned mitigation policy.
P.4.2(d) Enforcement.If mitigation is mandatory, execution must not proceed to steps that would consume additional tolerance without satisfying mitigation obligations. Violations cause deterministic reject+revert and halt if no feasible mitigation exists.
P.4.3 Loop diagnostic, holonomy measurement, and repair routing
P.4.3(a) Closed-route diagnostic.For a route (r) that returns to the same lens/spec, execute a loop test plan that:
applies (r) to a pinned test basis of inputs,
measures holonomy defect (h_I(r;x)) for required invariants,
classifies (\mathsf{OK/NEAR/FAIL}) deterministically.
P.4.3(b) Loop evidence and proofs.Emit:
loop evidence objects with drift statistics,
optional loop-equivalence proof (\mathsf{ProofLoop}) when (\mathsf{OK}) at required verification level.
P.4.3(c) Repair routing.If loop defect is (\mathsf{NEAR}) or (\mathsf{FAIL}), compile repair plan:
if defect is convention-related: insert minimal bridge chain + normalizers, rerun loop test,
if defect is accumulation-related: reroute to reduce approximate steps, insert stabilization checkpoints,
if defect is lossy: forbid loop or acknowledge quotient semantics explicitly.Mapping is deterministic under repair policy.
P.4.3(d) Post-repair loop closure.Repair is closed when the loop diagnostic passes at the required claim boundary and the repaired route is admissible under metro map feasibility filters without additional hidden bridges.
P.4.4 Certified tunneling jump with minimal-bridge property
P.4.4(a) Tunnel goal.Given source lens/spec and target lens/spec, compile a tunneling program that:
begins at a stability anchor checkpoint,
performs required bridges and normalizers,
ends at a stability anchor checkpoint in the target region.
P.4.4(b) Minimal-bridge objective.Select tunnel route minimizing:[(\varepsilon\text{-priority},C_{\varepsilon},#\text{bridges},C_{\mathsf{wit}},C_{\mathsf{budget}},\mathsf{LexKey}),]subject to feasibility under (\Omega) and claim constraints. Exact bridge chains dominate approximate chains unless explicitly permitted.
P.4.4(c) Required bindings.Tunnel execution must bind:
source checkpoint hash,
tunnel plan hash,
bridge chain delta root,
destination state hash,
policy hashes.Tunnel receipt commits:
bridge ids used,
output roots and witness roots,
stability anchor evidence at destination.
P.4.4(d) Post-jump normalization and commitments.After the bridge chain, mandatory normalizers enforce canonical normal forms and contradiction policies. Only permitted commitments (tunnel receipt, optional navigation indices) may be written. Any additional writes require explicit scope authorization and are typically forbidden for tunnel-only operations.
APPENDIX P — WORKED EXAMPLES AND CANONICAL WORKFLOWS
APPENDIX METRO MAP — NAVIGATION AND EXTRACTION ROUTES
This appendix defines the human-readable navigation and extraction layer (“metro map”) as a deterministic, auditable routing overlay on the full corpus. The metro map is not illustrative. It is a formal navigation object: (i) a station index binding canonical references to chapters/appendices/sections, (ii) a line system binding common intent paths as ordered station sequences, (iii) transfer rules (“gates”) specifying admissibility predicates and required artifacts when switching lines, and (iv) deterministic extraction recipes specifying canonical sequences of compilation/execution/verification actions and the artifacts they must emit.
METRO.0 Metro map object model
METRO.0.1 Canonical metro map schema
Define the metro map as a COS object:[\mathsf{MetroMap}:=(\mathsf{metro_id},\mathsf{ver},h(\mathsf{Stations}),h(\mathsf{Lines}),h(\mathsf{Gates}),h(\mathsf{Recipes}),h(\mathsf{OrderPol}),h(\mathsf{Legend}),h(\mathsf{PolicyBind})).]
(\mathsf{metro_id}:=h(\mathsf{MetroMap})).
(\mathsf{Stations}) is the station index (METRO.1).
(\mathsf{Lines}) is the line catalog (METRO.2).
(\mathsf{Gates}) is the transfer rule catalog (METRO.3).
(\mathsf{Recipes}) is the deterministic extraction recipe catalog (METRO.4).
(\mathsf{OrderPol}) pins canonical ordering of stations/lines/gates/recipes and tie-break rules for route choice among equally applicable lines.
(\mathsf{Legend}) pins canonical abbreviations and reference encoding rules.
(\mathsf{PolicyBind}) binds the policy hashes under which the metro map is intended to be interpreted (at minimum (h_\Omega,h_{\mathsf{Canon}},h_{\mathsf{ObjStd}},h_{\mathsf{VerSet}})).
METRO.0.2 Station object schema
A station is a COS object:[\mathsf{Station}:=(\mathsf{st_id},\mathsf{name},\mathsf{ref},\mathsf{role},h(\mathsf{IO}),h(\mathsf{Pre}),h(\mathsf{Post}),h(\mathsf{Adj})?,\mathsf{tags}).]
(\mathsf{st_id}:=h(\mathsf{Station})).
(\mathsf{ref}) is a canonical reference record (METRO.0.3).
(\mathsf{role}) is a pinned enum describing the station’s function in the system.
(\mathsf{IO}) binds the station’s expected input artifact types and output artifact types.
(\mathsf{Pre}) and (\mathsf{Post}) bind preconditions and postconditions (as predicate ids) required to “enter” and “exit” the station in a route.
(\mathsf{Adj}) optionally binds adjacency hints for human readability; it does not override formal routing (formal routing is Chapter 18).
(\mathsf{tags}) is a canonical tag set (e.g., ({\mathsf{KERNEL},\mathsf{VERIFY},\mathsf{ROUTE},\mathsf{GOV}})).
METRO.0.3 Canonical reference record
A reference record is a typed object:[\mathsf{Ref}:=(\mathsf{doc_kind},\mathsf{doc_id},\mathsf{sec_path}),]where:
(\mathsf{doc_kind}\in{\mathsf{CHAPTER},\mathsf{APPENDIX}}),
(\mathsf{doc_id}\in{1,\ldots,21}\cup{\mathsf{A},\ldots,\mathsf{P},\mathsf{METRO}}),
(\mathsf{sec_path}) is an address path (Appendix A Path index set) locating the section/subsection group.
METRO.0.4 Line object schema
A line is a COS object:[\mathsf{Line}:=(\mathsf{line_id},\mathsf{name},\mathsf{intent},[\mathsf{st_id}_1,\ldots,\mathsf{st_id}_k],h(\mathsf{EntryReq}),h(\mathsf{ExitPost}),h(\mathsf{GateUse}),h(\mathsf{Artifacts}),h(\mathsf{TieBreak}),\mathsf{tags}).]
(\mathsf{intent}) is a pinned enum of user intents (run, verify, publish, repair, optimize, extend).
(\mathsf{EntryReq}) binds required gate satisfactions before entering the line.
(\mathsf{ExitPost}) binds postconditions required to consider the line “complete.”
(\mathsf{GateUse}) binds required transfer gates that must be applied along the line.
(\mathsf{Artifacts}) binds the canonical artifact outputs expected when traversing the line successfully.
(\mathsf{TieBreak}) binds deterministic selection among multiple applicable lines for the same intent.
METRO.0.5 Gate object schema
A gate is a COS object:[\mathsf{Gate}:=(\mathsf{gate_id},\mathsf{name},\mathsf{gate_kind},h(\mathsf{Pred}),h(\mathsf{ReqArtifacts}),h(\mathsf{EmitArtifacts}),h(\mathsf{FailCodes}),h(\mathsf{Mitigate})?,\mathsf{tags}).]
(\mathsf{gate_kind}\in{\mathsf{CHECKPOINT},\mathsf{CONVENTION},\mathsf{WITNESS},\mathsf{EVIDENCE},\mathsf{POLICY}}).
(\mathsf{Pred}) is a deterministic predicate id checked to pass the gate.
(\mathsf{ReqArtifacts}) and (\mathsf{EmitArtifacts}) specify required inputs and outputs for the gate.
(\mathsf{FailCodes}) binds deterministic failure evidence codes when the gate fails.
(\mathsf{Mitigate}) optionally binds mandatory mitigation actions if gate fails or is near-threshold.
METRO.0.6 Recipe object schema
A recipe is a COS object:[\mathsf{Recipe}:=(\mathsf{rcp_id},\mathsf{name},\mathsf{intent},h(\mathsf{Inputs}),h(\mathsf{Steps}),h(\mathsf{Outputs}),h(\mathsf{Gates}),h(\mathsf{SuccessPred}),h(\mathsf{FailPred}),h(\mathsf{TieBreak})).]Recipes are deterministic extraction procedures: given (\mathsf{Inputs}), follow (\mathsf{Steps}) to emit (\mathsf{Outputs}) or emit deterministic failure artifacts.
METRO.1 Station index and hub lattice
The station index provides canonical “stations” corresponding to key chapters and appendices, structured as hubs and spokes. Hubs are stations with high transfer degree: many lines pass through them; they correspond to system-level invariants.
METRO.1.1 Core kernel stations (Ch3, Ch4, Ch5, Ch6, AppG, AppB)
METRO.1.1(a) Kernel Hub: Legality + State Transition
Station: (\mathsf{ST_KERNEL})
Reference: (CHAPTER, 4, “4”) + (APPENDIX, G, “G”)
Role: execution semantics; expand/apply/project; state hash and deltas
Inputs: (\mathsf{Plan}), (\mathsf{StepDesc}), (\Psi), (h_\Omega)
Outputs: (\mathsf{StepRec}), (\mathsf{Chk}), deltas, masks, evidence hashes
Preconditions: runtime header hashes available; canonical object standard enforced
Postconditions: step record hashes match deterministic state transitions; reject+revert invariants hold on failure
METRO.1.1(b) Corridor Hub: Feasibility + Safety Envelope
Station: (\mathsf{ST_OMEGA})
Reference: (CHAPTER, 3, “3”) + (APPENDIX, I, “I”)
Role: budgets; scope; determinism; contradiction policy; detector schedule; governance hooks
Inputs: (h_\Omega), cost model, retention policy, throttle policy
Outputs: feasibility predicates; mitigation obligations; admissibility constraints
Preconditions: corridor policy pinned by hash
Postconditions: all route/plan legality claims are interpreted relative to this hash
METRO.1.1(c) Witness Hub: Proof-Carrying Authorization
Station: (\mathsf{ST_WITNESS})
Reference: (CHAPTER, 5, “5”) + (APPENDIX, B, “B”)
Role: witness taxonomy; provenance binding; anti-laundering; verifier rules
Inputs: witness objects; step context (\mathsf{Ctx}); verifier set
Outputs: verify/deny; deterministic failure evidence on mismatch
Preconditions: provenance witness exists; method registry resolvable
Postconditions: all authorizations bind to ((h_{\mathsf{pre}},r_\Delta,h_{\mathsf{post}}))
METRO.1.1(d) Object Standard Hub: Canonical Bytes + Hash Identity
Station: (\mathsf{ST_COS})
Reference: (CHAPTER, 6, “6”) + (APPENDIX, A, “A”)
Role: canonical serialization; hashing; Merkle roots; schema discipline
Inputs: schema hashes; encoders; type tags; numeric policies
Outputs: canonical bytes; object hashes; membership proofs
Preconditions: schema registry available
Postconditions: identical semantics → identical bytes → identical hashes
METRO.1.2 Execution/ledger/receipt stations (Ch13, Ch14, AppD, AppM)
METRO.1.2(a) Ledger Hub: Run/Log/Replay
Station: (\mathsf{ST_LEDGER})
Reference: (CHAPTER, 13, “13”) + (APPENDIX, D, “D”)
Role: ledger header; step record schema; replay verification; divergence localization
Inputs: step records; genesis checkpoint; policy hashes
Outputs: replay verdict; divergence evidence; terminal state commitments
METRO.1.2(b) Receipt Hub: Composition and Strength Algebra
Station: (\mathsf{ST_RECEIPT_ALG})
Reference: (CHAPTER, 14, “14”)
Role: receipt validity; refinement preorder; sequential composition; parallel merge; anti-equivocation
Inputs: receipts; dependency proofs; policy compatibility proofs
Outputs: composed receipts; merged receipts; supersession records
METRO.1.2(c) Receipt Formats Hub: Publication and Dependency Proof Packaging
Station: (\mathsf{ST_RECEIPT_FMT})
Reference: (APPENDIX, M, “M”)
Role: full/minimal/hash-only/selective receipts; publication bundles; indices and supersession graphs
Inputs: receipts; disclosure bundles; membership proofs
Outputs: publishable packages; indices; challenge artifacts
METRO.1.3 Library/routing stations (Ch11, Ch12, Ch18, AppE)
METRO.1.3(a) Library Hub: Transform Catalog and Admission
Station: (\mathsf{ST_LIB})
Reference: (CHAPTER, 11, “11”) + (APPENDIX, F, “F”)
Role: transform entries; hub transforms; bridge closure; extension plugins; admission packets
Inputs: legal transform contracts; implementation descriptors; admission receipts
